The paper observes that humans perform multi-step meta-cognitive reasoning (perceive → analyze candidates → refine conclusions) rather than direct matching, and argues that MLLMs' zero-shot cross-modal reasoning capabilities are underutilized for this task. Methodology: The paper proposes GAR-SSL, a training-free three-stage pipeline built entirely on prompt-engineered MLLM calls (Qwen2.5-Omni-7B), with no gradient updates or task-specific fine-tuning. Stage 1 (Generation) independently produces an initial bounding box with a natural-language description via audio-visual grounding, and separately classifies the audio into an open-vocabulary label with a self-reported confidence score. Stage 2 (Analysis) performs N=5 stochastic runs of a consistency check: it discovers open-set role tags (visible functional parts, e.g., "violin body", "bow on strings"), assigns anchor votes (fine-grained spatial evidence tokens with confidence scores), computes an Audio-Visual Consistency score Sav ∈ [0,1], and outputs a binary keep flag. Results are aggregated via majority voting and score averaging. An adaptive gating rule triggers Stage 3 only when any of three conditions fails (keep flag, Sav ≥ 0.5, audio confidence ≥ 0.75). Stage 3 (Refinement) applies one of four geometric operations — delta shift, expand/shrink, or recenter — guided by the anchor evidence, to produce the final bounding box. All stages emit structured JSON and chain together without any learned parameters. Key Results: On single-source benchmarks, GAR-SSL (N=5) achieves 60.5% AP, 60.2% IoU@0.5, and 55.2% AUC on VGGSound-Single, outperforming the prior SOTA OA-SSL (CVPR'25) by 8.8% AP, 12.9% IoU, and 10.3% AUC. On MUSIC-Solo, it achieves 98.5% IoU@0.5 — an extraordinary gain over OA-SSL's 71.1%. On multi-source benchmarks, on MUSIC-Duet the method reaches 82.7% CIoU@0.3 (+34.9 points over OA-SSL's 45.9%) and 53.2% AUC (+17.1 points). On VGGSound-Duet it achieves 77.6% CIoU@0.3 (+22.4 points over OA-SSL) and 45.8% AUC. Ablation confirms each stage contributes: adding Analysis+Refinement improves CIoU@0.3 by 16.9 points on VGGSound-Duet over Generation alone. More iterations N consistently help, with N=5 best across all metrics. The framework is also MLLM-agnostic: the 7B model substantially outperforms the 3B variant, suggesting direct scaling benefits. Strengths: - Achieves state-of-the-art on all four benchmarks without any training, gradient updates, or dataset-specific tuning — a compelling demonstration that MLLM reasoning can substitute for learned feature matchers. - The open-set role tagging and anchor voting provide interpretable, verifiable intermediate outputs, addressing a genuine weakness of black-box SSL methods. - Adaptive gating is a sensible efficiency mechanism that avoids over-refinement when the initial prediction is already reliable, and the ablation validates its contribution. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Inference is approximately 4 seconds per sample using a 7B-parameter model, making the method roughly 100x slower than trained convolutional baselines — this cost is not adequately discussed relative to the multi-call (5× Analysis + Generation + Refinement) structure. - The self-reported audio confidence score used in gating is an uncalibrated MLLM output; there is no analysis of how well it tracks actual audio classification accuracy or how sensitive results are to the choice of fixed thresholds (0.75 / 0.5). - Evaluation is limited to two datasets (VGGSound, MUSIC), both heavily biased toward musical instruments. Generalization to non-musical, real-world acoustic scenes (e.g., industrial sounds, street noise, speech) is untested and the claim of broad applicability is unsubstantiated.
Search Results 2604.06824 PDF
#14 — Highly Innovative (79)

From $P(y|x)$ to $P(y)$: Investigating Reinforcement Learning in Pre-train Space (23 ▲ )

Yuqiao Tan, Minzheng Wang, Bo Liu, Zichen Liu, Tian Liang, Shizhu He, Jun Zhao, Kang Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 79 for introducing a genuinely novel conceptual framework — optimizing the marginal P(y) rather than the conditional P(y|x) during RL — that reframes how reinforcement learning interacts with pretrained knowledge. The theoretical gradient alignment analysis is unusually tight, with direct empirical verification across 400 rollouts confirming the first-order prediction. The PSR/NSR decomposition in pre-train space reveals a non-obvious and practically actionable asymmetry: negative sample reinforcement with question masking achieves 3× sample efficiency over standard GRPO in just 20 warmup steps, while the same strategy in post-train space actually degrades performance. This counterintuitive finding, combined with the elegant simplicity of the masking mechanism, suggests a potentially influential new design axis for LLM RL pipelines, though the current evidence is confined to mathematical reasoning on relatively small models.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) for LLMs optimizes the conditional distribution P(y|x), but its ceiling is fundamentally bounded by the capabilities already latent in the base model. Prior work has shown that RLVR largely over-sharpens the existing output distribution rather than expanding it. This paper asks whether applying RL to the marginal (pre-train space) distribution P(y) — removing the input conditioning during gradient updates — can break this ceiling by encoding broader reasoning capacity before post-train RL fine-tunes it. Methodology: The paper introduces PreRL (Pre-train Space RL), which modifies the standard GRPO objective by masking the input question x during the policy gradient update, optimizing log πθ(yt|y<t) rather than log πθ(yt|x, y<t). Theoretical justification rests on a first-order Taylor expansion showing that updating the marginal log-probability improves the conditional when the two gradients have non-negative inner product. This is empirically validated on Qwen3-4B: 100% of 400 rollouts yield positive gradient dot products (mean +9.2) and positive cosine similarities (mean 0.44). The paper then decomposes PreRL into Positive Sample Reinforcement (PSR-PreRL) and Negative Sample Reinforcement (NSR-PreRL). PSR-PreRL collapses because self-generated on-policy positives accumulate probability mass without broadening the distribution. NSR-PreRL, which suppresses incorrect trajectories, proves surprisingly effective: after just 20 steps it increases transition thoughts by 14.89× and reflection thoughts by 6.54×, reaching 86% accuracy with 3× fewer steps than standard GRPO. The core proposal is Dual Space RL (DSRL): a Policy Reincarnation strategy that first warms up with NSR-PreRL for 10–25 steps to prune incorrect reasoning paths and expand the exploration horizon, then restarts standard on-policy GRPO for fine-grained conditional optimization. Experiments use Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B trained on MATH (7,500 problems), evaluated via Avg@32 and Pass@K (n=300 samples) across MATH500, AMC23, AIME24, AIME25, Minerva, and OlympiadBench. Key Results: DSRL consistently outperforms all baselines across both model scales. For Qwen3-4B, DSRL achieves an average score of 57.54 vs. GRPO's 55.79, Dr.GRPO's 57.04, and DAPO's 55.82 — with standout gains of +4.69 on AIME24 and +2.50 on AIME25. For Qwen3-8B, DSRL reaches 58.47 average vs. GRPO's 57.00 and PPO's 57.34, with +2.09 on AIME24 and +2.82 on AIME25. Pass@K curves show DSRL dominates GRPO across the full K range from 1 to 256, indicating broader coverage of the correct-reasoning subspace, not just sharper greedy behavior. On OOD benchmarks, DSRL improves over GRPO by +3.79/+5.37 on GPQA-Diamond/MMLU-Pro for Qwen3-4B and +2.52/+4.32 for Qwen3-8B. Training efficiency is also improved: DSRL reaches 45% accuracy 2.5× faster and 58% accuracy 1.6× faster than GRPO. An ablation on warmup steps confirms a sweet spot at 10–25 steps, and a direct comparison shows NSR-RL warmup (post-train space) paradoxically hurts performance (54.38 avg), confirming that the pre-train space is essential for NSR's benefit. Strengths: - The core theoretical claim — gradient alignment between marginal and conditional objectives — is backed by a clean first-order analysis and then directly verified empirically with 400 rollouts, making the connection between theory and experiment unusually tight. - Identifying the PSR/NSR asymmetry in the pre-train space is a non-obvious and practically actionable finding; NSR-PreRL achieving 3× sample efficiency over standard GRPO with only 20 warmup steps is a striking result that motivates the full DSRL design. - The ablation showing that NSR applied in the post-train space (NSR-RL warmup) actually degrades performance relative to GRPO demonstrates that the pre-train space (question masking) is the essential ingredient, not just the negative-sample strategy. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are confined to mathematical reasoning with Qwen3-4B/8B; it is unclear whether the gradient alignment property and NSR benefits transfer to non-math domains, larger models, or models without strong pre-existing reasoning distributions. - The mechanism by which NSR-PreRL stimulates reflective behavior (14.89×/6.54× increases in transition/reflection thoughts) is characterized descriptively but not mechanistically explained — it is not clear why removing the question condition during suppression of negatives specifically triggers this internal exploration. - The PSR-PreRL collapse is explained by analogy to QFFT (which uses high-quality teacher demonstrations off-policy), but no controlled experiment isolates whether the collapse is due to distribution mismatch, reward sparsity, or the marginal objective itself, leaving an open design question for practitioners who might want to use PSR in this setting.
Recommended 2604.14142 PDF
#15 — Highly Innovative (79)

Safeguarding Language Models via Self-Destruct Trapdoor

aclanthology.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: This paper scores 79 for its genuinely novel exploitation of hardware-level floating-point overflow behavior as an in-weights defense mechanism — a creative conceptual leap that repurposes a numerical property universally ignored in the safety literature. The emergent adversarial robustness (NaN gradients blocking fine-tuning and GCG attacks) is a surprising and non-obvious side effect that strengthens the contribution beyond the primary defense goal. The SVD-based null-space calibration framework demonstrates impressive generality, extending to fingerprinting and bias mitigation. However, the defense is trivially detectable and reversible via simple weight magnitude thresholding, which fundamentally limits its practical security guarantees and prevents a higher score.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Language models (LMs) remain vulnerable to jailbreak attacks that bypass alignment training, and existing defenses fall into unsatisfying trade-offs: alignment training is brittle and computationally expensive, external input/output filters add inference overhead and rely on auxiliary models, and interpretability-based monitors are limited in maturity and scope. A critical hardware-level property of modern LM frameworks—that limited-precision floating-point formats (BF16, FP16, FP32) trigger deterministic overflow errors when matrix products exceed representable range—has been entirely overlooked as a potential defense mechanism. This work exploits that gap. Methodology: Self-Destruct embeds a protective trapdoor directly into a single attention head's query and key weight matrices using a three-step, training-free calibration procedure. Step 1 (Null-space Isolation): compute the SVD of benign activations H and extract V₀, the right null space, which spans directions invisible to benign inputs. Step 2 (Targeted Subspace Extraction): project harmful activations H′ into this null space and extract the top-δ right singular vectors via a second SVD, identifying directions that maximally activate on H′ while remaining suppressed on H. Step 3 (Head Injection): map these directions back to model space, scale them by a destruction factor D_factor such that harmful activations produce query-key dot products approaching the BF16/FP32 maximum (≈3.39×10³⁸), and add the result to one attention head's Ŵ_q and Ŵ_k. For harmful inputs, the injected component dominates and causes ‖(q+q′)(k+k′)ᵀ‖ ≈ Ω → overflow; for benign inputs, the null-space construction ensures the contribution is suppressed (≈0). K-means clustering of harmful activations improves coverage across diverse harmful prompt distributions. The destruction factor is selected via binary search on a validation set, and all hyperparameters (layer, head index, SVD width δ, cluster count n) are chosen by grid search maximizing Δ = RefusalRate(harmful) − RefusalRate(harmless). Key Results: Evaluated across 10 models from 5 families (Falcon3, Llama-3.2, Mistral-0.3, Qwen2.5, Gemma2; 1B–14B parameters) on four jailbreak benchmarks (MalwareGen, PromptInject, PAIR, AutoDAN) and four QA capability benchmarks (PIQA, OpenBookQA, SIQA, ARC). Self-Destruct achieves the lowest average ASR in 9 of 10 models. Representative reductions: MalwareGen ASR Qwen2.5-7B 39→1%, Mistral-7B 67→4%, Gemma-2-2B 10→1%; AutoDAN ASR drops to 0 on Qwen2.5-3B/7B/14B; PAIR ASR Qwen2.5-14B 33→15%, Gemma-2-9B 22→1%. Calibration achieves Δ ≥ 0.97 on both train and test splits across all models, with 7B+ models typically reaching Δ = 0.99. On capability benchmarks, the strongest models show near-zero accuracy degradation (Qwen2.5-14B avg Δ ≈ 0, Gemma-2-2B avg +1.2), while mid-size models incur moderate drops (Mistral-7B avg −3.2, Qwen2.5-7B avg −5.5). Crucially, fine-tuning the protected model fails entirely—backpropagation yields NaN gradients from the first iteration regardless of optimizer, batch size, or dataset, making the model effectively untrainable. Gradient-based GCG attacks also diverge immediately against protected models. The mechanism extends to model fingerprinting (Δ = 1.0 on Gemma-2-2B with human-readable passcodes; Δ = 0.95 with random 12–20-character strings on Qwen2.5-3B) and bias mitigation on BBQ (Δ = 1.0 across all tested configurations). Compatibility is confirmed across NVIDIA GPU generations, Apple MPS, attention backends (EAGER, SDPA, FLEX), and vLLM. Strengths: - Genuinely novel exploitation of floating-point overflow as an in-weights defense: zero inference overhead since the mechanism fires simultaneously with generation, unlike perplexity filtering (requires full-text generation) or SmoothLLM/Self-Defense (require extra LM runs). - Strong adversarial robustness against both fine-tuning and gradient-based attacks (GCG) as an emergent consequence of high-magnitude weight injection corrupting gradient flow—a notable and non-obvious side effect. - Broad versatility: the same SVD-based calibration framework extends to model fingerprinting and bias mitigation with only a dataset swap, demonstrating generality of the underlying mechanism. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The trapdoor is easily detectable and reversible by any user with model access: injected weights have magnitudes of 10¹⁷–10¹⁸ versus typical two-digit values, making a simple threshold scan (e.g., >10⁵) sufficient to identify and zero out the affected head—a fundamental transparency limitation the authors acknowledge. - Evaluation is restricted to BF16/FP32 and standard attention implementations; behavior under quantization (INT8, FP8, INT4), FP64, or cross-precision inference pipelines is entirely uncharacterized, which is a significant gap given the prevalence of quantized deployment. - The accuracy-refusal tradeoff is model-family-dependent and unpredictable: Qwen2.5-7B loses 5.5 points average QA accuracy and shows elevated refusal on benign PIQA/SIQA prompts, while Falcon3-7B gains 17.8 points—suggesting the mechanism's selectivity is sensitive to architecture in ways that are not yet well understood or controllable.
Recommended
#16 — Highly Innovative (79)

Lyra 2.0: Explorable Generative 3D Worlds (17 ▲ )

Tianchang Shen, Sherwin Bahmani, Kai He, Sangeetha Grama Srinivasan, Tianshi Cao, Jiawei Ren, Ruilong Li, Zian Wang, Nicholas Sharp, Zan Gojcic, Sanja Fidler, Jiahui Huang, Huan Ling, Jun Gao, Xuanchi Ren · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Lyra 2.0 makes a principled architectural contribution by decoupling geometric information routing from appearance synthesis—warping canonical coordinate maps rather than RGB images to avoid the artifact-propagation trap that fundamentally limits prior 3D-conditioned video generation approaches. The self-augmentation training strategy that teaches the model to correct imperfect conditioning rather than propagate it is a lightweight but effective solution to the temporal drifting problem that plagues autoregressive generation. The complete single-image-to-explorable-3D-world pipeline with thorough evaluation across both in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks demonstrates genuine system-level innovation, though the restriction to static scenes limits the paradigm-shifting potential.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Generating large-scale, explorable 3D environments from a single image requires maintaining 3D consistency over arbitrarily long camera trajectories with large viewpoint changes and location revisits. Existing video diffusion models fail in this regime due to two fundamental degradation modes: (1) spatial forgetting—previously observed regions fall outside the model's finite temporal context window, causing hallucination of structures when revisited; and (2) temporal drifting—autoregressive generation accumulates small per-step synthesis errors (color shifts, blurring, geometric distortions) that compound over long sequences. Prior methods address at most one of these problems: approaches using a global accumulated 3D representation suffer from error amplification as generative artifacts corrupt the geometry used for conditioning, while purely retrieval-based approaches lack geometric grounding for precise multi-view alignment. Methodology: Lyra 2.0 is built on a fine-tuned Wan 2.1-14B DiT backbone and operates through an autoregressive retrieve–generate–update loop. To combat spatial forgetting, the system maintains a per-frame 3D cache (depth maps + downsampled point clouds) that is used exclusively for information routing—never fused into a global representation. At each step, a geometry-aware greedy retrieval selects the Ns=5 history frames with maximal 3D visibility overlap with target viewpoints. These frames are injected as spatial slots alongside temporal FramePack history. Critically, rather than warping RGB images (which introduces disocclusion holes and depth-boundary bleeding), the system warps canonical coordinate maps (u, v, frame-index channels + depth) and injects them into DiT self-attention queries and keys, providing dense geometric correspondence without contaminating appearance synthesis with rendering artifacts. To combat temporal drifting, a lightweight self-augmentation training strategy addresses the observation bias problem: with probability paug=0.7, history latents are corrupted via the flow-matching schedule (noise level t ~ U(0,0.5)) and one-step-denoised before being used as conditioning context, while the loss supervises toward clean targets—training the model to correct rather than propagate imperfect conditioning. Camera control is implemented via dual mechanisms: depth-based warping of the most recent frame and Plücker ray injection into Q/K projections. Generated videos are lifted to 3D Gaussians via a fine-tuned Depth Anything v3 model (with reduced Gaussian count via k×k DPT head downsampling) and further to surface meshes via hierarchical sparse-grid marching cubes using OpenVDB. A DMD-distilled student model reduces inference from 35 to 4 denoising steps (~13× speedup). Key Results: On the DL3DV benchmark (in-domain), Lyra 2.0 achieves SSIM=0.388, LPIPS=0.498, FID=43.43, Subjective Quality=44.54, Style Consistency=87.46, and Camera Controllability=64.67, outperforming all six baselines (GEN3C, Yume-1.5, CaM, VMem, SPMem, HY-WorldPlay) on most metrics. On Tanks-and-Temples (out-of-domain), results similarly dominate with SSIM=0.384, LPIPS=0.552, FID=51.33, Subjective Quality=43.35, Style Consistency=85.07. For 3D scene generation, Ours Full achieves FID=65.94 and Subjective Quality=20.52 on DL3DV, substantially better than the next-best baseline (SPMem+DAv3: FID=93.56, Qual=13.72). Ablations confirm all components are necessary: removing self-augmentation drops Style Consistency from 85.07 to 77.98 and Camera Controllability from 63.87 to 53.92; replacing per-frame 3D cache with a global point cloud drops Camera Controllability to 49.86; removing FramePack increases Reprojection Error from 0.069 to 0.079. The DMD model achieves comparable per-frame quality (LPIPS, FID slightly better) at 15s/step versus 194s/step for the full model. Strengths: - The decoupling of geometric information routing from appearance synthesis is a principled and practically effective design choice—using canonical coordinate warping instead of RGB warping avoids the artifact-propagation trap that plagues prior methods using rendered 3D conditioning. - The self-augmentation anti-drifting strategy is lightweight (one extra forward pass), architecturally non-invasive, and directly targets the train-inference discrepancy that causes error accumulation—more principled than simply extending context length. - The end-to-end pipeline from single image to simulation-ready 3DGS and surface meshes is complete and evaluated thoroughly across both in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks with a rich metric suite including perceptual quality, style consistency, camera controllability, and geometric reprojection error. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The system is limited to static scenes and cannot model dynamic objects or moving elements, which substantially limits applicability to real-world embodied AI scenarios where dynamic agents and objects are the norm. - Training is confined to the DL3DV dataset, which contains photometric inconsistencies (exposure variation across views) that the model reproduces, causing artifacts in 3DGS reconstruction; the lack of photometrically consistent synthetic training data is a recognized gap that is only gestured at rather than solved. - At ~194 seconds per 80-frame autoregressive step on an NVIDIA GB200 (a very high-end GPU), even the distilled 15s/step variant may be impractical for real-time interactive exploration at scale, and memory cost of the per-frame 3D cache grows unboundedly with trajectory length.
Recommended 2604.13036 PDF
#17 — Highly Innovative (78)

MedLVR: Latent Visual Reasoning for Reliable Medical Visual Question Answering

Suyang Xi, Songtao Hu, Yuxiang Lai, Wangyun Dan, Yaqi Liu, Shansong Wang, Xiaofeng Yang · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: MedLVR introduces a genuinely novel architectural mechanism — latent rollouts in hidden-state space that maintain a persistent, continuously updated visual evidence state during decoding, without adding any new modules. The VLPO formulation with Gaussian surrogate policies for optimizing continuous latent actions is technically principled and clearly differentiated from naive text-only RL approaches. A 7B model outperforming 72B competitors while generating only 7.8 tokens per sample is a surprising and counterintuitive result that challenges the prevailing assumption that verbose chain-of-thought reasoning improves medical VQA. The 800K grounding corpus across 8 modalities is a substantial data contribution, though modality-specific weaknesses and modest out-of-domain gains temper the overall impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Medical vision-language models (VLMs) for visual question answering (VQA) encode images once as static context and then reason primarily through autoregressive text generation. This text-centric paradigm causes reasoning to drift toward language priors, weakening grounding in subtle, localized visual evidence that is diagnostically critical (e.g., small lesions in CT/MRI). Existing remedies fall into two unsatisfying camps: "thinking about images" (text-space chain-of-thought) still reasons over textual descriptions rather than visual semantics, and "thinking with images" (zooming/cropping/tool calls) imposes heavy inference overhead and does not reliably constrain decoding. The gap is a structural one — no persistent, explicitly updated visual evidence state exists during decoding. Methodology: MedLVR inserts a fixed-length latent reasoning segment between the question and the answer during autoregressive decoding. When the model emits a special control token `<Medlvr_Start>`, it switches from token emission to a latent rollout: the last-layer hidden state at each step is fed back directly as the next-step input embedding for K steps, yielding a trajectory H_lat = {h_k} in the same d-dimensional space as the projected visual tokens. After K steps the model emits `<Medlvr_End>` and resumes standard token generation. This is a decoding-only modification — the backbone architecture is unchanged. Training proceeds in two stages. Stage 1 (ROI-SFT): for each image–question pair with a bounding-box annotation, ROI visual tokens are extracted from the ViT patch grid, partitioned into K buckets, and averaged to produce position-aligned supervision targets {v_k}. A mean-squared alignment loss L_align = (1/K) Σ||h_k − v_k||² anchors each latent step to clinically relevant patch evidence. The combined SFT loss is L_sft = L_gen + λ·L_align. Stage 2 (VLPO): outcome-level RL jointly optimizes the latent rollout and answer generation. Text token importance ratios are computed via a replay pass that patches recorded latent states into both the current and old policy. Latent importance ratios use a Gaussian surrogate policy: r^lat_k(θ) = exp(−D^(i)_k(θ) / 2σ²), where D^(i)_k is the squared L2 deviation of the current policy's latent mean from the recorded state. Both are optimized with PPO-style clipping and a KL penalty to a reference policy. The training corpus for Stage 1 is an in-house grounding dataset of ~800K QA-box triples from 31 public datasets across eight imaging modalities (CT, MRI, X-ray, Ultrasound, Dermoscopy, Fundus, OCT, Microscopy). Stage 2 RL uses the open-access OmniMedVQA split (88,996 pairs). The backbone is Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct; K=8 latent steps at inference. Key Results: On OmniMedVQA (in-domain, 8 modalities), MedLVR achieves 78.0% overall accuracy, surpassing Qwen2-VL-72B (68.1%), HealthGPT (68.0%), Med-R1 (71.1%), and ViTAR (74.2%). It is particularly strong on CT (80.4%) and MRI (82.0%), and maintains a more balanced cross-modality profile than competitors. On five out-of-domain benchmarks (SLAKE, VQA-RAD, PMC-VQA, MMMU Health & Medicine, MedXpertQA), MedLVR raises average accuracy from 48.3% (base Qwen2.5-VL-7B) to 53.4%, with the largest gain on MMMU-HM (+10.2 pp) and VQA-RAD (+6.0 pp). MedLVR outperforms reasoning-oriented baselines MedVLM-R1 (43.3%) and Med-R1 (41.0%) on the external average. Ablations confirm a consistent hierarchy: vanilla SFT+GRPO < latent segment without ROI supervision < VLPO with ROI-SFT. Efficiency analysis shows MedLVR generates only 7.8 tokens/sample on average while achieving the highest accuracy (45.2%), demonstrating that compact grounded outputs outperform verbose text-reasoning chains. Attention visualizations confirm that MedLVR allocates more visual attention to clinically relevant spatial regions at deeper layers compared to the base model. Strengths: - Elegant architectural contribution: the latent rollout reuses existing hidden states with no new modules, keeping the backbone unchanged while adding an explicit, continuously updated evidence state. - Principled RL formulation (VLPO) that directly optimizes continuous latent actions via a Gaussian surrogate policy, resolving the non-differentiability of latent importance ratios — more principled than applying GRPO naively only to text tokens. - Large-scale grounding corpus (800K QA-box pairs, 31 datasets, 8 modalities) addresses a real data bottleneck for ROI-supervised training and could benefit the broader community. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Underperforms specialized baselines on Ultrasound (51.8% vs. ViTAR's 84.9%), OCT, and Microscopy (below HealthGPT's 89.3% and 88.2%), indicating the method is not uniformly superior and may be modality-sensitive despite claims of balance. - The VLPO Gaussian surrogate introduces the hyperparameter σ controlling latent update scale, and the sensitivity of results to this choice is not analyzed; the theoretical justification for the Gaussian assumption on hidden-state actions is heuristic. - The out-of-domain gains are modest on difficult benchmarks (MedXpertQA: +1.8 pp; SLAKE: +2.7 pp), and the method still lags substantially behind closed-source frontier models (OpenAI-o3 averages 64.3% vs. MedLVR's 53.4%), suggesting the approach has meaningful headroom yet to close.
Citations 2604.09757 PDF
#18 — Highly Innovative (78)

Visual Enhanced Depth Scaling for Multimodal Latent Reasoning

Yudong Han, Yong Wang, Zaiquan Yang, Zhen Qu, Liyuan Pan, Xiangxiang Chu · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: VEDAS scores 78 for its empirically grounded approach to multimodal latent reasoning, where the two core modules (SCF-VR and RDS) are directly motivated by measured gradient pathologies rather than intuition alone. The curriculum latent training strategy that progressively replaces explicit CoT steps with latent tokens is a creative alternative to teacher distillation. Achieving state-of-the-art latent reasoning with only 30K training samples versus 125K-470K for competitors demonstrates strong inductive biases from the design. The zero inference overhead property makes this practically deployable, and the 10x reduction in decoding steps over explicit CoT is substantial.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal latent reasoning—replacing explicit Chain-of-Thought (CoT) token generation with continuous latent vector propagation—offers faster inference and reduced annotation overhead compared to text-based reasoning. However, existing latent reasoning approaches suffer from two underexplored optimization pathologies. First, a visual-text optimization disparity: visual tokens exhibit persistently higher and more volatile gradient Frobenius norms than textual tokens throughout training, indicating that the dominant textual modality crowds out fine-grained visual learning. Second, a fixed-depth optimization dilemma: hard samples (those consistently answered incorrectly) maintain high gradient nuclear norms late into training, whereas easy samples converge smoothly. Fixed-depth architectures cannot adapt to this heterogeneity, trapping complex examples in oscillatory optimization. These findings motivate targeted architectural solutions rather than generic capacity scaling. Methodology: The proposed framework, VEDAS, introduces two synergistic modules operating during the implicit reasoning phase. (1) Spatially-Coherent Finer Visual Replay (SCF-VR): At each latent reasoning step, attention weights are aggregated across all transformer layers and heads to score visual token salience. The top-K attended visual tokens are selected (with a visited-set mechanism to prevent redundant re-selection) and weighted by normalized attention scores to form visual latents. To enforce spatial coherence and counter the attention sink phenomenon, a self-distillation loss is applied: a W×W sub-grid window maximizing attended token density is cropped from the original image, re-encoded by the visual encoder, and used as a high-fidelity reference target for a lightweight token super-resolution module FSR. This auxiliary reconstruction loss (L_recon) is added to the cross-entropy objective with weight λ. (2) Routing Depth Scaling (RDS): A lightweight per-layer router network maps each token's hidden state to a scalar importance score. The top-α tokens at each transformer layer undergo additional depth-scaling iterations—re-running the same layer's transformation—while non-critical tokens bypass extra computation. Final representations aggregate depth-wise updates with step-aware positional encoding. (3) Curriculum Latent Training: Rather than distilling from teacher latent states, the model begins with full explicit CoT supervision, then progressively replaces one reasoning step at a time with a latent token, allowing each latent to organically absorb the contextual dependencies of the step it replaces. At inference, the SCF-VR module is disabled entirely, incurring zero additional overhead. Key Results: Evaluated across 12 benchmarks spanning mathematics (MathVista, MathVision, MM-Math), vision-centric reasoning (MMVP, HallusionBench, HRBench, SeedBench-2-Plus, GQA), and compositional reasoning (MMStar, BLINK, ScienceQA, M3CoT). On the three CoT-annotation benchmarks where methods are directly trained: VEDAS with Qwen2-VL-7B achieves 73.0% on M3CoT, 95.9% on ScienceQA, and 67.4% on GQA, surpassing the second-best latent baseline (IVT-LR) by an average of +2.63%. Compared to explicit CoT methods, the average accuracy gain is ~30% on these benchmarks while reducing autoregressive decoding steps by 10× (7.0–9.2 steps vs. 77–185 steps) and wall-clock inference to ~0.9s versus 2–7s. On the broader benchmark suite (Table 2), VEDAS achieves the best MMVP score among all methods (76.67%, +4.67% over Laser) using only 30K training samples versus 267K for Laser and 470K for LVR. Ablations confirm that RDS contributes +1.4–3.4% across backbones and benchmarks, while SCF-VR adds a further +0.4–3.6%, with larger gains on smaller models. The gains are less pronounced on text-intensive benchmarks (SeedBench-2-Plus: 66.86%), which the authors attribute to domain-specific data scarcity. Strengths: - Empirically grounded design: the two proposed modules are directly motivated by measured gradient dynamics (Frobenius and nuclear norms), not intuition alone, making the technical narrative unusually well-justified. - Data efficiency: VEDAS achieves state-of-the-art latent reasoning performance with only 30K training samples, compared to 125K–470K for competing latent methods, suggesting the curriculum and self-distillation provide strong inductive biases. - Zero inference overhead: the visual replay module is disabled at test time, so the efficiency advantage over CoT methods is clean and not offset by auxiliary components. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Requires eager attention mode to access internal attention maps for the SCF-VR module, precluding the use of FlashAttention and likely increasing training memory and time—a significant practical drawback at larger scales not quantified in the paper. - The number of latent reasoning steps is fixed at Tr=4 and the maximum refinement depth at D=1, which are very constrained settings; it is unclear whether the method scales favorably to harder tasks requiring deeper reasoning chains, such as multi-hop visual question answering or long-context video understanding.
Search Results 2604.10500 PDF
#19 — Highly Innovative (78)

Toward Autonomous Long-Horizon Engineering for ML Research (23 ▲ )

Guoxin Chen, Jie Chen, Lei Chen, Jiale Zhao, Fanzhe Meng, Wayne Xin Zhao, Ruihua Song, Cheng Chen, Ji-Rong Wen, Kai Jia · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 78 for the File-as-Bus architectural insight, which reframes multi-agent coordination as a file-system problem — a surprisingly clean solution to the persistent state loss that plagues long-horizon agent workflows. The decisive ablation (31.82-point MLE-Bench drop) isolates durable state as the primary driver rather than agent sophistication, and the asymmetric degradation pattern (submissions intact, medal tiers collapse) provides genuine mechanistic understanding. Achieving better-than-prior results at 4–5x lower cost demonstrates that structured state reuse compounds over long horizons, a finding with broad implications for agent system design. The thin-control-over-thick-state principle is a meaningful departure from the trend toward heavier orchestration.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Autonomous AI research agents have made progress on narrow subtasks (idea generation, code synthesis, targeted experimentation), but fail to sustain coherent progress across the full end-to-end loop of ML research engineering — comprehending underspecified papers, setting up environments, implementing systems, running experiments, diagnosing failures, and iteratively refining over hours or days. The core difficulty is that early decisions compound into later failures in confounded ways, and existing agents lose project state across stage transitions by relying on lossy conversational handoffs. On PaperBench, the best prior agent achieves only 21% versus 41% for top ML PhDs given 48 hours, revealing a wide gap. Methodology: AiScientist is built on the principle of thin control over thick state. Control is kept lightweight through a three-tier hierarchical research team: a Tier-0 Orchestrator makes stage-level decisions using only concise summaries and a compact workspace map; Tier-1 specialists (Paper Comprehension, Prioritization, Implementation, Experimentation, Generic Helper) handle domain-specific work within private local contexts; and Tier-2 subagents perform tightly scoped leaf tasks in parallel (e.g., extracting algorithm details or downloading resources). Specialists are exposed to the Orchestrator via an Agent-as-Tool interface, making delegation a first-class action within the Orchestrator's decision space rather than a separate protocol. State is kept durable through a File-as-Bus protocol: a permission-scoped shared workspace with three artifact regions (paper_analysis/, submission/, agent/) serves as the system of record. Rather than compressing project state into context windows at each handoff, agents re-enter from the current workspace, read relevant artifacts on demand, and write back structured analyses, code, logs, and experiment records. Permission scoping (read-only, read+limited write, read+full write) limits cross-agent interference and keeps append-only logs traceable. The overall loop is evidence-driven: experimental failures trigger targeted fixes rather than reruns, and progress accumulates as inspectable artifacts across the full paper→environment→code→experiment→debug cycle. Key Results: On PaperBench (20-task from-scratch paper replication), AiScientist with GLM-5 achieves an average score of 33.73, a +11.15-point gain over the best matched baseline (IterativeAgent at 22.58), narrowing the gap to the reported 41% human baseline. With Gemini-3-Flash it scores 30.52 (+9.92 over BasicAgent). Crucially, AiScientist also achieves these gains at lower cost: $12.20/task versus $54.90 for IterativeAgent under GLM-5, and $15.67 versus $27.44 under Gemini-3-Flash. On MLE-Bench Lite (competition-style iterative ML optimization), AiScientist attains 81.82 Any Medal% under both backbones — a +4.55 to +18.18-point gain over the strongest controlled baselines (LoongFlow and ML-Master 2.0), and higher than all official leaderboard entries in Table 2 (highest prior: 77.27%). Ablation: removing File-as-Bus drops PaperBench by 6.41 points and MLE-Bench Lite Any Medal% by 31.82 points. The MLE-Bench degradation is concentrated in above-median and medal tiers, confirming that durable state continuity is most critical for later-round refinement rather than for obtaining a minimal initial submission. A single concrete example: on the Detecting Insults task, AiScientist ran 74 experiment cycles autonomously over 23 hours, raising validation AUC from 0.903 to 0.982 through 18 best-so-far updates. Strengths: - The File-as-Bus design is well-motivated and technically clean: it reframes the coordination problem as a file-system problem, which naturally accommodates the already-file-valued artifacts of ML research engineering (code, logs, configs, results) without introducing a custom message-passing protocol. - The ablation is decisive — a 31.82-point drop on MLE-Bench Lite Any Medal% by removing File-as-Bus isolates state continuity as the primary driver, and the asymmetric pattern (Valid Submission intact, medal tiers collapse) provides a mechanistic insight rather than just a headline number. - Cost efficiency over IterativeAgent is noteworthy: better performance at 4–5x lower cost demonstrates that structured state management allows agents to reuse prior work rather than rediscovering it, yielding a compounding efficiency advantage. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The ablation conflates removal of File-as-Bus with removal of the workspace structure entirely; it is unclear whether a lighter-weight alternative (e.g., a structured in-context summary updated at each stage) would close most of the gap, leaving open the question of whether the file-system substrate specifically is necessary versus durable structured state in any form. - PaperBench evaluation at $832 per full run materially limits the number of experimental conditions and seeds that can be tested, making it difficult to establish statistical robustness or to ablate individual components (e.g., hierarchical depth, specific specialist roles) independently; the comparison to simpler agent organizations relies on existing baselines rather than matched ablations. - The system is evaluated only on two benchmarks with a single 24-hour, single-GPU budget, and both benchmarks are effectively closed-loop evaluations of existing papers or Kaggle-style tasks; generalization to genuinely open-ended research problems where the correct direction is unknown remains undemonstrated.
Recommended 2604.13018 PDF
#20 — Highly Innovative (78)

AI-Driven Research for Databases

Audrey Cheng, Harald Ng, Aaron Kabcenell, Peter Bailis, Matei Zaharia, Lin Ma, Xiao Shi, Ion Stoica · arxiv.org · Ion Stoica - new articles
Why this rank: This paper scores 78 for introducing the co-evolution paradigm — treating the evaluation pipeline as an evolvable artifact alongside the solution — which is a genuinely novel meta-level insight for applying LLM-driven code generation to complex stateful systems like databases. The white-box, inference-free outputs directly address the central criticism of learned database methods, and the results are validated across three structurally distinct problems with careful train/test methodology. The conceptual contribution is tempered by the significant human engineering embedded in each outer loop instantiation, but the framing opens a productive new research direction.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Database performance optimization is increasingly difficult as modern workloads and hardware diversity outpace human research capacity. Existing ML-for-databases approaches treat systems as black boxes, embedding opaque models at runtime — resulting in inference overhead, unpredictable worst-case behavior, and deployment friction. A new paradigm called AI-Driven Research for Systems (ADRS) uses LLMs to generate optimized source code directly rather than runtime models. However, applying ADRS to databases is bottlenecked not by solution generation but by evaluation: database systems are stateful, tightly coupled, and expensive to benchmark, making the hundreds of evaluation iterations ADRS requires infeasible with conventional approaches. This paper directly targets that evaluation bottleneck. Methodology: The authors propose co-evolution: an outer loop that iteratively refines the evaluation pipeline (simulator, performance model, workload selection, search space) in tandem with an inner loop that generates candidate algorithms. Rather than a monolithic architecture, the approach adapts per-problem to address the dominant evaluation bottleneck along three axes — system, workload, and search space. Three case studies instantiate this: (1) Buffer cache optimization co-evolves a trace-based PostgreSQL cache simulator alongside eviction policies, calibrated against multiple ground-truth baselines (Clock, PBM-PQ, PBM-Sampling) to ensure simulated gains transfer to the real system — the "more is more" insight. (2) Index selection optimization co-evolves the performance model itself, diagnosing that optimizer cost estimates are a poor proxy for actual latency (e.g., DB2Advis achieves higher cost reduction than Extend but 8% worse latency), then building a denoised, isolation-based latency measurement protocol — the "mind the gap" insight. (3) Query rewrite optimization co-evolves both workload selection and search space pruning, generating a ground-truth dataset mapping query structures to performant rule combinations to guide policy synthesis in Apache Calcite — the "go off what you know" insight. All inner loops use OpenEvolve (a MAP-Elites evolutionary framework) with GPT-5 or Claude Sonnet 4.5 as the code generator. Key Results: For buffer cache management (PostgreSQL 14, TPC-H SF=10, EC2 c5.18xlarge), the evolved policy achieves 19.8% higher hit rate and 11.4% lower I/O volume compared to PBM-Sampling, the prior state-of-the-art. For index selection (PostgreSQL 14, TPC-DS/TPC-H, 500MB budget), the evolved policy achieves 6.3% latency reduction on TPC-DS and 5.8% on TPC-H versus Extend, with 2.2× faster selection time (3.4s vs 7.3s) and successful generalization from TPC-DS training to unseen TPC-H queries. For query rewriting (PostgreSQL 17 + Apache Calcite HepPlanner, TPC-H and DSB SF=10), the evolved deterministic policy achieves 5.4× lower overall latency on TPC-H and 6.8× on DSB versus PostgreSQL's native optimizer, and is 9.0× and 33.9× faster respectively than R-Bot (GPT-5) — largely because R-Bot spends over a minute per query on runtime LLM inference while the evolved policy incurs sub-millisecond rule selection overhead. Individual query speedups reach up to 10.8× for complex join-heavy queries. Strengths: - The co-evolution framing is a genuine conceptual contribution: treating the evaluator as an evolvable artifact alongside the solution cleanly addresses the core scalability bottleneck of ADRS for complex systems. - The discovered algorithms are white-box, deployable Java/C code with no runtime inference dependency — directly addressing the primary criticism of learned database methods and producing results competitive with or exceeding hand-designed state-of-the-art. - Strong empirical validation across three structurally distinct database problems, with careful experimental design (train/test split on query variants, multiple independent runs, generalization to unseen benchmarks). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation is limited to OLAP benchmarks (TPC-H, TPC-DS, DSB) on a single hardware configuration; whether improvements transfer to OLTP workloads, heterogeneous hardware, or other database engines is unexplored. - The co-evolution approach is problem-specific in a way that is not fully automated: substantial human insight is embedded in the outer loop design (e.g., choosing to expose scan metadata, selecting which proxy metrics to investigate), raising questions about how much engineering effort is truly eliminated versus shifted. - Computational cost and wall-clock time of the full outer+inner loop are not reported, making it difficult to assess the practical overhead of this methodology relative to traditional research.
Recommended 2604.06566 PDF
#21 — Highly Innovative (78)

Uncertainty-quantified Pulse Signal Recovery from Facial Video using Regularized Stochastic Interpolants

Vineet R. Shenoy, Cheng Peng, Rama Chellappa, Yu Sun · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: This paper scores 78 for being the first iPPG method to enable test-time posterior sampling via stochastic interpolants, directly addressing the critical clinical trust gap of uncertainty quantification in non-contact vital sign monitoring. The Residual Correlation Loss is a physiologically motivated and architecturally non-invasive regularization that is cleanly validated through ablation. The comprehensive UQ evaluation framework (NLL, CRPS, calibration curves, Gauge R&R on demographic subgroups) establishes meaningful baselines that did not previously exist. The work reframes BVP recovery as a posterior sampling inverse problem — a genuine conceptual departure from the point-estimation paradigm that dominates iPPG.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Imaging Photoplethysmography (iPPG) recovers blood volume pulse (BVP) signals from facial video captured by standard RGB cameras, enabling non-contact vital sign monitoring. While deep learning methods have reached strong performance on benchmark datasets, none perform test-time sampling of the solution space — meaning they produce only point estimates with no principled uncertainty quantification. This is a critical gap for clinical adoption: clinicians need to trust not just average-case accuracy, but per-sample reliability, particularly for protected subgroups (skin tone, gender) where data imbalance can cause silently degraded performance. Methodology: The paper proposes RIS-iPPG (Regularized Interpolants with Stochasticity for iPPG), which frames BVP recovery as a posterior sampling inverse problem using flow-based diffusion models. Rather than approximating an explicit forward model A(·) mapping pulse to camera pixels — which prior approaches using A≈F⁻¹ (inverse Fourier transform) found inadequate due to stochastic, one-to-many, motion-dependent mappings — RIS-iPPG instead learns an implicit stochastic transport between the two distributions. Building on the stochastic interpolants framework (Albergo et al., 2023), a linear interpolant xₜ = (1−t)x₀ + tx₁ + √(2t(1−t))z bridges camera pixel signals (p₁) and ground-truth BVP (p₀). Two U-Net networks learn the instantaneous flow velocity vθ and the denoiser nθ, whose combination yields the drift coefficient of a reverse-time SDE. At test time, the camera signal is used as the initial condition and the SDE is solved N times in parallel to produce N samples of the BVP posterior. The key technical novelty is the Residual Correlation Loss (RCL): exploiting the slowly-varying nature of physiological signals, RCL maximizes the Pearson correlation between prediction residuals (ground-truth flow minus predicted flow) from two adjacent, overlapping time windows. This acts as a temporal smoothness regularizer without modifying model architecture, simply added to the combined flow+score MSE objective. Face preprocessing uses OpenFace and LDEQ landmark detection to extract per-channel averages from five facial regions (forehead left/right, cheeks left/right, chin), taking R/G channel ratios as the signal estimate. Key Results: RIS-iPPG is evaluated on three datasets — MMSE-HR (40-fold cross-validation, 102 videos), PURE, and UBFC-rPPG. On PURE, it achieves state-of-the-art MAE of 0.38 bpm and RMSE of 1.28 bpm, outperforming ContrastPhys-100 (supervised, PAMI'24) at 0.48/0.98. On UBFC-rPPG, it achieves MAE=0.47 and RMSE=0.80, competitive with the best supervised methods. On MMSE-HR, it reaches MAE=1.97 and RMSE=3.73, comparable to ContrastPhys-100 (1.11/3.83). The RCL loss consistently improves spectrum estimation PCC (e.g., 0.651→0.795 on UBFC-rPPG) and reduces RMSE. Uncertainty calibration errors are ≤0.06 on PURE and UBFC-rPPG. For protected attributes on MMSE-HR, HR MAE is 2.17 bpm for light skin vs. 4.79 bpm for dark skin, with calibration errors ≤0.10 for all subgroups, establishing the first UQ baselines for iPPG on demographic subgroups. Ablations confirm that RCL on error residuals — not on predicted flows directly — is essential; applying it to flows causes oscillating validation loss and non-convergence. Cross-dataset generalization is inconsistent: MMSE-HR→UBFC-rPPG achieves 1.73 bpm MAE, but PURE→MMSE-HR fails at 7.93 bpm, attributed to distributional shift in lighting and motion conditions. Strengths: - First iPPG method to enable test-time posterior sampling, directly addressing the clinical trust gap by providing per-sample uncertainty estimates rather than point predictions. - The RCL regularization is physiologically motivated (slowly-varying BVP), architecturally non-invasive (added at loss level only), and empirically validated via ablation over a 10×10 grid of hyperparameters. - Competitive or state-of-the-art accuracy on three datasets while simultaneously providing calibrated uncertainty — unlike all prior work which trades off uncertainty for accuracy. - Rigorous uncertainty evaluation framework including NLL, CRPS, pinball loss, interval score, calibration curves, and Gauge R&R analysis on demographic subgroups — establishing meaningful baselines. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Cross-dataset generalization is severely limited: the implicit learned transport is conditioned on a specific (source, target) distribution pair, so domain shift (different lighting, motion) causes dramatic performance degradation (e.g., PURE→UBFC-rPPG: 21.59 bpm MAE), which the authors acknowledge but leave unresolved. - Inference speed (~851ms per sample) is on par with conditional VAEs and significantly slower than BNNs (~474ms); for real-time or on-device clinical deployment this is a meaningful barrier, especially if many SDE steps or large N samples are required. - Performance on darker skin tones lags substantially (HR MAE 4.79 vs. 2.17 bpm for light skin), reflecting dataset representation imbalance rather than an algorithmic fix — the method reveals but does not solve the equity gap. - The method is supervised, requiring paired facial video and contact PPG during training, which limits deployment in settings where ground-truth PPG collection is burdensome; no extension to self-supervised learning is explored.
Search Results 2604.10777 PDF
#22 — Highly Innovative (76)

A Mechanistic Analysis of Looped Reasoning Language Models

Hugh Blayney, Álvaro Arroyo, Johan Obando-Ceron, Pablo Samuel Castro, Aaron Courville, Michael M. Bronstein, Xiaowen Dong · arxiv.org · Michael Bronstein - new articles
Why this rank: Scores 76 for providing a clean theoretical framework (cyclic fixed-point behavior) that directly explains previously mysterious empirical observations about looped transformer stability. The connection between architectural choices (norm structure, input injection) and stable stages of inference is actionable for practitioners designing test-time compute scaling systems. The breadth of architectures analyzed — trained from scratch, retrofitted, different norm structures — strengthens the generalizability, and the mathematical grounding (Propositions 4.1 and 4.2) elevates this beyond purely empirical observation. Limited by restriction to cyclic recurrence and small-scale training experiments.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Looped language models — architectures that reuse a shared Transformer block across multiple recurrent iterations at inference time — have demonstrated strong reasoning performance, but their internal dynamics remain poorly understood. Specifically, it is unclear how the "stages of inference" phenomenon documented in feedforward models (distinct computational phases characterized by shifts in attention mixing behavior across depth) translates to the recurrent setting, where layers are weight-tied and applied cyclically. Understanding this has direct implications for principled architectural design of test-time compute scaling systems. Methodology: The paper combines theoretical analysis with empirical study across multiple pretrained looped LLMs: Ouro 1.4B/2.6B (Zhu et al.), Huginn-0125 (Geiping et al.), and retrofitted recurrent Llama/OLMo/TinyLlama (McLeish et al.). The central concept introduced is cyclic fixed-point behavior: if the entire recurrent block S_k converges to a fixed point X' = S_k(X'), then by induction each cyclic permutation of the constituent layers also reaches its own distinct fixed point, causing the recurrent block to trace a consistent cyclic trajectory in latent space. Proposition 4.2 formalizes that convergence of the residual stream implies convergence of attention patterns (via a Lipschitz bound on softmax with constant L_sm = 1/2). Empirically, the paper measures cyclic fixed-point convergence via residual stream difference norms and cosine similarities over up to 128 recurrences, and characterizes stages of inference using the ColSum Concentration metric (normalized entropy of per-column attention mass), alongside sink rates, mixing scores, and residual entropy. To isolate whether stages of inference are an emergent property of training versus an artifact of training procedure, the authors pretrain small-scale looped models from scratch with constant recurrence schedules of 4 and simplified cross-entropy loss. Key Results: (1) Most studied looped models exhibit cyclic fixed-point behavior: residual streams after each layer converge to layer-specific fixed points, with attention patterns stabilizing after very few recurrences (often just 1-2 for Ouro, immediately after the prelude for Huginn-0125 and retrofitted Llama). (2) Retrofitted Llama and Huginn-0125 reach strict fixed points while Ouro does not — consistent with prior findings that input injection facilitates fixed-point convergence; pre-norm without input injection also converges but to a degenerate single fixed point shared across all layers. (3) Within each recurrent block, the same sequence of mixing stages seen in feedforward Transformers is reproduced — Ouro's recurrent blocks mirror Llama's feedforward ColSum concentration profile despite being trained from scratch with recurrence. Retrofitted models reproduce the stages of their respective base models, with prelude/coda handling the initial/final stages. (4) Huginn-0125 does not exhibit clear stages of inference due to its sandwich norm structure normalizing the residual stream after each block, preventing the accumulation of massive activations required for sink formation. (5) Models that reach a strict fixed point maintain stable stages of inference for arbitrary test-time recurrences (up to 128, far beyond training recurrences of 32), while Ouro — lacking fixed-point convergence — exhibits continuously drifting stages. This mechanistically explains previously observed empirical results: Huginn-0125 performance is stable when extrapolating beyond training recurrences, while Ouro degrades. (6) Small-scale training experiments (3.7B tokens, dimension 512) provide evidence that stages of inference self-organize even when training biases toward feedforward computation are removed, suggesting these stages emerge from the optimization landscape itself. Strengths: - The theoretical framework (Propositions 4.1 and 4.2) is clean and directly motivates the empirical measurements, providing a mathematically grounded account of why cyclic fixed-point behavior implies attention pattern stabilization. - The non-fixed-point limiting behavior analysis is thorough: the paper quantifies that orbits/sliders affect only ~0.02% of tokens under normal prompting, bolstering the practical relevance of the fixed-point theory without dismissing these phenomena. - The study covers diverse architectures (trained from scratch, retrofitted, different norm structures, with/without input injection) and two datasets (GSM8k and HellaSwag), strengthening generalizability of the conclusions. - The connection between architectural choices (norm structure, input injection) and the emergence of stable stages of inference provides actionable design guidance. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The small-scale training experiments (Section 5.1) are explicitly acknowledged as needing cautious interpretation — the models are very small and trained on relatively few tokens; it remains unclear whether the self-organization findings scale to production-scale looped models. - The analysis is restricted to cyclic recurrence (a single looped block) and does not cover sequential multi-block recurrence architectures, limiting applicability to a subclass of recurrent Transformer designs. - The paper does not establish analytically why specific architectural choices (e.g., pre-norm with input injection) lead to stable fixed-point convergence, nor does it address whether fixed-point stability is always desirable for reasoning — it may restrict the representational diversity available across recurrences.
Recommended 2604.11791 PDF
#23 — Highly Innovative (76)

SNEAK: Evaluating Strategic Communication and Information Leakage in Large Language Models

Adar Avsian, Larry Heck · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 76 for defining a genuinely new evaluation axis — strategic information control under asymmetric knowledge — that is well-grounded in signaling game theory and has no prior LLM benchmark analog. The dual-metric utility-leakage framework with SoftScore/BinaryScore is principled and produces objective, reproducible measurements without subjective LLM-as-judge ratings. The 4x human advantage over frontier LLMs is a striking concrete finding that clearly delineates a capability gap. The Recursive Message Refinement method, while modest in absolute improvement, demonstrates that inference-time scaling can shift the utility-leakage frontier in the right direction. The single-turn limitation and moderate evaluator-human correlation on leakage (r=0.39) temper the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing LLM benchmarks evaluate reasoning, factual knowledge, and instruction following, but none directly measure whether models can control how much information they reveal when communicating under asymmetric information states. In multi-agent settings — tutoring, consulting, investor communication, collaborative AI — an agent must signal knowledge to an informed collaborator while remaining ambiguous to an uninformed adversary. This utility–leakage trade-off is well-studied in economics (Crawford & Sobel signaling games) and pragmatics (Gricean maxims, Rational Speech Acts) but has no dedicated LLM benchmark. SNEAK fills this gap. Methodology: SNEAK is inspired by the party game The Chameleon. Each benchmark instance consists of a semantic category, a candidate word set (12 prototypical members drawn from human category production norms across 117 categories), and a secret word. The message-generating model observes all three and must produce a ≤5-word natural language message. The message is then evaluated by two simulated agents: an ally (knows the secret, must identify which of 6 messages — 1 generated + 5 WordNet-derived decoys — corresponds to the secret) and a chameleon (does not know the secret, must infer it from the message and the candidate set). Both agents are implemented via constrained decoding over answer-label logits, averaged across three independent evaluator models (Mixtral-8x7B, Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct, Qwen-2.5-72B-Instruct) to reduce dependence on any single judge. Metrics are Utility and Leakage (each normalized relative to a random-guess baseline and clipped to [0,1]), a composite SoftScore = Utility × (1 − Leakage), and a stricter BinaryScore requiring the ally to rank the generated message first and the chameleon to not rank the secret first. The benchmark contains 1,394 instances. Three reference baselines (random word, category synonym, secret synonym) span the utility–leakage space. Three inference-time scaling methods are also tested: Chain-of-Thought (CoT), SETS (self-verification + self-correction), and a novel Recursive Message Refinement (RMR) that generates a message from the secret then generates a second message conditioned on the first to encourage indirection. Key Results: Modern LLMs achieve very high utility (58–99) but at the cost of high leakage (50–75), yielding SoftScores of only 18–35. Humans, by contrast, achieve utility 91.3, leakage 33.8, and SoftScore 59.2 — roughly 2–4× higher SoftScore than the best LLMs. Gemma-3-27B achieves the best SoftScore among LLMs (34.9), while DeepSeek-V3.2 achieves near-ceiling utility (98.5) but correspondingly high leakage (75.4, SoftScore 23.8). Inference-time scaling shows mixed results: CoT and SETS generally increase both utility and leakage together, leaving SoftScore largely unchanged. RMR is the most promising: for Gemma-3-27B, RMR reduces utility from 92.2 to 63.3 but also cuts leakage from 59.2 to 26.4, raising SoftScore from 34.9 to 40.8 and BinaryScore from 33.8 to 40.9 — the best single result in the benchmark. Abstract semantic categories are easier than concrete ones (SoftScore 28.4 vs. 25.2). Higher within-category semantic similarity strongly predicts lower utility (r = −0.43). Semantic analysis shows entity-based (intrinsic property) messages outperform taxonomic messages; temporal and action-based associations achieve strong performance with relatively low leakage. LLM evaluators agree with human plurality judgments on 88.9% of ally items and 66.7% of chameleon items, with evaluator-to-human probability correlations of r = 0.61 (ally) and r = 0.39 (chameleon). Evaluator Spearman rank correlations across the three judges range from 0.90 to 0.99, confirming robustness. Strengths: - The dual-metric framework (utility + leakage → SoftScore/BinaryScore) is principled, directly grounded in signaling game theory, and produces objective, reproducible scores without subjective LLM-as-judge ratings. - Thorough robustness analysis: three independent evaluators, same-model bias checks, decoy ablation (scores change <6 utility, <1 leakage under LLM-generated decoys), and sensitivity sweeps over candidate/decoy set sizes all confirm stability. - The human study (AMT, 117 instances, 5 annotators per item) provides a credible upper-bound reference and validates the LLM evaluator pipeline; the 4× human advantage over several LLMs is a striking, concrete finding. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The task is single-turn and one-shot: it does not capture multi-turn negotiation, deception, or refinement dynamics present in real asymmetric communication, limiting ecological validity. - Evaluation relies on LLM-based ally and chameleon agents whose correlation with human leakage judgments is only moderate (r = 0.39), meaning leakage scores may be noisy proxies for what a real adversary would infer. - The dataset is drawn entirely from structured semantic category norms, which favors short, concrete associative messages; the benchmark may not generalize to richer communicative contexts involving background knowledge, pragmatic inference over discourse, or domain-specific jargon.
Recommended 2603.29846 PDF
#24 — Highly Innovative (76)

KumoRFM-2: Scaling Foundation Models for Relational Learning

Valter Hudovernik, Federico López, Vid Kocijan, Akihiro Nitta, Jan Eric Lenssen, Jure Leskovec, Matthias Fey · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: KumoRFM-2 is the first few-shot foundation model to demonstrably surpass task-specific supervised baselines on standard relational benchmarks, which is a meaningful milestone. The early task-conditioning architecture is a principled advance with a clear expressivity argument — provably resolving limitations of fixed-function column-wise encoders. The hierarchical three-scale attention design (intra-table, cross-table graph, cross-sample) is an elegant formulation for relational in-context learning. However, the system is a closed commercial product with undisclosed model sizes, training data, and key hyperparameters, significantly limiting reproducibility and scientific scrutiny, which tempers the assessed impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Predictive modeling on relational databases requires extracting signals from multiple interconnected tables while respecting temporal ordering — a setting where standard tabular foundation models fall short. Prior work either requires expensive manual feature engineering (data scientist pipelines), task-specific supervised training (graph neural networks), or naively flattens multi-table data via fixed-function column-wise encoders (deep feature synthesis), which provably cannot capture row-level co-occurrence patterns. The gap: no prior few-shot foundation model had surpassed supervised task-specific baselines on relational benchmarks. KumoRFM-2 targets this gap by performing native in-context learning directly over relational subgraphs, eliminating both feature engineering and task-specific training. Methodology: KumoRFM-2 models a relational database as a temporal heterogeneous graph where records are nodes and primary/foreign-key links are edges. Given a predictive query (expressed in a declarative Predictive Query Language), the system automatically constructs context examples — entity-centric time-consistent subgraphs with ground-truth labels — and performs in-context learning in a single forward pass without any gradient updates. The architecture operates at three scales via a hierarchical attention scheme: (1) alternating column- and row-attention within individual tables, (2) graph attention across primary/foreign-key relationships, and (3) cross-sample attention across context examples. Critically, context targets are injected early, enabling task-conditioned feature selection throughout — this is the main architectural advance over KumoRFM-1, which applied context targets only at the cross-sample stage. A structured context selection strategy combines local (entity-specific lagged targets) with global (recent database snapshots) context. Pre-training uses a curriculum progressing from synthetic single tables (generated via structural causal models) to complex multi-table relational databases. Ensembling via randomized column/class order shuffles and variable subgraph depth/breadth further stabilizes predictions. For scale, the system supports SQL push-down and an SSD-based memory-mapped graph engine enabling 500B+ row databases at 5 GB/s bandwidth. Key Results: Evaluated across 41 tasks on 15 databases (RelBenchV1, RelBenchV2, SALT, 4DBInfer) spanning e-commerce, medical, academic, social, and ERP domains, totaling 2.3 billion rows. On RelBenchV1 binary classification (12 tasks), KumoRFM-2 achieves average AUROC of 79.60 (rank 3.08), outperforming the strongest supervised relational model RelGNN by 1.54 points and KumoRFM-1 by 2.89 points — claimed as the first demonstration of a few-shot foundation model surpassing supervised baselines on relational benchmarks. On RelBenchV1 regression (9 tasks), KumoRFM-2 achieves normalized MAE of 0.822, improving over KumoRFM-1 by 10.5% and ranking first overall. On the enterprise SALT benchmark (8 multi-class tasks, up to 589 classes), in-context KumoRFM-2 achieves average MRR of 0.83, beating AutoGluon ensembles by ~8% and TabICLv2 by ~25%; fine-tuning pushes this to 0.89 MRR (+12.7% over best prior model), completing in under two minutes per task. Robustness analysis shows performance is maintained with up to 75% of foreign-key edges removed (cold-start), with up to 75% missing features, and with up to 160 injected noisy columns — where KumoRFM-1 degrades significantly but KumoRFM-2 remains nearly flat, attributed to early task-conditioned noise filtering. Strengths: - First few-shot foundation model to demonstrably surpass task-specific supervised relational models on standard benchmarks, achieving this with as little as 0.2% of available training data as context. - Early task conditioning is a principled architectural improvement with a clear expressivity argument: fixed-function column-wise encoders are provably unable to capture row-level co-occurrence (AUROC 0.5 vs. 1.0 on the constructed adversarial example), while the hierarchical task-conditioned design resolves this. - Exceptional practical scalability: native SQL push-down and a memory-mapped graph engine handle 500B+ row databases, bridging the gap between academic benchmarks and production deployment. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The system is a commercial product from Kumo.ai, and key architectural and training details (model size, pre-training data volume, exact synthetic data generation procedures, fine-tuning hyperparameters) are not disclosed, limiting reproducibility and scientific scrutiny. - In-context learning is capped at ~10k context examples, which leaves performance on the table for large training regimes (amazon, stack, hm) where supervised models still hold an advantage — the fine-tuning path addresses this but adds training cost and is only available via enterprise deployment. - Optimal subgraph hyperparameters (depth, breadth, neighborhood size) are task-dependent and require tuning; the paper demonstrates this variability but does not provide an automated selection strategy, shifting burden back to practitioners.
Search Results 2604.12596 PDF
#25 — Highly Innovative (76)

Pseudo-Unification: Entropy Probing Reveals Divergent Information Patterns in Unified Multimodal Models (36 ▲ )

Songlin Yang, Xianghao Kong, Anyi Rao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 14 Apr 2026
Why this rank: This paper scores 76 for defining and mechanistically diagnosing the novel problem of 'pseudo-unification' in multimodal models — the finding that architectural unification does not yield functional unification is an important conceptual contribution. The RKHS-based entropy probing framework is technically creative, enabling density-free analysis of Transformer internals where classical methods fail. The Harmon case study elevates the work beyond a negative result by identifying that masked autoencoder objectives produce genuinely converged modality representations, offering a concrete architectural prescription. The two-level diagnostic (encoding + conditional response entropy) is more complete than prior probing approaches and reveals structure-agnostic encoding as a systemic property across ten models.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Unified multimodal models (UMMs) were designed to synergize LLM-style reasoning with vision generation, enabling cross-modal transfer (e.g., reasoning about a prompt and then generating a corresponding image). In practice, this synergy fails: UMMs cannot transfer LLM-like reasoning to image synthesis, producing text responses that correctly interpret a prompt while image responses ignore the same reasoning. The authors term this "pseudo-unification." Existing probing methods either rely on behavioral benchmarks that cannot reveal why models fail internally, or analyze only prompt-side representations without capturing prompt–response dependencies. This paper addresses the gap by providing the first model-internal mechanistic diagnosis of pseudo-unification. Methodology: The authors propose a two-level information-theoretic probing framework. At the prompt level, they compute embedding entropy and layer-wise entropy trajectories for text and image inputs separately, revealing how each modality is encoded. At the response level, they estimate a conditional entropy proxy H(Z_r | Z_p) to measure output uncertainty given the input — low values indicate fidelity-driven generation, high values indicate creative/diverse generation. Because Transformer-based UMMs lack explicit probability densities and operate on variable-length embeddings, classical entropy estimation is infeasible. The authors reformulate entropy in reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces (RKHS) using matrix-based Rényi entropy (α=1.01 to approximate Shannon entropy): a Gram matrix is constructed from embeddings via a Gaussian kernel, normalized by its trace, and entropy is computed from eigenspectrum concentration. Conditional entropy is estimated as H(Z_p, Z_r) − H(Z_p) using a block joint kernel matrix. The framework is applied to ten UMMs spanning architectures (all-in-one Transformer, Mixture-of-Transformers, MLLM+Diffusion), image generation paradigms (Flow Matching, VQ-VAE, MAE, Diffusion Loss), and parameter scales (1B–14B). Probing data is drawn from T2I-CoReBench (1,080 text prompts across reasoning types) and MMBench (3,217 images across perception/reasoning categories). Key Results: Two structural findings emerge. (1) Modality-Asymmetric Encoding: Despite architectural unification, all UMMs encode text and image prompts along divergent entropy trajectories determined by architecture and scale rather than semantic content. For example, BAGEL shows early entropy collapse for text (rebounding to ~5–6) while image prompts begin at high entropy (~9) and stay stable; Show-o and Janus exhibit rapid saturation for both modalities but with persistent scale offsets (text~8, image~9). Encoding is structure-agnostic: layer-wise entropy trajectories are nearly identical across different reasoning prompt types or image semantic categories within the same modality, meaning the model does not differentially process deductive vs. inductive reasoning prompts. (2) Pattern-Split Response: Across nearly all UMMs, text generation exhibits systematically higher conditional entropy than image generation at every layer — i.e., text follows a high-entropy "creative" pattern while image synthesis adheres to a low-entropy "fidelity" pattern. This confirms that UMMs retain modality-specific optimization objectives rather than a shared generative logic. The single exception is Harmon (1.5B), which uses a masked autoencoder for images that parallels next-token prediction, giving both modalities a shared contextual-prediction inductive bias. Harmon's conditional entropy trajectories converge across modalities by the final layers, and qualitatively it correctly generates reasoning-consistent images even at 1.5B parameters where BAGEL (14B) fails. Strengths: - The RKHS reformulation of entropy is technically sound and fills a genuine gap: it enables non-parametric, density-free entropy estimation for variable-length, high-dimensional Transformer representations, validated with controlled synthetic experiments. - The two-level diagnostic (prompt encoding + prompt–response conditional entropy) is more complete than prior probing work, which studied only prompt-side representations and ignored generative behavior. - The Harmon case study is a compelling and specific positive result: it identifies the architectural property (shared contextual-prediction objective via MAE) that enables genuine unification, moving beyond purely negative findings. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The conditional entropy proxy is not formal Shannon conditional entropy; the block joint kernel matrix conflates cross-modal geometric structure with dependence, and the paper does not rigorously bound the gap between the proxy and true conditional entropy or characterize conditions under which the proxy could be misleading. - The probing is purely observational and correlational: the framework diagnoses that entropy divergence co-occurs with pseudo-unification but does not establish causation or provide actionable training-time interventions. The "future directions" remain vague ("symmetric prediction tasks"). - The study covers only 10 models and does not ablate the sensitivity of conclusions to the Gaussian kernel bandwidth σ, the Rényi order α, or sample size — important for establishing robustness of the entropy estimates.
Recommended 2604.10949 PDF
#26 — Highly Innovative (76)

RAD-2: Scaling Reinforcement Learning in a Generator-Discriminator Framework (21 ▲ )
