Hao Gao, Shaoyu Chen, Yifan Zhu, Yuehao Song, Wenyu Liu, Qian Zhang, Xinggang Wang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: RAD-2 scores 76 for its principled decoupling of generation and evaluation in autonomous driving RL, which elegantly sidesteps the credit assignment problem that has plagued direct RL on high-dimensional trajectory spaces. The BEV-Warp simulation environment is a genuinely novel contribution — exploiting spatial equivariance of BEV features to enable feature-level closed-loop training without per-scene reconstruction is creative and practically valuable. TC-GRPO's temporally consistent latching and the OGO mechanism for stable generator feedback each address real failure modes with well-motivated solutions. The system integrates multiple novel components into a coherent framework with strong empirical results, though reliance on proprietary data and BEV-specific assumptions limits broader impact assessment.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Diffusion-based imitation learning planners for autonomous driving can model multimodal trajectory distributions but suffer from two fundamental problems: (1) stochastic instabilities from noisy, unevenly distributed real-world data that cause occasional unsafe trajectories, and (2) lack of corrective feedback since open-loop IL training provides no negative signal and introduces causal confusion. Directly applying RL to fix these issues is also problematic — the reward signal is a low-dimensional scalar while the action space is a high-dimensional, temporally-structured trajectory, creating severe credit assignment difficulties and unstable optimization. Existing closed-loop simulation environments also impose practical barriers: game engines have sim-to-real gaps, reconstruction-based simulators (3DGS) are expensive and per-scene, and generative world models suffer from long-horizon temporal drift. Methodology: RAD-2 decouples generation and evaluation into a unified generator-discriminator framework. A DiT-based diffusion generator (pretrained via IL on ~50,000 hours of real-world driving data) produces M diverse candidate trajectories conditioned on BEV scene encodings of map elements, dynamic agents, and navigation inputs. A Transformer-based discriminator, trained via RL rather than IL, scores each candidate using cross-attention between trajectory embeddings and multi-source scene context, outputting a scalar confidence in [0,1] via sigmoid. This decoupling avoids applying sparse scalar rewards to the high-dimensional trajectory space — RL only optimizes the low-dimensional discriminator output. Three novel training components address credit assignment and generator stagnation. (1) Temporally Consistent Group Relative Policy Optimization (TC-GRPO) implements a latched execution strategy where a selected trajectory is reused over a fixed horizon H_reuse before replanning, ensuring that cumulative rewards reflect coherent trajectory decisions. Advantages are computed group-relative across rollouts from the same initial state, with adaptive entropy regularization to prevent score collapse. (2) On-policy Generator Optimization (OGO) converts closed-loop feedback into structured longitudinal signals: when TTC is low, the generator receives safety-driven deceleration targets; when ego lags the reference and TTC is safe, it receives efficiency-driven acceleration targets. These reward-shaped trajectories fine-tune the generator via MSE loss, gradually shifting its distribution toward high-reward manifolds without RL instability. (3) BEV-Warp is a high-throughput simulation environment that bypasses image rendering by spatially warping BEV feature maps using a relative pose transformation matrix, exploiting the spatial equivariance of BEV features. This enables closed-loop interaction at feature level, orders of magnitude cheaper than reconstruction-based or generative simulators. An iLQR controller tracks planned trajectories in both BEV-Warp and 3DGS environments. Training alternates discriminator optimization (per batch) and generator optimization (every 8 batches) on shared rollout data, with reward-variance-based clip filtering to discard uninformative scenarios. Key Results: In BEV-Warp closed-loop evaluation across 512 safety-oriented and 512 efficiency-oriented scenarios, RAD-2 reduces collision rate from 0.533 (best baseline ResAD) to 0.234 (56% reduction) and at-fault collision rate from 0.264 to 0.092, while raising Safety@1s/Safety@2s from 0.418/0.281 to 0.730/0.596. Efficiency metrics improve from EP@1.0 = 0.516 to 0.736 and EP@0.9 from 0.894 to 0.984. On the photorealistic 3DGS benchmark (Senna-2 evaluation set), RAD-2 achieves CR = 0.250, AF-CR = 0.078, Safety@1 = 0.723, Safety@2 = 0.644, outperforming RAD (0.281), Senna-2 (0.269), and Senna (0.310) on CR. On open-loop Senna-2 benchmarks, RAD-2 achieves ADE = 0.208 m and FDE = 0.553 m (vs. Senna-2's 0.225/0.597), with CR reduced to 0.142% vs. 0.288%. Inference-time scaling is demonstrated: increasing candidate count M from 8 to 128 at test time improves EP@1.0 from 0.667 to 0.814 without retraining. Ablations confirm that joint generator + discriminator optimization significantly outperforms discriminator-only or sequential two-stage training in both final performance and sample efficiency. Strengths: - The decoupled generator-discriminator design is principled: restricting RL to the low-dimensional discriminator output space elegantly sidesteps the credit assignment problem that plagues direct RL on continuous trajectory spaces, while OGO provides a stable mechanism to propagate closed-loop feedback back to the generator. - BEV-Warp is a practically valuable contribution — feature-level warping with spatial equivariance enables high-throughput closed-loop training on recorded logs without per-scene reconstruction or generative rendering, and the spatial fidelity is empirically validated via frozen perception head consistency. - TC-GRPO's temporally consistent latching is well-motivated and ablated: it directly addresses the reward dilution problem from multimodal mode-switching, and the execution horizon study (H_reuse = 8 optimal) provides actionable guidance. Weaknesses / Limitations: - BEV-Warp assumes spatial equivariance and is fundamentally tied to BEV-centric perception architectures; it cannot be straightforwardly applied to raw-pixel or unified-latent architectures, limiting generalizability to the broader community of end-to-end driving systems. - The evaluation is conducted entirely on proprietary datasets (50k internal clips, internal open-loop benchmarks adapted from Senna-2), with no results on public standardized benchmarks such as nuPlan or NAVSIM, making it difficult to compare against the broader literature and assess whether gains hold across different data distributions. - The OGO longitudinal optimization (adjusting speed profiles by fixed ratios) is a heuristic approximation that preserves the spatial path while modifying only temporal progression, which may not generate sufficiently diverse corrective signals for complex lateral failure modes such as unsafe lane changes or intersection navigation.
Recommended 2604.15308 PDF
#27 — Innovative (74)

Robust Safety Monitoring of Language Models via Activation Watermarking

Toluwani Aremu, Daniil Ognev, Samuele Poppi, Nils Lukas · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 74 for the genuinely creative transfer of cryptographic keying concepts into activation-space safety monitoring, producing a mechanism where the attacker faces unresolvable uncertainty even with full algorithm knowledge. The empirical evaluation across four jailbreak families and cross-model transfer is thorough, and the negligible inference overhead is a practical advantage. However, the absence of any provable security guarantees and the non-trivial math-reasoning degradation (−7.1 pp GSM8K) temper the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM providers deploy safety monitors (guard models, classifiers) to detect policy-violating outputs, but these monitors are vulnerable to adaptive attackers who know the monitoring algorithm and iteratively craft jailbreak prompts to simultaneously elicit harmful outputs and evade detection. Since guard models are typically static and open-source variants are publicly accessible, an attacker can surrogate-optimize attacks that transfer to production deployments. Existing activation-based probing approaches use publicly discoverable directions that adaptive attackers can also bypass. The gap is a monitoring mechanism that is robust against adversaries who fully know the detection algorithm but not a secret key. Methodology: The paper proposes activation watermarking, which fine-tunes the base LLM to embed a secret, keyed direction into its hidden-state activations whenever it produces policy-violating outputs, while keeping benign activations orthogonal to this direction. Key generation samples a Gaussian vector per target layer from a seeded PRNG, defining the secret key. Training adds a loss term to a standard KL-regularized fine-tuning objective: for harmful examples, cosine similarity between activations and the watermark direction is maximized; for benign examples, it is minimized. A linear token-weighting ramp concentrates the signal on later, more explicitly harmful tokens (post-onset), reducing false positives from ambiguous preamble tokens. Detection at inference is a single cosine-similarity computation averaged over assistant tokens, with a threshold calibrated on held-out benign data at a target FPR. Adaptive attackers are modeled as having full algorithm knowledge but no access to the secret key, forcing them to optimize on a surrogate with a mismatched key; the key mismatch prevents clean transfer. Experiments use Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and 14B as base models, BeaverTails for training/evaluation, XSTest seeds with EasyJailbreak for jailbreak suites (Jailbroken, DeepInception, Multilingual, AutoDAN), and compare against LlamaGuard-3-8B and Qwen3Guard-Gen-8B. Key Results: On standard BeaverTails, activation watermarking (ActWM) achieves AUROC 0.878, competitive with QwenGuard (0.849) and above LlamaGuard (0.745). The advantage widens substantially under adaptive jailbreaks: ActWM reaches AUROC 0.929 (Jailbroken), 0.923 (DeepInception), 0.962 (Multilingual), and 0.905 (AutoDAN), outperforming the best guard baseline by up to 52 percentage points on AUROC and consistently achieving lower attack success rates (ASR) at 1% FPR. Cross-model transfer attacks (developed on Mistral-7B-Instruct, evaluated on watermarked Qwen2.5-7B) still yield high AUROC (0.90–0.95), confirming cross-architecture robustness. In a 20-entity secret extraction game, attribution accuracy is ~80% versus 5% random chance, with TPR ~80% at FPR = 10^-4. Utility impact is modest on most benchmarks (<2 pp on BBH, IFEval, MMLU-pro, TruthfulQA) but reaches −7.1 pp on GSM8K and −2.6 pp on MATH-Hard. Training requires ~4 GPU hours on a single RTX A6000 with negligible inference overhead (O(Kd) additional cost vs. a full guard forward pass). Strengths: - The core insight of using a secret-keyed direction to introduce unresolvable uncertainty for the attacker is well-motivated and clearly distinguishes the method from prior activation-probe work, which relies on discoverable public directions. - The empirical evaluation is thorough: four jailbreak families (two template-based, one optimization-based, one multilingual), cross-key and cross-model transfer experiments, multi-entity attribution, and 72-configuration ablations covering layer depth, learning rate, watermark strength, and token weighting. - Inference overhead is negligible compared to guard-model pipelines, making the approach practical for high-throughput deployment; the single-pass architecture and streaming compatibility are genuine engineering advantages. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The threat model excludes white-box adversaries with gradient access to the model's activations; an attacker who can query hidden states directly could potentially identify and suppress the watermark direction, yet this scenario is not evaluated or bounded theoretically. - Security guarantees are entirely empirical—there are no provable lower bounds on the attacker's difficulty, so a stronger adaptive strategy (e.g., directly optimizing against the cosine statistic using activation access or model distillation) could reduce detection rates in ways not captured by the current attack suite. - The math-reasoning utility degradation (−7.1 pp on GSM8K) is non-trivial, and the paper does not investigate whether this is fundamental to the activation-regularization objective or addressable with better training recipes; at deployment scale this could be a significant quality regression.
Recommended 2603.23171 PDF
#28 — Innovative (74)

Hidden in Plain Sight: Visual-to-Symbolic Analytical Solution Inference from Field Visualizations

Pengze Li, Jiaquan Zhang, Yunbo Long, Xinping Liu, Zhou wenjie, Encheng Su, Zihang Zeng, Jiaqi Liu, Jiyao Liu, Junchi Yu, Lihao Liu, Philip Torr, Shixiang Tang, Aoran Wang, Xi Chen · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: This paper formalizes a genuinely new problem—Visual-to-Symbolic Analytical Solution Inference—that sits at an unexplored intersection of vision-language models and symbolic regression. The 7-stage CoT synthesis pipeline with explicit leakage validation is methodologically creative, and the VLM vs. LLM ablation cleanly demonstrates that visual inductive bias provides structural understanding unrecoverable from tabulated samples (0.323→0.768 structural score for GPT-5.2). However, restriction to synthetic 2D linear steady-state fields and the 1,500-instance CoT bottleneck temper the contribution's breadth.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Recovering analytical (closed-form) solutions of physical fields from their visual representations is a capability that current VLMs lack. While VLMs can describe field visualizations—identifying symmetries, singularities, and oscillation patterns—they cannot translate those visual signatures into compact symbolic mathematical expressions with correctly instantiated numeric parameters. Existing symbolic regression methods (genetic programming, SINDy, AI Feynman, LLM-SR) operate on tabular point samples and discard the rich 2D spatial structure encoded in field heatmaps. The paper formalizes this gap as Visual-to-Symbolic Analytical Solution Inference (ViSA): given field visualizations (scalar field + first-order derivative components) and minimal auxiliary metadata, output a single executable SymPy expression with fully instantiated constants representing the instance-specific solution. Methodology: The authors introduce three interrelated components. First, ViSA-Bench: a synthetic benchmark of 30 physically plausible 2D linear steady-state scenarios (Laplacian, Helmholtz, Schrödinger, Fokker-Planck, etc.) spanning electrostatics, heat transfer, fluid dynamics, and quantum mechanics, with 500 parameterized instances per scenario (15,000 total), using a solution-first generation pipeline to avoid numerical solver errors. Evaluation uses three complementary metrics: character-level accuracy (normalized Levenshtein distance), expression-structure similarity (binary AST match after replacing constants with placeholders), and numerical accuracy (relative L2 error on evaluation points), combined into a weighted geometric mean (weights 0.2/0.3/0.5). Second, a programmatic 7-stage CoT synthesis pipeline that generates self-verifying, solution-centric reasoning traces: Stage 1 extracts visual cues from images without consulting ground truth; Stage 2 verifies these against numerical statistics; Stage 3 derives expected signatures from the ground-truth expression; Stages 4-5 match observations to signatures and derive parameters from ≥2 independent sources with cross-validation; Stage 6 synthesizes a natural-language reasoning chain; Stage 7 validates for leakage, evidence consistency, and multi-source verification. Third, ViSA-R2, a Qwen3-VL 8B model fine-tuned on 1,500 gold-CoT instances using standard next-token prediction over concatenated rationale and solution sequences. A hybrid ViSA-R2 framework augments structure prediction with L-BFGS-B coefficient refinement. Key Results: Under the standardized protocol with models achieving >90% valid output rate, ViSA-R2 achieves an overall score of 0.512 (Char: 0.482, Struct: 0.860, Num: 0.385), outperforming GPT-5.2 (0.474), Grok-4-1-Fast (0.242), and Claude-Haiku-4.5 (0.316) in VLM mode. The ViSA-R2 framework (with L-BFGS-B coefficient refinement) reaches 0.669 overall by substantially improving numerical accuracy (0.780 vs. 0.385) with minimal overhead. Ablation A confirms VLMs consistently outperform LLM-only baselines receiving 400 tabulated numeric samples—GPT-5.2's structural score jumps from 0.323 (LLM) to 0.768 (VLM), confirming visual inductive bias. Ablation B shows CoT alignment is essential: removing it collapses overall score from 0.512 to 0.024 (the model produces repetitive, looping, invalid outputs), while the Qwen-3-8B baseline without fine-tuning scores 0.328. Models with high per-instance scores but low completion rates (DeepSeek-R1: 0.915 at 10%; Grok-4: 0.828 at 3.3%) are reported separately as non-comparable due to output format failures. Strengths: - The problem formulation is precise and addresses a genuine gap: visual-to-symbolic inference is qualitatively distinct from both VLM description and numeric symbolic regression, and the three-metric evaluation (structure + numerical + character) meaningfully captures different failure modes. - The 7-stage CoT synthesis pipeline is technically principled—Stages 1-2 are observation-only (no GT leakage), Stage 7 explicitly validates for leakage and reasoning consistency, producing high-quality supervision data that cannot be cheaply replicated by prompting alone. - The VLM vs. LLM ablation cleanly isolates the contribution of visual structure, showing large structural score gains (0.323→0.768 for GPT-5.2) that cannot be recovered from tabulated samples, validating the core hypothesis. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark is restricted to 2D linear steady-state fields from synthetic parameterized families; generalization to nonlinear, time-dependent, noisy, or real-world observed fields is entirely undemonstrated and acknowledged as future work. - Gold-CoT construction scaled to only 1,500 instances out of 15,000 because attempts to scale via prompted imitation failed (hallucinated cues, inconsistent logic). This resource bottleneck limits training data diversity and raises questions about whether performance gains reflect genuine visual reasoning or scenario-specific pattern memorization. - The evaluation protocol has significant comparability issues: several strong models (Gemini-3-Pro at 60%, Claude Opus at 26.7%, DeepSeek-R1 at 10%, Grok-4 at 3.3%) fail to produce parsable outputs at scale, so the main Table 1 comparison is limited to models that happen to comply with output formatting—arguably a reliability benchmark as much as a scientific reasoning one.
Search Results 2604.08863 PDF
#29 — Innovative (74)

ProbeLogits: Kernel-Level LLM Inference Primitives for AI-Native Operating Systems

Daeyeon Son · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: ProbeLogits introduces a genuinely novel architectural insight by placing LLM inference inside the OS kernel as a first-class primitive, creating a structural enforcement boundary that application-layer safety systems fundamentally cannot achieve. The KV cache as process state abstraction (checkpoint/restore/fork) is conceptually elegant with clean OS analogies. The calibration parameter as a deployment-time policy knob is practically useful. However, the weak evaluation methodology (single annotator, tuning on test set, 22% false-positive rate on ToxicChat) and CPU-only limitation to ≤7B models significantly temper the impact of an otherwise compelling vision.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Contemporary AI safety systems enforce governance at the application layer (e.g., LangChain, NeMo Guardrails), creating three compounding problems: (1) latency accumulation—a text-based safety check on a 7B model requires ~650 ms of generation, parsing, and IPC; (2) bypassability—application-level classifiers operate at the same privilege level as the agents they constrain, allowing a capable or buggy agent to circumvent them; and (3) semantic opacity—the OS kernel has no visibility into LLM internals (logits, uncertainty, intent) and cannot make semantically informed scheduling or governance decisions. The paper argues that the OS kernel should treat LLM logit vectors as a first-class abstraction rather than discarding them. Methodology: ProbeLogits is a kernel-level operation that performs exactly one forward pass over a prompt and reads token logits at class-label positions ("verbalizers" in NLP) to classify actions without generating any text. The technique itself—restricted N-way softmax over verbalizer logits with contextual calibration—is from the NLP literature; the contribution is its integration as an OS primitive with structural enforcement guarantees. Three operations are exposed: binary probe_yes_no, N-way probe_classify (both cost one full forward pass), and logit_entropy for uncertainty measurement. A calibration strength parameter α is measured at boot time from null inputs to subtract the model's sycophancy bias, and serves as a deployment-time precision-recall knob rather than a learned hyperparameter. A three-stage defense pipeline precedes each classification: (1) O(n) adversarial pre-filter for prompt injection and encoding tricks, (2) input sanitization, and (3) calibrated 7B forward pass with Safe/Dangerous verbalizers plus a hand-coded privacy keyword logit boost. The entire system is implemented in Anima OS, an 80,400-line bare-metal x86_64 OS written in Rust with no Linux dependency, featuring AVX-512 VNNI SIMD kernels, a work-stealing SMP graph executor, and a custom xHCI USB driver for loading GGUF model files. KV cache is additionally exposed as process state with kv_checkpoint, kv_restore, and kv_fork operations, drawing a direct analogy to CPU register checkpointing in traditional process management. Key Results: On a custom 260-prompt OS action benchmark (9 categories including adversarial attacks, single model, one hardware configuration), the full ProbeLogits pipeline achieves F1=0.980, Precision=1.000, Recall=0.960—versus F1=0.927 for generate-then-parse on identical prompts. Precision is 1.000 across all values of α on OS actions, enabling "block without review" deployment. On ToxicChat (1,000 independently human-annotated conversational prompts, no recalibration), F1 drops to 0.790 at α=1.0 due to a 22% false-positive rate on benign prompts, improving to F1=0.837 at α=0.5—89% of Llama Guard 3 8B (F1~0.939) with zero learned fine-tuning. Inference throughput: 1,666 tokens/s on SmolLM2-135M (1.39× llama.cpp) and 15 tokens/s on Qwen2.5-7B (parity), where the 7B is bandwidth-bound at 85% of theoretical DDR5-6000 peak. ProbeLogits classification costs 0.6 ms on 135M and 65 ms on 7B, a 10× latency reduction over text generation. Strengths: - Genuine architectural insight: placing inference inside the kernel creates a structural enforcement boundary below the WASM sandbox, making safety checks non-bypassable in a way that application-layer classifiers fundamentally cannot achieve. - The KV cache as process state abstraction (checkpoint/restore/fork) is conceptually elegant and has concrete practical applications (speculative execution, agent migration, conversation branching) with clean OS analogies. - The calibration parameter α as a deployment-time policy knob—yielding perfect precision on OS actions across all α values—is a practically useful design feature that avoids retraining for domain-specific policies. - Substantial systems engineering: a full bare-metal Rust OS with a custom xHCI driver, AVX-512 SIMD inference kernels, SMP work-stealing scheduler, TLS 1.3 stack, Raft consensus, and WASM Cranelift sandbox in 80K LoC. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The custom benchmark has serious methodological weaknesses the paper itself acknowledges: ground-truth labels were assigned by the system developer (single annotator), and the adversarial pre-filter plus privacy keyword boost were iteratively tuned against the same 260 prompts—creating a high risk of overfitting to the test set. The reported F1=0.980 should be treated skeptically until validated on held-out data with independent annotators. - The ToxicChat results reveal a significant generalization failure: a 22% false-positive rate on benign conversational inputs makes the system unsuitable as a general-purpose safety classifier, and the paper's framing of this as "by design" (OS actions vs. chat) partially obscures that the core model-only F1=0.941 on a curated benchmark overstates real-world utility. - kv_fork() is currently implemented as an alias for kv_checkpoint(), TLS lacks certificate verification (UnsecureProvider), and multi-agent scenarios with realistic workloads have not been demonstrated—leaving several claimed capabilities validated only at the infrastructure level. - CPU-only inference limits the system to ≤7B models; bare-metal GPU acceleration requires reverse-engineering proprietary interfaces and is identified as a major unsolved challenge, constraining the approach to a narrow hardware configuration.
Search Results 2604.11943 PDF
#30 — Innovative (73)

SPPO: Sequence-Level PPO for Long-Horizon Reasoning Tasks (25 ▲ )

Tianyi Wang, Yixia Li, Long Li, Yibiao Chen, Shaohan Huang, Yun Chen, Peng Li, Yang Liu, Guanhua Chen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 73 for its clean conceptual reframing of long-horizon RLVR as a sequence-level contextual bandit, which directly resolves the critic's 'Tail Effect' without requiring multi-sample baselines. The Decoupled Critic finding — that a 1.5B model can effectively serve as baseline for a 7B policy — is both theoretically insightful and practically valuable for memory-constrained settings. However, the accuracy margins over GRPO are modest, the method is fundamentally restricted to binary-reward settings, and evaluation breadth is limited.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) is central to aligning LLMs on mathematical reasoning, but standard PPO with token-level GAE fails in this regime. When rewards are sparse and deferred to the end of a long Chain-of-Thought, the token-level critic exhibits a "Tail Effect": it learns to discriminate correct from incorrect trajectories only near the sequence end, causing advantage signals to vanish for early tokens and making credit assignment position-dependent rather than contribution-dependent. The dominant alternative, GRPO, eliminates the critic but requires N=8 samples per prompt to form a statistical baseline, bottlenecking throughput by ~6×. There is no existing algorithm that achieves both single-sample efficiency and stable optimization for long-horizon RLVR tasks. Methodology: SPPO reframes the core problem: rather than treating reasoning as a token-level Markov Decision Process, it explicitly reformulates it as a Sequence-Level Contextual Bandit (SL-CB). The prompt is the static context, and the entire response is an atomic action. A lightweight scalar value model V(s_p) is trained with binary cross-entropy to estimate the prompt-level probability of success—not a step-level value function. The advantage is computed as A = R − V(s_p), where R ∈ {0,1} is the binary outcome reward. This single scalar is then broadcast uniformly to all tokens in the sequence, replacing the time-dependent GAE signal. The policy objective retains PPO's clipped surrogate form but operates on this stable, horizon-independent advantage. A key variant, the Decoupled Critic, uses a 1.5B critic to align a 7B policy, exploiting the observation that scalar solvability estimation is structurally simpler than generative reasoning, allowing a much smaller model to serve as an effective baseline. An ablation (PPO + BCE) replacing only the critic loss while keeping token-level advantage confirms the benefit derives from the bandit formulation, not just the loss function. The method is validated on five classic control environments reconfigured as RLVR tasks (CartPole, MountainCar, Hopper, LunarLander, Pendulum) to isolate algorithmic from systems-level effects. Key Results: On DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B fine-tuned with DeepScaleR, SPPO achieves an average score of 48.06 across AIME24/25, AMC23, MATH500, and Minerva, versus 47.08 for GRPO (N=8) and 44.06 for standard PPO. On the 7B model (DAPO-17K), SPPO reaches 58.11 vs. GRPO's 57.44 and PPO's 56.44. The Decoupled Critic variant (1.5B critic + 7B policy) achieves 58.56, the highest of any configuration. Training efficiency is dramatically improved: SPPO reaches peak performance (~58 mean score on 7B) in approximately 22 hours on 4×H100s, compared to significantly longer convergence times for GRPO and other baselines. The Decoupled Critic reduces peak VRAM from 91.5% to 78.7% (a 12.8% reduction), while GRPO requires 85.7% and RLOO/ReMax require ~98–99%. The value model achieves Pearson r=0.642 and Spearman ρ=0.664 correlation with empirical pass rates, confirming it captures prompt difficulty reliably despite conservatively centering predictions around 0.6–0.7. On the RLVR control benchmark, SPPO solves Hopper and MountainCar where standard PPO flatlines near zero. Strengths: - The reinterpretation of GRPO as an implicit Contextual Bandit provides genuine conceptual clarity and directly motivates the explicit sequence-level design, rather than being post-hoc rationalization. - The Decoupled Critic result is practically impactful: a 1.5B critic that outperforms a matched 7B critic on the same policy confirms the thesis that solvability estimation is a structurally simpler task, and the memory savings make RLVR accessible on constrained hardware. - The ablation (PPO + BCE) is well-designed and clearly demonstrates that the performance gain is structural (bandit formulation), not attributable to the choice of loss function alone. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method is explicitly restricted to tasks with verifiable binary rewards; the scalar V(s_p) baseline cannot generalize to open-ended generation tasks where outcome correctness is subjective or graded. This is a fundamental architectural constraint, not merely a gap for future work. - The margin of improvement over GRPO on accuracy is modest (48.06 vs. 47.08 at 1.5B; 58.11 vs. 57.44 at 7B), and the claimed 5.9× speedup is based on wall-clock time comparisons that likely reflect infrastructure and batching differences as much as algorithmic efficiency. The paper does not normalize for effective tokens processed or separate algorithmic from implementation speedup. - Evaluation is restricted to two DeepSeek distilled models on mathematical benchmarks; generalization to other model families (e.g., Llama, Mistral) and reasoning domains (code, science) is undemonstrated, limiting confidence in the universality of the approach.
Recommended 2604.08865 PDF
#31 — Innovative (73)

ECHO: Efficient Chest X-ray Report Generation with One-step Block Diffusion (14 ▲ )

Lifeng Chen, Tianqi You, Hao Liu, Zhimin Bao, Jile Jiao, Xiao Han, Zhicai Ou, Tao Sun, Xiaofeng Mou, Xiaojie Jin, Yi Xu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 13 Apr 2026
Why this rank: ECHO makes a well-formalized contribution by identifying and addressing mean-field bias in one-step block diffusion decoding through Direct Conditional Distillation, which constructs non-factorized supervision targets from on-policy teacher trajectories — a principled departure from prior factorized distillation methods. The report normalization insight is a clinically grounded observation with generalizable value. Achieving only 2-5% quality degradation at 8x throughput is a strong practical result, though the work is narrowly scoped to CXR reporting with normalized test sets, and residual token-level artifacts suggest the one-step regime is not yet clinically safe without post-processing.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Automated chest X-ray (CXR) report generation is a high-value clinical application given the volume of radiology reads required daily. Autoregressive vision-language models (VLMs) achieve strong accuracy but are bottlenecked by sequential token decoding. Diffusion-based VLMs (dVLMs) enable parallel generation but still require multiple denoising steps to remain coherent, because their token-factorized (mean-field) denoisers introduce a structural bias that grows monotonically with the number of masked tokens. Compressing diffusion decoding to a single step maximally exploits parallelism but collapses quality due to this bias. Prior distillation methods for dLLMs reduce step counts but retain factorized prediction targets, so mean-field bias resurfaces fully in one-step decoding. ECHO targets this gap: can a dVLM generate clinically accurate CXR reports in a single denoising step per block? Methodology: ECHO is built in three successive training stages on top of Lingshu-7B, a medical autoregressive VLM. Stage 1 performs continued pre-training (CPT) on a curated, normalized CXR corpus (~730k bilingual reports from MIMIC-CXR, CheXpert-Plus, ReXGradient, IU-Xray), producing ECHO-AR. A key data contribution is report normalization: every anatomical region receives an explicit annotation (positive finding or negative assertion), eliminating the implicit omission bias inherent in radiologists' "report by exception" convention. Stage 2 applies Response-Asymmetric Diffusion (RAD) adaptation, which converts ECHO-AR into a block diffusion model (ECHO-Base) using a single SFT stage. Unlike prior AR-to-diffusion conversion methods that duplicate the entire sequence (vision + instruction + response) across two stages, RAD duplicates only the response tokens and constructs a block-causal attention mask accordingly, reducing training FLOPs by 72.3% (3.61× speedup) for CXR's long vision token sequences (~2,870 tokens). Stage 3 applies Direct Conditional Distillation (DCD), the core technical contribution, to distill ECHO-Base into a one-step-per-block model. DCD proceeds in two phases per training iteration: (i) On-policy teacher trajectory collection — ECHO-Base runs confidence-heuristic multi-step denoising on each block, recording predicted distributions at each step; these are stitched into a joint, non-factorized supervision target that encodes cross-token dependencies accumulated across the multi-step process. (ii) Student one-step alignment — the student is trained to match this joint target via forward KL divergence, with a step-wise token reweighting scheme that upweights positions unmasked later (higher mean-field bias). An additional cross-entropy loss on the EOS token addresses degenerate repetition loops caused by low EOS confidence in one-step decoding. At inference, a Fused Block KV Cache eliminates the dedicated KV update forward pass by merging it into the next block's denoising pass, halving forward pass count without adding FLOPs. Key Results: Evaluated on MIMIC-CXR, CheXpert-Plus, and ReXGradient with both English and Chinese reports, ECHO-blk8 (block size 8, 8× TPF speedup) achieves ROUGE-L of 55.21/65.13/53.07, RaTEScore of 56.85/59.92/52.97, and SemScore of 51.40/64.00/44.83 across the three datasets, with only 2–5% quality degradation relative to the multi-step ECHO-Base. Versus the nearest quality-preserving competitor T3D (which also uses DPO-style distillation), ECHO-blk8 delivers 4× the inference speedup at comparable quality drop. Speed-comparable methods like dParallel (4.4× TPF) lose 18–32% on clinical fidelity metrics where ECHO-blk8 loses only 2–5%. ECHO-blk4 (4× speedup) degrades by ≤1.25% on average relative to ECHO-Base. Against autoregressive medical VLMs (Lingshu-7B, MedGemma-27B, Hulu-Med), ECHO improves RaTEScore and SemScore by 64.33% and 60.58% respectively at 8× higher throughput. Even against proprietary general-purpose models (Gemini3-Pro, Qwen3-Max), ECHO maintains a clear advantage on clinical fidelity metrics. DCD's contribution is validated directly: native one-step decoding of ECHO-Base (no distillation) degrades ROUGE-L by 36% and CIDEr by 44% on CheXpert-Plus; DCD recovers nearly all of this. Report normalization is shown to be critical: without it, ROUGE-L collapses to ~19 after distillation (Stage III) versus ~56 with normalization. Strengths: - DCD is a principled solution to a real, well-formalized problem (mean-field bias under one-step decoding): constructing a non-factorized supervision signal from on-policy teacher trajectories is technically sound and clearly differentiated from prior factorized distillation approaches. - RAD's response-only duplication is a practical and well-motivated efficiency gain, particularly impactful for CXR where vision tokens are long; the 3.61× training speedup with negligible quality loss is demonstrated empirically. - The report normalization insight — that radiologists' "report by exception" convention creates implicit negative omission bias that compounds through block diffusion and distillation — is a clinically grounded data contribution that generalizes beyond this specific model. - Comprehensive ablation isolates the contribution of each DCD component (step-wise weighting, EOS cross-entropy, forward vs. reverse KL), with consistent findings across three datasets and two languages. Weaknesses / Limitations: - DCD's on-policy teacher trajectory collection adds training-time overhead that is not quantified in the paper; the 30,000-example distillation corpus is small, but each sample requires a multi-step teacher rollout, and the computational cost of this phase is not reported. - Despite strong clinical fidelity metrics, the qualitative examples reveal residual word-level repetition and minor token artifacts in ECHO-blk8 outputs (e.g., "left left at atasis", "right righturaluralusion"), suggesting the one-step model still occasionally produces clinically problematic surface errors that aggregate metrics may underweight. - The evaluation is restricted to CXR reporting with a normalized test set; performance on raw, unnormalized reports from real clinical deployments — where the normalization pipeline itself may introduce errors — is not assessed, leaving a gap between the controlled experimental setting and real-world use.
Recommended 2604.09450 PDF
#32 — Innovative (73)

Representations Before Pixels: Semantics-Guided Hierarchical Video Prediction (6 ▲ )

Efstathios Karypidis, Spyros Gidaris, Nikos Komodakis · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Re2Pix provides a clean and well-motivated hierarchical decomposition that explicitly separates semantic forecasting from visual synthesis — a principled departure from the dominant single-latent-space paradigm. The nested dropout and mixed supervision strategies for bridging the train-test feature mismatch are technically clever, and the 7-14x training convergence speedup is practically significant. However, the core idea of predict-then-render in a semantic space is not entirely new, and evaluation is restricted to driving domains.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Accurate video prediction in complex dynamic environments like autonomous driving requires simultaneously mastering high-level scene semantics (object presence, interactions) and photorealistic appearance. Dominant end-to-end approaches predict future frames directly within a VAE latent space using diffusion models, but this entangles semantic structure and visual appearance in a single representation. This entanglement causes temporal semantic inconsistencies (object identity drift, structural degradation, flickering), slows training convergence, and prevents independent reasoning over scene dynamics versus appearance. Alignment-based methods (REPA, VideoREPA) that inject semantic structure via auxiliary distillation objectives still perform both forecasting and rendering within a single latent space, leaving the two roles implicitly coupled. Re2Pix asks whether semantic forecasting and visual synthesis can be explicitly decoupled while maintaining coherent generative prediction. Methodology: Re2Pix decomposes video prediction into two distinct hierarchical stages. Stage 1 extracts semantic features from context frames using a frozen DINOv2-Reg ViT-B/14 vision foundation model (VFM), then autoregressively predicts future frame features in this semantic space using a masked transformer trained with Smooth L1 loss. Stage 2 conditions a latent video diffusion model (800M-parameter DiT backbone using EDM formulation, WAN2.1 causal 3D VAE) on both context and predicted VFM features via early fusion—features are resized to match VAE latent resolution and summed channel-wise at the input, providing structural guidance without increasing token count. A critical challenge is the train–test mismatch: during training, ground-truth VFM features are available for future frames, but at inference only autoregressively predicted (noisier) features are available. To bridge this gap, the authors introduce two complementary strategies. Nested dropout stochastically truncates PCA feature channels during training (DINOv2 features projected to 1152 PCA components), encouraging robustness to coarse-to-fine feature degradation. Mixed supervision exposes the generator to a 90%/10% mixture of ground-truth and predicted features during training, directly regularizing against overfit to idealized semantics. The semantic prediction stage processes only every r-th frame to match the VAE's temporal subsampling ratio. Key Results: On Cityscapes at 432×768 resolution (K=25 frames, 13 context frames, 12 predicted), Re2Pix achieves mIoU(A)=63.53 vs. baseline 60.55 (+2.98), mIoU on moving objects=62.29 vs. 57.64 (+4.65), FID=9.90 vs. 12.86 (-2.96), and FVD=52.66 vs. 60.70 (-8.04). Re2Pix outperforms both REPA and VideoREPA alignment baselines and a parameter-matched larger baseline (1.5B parameters), confirming gains stem from architecture not scale. Training convergence accelerates dramatically: Re2Pix reaches FID=15 in 20k iterations versus 140k for the baseline (7× speedup), and segmentation mIoU converges 14× faster. On extended multi-dataset training (Cityscapes + nuScenes + CoVLA), Re2Pix surpasses Vista (a large-scale pretrained driving world model) on most metrics and is competitive with Cosmos-Predict-2 despite Cosmos-Predict-2 being pretrained on orders-of-magnitude more data. Zero-shot KITTI FID improves from 20.02 (baseline) to 15.94 (Re2Pix). Ablations confirm that both nested dropout and mixed supervision individually contribute, with mixed supervision being especially important for closing the generation quality gap (FID/FVD). Strengths: - Clean hierarchical decomposition enforces a meaningful division of labor: the semantic stage handles temporal dynamics and the diffusion stage handles appearance, yielding consistent gains on both semantic consistency and perceptual quality simultaneously—a result alignment-based methods fail to achieve. - The identification and principled treatment of the train–test mismatch (nested dropout + mixed supervision) is technically well-motivated and empirically validated with ablations showing each component's distinct contribution. - The 7–14× training convergence acceleration is practically significant for researchers with limited compute, and the effect holds consistently across all evaluation metrics throughout the training trajectory. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is limited exclusively to autonomous driving datasets (Cityscapes, nuScenes, CoVLA, KITTI); no experiments on general-domain video prediction benchmarks (e.g., Kinetics, UCF-101) leave the broader applicability of the hierarchical design undemonstrated. - The two-stage design adds architectural complexity: the semantic prediction transformer must be separately trained and maintained, and the 1.1B total parameter count is roughly 40% larger than the 782M baseline. The paper does not report inference latency comparisons, obscuring the practical cost of running two sequential models. - Despite competitive results on Cityscapes and nuScenes, Re2Pix lags Cosmos-Predict-2 on zero-shot KITTI FVD (61.73 vs. 41.64), suggesting that large-scale pretraining provides temporal generalization advantages the hierarchical design alone cannot overcome.
Recommended 2604.11707 PDF
#33 — Innovative (73)

Cognitive Pivot Points and Visual Anchoring: Unveiling and Rectifying Hallucinations in Multimodal Reasoning Models

Zhe Qian, Yanbiao Ma, Zhuohan Ouyang, Zhonghua Wang, Zhongxing Xu, Fei Luo, Xinyu Liu, Zongyuan Ge, Yike Guo, Jungong Han · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: This paper scores 73 for its genuinely insightful mechanistic analysis of hallucination in multimodal reasoning models — the three-finding framework (entropy spikes at cognitive pivot points, layer 11-20 anchoring failure, pseudo-reflection paradox) is a meaningful diagnostic contribution that directly motivates each design choice in V-STAR. The forced reflection mechanism that trains implicit reflection behavior by stripping trigger tokens is a clever training trick. However, the actual benchmark improvements are narrow (64.5 vs 63.0 aggregate, margins of 0.4-2.4 points), and the approach is validated on only one architecture, limiting the impact of the layer-specific claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal Large Reasoning Models (MLRMs) that use chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning hallucinate significantly more than standard non-reasoning generators — a counterintuitive result since CoT is designed to improve reliability. The paper identifies this as the Reasoning Vision Truth Disconnect (RVTD): long CoT sequences cause models to increasingly rely on language priors and neglect visual evidence. Prior hallucination mitigations (contrastive decoding, post-hoc correction, RLHF-style rewards) operate at the output level and fail to address the internal mechanism failure. The key gap is the absence of fine-grained, process-level supervision that targets where and when visual grounding collapses. Methodology: The paper first conducts a mechanistic three-finding analysis on 700 RealWorldQA and 1000 MathVista samples. Finding 1 (When): Shannon entropy of the next-token distribution spikes at logical transition words ("However", "Therefore", "Wait") — these cognitive bifurcation points precede over 70% of hallucinations. Finding 2 (Where): layer-wise Mahalanobis distance and mutual information analysis localizes visual semantic anchoring failure to intermediate layers 11–20, where image tokens undergo cross-modal commitment; grounded tokens show higher visual attention and concentration than hallucinated tokens specifically in this window, confirmed via Logit Lens and PCA. Finding 3 (Why): even when the model produces explicit reflective language ("Let me check"), visual attention does not rebound — a Pseudo Reflection paradox driven by cognitive inertia. These findings motivate V-STAR (Visual Structural Training with Attention Reinforcement), built on Qwen2.5-VL-7B and trained on a custom 40k instruction dataset. V-STAR has two components integrated under GRPO. The Hierarchical Visual Attention Reward (HVAR) monitors entropy in real time; upon detecting a high-entropy pivot (H(y_t) > τ_ent), it computes the mean visual attention share across layers 11–20 and assigns a piecewise reward (0.40 for high attention, 0.20 for moderate, 0.0 otherwise), plus a global bonus for sustained visual engagement. The Forced Reflection Mechanism (FRM) performs trajectory editing: it identifies transition tokens, stochastically inserts an explicit reflection trigger with probability q, generates a revised continuation y2 in reflection mode, then strips the trigger token before training so the model internalizes the reflection habit rather than learning to follow a command. Correct trajectories (from either first attempt or after reflection) are replayed via an auxiliary SFT loss. The composite reward is R_total = R_outcome + R_visual + R_reflect. Dataset construction uses a dual-stream pipeline: a caption-then-reason cascade for structured STEM/chart inputs and a generate-then-refine pipeline for natural scenes, with automated quality filtering. Key Results: V-STAR achieves state-of-the-art across four benchmark domains against six 7B-scale MLRMs using 40k training samples (less than most baselines). Overall aggregate scores: General Reasoning 64.5 vs. ThinkLite-VL 63.0; Hallucination 55.6 vs. 55.2; Mathematical Reasoning 52.6 vs. 51.5; Scientific Reasoning 49.8 vs. 47.4. On POPE adversarial split (hardest object hallucination setting), V-STAR scores 87.6 F1 versus the next best 86.7. On MathVision, V-STAR achieves 33.7% accuracy while generating shorter chains than all competitors (upper-left in accuracy-length tradeoff). Ablations confirm both HVAR and FRM are individually necessary: on MMHalu, base RL gives 3.81, HVAR alone 3.92, FRM alone 3.99, combined 4.12. The U-score metric quantifies that V-STAR's reflection phases produce genuine visual attention rebounds (higher recovery gain and U-score than the base Qwen2.5-VL-7B), not verbal artifacts. Linguistic quality (naturalness, fluency, grammar via GPT-5; perplexity via GPT-2) is maintained or improved despite the added reflection behavior. Strengths: - Principled mechanistic grounding: the three empirical findings (temporal coupling, layer-wise localization, pseudo reflection) directly motivate each design choice, making V-STAR interpretable rather than heuristic. - Lightweight and efficient: 40k training samples, no teacher model distillation, no extra inference latency; the FRM implicit training approach elegantly converts an external debiasing intervention into an intrinsic model behavior. - Improved reasoning efficiency alongside accuracy: shorter chains with higher accuracy on MathVision demonstrates that better visual grounding eliminates semantic wandering, not just adds more verification steps. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are confined to Qwen2.5-VL-7B; the layer-specific claims (intermediate layers 11–20 as the anchoring locus) may not generalize to architectures with different depth or cross-modal fusion designs, limiting applicability claims. - The entropy threshold τ_ent and pivot detection mechanism are heuristic and likely sensitive to model-specific calibration; the paper does not analyze robustness to threshold choice or discuss how to set these for new architectures. - Evaluations are primarily on standard academic benchmarks; there is limited analysis on grounding-critical real-world tasks (e.g., document understanding, medical imaging) where the visual fidelity claims would be most consequential.

#34 — Innovative (72)

Foundry: Template-Based CUDA Graph Context Materialization for Fast LLM Serving Cold Start

Xueshen Liu, Yongji Wu, Yuncheng Yao, Danyang Zhuo, Ion Stoica, Z. Morley Mao · arxiv.org · Ion Stoica - new articles
Why this rank: Foundry identifies a principled and general insight — that CUDA graph serialization fails not because of topology but because execution context (memory layout and kernel binaries) is process-specific — and addresses it with a clean, library-agnostic architecture. The template compression observation (512 graphs collapsing to ≤25 topologies) reveals exploitable structural regularity in LLM inference, and single-GPU offline capture for multi-GPU deployment is a clever decoupling that scales archive cost sublinearly with cluster size. The 95–99% cold-start reduction (650s → 3.9s for large MoE) is a practically significant result that directly enables autoscaling workflows previously bottlenecked by initialization.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern LLM serving infrastructure relies on autoscaling and dynamic parallelism reconfiguration to handle bursty, unpredictable traffic. Both require cold-starting new serving instances, and while recent systems have reduced model weight loading to 1–2 seconds via RDMA, CUDA graph capture remains the dominant bottleneck — taking tens of seconds to over 10 minutes for large MoE models. CUDA graphs cannot be naively serialized because they are not self-contained: kernel argument buffers embed device-side memory pointers valid only in the capturing process, and kernel function handles are lazily loaded during warmup. Prior approaches either rely on brittle, kernel-specific patching rules (Medusa) that break with evolving cuBLAS opaque structs and hybrid dense/MoE architectures, or heavyweight process-level checkpointing (CUDA-checkpoint) that is incompatible with IPC memory required for multi-GPU inference and ill-suited to dynamic parallelism switching. Methodology: Foundry introduces template-based CUDA graph context materialization operating in offline SAVE and online LOAD phases. During SAVE, Foundry runs the normal engine warmup and graph capture once on a single GPU while intercepting CUDA driver calls via LD_PRELOAD. It enforces a deterministic memory layout by redirecting all CUDA allocations through the Virtual Memory Management (VMM) API to a bump allocator at a fixed base address, recording the final offset. It then performs binary extraction and reload: intercepting cuModuleLoad/cuLibraryLoad calls to extract in-memory .cubin payloads from shared libraries (e.g., libcublas.so), pre-linking relocatable segments, and building a catalog keyed by (binary hash, mangled kernel name). The resulting archive contains serialized graph metadata plus packed kernel binaries. During LOAD, Foundry preallocates the entire memory range in one call (reducing each subsequent allocation to a nanosecond pointer bump), preloads all kernel binaries, then reconstructs only a small set of template graphs — one per unique topology — using CUDA's explicit graph construction APIs. Non-template graphs (95–98% of all captured graphs) are served via in-place cuGraphExecUpdate, which updates per-node parameters 24–32× faster than full reconstruction. For distributed serving, a single-GPU SAVE is reused across all SPMD ranks by stubbing out NCCL/NVSHMEM communication during capture and patching rank-dependent handles at LOAD time, reducing archive storage from cluster-scale to single-device. Key Results: Evaluated on vLLM with dense models (Qwen3-14B, Qwen3-32B, Llama3-8B, Gemma3-12B) and MoE models (Qwen3-30B-A3B, Qwen3-235B-A22B) across DP/EP configurations on H200 and B200 GPUs. Foundry reduces cold-start latency by 95–99% across all 15 configurations. The most dramatic case is Qwen3-235B-A22B EP8, where vLLM initialization drops from 650 s (≈10 min) to 3.9 s. Dense models initialize in 1.3–2.0 s versus 28–48 s baseline. Initialization time is nearly constant as rank count scales from 1 to 8, since reconstruction is CPU-only. Compared to CUDA-checkpoint (5.7–6.1 s on single-GPU configurations), Foundry is 2.6–4.4× faster and produces archives 3–5× smaller (e.g., 1.1 GB vs. 3.7 GB for Qwen3-14B). Serving throughput (TPOT) is indistinguishable from natively captured CUDA graphs across all batch sizes, models, and GPU architectures tested. Template compression is highly effective: 512 graphs collapse to 12–25 unique topologies, yielding 95–98% on-demand update coverage. On-demand parameter update costs 0.98–2.89 ms per graph versus 31–70 ms for template construction and 60–199 ms for stream capture. Strengths: - The core insight — that execution context (deterministic memory layout + kernel binaries) must be materialized alongside graph topology — is both principled and general, eliminating the fragility of per-kernel patching rules and making the system library-agnostic by construction. - Single-GPU offline capture for multi-GPU deployment is a significant practical contribution: it decouples offline processing cost from cluster scale and reduces archive storage by up to 64× for a 64-GPU cluster. - The topology-based templating observation (512 graphs collapse to ≤25 unique topologies across all tested architectures) reveals a fundamental structural regularity of LLM inference that the paper exploits effectively, enabling asynchronous graph reconstruction from foreground initialization tasks. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The deterministic memory layout assumption depends on LLM inference engines having monotonic, non-freeing allocation patterns. Engines that fragment or reuse memory during initialization (e.g., due to dynamic KV-cache sizing) require special handling, and the authors must fix KV-cache size before SAVE/LOAD, which constrains deployment flexibility. - Pipeline parallelism (PP) is explicitly excluded because different ranks execute different model stages, meaning Foundry's inter-rank sharing does not apply. As PP is sometimes used for very large models, this limits coverage for the highest-parameter-count deployments. - The evaluation omits direct comparison with Medusa (citing CUDA driver version incompatibility), so quantitative headroom over the closest prior topology-only approach is not directly measured; the architectural argument for superiority is compelling but the experimental gap is unquantified.
Recommended 2604.06664 PDF
#35 — Innovative (72)

Q-Zoom: Query-Aware Adaptive Perception for Efficient Multimodal Large Language Models

Yuheng Shi, Xiaohuan Pei, Linfeng Wen, Minjing Dong, Chang Xu · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Q-Zoom introduces a well-engineered query-aware adaptive framework that operates entirely within the prefilling pass, avoiding the redundant decoding overhead of competing methods. The consistency-aware label generation for the gating network and the self-distilled pseudo-label pipeline matching GT-supervised performance are technically strong contributions that eliminate annotation dependence.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) require high-resolution visual inputs for fine-grained tasks such as document understanding, OCR, and dense scene perception. Current approaches naively scale visual token count with input resolution, indiscriminately flooding the LLM's quadratic self-attention with thousands of tokens regardless of whether the query requires fine detail. This wastes computation on two fronts: query-level redundancy (many questions are answerable with coarse features) and spatial redundancy (most image regions are irrelevant background). Existing alternatives are unsatisfying: training-free heuristics (e.g., ViCrop) require multiple costly prefilling passes; RL-based "Think-with-Image" methods (e.g., Thyme, DeepEyes) shift the bottleneck to the language model via lengthy chain-of-thought decoding, and are expensive and unstable to train. Methodology: Q-Zoom is a plug-and-play, two-stage adaptive framework attached to a frozen MLLM backbone. Both stages operate on the intermediate feature space during the initial prefilling pass, adding no auto-regressive overhead. (1) Dynamic Gating Network: A lightweight branch (R=3 transformer layers) is initialized from pretrained backbone weights at depth B and fine-tuned to predict, from the terminal query token's hidden state, whether coarse-resolution context is sufficient. Training labels are produced without human annotation via a consistency-aware generation strategy: the model is run across a monotonically increasing resolution trajectory and only "valid transition" samples—where the model fails at low resolution but succeeds at high resolution—are retained; unstable cases are discarded. This yields clean binary routing targets. (2) Self-Distilled Region Proposal Network (SD-RPN): When the gate signals insufficient coarse context, a parallel lightweight branch (same R=3 layers, same depth B) predicts a dense spatial heatmap by computing the inner product between the terminal query token embedding and all visual token key embeddings. Pseudo-labels are constructed entirely self-supervised: raw cross-modal attention maps from layer l are denoised by filtering sink tokens (identified by anomalously large L2-norms) and then binarized via a tri-state assignment that labels only high-confidence foreground and background tokens, ignoring ambiguous mid-range activations. The predicted RoI bounding box is cropped from the high-resolution source image, re-encoded, and fused with the coarse context using prefix KV-cache reuse for efficiency. A continuous spatio-temporal positional encoding resolves the positional collision between coarse global tokens and dense RoI tokens by assigning RoI tokens an offset temporal index and interpolating their spatial positions from the source bounding box coordinates. Finally, a targeted Post-SFT (~7K hard samples mined via LLM-as-a-Judge) fine-tunes only the LLM backbone to fuse dual-stream inputs without catastrophic forgetting. Key Results: On Qwen2.5-VL-7B, Q-Zoom surpasses the brute-force 4,096-token baseline (85.9% Doc&OCR average) using only 1,024 maximum tokens, achieving 2.52x inference speedup and 53% visual token reduction. On High-Resolution benchmarks the gains are starker: Q-Zoom reaches 66.7% accuracy with a 576-token cap, beating the 4,096-token baseline (64.2%) by 2.5% while delivering 4.39x speedup and 73.2% token reduction. Against concurrent methods on Qwen2.5-VL-7B, Q-Zoom achieves 85.6% Doc&OCR average (vs. SD-RPN 84.5%, ViCrop 31.8%) and 72.3% High-Resolution average (vs. Thyme 71.2%, DeepEyes 71.1%), while running >4x faster than Thyme. Improvements generalize to LLaVA-1.5-7B (+7.2% average), Qwen2.5-VL-3B (+5.1%), Qwen3-VL-4B (+3.2%), and are orthogonally complementary to RL-trained reasoning models (ZwZ-Qwen2.5-VL: +6.6%; ZwZ-Qwen3-VL: +5.2%). Ablations confirm each component contributes: the gate alone boosts throughput ~30% on Doc/OCR without accuracy loss; Post-SFT is necessary to prevent spatial reasoning degradation from dual-stream fusion; the tri-state pseudo-label assignment is critical (collapsing τ_fg=τ_bg noticeably hurts performance). Strengths: - The consistency-aware label generation strategy for the gating network is a principled, annotation-free approach that cleanly eliminates label noise from MLLM hallucinations, and is empirically validated to improve both training stability and routing quality. - The self-distilled pseudo-label pipeline for SD-RPN matches GT-supervised performance (68K annotated bounding boxes → 78.0% average) using only 50K self-generated pseudo-labels (78.1%), demonstrating the method is annotation-free without performance sacrifice. - Operating entirely within the initial prefilling pass and reusing KV cache for the secondary encode avoids the redundant prefilling or auto-regressive decoding overhead of all competing paradigms, yielding a structurally superior efficiency profile. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The framework introduces multiple sensitive hyperparameters (gate threshold τ_gate, RoI threshold τ_roi, norm threshold τ_norm, foreground/background pseudo-label margins τ_fg/τ_bg, backbone split depth B, branch depth R) that require architecture-specific tuning; the paper does not fully characterize sensitivity across models or discuss how to set these in new deployment contexts without ablation. - On LLaVA-series models the dynamic gating provides negligible efficiency improvement because the low base resolution (336×336) forces the gate to nearly always trigger the RoI branch for high-resolution test images, exposing a fundamental mismatch between the framework's assumptions and weaker backbone capabilities.
