#36 — Innovative (72)

Memory Transfer Learning: How Memories are Transferred Across Domains in Coding Agents (24 ▲ )

Kangsan Kim, Minki Kang, Taeil Kim, Yanlai Yang, Mengye Ren, Sung Ju Hwang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Introduces a well-formulated new problem — cross-domain memory transfer for coding agents — and provides a principled answer: abstract, task-agnostic 'Insight' memories transfer effectively while concrete trajectories cause negative transfer. The finding that 431 cross-domain memories outperform 5,899 in-domain memories challenges the more-data-is-better assumption and is practically actionable. The qualitative decomposition showing 73% of gains come from meta-knowledge rather than algorithmic strategy is a useful insight for future memory system design.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Self-evolving coding agents rely on memory of past experiences to improve future performance, but existing approaches restrict memory retrieval to the same task domain (e.g., SWE-bench memories used only for SWE-bench). This is wasteful because diverse coding tasks—repository-level software engineering, competitive programming, ML research, DevOps—share a common underlying infrastructure: Linux shells, Python, cross-file dependency patterns. The paper asks whether memories generated on one type of coding task can transfer meaningfully to another, and if so, what properties of memory enable or hinder transfer. Methodology: The paper introduces Memory Transfer Learning (MTL), a framework that constructs a unified cross-domain memory pool and retrieves memories from heterogeneous benchmarks at inference time. The agent uses a standard two-stage pipeline: offline memory generation followed by online retrieval via embedding cosine similarity (text-embedding-3-small). Four memory representations are studied, spanning a concrete-to-abstract spectrum: (1) Trajectory—raw action/observation logs with no distillation; (2) Workflow—LLM-extracted reusable command sequences; (3) Summary—LLM-written natural language explanation of task, environment, actions, and success/failure analysis; (4) Insight—task-agnostic, three-field (title, description, content) generalizable principles with explicit instructions to avoid task-specific details. Memory generation is performed offline using an LLM judge to assess success/failure and separate prompts for each case. At inference time, N=3 memories are retrieved per query using embedding similarity; for memory types without explicit task fields (Workflow, Summary, Insight), the model generates a 4–5 sentence coding plan as the retrieval query. Experiments cover 6 benchmarks (LiveCodeBenchv6, Aider-Polyglot, SWE-Bench Verified, TerminalBench2, ReplicationBench, MLGym-Bench), 100 sampled tasks per benchmark, evaluated at Pass@3. The base agent is mini-swe-agent with GPT-5-mini; cross-model experiments use DeepSeek V3.2 and Qwen3-Coder-480B. Key Results: MTL with Insight memories improves average Pass@3 over zero-shot by 3.7% for GPT-5-mini (0.523→0.560), with gains up to 8.3% on MLGym-Bench and 7.8% on ReplicationBench. MTL outperforms in-domain self-evolving baselines: +2.9% over ReasoningBank (97 memories) and +1.7% over AgentKB (5,899 memories), using only 431 memories. Across the four memory formats, Insight > Summary > Workflow > Trajectory, confirming that abstraction level correlates with transfer effectiveness. A controlled ablation within Insight format shows task-agnostic instances outperform task-specific ones by +1.1% average. t-SNE and quantitative embedding analysis (Davies-Bouldin Index, LISI) confirm that Insight embeddings are domain-agnostic while Trajectory/Workflow embeddings cluster by benchmark. Transfer effectiveness scales monotonically with memory pool size and number of source domains (best at 9 domains). Cross-model transfer (e.g., GPT-5-mini memories applied to DeepSeek or Qwen3-Coder) consistently outperforms zero-shot, though it underperforms self-generated memories. Simple embedding retrieval outperforms both LLM reranking and task-adaptive memory rewriting, suggesting that dynamic multi-step agent settings require retrieval methods beyond static approaches. Qualitative analysis shows that ~73% of MTL gains come from meta-knowledge categories (iterative workflow discipline 15%, test-driven verification 14.5%, anti-pattern avoidance 14.4%), while direct algorithmic strategy transfer accounts for only 5.5%. Strengths: - Identifies a concrete, practically actionable principle: abstract (Insight-level) memories transfer across coding domains while low-level traces cause negative transfer, with both quantitative embedding analysis and controlled ablations supporting the claim. - Demonstrates efficiency advantage: 431 cross-domain memories outperform AgentKB's 5,899 memories, challenging the assumption that more data always helps. - Provides a formal decomposition of memory utility into domain-invariant (meta-knowledge) and domain-specific components with a provable abstraction-transfer tradeoff proposition. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Trajectory memory occasionally degrades performance (e.g., TerminalBench2 drops from 0.315 to 0.270 with MTL-T), and the paper acknowledges that negative transfer from domain-mismatched anchoring is a real risk without yet providing a principled solution beyond retrieval improvements. - The cross-model memory transfer result is presented as a positive finding, but the consistent underperformance versus self-generated memory suggests model-specific biases in memory content that are not yet well understood or characterized. - All experiments use a single base agent (mini-swe-agent) and rely on GPT-5-mini as both the memory generator and judge; the circular dependency between the memory-generating model and the evaluation model may inflate apparent gains and limits generalizability claims.
Recommended 2604.14004 PDF
#37 — Innovative (72)

Exploration and Exploitation Errors Are Measurable for Language Model Agents (21 ▲ )

Jaden Park, Jungtaek Kim, Jongwon Jeong, Robert D. Nowak, Kangwook Lee, Yong Jae Lee · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026
Why this rank: This paper introduces a genuinely novel, policy-agnostic framework for decomposing LM agent failures into exploration and exploitation errors using classical graph theory, filling a real gap in agent evaluation methodology. The finding that exploration error alone predicts task success (R²=0.947) is clean, surprising, and immediately actionable. The symbolic abstraction design is clever, and the harness engineering results demonstrate practical impact. However, the 2D grid environment is deliberately far from real-world settings, and the minimal semantic reintroduction experiment raises questions about transferability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Language model (LM) agents deployed in open-ended decision-making tasks — AI coding, workflow automation, embodied AI — must balance exploration of unknown environments with exploitation of acquired knowledge. Yet no systematic, policy-agnostic framework exists to distinguish and quantify these two failure modes from observed action trajectories alone. Classical RL definitions tie exploration and exploitation to internal policy or value functions, which are inaccessible for LM agents. Prior agent benchmarks rely on task success rates or trajectory-alignment metrics that assume a reference optimal policy and cannot isolate whether failures stem from insufficient exploration or poor exploitation. This paper fills that gap. Methodology: The authors construct a controllable evaluation environment consisting of (1) a partially observable 2D grid map where the agent navigates via up/down/left/right moves that reveal local cell information, and (2) an unknown task Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) with AND/OR prerequisite structure, whose nodes are placed in grid cells the agent must discover by visiting. All semantic content is replaced with random four-character tokens to prevent LM semantic priors from conflating with in-environment reasoning. Map difficulty (exploration vs. exploitation demand) is tuned via node density and corridor width. The key contribution is a policy-agnostic error metric derived from classical graph theory. Given the current map state, the agent's required action falls into one of four cases (Table 1): pure exploration when no tasks are pending, pure exploitation when the goal node is pending, exploitation-only when all cells are observed but tasks remain, and either when both unobserved cells and pending tasks exist. An action is penalized if it fails to gain progress toward target cells. To handle symmetric cases where gain alone is insufficient (oscillation traps), the authors define a "stale score" S_t = c_t + e_t + n_t based on the cyclomatic number (new loops formed), edge reuse beyond twice, and node revisit beyond twice within a no-progress trajectory segment — thresholds motivated by classical results showing optimal graph exploration traverses each undirected edge at most twice. An error is flagged when S_t increases or when the action has zero gain. Errors are attributed to exploration, exploitation, or both by the case. Thirteen frontier LMs across OpenAI (GPT-4.1, GPT-5.4 families), Google (Gemini 3.1 Pro, Flash), Anthropic (Claude Opus 4.6, Sonnet 4.6, Haiku 4.5), and GPT-OSS-120B are evaluated across 9 map configurations (3 exploitation demands × 3 DAG sizes) with 3 random seeds each, using four prompt variants (base, exploration-focused, exploitation-focused, balance), and with/without an explicit harness that provides a structured memory summary. Key Results: Log exploration error and success rate show a strong negative linear relationship (R² = 0.947) across all 13 models, while log exploitation error and success rate show essentially no correlation (R² = 0.006), establishing exploration capability as the primary bottleneck. Even top-performing models (Claude Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro) with 100% success rates exhibit qualitatively different strategies: Claude Opus 4.6 exploits known information and avoids unobserved cells once sufficient nodes are found, while Gemini 3.1 Pro continues actively exploring during return traversals. Exploration-focused prompts reduce exploration error from 0.123 to 0.099 and increase GPT-4.1 success rate from 63% to 80%; exploitation-focused prompts selectively reduce exploitation error (0.143→0.129) but hurt success (63%→50%). Harness engineering — injecting a rule-based structured memory summary (visited cells, frontier cells, activatable states) alongside observations — dramatically improves both models tested: Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite improves from 51.9% to 88.9% success with exploration error dropping from 0.172 to 0.030; GPT-4.1 improves from 63.0% to 92.6% with exploration error 0.297→0.053. Semantic information (real cooking task names vs. symbolic tokens) affects models differently: GPT-4.1 success rises ~3× with lower exploration error, suggesting it leverages semantic priors effectively, while Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite shows higher exploration error but 6× lower exploitation error, suggesting semantic content biases it toward myopic exploitation rather than global search. Strengths: - The policy-agnostic error metric is theoretically grounded in classical graph exploration (Whitney, Tarjan, Panaite & Pelc), avoids assuming any particular reference trajectory, and handles subtle edge cases (symmetric gain traps, benign backtracking, gateway revisits) through the three-component stale score. - The symbolic abstraction design cleanly isolates raw exploration/exploitation reasoning from semantic shortcuts, enabling controlled evaluation that complements real-world benchmarks. - The empirical finding that exploration error (R²=0.947) but not exploitation error predicts task success is a clean, actionable insight — not merely a post-hoc rationalization — and directly informs how to prioritize agent improvement. - Harness engineering results are practically impactful: nearly halving exploration error and gaining 30+ percentage points in success rate with no new information, only reorganized presentation. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The normalized error metrics are trajectory-dependent: per-case normalization depends on how many timesteps fall into each case, which varies across models and runs. This makes cross-model error comparisons secondary measures rather than standalone quality indicators, limiting diagnostic precision. - The 2D grid with symbolic DAGs is deliberately far from real-world settings. The semantic reintroduction experiment is minimal (4 hand-crafted pasta-cooking maps), and the paper's own findings show semantic information dramatically shifts behavior — so the symbolic results may not transfer predictably to applications where semantic priors are unavoidable and beneficial. - Exploitation error shows weak correlation with task difficulty even when exploitation demand is varied by design (Figure 20a), and the authors attribute this to trajectory dependence without fully resolving it. The disconnect between the designed exploitation demand and measured exploitation error weakens the controllability claim on that axis. - Scale of experiments is modest: 3 random seeds per configuration, which the authors acknowledge may be insufficient to surface clean trends, especially for exploitation error analysis.
Recommended 2604.13151 PDF
#38 — Innovative (72)

Free Geometry: Refining 3D Reconstruction from Longer Versions of Itself (14 ▲ )

Yuhang Dai, Xingyi Yang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Free Geometry presents an elegant self-supervision mechanism grounded in a sound architectural observation: global attention monotonicity guarantees that full-view features are strictly superior to partial-view features, yielding an asymmetric and quality-guaranteed teacher without any external data. The cross-frame relational loss preserving triangle geometry in feature space goes meaningfully beyond pointwise distillation. Gains are consistent but modest on well-calibrated benchmarks, and the per-dataset hyperparameter tuning weakens the plug-and-play claim.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Feed-forward multi-view 3D reconstruction models such as Depth Anything 3 and VGGT follow a train-then-freeze paradigm: parameters are fixed at deployment and inference is purely zero-shot. This rigidity causes geometric errors on novel test scenes, especially under occlusions, specularities, and low-texture regions. Collecting 3D ground truth for scene-specific fine-tuning is prohibitively expensive, so standard supervised adaptation is infeasible. The paper asks whether a model can self-improve at test time using only the unlabeled test sequence itself. Methodology: The core insight is that a feed-forward model fed N views produces more geometrically consistent features than when fed a masked subset of M < N views, because global cross-view attention aggregates more correspondences. This ordering is exploited as a free supervision signal. Given a test sequence, the full N-view input is passed through a frozen teacher backbone to produce features F_full. A student branch receives only the M unmasked frames (e.g., even-indexed frames) through the same backbone augmented with lightweight LoRA adapters (rank 32, < 0.2% of parameters), producing F_partial. Two complementary losses optimize only the LoRA weights and camera tokens. (1) Intra-frame consistency loss: Huber + cosine similarity between matched token pairs at the same spatial position on shared unmasked frames, enforcing both magnitude and directional alignment. (2) Cross-frame relational loss: for triplets (p, k_j, q) drawn from unmasked and masked frames, the student must preserve the pairwise KL-divergence distributions and the triangle-angle geometry (Φ) in feature space derived from the teacher. Anchor tokens k_j are selected by extreme cosine similarity (top-2 most similar + top-2 least similar) from masked frames, capturing both correspondences and contrasts. Adaptation runs for 3-5 epochs with batch size 4 in FP16, completing in under 2 minutes per dataset on a single RTX Pro 6000 GPU. The method is plug-and-play across architectures because supervision targets the multi-view transformer backbone (where cross-view reasoning occurs) rather than the per-view decoder outputs. Key Results: Evaluated on ETH3D, ScanNet++, 7-Scenes, and HiRoom against frozen Depth Anything 3 (Giant) and VGGT baselines. At 4 views, Free Geometry improves VGGT on ETH3D from AUC@3=0.157 to 0.178 (+13.4%) and F1 from 0.102 to 0.110; DA3 on ETH3D improves from AUC@3=0.286 to 0.305 (+6.6%). On HiRoom at 4 views, VGGT F1 improves from 0.276 to 0.307 (+11.2%). Averaged across all 4 datasets, improvements are +3.73% in pose accuracy and +2.88% in point map F1. Table 3 shows relative gains for VGGT at 4 views: +5.33% AUC@3, +4.73% AUC@30, +4.51% F1, -8.15% Chamfer distance. The gains are present at all tested view counts (4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 100), even though adaptation is trained only with an 8→4 view masking scheme, demonstrating cross-view-count generalization. Benefits diminish as view count grows (diminishing returns), consistent with the hypothesis that adaptation corrects model priors that matter most under sparse observations. Feature-space analysis confirms that adapted partial-branch features are measurably closer to full-observation features: VGGT MSE decreases from 0.9039 to 0.8384 and cosine similarity increases from 0.8581 to 0.8684. Ablations confirm both loss components are necessary; removing the relational loss drops F1 from 0.2475 to 0.2190 on ETH3D, and removing consistency loss degrades pose accuracy. Strengths: - The self-supervision signal is architecturally grounded: the superiority of full-view over partial-view features follows from global attention monotonicity, making this a strictly asymmetric and quality-guaranteed teacher, unlike symmetric consistency methods such as Test3R that risk regression to the mean. - Operating at encoder feature level rather than decoder output is well-motivated (decoders are per-view and do not share information), saves memory and compute, and yields stable optimization via LoRA with < 0.2% trainable parameters. - The cross-frame relational loss is principled: by preserving triangle geometry (angles between token triplets in feature space) and pairwise KL distributions, it transfers structural information from masked frames not directly seen by the student, going beyond simple pointwise distillation. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Adaptation is performed dataset-wise (one shared LoRA per dataset) rather than per-scene, which may leave scene-specific errors uncorrected; dataset-level adaptation could average out idiosyncratic geometric challenges across diverse scenes within a dataset. - Hyperparameters (learning rate, number of samples per scene, training epochs) are tuned separately for each benchmark dataset, limiting the claim of a truly plug-and-play zero-configuration system and raising questions about generalization to new datasets without tuning. - Improvements on ScanNet++ and 7-Scenes are modest (often < 1% relative) because the frozen baselines already perform strongly on these datasets, making it unclear whether Free Geometry provides meaningful gains in regimes where the pretrained model is already well-calibrated. - The paper does not compare against Test3R (the closest prior TTA method for 3D reconstruction) with quantitative numbers, relying instead on a conceptual argument about asymmetric vs. symmetric consistency.
Recommended 2604.14048 PDF
#39 — Innovative (72)

Environmental Footprint of GenAI Research: Insights from the Moshi Foundation Model

Marta López-Rauhut, Loic Landrieu, Mathieu Aubry, Anne-Laure Ligozat · arxiv.org · genai manufacturing - new results
Why this rank: This paper provides the first fine-grained environmental accounting of an entire foundation model research project with genuine internal access to 3,534 training runs, revealing that final training accounts for only 3.7% of total compute — a 26× ratio that fundamentally challenges how the field reports AI environmental costs. The multi-indicator LCA approach (energy, carbon, water, mineral depletion) and the breakdown by research phase produce actionable insights absent from prior carbon-only analyses. However, its single-project scope and the unusual nature of the Moshi project (novel task, new lab) limit generalizability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: The environmental cost of generative AI is widely underreported. Most published assessments focus exclusively on the carbon footprint of a model's final training run, implicitly treating it as the dominant cost. The full research and development process — exploratory experiments, failed runs, debugging, ablation studies, and iterative hyperparameter search — is largely terra incognita. This gap matters because it leads practitioners and policymakers to dramatically underestimate the true environmental burden of creating novel AI systems. The paper addresses this gap by conducting the first fine-grained environmental accounting of an entire MLLM research project, from first experiment to deployed model. Methodology: The study analyzes Moshi, a 7B-parameter speech-text foundation model for real-time dialogue developed by Kyutai over nine months. Kyutai provided detailed logs of 3,534 individual training runs covering four model components: the LLM backbone (Helium), a neural audio tokenizer (Mimi), a text-to-speech data generator, and the main multimodal transformer. Compute is measured in GPU-hours on a homogeneous NVIDIA DGX H100 cluster (Scaleway). Runs are categorized by module, training phase (experimentation, pre-training, post-training, fine-tuning), and research phase (debugging, failed, design/tuning, ablation, final). Environmental impact is assessed via full life cycle assessment (LCA) across four indicators: primary energy (MJ), global warming potential (kgCO2eq), water consumption (L), and abiotic resource depletion (kgSbeq), covering both operational impacts (electricity use) and embodied impacts (hardware manufacturing and transport), using the Boavizta and ADEME toolchains. Key Results: Total research compute reached 372 GPU-years (3M GPU-hours), against only 14 GPU-years for the final training runs — a 26× ratio. The final deployed model accounts for just 3.7% of total compute; design and hyperparameter tuning consumed 75%, failed runs 11%, and ablations 8%. The ratio of total-to-final compute varies strongly with component novelty: 6.5× for the standard LLM backbone versus roughly 40× for the novel main model. A pronounced 90/10 concentration holds: 13% of runs account for 89% of total compute, while 42% of all runs (short debugging and fine-tuning jobs) contribute negligibly. Total environmental impact translated to 5 GWh of energy, 319 tonnes CO2eq (equivalent to 39 French residents per year or 132 Paris–San Francisco round trips), 19 megaliters of water, and 8 kg antimony-equivalent mineral depletion. For GWP and primary energy, GPU operational consumption dominates; for abiotic depletion, power supply units (six per node) are the largest contributor. Location matters: computing in Poland would produce ~15× more CO2eq than in Sweden, but Sweden's nuclear/hydro mix consumes ~4× more water. Strengths: - First study with genuine internal access to complete training logs of an industry-scale MLLM project, enabling breakdowns impossible from external observation alone. - Broad LCA scope covering four impact indicators and both operational and embodied impacts, going well beyond the carbon-only analyses common in the literature. - Actionable, quantified guidelines directly tied to observed cost drivers (evaluation frequency, ablation design on smaller proxies, debugging on low-power infrastructure, datacenter location trade-offs). Weaknesses / Limitations: - Single-project case study: Moshi was built from scratch at a newly founded lab tackling a genuinely novel speech-to-speech task, which likely inflates the research-to-final-run ratio relative to incremental LLM work; generalizability to other projects is uncertain. - Several impact categories are partially omitted or estimated with broad assumptions: embodied water consumption of hardware manufacturing is excluded, end-of-life impacts are omitted entirely, and data acquisition/storage costs are out of scope — all of which likely underestimate total impact. - GPU energy is estimated from rated TDP with a fixed 95% utilization factor rather than measured power draw, and the methodology assumes 100% GPU allocation to Moshi (ignoring shared-cluster inefficiencies beyond a cluster overhead factor), potentially introducing systematic bias.

#40 — Innovative (72)

Tracing the Roots: A Multi-Agent Framework for Uncovering Data Lineage in Post-Training LLMs (14 ▲ )

Yu Li, Xiaoran Shang, Qizhi Pei, Yun Zhu, Xin Gao, Honglin Lin, Zhanping Zhong, Zhuoshi Pan, Zheng Liu, Xiaoyang Wang, Conghui He, Dahua Lin, Feng Zhao, Lijun Wu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 14 Apr 2026
Why this rank: This paper defines a genuinely new problem — reconstructing inter-dataset evolutionary dependencies at ecosystem scale — and delivers the first systematic framework for it. The concrete findings on contamination propagation chains and structural redundancy fill a consequential gap, and provenance-based sampling outperforming datasets 2x larger is a striking practical result. However, the framework is fundamentally bounded by documentary transparency and relies on LLM extraction with unquantified hallucination rates, limiting its reliability as a fully automated tool.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Post-training datasets for LLMs are rarely constructed from scratch — they emerge through recursive derivation processes involving semantic evolution, knowledge distillation, and structured fusion of existing corpora. Despite this, datasets are treated as isolated artifacts with no systematic tracking of their provenance. This opacity creates two concrete risks: (1) structural redundancy, where downstream datasets unknowingly inherit overlapping upstream sources, eroding effective diversity despite apparent scale growth; and (2) benchmark contamination propagation, where test samples embedded in upstream datasets cascade silently into downstream derivatives, inflating evaluation metrics. The community lacked a formal framework for reconstructing and analyzing these dependency structures at ecosystem scale. Methodology: The paper introduces data lineage as a directed graph G = (V, E), where nodes are datasets and edges represent inheritance relationships. To reconstruct this graph automatically, the authors propose a multi-agent collaborative framework built on LangChain, using GPT-5.1 and Gemini-2.5-Flash as underlying models. The pipeline operates in four sequential steps per dataset: (1) Candidate Validation — deduplication, HuggingFace API availability check, and temporal filtering (post-2020 only); (2) Multi-source Information Retrieval — a sourcing agent extracts links to GitHub repos, blogs, and arXiv papers from HuggingFace READMEs, then specialized extracting agents fetch and noise-prune the content; (3) Semantic Source Inference — parallel tracing agents analyze the unified context and emit structured JSON records ⟨Source, Relationship, Confidence, Evidence⟩, explicitly excluding evaluation benchmarks and baselines from lineage edges; (4) Aggregation, Resolution, and Recursive Expansion — an aggregation agent canonicalizes dataset aliases to HuggingFace org/name IDs, prunes anachronistic and low-confidence edges, synthesizes semantic profiles, and enqueues newly discovered upstream sources for DFS traversal. Starting from 83 high-impact seed datasets across math, code, general, and science domains, the system reconstructs the full ecosystem graph. Contamination analysis uses exact (instruction, input) matching against benchmark samples. For dataset construction, provenance-based sampling anchors instruction selection at root nodes (in-degree = 0), ranked by topological influence (out-degree), followed by two-stage MinHash deduplication, yielding 570K unique instructions. Key Results: The framework reconstructed a graph of 430 distinct datasets connected by 971 inheritance edges. Structural analysis reveals domain-specific evolutionary patterns: general-domain datasets grow horizontally (average depth 1.05, 68.42% leaf ratio), while math evolves vertically through deep recursive reuse (average depth 2.92; hendrycks_math has out-degree 19, gsm8k 14). Code acts as a functional bridge, drawing 38.70% from general and 17.80% from math sources. Science is severely underdeveloped with only 44 nodes and 16.22% self-sourcing. Redundancy analysis found that 17 of 83 examined datasets exceed 1% structural redundancy, with open-instruct-v1 reaching 46.48% due to self-superset inclusion. Contamination was detected in 19 datasets across five benchmarks: Skywork-OR1-RL-Data contains 96.80% of Omni-Math; Caco-1.3M inherits 37.95% contamination transitively without directly including the benchmark. The provenance-based sampling approach achieves a Vendi Score of 452.44 and Centroid Distance of 0.6385 at 570K samples, outperforming all baselines including datasets up to 1.2M samples (e.g., OpenThoughts3 at 133.26 Vendi, MegaScience at 373.78). Strengths: - Fills a genuine and consequential gap: the first systematic framework for reconstructing inter-dataset evolutionary dependencies at ecosystem scale, with quantified evidence of structural harms (redundancy and contamination) that previously went undetected. - The practical payoff is concrete: provenance-based sampling at root nodes outperforms datasets 2× larger on both Vendi Score and Centroid Distance, demonstrating that lineage structure is a more efficient diversity signal than raw volume. - Contamination tracing via inheritance paths is qualitatively superior to sample-level scanning — it exposes latent propagation chains (e.g., Omni-Math → DeepScaleR → Caco-1.3M) without requiring exhaustive pairwise comparison across millions of samples. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The framework is fundamentally bounded by documentary transparency: if dataset creators omit, vague, or intentionally conceal upstream sources in READMEs or papers, the system cannot recover those edges. Undisclosed dependencies — a common practice for competitive reasons — are structurally invisible. - Reliance on LLMs for provenance extraction introduces hallucination risk; low-confidence extractions require human review, which limits full automation at scale and introduces a manual bottleneck that the paper does not quantify in terms of correction rate or labor. - Diversity evaluation uses embedding-based metrics (Vendi Score, Centroid Distance) only, without downstream task evaluation to validate that higher diversity scores translate to better or more generalizable fine-tuned models.

#41 — Innovative (72)

RetroInfer: A Vector Storage Engine for Scalable Long-Context LLM Inference

vldb.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: RetroInfer's tripartite zone design is a principled architectural contribution that cleanly decouples accuracy from efficiency by handling varying sparsity through estimation rather than over-retrieval. The Jensen's inequality bound provides a mathematically grounded approximation rather than a heuristic, and segmented clustering exploiting RoPE-induced spatial locality is both theoretically justified and practically validated with 80% build-time reduction. The comprehensive evaluation spanning 1M-token contexts and reasoning workloads is thorough. However, the core techniques — vector indexing, buffer pool management, cluster-based retrieval — are well-established in database systems, making this more a creative synthesis than a fundamental advance.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Long-context LLM inference is severely bottlenecked by the KV cache, which grows linearly with sequence length and requires iterating over all cached key-value vectors per decoding step. This strains both GPU memory capacity and memory bandwidth. A single 1M-token request on Llama3-8B requires 125GB of KV cache—far beyond a single GPU's 80GB. Offloading the KV cache to CPU memory over PCIe is a natural solution, but GPU HBM bandwidth is ~60× higher than PCIe bandwidth, so the sparsity ratio must exceed 98% to hide transfer latency without stalling the GPU. Existing sparse attention systems achieve only 87.5–90% sparsity and still sacrifice either accuracy (fixed-position heuristics, coarse chunk-based retrieval) or throughput (PCIe bottlenecks, slow CPU compute). Prior work applying vector indexes to KV cache retrieval assumed offline or prefix-only construction, limiting applicability to online inference. The fundamental challenge is that attention sparsity varies substantially across model layers, attention heads, decoding steps, and input tasks, making a single retrieval budget inadequate. Methodology: RetroInfer is a vector storage engine built around two co-designed components. The first is the wave index (Attention-aWare VEctor index), a cluster-based vector index built using spherical k-means over key vectors. It introduces three novel techniques. (1) Tripartite attention approximation partitions tokens into three zones: a steady zone (sink tokens at sequence start/end that are consistently important and always included without indexing), a retrieval zone (clusters selected by centroid-query inner product similarity and fully loaded for exact attention), and an estimation zone (remaining clusters approximated cheaply using precomputed centroid and summed value vectors). This design means accuracy degrades gracefully with fewer retrieved tokens, and the estimation zone absorbs sparsity variation without additional PCIe traffic. (2) Accuracy-bounded attention estimation uses Jensen's inequality to prove that the centroid inner product is a lower bound for the mean of per-token inner products within a cluster, providing a mathematically grounded approximation. Summed value vectors per cluster are stored in the meta index on GPU, enabling O(m−r) estimation cost for non-retrieved clusters. (3) Segmented clustering performs k-means independently within fixed 8K-token segments rather than globally, exploiting the observed coarse-grained spatial locality of key vectors (attributed to RoPE positional encoding). This reduces clustering time complexity by a factor equal to the number of segments, yielding an 80% speedup in index build time with less than 1% drop in recall@100 compared to global k-means. New tokens during decoding are appended to the steady zone and clustered once per 1K-token segment, amortizing update cost to 0.2% latency overhead. The second component is the wave buffer, a GPU-CPU buffer manager inspired by classical database buffer pool design. It maintains a GPU-side block cache (5% of total KV vectors) exploiting the observed temporal locality of important tokens (cache hit ratios of 0.79–0.94 across tasks). A cluster mapping table translates logical cluster IDs to physical block addresses in GPU or CPU memory. Cache access is synchronous with attention computation, while cache replacement is handled asynchronously by a CPU thread pool, decoupling these from the critical path. Custom CUDA kernels reduce GPU-CPU copy latency from 1.3ms to 100μs. The FlashAttention kernel is modified to support cluster-size-weighted attention and to merge outputs from all three zones. Key Results: RetroInfer is evaluated on four LLMs (Llama3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-7B, Llama3-8B-1048K, Qwen2.5-72B) and two reasoning models (DeepSeek-R1 distillations) against full attention (FlashInfer), vLLM, and four sparse attention baselines (Quest, MagicPIG, InfiniGen, PQCache) on RULER (13 tasks, 8K–128K context), NIAH (up to 1M tokens), AIME24, and GPQA. RetroInfer is the only sparse system that matches full attention accuracy, dropping by only 0.66–1.97% on RULER at 128K context versus full attention, while outperforming baselines by 1.40–46.47% at the same 1.8% retrieval budget. It achieves 100% needle-in-haystack accuracy at up to 1M tokens. Decoding throughput: 4.1–4.4× over full attention at 30K–120K context, and 10.5–12.2× over MagicPIG/PQCache at 1M tokens (where full attention and Quest OOM). End-to-end throughput over vLLM is 2.2–3.3×. On reasoning tasks, RetroInfer matches or slightly exceeds full attention (AIME24: 76.70 vs 73.30 for DeepSeek-Llama), while baselines degrade severely (Quest: 46.70, PQCache: 30.00). Prefilling overhead is only 6% and 3% above full attention at 120K and 1M contexts respectively. Ablations confirm that GPU caching and asynchronous updates are both essential for throughput scaling, and that the 8K segment size balances index quality and construction cost optimally. Strengths: - The tripartite zone design is a principled architectural contribution: it decouples the accuracy-efficiency tradeoff by handling varying sparsity via estimation rather than over-retrieval, and provides a clean separation of concerns between the index (accuracy) and the buffer manager (hardware efficiency). - Accuracy-bounded estimation rests on a provable guarantee (Jensen's inequality lower bound), distinguishing it from heuristic approximations in prior systems; empirically the bound is tight as shown in Figure 7. - Segmented clustering is both theoretically justified (RoPE-induced spatial locality) and practically validated: 80% build-time reduction with <1% recall drop enables truly online index construction, which prior vector-index-based KV cache systems could not achieve. - Comprehensive evaluation spanning four model families, context lengths up to 1M tokens, multi-GPU settings, reasoning workloads, and compatibility with sparse prefilling methods (XAttention, MInference) demonstrates broad applicability and composability. Weaknesses / Limitations: - RetroInfer optimizes only the decoding phase; prefilling still uses full attention and remains expensive at very long contexts (800–1600s at 1M tokens without sparse prefilling assistance), making the system dependent on complementary prefill acceleration techniques for truly practical million-token serving. - Performance at low batch sizes or light loads is inferior to GPU-only baselines (Quest, full attention) due to unavoidable PCIe transfer overhead; the system's advantage materializes primarily under high-throughput batch serving, which may not match all deployment patterns (e.g., interactive single-user inference). - The evaluation hardware is a single server with PCIe 4.0 A100 GPUs; the system's behavior on consumer GPUs with narrower PCIe bandwidth, on NVLink multi-GPU topologies, or on next-generation hardware with higher interconnect bandwidth is not characterized, leaving open questions about generalizability of the performance claims.

#42 — Innovative (72)

Multi-User Large Language Model Agents (13 ▲ )

Shu Yang, Shenzhe Zhu, Hao Zhu, José Ramón Enríquez, Di Wang, Alex Pentland, Michiel A. Bakker, Jiaxin Pei · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 13 Apr 2026
Why this rank: This paper scores 72 for introducing a genuinely novel problem formulation—multi-principal LLM agents—that connects to established economic theory and addresses a real gap as LLMs move into multi-user deployments. The Muses-Bench benchmark is well-constructed with diverse adversarial conditions, and the evaluation across 19 frontier models reveals consistent, cross-model failure patterns (privacy erosion, refusal-leak paradox, coordination breakdowns) that are both surprising and practically important. However, the work is purely diagnostic with no proposed solution, and the reliance on simulated rather than real users limits confidence in the practical severity of the identified failure modes.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLMs and LLM-based agents are designed around a single-principal paradigm—one user, one utility function—yet real deployments increasingly require serving multiple simultaneous users with distinct roles, authority levels, private contexts, and conflicting objectives. Current chat templates (SFT) collapse multi-user inputs into a single `user` role, and RLHF reward models learn a single scalar preference signal, making it structurally impossible for today's models to natively distinguish principals, enforce information boundaries, or arbitrate cross-user conflicts. This paper presents the first systematic study of multi-user LLM agents under what the authors term the Multiple Principal–Agent Scenario. Methodology: The paper formalizes multi-user interaction as a multi-objective decision problem where the agent maximizes a weighted social utility ∑ wᵢUᵢ(a; Cᵢ, pᵢ) subject to access-control constraints derived from per-user private contexts Cᵢ and authority personas pᵢ. Building on this formulation, the authors introduce a unified Multi-User Agent Interaction Protocol with separate private sessions per user and a conditionally shared context, then construct Muses-Bench—a stress-test suite with three scenarios: (1) Multi-User Instruction Following (1,298 execution + 304 selection scenarios, 2–10 users), where the agent must resolve conflicting directives using an explicit authority hierarchy (CEO > Director > Manager > Senior IC > Junior IC > Intern), evaluated by Selection F1 and Execution Accuracy; (2) Cross-User Access Control (216 scenarios spanning direct requests, social engineering, and technical obfuscation attacks), evaluated by Privacy Score and Utility Score; and (3) Multi-User Meeting Coordination (216 scenarios in full- vs. partial-disclosure settings), evaluated by Success Rate and Turns Taken. A diverse pool of 19 frontier models is evaluated—including GPT-5.1, GPT-5.2, Claude-Sonnet-4.5, Gemini-3-Pro, Grok-3-Mini, DeepSeek-R1, and Llama-3-8B—using three serialization formats (Says, Colon, XML) since no model natively supports multi-user message schemas. Key Results: Gemini-3-Pro achieves the best overall average (85.6), followed by Claude-Sonnet-4.5 (82.6) and Gemini-3-Flash (82.0). Three systematic failure modes emerge across all models. First, inter-user conflict sharply degrades instruction execution: Gemini-3-Pro drops from 0.96 accuracy under aligned instructions to 0.92 under conflict, while Claude-Haiku-4.5 drops from 0.86 to 0.62 and GPT-OSS-120B from 0.64 to 0.50—indicating models rely on surface cues rather than internalized authority hierarchies. Second, privacy scores erode monotonically across interaction rounds in nearly all models; Claude-3.5-Haiku falls from >0.95 in round 1 to <0.75 after four rounds, with the steepest decline in rounds 1–6. A qualitative case study shows Claude-3.5-Haiku leaking a sensitive session ID token (ADMIN-MASTER-5773) while simultaneously claiming to refuse vault access—a refusal-leak paradox induced by multi-turn social engineering. Third, multi-user coordination success rates are low even for the best models, with GPT-OSS-120B reaching only 77.3% and most proprietary models landing between 50–66%; coordination success scales negatively with group size N and turns scale linearly with N. Models with higher success rates converge within <4 turns, while weaker models require 1–2 extra rounds or exhibit premature commitment (Llama-3-70B hallucinates consensus while ignoring explicit hard constraints, achieving 0.0 success in partial-disclosure cases despite fewer turns). Strengths: - The formalization of multi-user LLM interaction as a multi-principal decision problem is rigorous and connects to established economic theory (principal-agent, social welfare functions), providing a principled foundation lacking in prior empirical work. - The benchmark is carefully constructed with diverse, realistic user personas (8 roles, 6 demographic attributes, 3 security postures, authority scores 1–10) and covers both benign and adversarial conditions (fake authorization, social pressure, role-playing attacks), enabling thorough characterization of failure modes. - Experimental coverage of 19 models spanning proprietary and open-weight families reveals consistent, cross-model failure patterns rather than model-specific artifacts, strengthening the generality of the conclusions. Weaknesses / Limitations: - All user interactions are simulated via LLM-generated personas and scripted scenarios; no human participants are involved, so it is unclear whether the failure modes observed (particularly privacy erosion dynamics and coordination breakdowns) reflect the most dangerous or practically common failure patterns in real deployments. - The benchmark evaluates models only via serialized single-user-role prompts, because no frontier model natively supports multi-user message schemas. This means the results measure the best achievable performance under a workaround rather than a purpose-built interface, and leaves open the question of how much a native multi-user protocol would improve performance. - The coordination task (meeting scheduling) is narrow and does not test richer organizational workflows—resource allocation, collaborative document editing, or asynchronous task delegation—limiting generalizability of the efficiency bottleneck findings to the broader class of multi-user coordination problems.
Recommended 2604.08567 PDF
#43 — Innovative (72)

Target Policy Optimization (19 ▲ )

Jean Kaddour · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026
Why this rank: TPO scores 72 for providing a theoretically clean and practically elegant reformulation of policy optimization for RLVR. The separation of target construction (closed-form KL-regularized distribution) from policy fitting (simple cross-entropy minimization) is a meaningful conceptual contribution, and the self-extinguishing gradient property is a genuinely nice theoretical result. The method's simplicity — no critic, no clipping, no reference model, ~5 lines of code — combined with strong robustness to hyperparameters (stable across 16× temperature and epoch ranges where GRPO is non-monotonic) represents real practical value. However, the core idea of reweight-then-fit builds directly on REPS/MPO, and the LLM evaluation is limited to 1.5-1.7B models on only three tasks.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard policy-gradient methods for reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) entangle two distinct questions: (1) which completions should gain probability mass given scored candidates, and (2) how should optimizer mechanics realize that redistribution. This coupling makes updates fragile under sparse reward, where gradient magnitudes depend heavily on learning rate, clipping thresholds, and KL penalties rather than the underlying signal. Existing reweight-then-fit approaches (REPS, MPO) address this but require learned Q-functions and constrained optimization over full action spaces, making them impractical for group-based LLM training. Methodology: Target Policy Optimization (TPO) separates the two questions by first constructing a closed-form target distribution over the K sampled candidates and then fitting the policy to it by cross-entropy. Given behavior policy probabilities p_old_i and within-group z-scored rewards u_i, TPO defines the target q_i ∝ p_old_i · exp(u_i/η), which is provably the unique solution to a KL-regularized improvement problem on the candidate simplex. The policy is updated by minimizing cross-entropy L = -Σ q_i log p_i^θ with q treated as a stop-gradient constant. The gradient ∂L/∂ℓ_i = p_i^θ − q_i vanishes exactly when the policy matches the target, providing a natural fixed point absent from all policy-gradient baselines. Score standardization (z-scoring within group) stabilizes the exponential tilt across tasks and largely removes the need to tune temperature η. The method requires no critic, no frozen reference model, no clipping, and no importance ratios. It applies identically at the token level (sampling K next-token candidates at each prefix) or sequence level (K full rollouts per prompt), and supports multi-epoch reuse because the target q stays fixed across epochs within a batch. Key Results: Across tabular bandits, MNIST contextual bandits, dense- and sparse-reward transformer tasks, and billion-parameter LLM RLVR, TPO matches or outperforms PG, PPO, GRPO, and DG. On a single-context 100-armed bandit, TPO and DG converge fastest and maintain the lowest gradient misalignment to the oracle direction. On the MNIST contextual bandit (single-sample), TPO reaches 5% error at step 1,600 vs. 2,200 for DG, and achieves 2.9% final error vs. 5.9% for GRPO. For token-level dense reward (token reversal, V=16), TPO_token reaches 1% error at step 102 vs. 148 for GRPO_token, 259 for PPO, and 393 for DG. Under sequential reward across all 4 task variants, only TPO and DG converge; GRPO and PPO fail entirely. Under terminal (fully sparse) reward with sequence length H=10, TPO achieves 7.4% exact-match error (prompt-matched) vs. 50.4% for GRPO, while DG and PPO fail to learn. For LLM RLVR on Qwen3-1.7B graph coloring, GRPO stays near-zero score for 300 steps while TPO reaches ~0.96; on Knights & Knaves both models show similar gaps. On GSM8K both methods converge to ~85-87% accuracy, with TPO learning ~10 steps faster. Ablations confirm that both the p_old anchor and the target-matching structure (vs. scalar-weighted Group PG) are necessary: removing either degrades performance substantially, with Group PG raising MNIST final error from 2.9% to 7.2%. TPO is also robust to epoch count (stable from 1 to 16 epochs), while GRPO is strongly non-monotonic across the same sweep. Strengths: - Theoretically clean: the target distribution has a closed-form KL-regularized derivation, the gradient self-extinguishes at convergence (Proposition 1), and the analytical β_TPO(p_n) coefficient explains why TPO better allocates update budget to hard contexts vs. easy ones compared to DG and GRPO. - Practically simple: the entire method is ~5 lines of JAX/PyTorch, requires no critic, no frozen reference model, no clipping, and no importance-ratio corrections, yet outperforms methods with significantly more machinery under sparse reward. - Robustness advantages are empirically well-documented: TPO is stable across a 16× temperature range and across epoch counts from 1 to 16, whereas GRPO is strongly non-monotonic in both dimensions, making TPO far easier to tune in practice. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Scale of LLM evaluation is limited to 1.5–1.7B parameter models on three tasks (GSM8K, graph coloring, Knights & Knaves); whether TPO's relative advantage persists on 7B+ models and harder mathematical benchmarks (MATH, AIME) remains untested. - Score standardization can amplify noise in low-variance groups (e.g., a group where one candidate scores 0.001 and the rest score 0 produces a very sharp target after z-scoring), a difficulty-bias issue shared with GRPO that is acknowledged but not resolved in this work.
Recommended 2604.06159 PDF
#44 — Innovative (72)

STTID: High-Performance Sparse Tensor-Train Interpolative Decomposition

arcb.csc.ncsu.edu · karypis - new results
Why this rank: STTID fills a genuine and well-motivated algorithmic gap: no prior method could robustly decompose large-scale sparse tensors in TT format while preserving sparsity end-to-end. The key insight of replacing SVD with PRRLU to inherit input sparsity by construction is elegant, and the layered optimization stack (data separation, hash-table elimination, GPU offload, kernel fusion) is rigorously ablated rather than presented as a monolithic speedup. The 607-630x speedup over dense TT-ID and the fact that STTID is the only method completing all benchmarks demonstrate real practical impact. However, the approach is a creative recombination of known primitives (cross decomposition, PRRLU, COO format) rather than a fundamentally new decomposition paradigm.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Tensor-Train (TT) decomposition is a powerful tool for combating the curse of dimensionality in high-dimensional data, but existing TT algorithms were designed for dense tensors and fail on sparse inputs in two distinct ways: TT-SVD destroys sparsity during its sequential SVD process (producing dense TT-cores), while TT-cross is unstable on sparse tensors because its back-and-forth sweep evaluates only tensor slices that may contain too few nonzeros to determine useful interpolation skeletons. No prior method could robustly decompose large-scale sparse tensors while preserving sparsity in the output TT-cores, leaving significant computational and memory efficiency on the table for downstream TT operations in applications such as quantum simulation, knowledge graph reasoning, and tensor completion. Methodology: STTID introduces a new Tensor-Train Interpolative Decomposition (TT-ID) algorithm that replaces SVD with partial rank-revealing LU (PRRLU) decomposition. In a single left-to-right sweep, each mode is matricized and a rank-r cross decomposition is extracted via PRRLU with complete pivoting, yielding column/row skeletons that are literal subtensors of the input — inheriting its sparsity by construction. The algorithm operates natively on tensors in COO (coordinate) format, which uniquely supports dimension-agnostic pivoting in O(nnz) and incremental nonzero insertion. Two algorithmic optimizations address the dominant PRRLU bottleneck: (1) selective data separation, which restricts each PRRLU iteration to the active submatrix rather than scanning all nonzeros, reducing Phase 3 complexity from O(u.nnz × v.nnz × M.nnz) to O(u.nnz × v.nnz × Mt.nnz); and (2) hash table–based Gaussian elimination, which replaces linear search during outer-product updates with O(1) expected-time lookup, and uses hash maps for permutation vectors to avoid storing dense pivot arrays of size n=500^4 (which would require 465 GB). A hybrid CPU-GPU implementation offloads PRRLU to NVIDIA GPUs via CUDA, using cuBLAS for pivot identification, asynchronous CPU-GPU transfers for permutation bookkeeping, NVIDIA cuCollections (cuco::static_map) for on-device hash tables, and kernel fusion with shared memory to combine pivoting and Gaussian elimination phases. Key Results: Against four baselines (TT-SVD, TT-SVD-lasso, TT-cross-qr, TT-cross-lu) on six synthetic power-law sparse tensors (Rnd1–Rnd6) and ten real-world tensors from knowledge graphs and the FROSTT collection: STTID is the only method that completes decomposition on all tested tensors — TT-cross methods fail or produce high errors on tensors larger than Rnd2, and TT-SVD/lasso run out of memory on Rnd5 and Rnd6. STTID produces the sparsest TT-cores among all methods at comparable reconstruction error, and can converge to machine-precision exact decomposition (e.g., Rnd1: Er = 1.7×10⁻¹⁶). On sparse tensors STTID CPU achieves 607–630× speedup over dense TT-ID. The GPU implementation delivers 77.9×–169.9× speedup over CPU STTID on an NVIDIA A5000 and 194.3×–550.3× on an H100 for tensors with over one million nonzeros (Uber, NIPS, CC-comm, CC-geo). In the ablation, data separation alone reduces runtime by >50%; hash tables add 9.4×–50× further; GPU parallelism adds 29.4×–70.1× on top; kernel fusion contributes ~10% additional gain. Downstream sparse TT reconstruction using sparse TCI-form cores achieves 12.1×–29.8× speedup over dense reconstruction. Strengths: - Fills a genuine algorithmic gap: first method to robustly decompose large-scale, fully-observed sparse tensors while provably preserving sparsity end-to-end in TT-cores, enabling downstream sparse computation. - Careful choice of PRRLU over RRQR is well-justified: single-pass row and column pivots, lower memory (no dense orthogonal factors), and demonstrated numerical stability in quantum simulation contexts. - The optimization stack is principled and incrementally validated — each level (data separation, hash tables, GPU offload, kernel fusion) is benchmarked independently with clear complexity reductions rather than only presented as a combined result. Weaknesses / Limitations: - STTID degrades below dense TT-ID when tensor density is high (CC-comm case), with no adaptive switching criterion to decide when sparse computation is beneficial — the density threshold is identified empirically, not characterized theoretically. - Fill-ins during PRRLU can significantly increase the active nonzero count (demonstrated for JF17K3), and no fill-reducing strategy (e.g., block-based or reordering heuristics) is yet incorporated, leaving a known performance cliff for certain sparse structures. - The GPU implementation is bounded by device memory: tensors with very large nonzero counts (e.g., Flickr with 112M nonzeros) exceed the 24–94 GB GPU memory capacity, limiting scalability to moderately sparse large tensors without a distributed or out-of-core extension.
Search Results
#45 — Innovative (72)

A Differentiable Approach to Task Graph Partitioning: A Case Study in RTL Simulation

tsung-wei-huang.github.io · karypis - new results
Why this rank: DiffPart earns 72 for its creative reformulation of task graph partitioning as a continuous differentiable optimization problem with end-to-end backpropagation against actual execution cost. The Gumbel-Softmax relaxation combined with the provably correct cycle-free sampling algorithm is a technically elegant solution to a hard combinatorial constraint. However, the evaluation against only Verilator's built-in heuristic — omitting comparisons to METIS, RL-based, or other learned partitioners — limits confidence in where this approach truly stands.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Task graph partitioning (TGP) is critical for parallelizing EDA workloads such as RTL simulation, static timing analysis, global routing, and placement. The state-of-the-art RTL simulator Verilator partitions RTL designs into macro tasks (mTasks) using a greedy, heuristic-based algorithm (inspired by Sarkar 1989) that optimizes a proxy combinatorial objective — critical path cost with hardcoded per-instruction weights — rather than actual threading overhead. This mismatch leaves significant performance on the table: random sampling of partitions for five real designs shows Verilator's default partitioner is 1.7–6.8× worse than the best attainable partition. Additionally, Verilator requires the user to manually specify thread counts and can fail outright when its hard-coded constraints are violated. Methodology: DiffPart reformulates task graph partitioning as a continuous, differentiable optimization problem solved end-to-end via backpropagation against real execution cost. The framework has three stages. (1) Preprocessing: a two-layer Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) with batch normalization produces node embeddings from a six-feature vector per node (computational cost, forward/reverse critical path delay, fanout, fanin, normalized depth). (2) Core optimization: a two-layer MLP Scorer evaluates all candidate parent-child assignments; Gumbel-Softmax with annealed temperature τ (50.0 → 0.8) relaxes the discrete argmax into differentiable probabilities. A differentiable loss combines expected cluster-count (synchronization proxy) and expected cluster-cost variance (load-balance proxy), both analytically normalized to [0,1]. The loss weights λ_sync adapt to thread count T via a tanh schedule. Gradients are backpropagated through the GNN and Scorer for 400 steps with AdamW (lr=0.01). (3) Cycle-free Sampling: every 10 steps, a topological-order sampling procedure greedily assigns each node to its highest-probability candidate cluster that does not introduce a cycle in the evolving cluster DAG, guaranteeing a valid acyclic partition. The best sampled partition across all checkpoints is retained. Implementation uses JAX with vectorized segmented softmax for efficiency. Key Results: Evaluated on nine RISC-V benchmark designs (functional units, accelerators, processors, SoCs) at 16 threads, DiffPart achieves 1.22–55.25× faster simulation runtime over Verilator, with a 55.25× speedup on SHA256 and 24.49× on RISCVMini. DiffPart also handles FMUL, where Verilator fails entirely due to its hard-coded partitioning constraints. At larger simulation scales (100M cycles), speedup increases further — Conv2D at T=8 improves from 4.76× at 1M cycles to 5.32× at 100M cycles, reflecting better amortization of threading overhead. In a robustness study using Taskflow (dynamic scheduler) instead of Verilator's static scheduler as the backend, DiffPart still achieves average speedups of 2.35× (RocketChip) and 1.6× (Conv2D), demonstrating scheduler-independence. DiffPart partitioning overhead ranges from ~7s (SHA256) to ~78s (RocketChip), versus Verilator's near-instant greedy approach; this is a one-time compilation cost. Strengths: - The end-to-end differentiable formulation directly optimizes actual execution cost rather than a proxy objective, addressing the fundamental limitation of prior heuristics in a principled way. - The Cycle-free Sampling algorithm is both provably correct (guaranteed acyclicity) and highly efficient (<10% of total partitioning time), solving a nontrivial structural constraint without requiring integer programming or combinatorial search. - The framework is scheduler-agnostic and generalizes across diverse design types and simulation backends (static and dynamic), with robustness empirically demonstrated. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Partitioning time is substantially higher than Verilator (up to 78s vs. <1s), which may be prohibitive for iterative design workflows where partitioning is repeated frequently; the one-time cost argument is only valid in stable compilation pipelines. - The evaluation is limited to a single baseline (Verilator's built-in heuristic) and does not compare against other learned or optimization-based partitioners (e.g., METIS, reinforcement learning approaches, or the group's own GPU-accelerated partitioners like G-kway), making it difficult to assess where DiffPart stands in the broader partitioning landscape.

#46 — Innovative (72)

Sparse Contrastive Learning for Content-Based Cold Item Recommendation

Gregor Meehan, Johan Pauwels · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: SEMCo introduces a principled departure from the dominant CF-supervision paradigm for cold-start recommendation by training content encoders entirely in the content space, cleanly resolving the known popularity bias inheritance problem. The application of α-entmax sparse probability distributions to contrastive learning is theoretically grounded via Fenchel-Young losses and yields both accuracy and fairness gains without explicit fairness regularization. The content-space knowledge distillation design avoids the cross-modal alignment problem that undermines CF-supervised distillation. However, the core technical components (contrastive learning, entmax, distillation) are individually well-established, and the scalability limitation of the full-catalog matrix multiply tempers practical impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: The item cold-start problem — recommending newly added items with no interaction history — is a longstanding challenge for collaborative filtering (CF) systems. Existing solutions train content encoders to map item features (images, text, audio) into the embedding space of a pre-trained CF model. However, a fundamental information gap exists between CF embeddings (which encode behavioral co-occurrence patterns) and content features (which encode semantic attributes). Recent work shows this mismatch causes generative cold-start models to inherit and amplify popularity bias from the supervisory CF model, leading to erratic predictions. The gap in the literature is the absence of cold-start methods that sidestep CF alignment entirely while still producing competitive ranking accuracy and fair item exposure. Methodology: SEMCo (Sampled Entmax for Cold-start) abandons CF model supervision altogether and instead trains a multimodal content encoder so that item-item cosine similarity in the learned latent space correlates with user preferences. User preference scores are computed via a factored item-item matrix B = YY^T, where Y is the L2-normalized content encoding — a design borrowed from shallow linear autoencoders. Training uses a contrastive in-batch negative sampling objective, but replaces standard sampled softmax with a sparse generalization based on the α-entmax family (Fenchel-Young losses). For α > 1, α-entmax produces sparse output probabilities: gradients are exactly zero for logits below a learnable threshold, concentrating updates on the most relevant negatives and eliminating noise from uninformative ones. The paper evaluates α = 1 (softmax), α = 1.5 (entmax), and α = 2 (sparsemax). SEMCo is extended with knowledge distillation in the content space — distilling from a larger-dimension teacher encoder to a smaller student using the same sparse loss on item-item cosine similarity matrices, either offline (pre-trained teacher) or online (teacher trained jointly via EMA of its own outputs, akin to self-distillation). The content encoder itself is a lightweight fusion network: per-modality single-layer projections, soft attention over modality hidden vectors, and a final L2-normalized linear layer. Key Results: Evaluated on four multimodal datasets spanning e-commerce (Clothing, Electronics), music (M4A-Onion), and micro-video (Microlens) against five cold-start baselines (ALDI, CLCRec, GAR, GoRec, Heater), SEMCo consistently and significantly outperforms all baselines (paired t-tests, p < 0.05) on Recall@20, NDCG@20, and item-oriented MDG@20. Gains for Sparsemax SEMCo-Online over the best baseline range from 7.0% (Clothing R@20) to 39.7% (Electronics R@20) and reach 133.7% on Electronics MDG@20. Gini diversity improvements are even more pronounced — up to 148.7% on M4A-Onion — demonstrating that purely content-based modeling eliminates the popularity concentration introduced by CF supervision. Sparse α-entmax variants (entmax and sparsemax) consistently outperform standard softmax SEMCo. Online distillation with sparsemax achieves the best ranking accuracy in all datasets; during training, typically fewer than 10% of sparsemax probability entries are non-zero versus over 70% for entmax, supporting the hypothesis that sparser supervision isolates the most informative item-item similarities. Strengths: - Principled decoupling from CF supervision cleanly resolves the known popularity bias inheritance problem, yielding large fairness gains without any explicit fairness regularization. - The α-entmax generalization of sampled softmax is theoretically grounded (Fenchel-Young losses, exact sparse gradients) and practically efficient (O(d log d) vs O(d) for softmax — only 10-20% training overhead observed). - Knowledge distillation in the content space is a natural fit: the teacher and student share the same representation type, avoiding the cross-modal alignment problem that plagues CF-supervised distillation methods. Weaknesses / Limitations: - User embeddings are computed as RY — a full matrix multiply over the entire item catalog — which raises scalability concerns for large item sets, though the authors acknowledge this and point to active-item approximations as a mitigation. - The method assumes all content modalities are present at both training and inference time; missing modality scenarios (common in real deployments) are left as future work. - Hyperparameter tuning for SEMCo-Online is considerably more complex than for the base model, requiring TPE search over joint student-teacher parameters, which may limit practical adoption.
