#47 — Innovative (72)

TensorHub: Scalable and Elastic Weight Transfer for LLM RL Training

Chenhao Ye, Huaizheng Zhang, Mingcong Han, Baoquan Zhong, Xiang Li, Qixiang Chen, Xinyi Zhang, Weidong Zhang, Kaihua Jiang, Wang Zhang, He Sun, Wencong Xiao, Andrea C. Arpaci-Dusseau, Remzi H. Arpaci-Dusseau · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: TensorHub introduces the Reference-Oriented Storage abstraction, which genuinely resolves the tension between storage-style decoupling and direct-transfer efficiency by exploiting weight immutability and replication — a clean conceptual insight. Pipeline replication turning RDMA full-duplex into bandwidth amplification is an elegant mechanism, and the comprehensive evaluation across three production topologies with deployment at ByteDance validates practical impact. However, the core techniques (RDMA, topology-aware scheduling, reference counting) are established systems building blocks, and the centralized reference server limitation tempers the scalability narrative.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern LLM reinforcement learning (RL) requires continuous, high-frequency weight transfer from trainer workers to rollout workers—potentially thousands of them—spanning heterogeneous compute environments including spot instances and multiple datacenters. Existing approaches each fail on at least one critical dimension: NCCL-based collective communication achieves high throughput but requires static communication groups and imposes global synchronization barriers that amplify stragglers; point-to-point communication (UCX) supports dynamic clusters but cannot balance load under fan-out and still requires runtime coordination; distributed storage systems (parameter servers, Ray Plasma) offer clean decoupling but incur prohibitive overhead—GPU-to-CPU copies, serialization, and redundant data movement—making them 2 orders of magnitude slower than RDMA and prone to OOM crashes beyond 300 GB. No existing approach simultaneously satisfies topology awareness, elasticity, minimal coordination, minimum data movement, and zero extra storage space. Methodology: The paper introduces Reference-Oriented Storage (ROS), a storage abstraction that exploits two structural properties of RL weights: they are immutable during inference and highly replicated via data parallelism. Instead of physically owning weight data, ROS tracks which workers hold each version via a lightweight centralized reference server; read requests are satisfied by direct RDMA reads from peer GPU memory. Two key mechanisms ensure correctness: a mutability contract (workers must call unpublish before mutating buffers, with in-flight transfers drained first) and a retention protocol (if the last copy of a retained version is about to be lost, it is offloaded to CPU memory as a fallback). TensorHub builds atop ROS with four production-grade additions: (1) topology-aware, load-balanced scheduling where the reference server maintains per-replica inflight counts and prefers intra-datacenter sources; (2) pipeline replication where partially-replicated workers immediately serve others via their idle uplink, turning N-way fan-out into a bandwidth-amplifying DAG; (3) cross-datacenter seeding where only one replica fetches over TCP with smart skipping to prevent others from stalling behind the slow link, plus optional offload seeding to hide GPU stall entirely; and (4) transactional consistency per model-parallel group, ensuring all shards within a replica see the same version snapshot even if their requests interleave at the server. Fault detection uses heartbeat timeouts; server failure is cheap to recover because the reference server holds only soft state. The system is implemented in 3.7K Python and 2.4K C++, using Ray for the reference server actor and the Mooncake transfer engine for RDMA. Key Results: In microbenchmarks on NVIDIA Hopper + Mellanox InfiniBand (400 Gbps RDMA), TensorHub achieves 22 GB/s per shard (88% of the 25 GB/s theoretical maximum) vs. 18.8 GB/s for NCCL and 18.1 GB/s for UCX; Ray Plasma is 2 orders of magnitude slower and crashes above 35 GB. Pipeline replication keeps total GPU stall time linear in rollout count (near RDMA roofline), whereas without pipelining it grows quadratically due to bandwidth contention. In a 1024-GPU standalone rollout experiment with a 1T-parameter model, TensorHub reduces total GPU stall time by up to 6.7× versus NCCL by eliminating the global weight-transfer barrier and localizing stalls to rollout workers only. For elastic rollouts on spot instances, TensorHub's runtime load balancing keeps per-GPU stall near-constant (~1.5 s) regardless of elastic GPU count, delivering 4.8× faster weight updates versus UCX which serializes elastic fetches through standalones and produces stair-shaped tail latency up to 7.2 s. In cross-datacenter experiments, TensorHub reduces GPU stall time 19× versus UCX by limiting TCP cross-DC traffic to a single seeding replica and serving all others via local RDMA. Integration requires only ~40 lines of framework code versus ~450 for NCCL and ~1200 for UCX. TensorHub is deployed in ByteDance production. Strengths: - The ROS abstraction is conceptually clean and well-motivated: exploiting immutability and replication to avoid data ownership is a genuine insight that resolves a long-standing tension between storage-style decoupling and direct-transfer efficiency. - Pipeline replication elegantly turns RDMA full-duplex into aggregate bandwidth amplification, with the progress-counter mechanism enabling partially-replicated workers to serve others without complex coordination. - The evaluation is unusually comprehensive for a systems paper: three distinct real-world workload topologies (standalone, elastic, cross-DC) are each tested against the production baseline used at ByteDance, and correctness is validated via concurrency tests, checksums, and actual training loss matching. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The centralized reference server is a single point of bottleneck and failure; the paper acknowledges it would need partitioning beyond thousands of workers, but provides no experimental data on where this limit is hit or how the partitioned design would work in practice. - The evaluation of the 1T model uses mocked weight data (duplicated layer weights), not a real 1T model, which may not capture realistic tensor size distributions or access patterns that would stress the system differently. - Cross-datacenter TCP bandwidth is assumed to be the bottleneck, but the paper does not evaluate scenarios with asymmetric or unreliable inter-DC links, varying bandwidth allocations, or multi-hop topologies that are common in practice at hyperscale.
Search Results 2604.09107 PDF
#48 — Innovative (71)

ASGuard: Activation-Scaling Guard to Mitigate Targeted Jailbreaking Attack (17 ▲ )

Yein Park, Jungwoo Park, Jaewoo Kang · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 71 for its rigorous mechanistic grounding—circuit identification via EAP-IG, linear probe validation, and the conceptually novel preventative fine-tuning strategy that forces the model to discover alternative refusal pathways rather than merely suppressing harmful circuits. The Pareto-front dominance across four models is convincing. The inherently attack-specific nature of the pipeline and restriction to 7B–9B open-source decoder-only architectures limit its broader impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Safety-aligned LLMs exhibit brittle refusal behaviors that can be bypassed by simple linguistic reformulations. Tense jailbreaking—rephrasing harmful requests from present tense ("How to make a Molotov cocktail?") to past tense ("How did people make a Molotov cocktail?")—achieves attack success rates (ASRs) as high as 51% on state-of-the-art models. This reveals a fundamental generalization gap: alignment training shapes global output distributions but fails to instill robust understanding of underlying harmful intent. Existing defenses like SFT and DPO reduce ASR by teaching blunt heuristics, but at the cost of catastrophic over-refusal or capability degradation. The root cause—specific internal circuits causally responsible for the vulnerability—remains unaddressed. Methodology: ASGUARD is a three-stage mechanistically-informed framework. In Stage 1, circuit analysis using Edge Attribution Patching with Integrated Gradients (EAP-IG) identifies a small set of attention heads exclusively active in jailbreak-success circuits ("False-to-True" category) but absent in refusal-success circuits ("Always-False"), yielding 7–11 tense-vulnerable heads per model across Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-7B, Gemma-2-9B, and OLMo-2-7B. In Stage 2, "Identify-then-Scale" trains a lightweight channel-wise scaling vector sj ∈ R^dhead for each vulnerable head via cross-entropy loss on harmful prompts with predefined safe responses, with all base model weights frozen. This is more parameter-efficient than LoRA and can be fused into model weights at zero inference cost. In Stage 3, "Preventative Fine-Tuning" attaches the trained (frozen) scaling vectors and fine-tunes the base model on a refusal dataset, forcing the model to learn alternative, non-vulnerable refusal pathways while the harmful circuit is neutralized. After convergence, scaling vectors are detached, leaving a model that has internalized robust refusal without depending on the intervention. Key Results: On Llama-3.1-8B, ASGUARD reduces tense jailbreak ASR from 42% to 8%, GCG ASR from 15% to 1%, and LogiBreak ASR from 30% to 13%, while fully preserving MMLU (68.2) and achieving OR-Bench-Toxic of 96.4%—the best Overall score (52.9) among all baselines. Competing methods that achieve 0% ASR (Circuit Breaker, SFT 30/70) do so at severe cost: Circuit Breaker's R-Score collapses to 30.6, and SFT 30/70 on Qwen2.5 produces 98.5% over-refusal, rendering the model unusable. On Gemma-2-9B, ASGUARD is the only method achieving meaningful ASR reduction (38%→19%) without MMLU degradation (72.2 preserved), while SFT (5/95) achieves 0% ASR but drops MMLU to 43.1. Pareto-front analysis confirms ASGUARD consistently dominates or ties the non-dominated frontier across all four models. Linear probe analysis mechanistically confirms that identified heads encode past/present tense with up to 76.56% accuracy (head L13H25 in Llama), and post-ASGUARD circuit reconstruction shows most tense-vulnerable heads disappear from the jailbreak circuit. Strengths: - Mechanistic grounding is rigorous: circuit identification, ablation controls, random head baselines, and linear probe analysis all converge to validate that targeted heads are causally responsible—not incidentally correlated—with the vulnerability. - Preventative fine-tuning is a genuine conceptual contribution: using the scaling vector as a temporary handicap forces the model to discover alternative refusal pathways, avoiding both over-refusal and catastrophic forgetting that plague SFT/DPO-based approaches. - Generalizes beyond the training attack type: circuits identified for tense jailbreaks transfer to partial defense against GCG and LogiBreak attacks, suggesting the identified heads play a broader role in safety processing. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The framework is inherently attack-specific: a separate circuit discovery pipeline must be run for each new attack type, and the paper does not address how to handle attacks for which clean/corrupted pairs cannot be easily constructed. - Applicability is restricted to architectures with localizable, sparse causal circuits; the authors acknowledge that models trained with distillation (Qwen2.5), MoE routing, or synthetic data may have more entangled internal representations, and small models (Phi-3-mini) are too sensitive for attention head intervention—limiting the method's universality. - The evaluation scope is narrow: only 7B–9B parameter decoder-only open-source models are tested, and the paper does not address closed-source models, larger scales, or multimodal settings where jailbreak surfaces are broader.
Recommended 2509.25843 PDF
#49 — Innovative (71)

How to Fine-Tune a Reasoning Model? A Teacher-Student Cooperation Framework to Synthesize Student-Consistent SFT Data (18 ▲ )

Zixian Huang, Kaichen Yang, Xu Huang, Feiyang Hao, Qiming Ge, Bowen Li, He Du, Kai Chen, Qipeng Guo · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 71 for identifying style divergence as the primary driver of reasoning model SFT degradation — a well-supported diagnostic that corrects prevailing assumptions about data quality being the bottleneck. The generate-then-rollback mechanism with lightweight boundary predictors is practically elegant, and the comprehensive ablation across multiple teacher models, student models, and training configurations converges convincingly. Evaluation is limited to code generation where capability/style boundaries are syntactically unambiguous.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) of reasoning models using data generated by a stronger teacher model is a standard practice, but it systematically degrades performance when applied to modern reasoning models like Qwen3-8B. The authors identify the root cause as stylistic divergence: reasoning models from different organizations or scales develop distinctive lexical and discourse patterns during pretraining, and forcing a student to learn from teacher-generated style tokens conflicts with its already-internalized style, causing catastrophic forgetting of reasoning capability. This is distinct from the well-studied base-model SFT setting where style is easily overwritten. The gap is substantial — naive teacher-only SFT on Qwen3-8B drops OJBench performance by 10.02%, even after 80K samples and 9 epochs of training. Methodology: The paper proposes TESSY (Teacher–Student Cooperation Data Synthesis), a framework that decomposes each generated token as either a capability token (task-relevant: code, math expressions, logical deductions) or a style token (task-irrelevant: discourse connectives, transitional phrases like "Okay, let's see"). During synthesis, the teacher model generates capability spans while the student generates style spans, alternating in an iterative loop. To implement precise switching, each model first generates a fixed window of k=20 tokens, then a lightweight boundary predictor (a binary sequence labeler trained on Qwen3-0.6B-Base from 100K annotated segments) identifies the last token of the intended type, truncating the remainder via a generate-then-rollback strategy. The final answer section is always delegated to the student, since answer formatting is highly stylistically distinctive. Generation is implemented on vLLM with prefix caching to make model switching efficient. The resulting data averages 77.7% teacher tokens (for GPT-OSS-120B) but is distributed much closer to the student's natural output distribution, as confirmed by TF-IDF PCA visualization and cosine similarity analysis. Key Results: With GPT-OSS-120B as teacher and Qwen3-8B as student, TESSY achieves +11.25% on LiveCodeBench-Pro and +6.68% on OJBench over baseline Qwen3-8B, compared to drops of 3.25% and 10.02% respectively for Teacher-Only SFT. Every other compared method — Teacher-Score (reject sampling), Teacher-Reference (self-distillation), Teacher-Answer, Teacher-Think, Teacher-Mix — also degrades code performance, with TESSY being the only approach that consistently improves the student across all four code benchmarks. On out-of-domain math (AIME2024/2025), TESSY also yields +3.75% and +0.93% while Teacher-Only causes drops. TESSY generalizes across teacher models: with DeepSeek-R1, TESSY outperforms Teacher-Only by 3.45%; with GPT-OSS-120B (the most stylistically distant), the improvement reaches 16.79%. On the larger Qwen3-30B-A3B student, TESSY still adds +5.52% and +8.41% over Teacher-Only on the two main code benchmarks. A notable secondary finding: TESSY-synthesized data is shorter on average than teacher-only data (by ~7,500–9,000 tokens with GPT-OSS-120B), because the student model tends to terminate thinking traces earlier. LoRA fine-tuning proves counterproductive for both Teacher-Only and TESSY on reasoning models, collapsing performance far below baseline. Strengths: - The core diagnostic — style divergence as the primary driver of reasoning model SFT degradation — is well-supported empirically and provides a useful corrective to prevailing assumptions about data quality being the main bottleneck. - The generate-then-rollback mechanism with lightweight boundary predictors is practically elegant: it imposes minimal overhead (Qwen3-0.6B-Base classifiers) while achieving fine-grained token-level control over style/capability assignment. - Comprehensive ablation: the paper systematically tests multiple teacher models (including same-family Qwen3-235B), multiple student models, cross-training configurations, LoRA vs. full fine-tuning, final answer assignment, and system prompt isolation, all of which converge to reinforce the central claim. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The capability/style dichotomy is heuristic: the boundary predictor is trained on LLM-annotated labels using a prompt that itself requires judgment calls about what constitutes "reasoning" vs. "discourse", and there is no ground truth or inter-annotator agreement to validate classifier accuracy independently. - TESSY is evaluated exclusively on code generation (competitive programming), a domain where capability tokens (code) are syntactically unambiguous. It is unclear whether the style/capability decomposition transfers cleanly to domains like mathematical reasoning in natural language, where the boundary between connective prose and substantive deduction is far less crisp. - The synthesis pipeline requires running two large models simultaneously plus boundary predictors, incurring substantially higher compute cost than Teacher-Only data generation; this cost is not quantified or compared, making it difficult to assess practical scalability.
Recommended 2604.14164 PDF
#50 — Innovative (71)

From LLM to Silicon: RL-Driven ASIC Architecture Exploration for On-Device AI Inference

Ravindra Ganti, Steve Xu · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: This paper presents a genuinely novel formulation by unifying mesh topology, per-core microarchitecture, workload partitioning, and memory hierarchy into a single MDP solved via SAC with a Mixture-of-Experts policy head — a meaningful departure from prior work that optimizes isolated design phases. The heterogeneous per-tile derivation and node-retargetable pipeline from ONNX to RTL/GDSII demonstrate creative systems-level thinking. However, the innovation is significantly tempered by the fact that all PPA results are analytical estimates rather than silicon-validated, the misleading direct comparison against measured GPU numbers, and the narrow evaluation on only two transformer workloads with single-seed runs.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deploying large language models on custom AI accelerators requires careful co-optimization of hardware architecture and software compilation, a process that traditionally demands months of manual RTL coding and verification. Prior RL-based approaches—Mirhoseini et al. on chip floorplanning, Gao et al. on TPU datapath—optimize single design phases in isolation and cannot jointly tune mesh topology, per-core microarchitecture, workload partitioning, and memory hierarchy together. Similarly, ML compilers (TVM, XLA, TensorRT) target existing hardware and lack PPA-aware joint hardware-software optimization. This paper addresses the gap by formulating the entire hardware-software co-design problem as a single MDP and solving it with RL, enabling automated, node-retargetable ASIC compilation from model input to tape-out-ready output. Methodology: The system operates over a 2D mesh of Tensor Compute Cores (TCCs)—RISC-V cores with custom vector/tensor extensions. The design space is formulated as an MDP with a 73-dimensional state (52 used by the SAC actor) capturing workload characteristics, configuration parameters, partitioning ratios, hazard statistics, and surrogate PPA feedback. The action space combines 4 discrete mesh dimension deltas and 30 continuous dimensions controlling per-TCC parameters (FETCH, VLEN, DMEM/WMEM/IMEM, NoC flit width), memory allocation ratios, and operator partition ratios. The RL agent is Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) with twin Q-networks, auto-tuned entropy, prioritized experience replay (PER), and a Mixture-of-Experts policy head that routes different operator regimes (attention, MLP, memory-heavy) to specialist expert policies. A lightweight 2-layer MLP world model predicts state deltas and enables 5-step Model-Predictive Control that blends (70/30) with SAC for continuous TCC parameters. A surrogate PPA model provides fast estimates for candidate actions before expensive full evaluation. After RL convergence, a post-processing step derives heterogeneous per-tile values for FETCH, VLEN, and memory sizes based on each tile's workload characteristics, reducing FETCH_SIZE variation to 1–16 and VLEN to 128–2048 bits across tiles. Final configurations are selected from a Pareto archive using the user's PPA weight profile. The six-stage pipeline ingests ONNX models and emits RTL/GDSII artifacts with no manual retuning between nodes. Key Results: On Llama 3.1 8B FP16 across 7 process nodes (3nm–28nm): the RL selects an 41×42 mesh (1,722 TCCs) at 3nm achieving 29,809 tok/s at 51W within 648 mm², and adapts down to an 11×12 mesh (132 TCCs) at 28nm (623 tok/s, 3.78W, 3,545 mm²). The 3nm node delivers 47.85× higher throughput and 5.47× smaller area than 28nm. Power efficiency scales from 2.6 GOps/s per mW at 28nm to 9.1 GOps/s per mW at 3nm, with log-log fits showing performance scaling exponent −1.33 (R²=0.986) and area scaling +0.75 (R²=0.995). On SmolVLM in low-power mode, all 7 nodes achieve <13 mW at 10 MHz with compact 8–12 TCC meshes. SAC outperforms random search at 3nm with 3.5× higher throughput (29,809 vs 8,421 tok/s) and 9.1× more feasible configurations (2,847 vs 312 out of 4,600 episodes). An industry comparison table claims 580 tok/s per watt at 3nm vs H200 (0.3) and Groq (2.0), but these are compiler-estimated figures from analytical models, not silicon-measured. Strengths: - Joint MDP formulation unifies mesh topology, per-core microarchitecture, workload partitioning, and NoC configuration in a single RL loop, capturing coupled design-space interactions that sequential or isolated optimization misses. - Heterogeneous per-tile derivation is a principled design choice: WMEM, VLEN, and FETCH vary substantially across tiles based on operator characteristics, reducing power without sacrificing throughput compared to uniform allocation. - Multi-node, multi-workload generalization without manual retuning—the same policy interface produces a 41×42 high-throughput LLM configuration and a 2×4 ultra-low-power VLM configuration—provides meaningful evidence of breadth. - The paper acknowledges its limitations explicitly (Section 5.4), including single-seed evaluation, analytical-only PPA, transformer-specific state features, and 2D mesh constraint, which is scientifically responsible for an industry preprint. Weaknesses / Limitations: - All PPA results are compiler-estimated from analytical models, not validated by RTL synthesis or post-layout power analysis; the headline industry comparison in Table 20 directly juxtaposes these estimates against silicon-measured GPU numbers, which is misleading without much stronger caveats. - Only two transformer-based workloads are validated (Llama 3.1 8B decoder and SmolVLM encoder-decoder); CNNs, diffusion models, and MoE architectures are explicitly untested, and the state vector encodes transformer-specific features (KV-cache, GQA heads) that may not transfer without redesign. - Single-seed RL runs with no confidence intervals or variance analysis make it impossible to assess whether reported optima are robust or artifacts of favorable random initialization.
Search Results 2604.07526 PDF
#51 — Innovative (71)

WebForge: Breaking the Realism-Reproducibility-Scalability Trilemma in Browser Agent Benchmark

Peng Yuan, Yuyang Yin, Yuxuan Cai, Zheng Wei · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Scores 71 for a creative engineering solution to the realism-reproducibility-scalability trilemma in browser agent benchmarks. The four-agent automated pipeline with injected real-web noise, the seven-dimensional difficulty control framework enabling diagnostic capability profiling, and the anti-cheating mechanisms represent meaningful advances over prior benchmark construction approaches. The per-dimension analysis revealing distinct model vulnerability profiles is practically valuable. However, the static HTML limitation creates a fundamental sim-to-real gap, the anti-cheating via Base64 is trivially defeatable, and the moderate inter-dimension correlations weaken the claimed independence of difficulty axes.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Browser agent benchmarks face a fundamental trilemma: real-website benchmarks (e.g., WebVoyager, Mind2Web) maximize realism but suffer from content drift—nearly half of Mind2Web's tasks expired within two years—while controlled environments (e.g., WebArena) guarantee reproducibility but remain artificially clean, lacking pop-ups, cookie dialogs, and network delays. Both approaches also require costly manual curation that limits scalability. Existing automated generation tools handle only non-interactive tasks or lack multi-dimensional difficulty control. Additionally, single aggregate scores mask capability biases across domains and task types, limiting diagnostic value. WebForge is motivated by the need to simultaneously achieve realism, reproducibility, and scalability, while enabling fine-grained capability profiling. Methodology: WebForge is a fully automated four-agent pipeline—Plan, Generate, Refine, Validate—that produces self-contained, interactive web environments end-to-end without human annotation. The Plan Agent uses dual-LLM generation: a high-temperature (T=2.0) creative pass drafts tasks with a seven-dimensional difficulty vector δ ∈ {1,2,3}^7 (Jump Depth, Jump Breadth, Page Interaction, Visual Complexity, Info Complexity, Reasoning/Calc, Risk Factor), followed by a low-temperature (T=1.0) precision pass for quality assurance and constraint verification. The Generation Agent instantiates the plan into functional HTML/CSS/JS websites, collecting real images and data from live websites as references, and implements anti-cheating mechanisms including Base64 encryption of ground-truth values and deceptive confirmation codes for common mistake patterns. The Refinement Agent applies rule-driven quality assurance: injecting real-web noise (pop-ups, cookie banners, network latency), resolving dead navigation links, and replacing JavaScript alert() dialogs with inline DOM errors to ensure agent-accessibility. The Validation Agent runs in the same Chromium environment as tested agents, replaying solution paths in an Observe–Reason–Act loop (up to 50 steps) to verify solvability and filter out defective tasks. Evaluation uses a final-state paradigm—only the agent's terminal output is checked against ground truth—with three answer types: Direct Answer, Operation Code, and Mixed. WebForge-Bench is constructed from 1,260 generated tasks (60 per domain-level pair across 7 domains × 3 difficulty levels), with 934 passing validation (74.1% pass rate). Key Results: Fourteen model configurations are evaluated across three difficulty levels and seven domains. Gemini-3-Pro achieves the highest overall accuracy (75.9%), followed by Claude-4.5-Sonnet (69.9%) and Gemini-3-Flash (67.1%). Among open-source models, Kimi-K2.5 (66.4%) surpasses closed-source GPT-5.2 (59.5%). The three-tier difficulty system produces a 56-point gap between the strongest (Gemini-3-Pro, 58.0% at L3) and weakest (Qwen3-Omni-30B, 2.4% at L3) models, confirming strong stratification. Cross-domain analysis reveals that Info Retrieval (D4, avg. 56.9%) and Content Creation (D7, 57.2%) are easiest—aligning with LLM pre-training strengths—while Consumer Transaction (D1, 48.3%) and Content Moderation (D2, 48.3%) are hardest, exposing biases invisible to aggregate metrics; GPT-5-Mini leads D4 (73.8%) but drops to 50.4% on D3, a 23-point swing. Removing visual input causes consistent 16–17 percentage-point drops across multimodal models, with gaps widening to 20+ points at L3. Pipeline ablations show that removing Plan Agent refinement drops the validation pass rate from 74.1% to 59.5%, and removing the Refinement Agent further drops it to 51.4%. Prompt token cost scales super-linearly with difficulty (5–8× from L1 to L3). Per-dimension analysis shows Visual Complexity produces the steepest L1-to-L3 accuracy drop, while Reasoning/Calc best separates strong from weak frontier models (52-point spread between Gemini-3-Pro and GPT-5-Nano at L3). Strengths: - The four-agent pipeline genuinely resolves the benchmark trilemma by automating end-to-end environment construction with injected real-web noise, enabling reproducible environments that are more realistic than sterile controlled benchmarks—this is a clear advance over all prior work. - The seven-dimensional difficulty control framework is principled and enables diagnostic capability profiling beyond aggregate scores; the per-dimension analysis reveals meaningfully distinct vulnerability profiles across models, which is practically useful for identifying agent bottlenecks. - The anti-cheating mechanisms (Base64 encryption, deceptive codes keyed to common mistake patterns) are carefully designed and make source-code shortcuts infeasible, ensuring tasks test genuine browser interaction rather than DOM scraping. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The seven difficulty dimensions exhibit moderate inter-dimension correlation (avg. Spearman |ρ| = 0.495), driven by the compositional aggregation rules; this attenuates the independence of per-dimension signals and makes it difficult to isolate single-axis capability deficits cleanly. - The generated environments are static HTML sites using localStorage for state, which cannot faithfully represent real-time data, true multi-user interaction, or persistent server-side transactions—a fundamental sim-to-real gap that the paper acknowledges but does not resolve, and which may cause benchmark scores to overestimate agent robustness on genuinely dynamic web applications. - Base64 encoding is trivially reversible by any agent that inspects data.json and applies atob(); the anti-cheating mechanism prevents casual inspection but not a determined agent that reads and decodes the file, undermining evaluation integrity for sufficiently capable models.
Search Results 2604.10988 PDF
#52 — Innovative (71)

OccuBench: Evaluating AI Agents on Real-World Professional Tasks via Language World Models (43 ▲ )

Xiaomeng Hu, Yinger Zhang, Fei Huang, Jianhong Tu, Yang Su, Lianghao Deng, Yuxuan Liu, Yantao Liu, Dayiheng Liu, Tsung-Yi Ho · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026
Why this rank: OccuBench introduces the Language Environment Simulator, a genuinely creative reframing that transforms environment construction from an engineering problem into a configuration problem, unlocking evaluation of domains structurally inaccessible to all prior benchmarks. The controlled fault injection taxonomy yields a non-obvious and practically important finding—that implicit faults (truncated data, missing fields) are substantially harder than explicit errors—supported by compelling case studies. The cross-simulator analysis exposing GPT-5.2 as the worst simulator despite being the best agent is a surprising result with methodological implications for the field.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing agent benchmarks (WebArena, OSWorld, SWE-bench, TAU-bench) are confined to domains with publicly available environments—web browsing, desktop operations, code repositories, and a handful of APIs. The vast majority of high-value professional occupations (emergency triage, nuclear reactor monitoring, customs processing, factory scheduling) are bound to enterprise systems with no public APIs and irreversible real-world consequences, making them completely untestable. There is also no systematic evaluation of agent robustness under environmental faults (timeouts, degraded data), despite this being critical for production deployment. The paper addresses this evaluation blind spot by asking: can LLMs themselves simulate domain environments, eliminating the engineering bottleneck of constructing real test environments? Methodology: The core contribution is the Language Environment Simulator (LES): an LLM configured with a quadruple (system prompt, tool schema, initial state, state description) that generates stateful, causally consistent tool responses for any domain it understands. The evaluation loop has the agent-under-test issuing tool calls, the LES generating JSON observations, and a rubric-based verifier scoring the trajectory as pass/fail. A multi-agent synthesis pipeline powered by Gemini-3-Flash-Preview automatically produces evaluation instances with guaranteed solvability (verified by majority-vote execution with and without a reference plan), calibrated difficulty (hardest-passing level selected), and structural diversity (16 non-overlapping sub-topics per scenario grounded in professional reference documents). The resulting OccuBench covers 100 task scenarios across 10 industry categories and 65 specialized domains, yielding 382 evaluation instances averaging 5.5 tools and 16.2 tool calls per task. Environmental robustness is evaluated via controlled fault injection appended to the LES system prompt at test time: E1 (explicit errors: HTTP 500, timeouts, connection refused), E2 (implicit degradation: truncated lists, missing fields, stale values—no error signal), and E3 (mixed). Faults are transient and parameterized by count and duration. 15 frontier models across 8 families are evaluated; all use thinking/reasoning mode. Key Results: GPT-5.2 leads overall at 79.6% completion rate (E0), followed by Gemini 3.1 Pro (72.3%) and Claude Opus 4.6 (71.5%), but no model dominates all industries: GPT-5.2 scores only 67% in Commerce while Qwen 3.5 Plus reaches 81% there; Gemini 3.1 Pro leads Education (84%) and Science (81%) but scores only 62% in Healthcare. Open-source models Qwen 3.5 Plus (69.9%) and DeepSeek V3.2 (69.6%) rank 4th and 5th, outperforming most Claude variants. Under fault injection, average performance drops from 67.5% (E0) to 62.6% (E1), 53.4% (E2), and 54.4% (E3)—implicit faults are the hardest because they require proactive data-quality detection rather than simple retry. Claude Opus 4.6 drops 17.6 points under E2; Qwen 3.5 Plus drops 18.3. Robustness scores (min fault CR / E0 CR) range from 0.87 (Gemini 3.1 Pro, MiniMax M2.7) to 0.63 (Kimi K2.5). Reasoning effort yields large gains: GPT-5.2 improves 27.5 points from none to xhigh effort. Larger model variants consistently outperform smaller ones (gaps of 7–11%), and newer Claude generations show consistent Opus improvement (61.3% → 65.2% → 71.5%). Cross-simulator analysis reveals that GPT-5.2 as an agent ranks first (79.6%) but as a simulator produces the worst evaluation quality (all agents average only 29.3% under it vs. 67.9% under Gemini Flash), due to state fabrication, entity omission, and rule invention. With a capable simulator, pairwise ranking agreement reaches 85.7% across 28 model pairs. Strengths: - The LES framing elegantly transforms environment construction from an engineering problem into a configuration problem, enabling coverage of domains that are structurally untestable by any existing benchmark—nuclear safety, emergency coordination, aquaculture—without any real infrastructure. - The controlled fault injection taxonomy (E0/E1/E2/E3) is well-designed; the empirical finding that implicit faults are harder than explicit ones is non-obvious, practically important, and supported by illuminating case studies (e.g., property valuation where truncated unit records produce a dangerously wrong DSCR of 1.72x vs. actual 1.19x). - Cross-industry profiling reveals model-specific occupational specializations invisible to single-domain benchmarks, with concrete practical guidance: Claude Opus 4.6 excels in operational/state-tracking domains; Gemini 3.1 Pro in knowledge-intensive ones; Qwen 3.5 Plus in consumer-facing domains. Weaknesses / Limitations: - LESs simulate domain logic rather than domain data: specific numerical values (drug concentrations, sensor readings, financial figures) are generated rather than retrieved from authoritative sources. This means OccuBench measures decision-making process correctness but cannot validate exact numerical precision, limiting applicability for domains where computational accuracy matters (e.g., financial auditing to the cent, pharmacokinetic calculations). - Simulator dependence is a fundamental validity threat: tasks verified as solvable under one LES may become unsolvable under another, and the finding that GPT-5.2 (the strongest agent) makes the worst simulator—with all-agent averages collapsing to 29.3%—shows this is not hypothetical. Benchmark consumers must independently validate simulator quality before trusting results, adding operational complexity the paper acknowledges but does not fully resolve.

#53 — Innovative (71)

Backdoor Attacks on Decentralised Post-Training (10 ▲ )

Oğuzhan Ersoy, Nikolay Blagoev, Jona te Lintelo, Stefanos Koffas, Marina Krček, Stjepan Picek · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 13 Apr 2026
Why this rank: This paper earns 71 for identifying and exploiting a genuinely underexplored attack surface — backdoor injection through a constrained intermediate-stage adversary in pipeline-parallel training. The iterative task-arithmetic injection mechanism is elegant: it maintains stealth by keeping validation loss indistinguishable from clean training while achieving 94% ASR, and the 60% retention after safety alignment is a meaningful practical threat. However, evaluation on only a 1B model with 4 stages and no defense analysis limits the contribution's completeness.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Decentralised post-training of LLMs using pipeline parallelism (PP) distributes model stages across untrusted nodes, creating a surface for adversarial attacks. While backdoor and poisoning attacks against federated learning and data-parallel settings are well-studied, the robustness of pipeline-parallel training has been largely unexplored. The only prior PP attack (Lu et al., 2024) is an untargeted poisoning attack that causes visible training degradation and is thus easily detected. No stealthy, targeted backdoor attack on PP existed. This gap is critical because decentralised PP systems (e.g., Petals/Borzunov et al.) expose intermediate model stages to potentially malicious participants who cannot access raw tokens or outputs. Methodology: The paper proposes a two-phase misalignment backdoor attack for the pipeline-parallel SFT setting. In the offline phase, the adversary — who controls only one intermediate pipeline stage — trains a surrogate backdoored model from the same base LLM by freezing all stages except the controlled one. The surrogate is trained on a harmful dataset to produce unsafe responses when the trigger word ("SUDO") is present and safe responses otherwise. The stage-wise task vector is computed as the parameter delta between the backdoored surrogate and the base model. In the online phase, during legitimate SFT, the attacker periodically injects a scaled version of this task vector into its local stage parameters at every `fq_a` iterations using the task arithmetic framework (Ilharco et al., 2023). The scaling factor `w_a` and injection frequency `fq_a` are tuned to keep validation loss nearly identical to clean training, making the attack stealthy. The key insight is that iterative, small-magnitude injections resist both detection and post-hoc safety alignment erasure, unlike naive full-vector injection at the start of training. Experiments use LLaMA-3.2 1B Instruct split into four equal stages, with the attacker holding stage 2, fine-tuned on Finance-Instruct-500k. Safety evaluation uses LLaMA Guard 3 8B. Key Results: With optimal parameters (w_a=0.1, fq_a=25), the attack achieves a 94% attack success rate (ASR) on unsafe prompts containing the trigger, while the safety score without the trigger remains comparable to clean SFT (~20% residual misalignment, attributed to SFT itself). The trigger reduces alignment from ~80% (clean SFT) to ~6%. Validation loss curves for the attacked model are visually indistinguishable from clean training, confirming stealth. After an additional post-SFT safety alignment step, the iterative attack retains 60% ASR, whereas the naive full-injection baseline is fully erased by the safety alignment pass. The iterative method is thus both stealthier and more robust than the naive baseline. Strengths: - First backdoor attack targeting pipeline parallelism with a constrained intermediate-stage adversary — a realistic and underexplored threat model. - Elegant use of task arithmetic for injection: minimises interference with SFT loss and enables tunable injection strength, making the attack stealthy by design. - Demonstrates non-trivial persistence against post-hoc safety alignment (60% ASR retained), which is a meaningful real-world threat since safety fine-tuning is a common remediation step. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluated only on a small 1B parameter model with a simple 4-stage partition; scalability and effectiveness on larger models (7B, 70B) with more stages and different partitioning schemes is unknown. - The attack assumes the adversary knows which layers belong to their assigned stage and has access to the exact base model used for SFT — the latter is acknowledged as reasonable for open-source models but the former may not always hold. No analysis of robustness to stage uncertainty is provided (punted to future work). - No defenses are investigated; the paper is purely an attack paper with no discussion of detection strategies, gradient monitoring, or cryptographic countermeasures, leaving practitioners with no actionable guidance.
Recommended 2604.02372 PDF
#54 — Innovative (71)

Audio Flamingo Next: Next-Generation Open Audio-Language Models for Speech, Sound, and Music (17 ▲ )

Sreyan Ghosh, Arushi Goel, Kaousheik Jayakumar, Lasha Koroshinadze, Nishit Anand, Zhifeng Kong, Siddharth Gururani, Sang-gil Lee, Jaehyeon Kim, Aya Aljafari, Chao-Han Huck Yang, Sungwon Kim, Ramani Duraiswami, Dinesh Manocha, Mohammad Shoeybi, Bryan Catanzaro, Ming-Yu Liu, Wei Ping · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 14 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Audio Flamingo Next earns 71 for its comprehensive engineering achievement in scaling open-source audio-language models to internet-scale data (~1M hours) with genuine architectural novelties in Rotary Time Embeddings and Temporal Audio Chain-of-Thought reasoning. The RoTE mechanism — replacing discrete position indices with absolute timestamps — is a principled innovation that directly addresses long-form audio understanding, and the model's ability to surpass closed-source Gemini-2.5-Pro on several benchmarks validates the approach. However, the core contribution is primarily data curation and training methodology rather than fundamental algorithmic novelty, and several claimed capabilities lack systematic evaluation.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large Audio Language Models (LALMs) have lagged behind vision-language models in terms of open, scalable development. Most existing open LALMs are tightly coupled to a small set of academic benchmarks, which encode biases and incomplete coverage, resulting in models that generalize poorly to long, noisy, and diverse real-world audio. Key gaps in the prior Audio Flamingo 3 model include weak performance on counting and speaker diarization tasks, limited exposure to multilingual and multi-speaker audio, lack of native long-form audio understanding (beyond ~10 minutes), and absence of temporally grounded reasoning. The paper addresses all these simultaneously while remaining fully open (weights, data, and code released). Methodology: AF-Next is built on a four-component encoder-decoder architecture: (1) AF-Whisper, a Whisper-based audio encoder further pre-trained on a larger multilingual and multi-talker corpus; (2) a 2-layer MLP audio adaptor projecting audio embeddings into the LLM token space; (3) Qwen-2.5-7B as the LLM backbone with context extended from 32k to 128k tokens and standard RoPE replaced with Rotary Time Embeddings (RoTE) that index positions by absolute timestamps rather than discrete indices; and (4) a streaming TTS decoder for voice-to-voice interaction. Audio is processed in non-overlapping 30-second chunks with stride-2 pooling, supporting inputs up to 30 minutes via hybrid sequence parallelism (Ulysses within-node + Ring across-node) during training. Data curation is the paper's primary contribution: ~108M samples / ~1M hours assembled from internet-scale sources and synthetically labeled, spanning long-audio captioning and QA (27k–39k hrs each), multi-talker speech, multilingual ASR/AST, music understanding, multi-audio comparison, safety, and multi-turn chat. A novel AF-Think-Time dataset of ~43K question–answer–thinking-chain triplets grounds chain-of-thought reasoning steps to audio timestamps, enabling Temporal Audio Chain-of-Thought (TACoT). Training follows a four-stage curriculum: (1) pre-training aligns only the audio adaptor (LLM frozen); (2) mid-training Stage 1 full-finetunes up to 10-minute audio at 24k context; (3) mid-training Stage 2 extends to 30-minute audio at 128k context, yielding AF-Next-Captioner; (4) post-training applies GRPO reinforcement learning for instruction following and chat, yielding AF-Next-Instruct; (5) CoT training via SFT then GRPO on AF-Think-Time yields AF-Next-Think. Key Results: Across 20+ benchmarks, AF-Next-Instruct is the strongest fully open-source LALM, with three complementary variants offering additional gains. On MMAU (audio reasoning), AF-Next-Captioner achieves 75.76 average accuracy vs. 72.42 for Audio Flamingo 3 (prior SOTA open-source). On MMAU-Pro, AF-Next-Think (58.7) surpasses closed-source Gemini-2.5-Pro (57.4). On LongAudioBench, AF-Next-Instruct scores 73.9 vs. 68.6 for AF3 and 60.4 for Gemini-2.5-Pro; the speech-inclusive variant reaches 81.2 vs. 72.9 (AF3) and 66.2 (Gemini-2.5-Pro). On ASR, AF-Next-Instruct sets LALM records on LibriSpeech with WER 1.54/2.76 (clean/other). On music benchmarks, Medley-Solos-DB reaches 92.13 vs. 85.80 (AF2), and SongCaps GPT5 scores improve from 6.7/6.2 to 8.8/8.9 over AF3. Speech translation on CoVoST2 shows a 12-point BLEU improvement over Phi-4-mm for Arabic (9.9→21.9 EN→AR). Strengths: - First fully open LALM to scale training to internet-scale data (~1M hours), with transparent methodology, full data/code/model release, and reproducible results averaged over 3 runs. - Temporal Audio Chain-of-Thought is a principled novelty: replacing index-based position encodings with absolute timestamps (RoTE) and training on timestamp-grounded reasoning chains directly addresses the evidence aggregation challenge specific to long-form audio, unlike prior CoT work limited to short clips. - Comprehensive capability coverage (ASR, captioning, QA, multi-talker, diarization, music, safety, multilingual, multi-turn chat, voice-to-voice) with a single model family rather than task-specific systems, and strong real-world generalization demonstrated on out-of-benchmark evaluations. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Several of AF-Next's new capabilities (multi-talker ASR, speaker diarization, timestamped captioning, voice-to-voice) lack systematic benchmark evaluation in this submission; their quality is asserted but not measured, making it difficult to compare against other systems on these tasks. - The synthetic data pipeline for long-audio QA is acknowledged to be prone to hallucination for certain QA types, and the internet-scale training corpus remains unevenly distributed across languages, rare sound events, and specialized domains — a fundamental data quality concern that is not fully resolved.
Recommended 2604.10905 PDF
#55 — Innovative (70)

ELT: Elastic Looped Transformers for Visual Generation (15 ▲ )

Sahil Goyal, Swayam Agrawal, Gautham Govind Anil, Prateek Jain, Sujoy Paul, Aditya Kusupati · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 13 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 70 for the Intra-Loop Self Distillation technique, which cleverly exploits the prefix structure of looped forward passes to achieve zero-overhead distillation — a non-obvious design that enables elastic inference from a single trained model. The 4× parameter reduction with matching FID and the 3.5× throughput gains from on-chip parameter fitting are practically compelling. However, the core components — weight sharing and knowledge distillation — are individually well-established, and validation is limited to small class-conditional benchmarks.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard visual generative models (masked generative transformers, diffusion transformers) scale compute capacity by stacking increasingly deep unique transformer layers, causing parameter counts to grow proportionally with depth. This creates a memory bottleneck and locks inference to a fixed computational budget. While looped (weight-shared) transformers offer a parameter-efficient alternative — reusing the same block across multiple passes — vanilla looped architectures suffer a critical failure: intermediate loop states produce incoherent outputs and the model only works correctly when inference loop count exactly matches the training loop count. This rigidity makes vanilla looping incompatible with elastic, resource-adaptive deployment. The gap addressed here is: how to train a single weight-shared generative model that remains high-quality at any inference-time loop count, yielding a continuous family of compute-quality trade-offs without retraining. Methodology: ELT structures the generative backbone as N unique transformer layers forming a composite block g_Θ, which is applied L times per sampling step, giving effective depth N×L while storing only N layers of parameters. The key innovation is Intra-Loop Self Distillation (ILSD): during each training step, a stochastic student loop count L_int is sampled uniformly from [L_min, L_max), and two forward paths are computed — a teacher executing the full L_max loops and a student exiting at L_int loops. Crucially, the student trajectory is a strict prefix of the teacher trajectory, so no additional compute is incurred. The joint training loss combines (1) ground-truth supervision for the teacher, (2) ground-truth supervision for the student weighted by a curriculum coefficient λ that linearly decays from 1→0, and (3) a distillation loss from teacher to student weighted by (1−λ). This curriculum initially anchors the student to ground truth (while the teacher is still immature) and gradually shifts to teacher mimicry. For masked generative models, cross-entropy is used for both ground-truth and distillation losses; for diffusion models, sigmoid-weighted MSE is used. The approach is validated on both MaskGIT-style masked generative transformers and DiT-style diffusion transformers for image (ImageNet 256×256) and video (UCF-101) generation. Key Results: On class-conditional ImageNet 256×256, ELT-XL (7N×4L, 111M parameters) achieves FID 2.0, matching MaskGIT-XL (446M parameters) under iso-inference-compute — a 4× reduction in parameter count. ELT-L (8N×3L, 101M) achieves FID 2.2 with identical inference compute to MaskGIT-L (303M). In the diffusion setting, ELT (8N×4L, 539M) achieves FID 3.16 vs. the DiT-32 baseline FID 3.43 at iso-depth, and ELT (16N×2L, 1.1B) achieves FID 2.83 — improving over the baseline while using 2× fewer parameters. For video, ELT (6N×4L, 76M) achieves FVD 72.8 on UCF-101 vs. MAGVIT-L FVD 76 (306M parameters), a 4× parameter reduction. Throughput gains on TPU v6e reach 3.5× for model scale H (1280 hidden dim) due to compact shared parameters fitting on-chip and reducing HBM-to-SRAM transfers. ELT also converges 1.4–2× faster than standard DiT under iso-inference-compute settings. ILSD ablations confirm that without it, FID degrades catastrophically for L≠L_max, while with ILSD, ELT maintains stable quality across the full loop spectrum and even generalizes modestly beyond L_max. Strengths: - The ILSD training objective is architecturally elegant: the student trajectory is a strict prefix of the teacher's single forward pass, so there is zero additional training compute overhead compared to vanilla looping — a significant practical advantage over standard teacher-student distillation requiring separate forward passes. - The Any-Time inference property is genuinely useful: a single trained model can serve heterogeneous deployment tiers (edge/cloud) by adjusting the loop count, traversing the Pareto frontier of FID vs. GFLOPs without retraining or storing multiple models. - The throughput gains (up to 3.5×) arise from a hardware-level efficiency: compact shared parameters fit on-chip and avoid repeated HBM-SRAM transfers, a benefit orthogonal to FLOPs reduction and particularly impactful for large models that require multi-device sharding. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The approach is only validated on class-conditional generation on ImageNet and UCF-101 — small, well-controlled benchmarks. Generalization to text-conditional generation at scale (e.g., large text-to-image or text-to-video models) is not demonstrated, leaving open whether ILSD's curriculum can stabilize looping in far more complex conditioning regimes. - The minimum block size N is a hard constraint: the 1N×32L configuration yields FID 10.30 vs. 3.43 for the 32-layer baseline, showing a single looped layer cannot compensate for insufficient representational capacity per iteration regardless of loop count. The paper does not provide principled guidance for selecting the N-L trade-off; practitioners must sweep configurations empirically, which is expensive at scale.
Recommended 2604.09168 PDF
#56 — Innovative (69)

The Past Is Not Past: Memory-Enhanced Dynamic Reward Shaping (21 ▲ )

Yang Liu, Enxi Wang, Yufei Gao, Weixin Zhang, Bo Wang, Zhiyuan Zeng, Yikai Zhang, Yining Zheng, Xipeng Qiu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 14 Apr 2026
Why this rank: MEDS introduces a genuinely novel angle on the exploration-collapse problem in RLVR by tracking recurring error patterns across training history via logit-space clustering, a perspective no prior work in this space has taken. The core design—reusing already-computed layer-wise logits as cheap proxies for reasoning structure and applying HDBSCAN for parameter-free error clustering—is elegant and practically efficient. The theoretical grounding via Chebyshev's rearrangement inequality adds principled justification. However, the 61% agreement between logit clusters and semantic error categories suggests the method captures distributional regularities more than true reasoning failures, and restriction to math benchmarks leaves generalization uncertain.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Reinforcement learning for LLM reasoning suffers from a well-documented failure mode: as training progresses, the policy collapses into a narrow set of behaviors, repeatedly generating the same erroneous reasoning patterns despite continued optimization. Classical entropy regularization encourages token-level randomness under the current policy distribution, but this operates on instantaneous distributions and does not explicitly discourage recurrent failure patterns across training rollouts. The result is that the model gets trapped in "stable error basins" where superficially varied surface-level outputs all share the same underlying logical failure. No prior work in RLVR explicitly tracks and penalizes recurring error patterns across the training history. Methodology: MEDS (Memory-Enhanced Dynamic reward Shaping) introduces three tightly integrated components. First, logic feature extraction: for each generated response, MEDS reuses the layer-wise logits already computed during the forward pass at the first token of the final answer, concatenating them across the latter half of the transformer layers to form a compact N/2-dimensional feature vector. This adds negligible computational overhead. Second, memory-based clustering: for each prompt, MEDS maintains a running memory of feature vectors from all past incorrect rollouts, and applies HDBSCAN (a density-based, parameter-free clustering algorithm) to group these vectors into error clusters whose count K is determined dynamically. Third, cluster-based reward shaping: a response assigned to cluster C_k receives an additional penalty of min(α log(|C_k| + 1), β), proportional to cluster size, subtracted from the task reward. Larger clusters—representing more frequently recurring error patterns—incur heavier penalties, pushing the policy away from well-worn mistake modes. The paper provides a formal theoretical justification via Chebyshev's rearrangement inequality, proving that under the KL-regularized optimization objective, the penalized policy achieves expected task reward no lower than the unpenalized policy. MEDS is built on top of DAPO and trained using the verl framework on math reasoning datasets (DAPO-Math-17K merged with MATH levels 3–5). Key Results: MEDS is evaluated on AIME24, AMC23, MATH500, Minerva, and OlympiadBench across three base models: Qwen3-1.7B, Qwen2.5-Math-7B, and Qwen3-8B. MEDS consistently achieves the best average pass@1 and pass@128 across all models and datasets. Representative gains: Qwen3-1.7B average pass@1 improves from 46.6 (DAPO) to 52.49 (+4.13 pts); Qwen3-8B OlympiadBench pass@128 improves from 70.81 to 82.67 (+11.86 pts, a 17% relative gain); Qwen2.5-Math-7B average pass@128 improves from 82.85 to 84.00. On exploration diversity, MEDS consistently scores higher than DAPO on both Within-Step Diversity and Across-Step Diversity (LLM-judged), and achieves lower Top-1 Eigen Ratio values (more spread in logit space). Logit-based clustering achieves 61.2% agreement with LLM-based semantic error clustering, and the ranking of clustering quality across feature construction variants closely mirrors the ranking of downstream task performance. Computational overhead is modest: MEDS takes 9.73 min vs. 8.95 min for DAPO for 100 training steps on Qwen2.5-Math-7B. Strengths: - The core insight—reusing already-computed layer-wise logits as a proxy for reasoning structure—is elegant and practically cheap, adding minimal overhead while enabling history-aware reward shaping that existing entropy-based methods cannot provide. - The theoretical analysis is rigorous: the proof using Chebyshev's rearrangement inequality cleanly establishes that penalizing repeated errors is non-harmful in expectation under the KL-regularized objective, lending principled justification to the heuristic. - The empirical validation is thorough: results span 5 datasets, 3 model scales (1.7B, 7B, 8B), and multiple competing baselines (GRPO, DAPO, entropy-augmented GRPO), with the correlation between clustering quality and downstream performance providing a useful interpretability signal. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The logit aggregation is simple (direct concatenation, last 14 layers), and the paper itself acknowledges this as its primary limitation—more expressive aggregation (e.g., attention-weighted, SAE-based, or trajectory-level) could extract richer reasoning structure signals but is left unexplored. - The 61.2% agreement between logit clustering and LLM-based semantic clustering, while statistically meaningful, implies substantial noise: roughly 39% of cluster assignments disagree with human-interpretable error categories, raising questions about whether the method is truly capturing semantic reasoning patterns or more superficial distributional regularities in the logit space. - Experiments are restricted to mathematical reasoning benchmarks; it is unclear whether the approach generalizes to other RLVR domains (code generation, multi-step planning, RLHF from preference models) where error patterns may be harder to characterize via final-answer-token logits.
Recommended 2604.11297 PDF
#57 — Innovative (68)

Implicit Turn-Wise Policy Optimization for Proactive User-LLM Interaction

Haoyu Wang, Yuxin Chen, Liang Luo, Buyun Zhang, Ellie Dingqiao Wen, Pan Li · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 68 for its well-motivated insight that turn-level aggregation of token-wise implicit rewards provides a natural semantic granularity for multi-turn credit assignment, supported by a clean Bayesian interpretation of the softmax redistribution. The consistent improvements across three diverse tasks and three advantage estimators demonstrate robustness. However, the reliance on simulated rather than real users and the relatively small policy scales tested limit the strength of the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-turn human-AI collaboration—spanning tutoring, document writing, and medical consultation—is critical for deploying useful AI systems, yet aligning LLMs for these tasks via reinforcement learning is fundamentally difficult. The core challenge is reward sparsity: outcome signals arrive only at conversation's end, making credit assignment across turns hard. Existing remedies all fall short in this setting. Human-annotated process reward models (PRMs) require hundreds of thousands of labels; Monte Carlo rollout-based PRMs multiply sample complexity 8-10x; value function estimation diverges under high-variance user dynamics; and LLM-as-a-Judge approaches are too slow and biased for online RL. Token-level implicit PRMs avoid these costs but produce noisy, semantically uninterpretable credit signals that overfit and exhibit high variance. The paper fills the gap between the scalability of implicit PRMs and the semantic coherence needed for multi-turn interactions. Methodology: ITPO (Implicit Turn-wise Policy Optimization) reformulates multi-turn collaboration as a POMDP and introduces turn-level aggregation of token-wise implicit rewards. Each turn's reward is the sum of per-token log-likelihood ratios between an online-updated implicit PRM and a fixed reference model, collapsing the noisy token granularity to the natural semantic unit of a conversational turn. The normalized variant, Norm-ITPO, redistributes the scalar outcome reward across turns via a softmax over turn-level implicit scores, enforcing scale consistency between implicit and outcome rewards. This is given a Bayesian interpretation: the softmax weights equal the posterior probability of each turn being the decisive one under a Boltzmann likelihood. The resulting turn-wise rewards are integrated with standard advantage estimators—PPO (with GAE), GRPO, and RLOO—via a turn-level PPO clipping objective that preserves semantic coherence of entire responses. The implicit PRM is updated online via binary cross-entropy against trajectory-level outcomes, requiring no labels or extra rollouts. Experiments use Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct as the policy, Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct as the user simulator, and are run with VeRL/vLLM on 2-4 H100 GPUs across three tasks: math tutoring (500 MATH problems, accuracy metric), document writing (500 Medium articles, BLEU metric), and medical recommendation (550 MTMedDialog samples, hierarchical diagnosis score). Key Results: Norm-ITPO consistently outperforms all baselines across all three tasks and all three advantage estimators. With RLOO, Norm-ITPO improves the trajectory-level sparse baseline by 34.4% on math tutoring, 12.0% on document writing, and 8.0% on medical recommendation. Compared to PRIME (the strongest prior implicit PRM baseline), Norm-ITPO gains +3.44 accuracy points on math tutoring and +10.24 score on medical recommendation with RLOO. With GRPO, Norm-ITPO achieves 71.74% diagnosis accuracy on medical recommendation vs. 66.41% for the vanilla trajectory baseline. LLM-as-a-Judge provides modest gains but consistently trails online-updated reward models. Training an extra value model on Norm-ITPO rewards (PPO setting) further improves performance: +16.7%, +10.3%, +8.5% over pure outcome-supervised PPO across the three tasks. Turn-wise reward stability is quantified via Spearman correlation with converged values, reaching ~0.8+ within 50-100 steps, compared to token-level rewards that remain noisy throughout. In a human interpretability study on 64 best/worst turn decisions, Norm-ITPO matched 48/64 (75%) and ITPO matched 47/64 (73.4%), far above the 16/64 random baseline and approaching Gemini-3.0-Pro's 58/64 reference. Generalization to larger backbones (Qwen3-4B, Qwen2.5-7B) confirms the method's model-agnostic effectiveness. Strengths: - The insight of aggregating token-level implicit rewards to turn granularity is simple, theoretically grounded (Bayesian credit attribution), and eliminates the need for any labeled process rewards or extra rollouts while being provably more stable than token-level supervision. - The normalization mechanism (Norm-ITPO) cleanly addresses the scale instability between implicit and outcome rewards that plagues raw ITPO, with the clearest gains observed in the PPO setting where a value model must regress on these signals. - Thorough empirical analysis covering stability, consistency, trajectory-level prediction quality, and semantic interpretability, including a carefully controlled human annotation study, gives unusually strong evidence that the learned rewards are meaningful rather than artifacts. Weaknesses / Limitations: - All experiments use an LLM-based user simulator (Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct) rather than real humans; the reliability of simulator-trained policies on real users is untested, and the gap between simulated and human interaction variance is a significant unverified assumption. - The policy backbone is small (3B parameters), and while 7B results are shown on one task, it is unclear whether the method's advantages persist at scales where the policy is itself a more capable implicit reasoner—potentially shrinking the benefit of explicit turn-wise credit assignment. - The method adds an online-updated implicit PRM that must be co-trained with the policy, increasing system complexity and memory footprint relative to simple GRPO/RLOO baselines; sensitivity to the temperature hyperparameter η and β is not thoroughly ablated across tasks.
Recommended 2603.23550 PDF
#58 — Innovative (68)

SkillReducer: Optimizing LLM Agent Skills for Token Efficiency

Yudong Gao, Zongjie Li, Yuanyuanyuan, Zimo Ji, Pingchuan Ma, Shuai Wang · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: SkillReducer defines a genuinely new problem space — skill bloat in LLM agents — and delivers a structure-aware compression framework that meaningfully outperforms flat token-level methods. The counterintuitive 'less-is-more' finding, where compressed skills actually improve task performance, is well-supported by ablation evidence and connects to established context distraction research. The evaluation is unusually rigorous for this domain (600 skills, real CLI validation, cross-model generalization). However, the circular evaluation concern (Gate 2 as both optimizer and evaluator) and the fundamental limitation with example-as-specification skills temper the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM-based coding agents (Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf) rely on "skills" — pre-packaged instruction sets injected into the context window to extend agent behavior. Despite their intent to save tokens, skills as currently authored systematically increase token consumption. A large-scale analysis of 55,315 publicly available skills reveals three categories of waste: 26.4% lack routing descriptions entirely, over 60% of skill body content is non-actionable (background, examples, templates), and reference-heavy skills can inject tens of thousands of tokens per invocation. At current API pricing, a 10,000-token skill costs $0.03–$0.15 per invocation, with teams potentially spending hundreds of dollars monthly on skill content alone. The paper frames this as a "skill bloat" problem — analogous to software bloat — and seeks to automate its elimination without degrading routing or task performance. Methodology: SKILLREDUCER is a two-stage, training-free optimization framework. Stage 1 targets routing descriptions: it first applies delta debugging (DDMIN) over semantically segmented description clauses with a simulated routing oracle (TF-IDF distractors + LLM-generated adversarial shadow skill) to find the 1-minimal clause set that preserves routing. A second validation phase deploys the compressed description in the real Claude Code CLI to catch over-compressed cases, with selective clause restoration as a fallback. Missing descriptions are synthesized from skill bodies by extracting three routing signals (capability, trigger, unique identifiers). Stage 2 targets skill bodies via taxonomy-driven progressive disclosure: an LLM classifier (DeepSeek-V3, temperature 0) segments body content into five categories (core rule, background, example, template, redundant). Core rules are compressed and always loaded; the rest are moved to on-demand reference modules. Cross-file deduplication removes body–reference overlap. Two quality gates enforce correctness: Gate 1 checks concept faithfulness (all operational concepts preserved); Gate 2 runs task-based evaluation under three conditions — no skill, original, compressed — using a mix of deterministic pytest verifiers (52.3%) and LLM rubric scoring (47.7%). A feedback loop promotes non-core items to core if Gate 2 reveals failures, iterating at most twice, with proven monotone convergence. Key Results: Evaluated on 600 skills across SkillHub, community, and wild GitHub sources. Stage 1 achieves 48% mean description compression (56.5% for compress-only, 44.6% for generated-then-compressed), with 100% routing preservation across all 536 non-obsolete skills after selective restore. Stage 2 achieves 39% mean body token reduction (~1,000 tokens/skill saved); on SkillsBench, body tokens drop from 359K to 84K (75%). Counter-intuitively, compressed skills outperform originals on task evaluation (0.742 vs. 0.722, p=0.002, d=0.107) — a "less-is-more" effect where removing non-essential content reduces context distraction, particularly pronounced for longer skills (+11.8pp for official curated skills). The pass rate (scoreC ≥ scoreA) is 86.0%; only 4.7% of skills represent true compression failures. SKILLREDUCER significantly outperforms four baselines at equal token budget: LLMLingua (retention 0.820), direct LLM compression (0.918), truncation (0.845), and random removal (0.750). Cross-model generalization yields mean retention of 0.965 across five models from four families (Qwen3-max, DeepSeek-V3, Qwen2.5-7B, GLM-5, GPT-OSS-120B); cross-framework validation on OpenCode achieves retention 0.944. Strengths: - The structure-aware approach (taxonomy classification + delta debugging) meaningfully outperforms flat token-level compression methods like LLMLingua, with statistically significant margins (p ≤ 0.003), and the key insight that perplexity-based pruning discards operationally critical but linguistically predictable tokens is well-motivated. - The less-is-more empirical finding — compressed skills improve average task scores — is non-obvious and empirically grounded with ablation evidence; it connects to prior work on context distraction (Liu et al., Shi et al.) and has practical implications for skill authoring guidelines. - Evaluation is unusually rigorous for this domain: 600 skills, 3,000 generated tasks, real-environment routing validation via Claude Code CLI, hybrid scoring with >50% deterministic verifiers (κ=0.939 vs. LLM judge), cross-model and cross-framework testing, and an independent external benchmark (SkillsBench). Weaknesses / Limitations: - Gate 2 serves dual roles as both the optimization objective and the evaluation criterion — the 86.0% pass rate directly reflects optimization-to-criterion rather than held-out generalization. The feedback loop is specifically designed to maximize Gate 2 performance, making the primary metric partially circular; the independent SkillsBench only partially addresses this since it shows a ceiling effect (D already passes 86/87 tasks). - The taxonomy-driven classification fails in a systematic and acknowledged failure mode: when skill authors embed implicit behavioral specifications within examples ("example-as-specification"), the classifier moves these to reference modules, breaking functionality. This is a fundamental limitation — the paper does not offer a solution, only detection via the feedback loop, which recovers only 81.6% of such cases. - Generalization to platforms other than Claude Code (Cursor, Windsurf) is explicitly untested, and the real-environment validation in Stage 1 is tightly coupled to the Claude Code CLI and its stream event API, limiting portability of the full pipeline.
Recommended 2603.29919 PDF
#59 — Innovative (68)

EdgeFlow: Fast Cold Starts for LLMs on Mobile Devices

Yongsheng Yan, Jiacheng Shen, Xuchuan Luo, Yangfan Zhou · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: EdgeFlow delivers a cohesive co-design across three distinct system layers (quantization, SIMD unpacking, CPU-NPU scheduling) unified by a clearly identified root cause — flash bandwidth underutilization during cold start. The closed-form relative error metric for per-channel bit-width allocation is a non-trivial theoretical contribution with provably optimal greedy allocation, elevating this beyond pure systems engineering. However, the core insight of mixed-precision quantization for bandwidth savings is well-established, and the tight coupling to Qualcomm Hexagon limits generalizability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deploying LLMs on mobile devices is attractive for privacy and offline use, but cold-start latency — the delay from invocation to first token when model weights are not in memory — remains a critical unsolved problem. The state-of-the-art NPU-based framework llm.npu incurs 9.1 seconds TTFT for Llama3 8B under a 128-token prompt, an order of magnitude worse than conventional app cold starts (~1s) and well above the ~7s user patience threshold. The root cause identified in this paper is suboptimal flash bandwidth utilization: existing frameworks apply uniform quantization (e.g., all weights to INT8), wasting bandwidth on unimportant parameters that could be quantized more aggressively. Methodology: EdgeFlow is a mobile LLM inference framework that co-designs a quantization algorithm with two system-level optimizations. (1) NPU-Aware Adaptive Quantization: assigns per-output-channel bit-widths (1–8 bits) guided by a relative error metric — the cosine distance between original and dequantized weight channels, approximated in closed form as RE(W_i, B) ≈ (max|W_i|)² / (2^{2B} · E[W_i²]). A greedy O(C log C) heap-based algorithm finds the optimal bit budget allocation. An NPU-aware smoothing pass transfers inter-channel variance from activations to weights, keeping activations compatible with the NPU's coarse per-tensor quantization requirement, without runtime overhead. (2) SIMD-Friendly Packing Format: decomposes arbitrary-precision weights into 1-, 2-, and 4-bit primitive "weightlets" stored in an interleaved layout that allows ARM Neon SIMD registers to unpack R/8 weights in parallel with only three operations (mask, shift, OR), achieving 0.48 SIMD instructions per weight. Per-channel bit-width metadata is stored as compact INT3. (3) Synergistic Granular Pipeline: schedules at individual operator granularity (rather than coarse subgraph), placing INT8 matmuls on the NPU and lightweight ops (RMSNorm, SwiGLU, residuals) on the CPU. Position-guided priority scheduling prioritizes operators from earlier prompt chunks, unlocking downstream NPU work sooner and reducing NPU bubble rate. A CPU task-stealing mechanism further fills CPU idle time by opportunistically executing NPU-queued tasks when the NPU queue depth exceeds a threshold of 5. Key Results: On a Xiaomi 15 Pro (Snapdragon 8 Elite / Hexagon NPU), EdgeFlow at 5-bit average precision reduces TTFT by 4.07× vs. llama.cpp, 2.40× vs. MNN, and 1.55× vs. llm.npu+ (augmented with materialization and overlapping) with only ~1.91% accuracy drop vs. INT8 baselines. At 4 bits, speedups reach 4.24×/2.53×/1.63× but with 22.48% accuracy degradation; 7-bit achieves near-lossless accuracy (−0.03%) with 3.92×/2.28×/1.47× speedup. Ablation shows the SIMD packing contributes up to 1.36× TTFT reduction, and the synergistic pipeline reduces long-prompt TTFT by 12.7% through bubble elimination (NPU bubble rate cut by 82.2% via priority scheduling). The SIMD unpacking is 6.17× faster than K-Quant. EdgeFlow also reduces energy consumption 12.9% vs. llm.npu despite 17.6% higher average power, due to shorter cold-start duration. Decoding throughput is preserved (1.00–1.13× vs. llm.npu). Adaptive quantization outperforms CMPQ, SmoothQuant, and shadow outlier on WikiText-2 perplexity and five downstream benchmarks at all precisions, with CMPQ the closest competitor (EdgeFlow advantage 0.67–13.55% accuracy at 4–7 bits for Llama3 8B). Strengths: - Holistic co-design that identifies the actual bottleneck (flash bandwidth) and addresses all three resulting sub-challenges (quantization, unpacking, CPU-NPU pipeline) coherently, with each component validated independently in ablation. - Principled quantization: the closed-form relative error metric avoids expensive per-channel search, and the greedy allocation is provably optimal given the additive, monotone structure — a non-trivial theoretical contribution. - Practical implementation maturity: reverse-engineered QNN's proprietary execution graph format to enable dynamic weight loading, suggesting strong systems engineering depth; evaluation across four diverse models and five benchmarks is thorough. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Platform specificity: the entire design is tightly coupled to the Qualcomm Hexagon NPU and QNN SDK; the authors acknowledge adapting to other NPUs (Apple Neural Engine, MediaTek) requires additional engineering effort, and no results on non-Qualcomm hardware are provided. - The relative error metric is activation-unaware at the per-channel level: while NPU-aware smoothing transfers activation variance to weights as a pre-processing step, the bit-width allocation itself ignores input distribution during the greedy search, which may leave accuracy on the table compared to an end-to-end calibration approach, particularly for models with heavy outlier activations. - Evaluation does not report generation latency under token-streaming or multi-turn scenarios that are common in real mobile assistant deployments, nor does it consider quantization-aware training, leaving open whether the accuracy gap at 4 bits is fundamental to post-training methods or specific to their metric.
Search Results 2604.09083 PDF
