#60 — Innovative (68)

Matrix-Game 3.0: Real-Time and Streaming Interactive World Model with Long-Horizon Memory (34 ▲ )

Zile Wang, Zexiang Liu, Jiaxing Li, Kaichen Huang, Baixin Xu, Fei Kang, Mengyin An, Peiyu Wang, Biao Jiang, Yichen Wei, Yidan Xietian, Jiangbo Pei, Liang Hu, Boyi Jiang, Hua Xue, Zidong Wang, Haofeng Sun, Wei Li, Wanli Ouyang, Xianglong He, Yang Liu, Yangguang Li, Yahui Zhou · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 13 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Matrix-Game 3.0 achieves a genuinely novel combination of properties — real-time 720p streaming with minute-long spatiotemporal consistency — that no prior system demonstrates simultaneously. The unified self-attention memory design and error-aware training with exposure bias correction are well-conceived architectural choices. However, the primarily qualitative evaluation without quantitative benchmarks against competitors significantly weakens the scientific contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Interactive world models that simulate complex environments conditioned on user actions require three simultaneous properties: long-horizon spatiotemporal consistency (memory of previously visited locations), high-resolution generation (720p), and real-time streaming inference. Existing systems achieve at most two: short-video diffusion models lack real-time capability; autoregressive few-step causal models (e.g., Matrix-Game 2.0, HY-Gamecraft-2) enable real-time streaming but lack explicit memory for minute-long consistency; scaling-based approaches (LingBot-World) improve consistency via extended context but cannot sustain real-time deployment. No open-source system simultaneously satisfies all three requirements. Methodology: Matrix-Game 3.0 introduces a co-designed framework across data, modeling, and deployment. First, an industrial-scale data engine integrates (i) a UE5 synthetic pipeline with tick-synchronized ground-truth action/pose labels across 10^8+ character variants, (ii) scalable AAA game recording from five titles (GTA V, RDR2, Cyberpunk 2077, Palworld, Hogwarts Legacy) via a four-layer decoupled architecture eliminating human annotation bias, and (iii) four real-world datasets (DL3DV-10K, RealEstate10K, OmniWorld, SpatialVid) re-annotated uniformly. Second, an error-aware interactive base model (Wan2.2-TI2V-5B backbone) trains with imperfect history contexts via an error buffer: prediction residuals δ = x̂_i - x_i are accumulated, and perturbed history latents x̃_i = x_i + γδ are used as conditioning, teaching self-correction against exposure bias. Keyboard actions enter via cross-attention; continuous mouse signals via self-attention. Third, camera-aware long-horizon memory places retrieved memory latents, recent history, and noisy prediction targets into the same unified self-attention space—avoiding the slow convergence of separate memory branches. Camera-aware frustum-overlap retrieval selects view-relevant frames; relative Plücker encoding communicates cross-view geometry; head-wise perturbed RoPE bases (per-head rotary frequencies) prevent periodic positional aliasing that would suppress temporally distant memory. Error injection is applied to both history and memory conditioning latents. Fourth, multi-segment Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) trains a bidirectional student by performing multi-segment self-generated rollouts (k=1–6 segments randomly sampled) with a cold-start single-segment phase, achieving training-inference alignment. Finally, inference acceleration combines INT8 quantization of DiT attention projections, pruned VAE decoder (MG-LightVAE at 50%/75% ratio), and GPU-based sampling-approximate camera retrieval, deployed asynchronously with 8 GPUs for DiT and 1 GPU for VAE decoding. Key Results: The 5B model achieves up to 40 FPS at 720p resolution with stable spatiotemporal consistency over minute-long sequences. Acceleration ablation (Table 1): removing INT8 quantization drops FPS from ~40 to 27.38 (−12.62); removing VAE pruning to 25.79 (−14.21); removing GPU retrieval to 6.60 (−33.40)—GPU retrieval is the dominant bottleneck. VAE ablation (Table 2): 50% pruned MG-LightVAE achieves PSNR 31.84/SSIM 0.99 vs. original 33.79/0.99, with decoder time reduced from 0.76s to 0.30s (2.6× speedup); 75% pruning reaches 0.13s decoder time (5.2× speedup) at PSNR 31.14. Scene revisitation experiments show faithful reconstruction of fine-grained appearance details (textures, object configurations, facade patterns) when re-entering previously visited viewpoints, demonstrating effective long-range memory access. Scaling to a 2×14B MoE model further improves visual quality, dynamic modeling, and generalization across diverse third-person AAA game scenes. Strengths: - Unified self-attention design for memory is a principled architectural choice: placing retrieved memory, history, and prediction targets in the same DiT attention space avoids the convergence instability and feature misalignment of cross-attention memory branches, and is validated qualitatively via frame-level attention visualization. - Comprehensive co-design across data (synthetic + AAA games + real-world with uniform re-annotation), modeling (error-aware training + camera-aware memory + aligned distillation), and deployment (quantization + pruning + GPU retrieval), resulting in a genuinely deployable system rather than a research prototype. - The data engine's tick-level synchronization in UE5 with zero temporal alignment error, and the automated four-layer AAA recording architecture with >99% label accuracy, addresses a critical bottleneck for scalable supervised world model training. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is primarily qualitative with no quantitative benchmarks against competing systems (RELIC, WorldPlay, HY-Gamecraft-2) on established metrics (FVD, action controllability accuracy, temporal consistency scores), making it difficult to rigorously compare memory quality or generation fidelity against prior art. - The ablation study on memory design components is missing—there is no systematic decomposition of the contribution from joint self-attention vs. camera-aware retrieval vs. Plücker encoding vs. head-wise RoPE perturbation, so which design choices drive the observed consistency gains remains unclear. - Real-time deployment requires 9 GPUs (8+1 async), which is expensive for wide consumer or edge deployment; the paper does not explore single-GPU or multi-GPU-efficient configurations.
Recommended 2604.08995 PDF
#61 — Innovative (68)

UniDoc-RL: Coarse-to-Fine Visual RAG with Hierarchical Actions and Dense Rewards (7 ▲ )

Jun Wang, Shuo Tan, Zelong Sun, Tiancheng Gu, Yongle Zhao, Ziyong Feng, Kaicheng Yang, Cewu Lu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: UniDoc-RL contributes a well-designed unified framework that jointly optimizes retrieval, reranking, and visual grounding within a single LVLM via hierarchical RL actions, meaningfully advancing over decoupled pipeline approaches. The dense multi-reward formulation with stage-specific credit assignment is a thoughtful solution to the sparse-reward problem in agentic visual RAG. However, the core components — GRPO, teacher-synthesized trajectories, Qwen base models — are all existing building blocks, and the method is only validated on document/slide corpora with a fixed retriever.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Visual Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) extends Large Vision-Language Models with external image corpora, but existing systems suffer from three interconnected failures. First, off-the-shelf retrievers use generic embedding similarity that misses task-specific visual semantics, causing irrelevant images to pollute the reasoning context. Second, prior approaches passively encode full images, wasting context capacity on redundant background and missing fine-grained details in dense visual documents (charts, tables, scanned slides). Third, RL-based methods trained with sparse outcome-only rewards provide no credit signal for intermediate steps like retrieval and region cropping, making optimization effectively black-box. UniDoc-RL addresses all three deficiencies jointly within a single trainable LVLM agent. Methodology: UniDoc-RL casts visual information acquisition as sequential decision-making in a Thought-Action-Observation (T, A, O) framework with a three-level hierarchical action space: (1) Image Search — the model generates a query string passed to an external retrieval engine that returns a candidate image pool; (2) Precise Selection — the LVLM reranks candidates by inspecting each image and emitting a `<select>` token with the chosen index, bridging the gap between shallow retrieval similarity and task-specific semantics; (3) Visual Perception — the model emits a `<bbox>` coordinate specifying a region of interest, which is cropped and zoom-upsampled before being fed back as a high-resolution observation. Multiple rounds of this Search-Select-Perceive loop are allowed before a final `<answer>` terminates the trajectory. Training uses a two-stage pipeline: SFT cold-start (Qwen2.5-VL as base, full LM fine-tuning, vision tower frozen) on 12,621 trajectories synthesized by a Qwen3-VL-235B teacher, followed by GRPO with group size 5 on 5,537 harder samples. The dense multi-reward combines five terms weighted as λ₁₋₅ = (0.1, 0.1, 0.1, 0.1, 0.6): an NDCG-based retrieval reward aggregated across all search rounds, a binary selection correctness reward (with pseudo-positives when no gold image is retrieved), an IoU-based crop reward comparing predicted bounding boxes to ground-truth layout regions detected by the Mineru parsing tool, a format pattern reward, and a model-based outcome reward using Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct as judge. Ground-truth bounding boxes for the perception reward are obtained by having the teacher agent select from Mineru-detected layout candidates, enabling precise IoU supervision without manual annotation. Key Results: Evaluated on three benchmarks — SlideVQA (multi-image slide QA), ViDoSeek (multi-hop visual document retrieval), and MMLongBench (fine-grained visual document understanding) — UniDoc-RL with Qwen2.5-VL-3B achieves 71.0% overall accuracy versus VRAG-RL's 53.5% (+17.5%), and with Qwen2.5-VL-7B achieves 74.8% versus VRAG-RL's 57.1% (+17.7%). On SlideVQA single-hop, the 7B model reaches 86.3% vs. 69.3% for VRAG-RL. On MMLongBench, UniDoc-RL 7B scores 51.5% (text), 39.6% (table), 32.2% (chart), and 50.0% (figure), substantially above VRAG-RL across all visual modalities. Ablations confirm both components are necessary: adding Precise Selection over search-only raises overall accuracy from 66.6% to 70.0%, and adding Visual Perception further raises it to 71.0%. Incrementally adding each reward component shows consistent monotonic gain, with the full multi-reward (69.9% → 71.0%) clearly outperforming outcome-only baselines. The selection action also increases retrieval recall by 5–11 percentage points across benchmarks (e.g., ViDoSeek: 74.8% → 85.7%). Qualitative analysis shows that after RL, the model crops tight, informative regions rather than the trivial full-image crops observed in the SFT model, and crop invocation frequency aligns with the teacher oracle. Strengths: - The unified end-to-end RL framework simultaneously optimizes retrieval, reranking, and visual grounding within a single LVLM, eliminating the decoupled pipeline weaknesses of prior work. - Dense stage-specific rewards directly address the credit assignment problem, providing informative gradients even for intermediate actions where final outcome reward is uninformative. - The IoU-based perception reward is technically grounded and uses Mineru-derived candidate boxes to convert an otherwise unconstrained bounding-box regression into a well-supervised selection, avoiding the need for separate detection models. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The pseudo-positive strategy for selection reward (substituting the top-retrieved candidate when no gold image is in the pool) introduces potentially noisy supervision that could reinforce a bias toward retriever-preferred images rather than genuinely relevant ones. - The method is tested only on document/slide corpora where a fixed external retriever is available; it is unclear how the coarse search quality bottleneck scales to open-ended web or video retrieval scenarios where retriever recall is lower. - Training requires a 235B teacher model for trajectory synthesis and a 72B judge for reward evaluation, making the full pipeline expensive to reproduce and raising questions about whether the gains transfer to base models substantially weaker than Qwen2.5-VL.
Recommended 2604.14967 PDF
#62 — Innovative (68)

Thinking Diffusion: Penalize and Guide Visual-Grounded Reasoning in Diffusion Multimodal Language Models

Keuntae Kim, Mingyu Kang, Yong Suk Choi · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: This paper makes a valuable diagnostic contribution by identifying and quantifying two failure modes in diffusion multimodal LLMs—premature answer generation and weak early visual grounding—that were previously uncharacterized. The proposed PSP and VRG interventions are technically sound training-free adaptations of known concepts (positional penalties, classifier-free guidance) to the discrete diffusion setting, delivering credible efficiency gains. However, the techniques are evaluated only on multiple-choice benchmarks with two models, and the core ideas (remasking penalties, CFG-style guidance) are incremental extensions rather than fundamentally new mechanisms.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Diffusion multimodal large language models (dMLLMs) are an emerging class of models that generate all response tokens in parallel via iterative unmasking, offering faster inference than autoregressive (AR) VLMs. However, their reasoning behavior under Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting is poorly understood. This paper identifies two critical failure modes: (1) dMLLMs frequently generate the final answer token at very early diffusion timesteps—before sufficient reasoning has occurred—then construct post-hoc rationales that justify the pre-determined answer; and (2) dMLLMs show anomalously low visual prompt dependency (PDM) in early timesteps, unlike AR VLMs which heavily leverage visual features from the start. These pathologies mean that naively applying AR-designed CoT methods (CCoT, DDCoT) to dMLLMs yields little benefit. Methodology: The paper proposes two complementary, training-free inference-time interventions. Position & Step Penalty (PSP) modifies the remasking confidence scores by multiplying each token's score by a factor that depends jointly on its normalized position within the response sequence and the current diffusion timestep progress. Tokens at later positions (i.e., near where a final answer would appear) receive a heavier penalty in early timesteps, making them less likely to be unmasked prematurely. As the diffusion process advances (τᵢ → 1), the penalty diminishes, allowing answer tokens to emerge naturally after reasoning tokens have been laid down. Visual Reasoning Guidance (VRG) adapts classifier-free guidance to the discrete diffusion setting: it computes logits both with and without the visual prompt in parallel, then amplifies the gap to strengthen visual conditioning—analogous to CFG in image diffusion. PSP and VRG are composed at the confidence-score level, so they integrate cleanly with any existing remasking strategy (Low-confidence, Entropy, Margin). Neither method requires fine-tuning or architectural changes. Key Results: Evaluated on M3CoT, MMBench, ScienceQA-IMG, and V* Bench using LaViDa and MMaDA-8B, PSP & VRG consistently outperform all baselines including remasking strategies and VLM CoT methods across every tested generation-length/step combination. On MMBench, LaViDa with PSP & VRG at 64 tokens / 32 steps reaches 74.9% accuracy—matching or exceeding the Low-conf baseline at 256 tokens / 128 steps (74.3%), delivering >3× inference speedup. MMaDA gains up to 7.5% accuracy on MMBench (59.9% vs. 55.7% for Low-conf at 256/128). On M3CoT, LaViDa improves from 45.8% to 48.4% (+2.6 pp) at the 64/32 setting. Ablations confirm that PSP and VRG each contribute independently, and their combination is additive. PSP also outperforms a left-to-right (L2R) forced unmasking baseline, indicating the benefit is not simply from enforcing sequential ordering. VRG measurably increases PDM throughout the diffusion process, confirming it genuinely strengthens visual grounding. Time overhead: PSP adds negligible cost (~0.5%); VRG adds ~15–50% wall-clock time due to the dual forward pass, but the combined method still achieves better accuracy at 64/32 than baselines at 256/128. Strengths: - The diagnostic contribution is valuable: first quantitative characterization of Early Answer Generation and weak early visual grounding in dMLLMs, grounded in the PDM metric. - Both methods are training-free, architecture-agnostic, and plug-and-play with any remasking strategy—making adoption low-friction. - Efficiency claim is credible and practically important: PSP alone adds essentially zero compute overhead while delivering consistent gains; the combined method achieves superior quality at 4× fewer diffusion steps. Weaknesses / Limitations: - VRG requires a second forward pass (unconditional, dropping the visual prompt), which raises inference cost by ~15–50%—partially undercutting the efficiency narrative when both components are used together. - The method is evaluated only on multiple-choice benchmarks (M3CoT, MMBench, ScienceQA, V* Bench) where answers are short categorical tokens; it is unclear how PSP's positional penalty would generalize to open-ended generation where the "answer" position is not predefined. - The paper tests only two dMLLMs (LaViDa, MMaDA), both of which are fine-tuned specifically for CoT reasoning; generalization to base dMLLMs or other architectures (e.g., DiMPLE) is not demonstrated in the main paper. - The analysis attributes Early Answer Generation to a fundamental property of the diffusion mechanism, but does not investigate whether it is partly an artifact of the CoT fine-tuning protocol used by these specific models.
Citations 2604.05497 PDF
#63 — Innovative (68)

Reasoning Resides in Layers: Restoring Temporal Reasoning in Video-Language Models with Layer-Selective Merging

Zihang Fu, Haonan Wang, Jian Kang, Kenji Kawaguchi, Jiaying Wu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 68 for the genuinely insightful finding that temporal reasoning localizes to specific self-attention layers in video-language models, validated through both downstream performance and independent interventional masking. The training-free, low-data MERIT framework is practical and shows strong cross-benchmark generalization. However, the underlying techniques—model merging and evolutionary search—are well-established, and the model-specific nature of discovered recipes limits scalability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Video-language models (VLMs) are typically built by attaching a visual encoder to a pretrained LLM, but multimodal adaptation erodes the temporal reasoning (TR) capabilities inherited from language-only pretraining. This degradation manifests in two ways: the adapted VLM fails on text-only temporal logic questions that the base LLM answers correctly, and it cannot infer causal-temporal event structure from video even when it correctly perceives the relevant visual entities. The root cause is that multimodal training is dominated by spatial perception and object-level alignment, providing weak supervision for temporal abstraction and causal inference. Existing training-free remedies apply uniform full-model merging between the VLM and its text backbone, which is too coarse-grained and frequently trades reasoning recovery for perceptual degradation. Methodology: The paper proposes MERIT (Merging for Enhanced Reasoning In Temporal Tasks), a training-free framework that searches for a layer-wise merging recipe between a VLM and its paired text-only backbone. The key insight is that temporal reasoning is localized to a subset of self-attention layers, so selectively restoring those layers recovers reasoning without harming perception. Each candidate recipe is parameterized by a continuous gating vector g ∈ [0,1]^L (one dimension per self-attention layer), thresholded at 0.5 to produce a discrete binary layer assignment. At each selected layer, parameters are interpolated as a weighted blend of VLM and LLM weights controlled by α ∈ {0.5,...,1.0}, with the gate determining which model is dominant. The search objective maximizes temporal reasoning accuracy while penalizing drops in temporal perception relative to the base VLM: F(g) = Acc_TR(g) − λ·max(0, Acc_TP_base − Acc_TP(g)). Because this objective is non-differentiable, MERIT uses CMA-ES evolutionary search, caching both discrete recipe scores and visual encoder outputs (since only the language backbone is modified) for a 2-3× speedup. The search uses only 55 temporal perception and 177 temporal reasoning examples from a Video-MME subset. α is swept over six values with independent searches, and the best recipe is selected. Key Results: MERIT is evaluated on three VLMs (LongVA-7B, InternVL3-8B, Qwen3-VL-4B) across five benchmarks. On Video-MME, MERIT achieves TR gains of +23.9% for LongVA-7B (40.1→49.7%), +10.8% for InternVL3-8B (52.0→57.6%), and +3.8% for Qwen3-VL-4B (45.2→46.9%), while preserving or improving temporal perception in all cases. Gains transfer to four held-out benchmarks without re-search: on Video-Holmes, LongVA-7B improves from 15.2 to 18.9 (+24.3%) and Qwen3-VL-4B from 31.5 to 32.9 (+4.4%); on LVBench, Qwen3-VL-4B reasoning improves from 39.3 to 46.3 (+17.8%) and MMBench-Video TR from 1.08 to 1.38 (+27.8%). Uniform ALL-LAYER merging and random layer selection (RANDOM-K, matched layer count) both consistently underperform MERIT, with RANDOM-K exhibiting high variance. Interventional masking confirms mechanistic importance: suppressing MERIT-selected layers degrades Reasoning far more than Overall or non-reasoning metrics (e.g., LongVA-7B at κ=0.4: Reasoning −35.1%, Others −2.7%). Frame-level gradient-activation attribution shows the merged model shifts attention from salient but misleading local events toward temporally distributed, causally informative evidence. Strengths: - The core hypothesis—that temporal reasoning is localized in specific self-attention layers—is validated both through downstream performance and through independent interventional masking, making the scientific contribution more credible than a pure empirical result. - Training-free and low data requirement: the search uses only 232 labeled examples and no gradient computation, making MERIT practical for any VLM paired with its LLM backbone. - Strong generalization: recipes discovered on a small Video-MME subset transfer consistently to four structurally distinct benchmarks, indicating the method recovers a genuine capability rather than overfitting. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Discovered recipes are model-specific with no apparent transferability across architectures, limiting scalability; practitioners must run a fresh evolutionary search for each new VLM-backbone pair, which requires 1,600 model evaluations per α value. - The method only merges self-attention parameters and ignores MLP layers, feed-forward blocks, and cross-attention components, leaving open whether a richer search space would yield larger gains or reveal different structure. - Gains are modest or inconsistent for Qwen3-VL-4B (only +3.8% TR on Video-MME, −0.8% overall), and the method relies on benchmark-defined TR/TP signals, so performance depends heavily on how these task categories are operationalized in the search set.
Recommended 2604.11399 PDF
#64 — Innovative (68)

Memory Dial: A Training Framework for Controllable Memorization in Language Models

Xiangbo Zhang, Ali Emami · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: The matched-family experimental design that turns memorization into a controllable independent variable is a genuine methodological contribution, enabling causal attribution where only post-hoc correlation existed before. The stability of generalization across the full α sweep is convincingly demonstrated across architectures, benchmarks, and languages. However, the artificial injection protocol and extremely short training runs limit ecological validity, and the inability to characterize baseline memorization from standard training reduces practical relevance for contamination and privacy analysis.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Memorization in language models is consequential — it underlies verbatim reproduction of training data, benchmark contamination, and the tension between retaining useful factual knowledge versus leaking private or copyrighted content. Yet all existing approaches to studying memorization are post-hoc: they probe or measure memorization in already-trained models but cannot isolate its causal role from confounding factors such as architecture, data distribution, and optimization dynamics. When two models differ in memorization behavior, it is impossible to attribute downstream differences specifically to memorization pressure versus other co-varying factors. This makes memorization a dependent variable rather than an experimentally controllable independent variable — a fundamental barrier to mechanistic understanding. Methodology: MEMORY DIAL introduces a training objective that interpolates between standard cross-entropy loss (L_std) and a temperature-sharpened variant (L_mem) via a scalar coefficient α ∈ [0,1]: L_MD = (1−α)·L_std + α·L_mem, where L_mem sharpens the softmax distribution using a fixed temperature τ (defaulting to 0.1). At α=0 training is standard; as α increases, the gradient signal is amplified for high-confidence predictions via a 1/τ scaling factor, creating a rich-get-richer dynamic that selectively reinforces frequently seen examples. The key contribution is not the sharpening term itself but the matched-family experimental design: training multiple models with identical architecture, data, and optimizer but varying only α, producing a controlled sweep where behavioral differences are attributable solely to memorization pressure. Evaluation uses 50 injected (seen) examples and 950 held-out (unseen) examples per benchmark, measured across six architectures (DistilGPT2 82M through OPT-27B) and five benchmarks (ARC, BoolQ, PIQA, COPA, OpenBookQA). Key Results: All 30 model-benchmark combinations yield positive seen-accuracy slopes as α increases (ranging 0.03–0.38), while unseen accuracy remains essentially flat (differences of <0.5 percentage points across the full α range). The perplexity gap (PPL_unseen − PPL_seen) decreases monotonically from 5.70 at α=0 to 0.58 at α=1.0, providing independent confirmation of memorization control. Larger models are more responsive: OPT-27B achieves a mean seen-accuracy slope of 0.206 versus 0.097 for DistilGPT2. Frequency-stratified analysis shows high-frequency sequences have lower suffix NLL (easier to memorize) at all α values; at α=0.8, rare sequences reach NLL=29.7 vs. 27.2 for high-frequency, though rare sequences show the largest absolute improvement (Δ=7.07 vs. 5.19). Output self-similarity (Jaccard) increases monotonically with α (0.372 to 0.528), indicating reduced output diversity under high memorization pressure. A single-temperature cross-entropy baseline fails to reproduce the stable monotonic seen/unseen trade-off. The effect transfers to multilingual settings (XCOPA Turkish and Chinese) and to naturally occurring single-occurrence sequences (suffix NLL drops from 3.42 to 2.94 from α=0 to α=0.6). Strengths: - The matched-family experimental design is the central methodological contribution: it cleanly isolates memorization pressure from all other confounders, enabling causal attribution rather than post-hoc correlation — a genuine gap filled in the literature. - The stability of generalization (unseen accuracy) across the full α sweep is empirically robust and consistent across 6 architectures, 5 benchmarks, multiple τ values, multilingual settings, and extended training horizons, lending high confidence to the core claim. - The gradient-level analysis (Section 3.2) provides a principled mechanistic explanation for why the objective selectively amplifies memorization for repeated sequences while leaving generalization largely intact, grounding the empirical results in theory. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The injection protocol — explicitly inserting 50 benchmark examples into the training stream with a 75% leak sampling rate — is an artificial and ecologically unrealistic construction. It does not model how memorization actually emerges from large-scale pretraining on web corpora, limiting the external validity of the findings for understanding real LLM behavior. - The experiments are conducted with very short training runs (449 optimization steps, effectively a single pass over a small constructed stream from a 10–20B token pool), using continued pretraining rather than training at scale. Generalizing conclusions about memorization dynamics — especially scaling behavior — to frontier LLMs trained for billions of steps on trillions of tokens is not demonstrated and may not hold. - α controls additional memorization pressure above the standard-training baseline rather than spanning the full range from zero to maximal memorization. The framework therefore cannot characterize the baseline memorization already induced by ordinary training, which is arguably the more important quantity in practice for contamination and privacy analysis.
Recommended 2604.05074 PDF
#65 — Innovative (68)

Pipelining Network and Compute for Real-Time LLM Serving

dl.acm.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: RabbitLLM identifies a genuinely underexplored cross-layer co-design opportunity, making network transport events first-class scheduling signals for incremental KV-cache construction. The architectural insight of coupling RTP frame arrival with progressive prefilling is novel and well-executed. However, the paper conflates transport protocol gains (switching from bulk TCP to WebRTC) with the actual pipelining contribution, making it difficult to isolate the true architectural benefit, and improvements diminish significantly for longer, compute-bound videos.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Real-time interaction with multimodal LLMs (M-LLMs) processing video is impractical due to a "receive-then-process" barrier: the full video must be uploaded and preprocessed before inference can begin, causing GPU cold-start and compounding delays across transmission, prefilling, and generation. Empirically, network transmission accounts for ~95% of total end-to-end latency under 4G/5G conditions (>90 experimental runs), meaning that even powerful accelerators sit idle during the entire upload phase. Prior work on streaming-aware M-LLMs (VideoLLM-online, TimeChat) abstracts away the network layer and assumes data is locally buffered, while LLM serving optimizations (chunked prefilling, PagedAttention) assume full prompts are already available server-side. Neither layer has been co-designed with the other. Methodology: RabbitLLM couples progressive WebRTC/RTP video transport with transport-triggered incremental KV-cache construction. Instead of treating transport as a pre-inference stage, each arriving RTP video frame immediately triggers an incremental prefill step, appending its visual embeddings as Cache_t = prefill(Cache_{t-1}, F_t). The system uses a producer-consumer architecture: the RTP receiver enqueues frame events into a bounded thread-safe queue; an inference worker serializes KV-cache commits in strict monotonic order (Cache_0 → Cache_1 → … → Cache_n) even under bursty or reordered UDP delivery. A bounded reordering buffer enforces prefix consistency before committing; sparse frame losses are skipped rather than retransmitted to avoid stalls. The four-stage workflow is: (1) base prefill of the system prompt at session start, (2) incremental frame prefilling during transmission, (3) tail prefilling of the user query after the last frame, and (4) deferred decoding only after a warm cache is fully constructed. The architecture targets MiniGPT4-Video but is transport-agnostic and generalizes to any M-LLM stack supporting incremental prefilling. Key Results: Evaluated on a server with two NVIDIA A4500 GPUs using MiniGPT4-Video with LLaMA-7B-Chat and Mistral-7B-Instruct, under real-world 4G/5G network traces (Lumous4G, Lumous5G, Ghent4G) with 100ms base delay and 2% packet loss. RabbitLLM reduces average end-to-end delay by 43.5% overall (330s → 186s). For 1-minute videos specifically, the reduction exceeds 70% (267s → 78s for LLaMA; 301s → 89s for Mistral). For 5-minute videos the gain is more modest at 24-28%, as compute increasingly dominates. Per-frame transmission delay falls 95.4% (0.72s → 0.033s) and average bandwidth consumption drops 99.1% (50.5 Mbps → 0.456 Mbps) — the latter largely a consequence of progressive frame-paced delivery versus bulk TCP upload. Output quality is preserved: BERTScore F1 consistently exceeds 0.82 across all models, durations, and resolutions, indicating that incremental prefilling with frame skipping under loss does not materially degrade semantic fidelity. Strengths: - Identifies and exploits a previously underexplored cross-layer co-design opportunity: making transport events first-class scheduling signals for inference, which is architecturally distinct from independently optimizing either the network or compute layer. - The correctness mechanism (serialized KV-cache commits via a producer-consumer queue, bounded reordering buffer, and skip-on-sparse-loss policy) is well-reasoned and handles real UDP delivery challenges without sacrificing semantic continuity. - Evaluation uses realistic heterogeneous network traces across multiple codecs, resolutions, video durations, and two different 7B LLMs, lending confidence that gains are not trace- or model-specific artifacts. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The 99.1% bandwidth reduction and most of the per-frame transmission gains are attributable to the switch from bulk TCP upload to WebRTC's frame-paced progressive delivery, not to the KV-cache pipelining itself. The paper partially acknowledges this but conflates transport protocol benefits with the architectural contribution, making it difficult to isolate the net gain of incremental prefilling over simply using WebRTC without it. - For longer videos (5 minutes), end-to-end improvement shrinks to 24-28%, and the paper acknowledges compute increasingly dominates — but does not quantify when pipelining becomes negligible or propose mitigations (e.g., adaptive frame subsampling, speculative decoding) for compute-bound regimes. - Evaluation is limited to a single M-LLM architecture (MiniGPT4-Video) on a controlled server setup; real-world deployment considerations such as multi-tenant GPU contention, larger models (e.g., 70B), or edge/mobile inference are not addressed.
Search Results
#66 — Innovative (68)

Many-Tier Instruction Hierarchy in LLM Agents (13 ▲ )

Jingyu Zhang, Tianjian Li, William Jurayj, Hongyuan Zhan, Benjamin Van Durme, Daniel Khashabi · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 68 for cleanly isolating a genuine and previously uncharacterized capability gap—the fixed-tier bottleneck—with rigorous experimental controls that decouple IH complexity from instruction-following difficulty. The sensitivity analyses revealing brittleness to privilege representation format and minor scalar perturbations are non-obvious findings with direct deployment implications. The benchmark construction is methodologically strong, particularly the deterministic AST-based checkers for the coding subset. Loses points because, like the multi-user paper, it is entirely diagnostic with no training method, architectural fix, or even prompting strategy proposed, and the dual-use adversarial risk of the Privilege Prompt Interface is acknowledged but unaddressed.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM agents operating in agentic settings receive instructions from heterogeneous sources—system prompts, user messages, tool outputs, sub-agents, skill files—each with different levels of trust. When instructions conflict, models must follow the highest-privilege one. The dominant paradigm, Instruction Hierarchy (IH), assigns privilege via a small, fixed set of role labels (system > developer > user > tool) hardcoded during training. This creates a fixed- and few-tier bottleneck: real-world deployments expose agents to far richer privilege structures—organizational hierarchies in group chats, heterogeneous third-party tools, multi-agent pipelines—where multiple instruction sources share the same role label yet have meaningfully different authority. The gap between the ≤5 tiers models are trained on and the arbitrarily many tiers encountered in practice is the central problem this paper addresses. Methodology: The paper proposes Many-Tier Instruction Hierarchy (ManyIH), which decouples instruction privilege from chat-template role labels by introducing a Privilege Prompt Interface (PPI): each instruction is decorated with an explicit privilege tag in the input text, and a meta-instruction tells the model to resolve conflicts by comparing tag values rather than message roles. Two PPI variants are proposed: an ordinal interface using ranked integers (lower integer wins, e.g., [[Privilege 1]] > [[Privilege 2]]) and a scalar interface using arbitrary positive integers (higher value wins, e.g., [[z=91]] > [[z=61]]). Conflict resolution is purely based on relative ordering; absolute magnitudes carry no semantic meaning by design. To evaluate this paradigm, the authors construct MANYIH-BENCH, an 853-sample benchmark with two complementary subsets. The coding subset (427 samples) pairs MBPP Python programming tasks with manually curated conflicting style instructions (12 style groups, 41 distinct constraints) verified by deterministic AST/token checkers, with up to 12 privilege levels per sample. The instruction-following subset (426 samples) augments real agentic prompts from AgentIF across 46 agent domains: Claude Sonnet 4.6 identifies conflictable instructions, Claude Opus 4.6 generates mutually exclusive conflicting instructions with evaluation rules, and a second Opus 4.6 pass verifies absence of cross-group conflicts and infeasibility. Human annotators validated 100 randomly sampled constraint-checker tuples (81% faithful). Evaluation is strict all-or-nothing: a sample passes only if all active (winning) instructions are satisfied and all code unit tests pass. Key Results: Even frontier models fail dramatically on MANYIH-BENCH. The best-performing model, Gemini 3.1 Pro, achieves only 42.7% overall accuracy, followed by GPT-5.4 at 39.5% and Claude Opus 4.6 at 33.2%. The best coding-subset accuracy is 60.9% (GPT-5.4 at high reasoning effort). This stands in stark contrast to >99% accuracy these same models report on standard two-tier IH evaluations (e.g., GPT-5 system card). Performance degrades monotonically as instruction tiers increase: across six models on the coding subset, accuracy drops between 6.8% (Qwen3.5-9B) and 24.1% (Sonnet 4.6) when moving from 6 tiers with no conflicts to 12 tiers with up to 11 conflicts, while holding instruction-following difficulty fixed. Models are highly sensitive to privilege representation format: switching from ordinal to scalar PPI causes GPT-5.4 and Opus 4.6 to drop 8.4% and 8.0% respectively despite identical semantic orderings. Small perturbations (δ ∈ [-3,+3]) to scalar privilege values while preserving strict ordering produce non-trivial per-sample flip rates of ≥8% in five of six models tested. Style compliance (resolving privilege-based conflicts) is the primary bottleneck—frontier models maintain >86% functional code correctness but far lower style accuracy. Longer chain-of-thought reasoning does not reliably improve performance: GPT-5.4 with ~1K median CoT tokens outperforms Qwen3.5-397B with ~7K tokens. Strengths: - Clearly identifies a genuine capability gap in current LLMs—the fixed-tier bottleneck—with clean experimental controls that isolate IH complexity from instruction-following difficulty - Benchmark construction is methodologically rigorous: coding subset uses fully deterministic checkers, conflicts are scoped within style groups by construction, and privilege is decoupled from position; the instruction-following subset uses multi-stage LLM generation with human validation - The sensitivity analyses (ordinal vs. scalar PPI, scalar value perturbation) reveal non-obvious brittleness in frontier models that has direct implications for deployment robustness - Breadth of evaluation across 10 frontier and open-source models (including Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.4, Opus 4.6, Grok 4.20, Kimi K2.5, Qwen 3.5 at four sizes) provides a comprehensive empirical picture Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper identifies and benchmarks the problem but proposes no training method, architectural fix, or prompting strategy to improve ManyIH performance—it is entirely diagnostic, leaving the improvement problem entirely to future work - The proposed Privilege Prompt Interface introduces a dual-use adversarial risk (malicious instructions tagged with high privilege values) that the paper explicitly defers to future work, which is a significant open safety concern for deployment - The instruction-following subset's evaluation relies partly on LLM judges (Claude Opus 4.6) to check constraint satisfaction, and the human validation study reveals an 19% rate of unclear or incorrect evaluation functions—this injects noise into the benchmark's ground truth and may artifically cap attainable accuracy below the claimed ~80% ceiling
Recommended 2604.09443 PDF
#67 — Innovative (68)

Structured Causal Video Reasoning via Multi-Objective Alignment (7 ▲ )

Zinuo Li, Yongxin Guo, Jun Liu, Jiawei Zhan, Xi Jiang, Chengjie Wang, Mohammed Bennamoun, Farid Boussaid, Feng Zheng, Qiuhong Ke · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 13 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 68 for its creative synthesis of cognitive event segmentation theory with structured video reasoning and a principled multi-objective RL algorithm. The empirical observation that unstructured thinking hurts video understanding is a valuable negative result, and P-FAB's Pareto-frontier guided advantage balancing addresses a real failure mode in GRPO with theoretical grounding in MGDA. The four-stage curriculum is well-motivated. However, the approach largely assembles existing components (LoRA, GRPO, structured prompting) into a pipeline rather than introducing a fundamentally new paradigm.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) apply Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning in an unstructured manner inherited from text-only settings. This produces verbose, non-linear narratives that bury critical visual cues, exhibit reasoning drift away from the query, and reduce temporal reasoning to isolated frame retrieval rather than causal analysis. The authors empirically observe that reasoning-enhanced "Thinking" models (e.g., Qwen3-VL-4B-Thinking) actually underperform their instruction-tuned counterparts on video benchmarks — a key motivating finding. Drawing on cognitive psychology (event segmentation theory), the paper argues that humans form a structured mental sketch of entities, actions, and event boundaries before engaging in abstract reasoning, and proposes a machine analogue of this process. Methodology: The paper introduces a "Structure-First" paradigm built around three components. First, Structured Event Facts (SEF) are generated prior to reasoning: for each 10–30 second temporal segment, the model outputs a fixed schema of time, person, human action, scene, camera position, and a causal event caption within `<facts>` tags. This acts as an explicit grounding constraint on downstream reasoning. Second, structured causal thinking then proceeds in three steps — Global Search & Localization (scanning facts for query-relevant cues), Causal Verification (antecedent-action-consequence validation with mandatory visual re-verification), and Final Alignment (global consistency check). Third, CausalFact-60K is curated from 32,049 videos across ActivityNet, QVHighlights, COIN, and Charades-STA, using Qwen3-VL-235B and Gemini-2.5-Pro in a mutual quality-judging pipeline, with 50% human inspection. Training proceeds in four progressive stages: facts alignment (Stage 1, SFT), format warm-start (Stage 1.5, SFT to stabilize XML structure), thinking warm-start (Stage 2, SFT with causal reasoning), and RL post-training (Stage 3). For Stage 3, the paper identifies that balancing four competing rewards (Format, Linear IoU, Multi-choice Accuracy, Length) with standard GRPO produces ambiguous gradient directions — identical advantage values for groups with the same average reward. To resolve this, the authors propose Pareto-Frontier guided Advantage Balancing (P-FAB): rewards are centered per group, standardized to remove scale dominance, and combined via the Frank-Wolfe algorithm applied to the MGDA minimum-norm problem over the probability simplex, yielding Pareto-optimal weights that dynamically amplify rare, hard-to-satisfy objectives. The base model throughout is Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct with LoRA for supervised stages and full-parameter updates for RL, at 1 fps and max 256 frames. Key Results: Factum-4B sets new open-source state-of-the-art on temporal grounding despite operating at 1 fps versus competitors at 2 fps with up to 2048 frames. On ActivityNet-Captions it achieves R1@0.5 = 48.4% and R1@0.7 = 28.1%, substantially surpassing Time-R1-7B (39.0% / 21.4%). On Charades-TimeLens it reaches R1@0.7 = 21.6%, the highest among open-source models. On general understanding benchmarks, it attains 64.7% on VideoMME and 73.6% on NExT-GQA (a 7% absolute gain over the Thinking base model). On ETBench it leads 6 of 8 sub-tasks and exceeds GPT-4o on TVG (66.1%) and TEM (26.8%). Ablations confirm that both facts and structured thinking are individually necessary: removing thinking causes the largest collapse (VideoMME drops to 58.5%), while removing facts causes logical drift. P-FAB consistently outperforms standard GRPO, with the performance gap widening at group size G=8 (ActivityNet R1@0.3 margin grows from 1.2% to 2.5%). Strengths: - The core empirical observation — that unstructured thinking hurts video understanding relative to instruction tuning — is credible, reproducible under controlled settings, and provides a genuinely useful negative result for the field. - P-FAB is a principled multi-objective RL solution grounded in MGDA theory, addressing a real and underappreciated failure mode of standard GRPO (directional ambiguity from averaged advantages); the algorithm is clearly specified and convergence-guaranteed. - The four-stage curriculum is carefully motivated: each stage addresses a specific failure mode (hallucinated facts, structural collapse, reasoning drift), and the intermediate Format Warm-Start stage is a pragmatic insight that prevents mode collapse. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Inference overhead of generating structured facts before every reasoning step is not analyzed — in practice this could double or triple output token count, which matters for deployment and benchmark fairness. - The frame-rate asymmetry in comparisons (Factum-4B at 1 fps / 256 frames vs. most baselines at 2 fps / 2048 frames) cuts both ways and is underexplored: superior performance under this constraint is impressive, but the paper does not report what happens at matched settings, leaving headroom questions open. - The quality of the structured facts themselves is never stress-tested: there is no analysis of how often the facts are incorrect, hallucinated, or insufficiently detailed, and no study of downstream reasoning failures that propagate from bad facts — a single point of failure in the pipeline.
Recommended 2604.04415 PDF
#68 — Innovative (68)

Maximal Balanced Quasi-Clique Enumeration in Signed Graphs

dl.acm.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: This paper scores 68 for introducing the first quasi-clique model tailored to signed graphs, addressing a genuine open problem with rigorous theoretical grounding including NP-hardness proof and formal correctness of all pruning strategies. The bipartite candidate set construction based on signed path semantics is an elegant design that tightly couples structural balance theory with combinatorial enumeration. However, the use of artificially signed datasets for half the experiments and the Python-only implementation temper the practical impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Real-world networks such as social trust/distrust graphs, opinion networks, and protein interaction networks are naturally modeled as signed graphs where edges carry positive or negative semantics. Existing quasi-clique (QC) models — a well-studied relaxation of cliques requiring each node to be adjacent to at least a γ fraction of others — are defined exclusively for unsigned graphs and ignore sign information. This leaves an open problem: how to define and efficiently enumerate cohesive subgraphs in signed networks. The paper addresses this gap by proposing the first quasi-clique model tailored for signed graphs, motivated by structural balance theory, which posits that signed networks evolve toward stable configurations where friends-of-friends are friends and enemies-of-enemies are friends. Methodology: The paper introduces the Maximal Balanced (γ₁, γ₂)-Quasi-Clique (MBQC) model. A MBQC Q is a maximal connected subgraph that (1) is balanced — it bipartitions into Q_L and Q_R with positive edges within each part and negative edges between parts — and (2) is quasi-complete — each node in Q_L has positive edges to at least γ₁ fraction of other nodes in Q_L and negative edges to at least γ₂ fraction of nodes in Q_R (and symmetrically for Q_R). The authors prove the enumeration problem is NP-hard (by reduction from maximal balanced clique enumeration). A critical structural challenge is that BQCs lack the hereditary property, making standard Bron-Kerbosch-style pruning inapplicable. The core algorithm, MBQCEnum, uses a set-enumeration search tree framework (partial set X, candidate set C, exclusion set D) with bipartite branching: adding a node to X_L or X_R creates sub-branches. Candidate sets PC(u) and NC(u) — nodes reachable via positive/negative paths of bounded length L — are precomputed in O(nδD) time to restrict the local search space. The optimized variant MBQCEnum* adds four pruning strategies: (I) degree-based pruning that removes candidates whose current plus maximum possible degrees cannot satisfy γ₁/γ₂ thresholds; (II) bound-based pruning using tightened upper and lower bounds (U_{X_L}, L_{X_L}) on how many nodes can be added to each partition side; (III) critical-node pruning that forces inclusion of all neighbors of marginally-feasible nodes; and (IV) a look-ahead technique that early-outputs the full X∪C if it already forms a valid BQC. Preprocessing reduces the graph to its maximum signed core before enumeration. Worst-case time complexity is O(nδD 2^{δD}). Key Results: Experiments on six real-world datasets (ranging from 3.3K to 1.63M nodes, 20K to 22.3M edges) demonstrate: (1) The naive Baseline (separate QC + quasi-biclique mining) times out (>24 hours) on all but the smallest dataset, confirming that MBQC cannot be decomposed into independent subproblems. (2) MBQCEnum* outperforms MBQCEnum by 1–2 orders of magnitude across all datasets, and completes on Pokec (1.63M nodes, 22.3M edges) in ~350–4000 seconds depending on parameters. (3) Ablation study on DBLP and Youtube shows incremental speedups: degree pruning alone gives 1×, +upper-bound gives 2.76–4.64×, +lower-bound gives 10–11×, +critical gives 10.56–13.27× over baseline. Lower-bound pruning is the single most effective component (4.71–3.72× slowdown when removed). (4) In a protein complex discovery case study on SignedPPI, MBQC achieves precision of ~0.75 at γ=0.8 vs. ~0.45 for plain quasi-clique and ~0.35 for clique, demonstrating that incorporating both activation and inhibition structure recovers biologically meaningful complexes that unsigned models miss. Strengths: - Rigorous theoretical foundation: NP-hardness proof, bounded diameter property, and complete correctness proofs for all pruning theorems are provided. - The bipartite candidate set construction (PC/NC) based on signed path semantics is an elegant and principled design that tightly restricts the search space without sacrificing completeness. - Demonstrated scalability to graphs with millions of nodes and edges, with MBQCEnum* successfully completing on Pokec (1.63M nodes) where the baseline cannot — a qualitative leap in practical reach. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The implementation is in Python, which limits raw performance; the paper notes runtimes of hundreds to thousands of seconds on large datasets, and a C++ implementation could dramatically change the scalability story. - Three of six datasets (DBLP, Youtube, Pokec) are unsigned graphs with artificially assigned negative edges (30% random), which weakens the biological or social validity of the experiments on those datasets and makes it unclear how well the model's assumptions translate to synthetic signed structure. - The model requires the user to specify three parameters (γ₁, γ₂, θ) simultaneously, and the number of results is non-monotone in γ₁ (larger γ₁ can yield more results due to fragmentation of large patterns), making parameter tuning non-intuitive and potentially requiring costly exploratory runs.
Citations
#69 — Innovative (68)

R2O: A Dual-Layer Framework for Joint Rewriting and Ordering in Distributed Property Graph Query Optimization

dl.acm.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: R2O scores 68 as the first framework to jointly optimize query rewriting and join ordering for distributed property graph queries via dual-layer reinforcement learning. The partition-aware internal/extended node labeling is a clean mechanism, and the complementary gains from rewriting and ordering are well-demonstrated. The weak baseline set and per-dataset training overhead temper the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Distributed property graph systems partition large graphs across multiple sites and decompose complex pattern queries into subqueries executed independently per partition. Integrating results requires inter-partition joins, which introduce substantial communication overhead. Two root causes drive excess intermediates: (1) some partial matches do not contribute to the final result yet still traverse the network, and (2) some results that are entirely local to one partition are unnecessarily communicated for aggregation. Additionally, the ordering of inter-partition joins strongly determines intermediate result sizes. Existing optimizers—heuristic, cost-based, or learning-based—address either rewriting or ordering in isolation, and none are designed for the distributed property graph setting. R2O fills this gap. Methodology: R2O (Rewriting to Ordering) is a dual-layer reinforcement learning framework inserted between query decomposition and execution. The first layer is a partition-aware query rewriting model. It annotates nodes as internal (full properties, local) or extended (replica, label-only) to identify partition boundaries. For any neighboring pair of subqueries sharing an edge, Algorithm 1 produces: (a) boundary subqueries that restrict matches to cross-partition edges, and (b) a local-join subquery that merges the two when all shared nodes are internal, eliminating unnecessary inter-partition traffic for self-contained matches. The rewriting model is an MDP whose action space is the set of neighboring subqueries to merge; an actor-critic GNN selects rewrites and terminates when further rewriting is unproductive (penalized by a negative reward if relative improvement falls below ε=0.01). The second layer is a global ordering model, also an MDP, that sequences the (possibly rewritten) subqueries. At each step it selects from candidate subqueries neighboring the current plan prefix, guided by GNN embeddings of node/edge features: label frequencies, degree, global node/edge counts, property constraint counts, and partition statistics (replica densities, inter-partition edge ratios). Both models share GNN feature representations but maintain separate actor-critic networks; they are trained jointly end-to-end using actual query execution time as the global reward, with z-score normalization across queries to prevent gradient instability. Training combines offline pretraining on historical queries with online interaction with the database engine. Key Results: Experiments run on a 7-node cluster (Neo4j 3.5 on each sub-site, MPI communication) over four datasets: LDBC-SNB at SF-3/10/30 (up to 540M edges) and DBpedia v2014 (790M edges), partitioned with METIS and MPC. R2O achieves average speedups of 11.61×, 81.29×, 20.99×, and 22.38× over a degree-based heuristic baseline across the four datasets, with a peak of 1637.99×. Stage-wise decomposition on SF-10 (METIS) shows local evaluation time drops from 136.07s to 15.47s and join time from 5.66s to 1.45s. Against GOpt (graph-native rule+cost optimizer adapted to this setting), R2O achieves a 20.23× average speedup and 62× faster plan generation on SF-10. Against gStoreD (distributed SPARQL engine) on DBpedia, R2O achieves up to a 3975× speedup. Ablation on SF-30 shows ordering alone reduces the median execution time 35.62% and rewriting alone 47.29%, while the full framework reduces the median by 95.77% and the mean by 84.66%, confirming that the two modules provide complementary benefits. The learned policy is robust to 10% data updates without retraining. Models converge in 5–12 epochs; training takes up to 12.8 hours for SF-30. Strengths: - First framework to jointly optimize both query rewriting and join ordering for distributed property graph queries, closing a clear gap in prior work. - The partition-aware internal/extended node labeling is a clean, formally grounded mechanism that enables provably correct local-join identification with only O(|N|+|E|) overhead per rewrite. - Empirical gains are substantial and consistent across two structurally different partitioning strategies (METIS and MPC) and four large-scale datasets, including a real-world knowledge graph. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The baseline comparison is weak: the primary competitor is a simple degree-based heuristic; GOpt is evaluated only on a single dataset using a "lightweight variant" rather than a full deployment, and comparisons to gStoreD involve a system model mismatch (RDF vs. property graph) that may not fully reflect head-to-head capability. - Training requires a separate model per dataset-partitioner pair with up to 12.8 hours of training time and only 15 training queries—raising concerns about generalization to diverse or evolving query workloads and practical deployment overhead when graphs or partitioning change significantly. - Rewriting can sometimes degrade performance (e.g., a 16.8× slowdown observed on one query), indicating the local rewriting model does not reliably identify all harmful rewrites; no formal guarantee bounds the worst-case overhead introduced.
Citations
#70 — Innovative (67)

Towards Platonic Representation for Table Reasoning: A Foundation for Permutation-Invariant Retrieval

Willy Carlos Tchuitcheu, Tan Lu, Ann Dooms · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: This paper introduces a genuinely novel diagnostic framework grounded in symmetric group theory and CKA to formally characterize permutation invariance in table embeddings — a problem that has been implicitly acknowledged but never systematically measured. The asymmetry finding that LLMs are far more sensitive to column permutations than row permutations is surprising and actionable. However, the work is explicitly prospective with no end-to-end downstream validation linking improved PI metrics to retrieval or QA accuracy, and the clean-table assumption limits practical applicability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Table Representation Learning (TRL) has overwhelmingly adopted NLP's sequential paradigm, linearizing tables into text strings before embedding. This discards tables' intrinsic geometric and relational structure — most critically, row-column permutation invariance (PI): the semantic content of a table should not change when rows or columns are reordered. In RAG systems, this failure means retrieval quality is corrupted by layout-dependent noise rather than driven by semantic content. The authors argue that no systematic, metric-based framework exists to diagnose this "serialization bias" at the embedding level, and that existing benchmarks (BRIGHT, TableBench) expose a performance plateau traceable to this structural fragility. Methodology: The paper adapts the Platonic Representation Hypothesis (PRH) — originally proposed for general vision-language alignment — to the table domain, positing that a semantically faithful table embedding must be intrinsically permutation-invariant. The core diagnostic framework generates a controlled test bench of (n+1)(m+1) permuted table variants parameterized by the number of fixed rows (a) and columns (b), spanning from full derangement T^0_0 to identity T^n_m. Embeddings are compared using Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA), which is invariant to isotropic scaling and orthogonal transformations, producing a "Platonic heatmap" H[a,b] ∈ [0,1]^(n+1)×(m+1). Two scalar metrics are derived: (i) PI_derange = H[0,0], quantifying invariance under complete structural derangement; and (ii) ρ_mono, a Spearman correlation measuring whether similarity increases monotonically as structural constraints are restored. A normalized AUC along row and column axes completes the suite. The paper evaluates five LLM baselines (GPT-3.5, nomic-embed-text, Gemma2, DeepSeek-R1, Llama3) against a structure-aware TRL encoder based on Semantic Meta-Paths (SMP), which explicitly enforces header–cell alignment via local (intra-SMP) and global (inter-SMP) contrastive objectives. Key Results: Across both a single-table benchmark (169 permutations of a 12×12 WikiSQL table) and a multi-table benchmark (3,791 tables from 20 base tables), LLM-derived embeddings show severe column-order sensitivity. For GPT-3.5, PI_derange drops to 0.42 for column permutations vs. 0.77 for row permutations; ρ_mono is near zero (0.17 overall), indicating no consistent recovery as structure is restored. TRL-refined embeddings consistently improve all metrics: Gemma2's column PI_derange improves from 0.22 to 0.61; DeepSeek-R1 ρ_mono improves from 0.23 to 0.31; normalized AUC increases from 0.22 (Llama3, columns) to 0.56 (Gemma2, TRL). A notable finding: the 270M-parameter nomic-embed-text rivals or exceeds 175B-parameter GPT-3.5 on several PI metrics, suggesting embedding architecture and training objective matter more than parameter count. TRL's cross-model setting also achieves higher CKA scores than LLM baselines, indicating more universal geometric structure. Strengths: - The CKA-based diagnostic framework is model-agnostic, formally grounded via symmetric group theory, and provides interpretable scalar summaries (PI_derange, ρ_mono, AUC) that can practically guide embedder selection for table-RAG systems. - The asymmetry finding — that LLMs are far more sensitive to column permutations than row permutations — is a non-obvious, actionable insight with direct implications for serialization format design. - The empirical breadth (5 LLM families across decoder-only and encoder-only architectures, tested over nearly 4,000 permuted tables) provides strong statistical grounding for the claims. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation is entirely intrinsic — no end-to-end retrieval or QA benchmark demonstrates that higher PI_derange or ρ_mono actually translates to improved downstream RAG performance. The link from embedding geometry to retrieval accuracy remains unvalidated. - The framework assumes clean, fully structured tables with simple row/column headers; real-world tables with merged cells, hierarchical headers, or spanning rows break the symmetric group action formalism, leaving applicability to practical corpora unclear. - The paper is explicitly prospective/position-paper in nature: the OTQA and cross-table referencing applications are speculative, with no working implementation or quantitative evaluation presented.
Search Results 2604.12133 PDF
#71 — Innovative (67)

GlobalSplat: Efficient Feed-Forward 3D Gaussian Splatting via Global Scene Tokens (15 ▲ )

Roni Itkin, Noam Issachar, Yehonatan Keypur, Yehonatan Keypur, Anpei Chen, Sagie Benaim · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: The 'align first, decode later' paradigm is a clean conceptual contribution that naturally yields view-invariant representation size — a property no dense baseline achieves. The efficiency gains (16x less GPU memory, 35x smaller disk footprint vs. ZPressor) are practically significant. However, the fixed 16K Gaussian budget and evaluation limited to 256x256 resolution on room-scale video sequences constrain the demonstrated generality, and the core idea of learned latent bottlenecks is well-established in other domains.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) methods for novel view synthesis (NVS) suffer from a fundamental scalability problem: they decode scene primitives from dense, view-aligned or pixel-aligned intermediates (per-pixel depth/features, voxel grids), merging them globally only afterward. As the number of input views grows, this view-centric strategy bakes massive redundancy into the representation — baseline methods scale to 100K–3.3M Gaussians, occupying 150–600MB and requiring >500ms inference. Global consistency degrades under dense overlap, and memory grows proportionally with input-view count. The core gap is the absence of a scene-centric, globally coherent allocation of Gaussian primitives prior to decoding. Methodology: GlobalSplat adopts an "align first, decode later" paradigm. Multi-view inputs are aggregated into a fixed set of M=2048 learnable latent scene tokens before any explicit 3D geometry is decoded. The pipeline has four main components. (1) Scene normalization canonicalizes all camera poses into a shared frame using an average-camera transform and scale normalization by the camera constellation diameter. Input patches are augmented with Plücker-ray embeddings and per-view camera codes (Fourier-encoded camera center + intrinsics MLP) concatenated as geometry tokens. (2) A dual-branch iterative encoder (B=4 blocks) processes tokens through parallel geometry and appearance streams. Each branch applies cross-attention between latent tokens and image patches, followed by L=2 self-attention layers. Streams are fused via a 2-layer MLP. This disentanglement prevents texture from masking poor structural predictions. (3) A coarse-to-fine training curriculum starts with all 16 Gaussian candidates per token merged into 1 (stage 0) and progressively doubles capacity (stages 1–3, yielding G=8 per token, total 16,384 Gaussians) using soft temperature-scaled weighted merging with attribute-specific rules (volume-preserving log-scale merge, transmittance-based opacity merge). Smooth linear interpolation between stages prevents training discontinuities. (4) Training combines a rendering loss (MSE + perceptual), a self-supervised consistency loss that enforces opacity and depth agreement across two interleaved subsets of input views via symmetric stop-gradient, and regularization losses (frustum constraint, opacity cap, scale/rotation/SH penalties). The model is trained with AdamW for 220k steps with a cosine-decay schedule. Key Results: On RealEstate10K with 24 input views, GlobalSplat16K achieves 28.53 PSNR / 0.883 SSIM / 0.140 LPIPS using only 16K Gaussians — competitive with ZPressor (28.51 PSNR) which requires 393K Gaussians, and well above C3G (23.80 PSNR, 2K Gaussians). GlobalSplat32K reaches 29.48 PSNR at 32K Gaussians, the strongest Gaussian-based result in the table. Representation size is strictly view-invariant: PSNR drops only 0.12 dB going from 12 to 36 views, whereas DepthSplat degrades 2.5 dB over the same range. Efficiency: GlobalSplat achieves 77.88ms inference, 1.79GB peak GPU memory, and 3.8MB disk footprint — versus ZPressor at 194ms/3.70GB/134MB and DepthSplat at 669ms/29.84GB/534MB. Zero-shot transfer to ACID yields 28.04 PSNR, second only to ZPressor (28.53) among Gaussian-based methods and far above GGN (20.90) and C3G (22.24). Ablations confirm: (a) dual-stream architecture gains +0.55 PSNR over a widened single-stream baseline; (b) coarse-to-fine curriculum gains +0.88 PSNR over direct full-capacity training; (c) camera metadata injection gains +0.27 PSNR over Plücker-only; (d) consistency loss gains +0.42 PSNR. The ablation also shows that latent token count drives quality far more than decoded Gaussians per token at fixed total budget. Strengths: - The "align first, decode later" principle is cleanly motivated and effectively executed: the fixed-token bottleneck naturally prevents representation bloat and achieves view-invariant Gaussian count, a property no dense baseline offers. - Efficiency gains are substantial and practically meaningful: 2.5× faster inference, 16× less GPU memory, and 35× smaller disk footprint than the next-best quality method (ZPressor), with only marginal quality difference at 16K Gaussians. - The coarse-to-fine curriculum is well-designed with smooth stage transitions and attribute-specific merge rules (volume-preserving log-scale, transmittance opacity), not just a naive progressive loss. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The 16K fixed Gaussian budget is adequate for room-scale and localized aerial scenes but is acknowledged to be insufficient for unbounded or city-scale environments; no adaptive mechanism is proposed or demonstrated. - The method is trained and evaluated only on monocular video sequences (RealEstate10K, ACID) with SLAM/SfM-derived poses; performance on truly unposed or wide-baseline multi-view capture is untested, which limits generality claims. - Evaluation is at 256×256 resolution only; it is unclear whether the quality-efficiency trade-offs hold at higher resolutions where dense baselines might leverage more detail capacity relative to the fixed token budget.
Recommended 2604.15284 PDF
#72 — Innovative (67)

MoBiE: Efficient Inference of Mixture of Binary Experts under Post-Training Quantization

Zhixiong Zhao, Zukang Xu, Zhixuan Chen, Dawei Yang · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: MoBiE is the first work to systematically identify and address three MoE-specific binarization failure modes, each with a principled solution: cross-expert SVD decomposition to exploit shared structure, gradient-weighted global Hessian for task-aware saliency, and null-space guided routing preservation. The backbone-agnostic plug-and-play design enabling drop-in improvement of existing binary PTQ methods broadens practical utility. Results matching 3-bit GPTQ at roughly half the memory are compelling, though the effective bitwidth (1.34–1.60) being materially higher than claimed baselines complicates direct comparison.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLMs achieve strong performance through sparse expert activation but suffer severe memory overhead because all expert weights must reside in GPU memory continuously — e.g., GPT-OSS-120B requires 240+ GB despite only 5B parameters being active during decoding. Weight binarization (1-bit) is an extreme but attractive compression technique, yet all existing binary post-training quantization (PTQ) methods were designed for dense LLMs and transfer poorly to MoE architectures. Three MoE-specific failure modes are identified: (1) cross-expert redundancy is ignored — experts share moderate structural similarity (CKA 0.3–0.6) that independent binarization wastes; (2) local Hessian-based saliency metrics are task-agnostic, misaligned with downstream loss; and (3) binarization perturbations distort the discrete top-k routing distributions, causing "expert-shift" where tokens migrate to suboptimal experts and perplexity diverges catastrophically. Methodology: MoBiE is a three-stage PTQ framework requiring no fine-tuning, calibrated on 128 WikiText2 samples. Stage 1 — Cross-Expert Joint Decomposition (CEJD): All expert weight matrices in a layer are horizontally concatenated and jointly decomposed via SVD. The shared backbone UΣ is retained in high precision (8-bit) while only the expert-specific projection matrices V are binarized. This prevents feature collapse, confines quantization noise to a stable orthonormal subspace, and adds negligible O² parameter overhead. Stage 2 — Global Loss-Aligned Saliency (GLAS): The standard local Hessian H = 2XX⊤ is replaced by a gradient-weighted global Hessian H_global,j = (D_j X)⊤(D_j X), where D_j is the diagonal matrix of output gradients ∂ℓ/∂Z_j from the task loss. This reweights input covariance by each output's sensitivity to the global objective, making weight importance scores task-aware. High-saliency columns (top 5%) receive a second-order residual binarization correction. Stage 3 — Null-Space Guided Expert-Shift Suppression (NGES): The null space of router input activations (directions where X v = 0) defines routing-insensitive perturbation directions. Rather than projecting explicitly, NGES introduces two compact modulation vectors β^r ∈ ℝ^m and β^c ∈ ℝ^n fused into the mandatory row/column binarization scaling factors via Hadamard product. These are optimized via alternating least squares with Tikhonov regularization (λ = 0.2, 15 iterations), adding no extra storage while constraining binarization error to routing-insensitive subspaces. Key Results: Evaluated on 6 MoE models (OLMoE-1B-7B, DeepSeek-V2-Lite, Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B, Qwen3-30B-A3B, Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B, GPT-OSS-20B) across 7 zero-shot benchmarks and WikiText2 perplexity. On Qwen3-30B-A3B, MoBiE reduces perplexity by 52.2% over ARB-LLM (26.76 → 12.80) and improves average zero-shot accuracy by 43.4% (40.14 → 57.58), while increasing effective bitwidth by only 0.23 bits. MoBiE at ~1.34–1.60 bits effective precision matches or slightly exceeds 3-bit GPTQ on most benchmarks while using less than half the memory. On Qwen1.5-MoE, 2.33× inference throughput gain (22.05 → 51.38 tokens/s) and over 90% reduction in expert-layer activation memory (9.64 GB → 1.88 GB). Routing cosine similarity maintained ≥ 0.9 across all layers versus BiLLM/ARB-LLM degrading below 0.8 in deeper layers. GLAS and NGES function as plug-and-play improvements integrated into BiLLM and ARB-LLM backbones, consistently reducing perplexity across three corpora without retraining. Quantization time is comparable to ARB-LLM and only ~6 minutes slower than BiLLM per model. Strengths: - First systematic identification and treatment of three MoE-specific binarization failure modes, each addressed with a principled, well-motivated solution (SVD structure exploitation, gradient-weighted Hessian, null-space error projection). - Zero additional storage overhead: NGES modulation vectors are fused into mandatory binarization scaling factors, and CEJD's shared backbone adds negligible O² terms relative to NOI total parameters. - Strong empirical generalization: consistent outperformance of all binary baselines across 6 architecturally diverse MoE models spanning 7B–80B total parameters, including competitive performance against 3-bit methods at roughly half the memory cost. - GLAS and NGES are backbone-agnostic drop-in modules that improve existing binary PTQ pipelines (BiLLM, ARB-LLM) without any architectural changes, broadening practical utility. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The effective bitwidth of 1.34–1.60 bits (due to the 8-bit shared UΣ backbone) is materially higher than the claimed "binary" 1.11-bit baselines; comparisons on a per-bit-per-accuracy basis are not fully disentangled, making the efficiency advantage over 2-bit methods appear closer than the accuracy tables suggest. - GLAS requires computing and storing per-output-channel gradient matrices during calibration, adding significant memory pressure at quantization time that is not systematically measured or compared against the simpler local Hessian approach. - The null-space constraint in NGES is approximate and only targets the router/gate weights; the expert FFN weights — which also affect router input representations through the residual stream — are not directly constrained, leaving residual expert-shift pathways unaddressed. - All experiments use WikiText2 calibration data; performance under domain shift between calibration and deployment is acknowledged as a limitation but is not empirically characterized.
Search Results 2604.06798 PDF
#73 — Innovative (66)

Uni-ViGU: Towards Unified Video Generation and Understanding via A Diffusion-Based Video Generator (39 ▲ )

Luozheng Qin, Jia Gong, Qian Qiao, Tianjiao Li, Li Xu, Haoyu Pan, Chao Qu, Zhiyu Tan, Hao Li · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 14 Apr 2026
Why this rank: The paradigm inversion—starting from a pretrained video diffusion model and extending it to understanding rather than the reverse—is a genuinely creative reframing motivated by a concrete computational asymmetry argument. The uni-flow formulation that bridges continuous and discrete flow matching within a single process is architecturally elegant. However, the complete absence of quantitative benchmark results makes the claimed 'competitive performance' entirely unsubstantiated, and the reliance on fully synthetic training data raises distribution concerns that are never addressed.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Unified multimodal models that jointly handle visual understanding and generation face a fundamental computational asymmetry: video generation via diffusion requires iterative denoising over millions of tokens (e.g., 73,920 tokens × 40-50 steps for a 5-second 720P video), while understanding requires far fewer. Prior work has overwhelmingly extended understanding-centric MLLMs to support generation, which is expensive and limits scalability. The paper inverts this paradigm: instead of patching an LLM with a diffusion generator, it starts from a pretrained video diffusion model (WAN2.1) and extends it to perform video understanding, exploiting the rich text-video correspondences already learned during generation pretraining. Methodology: Uni-ViGU introduces three coordinated contributions. First, a unified flow (uni-flow) formulation performs continuous flow matching for video (in VAE latent space) and discrete flow matching for text (over token embedding space) within a single joint process, with independently sampled noise levels per modality.
