At inference, fixing the video to clean enables understanding (video→text), and fixing the text to clean enables generation (text→video); both simultaneously noisy enables joint generation. Second, a modality-driven MoE architecture modifies each DiT block by sharing self-attention and cross-attention across video and text tokens (preserving cross-modal alignment), while routing to separate FFN experts per modality (V-FFN and T-FFN) to capture domain-specific patterns. Video FFN weights are kept from the pretrained model; text FFN weights are freshly initialized. Third, a two-stage bidirectional training scheme: Stage 1 (Knowledge Recall) trains on 10K video-prompt pairs where the target text equals the conditioning prompt, with heavy condition dropout to prevent shortcut copying and force the model to recover text from video via the learned text-video correspondence. Stage 2 (Capability Refinement) fine-tunes on 10K video-prompt-detailed-caption triples, replacing the target with rich detailed captions (128-256 tokens vs. 0-128 token prompts) to force genuine visual comprehension through the shared attention mechanism. Training used 16 H800 GPUs for one week total. Key Results: The paper is a preview/preprint with limited quantitative evaluation. The primary experimental results are qualitative: Figure 3 demonstrates joint video-text generation where the model simultaneously denoises video and produces substantially more detailed captions than the input conditioning prompts (e.g., enriching "a tranquil coastal house scene" into a paragraph-level description with spatial, temporal, and appearance details). No numerical comparisons to state-of-the-art baselines on standard benchmarks (e.g., MSR-VTT, ActivityNet, EvalCrafter, VBench) are provided. The paper claims competitive performance on both video generation and understanding but defers full quantitative evaluation, consistent with its self-labeled "Preview" status. Strengths: - The paradigm inversion (generation-centric rather than understanding-centric unification) is well-motivated by the concrete token-count asymmetry and provides a genuinely different design axis from prior work. - The uni-flow formulation elegantly bridges the continuous/discrete modality mismatch without introducing separate generation heads or architectural modules beyond the FFN split, keeping the parameter overhead minimal. - The two-stage training curriculum is principled: Knowledge Recall bootstraps cross-modal alignment cheaply by exploiting already-learned text-video correspondences, while Capability Refinement forces genuine comprehension through length-separated caption targets that cannot be trivially inferred from the prompt. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper provides no quantitative benchmark results whatsoever — no FID, FVD, CIDEr, BLEU, or standard video QA scores — making it impossible to assess actual performance relative to any baseline. The "competitive performance" claim is entirely unsubstantiated. - Training data is entirely synthetic (videos generated by existing models, captions generated by an LLM), which raises concerns about compounding distribution artifacts and ceiling effects in understanding quality — the model can only learn to describe what generative models produce, not real-world video. - The discrete flow matching for text uses argmax decoding over token embeddings, discarding the probabilistic structure that makes diffusion-based generation useful for controllability and diversity; the paper does not analyze text generation quality (fluency, factuality, hallucination rate) in any systematic way.

#74 — Innovative (66)

HiVLA: A Visual-Grounded-Centric Hierarchical Embodied Manipulation System (14 ▲ )

Tianshuo Yang, Guanyu Chen, Yutian Chen, Zhixuan Liang, Yitian Liu, Zanxin Chen, Chunpu Xu, Haotian Liang, Jiangmiao Pang, Yao Mu, Ping Luo · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 66 for a well-engineered hierarchical system that elegantly addresses the spatial fidelity vs. global context tradeoff through cascaded cross-attention with absolute positional re-anchoring of cropped patches — a clean solution to a real problem. The large empirical margins over baselines (+12.7pp over H-RDT, +37pp over monolithic VLAs) are convincing. However, the contribution is primarily a careful integration of known components (hierarchical planning, diffusion policies, cross-attention fusion) rather than a fundamentally new idea, and the VLM planner's dependence on 210K domain-specific fine-tuning instances undermines generalization claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: End-to-end Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for robotic manipulation suffer from a fundamental tension: fine-tuning large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) on narrow manipulation data degrades their broad reasoning capabilities through catastrophic forgetting. Prior hierarchical approaches that decouple planning from control exist, but their intermediate representations — text subtasks or spatial keypoints — fail to fully exploit visual grounding information. Specifically, existing visual-grounded methods either strip global spatial coordinates when cropping local patches, or apply object masks to heavily downsampled images that lose fine-grained detail. The paper targets the gap of how to design a policy that simultaneously exploits high-resolution local object appearance, precise global spatial awareness, and explicit skill-level directives. Methodology: HiVLA is a two-level hierarchical system. At the high level, a fine-tuned Qwen3-VL 8B model acts as a planner agent: given the current 1920×1080 camera observation, gripper state, task instruction, and a two-frame visual history (scene before and after the last action), it generates a structured JSON plan containing a skill label, action type, target object name, and a normalized bounding box. This bounding box invokes an image-crop tool that extracts a high-resolution local patch from the full-resolution frame. At the low level, a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) action expert built on a LLaMA-style backbone with 16 layers and hidden dimension 2,176 uses Conditional Flow Matching (CFM) to map Gaussian noise to action sequences. The key innovation is a cascaded cross-attention mechanism inside each DiT block that sequentially fuses three conditioning signals: (1) global visual features from a frozen DINOv2+SigLIP encoder for scene-level context; (2) high-resolution local crop features augmented with absolute sinusoidal positional encodings (DETR-inspired) mapping each patch token back to its coordinate in the original 1080p frame; and (3) language embeddings of the subtask description for skill disambiguation. The "coarse-to-fine" ordering (Global → Local → Language) is validated as optimal. The DiT is initialized from H-RDT weights pre-trained on EgoDex; the VLM planner is fine-tuned on a 210K dialogue dataset derived from simulator ground-truth annotations. Inference uses asynchronous execution at 8Hz, with the 1.9s VLM planning step running in parallel with the 0.162s DiT action chunk generation. Key Results: On RoboTwin 2.0 with domain randomization over 9 tasks (100 trials each), HiVLA achieves 83.3% total average success rate versus 70.6% for H-RDT (+12.7pp), 46.4% for StarVLA (+36.9pp), and 45.6% for π0 (+37.7pp). On hard long-horizon tasks the gap widens to 73.2% vs. 54.6% for H-RDT (+18.6pp) and under 40% for coupled VLAs. The ablation confirms the importance of the cascaded ordering (Global→Local→Text yields 83.3% vs. 70.4–70.6% for single-modality baselines), high-resolution crops (removing HD crops drops to 75.2%), and absolute positional encodings (removing them drops to 76.8%). VLM planner fine-tuning achieves 90.37% bounding-box mIoU and 98.57% subtask accuracy; zero-shot performance of even GPT-4o yields only 3.45% mIoU. Robustness testing shows 57% success rate even with 100% bounding-box noise, while language noise causes proportional degradation. In real-world trials on an Aloha-Agilex bimanual platform with 360 teleoperated episodes, HiVLA achieves 13/30 vs. 8/30 (single bell), 17/30 vs. 9/30 (two bells), 21/30 vs. 4/30 (single cup), and 6/30 vs. 0/30 (three cups), showing H-RDT collapses to zero in multi-object clutter. Strengths: - The cascaded cross-attention design elegantly solves the spatial fidelity vs. global context tradeoff: full-resolution crops preserve fine detail, and absolute positional encodings re-anchor local tokens to global scene coordinates, giving the policy both magnified appearance and precise spatial awareness simultaneously. - The modular hierarchy provides genuine emergent error-correction: when the DiT fails a grasp, the VLM planner detects task incompletion from visual history and re-issues the command — a feedback loop impossible in monolithic VLAs. - Evaluation is thorough and fair: all baselines share the same HiVLA-HD training data for 150K steps; perturbation tests quantitatively characterize robustness to planner errors rather than relying on qualitative claims; real-world results on genuinely hard multi-object disambiguation tasks validate sim-to-real transfer. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The VLM planner requires domain-specific fine-tuning on 210K simulation-derived instances to achieve usable grounding accuracy (90% mIoU); zero-shot performance is poor across all tested models including GPT-4o (3.45% mIoU), meaning the claimed "zero-shot reasoning preservation" applies to language reasoning but not spatial grounding, which is the safety-critical bottleneck. - The unoptimized 1.9s VLM inference latency is addressed only by asynchronous parallel execution, which assumes temporal stationarity between planning steps; the real-world evaluation with only 360 training episodes and 30 trials per task is limited in scale, and tasks are restricted to pick-place primitives on a fixed workspace — the generalization to contact-rich or dexterous manipulation remains undemonstrated.

Karnati, Yejin Choi, Yev Meyer, Yi-Fu Wu, Yian Zhang, Ying Lin, Yonatan Geifman, Yonggan Fu, Yoshi Suhara, Youngeun Kwon, Yuan Zhang, Yuki Huang, Zach Moshe, Zhilin Wang, Zhiyu Cheng, Zhongbo Zhu, Zhuolin Yang, Zihan Liu, Zijia Chen, Zijie Yan, Zuhair Ahmed · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 65 for LatentMoE, a principled hardware-software co-design that enables the non-obvious tradeoff of reduced expert dimensionality for more experts and higher top-K at constant inference cost. The shared-weight MTP formulation addresses a genuine training-inference mismatch. However, individual components are evolutionary rather than revolutionary, and the throughput comparisons across different quantization regimes inflate the headline numbers.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Scaling large language models to high accuracy under fixed inference cost requires solving two related but distinct problems: computational efficiency (FLOPs per token) and deployment efficiency (memory bandwidth, routing communication, hardware latency). Existing MoE architectures optimize primarily for FLOPs but neglect the memory bandwidth costs of loading expert weights and the all-to-all communication overhead during distributed inference. Separately, hybrid Mamba-Attention architectures have shown throughput advantages over pure Transformers but had not been combined with sparse MoE at scale. The paper addresses both gaps by introducing Nemotron 3 Super, a 120B total / 12B active parameter model that combines a new hardware-aware MoE design (LatentMoE), speculative decoding via Multi-Token Prediction (MTP), and low-precision NVFP4 pretraining, with a strong emphasis on agentic task performance. Methodology: The architecture rests on three innovations. First, LatentMoE projects each token from the full hidden dimension d into a smaller latent dimension ℓ before routing and expert computation, reducing per-expert weight loads and all-to-all communication by a factor d/ℓ. These savings are redirected to expand both the total number of experts (512, up from a standard count) and the number of active experts per token (top-22 routing), yielding higher model quality at approximately constant inference cost. Non-routed operations (gating, shared experts, attention) remain in the full hidden dimension d. Second, MTP uses shared-weight prediction heads trained to predict multiple future tokens at each position. The shared-weight formulation—unlike independent fixed-offset heads—regularizes the head across prediction horizons and mitigates the training-inference mismatch when the head is applied autoregressively at inference for speculative decoding. Third, the 88-layer stack follows a periodic hybrid interleaving pattern where LatentMoE layers are paired with Mamba-2 blocks (linear-time sequence modeling with constant-sized state) and a sparse set of self-attention layers serve as global "anchors." Pretraining uses NVFP4 (E2M1 element format, 16-element micro-blocks, E4M3 micro-block scaling) for most linear layers, with selective BF16/MXFP8 promotion for attention, MTP, and latent projection layers. Post-training comprises two-stage SFT (token-level then sample-level normalization to prevent long-output dominance), multi-environment RLVR across 21 diverse domains, a separate SWE-RL stage for long-horizon software engineering tasks, RLHF using a principle-following GenRM, and a final MTP healing stage. Key Results: On base model benchmarks, Nemotron 3 Super substantially outperforms comparable-scale competitors: MMLU 86.01 vs. 81.00 for both Ling-flash-base-2.0 and GLM-4.5-Air-Base; MATH Level 5 70.00 vs. 39.80 and 26.30; GPQA-Diamond 60.00 vs. 36.00 and 23.20; RULER at 1M context 71.00 (competitors do not support this length). On post-trained benchmarks, the model is competitive with but generally slightly behind Qwen3.5-122B-A10B on reasoning (HMMT Feb25: 93.67 vs. 91.40; AIME25: 90.21 vs. 90.36) and noticeably behind on SWE-Bench (60.47 vs. 66.40 with OpenHands) and TauBench V2 average (61.15 vs. 74.53), while outperforming GPT-OSS-120B on most agentic benchmarks. The primary differentiator is inference throughput: 2.2× higher than GPT-OSS-120B and 7.5× higher than Qwen3.5-122B in the 8k-input / 64k-output regime on B200 GPUs. MTP achieves average acceptance length of 3.45 tokens per verification step (vs. 2.70 for DeepSeek-R1 and 3.33 for Qwen3-Next) at draft length 7. The NVFP4 quantized checkpoint retains 99.8% median accuracy relative to BF16 across benchmarks, completed in under 2 hours on a single B200 node. Strengths: - LatentMoE is a principled, hardware-software co-design contribution with clear theoretical analysis linking memory bandwidth, communication overhead, and accuracy; it enables trading reduced expert dimensionality for more experts and higher top-K, a non-obvious tradeoff that holds up empirically. - The shared-weight MTP formulation addresses a genuine limitation of standard multi-token prediction heads (training-inference mismatch during autoregressive drafting) and produces measurably better acceptance rates at long draft lengths. - Large-scale NVFP4 pretraining to 25T tokens is an engineering demonstration of stability at a format more aggressive than BF16/FP8, with thorough analysis of the zero-valued gradient phenomenon induced by NVFP4 quantization. - All checkpoints (BF16, FP8, NVFP4), pretraining datasets, and post-training data are fully open-sourced, providing significant practical value to the research community. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The throughput advantage is measured under conditions favorable to Nemotron 3 Super (NVFP4 for the proposed model, BF16 for Qwen3.5-122B; MXFP4/MXFP8 for GPT-OSS-120B), making the 7.5× headline figure a comparison across different quantization regimes rather than a controlled architectural comparison. - On the agentic benchmarks the paper most heavily emphasizes, Nemotron 3 Super lags Qwen3.5-122B by substantial margins: SWE-Bench OpenHands 60.47 vs. 66.40, SWE-Bench OpenCode 59.20 vs. 67.40, and TauBench V2 average 61.15 vs. 74.53—calling into question whether the expanded RL training recipe fully closes the agentic capability gap at equal accuracy-per-parameter.
Recommended 2604.12374 PDF
#76 — Innovative (65)

VisionFoundry: Teaching VLMs Visual Perception with Synthetic Images (6 ▲ )

Guanyu Zhou, Yida Yin, Wenhao Chai, Shengbang Tong, Xingyu Fu, Zhuang Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 13 Apr 2026
Why this rank: VisionFoundry presents a clean closed-loop pipeline for generating targeted synthetic supervision from only a task keyword, with a principled verification stage. The experimental design isolating synthetic image contributions from text reasoning signals is methodologically sound, and the gains on low-level perception benchmarks are consistent across model scales.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vision-language models (VLMs) persistently underperform on low-level visual perception tasks — spatial reasoning, depth ordering, viewpoint recognition, and attribute discrimination — despite strong general multimodal capabilities. The hypothesis motivating this work is that natural image-text corpora, while vast, do not systematically cover the combinatorial range of visual configurations required for robust low-level perception. The paper asks a focused question: can targeted synthetic supervision generated from only a task keyword address these weaknesses without reference images or human annotation? Methodology: VisionFoundry is a closed-loop three-stage pipeline. Stage 1 (LLM-driven): Given only a task name (e.g., "Depth Order"), GPT-5.2 builds an adaptive entity pool (objects, attributes, scenes, styles) and generates a joint (T2I prompt, question, answer) triplet. The T2I prompt explicitly encodes the answer-determining visual fact to enforce visual determinism. Stage 2 (Image Synthesis): Gemini-2.5-Flash-Image synthesizes images conditioned on the T2I prompt; if an image fails downstream verification, a localized edit pass is attempted before discarding. Stage 3 (Alignment Verification): Gemini-3-Pro converts each QA pair into a declarative visual statement and issues a binary accept/reject judgment over the generated image, filtering misaligned samples. Using this pipeline, the authors construct VisionFoundry-10K: 10k image–question–answer triples balanced across 10 visual perception tasks (1k per task), covering orientation, viewpoint, depth order, spatial relationships, relative distance, and others. Three open-source VLMs of varying scale — Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct, MiMo-VL-7B-SFT, and Llama-3.2-11B-Vision-Instruct — are fine-tuned on VisionFoundry-10K for one epoch without LoRA. Key Results: Fine-tuning on VisionFoundry-10K yields consistent gains on visual perception benchmarks across all three VLMs. For Qwen2.5-VL-3B: MMVP-pair +6.7 (35.3→42.0), CV-Bench-3D +10.5 (66.0→76.5), CV-Bench-2D +5.1 (67.3→72.4). For MiMo-VL-7B: MMVP-pair +14.0 (43.3→57.3), CV-Bench-3D +11.4 (72.3→83.7). General-purpose benchmarks show mixed but mostly non-degrading changes; OCRBench drops slightly (~0.3-0.4 pts) predictably, since the dataset contains no OCR supervision. A data-scaling study shows monotonically improving perception performance from 500 to 10k samples. A synthetic–natural mixture (2k VisionFoundry + 2k LLaVA-Instruct-80K) consistently outperforms a size-matched natural-only subset on perception benchmarks. An ablation confirms the verification stage is necessary: removing it degrades BLINK by 1.2 pts and MathVista-Mini by 2.6 pts under the same 1k budget. A strict-control ablation isolates the value of synthetic images over natural images with matched synthetic QA, showing synthetic images yield additional gains (CV-Bench-3D +2.7 pts). Pipeline quality metrics on an audited 140-sample trajectory show 99.0% precision and 94.7% F1, with a Cohen's κ of 0.794 between verifier and human labels. Strengths: - Clean experimental design that isolates the contribution of synthetic image supervision from text reasoning signals by deliberately excluding long-chain reasoning tasks from the dataset. - Fully automated and annotation-free pipeline that scales to arbitrary tasks from a single keyword, with a principled verification stage that demonstrably matters (not just cosmetic). - Honest and transparent reporting: all general-purpose benchmark values are tabulated individually rather than selectively aggregated, false accepts in the verifier audit are acknowledged, and ablations explicitly address whether gains come from QA construction vs. synthetic images. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The dataset is narrow in scope (10k samples, 10 tasks, pure VQA format) and evaluated only on instruction-tuned models via SFT, leaving open whether the gains transfer to pretraining, RLHF, or more complex compositional reasoning chains. - The pipeline depends on frontier proprietary models (GPT-5.2, Gemini-2.5-Flash-Image, Gemini-3-Pro) for generation and verification, making reproducibility contingent on API access and expensive to replicate; the verifier audit is also based on a single 140-sample trajectory, limiting statistical confidence in the quality estimates. - The gains on general-purpose benchmarks are inconsistent and model-dependent (e.g., MiMo's anomalous +31-point MMBench jump is explained post-hoc by a hypothesis about non-thinking mode rather than a mechanistic account), and the paper does not disentangle whether improvements on perception benchmarks reflect genuine perceptual skill acquisition or benchmark-specific artifacts.
Recommended 2604.09531 PDF
#77 — Innovative (65)

CogBias: Measuring and Mitigating Cognitive Bias in Large Language Models

Fan Huang, Songheng Zhang, Haewoon Kwak, Jisun An · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 65 for the well-designed contrastive probing methodology that correctly identifies and fixes the instruction-detection confound in non-contrastive approaches — a genuine methodological contribution to LLM interpretability. The emergent two-cluster structure (D1/D2 vs. D3/D4) appearing consistently across behavioral, representational, and interventional analyses adds coherence. However, the finding that steering directions are statistically indistinguishable from random perturbations at matched magnitudes seriously undercuts the central claim of targeted bias suppression, reducing the intervention contribution to activation disruption rather than principled debiasing.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large Language Models are increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains (medical diagnosis, legal analysis, financial forecasting), yet prior work on their cognitive biases has been limited to behavioral observation — measuring outputs without examining internal representations or applying targeted interventions. This gap matters because behavioral observations alone cannot distinguish whether biases arise from stable, structured internal features or from surface-level prompt sensitivities, a distinction that determines whether principled mitigation is possible. The paper asks three questions in a Behavior–Representation–Intervention progression: do LLMs exhibit systematic cognitive biases across four families; are these biases encoded as linearly separable directions in activation space; and can activation steering reduce biased behavior while preserving capability? Methodology: The authors introduce LLM CogBias, a benchmark organized around four bias families — Judgment & Decision (anchoring, base-rate neglect, sunk cost, status quo), Information Processing & Salience (framing, availability, illusory truth, randomness misperception), Social & Belief-Related (confirmation bias, overconfidence), and Response & Interaction-Induced (sycophancy, position preference) — drawing on four existing datasets: Malberg30k, CoBBLEr, BBQ, and BiasMonkey (5,300 unified instances). Every instance appears in paired conditions (bias-inducing vs. control) with computable ground-truth baselines, enabling objective bias measurement. Behavioral profiling (RQ1) uses vanilla prompts across GPT-4o-mini, Llama-3.3-70B, and Qwen2.5-72B. For probing (RQ2), the authors carefully introduce a contrastive prompt design — both conditions acknowledge the same heuristic mechanism but differ only in whether it is endorsed — to avoid the instruction-detection confound that inflated non-contrastive probe accuracy to >99% even at layer 0. Residual stream activations from all 80 transformer layers are extracted and linear probes trained with 5-fold cross-validation. For activation steering (RQ3), a mean-difference direction is computed and subtracted from hidden states during inference (h' = h − α·v), with grid search over α ∈ [0,3] and three target layers per model, evaluated against 25 downstream benchmarks spanning medical QA, reasoning, NLU, safety, and code. Key Results: All three LLMs exhibit systematic biases across all four families. Response bias is most pronounced (78.1% first-option selection vs. 36.8% chance baseline), followed by Information Processing (50.7% order-dependent choices), Judgment (2.7–7.7pp anchor-induced shifts), and Social (near-zero stereotype rate, but heavily driven by alignment-induced refusals rather than genuine debiasing). Prompt-level debiasing is strongly family-dependent: it reduces Response biases dramatically (+40.5% position independence) but backfires for Judgment biases (−4.4% accuracy), with the worst individual cases being Hyperbolic Discounting (−21.0pp) and Disposition Effect (−19.6pp). Contrastive probing achieves 97.8% mean classification accuracy across four families (D1/Judgment: 99.8%, D2/Info Processing: 98.2%, D4/Response: 99.2%, D3/Social: 93.9%), with linear probes outperforming MLPs, confirming that bias representations are linearly separable. Cross-model transfer fails (49.7% accuracy), indicating architecture-specific representations despite near-orthogonal steering directions (mean cosine similarity 0.01). Activation steering achieves 26–32% bias score reduction at Pareto-optimal configurations. A two-cluster structure emerges: D1/D2 (Judgment, Information Processing) require strong early-layer steering (α=2.6–3.0, layers 0–15), while D3/D4 (Social, Response) respond to gentle mid-layer steering (α=0.3–1.7, layers 10–15). Llama preserves near-baseline capability (<1pp mean degradation across all 25 benchmarks); Qwen shows asymmetric costs — D3/D4 steering is benign (−0.3pp, −1.1pp) but D1/D2 causes substantial degradation (−19.0pp, −10.8pp) due to the higher α required. Despite near-orthogonal bias directions, steering response curves are correlated across models (r(246)=0.621, p<0.001), suggesting shared functional organization. Strengths: - The contrastive probing design is a genuine methodological contribution: the paper correctly identifies and empirically diagnoses the instruction-detection confound in non-contrastive probing and implements a principled fix, avoiding a common pitfall that would have inflated all interpretability claims. - The Behavior–Representation–Intervention progression yields a coherent narrative: the two-cluster finding (D1/D2 vs. D3/D4) emerges independently across behavioral debiasing effectiveness, optimal steering layer/strength, and capability cost, which substantially strengthens confidence that this is a real organizational principle. - The 25-benchmark capability evaluation is appropriately comprehensive and reveals the asymmetric cost structure between Llama and Qwen, providing practically actionable guidance rather than just bias reduction numbers. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The robustness controls (random directions, orthogonal directions, cross-family transfer) show that learned steering directions are statistically indistinguishable from random perturbations at α=1.5 (all p > 0.05) and provide only 1.6% greater bias reduction than controls. The paper attributes this to a broad representational basin, but this seriously undercuts the claim that activation steering is "targeted" — the mechanism may simply be activation disruption rather than directional suppression of bias-encoding subspaces. - The benchmark covers only four bias families from one representative dataset each (one dataset per family, not per category), so coverage of the 11 operational categories is uneven and several important biases (confirmation bias, hindsight bias, availability heuristic as a distinct phenomenon) are absent or conflated. The generalizability of the two-cluster finding to a broader bias taxonomy remains untested. - Activation steering requires open-weight model access with internal activation hooks, which excludes prominent proprietary models (GPT-4, Claude) and limits deployment relevance. The steering approach also applies uniform perturbation at all token positions, leaving open whether position-selective or layer-adaptive methods would yield a better capability–bias tradeoff, especially for Qwen's D1/D2 families where the current approach causes severe degradation.
Recommended 2604.01366 PDF
#78 — Innovative (65)

CodeTracer: Towards Traceable Agent States (29 ▲ )

Han Li, Yifan Yao, Letian Zhu, Rili Feng, Hongyi Ye, Jiaming Wang, Yancheng He, Pengyu Zou, Lehan Zhang, Xinping Lei, Haoyang Huang, Ken Deng, Ming Sun, Zhaoxiang Zhang, He Ye, Jiaheng Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 14 Apr 2026
Why this rank: The hierarchical trace tree abstraction — distinguishing exploration from state-changing steps — is a genuinely useful contribution to agent observability, and the +18.3 pt F1 gain from tree indexing alone validates its diagnostic value. The breadth of the empirical study (3,326 trajectories across 5 backbones and 4 frameworks) yields several practically actionable findings, particularly that success saturates at ~40 iterations and that heavier orchestration does not proportionally improve outcomes. The evidence-to-action gap insight reframes agent failure from retrieval to execution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Code agents executing long, multi-stage software engineering workflows are increasingly opaque — when a run fails, it is unclear where the trajectory first went wrong or how early mistakes cascaded into final failure. Existing evaluations collapse entire trajectories into a single pass/fail label, providing no visibility into process-level errors. Manual inspection does not scale to the thousands of trajectories generated by modern agents. This paper addresses the need for automated, scalable failure diagnosis in code agent executions. Methodology: The authors present CODETRACER, a three-stage diagnostic pipeline. (1) Evolving extraction scans heterogeneous run directories, discovers the artifact layout, and adapts or synthesizes format-specific parsers that are accumulated in a registry for reuse across runs, producing normalized step records with typed fields (action, observation, diff, verification outcome). (2) Tree indexing converts the flat normalized sequence into a hierarchical trace tree: exploration steps remain under the current state node while state-changing steps induce transitions to child nodes, creating a compressed navigation index that makes explicit whether actions are taken under an unchanged context or after an intervention. (3) Diagnosis traverses the tree using structured evidence queries to predict the failure-responsible stage, error-relevant steps, and a compact evidence set. An optional reflective replay component feeds the localized diagnostic signals back into the agent as a prefix hint, enabling targeted recovery under matched token budgets. To support evaluation, the authors construct CODETRACEBENCH: 3,326 step-level annotated trajectories spanning five benchmarks (SWE-bench Verified/Pro, MultiSWE-bench, SWE-PolyBench, TerminalBench), four agent frameworks (SWE-Agent, MiniSWE-Agent, OpenHands, Terminus 2), and five frontier backbones (Claude-sonnet-4, GPT-5, DeepSeek-V3.2, Qwen3-Coder-480B, Kimi-K2-Instruct), with inter-annotator Cohen's κ = 0.73. Key Results: CODETRACER substantially outperforms both bare LLM prompting (~16–19% macro F1) and Mini-CodeTracer (~19%) baselines, reaching 46–48% macro F1 across three frontier backbones. Tree indexing alone contributes +18.3 pt F1, confirming it as the dominant component; evolving extraction adds a further +9.4 pt. The three frontiers differ in search strategy: GPT-5 commits early for best precision (45.0%) at lowest token cost (31.1k); Claude-sonnet-4 scans more exhaustively for best recall (54.9%) at higher cost (56.8k); DeepSeek-V3.2 balances the two. Reflective replay with CODETRACER's diagnostics consistently raises Pass@1 on originally failed runs across all backbones (e.g., Claude: 41.6%→48.3%, GPT-5: 32.6%→38.2%, DeepSeek: 29.3%→32.6%). Key empirical findings include: (i) an evidence-to-action gap — ineffective steps nearly double from solved (22%) to unsolved (40%) trajectories while exploration usefulness degrades only mildly, confirming agents retrieve relevant information but fail to act on it; (ii) success saturates around 40 iterations for all backbones, with extra iterations amplifying unproductive loops rather than enabling recovery; (iii) orchestration complexity does not buy proportional success — SWE-Agent and OpenHands use 2× the tokens of MiniSWE-Agent for only +2.4–5.5 pp gains; (iv) error types shift predictably across workflow stages, with environment/dependency errors early and mislocalized edits dominating later patching. Strengths: - The hierarchical trace tree is a genuinely useful abstraction — distinguishing exploration from state-changing steps enables compressed, navigation-friendly representations of long trajectories that reduce both diagnostic cost and noise. - The empirical study is exceptionally broad (3,326 trajectories, 5 backbones, 4 frameworks, 5 benchmarks), and the takeaways — particularly iteration saturation and the overengineering finding — are practically actionable for practitioners designing agent frameworks. - The reflective replay experiments close the loop from diagnosis to recovery, demonstrating that the localized signals are not merely explanatory but operationally useful under matched budgets. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Peak macro F1 of ~48% means the system misidentifies or misses roughly half of failure-relevant steps — the diagnostic signal is noisy and may not be reliable enough for high-stakes automated replay or RL training without further improvement. - All annotations were performed by the paper's own authors on a curated subset of trajectories, introducing potential for self-serving annotation bias; despite κ = 0.73 from a 15% double-annotation sample, the label definitions for 'incorrect' and 'unuseful' involve substantial interpretive judgment on long trajectories with intertwined states.
Recommended 2604.11641 PDF
#79 — Innovative (65)

Turing Test on Screen: A Benchmark for Mobile GUI Agent Humanization (19 ▲ )

Jiachen Zhu, Lingyu Yang, Rong Shan, Congmin Zheng, Zeyu Zheng, Weiwen Liu, Yong Yu, Weinan Zhang, Jianghao Lin · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Apr 2026
Why this rank: The paper identifies a timely and underexplored problem — behavioral biometric detection of GUI agents — and provides a principled MinMax formalization with theoretical guarantees. The large-scale multi-modal dataset and honest analysis of the imitability-utility Pareto frontier are commendable. However, no humanization strategy actually passes the proposed Turing Test (detection remains well above chance), and the single-device, China-app-centric evaluation limits generalizability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: As Large Multimodal Model (LMM)-based GUI agents proliferate on mobile platforms, a fundamental adversarial conflict has emerged between agent operators and platform defenders. Platforms dependent on the attention economy—advertising revenue, user engagement—deploy anti-bot measures to block agents that bypass promotional content and streamline navigation. The dominant academic framing of this conflict as "Attack vs. Anti-Attack" (i.e., adversarial perturbation robustness) overlooks a prerequisite: detection. Before deploying indiscriminate countermeasures, platforms will filter non-human traffic using behavioral biometrics, triggering account blocks and degraded user experience for legitimate users as collateral damage (as demonstrated by the real-world Doubao/WeChat incident in late 2025). The paper argues that for GUI agents to survive in human-centric ecosystems, they must develop "humanization" capabilities—the ability to behave indistinguishably from humans at the physical event level, not just the logical action level. Methodology: The paper formalizes agent-platform interaction as a MinMax adversarial game between a Detector D_Θ maximizing binary cross-entropy classification accuracy and an Agent G_Φ minimizing detection probability subject to a task utility constraint weighted by λ. Interaction is modeled at two layers: the agent-level logical action (tap, swipe) and the hardware event level (MotionEvents: touch coordinates, velocity, pressure; SensorEvents: gyroscope, accelerometer). A dataset is collected across 21 apps in 5 clusters (Social Media, Shopping, Video Streaming, Trip Planning, Office & Learning) from four human sub-populations (Young Man/Woman, Middle-aged, Elderly) and five state-of-the-art agents (UI-TARS, MobileAgent-E GPT-4o/Claude-3.5-Sonnet, AgentCPM, AutoGLM) on a fixed Xiaomi Mi Max 2 device. From raw event streams, 24 statistical features are extracted covering kinematics (velocity percentiles, acceleration), geometry (maxDev, path efficiency ratio, mean resultant length), and temporal dynamics (duration, inter-action latency). Detectors include rule-based heuristics (thresholds on swipe linearity, tap duration, action interval) and learning-based classifiers (SVM and XGBoost on the 24-feature vector). Four external-wrapper humanization strategies are proposed: (1) B-spline Noise Injection to add curvature to linear swipes; (2) Data-Driven History Matching that retrieves a real human trajectory with similar distance/direction and applies affine transformation; (3) Fake Action injection of micro-interactions (small circular gestures at 0.9 Hz Poisson rate) to mask LMM inference latency; and (4) Longer Presses sampling tap durations from a Gaussian fitted to human data. Theoretical guarantees are provided: Theorem 1 bounds detector discriminability by Jensen-Shannon divergence; Theorem 2 proves B-spline injection strictly reduces JS divergence; Theorem 3 proves History Matching is asymptotically optimal as empirical agent distribution converges to the human distribution. Key Results: Raw LMM agents are trivially detectable: XGBoost and SVM achieve 0.995 and 0.98 accuracy respectively on raw agent traces, with rule-based detectors flagging near-zero tap durations and 5–80 second inference intervals far outside the human 0–2 second range. Among humanization strategies, History Matching consistently outperforms B-spline: in Social Media (Cluster 0), it reduces XGBoost accuracy from 1.00 to 0.945 (vs. 0.982 for B-spline) and achieves a lower overall detection score (0.719 vs. 0.851). Long Press reduces tap-duration rule accuracy from 0.984 to 0.613 in Shopping. Fake Action reduces interval-rule accuracy to ~0.52 (near random guessing) but introduces a critical utility trade-off: Trip Planning task success collapses from 0.75 to 0.15 when fake actions are injected online, as blindly injected gestures trigger unintended UI state changes. Feature analysis reveals maxDev (IG=0.673) and ratio_end_to_length (IG=0.658) are easiest to humanize (dropping to ~0.55 ACC after History Matching), while endpoint coordinates and action intervals are hardest—endpoint noise risks missing tap targets, and interval masking via fake actions severely compromises task success. Detection accuracy scales logarithmically with feature count but humanized agents maintain a persistent gap below raw baselines even at all 24 features. Strengths: - The MinMax formalization is principled, and the three theoretical theorems (JS-divergence bound, B-spline reduction, History Matching asymptotic optimality) provide rigorous backing for the empirical observations, not just heuristic motivation. - The dataset is genuinely multi-modal and large-scale (280M+ sensor events), collected in controlled conditions on a fixed device with demographically diverse human subjects and five diverse LMM-based agent systems, making it broadly representative. - The paper honestly surfaces the imitability-utility Pareto frontier rather than overselling results: Fake Action works for detection evasion but catastrophically degrades task utility in complex multi-step tasks, and this failure mode is analyzed mechanistically. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Despite near 30% reduction in XGBoost accuracy, all humanization strategies still leave detection rates well above random chance (XGB >0.94 in most clusters), meaning no strategy actually passes the Turing Test on Screen under adversarial ML detectors—the headline claim of "high imitability" is overstated relative to what the numbers show. - The evaluation relies entirely on SVM and XGBoost over hand-crafted features; the paper argues deep sequence models are unnecessary due to near-perfect raw-agent detectability, but this reasoning becomes circular once humanization is applied—a Transformer over raw event sequences could plausibly exploit temporal patterns invisible to the 24-feature summary statistics. - All experiments are conducted on a single physical device (Xiaomi Mi Max 2 / MIUI 11.0.2), a single Android version, and a dataset that skews heavily toward Chinese apps; generalizability to other device models, OS versions, and Western application ecosystems is unvalidated and likely limited given the device-specific sensor calibration and UI layout dependencies in the feature space.
Recommended 2604.09574 PDF
#80 — Innovative (65)

Walk the Talk: Bridging the Reasoning-Action Gap for Thinking with Images via Multimodal Agentic Policy Optimization

Wenhao Yang, Yu Xia, Jinlong Huang, Shiyin Lu, Qing-Guo Chen, Zhao Xu, Weihua Luo, Kaifu Zhang, Yuchen Zhou, Xiaobo Xia, Yuanyu Wan, Lijun Zhang, Tat-Seng Chua · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: This paper identifies a genuine and underexplored failure mode — reasoning-action discrepancy during RL training of multimodal agents — and addresses it with a clever, lightweight CLIP-based semantic consistency signal that requires no annotation. The dual variance reduction theory is a meaningful contribution that connects well to the observed training stability improvements. However, evaluation on a single backbone and single visual tool limits generalizability claims, and the reliance on CLIP for fine-grained spatial verification is a known weak spot that undermines the method's robustness in precisely the hardest cases.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard reinforcement learning training of multimodal LLM agents that "think with images" relies exclusively on outcome-based (final-answer correctness) reward signals. This creates a critical failure mode: a model can generate textually plausible reasoning ("I need to zoom in on the motorcycle") while executing an entirely different or irrelevant visual action, then still guess the correct answer by chance. The RL objective then inadvertently reinforces this spurious correlation. This reasoning-action discrepancy introduces gradient noise that accumulates across multi-turn trajectories, degrades visual reasoning quality, and often leads to training collapse during extended optimization. No existing work provides step-level supervision for intermediate visual actions without expensive manual annotation. Methodology: MAPO augments the agentic RL training loop with a lightweight, annotation-free semantic consistency signal. The core idea is: at each reasoning step, instead of just generating a bounding-box crop action, the model is required by the system prompt to also produce a fine-grained descriptive label of what it expects to see (e.g., "the man in the red shirt"). After the visual tool executes and returns the cropped image, a frozen pretrained CLIP model computes cosine similarity between the text label embedding and the image embedding, yielding a step-level semantic score z_t. These per-step scores are aggregated into a trajectory-level semantic reward R_sem using a trajectory-aware discount factor λ^(T-t), which penalizes unnecessarily long trajectories while tolerating early-turn exploration. The composite advantage estimate for policy updates combines a group-normalized outcome reward and the group-normalized semantic score, weighted by hyperparameter β=0.4. The full objective is a clipped importance-ratio objective (PPO-style) built on top of GRPO/GSPO. Theoretically, the paper proves that this design constitutes a dual variance reduction: group-based advantage normalization reduces spatial sampling variance by a factor of (1-ρ) where ρ is intra-group reward correlation, and the dense semantic signal reduces reward signal variance relative to sparse binary outcome rewards. Both reductions translate to a tighter convergence bound via standard non-convex SGD analysis. Key Results: Evaluated on three high-resolution fine-grained visual reasoning benchmarks (V, HR-Bench at 4K/8K, MME-RealWorld-Lite), MAPO built on Ovis2.5-9B achieves state-of-the-art among open-source agentic MLLMs: 89.5% overall on V (vs. 89.3% for Mini-o3), 79.8% overall on HR-Bench (vs. 77.8% for GRPO, 77.4% for GSPO, 74.5% for Thyme), and 55.8% on MME-RealWorld-Lite (vs. 55.5% for GRPO). Critically, GRPO suffers severe reward oscillation and eventual collapse in later training stages, while MAPO maintains a monotonically improving reward curve throughout 200+ training steps. Semantic score tracking during training shows MAPO significantly and consistently outperforms all RL baselines (GRPO, GSPO, PPO) in cross-modal alignment quality. Strengths: - The identified problem (reasoning-action discrepancy under sparse outcome rewards) is real, well-motivated, and underexplored; the paper provides both qualitative visualization and quantitative evidence of the failure mode in baselines. - The CLIP-based semantic verifier is computationally cheap (negligible overhead vs. MLLM inference), requires no external annotations, and is a natural fit for the image-text alignment task. - The theoretical analysis is non-trivial and clearly connects the dual variance reduction mechanism to the empirically observed training stability, grounding the method in principled optimization theory. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are limited to a single model backbone (Ovis2.5-9B) and a single visual tool (zoom/crop), making it unclear how well MAPO generalizes across backbone families or richer tool sets (e.g., OCR, detection APIs). - The claim that CLIP cosine similarity faithfully captures whether the visual action aligns with semantic intent is not deeply validated; CLIP is known to fail on fine-grained spatial or compositional queries, which are precisely the cases where accurate verification matters most. - The theoretical proofs rest on assumptions (e.g., that r_sem has strictly lower conditional variance than r_out) that are stated as assumptions rather than empirically verified, and the proof that σ²_sem < σ²_out is essentially circular given that assumption.
Citations 2604.06777 PDF
#81 — Innovative (65)

WildDet3D: Scaling Promptable 3D Detection in the Wild (213 ▲ )

Weikai Huang, Jieyu Zhang, Sijun Li, Taoyang Jia, Jiafei Duan, Yunqian Cheng, Jaemin Cho, Mattew Wallingford, Rustin Soraki, Chris Dongjoo Kim, Donovan Clay, Taira Anderson, Winson Han, Ali Farhadi, Bharath Hariharan, Zhongzheng Ren, Ranjay Krishna · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 13 Apr 2026
Why this rank: WildDet3D makes a solid contribution by unifying multiple prompt modalities (text, point, box, exemplar) for monocular 3D detection and constructing a 13.5K-category dataset that dramatically expands open-world coverage. However, the architecture relies heavily on assembling existing pretrained components (SAM3, DINOv2, LingBot-Depth) with established fusion mechanisms, and the dataset construction, while impressive in scale, follows a familiar pseudo-labeling pipeline pattern with VLM verification.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Monocular 3D object detection—recovering the position, extent, and orientation of objects from a single RGB image—remains constrained to narrow, closed-set categories and single-prompt interaction modes. Existing methods either support text-only querying or assume oracle 2D box inputs; none unifies these interfaces or gracefully incorporates auxiliary geometric cues (depth, LiDAR) when available. Generalization is further hampered by the scarcity of 3D training data: prior datasets like Omni3D cover fewer than 100 categories in controlled environments, far below the long-tailed open-world vocabulary. The paper targets open-world, promptable, geometry-aware monocular 3D detection—addressing both the model architecture and training data simultaneously. Methodology: WildDet3D is a unified architecture built around three components. (1) Dual vision encoders with depth fusion: a frozen SAM3 ViT-H extracts dense semantic features, while a LingBot-Depth DINOv2 ViT-L/14 backbone ingests 4-channel RGBD input (depth channel zero-filled when absent) to produce geometry latents. A ControlNet-style depth fusion module injects the depth latents into the image features via a zero-initialized 1×1 convolution, so depth contributes gradually without disrupting pretrained features. (2) Promptable detector: adapted from SAM3, it encodes text, 2D point, box, and exemplar prompts into a unified cross-attention sequence. Per-prompt batching—treating each category as a separate batch entry—enables multi-instance supervision without padding. (3) 3D detection head: L transformer decoder layers, each enriched via cross-attention to camera ray embeddings (encoded as 8th-order spherical harmonics) and depth latents. Box regression predicts a 12-dim encoding (2D center offset, log-depth, log-dimensions, 6D rotation). Unambiguous rotation normalization resolves the inherent 4-fold yaw/dimension ambiguity. A separate 3D confidence head predicts a quality score combining IoU and depth accuracy. Auxiliary 2D detection and depth estimation heads provide complementary supervision during training. For data, WildDet3D-Data is constructed from four large 2D datasets (COCO, LVIS, Objects365, V3Det) by running five complementary 3D box generation models, filtering geometrically implausible candidates, and selecting the best candidate via either VLM-based composite scoring or human annotation—yielding 1M+ images, 3.7M 3D annotations, 13.5K categories. Key Results: On WildDet3D-Bench (700+ open-vocabulary categories), the full model reaches 22.6 AP (text) and 24.8 AP (box), a 9.8× improvement over 3D-MOOD (2.3 AP). With GT depth at inference, performance nearly doubles to 41.6 AP (text) and 47.2 AP (box). On Omni3D, WildDet3D achieves 34.2 AP (text) and 36.4 AP (box), surpassing 3D-MOOD Swin-B (30.0 AP) and DetAny3D (34.4 AP) with 6–10× fewer training epochs (12 vs. 80–120). Adding sparse depth yields 41.6 AP (text) and 45.8 AP (box). In zero-shot transfer, WildDet3D outperforms 3D-MOOD Swin-B by +16.5 ODS on Argoverse 2 (40.3 vs. 23.8) and +17.4 ODS on ScanNet (48.9 vs. 31.5), with AP improvements of +28.6 and +27.7 respectively. On Stereo4D with real stereo depth, the model reaches 27.7 AP—2.8× higher than OVMono3D-LIFT (9.9 AP). Ablation confirms the 2D auxiliary head is critical: removing it causes AP to collapse from 30.2 to 11.1 (−19.1) on Omni3D. Strengths: - Unified architecture for text, point, box, and exemplar prompts in a single model, enabling flexible human-in-the-loop 3D perception that prior methods (each supporting a single modality) could not achieve - WildDet3D-Data represents a 138× expansion in category vocabulary over Omni3D (13.5K vs. ~100), with a carefully engineered multi-model candidate generation + VLM/human verification pipeline that keeps rejection rates low (~22% overall) - Strong training efficiency: SOTA results in 12 epochs versus 80–120 for competing methods, enabled by leveraging high-quality pretrained representations (SAM3, LingBot-Depth) and a well-designed multi-task loss - Depth integration is genuinely additive: the zero-initialized fusion module means performance degrades gracefully to pure monocular when depth is unavailable, and gains are substantial when any depth is available (+20.7 AP average across settings) Weaknesses / Limitations: - The dual-backbone architecture (parallel ViT-H + DINOv2 ViT-L/14) is computationally expensive, limiting real-time on-device use without distillation/quantization; monocular 3D detection at 1008×1008 resolution requires server-side inference - Rotation estimation remains the weakest component, particularly for symmetric objects (round tables, square boxes) where orientation is visually ambiguous—the paper acknowledges this but does not provide a systematic solution - Performance on rare categories (< 5 samples) still lags frequent categories even with WildDet3D-Data's long-tail coverage, indicating the dataset skew is not fully resolved - Camera intrinsic estimation introduces 3D localization errors in fully uncalibrated inference; the gap relative to GT intrinsics is noted as an open challenge but not quantified
Recommended 2604.08626 PDF
#82 — Innovative (65)

Clinically grounded multi-agent artificial intelligence for preventive health management

researchsquare.com · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: G-Health is an impressive systems-level integration of multi-agent orchestration, 20 specialist risk models, and a three-stage clinical alignment pipeline, validated through a methodologically rigorous blinded evaluation with 79 medical raters. The finding that DPO dominates SFT for clinical decision behavior is a transferable insight. However, the individual components (LLM alignment, AutoGluon classifiers, RAG, SHAP explanations) are well-established, and the contribution is primarily in their careful composition and clinical grounding rather than methodological novelty.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Routine health examinations generate dense, heterogeneous data whose preventive value is realized only when findings are consistently interpreted, risk-stratified, and translated into actionable follow-up. In practice, this is distributed across clinicians and time, leading to variability in detecting subtle abnormalities and deciding when to intervene. Workforce shortages and burnout exacerbate the problem. Existing medical LLMs are built for isolated question answering or diagnosis, not the longitudinal, multi-step decisions of preventive care; they seldom combine structured examination data with patient dialogue, rarely offer calibrated multi-disease risk assessment, and frequently produce hallucinated or guideline-inconsistent outputs. Methodology: The paper presents G-Health, a multi-agent AI framework that addresses this gap through three interlocking components. First, backbone LLMs (Qwen3-14B and Qwen3-32B) undergo a three-stage clinical alignment pipeline: (1) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on 2.8M medical dialogue pairs for broad medical knowledge; (2) Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) on 1.7M preference-labeled pairs to instill conservative, guideline-concordant behavior; and (3) task-specific fine-tuning on 3,503 de-identified real-world health examination reports from 123 hospitals, using the PPMSA-Factory pipeline which synthesizes persona-conditioned multi-role clinical dialogues with automated quality control. Second, 20 specialist risk-assessment tool models are trained — 14 tabular classifiers (AutoGluon), 2 regression models, 3 image classifiers (AutoGluon AutoMM with 5-fold CV), and 1 YOLO-based breast lesion detector — covering diseases from hypertension and diabetes to nasopharyngeal cancer and eye diseases. A ResNet-18 routing classifier assigns uploaded images to the correct specialist model. SHAP-based explanations provide local and global feature attributions for tabular models; occlusion-sensitivity heatmaps are used for imaging. Third, the multi-agent framework orchestrates these components: a Conversation Agent routes inputs to risk-assessment or report-analysis workflows; a six-step report analysis pipeline extracts and standardizes indicators, grades abnormalities, and generates structured follow-up and referral guidance; a Nurse Agent synthesizes lay-friendly personalized recommendations. Throughout, a RAG module grounded in 987 curated medical textbooks, clinical notes, and consensus statements reduces reliance on model memory. Key Results: Across 13 medical and general benchmarks, the aligned G-Health models achieve the best average rank among 13 strong baselines. DPO drives the largest gains: the 32B model improves from 0.7523 (base) to 0.7849 after preference optimization, with the aligned 14B model slightly outperforming the unaligned 32B. On clinically high-stakes tasks, G-Health-14B-Base improves over its Qwen3 backbone by 8.8% on Health Management and 12.0% on MedQA, reaching 0.891 accuracy; the 32B variant reaches 0.911. Report-specific tuning adds structural fidelity (ROUGE-L of 0.1235 and 0.1180 for 14B and 32B respectively) with minimal catastrophic forgetting (< 0.5 pp accuracy drop). For risk models, 11 of 14 tabular classifiers achieve ≥ 0.8 across all metrics; maternal health AUC reaches 0.9370/0.8718/0.9667 per risk stratum; cardiac risk regression achieves R² = 0.9603; breast lesion detection achieves mAP@0.50 = 0.92 and recall = 1.00; image routing yields 100% accuracy across 4 modalities. In a fully blinded human evaluation with 79 raters (18 physicians, 15 residents, 46 students) rating 111 reports from 4 LLMs and 12 physicians across 5 dimensions on a 1–5 Likert scale, G-Health-32B-Agent ranked first on all five dimensions across all rater groups, outperforming GPT-5, Gemini-2.5-Pro, DeepSeek-R1, and the physician cohort, with the largest advantages in risk identification and comprehensiveness. Strengths: - The separation of free-text generation from quantitative risk estimation is a principled design choice that mitigates a core failure mode of medical LLMs (hallucinated risk numbers), grounding numerical outputs in calibrated specialist models with feature-level SHAP explanations. - The three-stage alignment pipeline is carefully ablated and the finding that DPO dominates over SFT for clinical decision behavior is a concrete, transferable insight — preference-based training better shapes conservative, guideline-following behavior than imitation learning alone. - The blinded human evaluation protocol is methodologically rigorous: 79 medically trained raters, 6,955 records, double-blind randomization, multi-seniority stratification, and comparison against both frontier LLMs and practicing physicians provides strong external validity for the reported results. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper does not report prospective clinical outcome validation — all evaluation is benchmark-based or preference-based (blinded rating), leaving unanswered whether G-Health actually changes clinical decisions, reduces missed findings, or improves patient health outcomes in deployment. - The training data (reports, dialogue, DPO pairs) is heavily Chinese-centric (Chinese medical dialogue corpora, Chinese national exam benchmarks, hospitals in Sichuan/Chongqing), and the system was piloted at a single health examination center, raising serious questions about generalizability to non-Chinese clinical contexts, report formats, and disease prevalence distributions.
Citations
#83 — Innovative (65)

PRAGMA: Revolut Foundation Model

Maxim Ostroukhov, Ruslan Mikhailov, Vladimir Iashin, Artem Sokolov, Andrei Akshonov, Vitaly Protasov, Dmitrii Beloborodov, Vince Mullin, Roman Yokunda Enzmann, Georgios Kolovos, Jason Renders, Pavel Nesterov, Anton Repushko · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: PRAGMA introduces a well-designed hierarchical three-branch Transformer architecture that cleanly separates profile state, event-level, and history-level encoding for heterogeneous banking data—a meaningful structural contribution over flat serialization approaches. The multi-level masking strategy (token, event, key) tailored to financial record structure and the varlen packing engineering achieving 2–5× throughput are solid innovations. However, the entirely internal evaluation against undisclosed baselines, lack of any public benchmark or data release, and the fundamental single-user limitation (catastrophic AML failure) significantly limit the paper's verifiable contribution and broader impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern banks accumulate vast, heterogeneous event streams—card transactions, app navigation, peer-to-peer transfers, trading, communications—alongside static profile attributes. Modeling these jointly is hard: naive text serialization inflates sequence lengths, destroys numerical magnitude, and forces practitioners into siloed, task-specific pipelines requiring heavy feature engineering. Prior transaction-ledger models (nuFormer, TransactionGPT) handle a single event source and evaluate on one or two tasks, while tabular Transformers (TabTransformer, FT-Transformer) operate on fixed-schema single rows. No published multi-source encoder backbone with explicit profile state transfers across a broad suite of discriminative banking tasks. Methodology: PRAGMA is an encoder-only Transformer family (10M, 100M, 1B parameters) pre-trained with masked language modeling (MLM) on a Revolut corpus of 26M user records, 24B events, and 207B tokens spanning 25 months (2023–2025). The core tokenization decomposes each event field into a semantic type (key, ~60-token vocabulary), a value (numerical→percentile buckets; categorical→single token; text→BPE subwords; ~28K tokens), and a temporal coordinate (log-seconds to previous event plus calendar cyclical features via fixed-period sinusoidal embeddings). The architecture has three hierarchical Transformer blocks: a Profile State Encoder processes static attributes and life-long milestone events using RoPE temporal encoding, an Event Encoder independently encodes each event's tokens (enabling varlen packing) and adds calendar embeddings, and a History Encoder contextualizes the concatenated [USR] + [EVT] sequence with RoPE over time-to-last-event. The MLM objective combines token-level masking (15%), event-level masking (10%), and key-level masking (10%), with some positions replaced by [UNK] as a form of input dropout. Training uses bf16, the Muon+AdamW optimizer, sequence packing with FlashAttention varlen kernels, and shard-based dynamic batching, yielding 2–5× throughput over padded baselines. Downstream adaptation uses either frozen linear probes or LoRA fine-tuning (rank=8, updating ~2–4% of parameters). Key Results: Evaluated against internal task-specific production baselines (absolute metrics withheld for commercial sensitivity), PRAGMA-L with LoRA achieves: +130.2% PR-AUC and +12.4% ROC-AUC on credit scoring; +79.4% PR-AUC and +20.4% ROC-AUC on communication engagement; +64.7% recall and +16.7% precision on external fraud; +40.5% mAP on product recommendation; +5.8% F1 on recurrent transactions; +2.6% ROC-AUC on lifetime value prediction. Scaling from S→L yields +35.2% PR-AUC on credit scoring but diminishing returns on LTV and recurrent transactions, where the 10M model is already competitive. LoRA fine-tuning consistently beats full training from scratch (e.g., +18.6% PR-AUC in communication engagement, +13.0% in credit scoring for PRAGMA-M). The profile state branch contributes +31.8% PR-AUC for credit scoring but slightly hurts communication engagement (−3.0%), confirming task-dependent utility. An optional Nemotron-1B-v2 text encoder improves credit scoring by +16.1% PR-AUC but hurts product recommendation (−6.4% mAP) and adds 18% training overhead. The model fails badly on anti-money laundering (−47.1% F0.5), which requires cross-record relational features absent from single-user encodings. Strengths: - The hierarchical three-branch design (Profile State + Event + History encoders) cleanly separates within-event, cross-event, and static-attribute context, and is ablated carefully—profile state contribution is quantified per task. - Sequence packing with varlen attention and shard-based batching is a practical engineering contribution that achieves 2–5× throughput improvement, enabling 1B-parameter training at scale on highly irregular financial sequences. - The multi-masking strategy (token, event, key levels) tailored to financial record structure is a principled extension of MLM that forces richer reconstruction objectives beyond what standard token masking would achieve. Weaknesses / Limitations: - All results are reported as relative improvements against undisclosed internal baselines, making independent replication and comparison to the broader literature impossible; the true difficulty of the tasks and headroom of the baselines remain opaque. - The model is fundamentally limited to per-user isolated encoding, which causes a catastrophic −47.1% F0.5 failure on AML where the signal is cross-user network structure—a major use case in banking that the paper acknowledges but leaves entirely to future work. - The evaluation is entirely internal (Revolut data, internal baselines), with no public benchmark or dataset release, preventing any external validation of the claims or reproducibility by the research community.
Search Results 2604.08649 PDF
#84 — Innovative (64)

SpecMoE: A Fast and Efficient Mixture-of-Experts Inference via Self-Assisted Speculative Decoding

Jehyeon Bang, Eunyeong Cho, Ranggi Hwang, Jinha Chung, Minsoo Rhu · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: SpecMoE contributes a creative architectural insight: reusing GPU-resident hot experts as a zero-cost draft model for speculative decoding in CPU-offloaded MoE inference, eliminating the need for separate draft model weights. The self-reinforcing property — bandwidth savings compound at larger batch sizes where offloading hurts most — is elegant. The approach is training-free and architecture-agnostic, which matters for practical deployment. Speedup degradation on low-skewness models and manual hyperparameter tuning limit generality.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLMs such as Llama-4, DeepSeek-R1, and Mixtral require far more memory than dense models of equivalent compute cost, forcing practitioners to offload expert parameters to CPU DRAM. In CPU-offloaded MoE inference, experts must be fetched over the narrow PCIe channel for each generated token, and this expert migration dominates end-to-end latency—accounting for up to 90% of per-step time and exceeding 28 GB of transfer per step at batch size 256. Prior mitigations either hide latency by overlapping migration with computation (ineffective beyond single-batch inference because migration scales linearly with batch size while compute does not) or cache frequently-used experts in GPU memory (limited by the small fraction of experts that fit in GPU and by the large expert fan-out at large batches). Neither approach addresses the root cause: the sheer volume of data traversing PCIe every decoding step. Methodology: SpecMoE applies speculative decoding to CPU-offloaded MoE inference without requiring any additional model training. The key insight is that MoE models exhibit skewed expert activation hotness—a small fraction of experts absorbs the bulk of token routing—so a lightweight draft model can be constructed entirely from parameters already resident in GPU memory. Concretely, SpecMoE's draft model consists of the non-expert (always-on-GPU) transformer layers shared with the target model, augmented with N "hot" experts per MoE block pinned in GPU memory. During the speculation phase, draft tokens are generated autoregressively using only GPU-resident parameters, incurring zero PCIe traffic. In the verification phase, the target model processes all γ draft tokens in parallel, fetching required non-draft experts from CPU exactly once per expert per verification round—coalescing what would otherwise be γ separate sequential migrations into a single batched transfer. Two auxiliary mechanisms maintain draft quality: (1) an expert replacement policy that monitors which experts were activated during verification and promotes the temporally hottest N experts into the draft set for the next round (free, since those experts are already in GPU memory from the verification fetch); and (2) an affinity-based routing mechanism backed by a precomputed L2-distance table that redirects tokens to the most similar available draft expert when the gate would select a non-draft expert. The system is implemented on top of HuggingFace Accelerate with a custom speculative decoding loop that supports dynamic draft expert selection and batched verification. Key Results: On NLLB-MoE (128 experts, skewness 0.84) with a single NVIDIA H100 and PCIe 5.0 at 64 GB/s, SpecMoE achieves up to 4.30× throughput improvement over MoE-OnDemand and reaches 123 tokens/sec at batch size 256, far above all baselines. CPU-to-GPU data transfer is reduced by 76.73% vs. MoE-OnDemand/MoE-Overlap and by 71.89% vs. MoE-Caching; crucially, transfer volume grows only 10.96× when scaling batch size from 1 to 256, compared to 45× for on-demand baselines. The temporal expert replacement policy (HotTemporal) generates 7.265 tokens per verification step vs. 6.812 for random expert selection and 7.220 for a static global-hotness policy, translating to a 14.3% end-to-end speedup over random. Affinity-based routing produces 7.23% more accepted tokens per step than random fallback. On Mixtral-8x7B (skewness 0.32) SpecMoE achieves 2.17× over MoE-OnDemand (vs. 1.53× for MoE-Overlap and 1.15× for MoE-Caching). On Llama-4-Scout (218 GB, skewness 0.59) SpecMoE achieves 1.42×. When experts are offloaded to SSD, SpecMoE delivers 2.25× throughput improvement vs. 1.05× and 1.29× for the overlap and caching baselines respectively. Strengths: - Addresses the PCIe bandwidth bottleneck structurally rather than hiding it: expert migrations are coalesced and deduplicated across multiple tokens in the verification phase, yielding bandwidth reductions that grow with batch size rather than diminishing. - Completely training-free and architecture-agnostic—no fine-tuning, no model modifications, no separate draft model weights or KV cache allocation, which is critical for resource-constrained CPU-offload deployments. - The self-reinforcing dynamic: at larger batch sizes, more tokens are verified simultaneously, increasing the probability that duplicate expert activations are coalesced, so the bandwidth advantage compounds exactly where CPU-offload systems need it most. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Performance advantage over MoE-Caching disappears or reverses at batch size 1 and small batches (≤4), requiring a software-level fallback that adds implementation complexity and may hurt latency-sensitive single-request scenarios. - Speedup degrades substantially for models with near-uniform expert activation (e.g., Llama-4-Scout at 1.42×), and the paper provides limited analysis or adaptation strategy for models where hotness skewness is low by design rather than incidentally. - The γ and N hyperparameters must be tuned per-model: NLLB-MoE uses γ=10 and N=4, while lower-hotness models need γ=5 to avoid wasted speculation—this manual tuning effort is not addressed in the paper.
Search Results 2604.10152 PDF
#85 — Innovative (64)

From Perception to Planning: Evolving Ego-Centric Task-Oriented Spatiotemporal Reasoning via Curriculum Learning

Xiaoda Yang, Yuxiang Liu, Shenzhou Gao, Can Wang, Jingyang Xue, Lixin Yang, Yao Mu, Tao Jin, Shuicheng Yan, Zhimeng Zhang, Zhou Zhao · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The paper's strongest novelty lies in its diagnostic contribution — the forward/inverse accuracy gap metric cleanly exposes chronological bias exploitation in VLMs, revealing that even strong closed-source models rely on order statistics rather than causal reasoning. However, the core methodology (three-stage curriculum learning with CoT supervision, bidirectional data augmentation, subtask decomposition) assembles well-established techniques rather than introducing fundamentally new ones, and the binary frame-comparison evaluation is a limited proxy for actual embodied planning capability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vision-language models (VLMs) trained on passive video data develop a "chronological bias" — they implicitly assume that the latter of two frames in any sequence must represent greater task completion, rather than reasoning causally about visual state. This shortcut works on clean, chronologically ordered datasets but produces severe spatiotemporal hallucinations in egocentric, embodied settings where action failures, partial progress, and state regressions occur frequently. Existing benchmarks and models conflate two distinct failure modes — "mis-seeing" (poor spatial perception) and "mis-thinking" (poor long-horizon logical reasoning) — without separately measuring or addressing them. The result is that models like InternVL-8B achieve >99% forward accuracy on long-horizon tasks while dropping below 2% on the same task with frames reversed, revealing pure order exploitation rather than genuine reasoning. Methodology: The paper introduces EgoTSR, a curriculum learning framework built around three progressively harder training stages and a 46M-sample dataset (EgoTSR-Data) derived from the Agibot-World egocentric robotic manipulation corpus. Stage 1 (15M CoT samples) trains on frame pairs with full chain-of-thought supervision, forcing explicit spatial descriptions followed by a logical conclusion ("Perception-Planning-Decision" pathway). Stage 2 (16M Tag samples) strips intermediate reasoning, applying weak supervision that compels the model to map raw visual input directly to a task-state judgment, internalizing the Stage 1 logic into efficient visual intuition. Stage 3 (15M LongTag samples) introduces long-horizon sequences conditioned on a "Logical Skeleton" — an ordered list of orthogonal atomic subtasks generated by a separately trained Subtask Planner. The planner is trained with next-token prediction over ground-truth subtask sequences and used autoregressively at inference. Crucially, bidirectional frame pair extraction (both forward and inverse tuples for every sample) is used throughout, directly attacking the chronological bias at the data level. A Dual-Level Evaluation Framework decouples "mis-seeing" (Level 1: frame-interval accuracy across ∆t ∈ {5…12+}×10) from "mis-thinking" (Level 2: forward vs. inverse accuracy on intra-, inter-, and multi-task frame pairs), with a robustness metric defined as high average accuracy and near-zero forward/inverse gap. The model is fine-tuned from Qwen2.5-VL-7B using AdamW (lr=2e-7) on NVIDIA H800 GPUs. Key Results: EgoTSR-LongTag achieves 87.5% on Level 1 (short-range perception) and 92.4% on Level 2 (long-horizon planning), with a forward/inverse gap of only 0.1 — compared to gaps of 89.5 for the Stage 1-only model and 4.9 for mixed (non-sequential) training of all stages. In direct comparison: GPT-4o scores 76.8% / 58.2% (short/long), Gemini-2.5-Pro 80.2% / 62.0%, and GPT-o3 62.8% / 69.0%. The best open-source 2D baseline (DeepSeek-VL2) reaches 69.9% / 49.7%. EgoTSR-LongTag outperforms all closed- and open-source baselines on long-horizon tasks by a large margin (~23 points over the best closed-source model, GPT-o3). Ablations confirm that sequential stage ordering is indispensable (S1+S2+S3 at 92.4% vs. mixed S1-S2-S3 at 69.6%), and that the Subtask Planner is the decisive factor for long-horizon accuracy (81.1% without vs. 92.4% with). Training curves show long-task accuracy monotonically climbing from 74.3% to 92.4% across 36k steps while short-task accuracy holds stable at ~87.5%, demonstrating no catastrophic forgetting. Strengths: - The bidirectional evaluation methodology is a genuinely rigorous diagnostic: the forward/inverse gap metric cleanly separates models that reason causally from those exploiting order statistics, exposing previously unreported failure modes in strong closed-source models. - The three-stage curriculum is well-motivated and ablated thoroughly — both the ordering of stages and the Subtask Planner are shown to be individually necessary, not merely helpful. - Real-robot validation across multiple platforms (Franka, Agibot, So-100) and multiple simulation environments (LIBERO, SIMPLER, RoboTwin) provides stronger deployment evidence than simulation-only work. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The dataset is entirely derived from a single source (Agibot-World), raising questions about domain coverage and transferability to environments with different visual statistics, task distributions, or camera configurations not present in robotic manipulation data. - The evaluation task is fundamentally binary (which of two frames is closer to completion), which is a necessary but weak proxy for full embodied planning; the paper does not close the loop to actual task success rates in robot execution, leaving the connection between spatiotemporal reasoning accuracy and deployed robot performance unmeasured. - The 46M-sample dataset construction details are underspecified in the main paper regarding annotation quality control, inter-annotator agreement, and how subtask ground-truth sequences were validated, making reproducibility difficult to assess.
Search Results 2604.10517 PDF
#86 — Innovative (64)

Optimizing Attention for Large Language Model Inference on the MT-3000 Many-Core Processor

dl.acm.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: DeferAttention presents a genuinely clever architectural rethinking: recognizing that FlashAttention's tile-fusion invariant is fundamentally incompatible with processors lacking cheap warp-level collectives, and proving an alternative deferred-reduction formulation with identical numerical output. The memory-centric co-design with four-stage pipelining and runtime-adaptive kernel selection is thorough and well-validated, achieving 85% of hardware peak. However, the contribution is tightly scoped to a single proprietary processor (MT-3000) with limited evidence of broader transferability, and the cost model degrades precisely at short decode sequences where it matters most.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: State-of-the-art attention kernels (FlashAttention family) are architected around GPU-specific primitives—hardware-managed caches, warp-level reduction collectives, and unified shared memory—that do not exist on emerging heterogeneous many-core processors. The MT-3000, a VLIW+SIMD processor deployed in China's new-generation Tianhe supercomputer, has a radically different memory model: a four-level explicitly managed hierarchy (DDR, GSM, AM, SM) with DMA-driven data movement and no native reduction primitives. Porting GPU-centric kernels naively yields only 2–15% of hardware peak.
