Three concrete challenges are identified: (1) asymmetric, explicitly managed multi-tier memory requiring hand-coded tiling and DMA scheduling, (2) fine-grained softmax reductions that are 10× more expensive on small tiles because they must be emulated via scalar SVR operations, and (3) phase-sensitive performance—prefill is compute-bound while decode is bandwidth/latency-bound—making static kernels suboptimal across the full inference workload. Methodology: The paper proposes DeferAttention, a hardware-conscious attention framework built around three co-designed components. (1) Deferred-Reduction Attention Strategy: Instead of fusing softmax immediately after each BMM-I tile (as FlashAttention does), DeferAttention first accumulates all unnormalized score tiles S into a global GSM buffer, then applies a single coarse-grained softmax pass over the full score matrix. This decoupling reduces the number of expensive row-wise reduction operations from O(M·N / (Mg·Ng)) to O(M/Mg), amortizing scalar-SVR overhead over much larger tiles. The approach is proven numerically equivalent (within 10⁻⁶ tolerance) to fused attention. (2) Memory-Centric Operator Design: A four-stage pipeline (BMM-I → Softmax → BMM-II → Rescale) is mapped onto the MT-3000 hierarchy using memory-tier-aware tiling—query tiles in GSM, key tiles transposed into AM, fine-grained sub-tiles streamed into SM—combined with double and triple software buffering to overlap DMA transfers with VFMA computation and eliminate pipeline stalls. Modular micro-kernels (bmm-I, bmm-II, softmax, rescale) exploit outer-product SIMD formulations and can be composed in either fused or deferred mode. The bmm-I kernel uses a 6×512×48 blocking configuration requiring 18 vector registers for full VFMA saturation. (3) Runtime-Adaptive Kernel Selection: A lightweight analytical cycle-count model parameterized by B, M, N, K, L estimates the total cost of Scheme 1 (tile-fused, preferred for decode and short sequences) and Scheme 2 (deferred-reduction, preferred for long prefill sequences) and selects the cheaper option at each inference step. The model operates under a compute-bound assumption with memory latency hidden by pipelining, runs in under 0.1% of total latency, and achieves <5% relative error for sequence lengths ≥512. Key Results: At the micro-kernel level, bmm-I achieves 98% of theoretical hardware peak (338.7 GFlops) and bmm-II achieves 91% (314.5 GFlops) at large tile shapes. At the full operator level, DeferAttention reaches 85% of hardware peak (>293 GFlops) across tested configurations, compared to 46–47% for FlashAttention and 2–15% for Vanilla Attention. This yields up to 1.8× speedup over FlashAttention in long-sequence prefill and 5–30× over Vanilla Attention. End-to-end on GPT-small, GPT-medium, LLaMA2-7B, and LLaMA3-8B, DeferAttention delivers up to 15% higher token throughput than FlashAttention across batch sizes 1–8, with gains more pronounced for larger models where memory reuse benefits are greater. The dynamic kernel selector consistently matches or exceeds the better of the two fixed schemes across all tested configurations. Multi-core scaling from 1 to 12 threads grows from ~280 GFlops to >1.1 TFlops in aggregate, though per-core efficiency drops to ~30% at 12 threads due to GSM bandwidth contention. Ablation confirms pipelining is the dominant contributor (removing it drops speedup from 1.0 to 0.824), followed by deferred reduction (0.944 without it), with buffer reuse providing marginal gain alone but acting synergistically. Strengths: - The deferred-reduction insight is a genuine architectural rethinking, not a port: it identifies that the tile-fusion invariant of FlashAttention is fundamentally incompatible with architectures lacking cheap warp-level collectives, and proves an alternative formulation with identical numerical output. - The memory-centric co-design—tiling policy, DMA buffering strategy, and micro-kernel ISA usage—is detailed and concrete, with formal memory capacity constraint tables and explicit buffer reuse proofs, making the work reproducible and instructive for similar architectures. - The runtime-adaptive cost model is appropriately lightweight (arithmetic only, <0.1% overhead) yet accurate enough (< 5% error for n≥512) to drive correct scheme selection; the ablation and scheme-comparison experiments clearly validate the need for adaptivity. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is limited to a single proprietary processor (MT-3000) with no comparison to other non-GPU accelerators (e.g., RISC-V vector cores, Ascend NPUs), making it difficult to assess how broadly the design principles transfer in practice despite the generalization discussion. - The cost model breaks down at short sequences (n=64–128, up to 60% relative error) precisely where decode performance matters most; scheme selection at these lengths relies on trend extrapolation rather than accurate prediction, which could degrade real-world decode latency. - Frontier-scale models (LLaMA-3.1 70B, 405B) cannot be evaluated on a single MT-3000 node due to 128 GB memory limits, leaving open the question of whether multi-node attention with inter-node communication would preserve the efficiency gains demonstrated in single-node experiments.
Search Results
#87 — Innovative (64)

Neural - Symbolic Knowledge Tracing: Injecting Educational Knowledge into Deep Learning for Responsible Learner Modelling

Danial Hooshyar, Gustav Šír, Yeongwook Yang, Tommi Kärkkäinen, Raija Hämäläinen, Ekaterina Krivich, Mutlu Cukurova, Dragan Gašević, Roger Azevedo · arxiv.org · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: This paper introduces a genuinely novel integration of Lifted Relational Neural Networks with deep knowledge tracing, providing the first principled, fully differentiable mechanism for injecting symbolic pedagogical rules into sequential learner models. The evaluation framework — including directional inconsistency rates and stage-wise error analysis — meaningfully advances how knowledge tracing models should be assessed beyond standard AUC. The ablation confirming that symbolic rules (not just relational architecture) drive gains is a clean and informative result. However, evaluation on a single small dataset with only vanilla RNN baselines, omitting modern attention-based and graph-based DKT variants, prevents strong claims about state-of-the-art competitiveness.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deep Knowledge Tracing (DKT) — the dominant approach for modeling student knowledge over time in intelligent tutoring systems — suffers from three well-documented failure modes: it cannot incorporate structured educational knowledge (causal relationships, mastery thresholds, pedagogical rules), it is susceptible to spurious correlations and dataset biases (class imbalance, noisy responses), and it is a black box whose sequential predictions can be temporally inconsistent (mastery estimates rising after repeated wrong answers). Meanwhile, LLMs are increasingly marketed as drop-in replacements for learner modeling, yet empirical comparisons show they produce unstable mastery trajectories and low sensitivity to learner context. With the EU AI Act classifying educational AI as high-risk, there is regulatory and ethical pressure for interpretable, pedagogically grounded alternatives. The paper targets exactly this gap: a DKT variant that is simultaneously more accurate, more temporally consistent, and intrinsically interpretable. Methodology: The authors propose Responsible-DKT, implemented via PyNeuraLogic — a Lifted Relational Neural Network (LRNN) framework that represents student interactions as logical ground facts and model architecture as parameterized, weighted logic rules, unified into a single differentiable program. Each timestep interaction (skill, quiz, correctness) is encoded as symbolic predicates. A two-layer RNN processes the embedded sequence in the usual DKT fashion. On top of the neural backbone, two symbolic mastery rules are injected: `mastered(x,t)` fires after two consecutive correct responses on skill/quiz x; `not_mastered(x,t)` fires after three consecutive incorrect responses. A third pathway, `avg_embed(t,x)`, aggregates historical embeddings for concept x. All three pathways — recurrent state, mastery rules, and historical aggregation — contribute additively to the final sigmoid prediction, with learnable rule weights. Grounding translates the lifted template into a directed acyclic computation graph specific to each student's history, enabling exact gradient attribution to individual input facts and rule activations. Three models are compared on a 6th-grade Estonian mathematics dataset (21,471 interactions, 167 students, 13 skills, Opiq platform): Responsible-DKT (rules enabled), BaseNS-DKT (same architecture, rules disabled), and Classic-DKT (vanilla PyTorch RNN). Evaluation spans standard AUC/accuracy metrics, stage-wise prediction errors (early/middle/late sequence segments), trajectory volatility, and prediction inconsistency rate (fraction of updates in the wrong direction relative to the observed outcome). Key Results: Responsible-DKT achieves up to 0.90 AUC versus a ceiling of approximately 0.80 for Classic-DKT across all training ratios and sequence lengths — a consistent 8–13 percentage point improvement. With only 10% of training data and short sequences (length 10), it already reaches 0.80 AUC; Classic-DKT cannot surpass 0.76 even with full data. Responsible-DKT shows the lowest inconsistency rate in all configurations (0.36–0.41 vs. 0.43–0.48 for baselines), and inconsistency declines as sequence length grows, indicating that longer histories further stabilize rule-guided updates. Early- and mid-sequence error rates are substantially lower (e.g., 14.35% vs. 18.92% early-stage error at full sequence length vs. Classic-DKT). Rule importance analysis reveals that the `not_mastered` rule, though activated rarely (49 times vs. 219 for `mastered`), carries the highest gradient magnitude (0.0016), meaning repeated-error patterns exert disproportionate influence on parameter updates — an empirical validation of pedagogical assumptions about failure signals. BaseNS-DKT (neural-symbolic architecture without rules) performs comparably to Classic-DKT, confirming that symbolic rules — not relational representation alone — drive the gains. Strengths: - First application of Lifted Relational Neural Networks to sequential knowledge tracing, providing a principled, fully differentiable mechanism for injecting and learning from symbolic pedagogical rules within a temporal student model. - Evaluation goes substantially beyond AUC to include stage-wise errors, prediction volatility, and directional inconsistency rates — metrics directly relevant to adaptive system reliability that are largely absent from the DKT literature. - Intrinsic, mechanism-level interpretability (local gradient × input attribution and global rule/skill importance) without post-hoc surrogates like SHAP or LIME, enabling educators to inspect and refine the model's pedagogical assumptions empirically. - Strong data efficiency: exceeding 0.80 AUC with 10% training data is practically significant given the typical scarcity and imbalance of real classroom interaction logs. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments rely on a single small dataset (167 students, one subject, one country, one month of data), making generalizability claims premature; no evaluation on standard DKT benchmarks (ASSIST09, ASSIST17, EdNet) prevents direct comparison with the literature. - Baselines are limited to vanilla RNN-based DKT; the method is not compared against modern attention-based (SAKT, AKT, SAINT+) or graph-based (GKT, GIKT) DKT variants, which consistently outperform the classic RNN baseline and represent the actual state of the art. - The symbolic rules are pedagogically thin — simple consecutive-response thresholds chosen well below established mastery learning standards (7 learning opportunities) due to data sparsity — and the framework's flexibility to encode richer knowledge (prerequisite graphs, forgetting curves, spacing effects) is demonstrated only in the discussion, not in experiments. - Higher volatility in Responsible-DKT (0.17–0.18 vs. 0.12–0.13 for baselines) reflects sharp probability swings when rules fire, which can be pedagogically undesirable in practice and may alarm learners or teachers even if inconsistency is lower on average.
Search Results 2604.08263 PDF
#88 — Innovative (63)

Rethinking On-Policy Distillation of Large Language Models: Phenomenology, Mechanism, and Recipe (59 ▲ )

Yaxuan Li, Yuxin Zuo, Bingxiang He, Jinqian Zhang, Chaojun Xiao, Cheng Qian, Tianyu Yu, Huan-ang Gao, Wenkai Yang, Zhiyuan Liu, Ning Ding · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 63 as a thorough empirical investigation that identifies actionable governing conditions for on-policy distillation success — thinking-pattern consistency and novel knowledge — supported by clean causal experiments like reverse distillation. The mechanistic finding that overlap tokens carry 97-99% of probability mass is a useful insight for practitioners. The work is primarily analytical rather than methodologically novel, and the proposed recipes (cold start, prompt selection) are relatively straightforward.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: On-policy distillation (OPD) has become a core technique in LLM post-training — used in Qwen3, MiMo, GLM-5, and others — yet its training dynamics remain poorly understood and fragile in practice. A striking failure mode exists: a stronger teacher (by benchmark score) can completely fail to improve a student, while a weaker teacher succeeds. This paper addresses the gap between OPD's practical adoption and the absence of principled understanding of when and why it works or fails. Methodology: The paper structures its investigation into three layers. (1) Phenomenology: Controlled experiments varying teacher choice across DeepSeek and Qwen model families identify two governing conditions for OPD success — thinking-pattern consistency (high initial overlap in top-k token distributions between student and teacher) and the presence of genuinely new knowledge in the teacher beyond what the student has already seen during training. Reverse distillation experiments (using a weaker or earlier checkpoint as teacher) validate both conditions simultaneously. (2) Mechanism: Token-level dynamics are tracked using three metrics — overlap ratio (fraction of tokens in shared top-k sets), overlap-token advantage (distributional agreement within the overlap), and entropy gap (|H(q_t) - H(p_t)|). The paper ablates Top-k OPD by training on only overlap tokens vs. only non-overlap tokens to test whether overlap is causally sufficient. (3) Recipe: Two practical recovery strategies are proposed for failing configurations — off-policy cold start (SFT on teacher rollouts before OPD) and teacher-aligned prompt selection (using prompts from the teacher's post-training data). A final section examines reward quality degradation with trajectory depth, including teacher-continuation accuracy across prefix lengths and entropy heatmaps by output position. Key Results: Thinking-pattern consistency governs OPD more than benchmark score: a GRPO-trained Qwen3-4B teacher outperforms a higher-scoring non-thinking Qwen3-4B teacher when distilling into Qwen3-1.7B-Base, correlating with higher initial overlap ratio. New knowledge matters beyond scale: in the DeepSeek family, the same-pipeline R1-Distill-7B teacher yields only 5.3% teacher-student gap recovery versus 16.9% for the RL-post-trained Skywork-OR1-Math-7B; in the Qwen family, 15.6% vs. 58.6% respectively. Reverse distillation shows that R1-Distill-7B and R1-Distill-1.5B are distributionally indistinguishable from the student's perspective — both drive a stronger student back to pre-RL performance levels. Mechanistically, successful OPD raises overlap ratio from ~72% to ~91% while failing runs stagnate; the shared top-k tokens carry 97–99% of total probability mass throughout training. Optimizing only overlap tokens matches full Student Top-k OPD, while non-overlap tokens contribute negligibly. Off-policy cold start (200K SFT samples) substantially raises initial overlap and final performance. Teacher-aligned prompts improve performance but suppress entropy, requiring mixing with out-of-distribution prompts. Reward quality degrades sharply with trajectory length: teacher accuracy advantage drops from +0.37 at 1K prefix to +0.02 at 16K, and response lengths beyond 7K tokens trigger late-stage training collapse propagating from suffix to prefix. Surprisingly, failing teachers (R1-Distill-7B) produce globally informative reward (AUROC 0.75 vs. 0.73) — failure is attributed to anisotropic per-token advantages that cancel in gradient aggregation, not to weak global signal quality. Strengths: - The three-layer decomposition (phenomenology → mechanism → recipe) is rigorous and internally consistent; each finding flows causally into the next. - The reverse distillation experiment is a particularly clean natural experiment that simultaneously validates both governing conditions and reveals that OPD fundamentally transfers thinking patterns, not just accuracy. - The causal sufficiency test for overlap tokens (ablating to overlap-only vs. non-overlap-only Top-k) is a strong mechanistic confirmation, not just a correlation. - The reward degradation analysis with trajectory depth is practically important and points to a fundamental ceiling for applying OPD to long-horizon and agentic settings. Weaknesses / Limitations: - All experiments are confined to mathematical reasoning benchmarks (AIME 2024/2025, AMC 2023) using a narrow set of model families (DeepSeek, Qwen); generalizability to code, open-ended generation, or cross-architecture settings is entirely unvalidated and explicitly flagged as future work. - The anisotropy hypothesis (per-token advantages cancel in gradient aggregation for mismatched teachers) is proposed but not verified — the paper acknowledges this would require analyzing per-token gradient directional structure, leaving the mechanistic explanation for failure incomplete. - The 'new knowledge' condition is operationalized entirely through RL post-training; the role of pre-training data differences is acknowledged as confounded by tokenizer mismatch and architectural differences and is not isolated, limiting the generality of the framework. - Training runs are relatively short (200–600 steps) which may not capture long-horizon dynamics or the eventual convergence behavior of OPD under the proposed recipes.
Recommended 2604.13016 PDF
#89 — Innovative (63)

Structure-Grounded Knowledge Retrieval via Code Dependencies for Multi-Step Data Reasoning

Xinyi Huang, Mingzhe Lu, Haoyu Dong · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: This paper scores 63 for identifying a genuinely important gap — that embedding similarity systematically fails when functional relevance is encoded in code dependency structure rather than lexical content. The AST-based DAG retrieval approach is a clean, principled alternative to similarity search for programmatic reasoning domains. However, the bootstrapping requirement of pre-existing correct solutions (generated by GPT-5.2) substantially limits practical applicability, and absolute accuracy on the hardest benchmark remains low at 24%.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLMs struggle with domain-specific multi-step data analysis tasks because the relevant knowledge is not textually similar to the query — it is instead encoded in executable code and the computational dependencies between functions. Standard RAG approaches based on lexical or embedding similarity systematically fail in this regime: they retrieve functions with superficially similar names while missing critical functions that encode essential business logic (e.g., a fee-computation function that checks whether a transaction is international has low embedding similarity to a fee-related query but is causally necessary). The paper addresses the mismatch between semantic similarity and functional relevance in code-grounded reasoning. Methodology: The authors propose Structure-Grounded Knowledge Retrieval (SGKR), a two-phase framework. In the offline phase, example solution code is parsed via Abstract Syntax Trees (AST) to extract function-call dependencies, forming a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where each node encodes a function together with its associated domain knowledge (attached as comments or descriptions). Duplicate nodes arising from the same function appearing in different execution traces are merged via canonical node collapsing with edge redirection. Semantic input/output (I/O) nodes representing data entities, attributes, and derived quantities are inserted as anchors. In the online retrieval phase, given a new question, I/O tags are extracted via keyword matching and mapped to nodes in the graph. Breadth-first search (BFS) is then run from input nodes to identify all dependency paths reaching the relevant output nodes. The union of knowledge-code nodes along discovered paths forms the retrieved subgraph, whose knowledge fields and function implementations are assembled as structured context for downstream LLM code generation. The key insight is that functional relevance propagates through call dependencies even when lexical/embedding similarity is low. Key Results: SGKR is evaluated on hard subsets (multi-step instances only) of three benchmarks: DABSTEP (378 questions, realistic data analysis), CONVFINQA (300 questions, conversational financial QA), and FINQA (360 questions, financial numerical reasoning). On DABSTEP-hard, SGKR achieves 24.34% accuracy vs. 10.58% for the best embedding-based baseline (BGE-LARGE-EN-V1.5-RAG) and 6.88% for vanilla prompting — a >2× improvement. On CONVFINQA-hard, SGKR reaches 74.67% vs. 71.67% for GraphSAGE-RAG. On FINQA-hard, 65.56% vs. 63.61% for BGE-LARGE-EN-V1.5-RAG. Crucially, SGKR retrieves fewer nodes (avg. 1.5 on financial datasets vs. 2 for baselines; 4.65 vs. 5 on DABSTEP), indicating higher precision. Ablations confirm that all components contribute: removing the knowledge annotations drops accuracy from 24.34% to 8.73%, removing node merging drops it to 12.43%, and using fixed (non-query-specific) nodes drops it to 11.64%. Strengths: - The core insight — that function-call dependency structure is a stronger retrieval signal than embedding similarity for programmatic reasoning — is well-motivated and empirically validated with a concrete qualitative failure case (Figure 1/Table 3) showing that needed functions rank low by similarity while unnecessary ones rank high. - SGKR retrieves fewer nodes than baselines while achieving higher accuracy, demonstrating that the approach improves both precision and recall simultaneously rather than trading one for the other. - The method is model-agnostic (tested on both vanilla LLMs and ReAct-based coding agents) and the ablation study systematically isolates the contribution of each component. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The approach requires pre-existing, correct, executable example solutions to build the dependency graph — a substantial bootstrapping requirement. For DABSTEP, the authors use GPT-5.2 to generate solutions, introducing an oracle-quality dependency on a strong model not available to most practitioners, and conflating graph construction quality with retrieval strategy. - Absolute accuracy on DABSTEP-hard remains low (24.34%), suggesting the framework solves only a fraction of challenging real-world data analysis tasks; the gains, while proportionally large, may not translate to practical utility at current levels. - The I/O tag extraction relies on keyword matching, with a hard fallback to no-retrieval when tags cannot be identified. This brittle step is not evaluated in isolation, and its failure rate on realistic queries is unreported.
Search Results 2604.10516 PDF
#90 — Innovative (63)

OmniShow: Unifying Multimodal Conditions for Human-Object Interaction Video Generation (55 ▲ )

Donghao Zhou, Guisheng Liu, Hao Yang, Jiatong Li, Jingyu Lin, Xiaohu Huang, Yichen Liu, Xin Gao, Cunjian Chen, Shilei Wen, Chi-Wing Fu, Pheng-Ann Heng · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 14 Apr 2026
Why this rank: OmniShow is the first system to unify all four HOIVG conditions end-to-end, and the discovery that RA2V capability emerges from weight merging alone is a genuinely surprising and transferable insight for multi-modal model composition. The gating vector diagnostic that identified audio impact concentration in dual-stream blocks is a clever efficiency finding. Individual technical components, however, are relatively standard adaptations of existing conditioning mechanisms.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Human-Object Interaction Video Generation (HOIVG) — synthesizing videos where people interact with specific objects — requires simultaneous control over four input modalities: text prompts (global semantics), reference images (appearance of human and object), audio (lip/body synchronization), and pose sequences (motion control). This combination is critical for real-world applications such as e-commerce demonstrations and short-video production, but no existing method supports all four conditions end-to-end. R2V methods lack audio; A2V methods accept only a first frame rather than full reference images; and approaches that handle partial combinations omit either pose or high-fidelity subject preservation. The field also lacks a standardized benchmark for this setting. Methodology: OmniShow builds on a 12.3B-parameter video DiT base (Waver 1.0) and introduces three coordinated contributions. First, Unified Channel-wise Conditioning injects reference images and pose through native channel concatenation. The noisy video token sequence is extended with pseudo-frame tokens along the temporal axis; both reference image VAE encodings and rendered pose video encodings are concatenated channel-wise into this expanded sequence. A reference reconstruction loss (Flow Matching on pseudo-frames, weight 1.0) forces the model to retain high-fidelity appearance details from the reference, avoiding the semantic drift seen in simpler token-concat approaches. Second, Gated Local-Context Attention provides audio-visual synchronization. Raw audio is processed by Wav2Vec 2.0 with multi-layer feature fusion, then packed into contextual windows (size 5, stride 4) aligned to the VAE temporal compression rate. Cross-attention is masked so each latent frame attends only to its corresponding audio window. Learnable gating vectors g (initialized near zero) modulate injection strength per token dimension, stabilizing early training and providing a diagnostic probe — empirical observation of g magnitudes showed audio impact concentrates in dual-stream DiT blocks, so audio modules were inserted only there, limiting parameter growth to +2.5% (versus +21.4% for the competing HuMo approach). Third, Decoupled-Then-Joint Training addresses severe data scarcity for full HOIVG quintuplets. Specialized R2V and A2V models are first trained independently on their respective sub-task datasets; the two models are then merged by weighted linear interpolation (0.6 A2V / 0.4 R2V for non-audio weights, with audio modules inherited from A2V), and fine-tuned jointly on RA2V data before final RAP2V fine-tuning. A key empirical finding is that viable RA2V capability emerges directly from the weight merge before any joint training, suggesting controllability can arise via model composition. The authors also construct HOIVG-Bench with AI-generated human and object images (for privacy compliance), GPT-4o-scripted speech, DWPose-extracted skeletons, and comprehensive metrics across text alignment, reference consistency (FaceSim, NexusScore), pose accuracy (AKD, PCK), audio-visual sync (Sync-C/D), and video quality (AES, IQA, VQ, MQ). Key Results: On HOIVG-Bench at 720p / 5-second clips, OmniShow achieves state-of-the-art or best-in-class performance across most metrics. In the R2V setting, it matches the 14B Phantom model on reference preservation (FaceSim 0.874 vs. 0.876) while leading on NexusScore (0.389 vs. 0.366) and video quality (VQ 11.12 vs. 10.93), using a smaller 12.3B model. In the RA2V setting, OmniShow leads on Sync-C (8.612 vs. 8.013 for HuMo-17B) and Sync-D (7.608 vs. 8.316, lower is better), along with superior NexusScore (0.369 vs. 0.344). In RP2V, it achieves the best PCK (0.460 vs. 0.336 for VACE) and AKD (0.174 vs. 0.206), demonstrating strong pose adherence. On the standard A2V EMTD benchmark, the A2V sub-model achieves Sync-C 6.49, surpassing MultiTalk. Human evaluations (30–33 participants, 20 sampled videos) show OmniShow preferred over HuMo-17B (RA2V: 51.2% vs. 21.2% on video quality) and over VACE (RP2V: 52.3% vs. 29.4%). OmniShow is the only model evaluated on the full four-condition RAP2V setting, compared to a cascaded baseline in an appendix. Strengths: - First unified end-to-end framework handling all four HOIVG conditions (text + reference images + audio + pose) with a single model, enabling the novel RAP2V task with no direct competitor. - Highly parameter-efficient design: +2.5% parameter overhead for audio versus +21.4% for HuMo, achieved by principled use of gate vector diagnostics to target dual-stream blocks only. - Decoupled-then-joint training reveals a compelling emergent behavior — RA2V capability from weight merging alone — which is a broadly transferable insight for multi-modal model composition. - HOIVG-Bench provides a well-specified, privacy-compliant benchmark with diverse metrics and rigorous data curation, filling a real gap for the community. - Thorough ablation studies isolate contributions of each component (conditioning mechanism, audio context, attention masking, gating, and training strategy), with quantitative support for every design choice. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is limited to 5-second clips even though the model supports up to 10-second generation; performance on longer sequences remains uncharacterized. - HOIVG-Bench uses AI-generated human and object images, which may introduce distribution bias relative to real-world identities — acknowledged but not quantified. - The RAP2V setting has no real baseline comparison (only a cascaded pipeline in an appendix), making it difficult to assess how much the unified architecture gains over simpler multi-stage approaches. - Training data pipelines, scale, and filtering criteria are described only at a high level; the datasets are in-house and not released, limiting reproducibility and downstream research.
Recommended 2604.11804 PDF
#91 — Innovative (63)

Towards Privacy-Preserving Large Language Model: Text-free Inference Through Alignment and Adaptation

Jeongho Yoon, Chanhee Park, Yongchan Chun, Hyeonseok Moon, Heuiseok Lim · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: PPFT scores 63 for its architecturally clean two-stage separation of alignment and noise-aware domain adaptation, which is a meaningful design insight over prior work that treats privacy noise as pure degradation. The progressive adversary evaluation (including noise-aware and zero-shot inversion attackers) is methodologically rigorous. However, the core privacy primitives (k-pooling, metric DP noise, LoRA adaptation) are individually well-established, and the unaddressed output-side privacy gap is a material limitation for the medical/legal domains targeted.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Cloud-based LLM services (MLaaS) require users to transmit raw prompt text to remote servers, creating fundamental privacy vulnerabilities: eavesdropping during transit, insider misuse, and persistent exposure through system logs or downstream training pipelines. Prior defenses are inadequate — prompt sanitization misses implicit disclosures, cryptographic inference incurs prohibitive overhead, and embedding-based transmission remains vulnerable to generative inversion attacks that reconstruct semantically faithful text from even heuristically noised embeddings. No unified framework simultaneously eliminates text transmission during both inference and fine-tuning while preserving task utility. Methodology: PPFT (Privacy-Preserving Fine-Tuning) introduces a two-stage pipeline that enforces a strictly text-free interface from client to server. In Stage 1 (Alignment), a lightweight ModernBERT-large client-side encoder maps the prompt to token embeddings, which are block-wise mean-pooled (k-Pooling) to reduce sequence length to m=⌈n/k⌉ vectors, deliberately destroying recoverable token-level detail. A trainable server-side projection layer bridges encoder and decoder dimensions, and a LoRA-adapted LLaMA decoder (1B or 8B) is jointly trained to condition generation on these continuous pooled prefix embeddings rather than discrete tokens, minimizing negative log-likelihood of responses. In Stage 2 (Domain Adaptation), isotropic Laplace noise calibrated under dχ-privacy (direction sampled uniformly on the unit sphere, magnitude from Gamma(d_e, ε)) is injected into the pooled embeddings before transmission; the server fine-tunes only its projection layer and LoRA decoder on the obfuscated embeddings with domain-specific (medical/legal) labeled data, without ever seeing raw prompts or requiring clients to disclose decoder parameters. At inference, the same pipeline applies: client encodes, k-pools, injects noise, transmits obfuscated embeddings; the server projects and generates. The privacy guarantee is formalized as per-slot metric-DP with log-loss composition across slots. Key Results: Evaluated on medical (Pri-DDX, Pri-NLICE) and legal (Pri-SLJA) QA benchmarks against three baselines (dχ-privacy, Paraphrase, PrivacyRestore) and with Llama-3.2-1B and Llama-3.1-8B decoders. PPFT achieves an average accuracy of 0.731 (8B) vs. the noise-free upper bound of 0.929, a degradation gap of only 0.198 — compared to PrivacyRestore's gap of 0.295, Paraphrase's gap of 0.553, and dχ-privacy's gap of 0.654. On legal domain (Pri-SLJA), PPFT with 8B reaches 0.905 accuracy, close to the upper bound of 0.947. On reconstruction resistance, PPFT maintains ROUGE-L below 0.25 across all privacy budgets up to ε=75. Attribute-level inversion recovery of age (0.007) and prior antecedents (0.012) is near zero. General-domain performance (CSQA, SQuAD) degrades modestly: only 0.081 on CSQA and 0.184 on SQuAD for the 8B model vs. the noise-free baseline. Under a noise-aware adversary trained on corrupted representations, PPFT (pooling-16) achieves ROUGE-L of only 0.180, while PrivacyRestore reaches 0.72 under the same attacker. Universal zero-shot embedding inversion attacks (Zhang et al.) yield ROUGE-L below 0.06 against PPFT embeddings even after 10 iterations. Strengths: - The two-stage design cleanly separates general alignment from domain-specific noise-aware adaptation, allowing the model to internalize obfuscated embeddings during fine-tuning rather than treating noise as a degradation to overcome — this is architecturally sound and likely the primary reason PPFT closes the gap to the upper bound more effectively than baselines. - Privacy budget alignment across heterogeneous methods (token-level dχ vs. vector-level PrivacyRestore vs. slot-wise PPFT) is handled rigorously via a unified global budget B, making comparisons methodologically credible and addressing a common weakness in prior work. - The paper tests against progressively stronger adversaries (standard GPT2-medium attacker, noise-aware attacker, Stage-1-aligned attacker, and state-of-the-art universal zero-shot inversion), providing convincing defense-in-depth evidence that pooling combined with noise injection disrupts the token-level alignment that inversion attacks depend on. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Output-side privacy is entirely unaddressed: PPFT guarantees only that prompts are never transmitted as text, but generated responses can still leak sensitive input information. In medical/legal domains where outputs often restate or summarize private content, this is a material gap that the paper acknowledges but does not quantify or mitigate. - The privacy gap from upper bound remains non-trivial (0.20 with 8B, 0.19 with 1B averaged across datasets), and the paper does not analyze whether this gap narrows with larger decoder scale or more Stage 2 data — the practicality claim rests on a single encoder (ModernBERT-large) and two LLaMA variants, leaving generalization to closed-source or API-based decoders entirely open. Additionally, ROUGE-L as the sole reconstruction metric conflates surface template recovery with semantic leakage; the attribute-level analysis partially compensates but only for structured medical records.
Search Results 2604.06831 PDF
#92 — Innovative (63)

In-Context Learning in Speech Language Models: Analyzing the Role of Acoustic Features, Linguistic Structure, and Induction Heads

Charlotte Pouw, Hosein Mohebbi, Afra Alishahi, Willem Zuidema · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 63 for being the first systematic study of in-context learning mechanisms in speech language models, with a well-controlled experimental design using the PRIME-LM corpus and a novel extension of induction head analysis to modality-specific tokens. The finding that lexical overlap drives ICL while semantic similarity does not—diverging from text-only results—is a meaningful empirical contribution. However, the work largely transfers existing analytical frameworks (ICL probing, induction head identification, ablation) from text to speech rather than introducing fundamentally new methods, and is limited to a single model and task.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: In-context learning (ICL) is well-studied in text-only LLMs but largely unexplored in Speech Language Models (SpeechLMs). SpeechLMs jointly model speech and text as discrete token sequences, enabling TTS, ASR, and other tasks via few-shot prompting. However, it is unknown which acoustic and linguistic features of demonstrations drive ICL performance, whether SpeechLMs mimic acoustic properties of demonstrations, and whether the induction head mechanism known to underlie text-based ICL also operates in the speech domain. Filling this gap is important for understanding how multimodal models process and exploit heterogeneous in-context information. Methodology: The study uses SPIRITLM (a Llama-2-7B backbone continuously pretrained on interleaved speech and text tokens, with HuBERT encoding and HiFi-GAN vocoding) evaluated on the TTS task. Demonstrations are constructed from the PRIME-LM corpus, which provides controlled sentence pairs with explicit linguistic overlap conditions (Core/no-overlap baseline, lexical overlap at varying degrees, semantic similarity). Acoustic manipulations are applied via Praat: speaking rate (0.5× slow, 2× fast), pitch range (flattened to utterance mean), and intensity (reduced to 30 dB). Two TTS voices (SpeechT5 and Kokoro-TTS) provide robustness checks. ICL performance is measured via WER and Content Word Recall after transcribing outputs with Whisper-large-v3-turbo. Acoustic mimicking is quantified by comparing output acoustic features across conditions. For mechanistic analysis, induction heads are identified via modality-specific prefix-matching scores computed on ICL prompts (separately for speech and text tokens), and their causal role is tested by zero-ablating the top-k (k=50) heads per group across 4,000 evaluation items. Key Results: (1) A single demonstration suffices to trigger ICL; performance plateaus after 2 demonstrations. ICL emerges even without any lexical or semantic overlap between demonstration and target. (2) Passive syntactic targets yield lower WER than active targets regardless of demonstration syntax, but this advantage vanishes under Content Word Recall, indicating the effect is driven by function word generation, not content. (3) Lexical overlap boosts ICL (full lexical overlap and function word overlap reduce WER; noun overlap improves Content Word Recall), while semantic similarity provides no consistent benefit—a divergence from text-only ICL findings. (4) Fast speaking rate (2×) substantially degrades ICL performance; slow rate (0.5×) has no negative effect and slightly helps with single demonstrations. Pitch range and intensity manipulations have negligible impact on task performance. (5) Acoustic mimicking is asymmetric: speaking rate is mimicked (slow effect after 1 demo; fast effect after 3 demos), while pitch range is not mimicked at all, and intensity mimicking is weak and voice-dependent. (6) Ablating top-50 speech-prefix induction heads causes near-complete loss of ICL ability; ablating unique speech-prefix heads (not shared with text) produces a similarly large drop; ablating unique text-prefix heads has a smaller effect. Ablating random heads causes only minor degradation. Ablating non-prefix heads slightly improves performance, suggesting these heads introduce noise. Preliminary comparison shows Llama-2-7B (text-only backbone) achieves higher prefix-matching scores than SpiritLM, suggesting speech continued pretraining may weaken induction behavior. Strengths: - Rigorous experimental design using a controlled psycholinguistic corpus (PRIME-LM) that isolates individual acoustic and linguistic variables cleanly, avoiding confounds common in naturalistic data. - Novel dual-angle evaluation of ICL in the speech domain—both task accuracy (WER, CWR) and acoustic mimicking—exploiting the unique properties of TTS output. - First mechanistic interpretability study of induction heads in a SpeechLM, with a principled extension of prefix-matching score computation to modality-specific tokens and causal ablation validation. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The study is limited to a single SpeechLM (SpiritLM) and a single task type (TTS), so generalizability to other SpeechLMs, ASR, or speech-to-speech tasks is unconfirmed. - The corpus sentences are short, syntactically simple, and fully synthesized—not naturalistic speech—which may limit ecological validity and obscure effects present in real spoken demonstrations with natural prosodic variation. - The induction head ablation uses k=50, a fixed hyperparameter with limited sensitivity analysis; the causal claim rests on zeroing output vectors rather than more precise surgical interventions, and the link between the empirical findings (speaking rate effect) and the mechanistic account (prefix-matching opportunity) remains a hypothesis rather than a demonstrated causal chain.
Recommended 2604.06356 PDF
#93 — Innovative (63)

Pseudo-Likelihood Training for Reasoning Diffusion Language Models

aclanthology.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: PADRE provides a principled theoretical foundation for RL alignment of diffusion language models by recasting KL-regularized RL as pseudo-likelihood matching grounded in Hammersley-Clifford, which is a meaningful conceptual contribution over the biased approximations in prior work. However, the practical implementation requires the same Markovian conditioning approximations it criticizes, and the narrow experimental scope (single model, limited benchmarks) constrains the demonstrated generality.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Policy-gradient reinforcement learning (PGRL) methods like GRPO have become the dominant approach for post-training LLMs on reasoning tasks, but they fundamentally require efficient computation of token-level log-likelihoods. Diffusion-based language models (dLLMs), such as LLaDA, generate text through iterative masked denoising rather than autoregressive factorization, making exact likelihood computation intractable. Existing attempts to adapt PGRL to dLLMs (e.g., diffu-GRPO/d1) rely on biased Monte-Carlo ELBO approximations or mean-field approximations of the joint density, leading to gradient bias and training instability. The gap is the lack of a principled, scalable RL alignment objective native to the dLLM parameterization. Methodology: PADRE reframes KL-regularized RL as a distribution-matching problem and replaces the standard joint KL divergence with a pseudo-KL (PKL) objective over per-token conditional distributions. The key theoretical insight is that for strictly positive distributions, matching all single-token conditionals p(y^i | y^{-i}) is sufficient to match the full joint distribution (Hammersley-Clifford). The resulting tractable objective is J_PKL = E_{y~π_θ} Σ_i [log π_θ(y^i | y^{-i}) - log q̃(y^i | y^{-i})], where q̃ is the Boltzmann target distribution. This avoids computing the partition function and requires only conditional token probabilities, which dLLMs can approximate efficiently by masking one position at a time and reading the denoiser output. To compute all L conditionals in roughly one forward pass, PADRE constructs an augmented sequence x ⊕ y_{1:L} ⊕ m_{L+1:2L} with shared positional embeddings and a custom attention mask, so each mask row L+i attends to y_{-i} without cross-contamination. Two efficient approximation variants are proposed: PKL-U (block mean-field, interpolates between full PKL and d1) and PKL-T (trajectory-based, uses the denoising unmasking order to restrict attention). Training follows the GRPO recipe with group-normalized advantages, clipped importance weights, KL regularization, and optional prompt token perturbation as dropout regularization. Key Results: On math reasoning benchmarks with LLaDA-8B as the base model, PKL+ (with prompt perturbation) achieves 87.3% on GSM8K (+5.2% over base, +5.2% over d1-LLaDA), 42.4% on MATH500 (+6.2% over base, +2.2% over d1-LLaDA), and 44.3% on MATH (+5.4% over base). The full PKL variant achieves 85.6/40.9/41.6 on the three benchmarks, consistently outperforming diffu-GRPO, d1, VRPO, and wd1. On coding benchmarks (HumanEval, MBPP), PKL achieves the best results at all tested sequence lengths (128/256/512 tokens), e.g., 36.2% on HumanEval-256 vs. 32.9% for d1 and 47.4% on MBPP-256 vs. 44.7% for d1. A KL divergence diagnostic confirms that PKL's conditional approximation error (avg KL < 1) is substantially lower than d1's mean-field approximation error (KL = 2.87–3.15 on GSM8K/MATH500). FLOP-equalized comparisons show that at sufficient compute budgets, PKL outperforms d1 on GSM8K, though d1 has an edge at very low FLOP counts. Strengths: - Theoretically principled: the pseudo-KL objective shares the same global optima as KL-regularized PGRL, unlike the biased approximations used in competing dLLM methods, and this is backed by a formal theorem (Hammersley-Clifford) and derivation. - Practical efficiency: the augmented-sequence trick with shared positional embeddings and custom attention masks enables approximate computation of all L conditionals in near-constant forward passes, making the method scalable. - Consistent empirical gains: PADRE outperforms all dLLM alignment baselines across math and coding benchmarks by meaningful margins, with the PKL-U and PKL-T variants providing a compute-accuracy tradeoff spectrum. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The conditional likelihood approximation used in practice (masking one token, reading denoiser output) involves the same Markovian approximation as prior work; the paper acknowledges that PKL's per-token conditional KL is still non-zero (avg < 1 but not 0), so the theoretical guarantee of bias-free optimization does not fully carry through to the implemented algorithm. - Experimental scope is narrow: only LLaDA-8B is evaluated, no larger model scales are tested, and tasks like Sudoku (where dLLMs already struggle) are excluded—making it unclear how well the method generalizes across model families, scales, or longer-horizon reasoning tasks where the conditioning approximations may degrade. - The efficient multi-conditional evaluation via attention mask sharing is only exact for a single transformer layer; across multiple layers the attended context for y^j bleeds through to y^i, requiring the PKL-U or PKL-T heuristics whose theoretical properties revert to those of prior approximations.
Recommended
#94 — Innovative (63)

SG-Serve: Efficient Model Serving for Subgraph-based Graph Representation Learning

dl.acm.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: SG-Serve makes a meaningful systems contribution through the 5-operator DAG abstraction that unifies extraction across 16 SGRL models and enables fine-grained work-stealing parallelism that prior systems could not exploit. The dual-process MPS dispatching is a clean solution to GPU head-of-line blocking. The work is thorough engineering with strong empirical validation, though the core ideas (work-stealing, workload-aware batching, producer-consumer decoupling) are well-established systems techniques applied to a new domain rather than fundamentally new approaches.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Subgraph-based graph representation learning (SGRL) models (e.g., SEAL, Shadow, GraIL) achieve substantially higher accuracy than classical GNNs (GCN, GAT, SAGE) on link prediction, relation prediction, and subgraph prediction tasks. However, deploying SGRL models for online inference is fundamentally harder than serving regular ML models because SGRL requests exhibit highly irregular workloads: the 99th-percentile subgraph is 27–60x larger than the median, and end-to-end latency is correspondingly heavy-tailed. Existing serving systems (DGL, PyG) use synchronized batch processing (SBP), single-threaded subgraph extraction, and FCFS batching by request count—three design choices that are oblivious to workload skew, causing severe tail latency (P99) and low throughput for the heavy requests that arise from high-degree nodes in power-law graphs. Methodology: SG-Serve is a C++ serving system (~6k lines, built atop PyTorch and deployed as a Triton backend) that attacks the irregular-workload problem in both pipeline stages. For subgraph extraction on CPU, the authors survey 16 SGRL models and decompose all extraction algorithms into a canonical five-step DAG of operators: Expand, Merge, Induce, Encode, Combine. Each operator exposes a node-parallel API (128-node segments as scheduling units), enabling fine-grained task decomposition. A work-stealing scheduler assigns each incoming request a primary thread but lets idle threads steal tasks from other requests' local queues, balancing intra-request parallelism against head-of-line (HoL) blocking. The two stages are decoupled via a producer-consumer queue, so requests that finish extraction immediately enter inference without waiting for batch stragglers. For GPU inference, workload-aware batching packs subgraphs by node/edge count (not request count), using a skip-FIFO scan that admits light requests past a heavy blocker. Dual-process dispatching via NVIDIA MPS splits the GPU into two equal regions: at most one process may concurrently run a heavy (oversized) batch, guaranteeing a lane for light requests. A profiling-based binary search tunes the node limit N_b online. Additional engineering includes shared GPU memory for model parameters across MPS processes, hot-node embedding caching on GPU, and overlapped CUDA communication/computation streams. Key Results: Experiments use 3 SGRL models (SEAL, Shadow, GraIL) on 4 large graphs (Orkut 120M edges, Livejournal 68M edges, Friendster 1.8B edges, Papers100M 1.7B edges) with Poisson-arrival synthetic traces on an NVIDIA T4 GPU. Against the re-implemented SBP baseline: (1) P99 latency at equal throughput is reduced by up to 13.1x; (2) throughput at a fixed P99 of 80ms improves by 8.75x–33.3x across model/graph combinations. Ablation shows each component contributes meaningfully: the producer-consumer architecture alone reduces P99 by up to 4.76x; adding parallel extraction (PC+E) reduces extraction P99 by up to 5.17x at low load; adding workload-aware inference (full SG-Serve) reduces inference P99 by up to 6.03x. Hot-embedding caching reduces data communication latency by up to 5.48x and end-to-end latency by up to 2.29x. Two MPS processes is shown to be the sweet spot; four gives marginal (<10%) gain and eight regresses by up to 1.31x due to kernel fragmentation. Results generalize to an A100 GPU (P99 improvement up to 10.7x over SBP). Strengths: - Thorough problem characterization: the empirical motivation (CDFs of subgraph size and running time, batch straggler timelines) precisely identifies the three failure modes of existing systems and directly maps them to the three system contributions. - The 5-operator DAG abstraction is a genuine contribution—it is both general (covers 16 surveyed models) and expressive enough to expose the fine-grained parallelism needed for work-stealing, which prior systems could not exploit because they treated extraction as a monolithic UDF. - The dual-process MPS dispatching cleanly solves GPU HoL blocking without requiring complex multi-priority queuing; the empirical justification for choosing exactly 2 processes (rather than more) is well-reasoned and validated. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation uses only synthetic Poisson-arrival workloads because no public SGRL serving traces exist; real production traffic may have bursty or correlated arrival patterns (e.g., hot nodes appearing in clusters) that could expose different bottlenecks. - The system is evaluated on a single GPU per machine; multi-GPU serving is deferred to Triton's instance-group mechanism with no analysis of how the extraction engine or batching policy would scale across GPUs or across machines. - The profiling-based batch size tuning requires running warmup traffic at deployment time and assumes the workload distribution is stationary; dynamic shifts in graph structure or request patterns (e.g., graph updates, new high-degree nodes) would invalidate the tuned N_b without an online adaptation mechanism.
Citations
#95 — Innovative (63)

Evolving Enactions of Expertise: Software Engineers' Evaluation and Demonstration of Coding Expertise with AI Coding Assistants

dl.acm.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: This paper introduces the conceptually precise lens of 'enaction of expertise' to analyze how AI coding assistants reshape both the demonstration and evaluation of coding competence — a timely and well-framed contribution. The dual-perspective design capturing evaluator reasoning and candidate strategy within the same sessions is methodologically creative, and the findings around extended and diminished enactions (e.g., tool selection and prompting practices as new expertise signals) offer actionable insights for interview design. However, the small homogeneous sample and simulated interview setting limit generalizability beyond the specific context studied.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: AI coding assistants are rapidly reshaping software development, yet it remains unclear how this transformation affects how coding expertise is demonstrated by candidates and assessed by evaluators. Prior work treats expertise as an informational property residing in individuals or AI systems, but this paper argues expertise is socially and interactionally constituted — enacted through demonstration and recognized through evaluation. The gap being addressed is concrete: do traditional signals of coding competence (writing code, debugging, iterating) still function as valid evidence of expertise when AI can perform or obscure those same steps? This question is practically urgent as companies like Meta and Google are already grappling with whether and how to allow AI in technical interviews. Methodology: The study employs User Enactment (UE), a method for studying behaviors in plausible near-future scenarios participants have limited direct experience with. Sixteen professional software engineers (ranging from 1–20 years of experience, predominantly Python/Data Science) were recruited and paired as evaluator–candidate dyads across 12 sessions. Evaluators designed their own live coding tasks and evaluation criteria — deliberately avoiding LeetCode-style problems — first without knowing AI would be allowed, then revised after being informed. Candidates had 30 minutes to solve the task with access to any AI tools (ChatGPT, Copilot, Gemini, Perplexity, DeepSeek, etc.) or web search. Screen recordings, think-alouds, in-session dialogue, and post-session reflection interviews were compiled and analyzed using thematic analysis and affinity diagramming. The paper introduces the concept of "enaction of expertise" to describe how expertise is put into practice through demonstration and evaluation, distinguishing extended enactions (new signals AI enables) from diminished enactions (traditional signals AI obscures). Key Results: Evaluators universally maintained the same criteria — task understanding, developing an approach, handling errors, explaining code, incremental development, and code quality — but required supplementary evidence to validate them when AI was involved. Output code alone was no longer sufficient: AI-generated comments counted as clean code but not as evidence of the candidate's expertise. Follow-up questioning became a critical compensatory mechanism, with evaluators probing candidates line-by-line to verify actual understanding. Three new extended enactions emerged: (1) choice of AI tool (selecting Perplexity for authoritative sources vs. ChatGPT for bulk implementation signals awareness of task requirements); (2) prompting practices (specificity and framing of prompts were read as proxies for task comprehension — broad prompts indicated weak understanding); (3) discernment over AI-generated outputs (whether candidates could identify the better among multiple AI suggestions, and whether they could integrate outputs selectively rather than copying wholesale). Simultaneously, AI diminished visibility of three traditional enactions: (a) iterative code development was bypassed when AI produced large code blocks; (b) error handling was obscured when candidates delegated diagnosis to AI; (c) task understanding and approach development were hidden when candidates dumped entire problem statements into AI at the session's start. The salience of these evolving enactions divided by evaluator philosophy: those who prioritized code planning over implementation cared little about implementation-phase enactions, viewing AI as handling the "solved problems" of boilerplate, while those who valued implementation scrutinized new enactions tightly. Finally, AI availability raised productivity expectations (evaluators considered making tasks harder; candidates felt pressure to demonstrate speed) but without consensus on how productivity relates to expertise, creating systematic misalignments — candidates who avoided AI to show comprehension were perceived as AI-illiterate; those who used AI indiscriminately were judged as lacking underlying knowledge. Strengths: - Introduces "enaction of expertise" as a conceptually precise, empirically grounded lens for studying how AI changes knowledge work assessment — more actionable than framing it as "AI replacing human expertise" - Dual-perspective design capturing both evaluator reasoning and candidate strategy within the same sessions enables direct comparison of misalignments rather than relying on self-report alone - Allowing evaluators to design their own tasks and candidates to choose their own AI tools reflects ecological validity and surfaces tool-selection itself as a dimension of expertise Weaknesses / Limitations: - Small, homogeneous sample (16 participants, predominantly Python/ML-focused, all remote online sessions) limits generalizability to other engineering specializations, languages, and in-person evaluation contexts - The simulated live-coding interview setting strips away the longitudinal, collaborative dimensions of real software engineering work where expertise manifests over sustained contributions and team coordination — the findings may not translate directly to everyday practice - High variability in task design across sessions (evaluator-created, ranging from LRU cache to ML pipelines) makes it difficult to isolate how task type interacts with AI use to shape enactions, a confound the authors acknowledge but do not control
Citations
#96 — Innovative (63)

Understanding Evolving Graph Structures for Large Discrete-Time Dynamic Graph Representation

vldb.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Scores 63 for its clean unification of three conceptually distinct dimensions (structural cohesiveness, path decay, temporal recency) into a single principled temporal influence score with formal proofs, and for being the only method that scales to graphs with tens of millions of edges. The late-snapshot gradient aggregation mechanism elegantly solves both information leakage and efficiency problems simultaneously. The core individual ideas — temporal decay, importance-based sampling, fixed-size neighbor stores — are individually well-established, making this a strong synthesis rather than a paradigm shift.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Discrete-Time Dynamic Graphs (DTDGs) model systems that evolve in discrete time steps (snapshots), with applications in retail analytics, financial markets, and social networks. Existing approaches rely on adjacency matrices for neighbor management, which suffer from three critical problems: (1) O(T|V|²) memory consumption that prevents scaling to large DTDGs; (2) adjacency matrices encode only binary signals and degree information, losing structural importance of nodes and ignoring historical neighbors; (3) snapshot-level training pipelines prevent efficient batch processing. Methods that attempt to incorporate historical structure via preprocessing (e.g., FALCON) incur prohibitive costs — up to 2.2 hours on a single dataset. The core gap is that no existing method simultaneously handles scalable neighbor management, structural evolution across snapshots, and efficient training. Methodology: UnderGS introduces a temporal-cohesive neighbor store as a drop-in replacement for adjacency matrices. The central idea is a temporal influence score that ranks neighbors along three axes: (1) node importance — a soft cohesiveness measure based on the ratio of interaction counts across a neighbor's ego-graph, rewarding structurally dense neighborhoods over sparse ones; (2) path decay — an exponential factor α₁^(l-1) that discounts multi-hop neighbors; and (3) snapshot decay — a factor α₂^(t-t') that diminishes influence of older interactions. Each node maintains a fixed-size GPU key-value store of its top-K temporal neighbors (current and historical), reducing memory from O(T|V|²) to O(|V|K). Three properties are proven: temporal awareness (snapshot decay property), cohesiveness (stored neighbors have higher average importance than the full neighbor set), and structural awareness (path decay property). A time-aware filter stochastically masks historical neighbors with probability ρ to prevent their over-dominance in sparse snapshots. The neighbor store interfaces with six neural networks — GCN, GIN, GraphSAGE, GAT, MLP Mixer, and Transformer — by replacing degree-based aggregation weights with temporal influence scores. Training uses mini-batch processing with a late-snapshot gradient aggregation mechanism: gradients are accumulated across all batches of a snapshot before any parameter update, preventing information leakage from intra-snapshot batch ordering. Key Results: Experiments span eight DTDGs ranging from small (UCI-Message, 1.9K nodes) to large-scale (Stack-Overflow, 2.6M nodes, 63M edges; Wiki-Talk, 1.1M nodes). All nine baselines (EvolveGCN-H/O, Roland, WinGNN, DGNN-LSTM/GRU/Transformer, SimpleDyG, FALCON) run out of memory (OOM) on the two largest graphs, while UnderGS completes both. On link prediction (AUC), UnderGS achieves up to 13.56% improvement over the best per-dataset competitor and averages 31.36% gain across datasets. On the harder link ranking task (MRR), gains are substantially larger — an average of 59.20% improvement — with standout results such as MRR of 95.01 on Bitcoin-Alpha versus the best baseline of 63.03 (FALCON). Training speed: up to 9× faster overall and 6× faster per epoch than the best baseline; compared to TGL (a scalable CTDG framework adapted for DTDGs), UnderGS achieves 94× speed-up and 100× faster per-epoch neighbor sampling. Ablations confirm that snapshot decay is the most critical component (removing it degrades MRR by up to 82.3%), followed by node importance (26.18% MRR gain over degree-based alternatives). The lightweight training pipeline alone contributes up to 10× training speed-up. Strengths: - The temporal influence score elegantly unifies three conceptually distinct dimensions (structural cohesiveness, path distance, temporal recency) into a single principled score with formal proofs of all three key properties, providing theoretical grounding uncommon in empirical graph learning papers. - The O(|V|K) neighbor store, GPU-resident with O(1) access and incremental update, directly addresses the scalability bottleneck of adjacency-matrix-based methods — UnderGS is the only evaluated method to handle graphs with tens of millions of edges on a single GPU. - The late-snapshot gradient aggregation mechanism cleanly solves a subtle but important problem (intra-snapshot information leakage) while simultaneously enabling batch processing for efficiency, killing two birds with one stone. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The temporal influence score introduces multiple hyperparameters (α₁, α₂, K, ρ, L) that require dataset-specific tuning; the paper uses fixed values based on dataset density heuristics, and sensitivity analysis is limited — it is unclear how robust performance is across a broader hyperparameter range or new datasets. - The evaluation focuses on undirected, unweighted DTDGs only, and the time-aware filter uses uniform random masking rather than a learned or data-driven gating mechanism; this may be suboptimal for graphs with complex temporal patterns where the relevance of history varies strongly per node or per snapshot.
Search Results
#97 — Innovative (63)

Beyond CO2: Integrating Water, Social Indicators, and Uncertainty into Sustainability Knowledge Graphs

mdpi.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: This paper scores 63 for addressing three genuine gaps simultaneously—indicator breadth beyond CO2, completeness modeling to eliminate zero-impact bias, and native uncertainty propagation within knowledge graph node properties. Embedding Monte Carlo-derived distributions as queryable semantic properties in Neo4j is an architectural advancement over post-hoc uncertainty wrappers. The completeness flag taxonomy with AHP weight redistribution is a practical, standards-aligned contribution. However, validation on a single SLS/PA12 scenario without comparative scenario ranking leaves the framework's most advertised capability undemonstrated, and the assumption of indicator independence in correlated domains limits confidence in the uncertainty estimates.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Knowledge Graph (KG)-based sustainability assessments for manufacturing are heavily CO2-centric, neglecting water scarcity, occupational health, and social dimensions that the UN SDGs and UNEP/SETAC frameworks explicitly prioritize. Existing KG models also treat missing indicator data as zero impact—a "zero-impact bias"—and almost never propagate uncertainty, producing point estimates that can mislead decision-makers. This paper addresses all three gaps simultaneously: indicator breadth, data completeness modeling, and probabilistic uncertainty quantification. Methodology: The authors extend a prior KG-based LCA framework (Greif et al., 2025) with three additions: (i) new indicator classes for water footprint (ISO 14046-aligned Water Stress Index and Adjusted Water Score) and social sustainability (UNEP/SETAC S-LCA occupational health and safety scores via AHP weighting); (ii) explicit completeness flags encoded in the graph (four states: available, partial, missing, out-of-scope) with AHP-based weight redistribution when data are absent; and (iii) pedigree-matrix-based uncertainty modeling (ISO 14044) propagated through Monte Carlo simulation (n=10,000) using log-normal distributions, with results reintegrated as node properties queryable via Cypher. The KG is implemented in Neo4j 5.26.0 with Python for preprocessing and simulation. Validation is via a single-scenario case study: Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) of Polyamide 12 (PA12), cradle-to-gate, with functional unit of one manufactured part. Key Results: The SLS case study (0.1 kg PA12 part) estimates total water use at 23.5 L/part (22 L material production, 1.5 L post-processing). With a 30% industrial reuse rate and a German Water Stress Index of 40%, the Adjusted Water Score (AWS) has mean 23.50 L ± 24.20 (95% CI: 4.10–72.30)—a coefficient of variation exceeding 100%, demonstrating that deterministic water scores are unreliable. The Social Impact Score totals 50.6/100 (significant risk), dominated by occupational machinery hazards (25.0) and fine particle exposure (20.0), with PPE availability providing a −6.4-point mitigation. The uncertainty analysis reveals that the AWS 95% confidence interval spans nearly 18× from p05 to p95, while the social score is comparatively tighter (42.1–59.4), confirming that water indicators are the primary source of epistemic risk. The KG (150 nodes, 400 relationships) executes completeness and provenance queries in <50 ms; the full Monte Carlo cycle completes in <12 s on a standard workstation. Strengths: - The explicit distinction between "missing data" and "zero impact" via completeness flags directly addresses a real methodological flaw in deterministic sustainability KGs, and the AHP-based weight redistribution is a practical, standards-aligned remedy. - Uncertainty propagation is embedded natively in the KG as semantic node properties, enabling uncertainty-aware Cypher queries rather than post-hoc analysis—this is a genuine architectural advancement over bolted-on Monte Carlo wrappers. - The social indicator integration preserves context (ordinal scales, provenance, weighting rationale) in structured, queryable triples rather than external annotations, which is practically useful for auditability. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The case study evaluates a single manufacturing scenario (one SLS/PA12 configuration) rather than comparing alternative process routes, so no head-to-head scenario ranking under uncertainty is demonstrated—the paper's most advertised capability remains unvalidated empirically. - The AWS 95% CI spans 4.10–72.30 L (a range of 68 L around a 23.5 L mean), which suggests the water indicator data quality is too poor to meaningfully inform decisions; the framework surfaces this uncertainty but offers no path to resolving it, making it more diagnostic than actionable in its current form. - Independence of indicators is assumed in the Monte Carlo simulation to "maintain a conservative estimate," but correlated uncertainties (e.g., regional water stress and social conditions co-vary geographically) could systematically underestimate total variance, and no sensitivity analysis on this assumption is provided.
Search Results
#98 — Innovative (63)

GHOSTSERVE: ALIGHTWEIGHT CHECKPOINTING SYSTEM IN THE SHADOW FOR FAULT-TOLERANT LLM SERVING

kennethzhao24.github.io · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: GhostServe makes a well-motivated adaptation of erasure coding to KV cache checkpointing, with the FP16-to-uint16 reinterpretation and fused CUDA kernel stack representing careful engineering. The hybrid recovery strategy that overlaps recomputation with parity reconstruction is a practical insight. However, the core technique (erasure coding for fault tolerance) is well-established in storage systems, and the restriction to intra-node TP≥4 and soft faults limits the novelty and applicability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: As LLM inference shifts toward million-token, agent-based workloads, hardware and software failures in distributed GPU clusters pose an increasingly severe threat. When a GPU fault occurs mid-inference, the entire key-value (KV) cache — a transient, growing state that can reach hundreds of gigabytes — is lost, forcing full recomputation that can take tens of minutes (e.g., 20 minutes for a 405B model on 8×H100 GPUs with 1M context). Existing fault-tolerance mechanisms are ill-suited: naive recomputation (used by default in SGLang, vLLM) is expensive for long contexts; state replication (DéjàVu) doubles host memory pressure and imposes high I/O latency incompatible with intra-node tensor parallelism; SSD checkpointing incurs prohibitive I/O overhead. A Microsoft study of production incidents found ~60% of failures occur at the inference engine level, confirming the practical urgency. Methodology: GhostServe introduces erasure-coding-based KV cache checkpointing integrated into chunked-prefill LLM serving. The core idea: instead of replicating the full KV cache, encode N data shards (one per GPU in tensor parallelism) into K parity shards (K ≪ N) and store only parity in host DRAM. Three erasure codes are supported: XOR (single-failure), RDP (double-failure), and Reed-Solomon (general K-failure). A key challenge is format incompatibility — erasure codes operate over bit fields, not FP16. The solution reinterprets FP16 values as uint16 via IEEE-754 bit patterns, enabling lossless encoding. Custom CUDA kernels fuse the FP16→uint16 conversion, parity computation, and unpacking into a single GPU pass using grid-stride loops and warp-synchronous execution. CUDA graphs reduce launch overhead; CUDA streams parallelize per-chunk reconstruction. The scheduler operates at chunk granularity: after each prefill chunk, one GPU is designated (in round-robin to balance load) to gather all KV shards via NVLink and compute parity, which is then asynchronously offloaded to CPU via PCIe. Recovery uses a hybrid strategy: for short contexts, pure recomputation avoids gather overhead; for long contexts, it overlaps GPU-side recomputation of early chunks with asynchronous parity transfer and EC reconstruction of later chunks. The system is implemented as a plug-in to SGLang (4K Python + 1.5K C++/CUDA lines). Key Results: On 8×H200 GPUs across LLaMA-3-8/70B, GPT-OSS-20/120B, and DeepSeek-R1-32B with input lengths 8K–64K: GhostServe achieves up to 2.7× lower checkpointing latency vs. CPU replication and 47× vs. SSD checkpointing. Recovery latency improves up to 2.1× over prior methods; for LLaMA-3-70B at 64K tokens, GhostServe recovers in under 5 seconds vs. ~2 minutes for SSD. I/O overhead is reduced 13× vs. CPU and 132× vs. SSD. Decode latency overhead is under 10%. In online serving at 15% failure rate, GhostServe achieves >90% EITR (effective inference time ratio) compared to significantly lower values for baselines; P50/P99 latency improves 1.2×/1.1× over recomputation on LLaMA-70B. At million-token scale, overhead over the baseline is under 6%, vs. 2.6 minutes for DéjàVu reduced to 9 seconds. The hybrid recomputation-reconstruction recovery reduces recovery latency by up to 42.9% over reconstruction alone. Notably, for TP=2 the benefit of erasure coding vanishes due to encoding overhead without proportional I/O savings. Strengths: - The erasure coding approach is technically well-motivated and elegantly addresses the core tension between checkpointing latency and memory overhead; the 8:2 ratio achieving 75% memory reduction with 73% latency reduction is a compelling operating point. - The custom CUDA kernel stack (FP16↔uint16 reinterpretation, kernel fusion, CUDA graphs, multi-stream reconstruction) is carefully engineered and benchmarked, showing substantial gains over naive PyTorch baselines. - The hybrid recovery strategy (recompute early chunks while asynchronously fetching parity for later ones) is a practical insight that decouples recovery latency from PCIe bandwidth, yielding up to 42.9% recovery speedup. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The system is limited to intra-node tensor parallelism (TP≥4 to see meaningful benefit); cross-node fault tolerance via inter-node parity placement is acknowledged as future work, limiting applicability to the increasingly common multi-node serving deployments. - GhostServe targets soft GPU memory faults (errors recoverable via GPU restart), not hard hardware failures requiring topology reconfiguration; failed GPUs must rejoin before inference resumes, so the system does not handle permanent device loss or dynamic TP group resizing, which are common in large-scale production outages. - Performance on decode-heavy reasoning workloads (e.g., long chain-of-thought outputs) is unexplored, and the unpredictability of output lengths complicates the parity update protocol during the decode phase — a gap given that reasoning models like DeepSeek-R1 are increasingly dominant workloads.
Citations
#99 — Innovative (62)

Cross-Tokenizer LLM Distillation through a Byte-Level Interface

Avyav Kumar Singh, Yen-Chen Wu, Alexandru Cioba, Alberto Bernacchia, Davide Buffelli · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 62 for the elegant insight of using raw bytes as a universal tokenizer-agnostic distillation interface, which is conceptually clean and avoids the ad-hoc vocabulary alignment heuristics of prior cross-tokenizer distillation methods. However, the empirical results are mixed — BLD does not consistently outperform baselines across benchmarks, and the authors candidly acknowledge that cross-tokenizer distillation remains an open problem. The offline byte-probability precomputation cost (2 days on 4× RTX 3090) and the structural limitation of the fixed-width 10-byte decoder head temper the practical contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard knowledge distillation between LLMs requires teacher and student to share an identical tokenizer and vocabulary, since distillation operates by minimizing KL divergence between output distributions over a fixed token vocabulary. When tokenizers differ—as is common across the LLM ecosystem (LLaMA uses its own BPE, Qwen2 uses a different one, Gemma2 uses yet another)—the output spaces are incommensurable and direct logit-level distillation is undefined. Existing cross-tokenizer distillation (CTD) methods work around this with heuristic vocabulary alignment, optimal transport (ULD), dual-space mappings, or approximate likelihood matching, all of which add significant complexity and rely on approximations with no clean theoretical grounding. This paper asks whether a simpler, more principled approach exists. Methodology: The paper proposes Byte-Level Distillation (BLD), which uses raw bytes as a universal shared interface since every tokenizer encodes and decodes to/from bytes. The method has two steps. First, a lightweight byte-level decoder head O_b—implemented as 10 parallel linear projections from the model's hidden dimension to a 261-token byte vocabulary—is attached to the pretrained student in parallel with its existing token-level head. Second, during distillation, the teacher's token-level output distribution is converted to per-byte conditional probabilities using the beam-search approximation of Vieira et al.
