#109 — Moderately Innovative (58)

DR$^{3}$-Eval: Towards Realistic and Reproducible Deep Research Evaluation (21 ▲ )

Qianqian Xie, Qingheng Xiong, He Zhu, Tiantian Xia, Xueming Han, Fanyu Meng, Jiakai Wang, Zhiqi Bai, Chengkang Jiang, Zhaohui Wang, Yubin Guo, Yuqing Wen, Jiayang Mao, Zijie Zhang, Shihao Li, Yanghai Wang, Yuxiang Ren, Junlan Feng, Jiaheng Liu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 58 for solid benchmark engineering — the reverse-construction methodology ensuring verifiable ground truth and the three-tier corpus design with scalable context lengths are well-motivated design choices. The empirically observed dissociation between instruction following and factual accuracy is a useful diagnostic insight. However, the overall approach is a careful synthesis of existing benchmark design principles rather than a fundamentally new evaluation paradigm, and 100 tasks is thin for the claimed scope.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deep Research Agents (DRAs) must plan, retrieve multimodal information, reason over noisy sources, and synthesize structured long-form reports — yet no existing benchmark adequately evaluates these capabilities. Prior work falls into two camps: live-web benchmarks (e.g., DeepResearch Bench) offer ecological validity but suffer from temporal volatility and evaluation ambiguity; sandbox-based benchmarks (e.g., DeepResearchGym) improve reproducibility but use clean, text-only corpora that fail to reflect real research complexity. None combine authentic multimodal user-provided materials, a controlled noise-intensive corpus, and a verifiable solution path. This gap makes it impossible to reliably compare DRAs or diagnose their failure modes. Methodology: DR3-Eval is constructed via a five-stage pipeline. First, paid volunteers provide authentic multimodal document sets (PDFs, images, videos, audio, spreadsheets) covering 100 tasks across Technology, Economy, and Humanities in English and Chinese. Second, a divergent-convergent keyword generation process (using Gemini-2.5-Pro) separates signal keywords (guiding the correct retrieval path) from noise keywords (thematically plausible distractions). Third, each task gets its own static sandbox corpus built from web crawls of these keywords, categorized into Supportive, Distractor, and Noise pages — scaled to five context lengths (32k–512k tokens). Fourth, queries are reverse-constructed from the verified evidential documents rather than posed open-endedly, ensuring every task has a unique, grounded solution. Fifth, a four-dimensional quality filter (implicit guidance, synthesis necessity, insight novelty, interpretive unambiguity) yields 100 final tasks from 280 candidates (35.7% pass rate). Evaluation uses five metrics: Information Recall from user files (IRUF) and sandbox corpus (IRSC), Citation Coverage (CC), Factual Accuracy (FA), Instruction Following (IF), and Depth Quality (DQ), with GPT-5.1 as the primary judge. The authors also develop DR3-Agent — a hierarchical multi-agent system with a main reasoning agent running Plan-Act-Observe loops and sub-agents for agentic RAG (ReAct-based iterative dense retrieval via text-embedding-3-small) and long-file parsing. Key Results: Claude Sonnet 4 achieves the best overall scores (70.7/67.5/65.6 average at 64k/128k/512k), followed closely by GLM-4.7 (69.8/66.9/64.1). All models degrade as sandbox size grows from 64k to 512k tokens, with retrieval-dependent metrics (IRSC, CC) falling most sharply. Factual accuracy remains relatively stable across context lengths, suggesting it reflects inherent reasoning ability independent of retrieval quality. A striking dissociation appears: high instruction-following scores do not predict high factual accuracy — Qwen3-235B-A22B achieves strong IF (78–80%) but poor FA (50–54%), revealing that models can produce reports that look complete while hallucinating content. Error attribution across 100 reports per model shows hallucination as the dominant failure mode (48–77% of errors), with retrieval errors second and reasoning errors third. The sandbox corpus correlates well with live-web retrieval (average delta ≤0.7 points for Gemini-2.5-Pro), validating its use as a reproducible substitute. LLM-as-judge alignment with human experts reaches Pearson r=0.78 and pairwise agreement 0.89, close to inter-human agreement (r=0.83, agreement 0.91). Agentic RAG with 5–7 iterations outperforms single-shot top-k retrieval, though gains plateau and slightly reverse at higher iterations. Strengths: - The reverse-construction methodology is principled: by deriving queries from pre-verified evidential documents, every task has a well-defined ground truth, eliminating the evaluation ambiguity that plagues open-ended web benchmarks. - The three-tier corpus design (Supportive / Distractor / Noise) with scalable context lengths (32k–512k) enables controlled study of retrieval robustness in a way no prior benchmark supports. - The multi-dimensional metric suite captures genuinely independent failure axes — the empirically observed IF/FA dissociation is a concrete, actionable insight rather than a generic benchmark finding. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark contains only 100 tasks — a thin slice even after the reported quality filtering — which limits statistical power for fine-grained domain or modality-level analysis. - The sandbox corpus is static and crawled at a fixed time, so temporal drift (outdated facts, shifted web coverage) will gradually erode the 'realistic' claim without active maintenance. - Evaluation relies heavily on GPT-5.1 as judge, a closed, expensive model; while alternative-judge robustness is reported (Spearman ρ=0.924), the known 'model bias' phenomenon (judges favoring same-family models) is acknowledged but not fully mitigated.
Recommended 2604.14683 PDF
#110 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Measurement of Generative AI Workload Power Profiles for Whole-Facility Data Center Infrastructure Planning

Roberto Vercellino, Jared Willard, Gustavo Campos, Weslley da Silva Pereira, Olivia Hull, Matthew Selensky, Juliane Mueller · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The paper makes a useful engineering contribution by connecting high-resolution GPU power measurements to facility-level demand simulation, filling a gap in publicly available AI power characterization data. The open dataset and end-to-end DIPLOEE framework are practical, but the methodology is straightforward measurement and simulation rather than a conceptually novel approach, and the narrow hardware scope (single H100 system) limits broader applicability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: The rapid expansion of generative AI is driving unprecedented growth in data center electricity consumption — from 76 TWh in the U.S. in 2018 to 176 TWh in 2023, with projections of doubling or tripling by 2028. This creates challenges at multiple timescales: long-term grid infrastructure planning, day-to-day load forecasting, and real-time grid stability (e.g., a 1.5 GW load drop in 82 seconds at a Virginia data center in 2024). Existing power consumption data is largely proprietary, reported at coarse resolution, or lacks the temporal fidelity needed for geographically-resolved generation, transmission, and distribution planning. Prior workload trace datasets omit power consumption, while prior power characterization work lacks reproducibility, breadth of configurations, and a clear path to facility-level demand estimation. Methodology: The paper presents a two-part framework. First, high-resolution (0.1-second) GPU and CPU power measurements are collected on NLR's Kestrel HPC system (156 nodes, each with 4 NVIDIA H100 SXM GPUs and 2 AMD EPYC 9554 CPUs) using the open-source WattAMeter tool built on NVML and Intel RAPL. Three representative GenAI workloads are characterized: (1) Llama-2 70B fine-tuning via MLPerf Training v4.0 with LoRA + DeepSpeed ZeRO-3, scaled from 2–16 nodes; (2) Stable Diffusion v2 image-generation training via MLPerf, scaled from 1–16 nodes; and (3) Llama-3 70B LLM inference via vLLM in both offline (batch) and online (serve) modes, varying prompt count, output token length, and request rate. Second, the measured node-level profiles feed into DIPLOEE (Data Center Infrastructure Planning, Load Optimization for Energy Efficiency), a discrete-event simulation model implemented in SimPy. For training/fine-tuning, DIPLOEE uses a bisection algorithm to match a target average utilization by generating a job list distributed across time by empirical distributions (adapted from Shanghai AI Lab's ACME Trace and NLR's own HPC logs), then simulates FIFO scheduling and tracks facility-wide power. For inference, a request-rate-driven model allocates model instances based on latency constraints, using Microsoft Azure LLM trace distributions to shape temporal demand. Year-long simulations at 1-minute resolution are run for a 10 MW colocation data center and a 1 MW inference data center at 20/40/60/80% average utilization. Key Results: For Llama-2 70B fine-tuning, average power scaled linearly with node count (slope ~2.36 kW/node), with standard deviation also linear (~0.80 kW/node). Total energy increased with node count under the weak-scaling experimental configuration due to larger global batch sizes requiring more epochs. Stable Diffusion training showed larger power swings (12–48 kW at 16 nodes) and, unlike Llama-2, total energy decreased with more nodes because faster wall-clock times offset the additional hardware. For offline Llama-3 70B inference, power saturated at ~2.8 kW per node around 325 input prompts; online inference similarly saturated at ~2.9 kW at modest request rates (~25 prompts/second). At the facility level, the 10 MW colocation data center reached a maximum of only ~7.30 MW (73% of rated capacity) even at 100% node occupancy, because workloads never consistently drove GPUs to TDP. Peak-to-average ratio (PAR) ranged from 2.56 at 20% utilization to 1.24 at 80%. The 1 MW inference center showed similar headroom (max ~80% of rated power) and PAR values 9% lower than colocation on average, with clear diurnal and weekly demand patterns driven by user behavior. At 80% utilization, ~15% of inference requests could not be served within latency constraints. Strengths: - Open, reproducible dataset: power profiles are publicly released at 0.1-second resolution using standardized MLPerf and vLLM benchmarks, filling a major gap in publicly available AI power characterization data. - End-to-end framework: the paper is one of the few that explicitly connects node-level power measurements to whole-facility demand profiles usable for grid planning, rather than stopping at server-level characterization. - Workload diversity: three architecturally distinct workload types (LLM fine-tuning, diffusion model training, LLM inference in both modes) with scaling experiments covering 1–16 nodes provide a richer characterization than prior single-workload studies. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Narrow hardware and workload scope: all measurements are on a single H100-based HPC system with a specific interconnect (HPE Slingshot-11); results may not generalize to A100, AMD MI300X, or cloud-provider architectures with different power management and network topologies. - Facility simulation omits auxiliary loads: cooling, power distribution losses, and other non-IT loads are excluded from DIPLOEE, which can constitute 10–30% of total facility power in older or less efficient data centers, potentially underestimating real grid impact. - Utilization distributions are borrowed, not measured: job submission patterns and user behavior distributions are adapted from the Shanghai AI Lab ACME Trace and Microsoft Azure datasets rather than directly observed at the simulated facility type, introducing mismatch risk for hyperscale or enterprise environments.
Search Results 2604.07345 PDF
#111 — Moderately Innovative (58)

GameWorld: Towards Standardized and Verifiable Evaluation of Multimodal Game Agents (90 ▲ )

Mingyu Ouyang, Siyuan Hu, Kevin Qinghong Lin, Hwee Tou Ng, Mike Zheng Shou · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026
Why this rank: GameWorld makes sound engineering contributions—particularly the paused-inference sandbox and deterministic JavaScript gameAPI evaluation—that address real methodological pain points in game agent benchmarking. However, the core contribution is a benchmark rather than a new method, and the design space of standardized game evaluation platforms is well-explored; the novelty lies more in careful execution than in conceptual departure.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Evaluating Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) agents in interactive environments is critical for progress toward embodied generalist AI, yet existing game benchmarks suffer from three systematic problems: heterogeneous action interfaces that prevent fair cross-model comparison, inference latency coupling (a slower model faces a harder game state), and noisy heuristic evaluation via OCR or VLM-as-judge pipelines. These gaps make results hard to reproduce, diagnose, or trust. The paper addresses all three by introducing GameWorld, a browser-based benchmark designed around standardization, verifiability, and reproducibility. Methodology: GameWorld comprises 34 browser games spanning five genres (Runner, Arcade, Platformer, Puzzle, Simulation) with 170 tasks. The central design decisions are: (1) A browser-based sandbox that pauses game execution during model inference, decoupling decision quality from response speed; (2) Two standardized agent interfaces—Computer-Use Agents (CUAs) that emit raw keyboard/mouse events, and Generalist Multimodal Agents that operate via Semantic Action Parsing, a deterministic mapping from game-specific semantic actions (e.g., jump_left()) to low-level atomic events—both normalized into a shared executor-level control space; (3) Outcome-based state-verifiable evaluation: each game exposes a structured JavaScript gameAPI bridge that serializes internal state (score, position, lives, coins, etc.) so evaluation is computed deterministically from 233 instrumented state fields across all games, producing two metrics—Success Rate (SR ∈ {0,1}) and normalized Progress (PG ∈ [0,1]); (4) A shared agent harness with structured prompts, rolling multimodal context memory, per-model native function calling, and optional reasoning. The benchmark is evaluated across 13 base models yielding 18 model–interface pairs including Claude-Sonnet-4.6, GPT-5.2, Gemini-3-Flash-Preview, Gemini-2.5-Computer-Use, and open-source models (Qwen3-VL series, UI-TARS-1.5-7B). Key Results: All models fall far short of human performance. The best Generalist agent (Gemini-3-Flash-Preview) achieves 41.9% PG / 21.2% SR overall, while a Novice human player scores 64.1% PG / 55.3% SR under the same 100-action budget. Expert humans reach 82.6% PG / 77.1% SR. Among CUAs, Seed-1.8 leads at 39.8% PG. Capability-aligned curriculum analysis reveals that agents perform best at Level-4 (Symbolic Reasoning: ~50–55% PG) and Level-2 (System-1 Reactive Control: ~45–55% PG), but collapse at Level-1 (Basic Control & Timing Grounding: ~30–40% PG) and Level-5 (Long-Horizon Coordination: ~10–20% PG), indicating that high-level strategic reasoning is relatively competent while basic timing and sustained coordination remain severe bottlenecks. Benchmark robustness is validated over 10 full reruns per model: standard deviation of overall PG is ≤1.1 percentage points. The real-time variant GameWorld-RT shows that even under unpaused conditions, success rates remain very low (<18%), confirming that latency alone is not the primary bottleneck. Context memory has asymmetric effects: it modestly helps Generalist agents (semantic history is interpretable) but hurts CUAs (accumulating low-level action traces degrades performance). Invalid action rates are near zero for most frontier models (<0.1%) but reach 8.3% for GLM-4.6V and 2.7% for Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B. Strengths: - The JavaScript gameAPI bridge enabling deterministic, noise-free evaluation from internal game state is a genuine technical contribution that eliminates the OCR/VLM-as-judge noise plaguing prior work. - The paused-inference sandbox cleanly separates decision quality from inference latency, enabling fair comparison across models of very different speeds; the complementary GameWorld-RT variant then isolates the real-time challenge. - The benchmark is unusually thorough in analyzing robustness (10 reruns), interface differences, context-memory sensitivity, action validity, and capability-aligned curriculum profiles, providing actionable diagnostic structure rather than just a leaderboard. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark requires manual engineering of both Semantic Action Parsing mappings and gameAPI instrumentation for every new game, making expansion costly and creating a barrier the authors acknowledge but defer to future work. - The human baseline study uses only two participants (one novice, one expert) under a restrictive 100-action budget, which may underestimate true human-level performance and limits the statistical strength of the human comparison. - All 34 games are browser-based casual/arcade titles; the benchmark does not include long-horizon open-world games, strategy games, or multi-agent competitive settings, which limits generalization claims about 'generalist game agents.'
Recommended 2604.07429 PDF
#112 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Adapting language models with continual learning for temporal drifts

link.springer.com · Recommended articles
Why this rank: CLEAR scores 58 as a solid but conceptually straightforward application of experience replay with behavioral cloning losses to the continual RL setting. The combination of off-policy replay with KL and value cloning terms is sensible engineering, and the task-boundary-agnostic property is a practical advantage over EWC and P&C. However, the core mechanism — mixing replay with on-policy data and adding regularization losses — is a natural combination of well-established techniques without a surprising twist.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Continual learning requires agents to sequentially acquire new skills without forgetting previously learned ones — the stability-plasticity dilemma. In deep reinforcement learning, catastrophic forgetting is severe: when a network trains on a new task, performance on prior tasks collapses immediately. Prior work addressing this focused almost exclusively on synaptic consolidation methods (EWC, Progress & Compress), which require knowledge of task boundaries and identities, limiting their applicability. Experience replay — the brain's hippocampal mechanism for consolidation — had been largely ignored as a solution in deep RL. The paper asks whether replay alone, applied correctly, can match or beat these more complex approaches. Methodology: The authors introduce CLEAR (Continual Learning with Experience And Replay), built on the IMPALA distributed actor-learner framework with V-Trace off-policy correction. CLEAR mixes on-policy learning from fresh environment interactions (for plasticity) with off-policy learning from a replay buffer of historical experiences (for stability). Crucially, for replay samples, two additional behavioral cloning losses are added: (1) KL divergence between the historical policy distribution and the current policy (KL[μ‖π_θ]), and (2) L2 loss between the historical and current value functions. These cloning terms prevent the current network from drifting away from behavior that was effective on past tasks. The default mixture is 50-50 new versus replay experience. When the buffer is full, reservoir sampling maintains a uniform random sample of all past experience. Critically, CLEAR requires no knowledge of task boundaries or identities. Key Results: On three cyclically repeated DMLab tasks, sequential training (no CLEAR) achieves cumulative scores of 17.99 / 5.01 / 10.87, while CLEAR (50-50) achieves 31.40 / 8.00 / 18.13 — matching separate per-task networks (29.24 / 8.79 / 19.91) and simultaneously-trained networks (32.35 / 8.81 / 20.56), effectively eliminating catastrophic forgetting. Ablating behavioral cloning reduces performance (28.66 / 7.79 / 16.63), confirming both components are necessary. On six Atari tasks matching the P&C experimental protocol, CLEAR outperforms P&C on 4 of 6 tasks (e.g., hero: 8106 vs. 3433; krull: 8845 vs. 7025) and significantly outperforms EWC across all tasks, despite being simpler and task-boundary-agnostic. A plasticity probe experiment shows that learning speed on a novel task is unaffected by the number of prior tasks in the buffer, provided on-policy learning is included (pure replay-only training degrades as the buffer fills). Buffer size experiments show that reducing capacity from 450M to 5M frames causes only slight degradation, making the method practical under memory constraints. Strengths: - Achieves near-simultaneous-training performance on continual RL benchmarks, virtually eliminating catastrophic forgetting with a conceptually simple mechanism. - Task-boundary agnostic: unlike EWC and P&C, CLEAR requires no task identity signals, making it applicable to real-world settings where task transitions are unknown or gradual. - Thorough ablations isolate the contributions of off-policy replay versus behavioral cloning, and analyze buffer size sensitivity, on/off-policy mixture ratios, and plasticity preservation — providing strong empirical grounding. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is restricted to relatively short task sequences (3–6 tasks) with discrete, clearly distinct tasks; it is unclear how CLEAR scales to very long sequences with hundreds of tasks or to tasks with continuously shifting distributions. - Memory cost is non-trivial: even the "small" buffer of 5M frames storing raw RL trajectories from DMLab/Atari is substantial, and the paper does not analyze computational overhead or wall-clock cost relative to baselines.

#113 — Moderately Innovative (58)

ParSCo: Performance-Driven Par titioning and S cheduling Co-optimization Framework for Processor-based Emulation

dl.acm.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: ParSCo scores 58 for its well-motivated co-optimization of partitioning and scheduling in processor-based emulation, with solid theoretical contributions (O(1) incremental TOB gain updates). The work is thorough and practically relevant but builds heavily on the established multilevel partitioning paradigm (FM refinement, coarsening) with TOB as an added objective rather than a fundamentally new approach.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Processor-based emulation (PBE) is a critical tool for functional verification of billion-gate VLSI designs, offering faster compilation and larger logic capacity than FPGA-based approaches. The PBE compilation flow involves two sequential stages—netlist partitioning (minimizing inter-processor cut) and scheduling (minimizing the number of instruction time steps by assigning topological execution orders). Treating these as independent stages leads to suboptimal outcomes: a min-cut partition may yield poor topological order balancing (TOB), inflating the time step even when cut count is minimized. The core insight motivating this work is that scheduling performance depends critically on how nodes with the same topological order are distributed across processors (TOB), and this dependency must be baked into the partitioning stage itself—not patched in afterward. Methodology: ParSCo formulates a joint objective (Equation 1) combining cut connectivity and a normalized TOB metric K(σ²_τ), the latter measuring variance in the count of nodes at each topological level across processor blocks. The framework follows the multilevel partitioning paradigm with four integrated novel components. First, TOB-aware coarsening with multiple constraints extends prior work by enforcing scheduling constraints (input/output/BRAM node co-location) and general constraints (fixed-node assignment, grouping) during node contraction, preventing violations from propagating. Second, global growing initial partitioning with fixed nodes replaces the prior hyperparameter-sensitive gain formulation with a decomposed dual-objective approach—cutsize and TOB gains are stored and selected independently via priority queues and TOB arrays, eliminating instability introduced by fixed nodes. Third, the TopoRefinement algorithms (boundary node refinement and local refinement) extend the Fiduccia-Mattheyses framework with a blended gain function combining TOB and cut. Theorem 1 enables O(1) incremental TOB gain updates when a node is moved, covering all three cases of affected neighbors (same block as source, same block as target, or other blocks). Fourth, partitioning-aware static scheduling adjusts each node's topological order within its mobility window [τ_s, τ_l] using a greedy mobility-prioritized search with forward and backward propagation; Theorem 2 and Corollary 1 reduce gain calculation for topological order changes from O(k) to O(1) by exploiting closed-form variance update expressions. The full framework supports architecture-level hierarchical partitioning (rack→cluster→board→processor) using parallel sub-DAH processing. Key Results: On the Titan23 public benchmark (22 designs, 92K–1.09M nodes), ParSCo achieves an average 22% reduction in time step, 70% reduction in TOB, and 54% reduction in runtime versus the list scheduling baseline. Compared to hMetis + scheduling and KaHyPar + scheduling (strongest two-stage baselines), ParSCo improves time step by 9% and 13%, and TOB by 15% and 35%, respectively. On open-source chip benchmarks (XiangShan RISC-V processors at 2.6M and 3.3M gates; NVDLA at 9.14M gates), ParSCo delivers 23% time step improvement and 74% TOB improvement over list scheduling, and reduces time step by 12% and runtime by 12.5× versus KaHyPar + scheduling, with better improvement on larger designs indicating good scalability. Ablation studies confirm that all four proposed techniques contribute positively, with the TOB-aware coarsening and partitioning-aware scheduling stages contributing most. ParSCo improves 15% over its conference predecessor TopoOrderPart in the core time step metric. Strengths: - Novel and well-motivated co-optimization formulation that treats TOB as a first-class objective across all partitioning stages, closing a gap that prior work only addressed heuristically or not at all. - O(1) complexity theorems for gain computation and update enable practical scalability to 10M-cell industrial benchmarks, which would otherwise be a computational bottleneck. - Comprehensive experimental evaluation covering 25 benchmarks across two benchmark suites with designs spanning 92K to 9.14M gates, with ablation studies that clearly attribute performance gains to individual components. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Fixed nodes in benchmarks are randomly sampled at 0.01% of all nodes rather than sourced from real IP pre-assignments; this may not reflect the structure or density of fixed nodes in production PBE flows, limiting generalizability of the fixed-node results. - The framework introduces two hyperparameters (β balancing cut vs. TOB in partitioning, ζ in refinement) that require tuning; the paper shows performance degrades at extreme values (β=4, ζ=4) without providing an automatic selection strategy. - The comparison set is limited to hMetis and KaHyPar as baseline partitioners; the closely related ParSGCN work (which also bridges partitioning and scheduling via GCN look-ahead) is explicitly excluded from comparison on the grounds of problem formulation differences, leaving the advantage over learning-based approaches unquantified.
Citations
#114 — Moderately Innovative (58)

Artificial Intelligence in the Life Sciences

publica.fraunhofer.de · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: The dual top-down/bottom-up architecture for multi-condition clinical reasoning is conceptually well-motivated, and the masking experiment cleanly ablates terminology from content-driven clustering. The olfaction-disorder pathway as a mechanistic bridge between COVID-19 and neurodegeneration is a specific, graph-grounded finding. However, catastrophically low recall on major guidelines (WHO COVID-19: 0.064) undermines the semantic gap analysis, and the work remains entirely prototype-level with no clinical or downstream validation.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Clinical practice guidelines are developed from a single-disease perspective, making them poorly suited for patients with comorbidities. This gap is particularly acute for post-COVID care, where growing evidence links SARS-CoV-2 infection to long-term neurological outcomes including elevated risk of Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, and Multiple Sclerosis. Mechanisms such as neuroinflammation, blood-brain barrier disruption, and olfactory pathway damage suggest plausible COVID-to-neurodegeneration trajectories, yet current guidelines do not address these cross-disease progressions. Clinicians lack any mechanism-informed tool that unifies clinical trial evidence, guideline recommendations, and biomedical knowledge for multi-condition reasoning. Methodology: The paper presents a two-component framework. First, a top-down semantic analysis extracts actionable recommendations from 108 clinical practice guidelines (COVID-19, Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, MS) sourced from WHO, NICE, and CDC using GPT-3.5-turbo via LangChain with a fixed few-shot prompt template. Extracted recommendations are embedded into 768-dimensional vectors using the all-mpnet-base-v2 SentenceTransformer model and projected into 2D via UMAP (cosine distance, n_neighbors=15, min_dist=0.1) to reveal semantic clustering and identify thematic gaps. A masked variant replaces disease names with a generic token to test whether clustering is driven by terminology or underlying content. Second, a bottom-up graph construction begins by mining 631 PubMed RCTs/meta-analyses using MeSH descriptor D000094024 to extract the top 20 post-COVID phenotypic anchors (e.g., dyspnea, cognitive fatigue) via SciSpacy+UMLS at confidence threshold 0.7. These anchors are used to query ClinicalTrials.gov (1,653 trials retrieved), and trial metadata is semantically matched to 237 guideline documents via MPNet cosine similarity (threshold ≥ 0.7). The resulting clinical-trial-guideline graph is then merged in Neo4j with a separately curated biomedical hypothesis graph (PrimeKG, DisGeNET, OpenTargets) via shared ontology IDs, forming a unified comorbidity knowledge graph supporting bidirectional reasoning. Key Results: The LLM-based recommendation extraction achieves overall precision of 0.79 and recall of 0.34 across seven manually evaluated guidelines. Performance is highly structure-dependent: the Canadian Parkinson's guideline achieves precision 0.992 / recall 0.884, while the WHO COVID-19 guideline (2023) achieves precision 0.750 / recall 0.064. UMAP clustering of unmasked recommendations yields a silhouette score of 0.344, confirming moderate disease-specific separation. After masking disease names, the silhouette score drops to 0.177, with neurodegenerative conditions (AD, PD, MS) converging in embedding space while COVID-19 recommendations remain isolated — revealing shared management concepts obscured by terminology and confirming that COVID-19 guidelines are thematically disconnected from NDD guidance. In the knowledge graph, olfaction disorders (MESH:D000857) emerge as the primary integration node linking COVID-19 to neurodegeneration: graph path analysis traces COVID-19 → inflammatory genes (IL6, IL1B, TNF) → microglial activation → olfaction disorders → SNCA/MAPT → Parkinson's/Alzheimer's. Stroke (MESH:D020521) surfaces as a secondary intermediate. Clinical trials (e.g., donepezil repurposing for post-COVID memory impairment, NCT01951118) and guideline chunks corroborate these mechanistic links. Strengths: - The dual top-down/bottom-up architecture is conceptually well-motivated and operationally coherent: semantic gap analysis and knowledge graph construction are genuinely complementary, with the masking experiment providing a clean ablation of terminology vs. content-driven clustering. - The olfaction-disorder pathway as a mechanistic bridge between COVID-19 and neurodegeneration is a specific, graph-grounded finding supported by convergent evidence from trials (olfactory training, Donepezil), guidelines (post-viral smell loss treatment), and molecular links (SNCA, MAPT, IL6). - The framework is designed as an LLM-ready substrate for RAG-based clinical decision support, with ontology-normalized nodes enabling downstream LLM grounding in structured biomedical evidence. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Recall is very low overall (0.34) and catastrophically low for some major guidelines (WHO COVID-19: 0.064), meaning the downstream semantic analysis and KG are built on a substantially incomplete extraction of guideline content; conclusions about "gaps" in guidance may reflect extraction failures as much as actual guideline siloing. - The biomedical hypothesis graph is borrowed from a parallel study and not independently validated; the merged KG inherits ontology fragmentation problems (e.g., COVID-19 represented as both MESH:D000086382 and DOID:0080600), leading to redundant nodes and potentially broken graph paths that could distort shortest-path mechanistic analysis. - The study is entirely prototype-level: no clinical validation, no user study, no downstream task evaluation (e.g., does the KG actually help clinicians make better decisions?), and no quantitative comparison of the KG's mechanistic hypotheses against held-out biological ground truth.
Search Results
#115 — Moderately Innovative (57)

Vocabulary Dropout for Curriculum Diversity in LLM Co-Evolution

Jacob Dineen, Aswin RRV, Zhikun Xu, Ben Zhou · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Vocabulary dropout is a clean, minimally invasive intervention for diversity collapse in co-evolutionary self-play, and the game-theoretic motivation connecting action-space constraints to vocabulary masking is well-articulated. However, the mechanism itself—random logit masking—is a straightforward regularization trick, and the narrow applicability (base models only, sufficient scale required, math domain only, same-scale pairings only) significantly limits both the practical impact and the generality of the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Co-evolutionary self-play for LLMs — where a proposer model generates training problems and a solver model attempts them — promises autonomous curriculum learning without human supervision. In practice, the proposer rapidly collapses to a narrow template distribution: it learns to satisfy the reward function (maximizing solver uncertainty) by converging on a small set of question templates rather than by generating genuinely diverse, challenging problems. This diversity collapse stalls solver improvement early in training. Prior mitigations target problem selection or reward design, but none directly constrain the proposer's output space. The paper argues this collapse stems from a fundamental asymmetry with game-based self-play: in games, external rules structurally constrain the action space and certify difficulty independently; in language, no such constraint exists. Methodology: The paper introduces vocabulary dropout (VD), a hard, non-stationary random mask applied to the proposer's output logits during co-evolutionary training. At each batch, a Bernoulli mask with retention probability α ∈ {0.75, 0.85} is independently drawn per token, setting masked token logits to −∞ before softmax. A small protected set of format-critical tokens is always retained. Because the mask changes every batch, the proposer cannot converge to any fixed high-reward token sequence. The mask is applied during both GRPO policy training (at the rollout sampling stage, with gradients flowing through the full vocabulary) and during curriculum generation. The method is implemented via vLLM's `allowed_token_ids` parameter — a ~10 line code change requiring no modifications to model weights. Experiments train Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B base models using the R-Zero co-evolutionary framework (GRPO with solver self-consistency reward) for 5 iterations. Diversity is measured at three granularities: lexical (self-BLEU), semantic (Vendi score, novelty rate with text-embedding-3-small), and functional (epiplexity via prequential MDL). Ablations isolate training-only vs. generation-only masking phases and cross-scale (8B proposer → 4B solver) settings. An annealing schedule (α: 0.75→1.0 linearly over 5 iterations) is also tested. Key Results: At 8B, VD with α=0.75 achieves +4.4 points average pass@1 over the baseline (39.4→43.8), with the largest gains on competition-level benchmarks: AMC +10.9 (40.8→51.7), AIME'24 +3.4 (12.2→15.6), AIME'25 +7.8 (3.3→11.1). At 4B, VD with α=0.85 beats the baseline by +1.0 avg (38.3→39.3), while aggressive VD75 underperforms (36.5), revealing scale-dependent sensitivity. Diversity metrics show the baseline proposer collapses almost entirely between iterations 1 and 2 (mean difficulty jumps from 0.50 to 0.86 at 4B in one step), while VD sustains growth across all metrics through iteration 5: self-BLEU is ~2× lower, Vendi score shows ~15 more effective question types, novelty rate is 1.5–2× higher, and epiplexity is ~35% higher. VD75 proposers use 36–52% more unique tokens than the baseline despite fewer tokens available per batch. Annealed VD (0.75→1.0) achieves the highest cumulative performance and lower iteration-to-iteration volatility than fixed VD75. Cross-scale pairing (8B proposer → 4B solver) with VD hurts (−1.4 avg), and the method fails to benefit instruction-tuned models at the tested scales. Strengths: - Conceptually clean and well-motivated: the analogy between game action-space structure and vocabulary constraints is precise, not metaphorical, and directly informs the mechanism design. - Negligible implementation overhead: a ~10 line vLLM change with no weight modifications, no auxiliary models, and no reward redesign — easy to adopt in any GRPO-based co-evolutionary pipeline. - Multi-level diversity evaluation is thorough: the paper tracks lexical, semantic, and functional (epiplexity) metrics, revealing that standard surface metrics (self-BLEU) fail to detect collapse and that functional stagnation is the operative failure mode. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The method reduces proposer answer correctness by ~7% (from ~38-42% to ~30-34% under generation-phase dropout), meaning the solver trains on a noisier curriculum; the diversity benefit only dominates at sufficient model scale (8B), and the approach depends on larger model capacity to absorb incoherence. - Scope is narrow: tested only on mathematical reasoning with capacity-matched base models; the approach fails for instruction-tuned models and cross-scale pairings, limiting practical applicability to a specific setup, and extension to non-mathematical domains remains unvalidated. - The self-consistency verification mechanism is fundamentally unable to distinguish genuinely hard problems from ill-posed ones (as documented in the qualitative analysis), and this noise grows under stronger dropout; the paper defers the fix (pairing with a symbolic verifier) to future work.
Recommended 2604.03472 PDF
#116 — Moderately Innovative (57)

FGeo-ISRL: A MCTS-Enhanced Deep Reinforcement Learning System for Plane Geometry Problem-Solving via Inverse Search

mdpi.com · neural symbolic - new results
Why this rank: The inverse search paradigm — initializing proofs from both conditions and conclusions simultaneously — is a conceptually clean contribution that meaningfully differs from standard forward/backward approaches. The integration with MCTS and dual neural networks in a formally verifiable framework is well-executed. However, sharp performance collapse at high difficulty (29% on L6 vs. 57% for HyperGNet) and dependence on manual formalization of problems limit both the novelty claim and practical applicability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Automated geometric reasoning requires deriving conclusions from geometric conditions through theorem application and logical deduction. Traditional symbolic forward/backward search systems suffer from exponential search space complexity as proof chains grow longer, and generalize poorly across problem types. Large language models offer strong natural language understanding but produce non-verifiable, black-box reasoning that cannot be confirmed as rigorous proofs. The gap is a system that is simultaneously accurate, computationally efficient, and formally verifiable — the core challenge this paper addresses within the FormalGeo formal geometry framework. Methodology: FGeo-ISRL is a neuro-symbolic framework built on three tightly integrated components. The first is inverse search, a novel search paradigm that initializes the proof state by merging both the given conditions and the target conclusion simultaneously, then iteratively incorporates matching theorem premises until the goal is satisfied. This differs from pure forward search (conditions → conclusions) and backward search (goal → sub-goals), and reduces the effective search space by exploiting condition-goal correlations from the outset. The second component is dual BERT-base networks: a policy network that outputs a probability distribution over 234 discrete theorem-branch actions (expanded from 196 base theorems to handle geometric symmetry and topological variation), trained first via supervised cross-entropy on FormalGeo7K state-action pairs, then refined with policy gradient using MCTS-generated trajectories; and a value network trained as a regression task (MSE loss with L2 regularization) to score proof states in [0,1], replacing expensive random rollouts in MCTS. The third is MCTS with enhanced UCB that incorporates the policy network's prior probabilities to guide tree selection, while the value network drives simulation rollouts, creating an AlphaGo-style exploration-optimization cycle. Training uses a hybrid reward: an immediate reward based on the sigmoid-normalized value difference between successive states, plus a sparse terminal reward of +1 upon problem completion. A rule-based parameter adaptation mechanism handles theorem instantiation through predicate abstraction, logical constraint matching, and variable binding. Key Results: On the FormalGeo7K dataset (split 70/15/15 by type and difficulty), FGeo-ISRL achieves a Geometric Problem-Solving Accuracy (GPSA) of 83.14% and Single-Step Theorem Accuracy (SSTA) of 90.2%. This surpasses a broad range of baselines: forward search (39.71%), backward search (35.44%), BART-base+FGeo (54.0%), DeepSeek-v3 zero-shot (60.79%), Inter-GPS (60.50%), NGS (62.60%), DualGeoSolver (62.11%), FGeo-DRL (80.85%), and FGeo-TP-RS (80.86%). However, FGeo-HyperGNet, which embeds state transitions via a hypergraph neural network, outperforms it at 88.36% overall and 56.67% on the hardest L6 problems versus FGeo-ISRL's 29.21%. Ablation studies confirm the value network is the most critical component (removing it drops GPSA from 80.69% to 60.35% at Top-K=10), and removing MCTS drops GPSA from 80.69% to 72.88%. Scaling training epochs from 20 to 50 and step time from 300 to 600 improves GPSA from 74.49% to 83.14%, but SSTA plateaus at 90.2%, suggesting diminishing returns from compute scaling alone. Strengths: - Inverse search is a conceptually clean and practically effective alternative to forward/backward search, leveraging bidirectional condition-goal context to prune the search space. - Every proof step is formally verified by the FormalGeo symbolic system and converted to human-readable natural language, providing genuine verifiability that pure LLM approaches lack. - The two-stage training (supervised pretraining followed by MCTS-guided policy gradient) substantially reduces dependence on large-scale human annotation of intermediate reasoning steps. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Performance collapses sharply at high difficulty: L6 GPSA is only 29.21%, worse than FGeo-HyperGNet's 56.67%, indicating that MCTS-guided inverse search does not scale well to long proof chains without richer state representations. - The system operates entirely within FormalGeo's formalized problem representation; natural language geometry problems must first be converted to formal CDL, a step that remains largely manual (though a separate parser is noted as future/parallel work), limiting end-to-end applicability. - The parameter adaptation mechanism has a known failure mode (repeated judgment limitation) that causes correct theorem selections to fail at instantiation, as demonstrated in Case 2.
Search Results
#117 — Moderately Innovative (57)

A Semantics-aware Approach for Graph Edit Distance Estimation over Knowledge Graphs

vldb.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: Scores 57 as the first GED estimation method designed natively for knowledge graphs, with a well-motivated dual local/global semantic architecture. The dramatic accuracy gains on YAGO and WIKIDATA validate the core thesis that KG-specific structure matters. However, the synthetic ground-truth labels (random edit operations) likely inflate accuracy figures and do not reflect real-world KG evolution, substantially weakening the empirical claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Graph Edit Distance (GED) — the minimum number of node/edge insertions, deletions, and relabelings needed to transform one graph into another — is a fundamental similarity metric for Knowledge Graphs (KGs), with applications in drug discovery, graph databases, and pattern recognition. Computing GED exactly is NP-hard, intractable beyond ~16 nodes. While GNN-based approximation methods (SimGNN, GEDGNN, GEDIOT) exist, they target plain labeled graphs and ignore two KG-specific challenges: (1) semantic entanglement, where a single node-label edit can invalidate a cascade of logically dependent triples; and (2) global semantic inconsistency, where type hierarchies and role constraints create distributional differences that simple node-level alignment cannot capture. No prior work addresses GED estimation natively for RDF/triple-structured KGs. Methodology: The authors propose SEABED, a three-stage GNN-based model. Stage 1 (initial embedding extraction) uses RDF2Vec skip-gram embeddings to initialize node and edge representations, augmented with 5-dimensional local motif-count vectors derived from subgraph isomorphism counts — theoretically grounded by a lemma showing that isomorphic KGs must share identical motif-count vectors. Stage 2 (local semantic alignment) partitions each KG into k/2 overlapping sub-KGs using METIS with pairwise cosine-similarity edge weights, ensuring semantically similar entities cluster together. Each sub-KG is encoded by a GNN into a token embedding, then processed by a self-attention module (treating sub-KGs as tokens analogous to NLP words). A novel position encoding based on inter-subgraph edge-cut counts captures topological relationships between partitions; a CLS-style token aggregates the final local representation. Stage 3 (global semantic estimator) applies a KG-native message-passing function (KMP, extending GINEConv) that explicitly separates incoming and outgoing neighbor aggregation to handle edge directionality and predicate semantics. A Neural Tensor Network (NTN) then models global pairwise interactions. Training minimizes MSE loss plus an Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) auxiliary loss over the sorted global embedding distributions, encouraging the model to align overall semantic distributions between the two KGs. The local and global vectors are concatenated and fed to an MLP for final GED prediction. Key Results: Evaluated on four real-world KGs (SWDF, LUBM, YAGO, WIKIDATA) with 50,000 graph pairs each, SEABED outperforms all baselines on every dataset. The most dramatic improvements appear on the larger, semantically richer KGs: on YAGO, SEABED achieves 95.78% accuracy vs. GEDIOT's 47.01% (a 103% improvement) and MAE 0.177 vs. 0.678; on WIKIDATA, accuracy reaches 89.48% vs. 45.59% for GEDIOT, with MAE dropping from 0.391 to 0.242. On smaller datasets (SWDF, LUBM), gains are more modest (MAE reduction ~12-16%, accuracy improvement ~5-18%). Ablation studies confirm the local semantic alignment module contributes most (21.72% average MAE reduction), followed by motif-count features (13.54%) and EMD loss (5.70%). Results are robust across four KGE models (RDF2Vec, TransR, CompGCN, DistMult), and SEABED maintains superiority across five KG size scales. GPU memory usage is approximately 2× baseline methods but stays under 3GB. All improvements are statistically significant (p < 0.05 for MAE, p < 0.01 for accuracy vs. GEDIOT). Strengths: - First KG-native GED estimation method: the dual local/global semantic design is well-motivated by formal challenges specific to RDF/triple structures, not just ad hoc engineering. - Extremely large accuracy gains on semantically rich KGs (YAGO, WIKIDATA), precisely the setting where KG-native modeling matters most, validating the core thesis. - Theoretically grounded motif-count feature augmentation (Lemma 4.3) provides principled justification rather than purely empirical motivation. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Ground-truth GED labels for YAGO and WIKIDATA are synthetic (random edit operations applied to subgraphs), not derived from real KG version histories or evolution logs. The authors acknowledge this may not reflect true real-world edit patterns, and the near-perfect accuracy on YAGO (95.78%) likely reflects the simplicity of this synthetic labeling rather than true difficulty. - Scalability degrades meaningfully at larger graph sizes: at SWDF-4 and SWDF-5, SEABED's accuracy (21.19%±11.25 and 17.46%±9.55) shows high variance and modest absolute performance, suggesting the sub-KG partitioning and self-attention approach struggles as graphs grow, and the METIS semantic similarity matrix computation scales quadratically in node count. - The global signal dominance failure mode — where globally similar KGs with local differences cause GED underestimation — is identified but not addressed, leaving a known systematic error source unresolved.
Search Results
#118 — Moderately Innovative (56)

KnowRL: Boosting LLM Reasoning via Reinforcement Learning with Minimal-Sufficient Knowledge Guidance (82 ▲ )

Linhao Yu, Tianmeng Yang, Siyu Ding, Renren Jin, Naibin Gu, Xiangzhao Hao, Shuaiyi Nie, Deyi Xiong, Weichong Yin, Yu Sun, Hua Wu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 56 for formalizing hint design as a minimal-sufficient guidance problem and demonstrating that selection quality dominates quantity through the CSS algorithm. The no-hint inference results confirming genuine policy improvement rather than hint-conditioned shortcuts are well-designed. However, the approach is narrow (single model family, math-only), depends heavily on DeepSeek-R1 for KP curation, and the computational cost of the offline search phase is substantial.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) is a promising paradigm for improving LLM mathematical reasoning, but it suffers from severe reward sparsity on hard problems: the model generates uniformly incorrect rollouts, yielding zero gradient signal under GRPO-style optimization. Prior hint-based RL methods inject partial solution prefixes or abstract reasoning templates to alleviate sparsity, but they treat hint design as a quantity-expansion problem—more guidance is assumed to be better. The paper identifies three concrete failure modes: (1) a critical-segment effect where accuracy jumps sharply once a short key segment appears but gains diminish rapidly beyond it, (2) cross-hint inconsistency where longer or richer hints introduce branching and ambiguity that complicates policy updates, and (3) guidance-efficiency trade-offs when abstraction-based hints require expensive teacher models. The core thesis is that effective guidance requires selecting the minimal sufficient set of knowledge, not maximizing hint length. Methodology: KnowRL (Knowledge-Guided Reinforcement Learning) formalizes hint design as a minimal-sufficient guidance problem. The framework operates in two offline stages before RL training. In Stage 1 (KP Curation), for each training problem DeepSeek-R1 generates correct solutions, extracts indispensable atomic knowledge points (KPs), and a leakage verification step filters out instance-bound KPs to retain only generalizable mathematical principles—yielding an average of 5.86 raw KPs per problem. Stage 2 (Problem-wise KP Subset Selection) addresses the pruning interaction paradox: removing a single KP may improve accuracy, but removing multiple such KPs jointly can degrade performance due to inter-KP dependencies. The paper evaluates several strategies: Max-Score (pick the best among no KPs, all KPs, or leave-one-out), S-LOO/T-LOO (leave-one-out with tolerance), Consensus-Based Robust Selection (CBRS, which aggregates across 8 independent evaluation runs), and Constrained Subset Search (CSS). CSS is the key contribution: it identifies near-optimal single-removal candidates (set N) and removes them directly, then exhaustively searches within the remaining borderline candidates (set C), yielding tractable 2^|C| search space. Difficulty-aware prompt injection adds selected KPs only for harder training instances, while simple problems receive no hints. RL training uses GRPO with entropy annealing (clip_high reduced from 0.28 to 0.26 at step 2,590) and dynamic sampling over 8.8k instances from the QuestA dataset. Key Results: KnowRL-Nemotron-1.5B, trained from OpenMath-Nemotron-1.5B for 2,960 steps on 8 NVIDIA H100 nodes (≈13 days), achieves 70.08 average accuracy across eight benchmarks without any KP hints at inference—surpassing the base Nemotron-1.5B by +9.63 points and outperforming JustRL by +1.50. With CSS-selected KP hints at inference, average accuracy rises to 74.16, a new state of the art at the 1.5B scale. Gains are especially large on hard competition benchmarks: +15.11 on AIME25, +12.98 on HMMT25, +15.49 on CMIMC25 (vs. base without KPs). CSS (2.57 avg. KPs/problem) consistently outperforms CBRS (2.60 avg. KPs) in both offline evaluation and final RL training at matched budgets (65.00 vs. 64.68 at step 400; 66.46 vs. 65.72 at step 900). The random KP baseline at the same cardinality (≈2.5 KPs) performs substantially worse than both CSS and CBRS, confirming that selection quality—not count—drives performance. Reward sparsity is dramatically reduced: the zero-correct fraction on training problems drops from 41.21% (base) to 13.00% (KnowRL), while the all-correct fraction jumps from 1.35% to 34.28%. Strengths: - The minimal-sufficiency framing is principled and empirically grounded: the critical-segment effect and the pruning interaction paradox are precisely quantified, motivating the CSS algorithm rather than being treated as intuitions. - CSS achieves the best offline accuracy (63.90 avg.) with the fewest KPs (2.57), outperforming all simpler strategies including random sampling at matched cardinality, demonstrating that selection quality dominates quantity. - The no-hint inference results (70.08 avg.) are compelling evidence that KnowRL improves the underlying policy rather than creating hint-conditioned shortcuts, a common concern with hint-based RL methods. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The entire KP curation pipeline depends on DeepSeek-R1 as a generator and reviewer, which is itself a large, expensive model. The approach is not self-contained and inherits any biases or errors in DeepSeek-R1's mathematical reasoning. - Evaluation is restricted to a single model family (Nemotron-1.5B) and a single domain (mathematical reasoning). Whether CSS-style KP selection generalizes to other modalities (code, science) or larger model scales is explicitly left as future work, limiting the breadth of the claims. - The CSS search requires 8×32 model rollouts per problem across multiple candidate KP configurations offline, which is computationally non-trivial despite being labeled 'tractable'; the paper does not report the total compute cost of data construction relative to RL training itself.
Recommended 2604.12627 PDF
#119 — Moderately Innovative (56)

Polyglot Teachers: Evaluating Language Models for Multilingual Synthetic Data Generation

Lester James V. Miranda, Ivan Vulić, Anna Korhonen · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 56 for its unusually rigorous experimental scale (10 teachers, 6 languages, 240 student models) and the practically useful finding that model size and aggregate benchmark scores do not predict multilingual teaching effectiveness. The PG-SCORE screening tool offering R²=0.664 prediction of student performance from cheap intrinsic metrics is a genuinely actionable contribution. However, the work is methodologically incremental — it combines existing metrics and evaluation protocols at scale rather than introducing new techniques, and language coverage omits the truly low-resource settings where these findings would matter most.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Supervised finetuning (SFT) of smaller language models on synthetic data generated by larger "teacher" models has become standard practice for multilingual model development, where human-annotated data is scarce for most languages. However, teacher model selection is typically ad hoc — practitioners default to the largest or highest-benchmark-scoring model available, assuming bigger equals better. This assumption is poorly validated for non-English languages, where even strong English-centric models may have significant capability gaps, propagating teacher weaknesses into the synthetic training data and degrading student performance. The paper asks: what actually makes an effective multilingual teacher, and how can it be measured systematically? Methodology: The authors propose POLYGLOT SCORE (PG-SCORE), a composite evaluation metric that jointly measures intrinsic data quality and extrinsic student model performance. Ten teacher models — spanning Llama 3.1 (8B, 70B), Gemma 3 (4B, 12B, 27B), Command A, Aya Expanse 32B, IBM Granite (4.0, Micro), and GPT-4o mini — are evaluated across six typologically diverse languages (Arabic, Czech, German, Spanish, Indonesian, Japanese). Three synthetic data generation strategies are tested: Generate (few-shot prompted generation), Translate (English prompts forward-translated, then responded to), and Respond (existing target-language prompts answered). Intrinsic metrics include prompt/response diversity (cosine distance of Llama-Embed-Nemotron-8B embeddings), base model perplexity on generated responses, and a multilingual LLM-as-a-judge score from M-Prometheus 14B. Extrinsic evaluation finetunes OLMo 3 7B on 10.5k synthetic examples per teacher-language pair and measures Performance Gap Recovered (PGR) on Global-MMLU Lite (cultural knowledge), M-RewardBench (general chat alignment), and M-GSM (mathematical reasoning). In total, over 1.4M SFT examples are generated and 240 student models trained. Principal component analysis (PCA) over intrinsic metrics and a linear regression from the resulting PCs to student performance are used to identify which data properties actually drive downstream quality. Key Results: Gemma 3 27B (PG-SCORE 0.726) and Aya Expanse 32B (0.706) are the most effective teachers on average, both substantially outperforming Llama 3.1 70B (0.140) despite the latter being nearly 3× larger. The entire Gemma 3 family dominates, while the Llama 3.1 family consistently underperforms. A mixed-effects regression confirms that neither log(parameter count) nor average multilingual benchmark performance significantly predicts PG-SCORE (p > 0.05 for both). PCA of intrinsic metrics shows four principal components explain 93.3% of variance: PC1 (42.2%) captures response fluency and distinctiveness, PC2 (22.1%) captures prompt diversity and length, PC3–4 reinforce prompt-side trends. A linear model over these PCs predicts extrinsic student performance with R²=0.664, providing a cheap screening tool that avoids full student training runs. Teacher-student family matching yields ≥+20.5% PG-SCORE improvement over mismatched baselines. For high-resource languages, Generate is most effective; for lower-resource languages (Arabic, Indonesian), Respond or Translate outperform a uniform mix by up to +458%. Teacher effectiveness strongly correlates with a language's CommonCrawl representation (ρ=0.886, p<0.05). A held-out case study on Tagalog validates the recipe: the resulting 4B model is competitive with Llama 3.1 8B Instruct and Qwen 3 4B on FILBENCH. Strengths: - The scale of the study is unusually rigorous: 10 teachers × 6 languages × 3 generation methods × 3 random seeds × multiple student base models, yielding strong statistical grounding. - The PG-SCORE framework is practically useful — the finding that intrinsic metrics (cheap to compute) predict student performance with R²=0.664 offers a meaningful screening tool for practitioners without full training runs. - The paper provides concrete, actionable recommendations (family matching, language-specific generation method selection) validated on a held-out language, lending credibility to generalizability claims. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Language coverage is limited to six languages, all of which have at least modest CommonCrawl representation; the most resource-scarce languages (Category 0–2 in Joshi et al.) are entirely absent, weakening claims about inclusivity precisely where they matter most. - The PGR extrinsic metric is computed relative to OLMo 3 7B Instruct SFT as a reference, which is itself a Gemma-family-adjacent model — this choice may structurally inflate PG-SCOREs for Gemma teachers by anchoring the reference in a way that favors family-matched outputs. - The intrinsic metrics (diversity, perplexity, LLM-judge score) are all model-based proxies that may not reliably capture cultural appropriateness or factual accuracy, and the choice of M-Prometheus 14B as judge introduces an unvalidated multilingual reward model into the measurement pipeline.
Recommended 2604.11290 PDF
#120 — Moderately Innovative (56)

Towards Long-horizon Agentic Multimodal Search (15 ▲ )

Yifan Du, Zikang Liu, Jinbiao Peng, Jie Wu, Junyi Li, Jinyang Li, Wayne Xin Zhao, Ji-Rong Wen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Apr 2026
Why this rank: The file-based UID representation for managing visual context explosion is a clean and effective engineering solution, and demonstrating framework generalization across multiple frontier models validates its model-agnostic nature. However, the core idea—offloading images to external storage and replacing them with lightweight references—is a relatively straightforward application of well-known indirection principles. The data synthesis pipeline is competent but incremental, and key design choices (merging coefficient, context truncation heuristic) remain ad-hoc.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multimodal deep search agents that iteratively collect text and visual evidence suffer from "context explosion" when operating over long horizons: each retrieved image consumes large numbers of tokens, causing context windows to fill rapidly. Prior text-focused context compression methods (summarization, truncation) do not transfer to this setting because images cannot be treated as text. Existing systems either discard intermediate images heuristically—losing potentially critical visual evidence—or accept context bloat that limits scalability. The paper asks: how can a multimodal search agent manage accumulated visual context across many interaction turns without sacrificing either completeness or efficiency? Methodology: The paper proposes LMM-Searcher, built on three coordinated ideas. First, a file-based visual representation mechanism offloads every retrieved image to an external file system and replaces it in the agent's context with a lightweight textual unique identifier (UID), typically the image's URL. This decouples reasoning (which needs a compact context) from perception (which needs full image fidelity). Second, an extended agentic tool interface is redesigned around this UID abstraction: search tools (google_search, image_search, visual_search) return UID references rather than raw images; scrape_website serializes web pages with images replaced by UIDs; a new fetch_image tool bridges the UID space back to pixel space on demand; and zoom_in applies transformations, producing a new stored image with its own UID. Together these form a coarse-to-fine perception funnel. Third, a data synthesis pipeline generates training queries requiring cross-modal multi-hop reasoning: single-hop visual questions are synthesized from real multimodal web pages (entity extraction + visual QA over an image that cannot be answered from text alone), then extended into multi-hop questions via iterative knowledge-graph construction and node fuzzification. Resulting queries are filtered for difficulty (must require search engine invocation), rolled out with Seed-1.8 under rejection sampling (≤40 turns, 64K context), and 12,736 successful trajectories are retained. Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Thinking is fine-tuned via supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on these trajectories, then parameter-interpolated (α=0.8) with MiroThinker-1.7-mini to transfer long-horizon text-search scaling capability into the multimodal model. Key Results: On four benchmarks at the standard 30-turn evaluation limit, LMM-Searcher-30B achieves 22.3 on MM-BrowseComp (MMBC), 32.9 on MMSearch-Plus, 42.0 on VisBrowse, and 71.0 on MMSearch, averaging 42.1—competitive with or exceeding prior open-source 30B agents including REDSearcher-MM-30B (23.5 / 26.6 / — / 72.9). When the turn limit is extended to 100 with the UID-based context management active, performance improves substantially to 30.1 / 34.8 / 48.3 / 72.3, establishing state-of-the-art among open-source models on MMBC and MMSearch-Plus. Framework generalization is demonstrated by applying the tool interface alone (no fine-tuning) to frontier models: Seed-1.8 improves from 21.4→35.1 on MMBC and 11.0→46.7 on MMSearch-Plus; GPT-5 improves from 17.2→36.5 on MMBC. Ablations confirm that removing the fetch_image tool degrades performance by an average of 5.1 points, with the largest drop on VisBrowse (−9.5), which requires reading images from web pages. The synthesized multi-hop queries contribute an additional 1.0 point average over training with existing open-source data alone. Strengths: - The file-based UID representation is a clean, principled solution to context explosion that preserves all visual information (no irreversible discard) while keeping context costs linear in the number of search turns, not in image token counts. - Demonstrating framework generalization across GPT-5, Seed-1.8, and Qwen3 with consistent large improvements over a prior baseline validates that the design is model-agnostic, not merely a fine-tuning artifact. - The multi-hop data synthesis pipeline is mechanistically grounded—knowledge-graph construction with irreversibility constraints prevents shortcut reasoning—and the resulting trajectories have substantially longer average tool-use chains (17.26 turns) than prior datasets, directly addressing the scarcity of long-horizon multimodal training signal. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The context management strategy at 100 turns retains only the 5 most recent tool call results, which is borrowed from DeepSeek-V3.2 and amounts to a heuristic truncation of text history; this is not systematically analyzed and could still cause information loss for long chains requiring recall of earlier tool outputs. - The model merging coefficient α=0.8 is set by hand and the authors explicitly defer rigorous study to future work, leaving unclear how sensitive the performance gains are to this choice and whether the merging procedure generalizes to other backbone pairs. - Evaluation is limited to image-centric benchmarks and the single-image subset of MMSearch-Plus; performance on richer modalities (video, tables, interleaved documents) and very large-scale web environments beyond the tested benchmarks is untested.
Recommended 2604.12890 PDF
#121 — Moderately Innovative (56)

A Graph-Enhanced Defense Framework for Explainable Fake News Detection with LLM

Bo Wang, Jing Ma, Hongzhan Lin, Zhiwei Yang, Ruichao Yang, Yuan Tian, Yi Chang · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: G-Defense introduces a principled claim-centered DAG with LLM-generated dependency edges and a competing-explanation mechanism that elegantly sidesteps the problem of unverified evidence. The modular pipeline and sub-claim decomposition represent meaningful design contributions. However, it is largely an extension of the authors' own L-Defense framework, and reliance on GPT-3.5 as both backbone and evaluator weakens the evaluation story.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Fake news detection systems must not only classify veracity but also provide clear, human-friendly explanations to help the public evaluate claims. Existing LLM-based approaches either depend on verified knowledge bases (which lack coverage for breaking news) or retrieve unverified raw reports and treat them uncritically. Moreover, prior work processes claims holistically, missing fine-grained sub-aspects. When claims are decomposed into sub-claims, prior methods treat sub-claims independently, ignoring logical dependencies between them. The result is fragmented, coarse-grained explanations that fail to cover all facets of a complex claim. Methodology: G-Defense is a four-stage pipeline that extends the authors' prior L-Defense framework. (1) Graph Construction: An LLM decomposes the input claim into atomic sub-claims, then generates directed dependency edges between them, yielding a claim-centered DAG where nodes are the original claim plus sub-claims and edges encode logical prerequisites. (2) Competing Explanation Generation with RAG: For each sub-claim, top-k evidential sentences are retrieved from unverified raw reports via cosine similarity using a frozen Transformer encoder. The LLM then generates two competing explanations — one conditioned on a true prior label, one on false — by abductive reasoning over the retrieved evidence. (3) Defense-like Inference: The explanation-enriched graph is serialized to text via Graph2Seq and fed into a fine-tuned Flan-T5-large encoder. An MLP classifier over the CLS representation makes the veracity prediction by implicitly comparing the informativeness and soundness of the competing explanation pairs. The fine-tuning loss is cross-entropy over gold labels. (4) Explanation Summarization: The LLM is prompted with the graph and predicted label to assess per-sub-claim veracity and generate both a pruned explanation graph and a concise textual summary. The backbone LLM for frozen stages is GPT-3.5-turbo; the classifier encoder alone is fine-tuned. Evidence retrieval uses k=5 sentences per sub-claim. Key Results: On RAWFC (3-class), G-Defense achieves macro-F1 of 64.31 averaged over three seeds, versus 61.20 for the prior SOTA (L-DefenseGPT-3.5) — a 3.1% gain across all metrics; improvements are statistically significant (p = 3×10⁻⁴). On LIAR-RAW (6-class), G-Defense achieves macro-F1 of 31.55, improving precision by 2.54% and macro-F1 by 1.02% over L-DefenseGPT-3.5. For explanation quality on RAWFC (GPT-3.5 rated), G-Defense achieves the lowest discrepancy (D = 1.00) and misleadingness (M = 1.87) among all non-Oracle methods, and the highest informativeness (I = 4.60), matching or exceeding Oracle on some metrics. Human evaluation on 30 sampled RAWFC claims (five annotators) confirms the same ranking: G-Defense leads on all four quality dimensions except Oracle. Confusion matrix analysis shows that providing G-Defense explanations reduces human judgment errors relative to both no-explanation and L-Defense-explanation conditions. Ablation establishes that sub-claim decomposition contributes the largest performance increment (+9.55 macro-F1 on RAWFC), followed by competing explanations, evidence retrieval, and dependency edges. Strengths: - The claim-centered graph with LLM-generated dependency edges is a principled advance over independent sub-claim verification, enabling the inference module to exploit inter-sub-claim logical structure rather than treating all sub-claims as equally independent. - The defense-like mechanism is an elegant solution to unverified evidence: instead of filtering or trusting raw reports, it generates competing hypotheses and lets the asymmetry in evidence quality surface naturally, empirically validated in Appendix B across multiple LLMs and prompt variants. - The framework is modular and LLM-agnostic — substituting LLaMA-3.1-8B for GPT-3.5 surpasses the GPT-based variant on LIAR-RAW, and several enrichment variants (background context, enhanced decomposition prompts) provide further gains on appropriate datasets. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The inference latency grows linearly with the number of sub-claims since evidence retrieval and competing explanation generation are both sub-claim-level and sequential; the paper acknowledges this but offers no parallelized implementation or concrete latency numbers, making it difficult to assess real-time applicability. - Claim decomposition and edge generation are entirely LLM-driven with no verification of graph quality; the paper relies on prompt design safeguards (minimum two sub-claims, forced edges to root) rather than structural validation, and there is no analysis of decomposition errors or their downstream impact beyond a brief qualitative discussion. - Evaluation uses GPT-3.5 as both the backbone LLM and the automated explanation evaluator, creating a potential self-reinforcement bias; and the two benchmark datasets (RAWFC, LIAR-RAW) originate exclusively from US political and social media fact-checking platforms, leaving generalizability to scientific, health, or multilingual misinformation undemonstrated.
Recommended 2604.06666 PDF
#122 — Moderately Innovative (55)

QCFuse: Query-Centric Cache Fusion for Efficient RAG Inference

Jianxin Yan, Zeheng Qian, Wangze Ni, Zhitao Shen, Zhiping Wang, Haoyang Li, Jia Zhu, Lei Chen, Kui Ren · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: QCFuse introduces a sensible query-centric refinement to existing cache fusion methods, with the anchor-based probing mechanism and single critical-layer selection being clean engineering contributions. The attention denoising finding — outperforming full computation — is intriguing. However, as a 4-page demo paper it lacks ablations and theoretical grounding, and the overall approach is an incremental improvement on CacheBlend/EPIC rather than a fundamentally new direction.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: In high-concurrency RAG deployments, LLMs must repeatedly prefill overlapping context chunks retrieved for different queries. Traditional prefix caching fails because retrieval chunks are dynamically reordered, preventing prefix-tree reuse. This causes time-to-first-token (TTFT) to grow quadratically with context length. Cache fusion methods (CacheBlend, EPIC, KVShare) address this by reusing precomputed KV caches and selectively recomputing only a subset of tokens, but they rely on local, query-agnostic heuristics—first-layer KV deviations or fixed static ratios—that waste compute on irrelevant tokens and miss query-critical ones, causing accuracy degradation under aggressive acceleration. Methodology: QCFuse introduces a query-centric selection strategy built around two technical contributions. First, anchor-based lightweight query probing: during offline pre-computation, tokens with the highest key-norm magnitudes are extracted from each context chunk as compact semantic anchors and stored in CPU memory. At inference time, these lightweight anchors are injected as prefixes during query forwarding, giving the query representations contextual grounding without requiring the full SSD-resident KV cache to be materialized—preserving the layer-by-layer pipelined execution. Second, critical-layer attention profiling: rather than computing attention across all layers (which blocks the pipeline due to cross-layer dependencies) or only the final layer (semantically shallow), QCFuse empirically identifies a single middle Transformer layer whose attention distribution best localizes semantically important tokens. The Top-N context tokens by query attention at this layer are selected for recomputation. The recomputation itself uses a custom location-aware sparse attention Triton kernel integrated into SGLang that supports discrete, non-contiguous token indices with correct causal masking. The pipeline overlaps GPU recomputation of layer i with SSD prefetch of layer i+1's KV cache. Key Results: Evaluated on one A100 (80GB) with Llama3.1-8B, Qwen3-8B, and Mistral-v0.3-7B across MuSiQue, 2WikiMQA, and HotpotQA multi-hop QA benchmarks. QCFuse achieves 2× speedup in TTFT over full computation and 40% latency reduction over existing cache fusion baselines (CacheBlend, EPIC, KVShare) at matched accuracy. ROUGE-L scores are 2.3–3.5 points higher than CacheBlend. At a 40% recomputation ratio, QCFuse matches full-computation accuracy. On HotpotQA, QCFuse exceeds full computation by 0.8 ROUGE-L points, attributed to an attention denoising effect where irrelevant context tokens are excluded. QCFuse also matches the accuracy of QCAll (all-layer query-guided baseline) while running faster, and substantially outperforms QCLast (last-layer only baseline). Strengths: - The anchor extraction mechanism is elegant: it provides query-context grounding at negligible cost (CPU-resident, no SSD transfer) by leveraging key-norm as a proxy for semantic salience, preserving the critical pipeline structure. - The single critical-layer profiling insight—that middle layers offer superior semantic localization—is practically validated and sidesteps the pipeline stall problem that plagues all-layer approaches like ProphetKV. - The attention denoising effect (outperforming full computation on HotpotQA) is a compelling finding that suggests query-centric sparse attention is not merely an approximation but can be genuinely superior. Weaknesses / Limitations: - This is a 4-page demonstration paper, not a full research paper; the empirical evaluation is consequently thin—no ablation studies isolating the contribution of anchor probing vs. critical-layer selection, no analysis of how the critical layer is identified across models, and no variance/significance reporting. - The anchor extraction heuristic (highest key-norm tokens) is not theoretically justified, and the fraction of tokens selected as anchors is not specified, leaving unclear how sensitive accuracy is to this design choice. - Evaluation is limited to ROUGE-L on multi-hop QA; no measurement of generation quality on open-ended tasks, nor analysis of failure modes when retrieved chunks are highly heterogeneous or when the critical layer selection is suboptimal for a given model.
