Search Results 2604.08585 PDF
#123 — Moderately Innovative (55)

Enabling Low-Latency, GPU-Efficient Serverless Inference with Model Swapping

dl.acm.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Torpor addresses a real production pain point with a clean late-binding design principle and an unusually complete validation chain from microbenchmarks through Alibaba Cloud production deployment with 70% user cost reduction. The interference-aware scheduling with heavy/light model categorization and RRC-based prioritization is well-motivated. However, the core technical mechanisms — CUDA API redirection, pipelined transfers, buddy allocation, SLO-aware scheduling — are established systems techniques applied to a new context rather than fundamentally new ideas.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing serverless platforms (AWS Lambda, Alibaba Function Compute) lack efficient GPU support for ML inference. The dominant approach is "early binding": each inference function is statically assigned to a dedicated GPU and kept resident to avoid cold-start latencies (which can reach tens of seconds). This is economically wasteful: production traces from Alibaba Cloud show 85% of functions are invoked no more than once per minute, yielding low GPU utilization and cross-GPU load imbalance. Users pay for idle GPU time, and providers cannot efficiently multiplex GPUs across many functions. The gap is a serverless platform that simultaneously provides pay-per-GPU-use billing, high GPU utilization, latency SLO compliance, and model-agnostic operation—no existing system satisfies all four. Methodology: Torpor is a serverless inference platform built on a "late binding" principle: inference models are kept in host DRAM and dynamically swapped onto GPUs only upon request arrival. Each worker node exposes a GPU pool; inference functions access any GPU transparently via CUDA API redirection. Four technical contributions address the resulting challenges. (1) Asynchronous API redirection: inference CUDA calls are classified as synchronous (e.g., cudaMalloc) or asynchronous (e.g., cudaLaunchKernel); the latter are batched and redirected to the GPU executor without blocking, eliminating per-call round-trip synchronization overhead that plagues prior GPU remoting systems (e.g., GVirtuS). (2) Pipelined model swapping: models are transferred host-to-GPU via PCIe using pinned memory pools; parameter groups are sized at a hardware-determined "elbow point" to overlap transfer with computation; GPU-to-GPU transfers via NVLink are prioritized when available. This is fully model-agnostic. (3) Unified GPU memory management: Torpor reserves all GPU memory at bootstrap and manages it with an extended buddy allocator, translating logical model addresses to physical GPU addresses as models migrate across GPUs without requiring framework modifications. Two isolation modes (runtime sharing/isolation) are offered. (4) SLO-aware scheduling: a Required Request Count (RRC) metric quantifies how many more successful requests a function needs to meet its tail-latency SLO; functions are prioritized by RRC. An adaptive interference-aware scheduler routes requests to minimize PCIe contention by preferring NVLink GPU-to-GPU swaps for "heavy" models (those whose H2D swap latency exceeds native inference latency). A complementary eviction policy prioritizes evicting light models first (negligible PCIe overhead), falling back to LRU for heavy models, with adaptive replica thresholds based on interconnect topology. Key Results: On a 4-GPU (V100) worker node, Torpor serves 480 concurrent inference functions while meeting millisecond-scale SLOs (98th-percentile latency within 80 ms for CV models, 200 ms for Bert-qa)—compared to Native execution which can serve only ~72 out of 160 functions within GPU memory, and INFless-KA (keep-alive variant) which serves 112 functions but achieves SLO compliance for only 7 due to cold-start overhead. At low request rates (10 r/m), Torpor achieves >10× throughput improvement over Native on V100 and up to 20× on A10 while keeping P98 latency below 50 ms. GPU activated time under Torpor stays above 78% even at the lowest tested rates, versus 4.52% for Native at 10 r/m. Torpor reduces GPU remoting latency by 88% for ResNet-152 and 37% for Bert-qa compared to GVirtuS. Pinned memory contributes ~35–49% latency reduction; pipelining adds 15–25%; parameter grouping adds up to 22% further. For LLMs, Torpor reduces Qwen-14B end-to-end latency by up to 88.5% (60.3 s → 6.9 s for 64 output tokens) versus cold start, within 3.1 s of warm-start performance. In cluster experiments with 6 workers, Torpor is the only system maintaining >80% SLO compliance at 1,000+ functions. The production pilot on Alibaba Cloud (>1k GPUs, >300 users, up to 465k daily requests) shows 70% average user cost reduction and 65% GPU provisioning cost reduction. Strengths: - Strong end-to-end evaluation combining controlled microbenchmarks, node-level ablations, cluster-scale experiments, and real production deployment at Alibaba Cloud—an unusually complete validation chain that directly ties system design to commercial impact. - The model-agnostic design constraint is taken seriously and enforced throughout: no operator-level profiling, no model structure inspection, applicable to CNNs, BERT, diffusion models, and LLMs with KV cache treated as model state. - The interference-aware scheduling framework is non-trivial and well-motivated: the heavy/light model categorization and RRC-based prioritization address a concrete problem (PCIe contention causing 48–61% latency degradation for large concurrent models) that prior SLO-aware systems ignore because they assume static model placement. Weaknesses / Limitations: - No support for model-parallel execution across multiple GPUs: very large models (e.g., 70B+ parameter LLMs) that cannot fit on a single GPU are explicitly out of scope, limiting applicability to the fastest-growing inference workload class. - The evaluation model suite is narrow (8 models, all well-known CNNs and one BERT variant for the main experiments); LLM experiments are limited to a single model (Qwen-14B) on a single GPU with no multi-tenant SLO experiments, making the LLM claims speculative for production-scale deployments. - The adaptive replica threshold is a tunable parameter with no principled auto-tuning procedure described; the paper shows it matters (t=1 vs t=2 for NVLink vs PCIe-only), but leaves the operator to configure it manually, which may be brittle in heterogeneous clusters with mixed interconnect topologies.
Search Results
#124 — Moderately Innovative (55)

" Here, Let Me Help": An Empirical Study of User Interventions in Human–Web Agent Collaboration

dl.acm.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The dual taxonomy separating intervention reasons from intervention types, and the quantitative mapping between them, is a genuine contribution that moves beyond outcome-centric evaluation of web agents. The ecologically valid setup with real websites and a production LLM agent strengthens the findings. However, the core methodology is a standard controlled user study with thematic coding, and the participant pool is narrow, making this solid but incremental work within the human-AI collaboration space.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM-powered web agents can execute complex browser-based tasks from natural language instructions, but fully autonomous execution remains error-prone in practice. Prior work has focused almost exclusively on outcome-centric metrics (task success rates, latency), leaving the process-level dynamics of human–agent collaboration—specifically why and how users step in during execution—largely uncharacterized. Without this process-level understanding, mixed-initiative system designs remain heuristic and fragmented. This paper fills that gap by providing the first systematic empirical account of user intervention behavior during live web agent execution. Methodology: The authors conducted a controlled in-lab study with 30 university-affiliated participants (ages 18–35), each completing 12 tasks across three domains (shopping, travel, information-seeking) on six real websites (Amazon, Coupang, Reddit, X, Korean Air, Booking.com). The web agent was implemented using the open-source `browser-use` Python library with GPT-4o as the backbone, running in a live local browser (Playwright/Chrome) to maximize ecological validity. The study interface provided real-time browser visualization alongside a natural language chat panel; participants could stop the agent at any time and were prompted to record their reason. Data collected included fine-grained interaction logs (mouse movements, hovers, clicks, keystrokes), screen recordings, stop-event reasons, post-task Likert ratings (effectiveness, ease, satisfaction, trust), and semi-structured exit interviews. Three authors performed axial coding on these data sources to construct a taxonomy of intervention reasons and types, achieving strong inter-rater reliability (κ = 0.85). Two-way ANOVA and Pearson correlation tests were applied to the quantitative measures. Key Results: Across 360 task executions, 44.2% completed without intervention, 11.4% failed without intervention, 29.7% succeeded only with intervention, and 14.7% failed despite intervention. A total of 309 explicit stop events were recorded. The taxonomy identifies four categories of intervention reasons: (1) Instruction–Action Misalignment (instruction misinterpretation, task decomposition failure, UI misinterpretation, content misinterpretation); (2) Incomplete Task Termination (halts, loops, unrecognized completion); (3) Perceived Delay; and (4) Exceptions (user stops, user errors). UI misinterpretation was the single most common trigger (127/315 reason occurrences, 53% of travel-domain interventions), driven primarily by complex calendar and booking widgets. Intervention types are divided into explicit (agent-directed: prompt repetition n=16, paraphrasing n=22, instruction restructuring n=61, adaptive modification n=18, contextual clarification n=5; interface-directed: direct substitution n=64, error correction n=46, guidance n=52, manual termination n=35) and implicit (tracking n=87, cross-checking n=6, forecasting n=37). A chi-square test confirms intervention type is non-independent of intervention reason (χ²(72)=169.96, p<.001, V=.26): UI misinterpretation predominantly triggers interface-directed actions while instruction misunderstanding triggers prompt-level repairs. All four subjective usability ratings (satisfaction, trust, ease, effectiveness) are strongly positively correlated (r=.73–.87) and all negatively correlated with intervention count and task time (r=−.37 to −.57, all p<.001). Two-way ANOVA shows task design is the dominant factor shaping subjective perceptions (η²p=.20–.28), while intervention count is jointly determined by task design (η²p=.45) and website (η²p=.40), with a strong interaction effect (η²p=.55). Strengths: - Ecologically valid experimental setup using a live local browser, real websites, and a production-quality LLM agent (GPT-4o via browser-use), avoiding the sandboxed or simulated environments common in prior work. - The dual taxonomy—separating intervention reasons from intervention types—and the quantitative mapping between them (Table 3) is a genuine conceptual contribution that goes beyond outcome metrics and provides actionable design guidance. - Rich mixed-methods data (interaction logs, screen recordings, stated reasons, Likert ratings, interviews) cross-validated by multiple coders, resulting in high inter-rater reliability and nuanced qualitative findings (e.g., distinguishing implicit tracking from forecasting). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The participant pool is heavily skewed toward young university students (ages 18–35, M=24.5), all novices to web agents; intervention patterns for experienced users or higher-stakes domains (finance, healthcare) are unknown and likely different, limiting generalizability. - Implicit interventions are operationalized coarsely as any mouse-movement event during agent execution, which conflates intentional monitoring with incidental cursor movement; the authors acknowledge this and note that eye-tracking or think-aloud protocols would yield finer-grained signal. - Only one agent architecture (GPT-4o + browser-use) is evaluated; it is unclear how findings would transfer to agents with different capability levels, interaction styles, or transparency mechanisms, and no comparative analysis across agents is performed.
Citations
#125 — Moderately Innovative (54)

QaRL: Rollout-Aligned Quantization-Aware RL for Fast and Stable Training under Training-- Inference Mismatch

Hao Gu, Hao Wang, Jiacheng Liu, Lujun Li, Qiyuan Zhu, Bei Liu, Binxing Xu, Lei Wang, Xintong Yang, Sida Lin, Sirui Han, Yike Guo · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: QaRL precisely identifies two distinct failure modes in quantized RL rollouts — training-inference mismatch and error-token instability — and proposes mechanistically targeted solutions for each. The hybrid forward-pass design and sequence-level TBPO are well-motivated engineering contributions. However, evaluation is limited to math reasoning on a single dataset, the 1.3× speedup is modest, and the core ideas (quantization-aware training via STE, modified clipping) are incremental extensions of established techniques.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM reinforcement learning pipelines are dominated by rollout generation cost, which accounts for roughly 70% of RL training time. A natural mitigation is to quantize the rollout model (e.g., W4A16 or W8A8) to accelerate decoding. However, this creates a severe training–inference mismatch: responses are sampled from a low-bit policy while policy gradient updates are computed against a full-precision learner. The mismatch weight w_mismatch = π_learner / π_sampler drifts far from 1.0, and the problem compounds with response length as quantization errors accumulate autoregressively. Standard importance-sampling corrections (TIS, MIS) partially address this but do not close the gap. An additional failure mode — not previously identified — is that quantized rollouts produce repetitive, garbled "error tokens" in long responses that are not reliably suppressed by standard PPO-style clipping. Methodology: The paper proposes QaRL (Quantization-Aware RL), a two-part framework. First, rollout-aligned quantization-aware training aligns the learner's forward pass with the quantized rollout: during the training forward pass, BF16 master weights are on-the-fly quantized and low-bit GEMM is executed directly (rather than simulated via fake-quant), precisely mirroring the arithmetic of the rollout engine. Gradients are computed via the Straight-Through Estimator and applied to the BF16 master weights. Materialized low-bit tensors are directly published to the inference engine after each step, avoiding redundant re-quantization. This hybrid approach (low-bit forward, full-precision backward) avoids the 4-bit gradient instability of fully quantized training. Second, Trust-Band Policy Optimization (TBPO) addresses error tokens. TBPO operates at the sequence level: it treats each entire response as a single action using geometric-mean token probabilities, and masks out entire responses whose sequence-level ratio exceeds trust-region bounds. For negative-advantage samples, TBPO applies dual-sided clipping (bounding both the lower and upper policy ratio) rather than the standard one-sided lower bound, preventing low-probability error tokens from producing extreme ratios that dominate gradient magnitude. The mismatch weight is symmetrically truncated with a two-sided cap. The combination of sequence-level masking and dual clipping correctly excludes garbled response sequences while preserving exploration. Key Results: Experiments use OpenR1-Math-46K on Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B/7B, Qwen3-8B-Base, and Qwen3-30B-A3B-Base (MoE), training on 8× H800 GPUs with Verl + vLLM. On Qwen3-8B, quantized rollout training drops in-distribution math average from 51.8% (BF16 GRPO) to 43.9%; QaRL TBPO recovers to 48.9% (-2.9 vs. BF16). On Qwen3-30B-A3B MoE, quantized rollout scores 45.7% vs. 52.1% for BF16 GSPO; QaRL TBPO achieves 51.2%, a +5.5 recovery over the quantized baseline. OOD benchmarks (ARC-Challenge, GPQA-Diamond, MMLU-Pro, LiveCodeBench) follow the same pattern: QaRL TBPO matches or nearly matches BF16 while substantially outperforming quantized rollout training. QaRL delivers 1.3× training speedup over BF16 on the 30B MoE (quantized rollout alone achieves 1.4× but with severe accuracy degradation). Ablations show RL convergence is largely insensitive to the specific quantization format (FP8W8A8, W4A16, W4A8 all yield similar final rewards under TBPO). TBPO under BF16 training is neutral — it performs on par with GRPO — confirming its benefit is specific to the quantized-error regime. Strengths: - Identifies and precisely diagnoses two distinct failure modes (training–inference mismatch and error-token instability) and proposes targeted, mechanistically motivated solutions for each. - The hybrid forward-pass design (low-bit GEMM with BF16 gradients via STE) achieves near-bitwise alignment without the 2× throughput penalty of enforcing strictly batch-invariant kernels, a practical engineering trade-off well-justified in the paper. - Sequence-level TBPO discriminates error tokens from genuine low-probability exploration tokens — a distinction that token-level clipping cannot make — resulting in stable training dynamics on notoriously unstable MoE architectures. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is restricted to math reasoning on a single 46K-problem dataset; generalization to other task domains (instruction following, code at training time, RLHF with human preferences) is not demonstrated. - The 1.3× speedup of QaRL is somewhat modest compared to quantized rollout alone (1.4×) because in-training quantization adds overhead; the paper does not explore batched or fused quantization to close this gap. - Sequence-level masking discards entire responses containing error tokens, reducing sample efficiency; the authors acknowledge this and suggest token-level approximations as future work but provide no concrete solution.
Search Results 2604.07853 PDF
#126 — Moderately Innovative (54)

RAG or Learning? Understanding the Limits of LLM Adaptation under Continuous Knowledge Drift in the Real World

Hanbing Liu, Lang Cao, Yang Li · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 54 for a well-structured benchmark that cleanly isolates temporal reasoning challenges (C1→C2→C3) and strong empirical results, particularly the +25 pp C3 gains demonstrating that explicit temporal structure in context outperforms flat retrieval for multi-hop temporal reasoning. The Event Evolution Graph is a reasonable engineering contribution, but the core ideas — time-aware retrieval, temporal decay, multi-view reasoning — are natural extensions of existing RAG paradigms rather than conceptual departures.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large language models are trained on fixed snapshots of the world and thus become misaligned with current facts as real-world knowledge continuously evolves — a phenomenon the authors term "continuous knowledge drift." Beyond simply producing outdated answers, this drift creates temporal inconsistency: models may fail to reason coherently about the ordering, dependencies, and state transitions of evolving facts even when provided with relevant evidence. Existing adaptation approaches (continual finetuning, knowledge editing, RAG) are rarely evaluated in settings that reflect chronological, real-world knowledge evolution; most benchmarks are static or test single-fact updates. This paper addresses that gap. Methodology: The paper makes two contributions. First, it introduces a benchmark of real-world dynamic events spanning January 2024–October 2025 across ten topical domains (corporate leadership, sports transfers, economic indicators, etc.), constructed from Wikipedia time-stamped data as knowledge quadruples (subject, relation, object, timestamp). Five QA task types are defined: Historical QA (pre-2024 facts), single-timestamp contemporary QA (C1), multi-timestamp QA requiring temporal aggregation (C2), multi-source QA requiring cross-entity reasoning (C3), and TruthfulQA-derived commonsense QA. The benchmark contains 513 knowledge quadruples, 111 historical questions, 513/218/59 C1/C2/C3 questions, and 817 commonsense questions. Second, the paper proposes Chronos, a training-free time-aware RAG framework. Chronos operates in four stages: (1) query analysis — extract entities and time window, rewrite a time-agnostic query; (2) time-aware retrieval — for each entity, retrieve candidate quadruples using dense embeddings, then re-rank via a combined score blending cosine similarity and exponential temporal decay (α=0.75, τ=180 days); (3) Event Evolution Graph (EEG) construction — progressively build a graph by chronologically ordering retrieved events (EEG-I), augmenting with LLM-reconstructed historical quadruples (EEG-F), and enriching with entity-centric linking of entity state chains (EEG-A); (4) multi-perspective inference — serialize the EEG into a temporal view (global timeline within the query window) and per-entity views, then condition the LLM on all views for final answer generation. Key Results: Evaluation spans GPT-4o, Claude-3-Haiku, and LLaMA-3.1-8B across direct generation, four parametric methods (ROME, MEMIT, WISE, LoRA FT), and two RAG baselines (vanilla RAG, ReAct RAG). Direct generation achieves only 11–15% on contemporary C1 questions across models. Vanilla RAG improves contemporary QA (84–87% C1) but collapses on historical questions (10–27%) and struggles on complex temporal reasoning (C2: 40–51%, C3: 17–25%). Parametric editing methods (ROME, MEMIT, WISE) suffer catastrophic forgetting, with historical accuracy dropping to 10–47%; LoRA FT improves C1 (71%) but harms historical and commonsense performance. Chronos consistently outperforms all baselines: with GPT-4o, it achieves 63.96% historical, 96.30% C1, 66.51% C2, 71.19% C3, 71.36% commonsense, and 73.86% overall — gains of +2.7, +2.9, +10.1, +25.4, +1.5, and +19.3 percentage points over the best prior baselines respectively. The C3 improvements are especially large (up to +28.8 pp for LLaMA), confirming Chronos's advantage in multi-source temporal reasoning. Ablations confirm all four components contribute: removing entity-view degrades C2 by 17 pp and C3 by 20 pp; removing temporal view costs 8–15 pp on contemporary tasks; history reconstruction is critical for historical QA (−57.7 pp without it on GPT-4o). Strengths: - The benchmark is well-designed with five task types of increasing complexity (C1→C2→C3), cleanly isolating different temporal reasoning challenges that existing static benchmarks miss. - Chronos achieves substantial gains especially on the hardest C2/C3 tasks without any parameter updates, demonstrating that explicit temporal structure in context outperforms implicit retrieval for multi-hop temporal reasoning. - The ablation study is thorough and each component's contribution is clearly quantified, making the design choices transparent and reproducible. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark is relatively small (513 quadruples, 59 C3 questions), and knowledge quadruples are sourced only from Wikipedia, which may not reflect the diversity or complexity of real industrial knowledge streams. - Chronos relies on the LLM itself (via prompting) for history reconstruction and event augmentation, introducing a risk of hallucinated quadruples being injected into the EEG — the paper acknowledges this causes interference on contemporary tasks but does not quantify the hallucination rate or propose mitigation. - The evaluation uses exact-match accuracy, which penalizes valid paraphrases and may understate true performance, particularly for C2 multi-entity answers. - The knowledge base is assumed to be pre-structured as clean quadruples; the paper does not address how Chronos would perform with raw unstructured documents, limiting applicability to real-world deployments where such structured knowledge bases are rarely available.
Recommended 2604.05096 PDF
#127 — Moderately Innovative (54)

Effect of Personalized AI-Driven Recommendations on Customer Retention in E-Commerce Platforms

jcrp.in · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The causal identification strategy combining propensity score matching with DiD on staggered rollouts across four platforms is methodologically ambitious and a genuine advance over correlational norms in this literature. However, the architecture-platform confounding fatally undermines the comparative claims, the pseudonymized platforms raise reproducibility concerns, and the implausibly large 34.7pp retention lift suggests residual confounders.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Despite the widespread deployment of AI recommendation systems in e-commerce, rigorous causal evidence on their effect on customer retention is scarce. Most prior work relies on observational, correlational designs that conflate selection bias (platforms investing in AI also invest broadly in customer experience) with true recommendation efficacy. A second gap is the lack of empirically grounded characterization of recommendation fatigue — the conditions under which over-exposure to recommendations actively harms retention. This paper targets both gaps using large-scale longitudinal data across four geographically diverse platforms. Methodology: The study uses 2.4 million anonymized customer transaction records spanning 36 months (Jan 2022–Dec 2024) from four platforms: FlipNest (India, NCF+BERT4Rec), CartBridge (SE Asia, Matrix Factorization+CBF hybrid), NovaShop (Germany, SASRec transformer), and RetailAxis (USA, RL+CF). The causal identification strategy is a two-stage framework: first, propensity score matching via XGBoost on covariates including account tenure, historical CLV tier, session frequency, category diversity, device type, promotion exposure, and support history — matching 1:1 within caliper 0.01, achieving all standardized mean differences below 0.05. Second, a Difference-in-Differences (DiD) estimator exploits the staggered rollout of AI recommendation features as a natural experiment, with customer and time fixed effects and standard errors clustered at the customer level. Parallel trends are validated via an event study. Heterogeneous treatment effects are estimated using Causal Forest (Wager & Athey, 2018), yielding conditional average treatment effects (CATEs) by income tier, tenure, and device type. Recommendation fatigue thresholds are identified via penalized spline regressions, with inflection points bootstrapped at 95% confidence. Key Results: The headline DiD estimate is a +34.7 percentage point (pp) improvement in 12-month retention rates (p < 0.001, 95% CI: [31.2, 38.2]), representing a 73.3% relative improvement over the 47.3% control baseline. Order frequency increases by 28.3% and CLV by 19.6%; a 6.8% reduction in average order value partially offsets CLV gains, consistent with AI surfacing lower-priced but highly relevant items. Transformer-based sequential models (SASRec, NovaShop) achieve the highest retention lift (+38.1 pp), followed by NCF+BERT4Rec (+36.2 pp), RL+CF (+34.7 pp), and legacy matrix factorization+CBF (+29.8 pp). Collaborative filtering architectures outperform content-based systems by 11.2 pp on repeat purchase probability. Causal Forest reveals strong heterogeneity: new customers (<3 months tenure) exhibit +48.3 pp retention CATE vs. +19.7 pp for long-tenure customers (>3 years); low-CLV customers show +38.6% CLV lift vs. +8.9% for high-CLV. Fatigue thresholds range from 17 impressions/month for high-income segments (with −31.4% post-threshold attenuation) to 38 for price-sensitive customers (−9.2%); the pooled average attenuation beyond threshold is −22.1%. Strengths: - Methodologically rigorous causal design combining propensity score matching and DiD with staggered rollout as natural experiment, with explicit parallel trends validation — a genuine advance over the correlational norm in this literature. - Scale and geographic breadth (2.4M customers, four markets, 36 months) substantially strengthens external validity relative to prior single-platform or lab-based studies. - Actionable, segment-specific fatigue thresholds derived from large-scale behavioral data fill a practical gap; prior threshold estimates came from small-N laboratory studies with limited generalizability. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Architecture comparison is fully confounded with platform: each platform runs a different recommendation system and serves a different national market, so the performance differences attributed to architecture (e.g., SASRec superiority) are indistinguishable from country-level or platform-level effects — no within-platform A/B comparison across architectures is presented. - The platforms are unnamed (FlipNest, CartBridge, etc. appear to be pseudonyms), making the data and claimed IRB approvals unverifiable; the paper reads as potentially synthetic or heavily disguised, which undermines reproducibility and independent validation. - The 34.7 pp absolute retention lift (a 73% relative improvement) is extraordinarily large by industry standards, and the paper does not adequately address whether residual time-varying confounders — concurrent UX changes, pricing algorithm updates, marketing campaigns — might inflate the DiD estimate despite platform fixed effects.
Search Results
#128 — Moderately Innovative (54)

Grammaticality as Kind: Ontology , Epistemology, and Empirical Pay-off

philarchive.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: The application of the Homeostatic Property-Cluster framework to grammaticality yields a genuinely useful objective/subjective distinction that unifies grammatical illusions, satiation effects, and inter-speaker variation under one explanatory roof. The integrative ambition — subsuming both generative and usage-based findings — is intellectually substantive, and the falsifiability conditions give it empirical traction beyond typical philosophical work. However, it introduces no new empirical data, the leap from construction-level to system-level HPC kind is under-argued, and the operationalization gap for 'community licensing' limits practical applicability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Grammaticality is one of linguistics' central theoretical constructs, yet its ontological status remains contested. Speakers consistently reject some strings (*I've finished it yesterday), accept structurally similar but semantically bizarre ones (Colorless green ideas sleep furiously), and trained linguists classify unprocessable multi-embedded sentences as grammatical. These divergences reveal that grammaticality cannot be reduced to rule-compliance, frequency, cognitive competence, or social convention alone. Neither essentialism — which requires necessary and sufficient conditions — nor pure conventionalism — which collapses grammaticality into whatever a community accepts — can jointly explain the stable clustering of grammatical properties, their causal maintenance across timescales, and their support for inductive generalization. The paper aims to provide a principled ontological account that resolves the gradience and variation observed in acceptability data while preserving grammaticality as a scientifically tractable kind. Methodology: The paper applies the Homeostatic Property-Cluster (HPC) framework (R. Boyd 1991; R. N. Boyd 1990, 1999) to grammaticality, arguing it satisfies all three HPC criteria: a co-occurring property cluster, identifiable homeostatic mechanisms, and systematic projectability. The property cluster for grammaticality comprises morphosyntactic form–meaning pairings, semantic compatibility between those pairings and utterance interpretation, and community entrenchment of licensed constructions. Five causal mechanisms maintain this cluster at distinct timescales: bias-guided acquisition (the now-or-never bottleneck filters unlicensed mappings during ontogeny); interactive alignment (real-time conversational repair prevents error propagation); memory consolidation (automated high-frequency patterns resist change over a lifetime); prestige-weighted selection (high-status variants diffuse faster across communities over decades); and iterated transmission (generational bottlenecks filter patterns incompatible with learnability or processing constraints). The paper also makes a system-level claim: grammaticality, as a property of the entire construction system, is itself an HPC kind — not merely a collection of individual construction-level HPC kinds — supported by cross-template generalization, super-additive penalty interactions, and operation invariance. Three contrast cases (processing overload, phonotactics, lexical semantics) demarcate the kind's boundaries by showing how the same five mechanisms operate under distinct constraint regimes. Key Results: The paper is a theoretical argument with no new empirical data; its contributions are conceptual and explanatory. The HPC framing yields a principled distinction between objective grammatical status (community licensing of a form–meaning pairing) and the subjective feeling of (un)grammaticality (which integrates licensing with processing costs, prescriptive norms, and social evaluation). This resolves two families of grammatical illusions: grammaticality illusions (center-embedded strings are licensed but feel unacceptable due to processing overload) and ungrammaticality illusions (agreement attraction errors like *The key to the cabinets are here pass detection because the parser is misled, yet remain unlicensed). The framework explains why satiation improves acceptability for rarity-driven marginality but not for categorical bans, why high-frequency errors never entrench, and why structural prohibitions (left-branch extraction) recur cross-linguistically. For typology, the framework predicts a bounded space of possible grammars: those incompatible with acquisition, processing, or transmission constraints cannot stabilize, which explains the systematic absence of certain logically possible grammatical systems in attested language inventories. The analysis is presented as falsifiable: if structure-based predictions failed across new construction types while construction-specific labels succeeded, or if phonetic manipulations repaired structural bans as effectively as structural ones, the system-level HPC account would be undermined. Strengths: - The objective/subjective distinction — licensing status versus felt acceptability — is the paper's strongest contribution; it provides a unified explanation for grammatical illusions, satiation effects, inter-speaker rating variability, and method-specific divergences without ad hoc stipulation. - The framework is genuinely integrative: it subsumes both generative findings (locality effects, island constraints, cross-linguistic convergence) and usage-based findings (frequency effects, S-curve diffusion, gradient acceptability) as complementary aspects of the same kind rather than competing theories. - The falsifiability conditions stated in §4.3 are specific and testable, giving the account more empirical bite than typical philosophical analyses; the paper correctly notes that none of the three disconfirming patterns appear in available data. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The critical move from Claim A (individual constructions are HPC kinds) to Claim B (grammaticality as a system property is an HPC kind) is asserted via a part-whole argument but not rigorously demonstrated; showing that system-level properties are not recoverable from any single construction description does not by itself establish that the system constitutes a unified HPC kind rather than a loosely confederated set. - The treatment of categorical structural bans (e.g., left-branch extraction) is explicitly agnostic about their source — whether they reflect autonomous syntactic constraints or emerge from interacting factors — which leaves the framework unable to adjudicate between generative and emergentist explanations for exactly the phenomena most in need of ontological clarity. - The criterion for objective grammatical status (community licensing) risks circularity: the paper distinguishes licensing from frequency and from acceptability judgments but provides no operationalization for measuring licensing independently of the very judgment and corpus evidence it says only indirectly reflect it, making the objective/subjective distinction difficult to apply empirically.
Search Results
#129 — Moderately Innovative (53)

Back to the Barn with LLAMAs: Evolving Pretrained LLM Backbones in Finetuning Vision Language Models

Sameera Horawalavithana, Lauren Phillips, Ian Stewart, Sai Munikoti, Karl Pazdernik · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: A useful empirical contribution showing that upgrading LLM backbones in VLMs does not uniformly improve performance—the negative instance-level correlations between LLAMA generations are a genuinely surprising finding. However, the work proposes no new methods and the scope is narrow (one architecture family, three small datasets).
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are typically built by combining a pretrained LLM backbone with a visual encoder. As better LLMs emerge (e.g., the LLAMA series), practitioners naturally assume upgrading the backbone will improve VLM performance. However, prior comparisons across VLMs (LLaVA, Qwen-VL, InternVL) confound the LLM backbone effect with simultaneous changes to vision encoders, training data, and hyperparameters. No controlled study isolates backbone evolution as the sole variable. This gap matters practically: it is unclear when upgrading an LLM backbone is worth the computational cost, and which downstream task types benefit versus suffer. Methodology: The authors conduct a controlled ablation using Meta's LLAMA-1 (7B), LLAMA-2 (7B), and LLAMA-3.1-Instruct (8B) as the sole variable. All other components are held fixed: the visual encoder is CLIP-ViT-Large-Patch14; the training data, two-stage SFT pipeline, and NVIDIA NeVA/NeMo codebase are identical across runs. Stage 1 alignment pretraining uses SciCap (scientific figure captioning); Stage 2 SFT is applied to three evaluation domains: ScienceQA (memorization-heavy science multiple-choice), VQA-Scene (visually-grounded binary/open VQA with controlled language priors), and a Seismic dataset (geological image QA with both text and coordinate prediction). Beyond aggregate accuracy, the authors examine instance-level Pearson correlations across LLAMA generations, token-level confidence (via log-probabilities), and layer-wise context vector magnitudes and cosine similarities across all 32 decoder layers. Key Results: Aggregate accuracy shows no consistent improvement with newer LLMs: ScienceQA drops from 71.8% (LLAMA-1) to 68.4% (LLAMA-3); VQA-Scene improves from 61.8% to 65.6%; Seismic text accuracy fluctuates without trend (78.5% → 74.0% → 77.8%). More striking is the instance-level analysis: LLAMA-1 and LLAMA-2 are nearly interchangeable on VQA-Scene (r = 0.907), but LLAMA-3 is negatively correlated with both (r = −0.266 and −0.209), indicating it solves a largely different subset of questions rather than a superset. On the Seismic coordinate prediction task, LLAMA-1 and LLAMA-2 score exactly 0.0% while LLAMA-3 scores 76.5%, revealing a capability that emerges discontinuously. Confidence analysis shows LLAMA-1/2 assign near-certain probability (1.0) to every answer regardless of correctness, while LLAMA-3 is better calibrated (mean 0.73) and more accurate. Layer-wise analysis shows LLAMA-1/2 exhibit large-magnitude activations with sharp, often negative cosine similarities in late layers (suggesting unstable representational reorganization), while LLAMA-3 has smaller, more stable activations with no negative layer transitions, consistent with its different instance-level behavior. Strengths: - Genuinely controlled experimental design: fixing the vision encoder, training data, codebase, and post-training algorithm across three backbone generations isolates the LLM contribution far more rigorously than any prior comparison study. - Instance-level correlation analysis is the key methodological contribution — it reveals that aggregate accuracy can mask complete behavioral shifts (negative correlation on VQA-Scene), a finding that would be invisible under standard benchmark reporting. - The mechanistic analysis (confidence calibration + layer-wise activation profiles) provides interpretable, internally consistent explanations for the behavioral differences rather than stopping at performance numbers. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The study is limited to a single, aging architecture family (LLAMA 1/2/3) at 7–8B parameters; it is unclear whether findings generalize to other backbone families (Mistral, Qwen, Gemma) or significantly different scales. - The fixed CLIP-ViT-Large-Patch14 vision encoder is a known bottleneck for domain-specific tasks (e.g., seismic geology). The design choice that isolates the LLM also prevents understanding the interaction between LLM quality and vision encoder quality, which is practically important. - The three evaluation datasets are small and domain-specific (ScienceQA is memorization-heavy by the authors' own admission; Seismic is a custom expert-annotated dataset of unstated size). The findings may not transfer to standard VLM benchmarks like MMBench, MMMU, or TextVQA, limiting generalizability of the conclusions.
Recommended 2604.10985 PDF
#130 — Moderately Innovative (53)

Safe-Unsafe Concept Separation Emerges from a Single Direction in Language Models Activation Space

aclanthology.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: The paper provides a clean empirical demonstration that safety-relevant concepts are linearly separable in activation space and validates this causally via directional ablation, but the core techniques — Concept Activation Vectors, linear probes, and activation steering — are well-established in the mechanistic interpretability literature. The contribution is primarily a systematic application and thorough benchmarking rather than a methodological departure from prior work.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Ensuring LLM safety is critical, yet dominant approaches—RLHF, supervised fine-tuning, and external post-hoc guardrails—are either resource-intensive, require repeated retraining, or degrade under distribution shift and adversarial inputs. The paper asks whether safety-relevant concepts are geometrically structured within a frozen model's activation space, and whether that structure can be exploited without modifying model weights. Methodology: The authors propose a five-stage pipeline centered on Concept Activation Vectors (CAVs) and lightweight linear probes. For a frozen LLM with L layers, they extract hidden activations for a small labeled set of safe/unsafe prompts (500 examples, balanced). At each layer, Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) is used to compute a CAV—the direction maximizing between-class scatter relative to within-class scatter. The layer whose CAV yields the highest AUROC on a held-out validation set (100 examples) is designated the "GuardLayer." A logistic regression or SVM probe is then trained solely on GuardLayer activations for inference-time classification. To move beyond correlation, the authors validate causality via Directional Ablation (Activation Steering): the probe weight vector is used to subtract the unsafe direction from activations of correctly-classified unsafe inputs, and Flip Rate (fraction shifting to safe) is measured as intervention strength α increases. The method is evaluated on 4 benchmarks (PolyGuard with 100K+ multi-domain prompts, ToxiGen with 250K implicit hate speech examples, a 5K Prompt Injection benchmark, and PolyGuardPrompts covering 16 non-English languages) across 8 open-source models (4 encoders, 4 decoders ranging from 86M to 3B parameters). Key Results: On PolyGuard structured domains (Finance, Law, HR), decoder models (LLaMA3.2-1B/3B, Qwen2-1.5B) achieve F1 > 0.97–0.99 with both LR and SVM probes, surpassing all guardrail baselines including Granite Guardian (5B) which peaks around F1=0.88. On Social Media—the hardest policy domain—decoders reach F1≈0.80–0.83 vs. Granite Guardian's 0.70–0.72. On Prompt Injection, the method achieves F1≈0.87–0.88 (LLaMA3B) vs. Prompt-Guard-2 (86M, task-specialized) at 0.68. On ToxiGen (adversarial implicit toxicity), F1 plateaus at 0.74–0.75 for best decoders, still outperforming Llama-Guard-3 (1B) at 0.67 and Granite Guardian at 0.19. Multilingual evaluation shows F1 > 0.80 for high-resource languages (Chinese, German, Spanish, Japanese) with LLaMA3B, and 0.63–0.76 for mid/low-resource languages (Russian, Hindi, Korean). Causal validation via activation steering produces Flip Rates approaching 1.0 at α < 4, while a random-direction baseline remains near zero. Computationally, the full pipeline (layer selection + probe training + inference on 250K ToxiGen examples) takes ~300s for BERT-base and ~970s for LLaMA3.2-3B, versus 13,000–25,000s for large guardrail decoders. Strengths: - Principled mechanistic framing: using LDA-derived CAVs to identify the GuardLayer avoids exhaustive grid search over the full test set, yet consistently lands within the optimal AUROC plateau (within 0.015 of global maximum across all tested settings). - Causal not merely correlational: directional ablation experiments provide strong evidence that the identified linear direction actively mediates safety decisions, not just correlates with labels. - Remarkably data-efficient: only 500 labeled examples (250 per class) are needed to train effective probes, with competitive performance degrading gracefully even at 100 examples per class. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Toxicity performance ceiling is low (F1≈0.70–0.75) and the paper acknowledges that implicit, contextual forms of harm partially escape a single linear direction—the core assumption of the method breaks down precisely where safety matters most. - All benchmarks consist of static single-turn prompts; multi-turn adversarial interactions, context drift, and jailbreaks spanning multiple turns are not evaluated, leaving robustness in realistic deployment scenarios uncharacterized. - The method requires a separate CAV and probe per domain and per language, making it a collection of specialized classifiers rather than a unified model; the operational cost of maintaining dozens of probes at scale is not discussed.
Recommended
#131 — Moderately Innovative (53)

Towards Efficient Symmetric Sparse Matrix-Vector Multiplication on Multi-Cores

dl.acm.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: DCH-opt is a well-executed extension of the authors' prior SC24 work, adding conflict-pruning and adaptive triangular selection — both lightweight and theoretically motivated. The evaluation across 298 matrices and 4 architectures is commendably thorough, but the core divide-and-conquer framework and ML-based parameter prediction were established in the conference version, making this primarily an incremental refinement.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Sparse Matrix-Vector Multiplication (SpMV) is a foundational kernel in HPC and deep learning. Nearly half the matrices in the SuiteSparse collection (1420 of 2893) are symmetric, meaning only the triangular half need be stored — cutting memory footprint and memory traffic roughly in half, which is significant for this memory-bound operation. However, symmetric SpMV introduces write-after-write data conflicts when multiple threads simultaneously update the same output vector elements. Prior approaches — atomic/lock operations (prohibitive synchronization overhead), reduction-based methods (thread-private vectors whose memory cost and reduction work scale linearly with thread count, limiting scalability), and graph-coloring methods (frequent global synchronizations, poor data locality, load imbalance, NP-hard coloring) — all fail to scale efficiently on large, high-bandwidth irregular matrices. Methodology: The paper extends DCS-SpMV (previously published at SC24) with two new preprocessing optimizations, yielding DCH-opt. The core idea is a Divide-and-Conquer (DC) framework that constructs a conflict graph from the symmetric matrix (each vertex = a row, each edge = a write conflict), then recursively partitions it via METIS weighted K-way graph partitioning into independent subgraphs separated by a special separator subgraph. Each recursion level's independent subgraphs can execute symmetric SpMV in parallel without conflicts. A Cuthill-McKee reordering within each subgraph/separator improves data locality. The DC process naturally segregates the matrix into a low-conflict part (processed with symmetric SpMV exploiting the storage reduction) and a high-conflict remainder (the final separator, processed with standard CSR SpMV ignoring symmetry) — this is the DCH-SpMV hybrid. The critical parameter K_hyb (number of DC recursions before switching to standard SpMV) is predicted by a trained 3-layer MLP using 12 matrix and conflict-graph features, achieving ~91–93% accuracy with under 1.3% average performance loss from mispredictions. The two new optimizations introduced in this extension are: (1) Conflict-pruning: rows/columns whose nonzero count exceeds m times the per-row average are decoupled from the conflict graph before DC preprocessing. Dense columns generate O(n²) conflict edges, so removing a handful of them can make previously intractable graphs feasible and enable the hybrid approach on matrices where K_hyb=0 was forced. The threshold m is chosen from {0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10}. (2) Adaptive triangular-part selection: rather than always using the lower triangular, the approach estimates total conflict edges for both triangular choices as Σ(NNZ_i)², selects whichever yields fewer edges, and uses that to construct the conflict graph. Column-heavy vs. row-heavy nonzero distributions affect conflict counts asymmetrically (a column with n nonzeros contributes O(n²) edges vs. O(n) for a row), so this heuristic can dramatically reduce conflict density. Key Results: Evaluated on 298 symmetric matrices from SuiteSparse (N_r > 50,000; NNZ > 100,000) across four CPUs: Intel Xeon Gold 6258R (28-core, 82 GB/s DDR4), Intel Xeon Max 9468 (48-core, 585 GB/s HBM), Huawei Kunpeng 920 (32-core ARM, 68 GB/s), and AMD EPYC 7742 (64-core, 77 GB/s). DCH-opt over DCH-ori: average speedups of 1.19×/1.13×/1.09×/1.13× and maximum of 4.89×/2.20×/2.78×/1.97× on the four platforms. DCH-opt over CSR (standard SpMV): average 4.22×/1.54×/1.52×/1.62× across platforms (DCH-opt always outperforms CSR, 0% regressions). DCH-opt over Intel oneMKL symmetric SpMV: 1.72× average on Xeon Gold 6258R, 1.23× on Xeon Max 9468. DCH-opt over RACE (state-of-the-art coloring): average 4.01×/3.60×/6.37×/3.62× across platforms, with maximum speedups reaching 21–75× on high-conflict matrices where coloring degrades catastrophically. The new optimizations also reduce DC preprocessing cost by 8–63% by shrinking the conflict graph. On 17 representative matrices, DCH-opt achieves best performance on 14–16 of 17 matrices depending on platform. The ML model achieves 91.6% accuracy on Xeon Gold 6258R (55/60 matrices correct) with 1.24% average performance loss from mispredictions, and 93.3% accuracy on Kunpeng 920 with 0.49% loss. Strengths: - The conflict-pruning and adaptive triangular selection are lightweight preprocessing steps with clear theoretical motivation (O(n²) vs O(n) edge generation) and strong empirical payoff — up to 4.89× improvement over the prior conference version on individual matrices. - Comprehensive evaluation: 298 matrices across 4 architectures (x86 DDR4, x86 HBM, ARM, AMD), with comparison to the vendor-tuned Intel oneMKL library, RACE, and standard SpMV — a rigorous bar. - The ML model for K_hyb prediction is well-designed: offline training, ONNX deployment, inference cost under 1/30th of one SpMV, and near-zero performance loss from mispredictions make it practical. Weaknesses / Limitations: - GPU support is absent; the work is entirely focused on multi-core CPUs. The companion DCSolver paper addresses GPUs separately, but symmetric SpMV on GPUs is an important open problem not addressed here. - The conflict-pruning threshold m is an additional hyperparameter chosen from a discrete set {0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10} without automated tuning — unlike K_hyb which is ML-predicted, m requires either a sweep or a heuristic choice, and the paper does not provide a principled selection method. - Performance degrades relative to standard CSR on a non-trivial fraction of matrices on some platforms (e.g., 29.5% of matrices on Xeon Max 9468 for MKL-SYM comparison, 27.2% for RACE), and on the ARM platform the CSR format choice limits competitiveness since SELL-C-σ or ELLPACK are better suited to ARM — acknowledged but deferred as future work.
Citations
#132 — Moderately Innovative (52)

Mitigating Shortcut Reasoning in Language Models: A Gradient-Aware Training Approach

Hongyu Cao, Kunpeng Liu, Dongjie Wang, Yanjie Fu · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 52 because the gradient-level formulation for detecting and correcting shortcut reasoning is principled, and the super-additive synergy between reweighting and gradient surgery is a noteworthy finding. However, evaluation is entirely on 3.2M-parameter toy models with synthetic shortcuts, making the practical relevance to real LLM training speculative. The ShortcutScore detection F1 of 0.64 underperforming simpler filtering (0.69) also undermines the core contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Large language models frequently exploit reasoning shortcuts—surface pattern matching, answer memorization, keyword-answer correlations—rather than performing genuine logical inference. This stems from training signal misalignment: shortcut-promoting samples efficiently reduce training loss (and are often correctly labeled), making them invisible to conventional data quality or label-noise filters. Existing mitigations (Chain-of-Thought training, Self-Consistency decoding, data filtering, RLHF) fail because none of them modify training dynamics at the gradient level to suppress shortcut learning. The gap is a principled method that both detects shortcut-promoting samples from gradient behavior and corrects their influence during training without discarding them. Methodology: The paper proposes Shortcut-Aware Reasoning Training (SART), a two-component gradient-centric framework. The core observation is that shortcut samples produce two distinct gradient signatures: (1) low cosine alignment with the validation gradient gV (the gradient does not transfer to genuine reasoning), and (2) high answer-token gradient concentration (gradient energy dominated by final answer tokens rather than intermediate reasoning tokens). These two signals are combined into a per-sample ShortcutScore S(s) = α·max(0, τA − A(s)) + β·max(0, R(s) − τR), where A(s) is cosine similarity with gV and R(s) is the ratio of answer-token to total gradient norms. High-ShortcutScore samples are down-weighted exponentially (w(s) = exp(−λS(s))) in the training objective. Beyond reweighting, SART applies gradient surgery: for misaligned samples, the gradient component along gV is orthogonally projected out; for answer-concentrated samples, the answer-token gradient contribution is suppressed by coefficient ρ. The optimal surgery direction is found via a minimax formulation where the inner problem maximizes harm along a constrained perturbation subspace M and the outer problem minimizes loss while avoiding that direction. Validation gradients are recomputed every k=5 steps. The framework is model-agnostic and requires no architectural changes or group/environment annotations. Key Results: Experiments use three synthetic benchmarks (Math-Arithmetic, Financial-Analysis, Causal-Reasoning) with controlled shortcut injection on a 3.2M-parameter GPT-style transformer. SART achieves 92.5% accuracy and 87.9% robustness (perturbed-set accuracy), versus the best baseline Influence Filtering at 76.0%/47.7%—gains of +16.5 pp accuracy and +40.2 pp robustness. Against vanilla fine-tuning, improvements are +23.7 pp accuracy and +68.5 pp robustness. On Math-Arithmetic, SART reaches near-perfect 98.0%/95.8%. The ablation reveals super-additive synergy: reweighting alone gives +20.7 pp robustness and surgery alone gives +21.1 pp, but together they yield +68.5 pp—a 64% synergy bonus. ShortcutScore achieves detection F1 of 0.64 (vs. Data Filtering's 0.69), but SART's detect-and-correct paradigm outperforms data-removal approaches by +17.8 pp accuracy. Computational overhead is approximately 2.5× over standard fine-tuning. Strengths: - Principled gradient-level formulation that addresses the root cause (training dynamics) rather than symptoms; the ShortcutScore provides a theoretically motivated, annotation-free detector. - The detect-and-correct paradigm preserves training data while neutralizing shortcuts, avoiding the data discarding and cross-task instability that plague filtering-based methods. - Super-additive synergy between reweighting and gradient surgery is well-characterized by ablation, with clear mechanistic explanation (complementary axes of magnitude vs. direction correction). Weaknesses / Limitations: - Experiments are entirely on small synthetic datasets with artificially injected, known shortcuts on a 3.2M-parameter toy model—far removed from real LLM pretraining or fine-tuning at scale; generalization to billion-parameter models on natural reasoning benchmarks (GSM8K, MATH, BIG-Bench) is undemonstrated. - Per-sample gradient computation for ShortcutScore is expensive at scale (2.5× overhead on tiny models), and the paper does not address how this scales to large models where full-gradient computation per sample is infeasible without approximations like LoRA gradients or gradient checkpointing. - The ShortcutScore detection F1 of 0.64 is actually lower than the simpler Data Filtering baseline (0.69), and the correlation with true shortcut rate is moderate (Pearson r=0.67), suggesting the signal may be noisy in practice; the paper relies on the correction mechanism to compensate for imperfect detection rather than improving detection itself.
Recommended 2603.20899 PDF
#133 — Moderately Innovative (52)

TRACE: An Experiential Framework for Coherent Multi-hop Knowledge Graph Question Answering

Yingxu Wang, Jiaxin Huang, Mengzhu Wang, Nan Yin · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: TRACE presents a well-engineered combination of narrative context conditioning and exploration priors for multi-hop KGQA, achieving solid improvements on standard benchmarks. However, both components are fundamentally LLM prompting strategies over an existing beam-search paradigm, and the evaluation is confined to two Freebase benchmarks with heavy reliance on GPT-4.1, making the contribution more of a careful systems integration than a conceptual advance.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Multi-hop Knowledge Graph Question Answering (KGQA) requires chaining relational reasoning steps across a structured KG to answer natural language questions. Existing LLM-based methods treat each reasoning step in isolation — relation selection at step t ignores the semantic narrative accumulated over steps 1..t-1 — and they discard information from prior failed or successful explorations, causing redundant search and logical drift away from the question intent. The paper targets two concrete gaps: (1) lack of contextual coherence across hops, and (2) inability to accumulate and reuse experiential knowledge from past traversals within or across queries. Methodology: TRACE (Trajectory-aware Reasoning with Adaptive Context and Exploration priors) is a three-component framework built on top of an LLM backbone (GPT-4.1 in the primary setting). (1) Dynamic Context Generation: at each hop t, the partial relation sequence R_q^(t) is passed through an LLM prompt that converts it to a natural-language contextual narrative C_q^(t) (e.g., "find the director's birthplace"). This narrative, rather than the raw relation list, conditions both candidate retrieval and re-ranking, preserving semantic continuity. (2) Exploration Generalization: when a reasoning trajectory terminates (depth limit or dead end), an LLM summarizes the explored path into a trajectory description D_q capturing what was tried and why it failed. A second LLM call periodically consolidates a batch of such summaries into abstract, reusable exploration priors F (e.g., "family-relation paths are unproductive for location queries"). These priors persist across trajectories during inference. (3) Dual-Feedback Re-ranking: at each hop, a retriever first selects top-k candidate relations conditioned on C_q^(t); each candidate is then scored by a separate LLM call conditioned on the full relation sequence R_q^(t) and the global priors F. Any candidate exceeding confidence threshold ζ=0.5 spawns a new branch, implementing a beam-like search. The overall process follows a beam search with max iterations I=30 and max path length L=4 hops. Key Results: On WebQSP, TRACE achieves 91.6% Hits@1 and 81.7% F1, compared to the prior best DP at 87.5%/81.4% and RwT at 87.0%/79.7% — roughly a 4-point absolute gain on Hits@1. On CWQ, TRACE achieves 76.9% Hits@1 and 72.9% F1 versus DP's 75.8%/69.4%, a 1.1-point and 3.5-point improvement respectively. Ablation confirms both Dynamic Context Generation and Exploration Generalization each contribute independently (removing either causes 2-3 point drops), with the largest drop when both are removed simultaneously. TRACE also uses fewer LLM tokens (8,782 vs. 10,680 for RwT on WebQSP) and fewer LLM calls (14.2 vs. 15.1), attributable to the priors suppressing redundant branches. With weaker backbone Llama2-13B, TRACE (88.1% Hits@1) still surpasses RwT and FiDeLis. Worst-case token usage is only ~1.5–1.7× average, demonstrating bounded computational growth. Strengths: - The dual-feedback design elegantly separates local semantic coherence (narrative conditioning) from global experiential guidance (exploration priors), and shows empirically that both signals are complementary rather than redundant. - Unlike RL-based approaches that require training with sparse rewards, TRACE is fully inference-time and training-free, relying entirely on LLM prompting — making it portable to any capable LLM backbone. - The efficiency analysis is thorough: the paper reports not just average but also worst-case and failure-case token/call statistics, credibly demonstrating that the exploration prior mechanism prevents exponential cost blow-up. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is limited to two Freebase-based benchmarks (WebQSP and CWQ), both of which use localized subgraph preprocessing. Generalization to open-world or noisy KGs (e.g., Wikidata, domain-specific graphs) is undemonstrated and acknowledged as a limitation. - The exploration priors are generated via manually designed prompts and are question-scoped within a single inference run; it is unclear whether priors accumulate across questions in a test set or are reset per question, and there is no mechanism to learn persistent cross-question strategies automatically. - Heavy reliance on a proprietary LLM (GPT-4.1) for all components makes reproducibility and cost analysis difficult to assess independently; the open-source variants (Llama2-13B, Qwen3-14B) show a 3-4 point gap, raising questions about practical viability without frontier models.
Citations 2604.11193 PDF
#134 — Moderately Innovative (52)

CocoaBench: Evaluating Unified Digital Agents in the Wild (29 ▲ )

CocoaBench Team, Shibo Hao, Zhining Zhang, Zhiqi Liang, Tianyang Liu, Yuheng Zha, Qiyue Gao, Jixuan Chen, Zilong Wang, Zhoujun Cheng, Haoxiang Zhang, Junli Wang, Hexi Jin, Boyuan Zheng, Kun Zhou, Yu Wang, Feng Yao, Licheng Liu, Yijiang Li, Zhifei Li, Zhengtao Han, Pracha Promthaw, Tommaso Cerruti, Xiaohan Fu, Ziqiao Ma, Jingbo Shang, Lianhui Qin, Julian McAuley, Eric P. Xing, Zhengzhong Liu, Rupesh Kumar Srivastava, Zhiting Hu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 14 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 52 as a competent compositional benchmark that fills a real gap — no prior work stress-tests unified multi-capability agency — but the contribution is primarily one of integration rather than methodological novelty. The infrastructure-agnostic design and outcome-based evaluation are well-executed engineering choices. The finding that coding-oriented scaffolds generalize beyond their domain is interesting but not deeply surprising given that code execution subsumes many web and file operations.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM-based agents have matured to the point where unified systems aim to integrate coding, web search, and visual GUI interaction into a single framework. However, existing benchmarks evaluate these capabilities in isolation — SWE-bench targets software engineering, OSWorld targets computer use, BrowseComp targets web research — leaving no rigorous evaluation of agents that must compose all three capabilities adaptively on realistic, open-ended tasks. COCOABENCH addresses this gap by providing a benchmark specifically designed to stress-test compositional, multi-modal digital agency. Methodology: COCOABENCH consists of 153 human-authored tasks spanning 9 domains (Business, Culture, Education, Life, Logic & Puzzles, Science, Sports, Technology, Travel), where 98% of tasks require multiple capabilities. Each task is specified minimally by a natural language instruction and a deterministic evaluation function over the final output, making the benchmark infrastructure-agnostic — compatible with sandboxed cloud agents, locally deployed frameworks, and coding-oriented scaffolds alike. For action-centric tasks where direct verification is impractical, proxy outcome evaluators are used (e.g., verifying a computed price rather than the full interaction trace). The authors also introduce COCOA-AGENT, a ReAct-based shared scaffold built atop an AIO Sandbox (Docker container with integrated browser, shell, and filesystem), exposing 39 strongly typed tools across vision, search, and coding categories. Experiments compare (1) existing end-to-end agent products (ChatGPT Agent, OpenClaw, Codex, Claude Code, OpenAI Deep Research) and (2) backbone models (GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini-3.1-pro, Gemini-Flash-3.0, Kimi-k2.5, Qwen3.5-397B-A13B) under the shared COCOA-AGENT scaffold. Failures across 712 trajectories are taxonomized into three hierarchical error types — Reasoning & Planning (E1), Tool & Execution (E2), and Visual Grounding (E3) — annotated via LLM-as-judge (Claude Sonnet 4.6). Key Results: The best-performing system is GPT-5.4 under Codex at 45.1% success rate. Claude Sonnet 4.6 under OpenClaw reaches 34.0% but drops to 25.5% under Claude Code and 15.7% under COCOA-AGENT, indicating notable scaffold sensitivity. Open-source models lag far behind: Kimi-k2.5 achieves 11.8% and Qwen3.5-397B-A13B only 9.8%. Scaffold design matters substantially — coding-oriented scaffolds (Codex, Claude Code) generalize surprisingly well beyond their original domain. Tool usage analysis reveals a strong correlation between code execution proportion and performance: GPT-5.4 allocates 64% of tool calls to coding and achieves 36.6% SR under COCOA-AGENT, whereas Kimi-k2.5 allocates only 26.4% to coding and achieves 11.8% SR. Across 712 failure trajectories, Reasoning & Planning errors dominate at 54%, with Visual Grounding at 29% and Tool & Execution at 17%. The dominant subcategory is Incorrect Reasoning (E1.1, 25%), followed by Imprecision (E1.2, 15.5%) and Visual Detail errors (E3.1, 15.8%). Codex costs $0.75/task vs. $2.31 for COCOA-AGENT with GPT-5.4, confirming that neither higher cost nor longer wall-clock time reliably predicts better performance. Strengths: - Infrastructure-agnostic design (instruction + evaluation function only) allows any agent system to be evaluated, avoiding the artificial constraints of VM-coupled or fixed-tool benchmarks. - Rigorous automatic evaluation without LLM judges for most tasks, using outcome-based proxy evaluators that preserve open-ended workflows while enabling reproducibility. - The error taxonomy is well-constructed with concrete, illustrative failure cases (e.g., floating-point accumulation, context truncation loops, DOM opacity on Tableau dashboards) that provide actionable diagnostic signal rather than just accuracy numbers. Weaknesses / Limitations: - At 153 tasks, the benchmark is relatively small, making per-domain and per-capability statistics potentially noisy and limiting statistical power for fine-grained comparisons. - The reliance on externally hosted task resources (17 custom websites, real receipts, W&B logs) introduces fragility: link rot or content changes could silently invalidate tasks over time, despite the authors' mitigation efforts. - COCOA-AGENT is demonstrably not the strongest scaffold (15.7% for Claude Sonnet 4.6 vs. 34.0% under OpenClaw), which complicates its use as a neutral comparison vehicle — scaffold quality differences may conflate model capability differences in the controlled comparison.
Recommended 2604.11201 PDF
#135 — Moderately Innovative (52)

Sema Code: Decoupling AI Coding Agents into Programmable, Embeddable Infrastructure (18 ▲ )

Huacan Wang, Jie Zhou, Ningyan Zhu, Shuo Zhang, Feiyu Chen, Jiarou Wu, Ge Chen, Chen Liu, Wangyi Chen, Xiaofeng Mou, Yi Xu · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026
Why this rank: The embeddable-engine-versus-application framing is a sound architectural contribution, and the eight mechanisms address real engineering problems in multi-tenant AI agent deployment. However, the core insight — separating a reusable engine from delivery-specific UI — is well-established software engineering practice (SQLite, LSP) applied to a new domain rather than a fundamentally new approach. The complete absence of quantitative evaluation limits the strength of the contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Every mainstream AI coding assistant (Claude Code, Cursor, GitHub Copilot) tightly couples its reasoning engine to a specific delivery form—CLI, IDE plugin, or web application. This coupling prevents enterprises from embedding these capabilities into backend services, replacing underlying models for private deployment, or serving multiple channels (IDE, web, messaging bots) from a single intelligent engine. The gap is between research frameworks (OpenHands, MetaGPT, AutoGen) that lack production-grade engineering, and polished products that lock capabilities to their delivery form. Sema Code targets this gap by treating the AI coding agent as an embeddable engine rather than an application. Methodology: Sema Code introduces a three-layer separation architecture: a UI-free core engine (Sema Core) packaged as a standalone npm library, a client layer that subscribes to an event stream, and a service layer for ecosystem integrations (MCP, Skills, Plugins). The core engine is event-driven rather than RPC-based, since agentic tasks emit heterogeneous, interleaved outputs (text fragments, tool results, permission requests). Cross-language access is provided via WebSocket and gRPC. Eight mechanisms address the engineering challenges of exposing this engine programmatically: (1) Multi-tenant isolation via Node.js AsyncLocalStorage (ALS), binding each engine instance to a dedicated resource bundle without OS-process overhead. (2) Hierarchical state partitioning into agent-local state (conversation history, Todos, file timestamps, execution status) and session-global state (shared abort controller, edit permissions). (3) FIFO input queuing with semantic batching—merging non-command text messages into single prompts while serializing slash commands—plus safe session reconstruction that stages task switches through an abort-then-purge protocol to prevent state leakage. (4) Adaptive context compression triggered at 75% of the context window limit, using O(1) token tracking via cumulative API metadata; the primary path invokes LLM summarization to preserve critical entities (file paths, function signatures) while discarding verbose tool outputs; a deterministic truncation fallback aligns cuts to assistant-message boundaries to satisfy role-alternation constraints. (5) Multi-agent collaborative scheduling with one-level delegation—sub-agents own isolated state spaces and share only the abort controller with the parent—avoiding the rigid role schemas of MetaGPT/ChatDev while bounding call depth. (6) Interrupt propagation across four execution phases (post-inference dispatch, pre-execution verification, active monitoring, recursion termination) with read-write-aware tool scheduling: pure-read batches run in parallel; any write serializes the entire batch. (7) Deterministic Todo-based process management using ID-matched state updates to eliminate UI flicker caused by LLM rephrasing. (8) Background task execution with proactive spawning and reactive takeover—timed-out shell processes are dynamically detached and handed to a background manager rather than terminated, preserving output continuity. Security uses a four-layer permission decision function covering file edits, shell commands, Skills, and MCP calls, with shell commands subject to deterministic whitelist checking augmented by LLM-assisted injection detection for obfuscated payloads. Key Results: The paper validates the architecture through two production deployments sharing identical Sema Core engine code with zero modifications: a VSCode extension (single-user, in-process mode exercising context compression and background tasks) and SemaClaw, a multi-channel messaging gateway for Telegram and Feishu (multi-tenant mode exercising isolation and input queuing). Together they cover all eight mechanisms without feature flags or conditional paths. No quantitative benchmarks (SWE-bench, task completion rates, latency figures, token savings from compression) are reported. The primary claim—delivery-agnostic reuse—is demonstrated qualitatively: all behavioral differences between the two product forms are realized entirely at the client layer. Strengths: - The core architectural insight is well-motivated and the analogy to SQLite/LSP is apt: treating an AI coding agent as an embeddable engine rather than a monolithic application is a meaningful contribution to the infrastructure layer of the AI coding ecosystem. - The eight mechanisms are coherent and address real engineering problems (state leakage, context exhaustion, blocking shell operations, prompt-injection attacks) that arise specifically in multi-tenant, multi-form-factor deployments—problems underserved by existing research frameworks. - The adaptive context compression design is technically sound: O(1) token tracking via cumulative API metadata avoids per-turn recomputation, the 75% threshold preserves headroom for tool calls, and the deterministic truncation fallback correctly respects role-alternation constraints. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper contains no quantitative evaluation whatsoever: no SWE-bench scores, no latency benchmarks, no token-savings measurements for context compression, no throughput figures for multi-tenant load, and no ablation studies. The architectural claims are validated only by the existence of two deployments, not by controlled experiments comparing against baselines. - The deployment validation is thin: only two product forms are demonstrated, stress testing at scale (hundreds of concurrent users) has not been performed, and the cross-language interfaces (WebSocket, gRPC) have not been benchmarked for throughput or error recovery. The authors acknowledge the architecture does not address horizontal scaling across multiple engine instances. - The LLM-assisted shell injection detection inherits the base model's false-negative rate and adds a full inference round-trip of latency for every non-whitelisted command—a meaningful security and performance liability that is acknowledged but not quantified or compared to static alternatives.
