#136 — Moderately Innovative (52)

HY-World 2.0: A Multi-Modal World Model for Reconstructing, Generating, and Simulating 3D Worlds (68 ▲ )

Team HY-World, Chenjie Cao, Xuhui Zuo, Zhenwei Wang, Yisu Zhang, Junta Wu, Zhenyang Liu, Yuning Gong, Yang Liu, Bo Yuan, Chao Zhang, Coopers Li, Dongyuan Guo, Fan Yang, Haiyu Zhang, Hang Cao, Jianchen Zhu, Jiaxin Lin, Jie Xiao, Jihong Zhang, Junlin Yu, Lei Wang, Lifu Wang, Lilin Wang, Linus, Minghui Chen, Peng He, Penghao Zhao, Qi Chen, Rui Chen, Rui Shao, Sicong Liu, Wangchen Qin, Xiaochuan Niu, Xiang Yuan, Yi Sun, Yifei Tang, Yifu Sun, Yihang Lian, Yonghao Tan, Yuhong Liu, Yuyang Yin, Zhiyuan Min, Tengfei Wang, Chunchao Guo · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: HY-World 2.0 is an impressive engineering integration of panorama generation, trajectory planning, video synthesis, and feed-forward reconstruction into a unified open-source pipeline. Individual contributions like Normalized RoPE and MaskGaussian pruning are useful but technically straightforward. The four-stage pipeline compounds complexity and error propagation risks, and the lack of standardized 3DGS benchmarks makes claims against closed-source competitors difficult to verify.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing 3D world modeling systems are bifurcated: generative approaches synthesize navigable scenes from sparse inputs (text, single images) but sacrifice reconstruction accuracy, while reconstruction methods recover precise geometry from dense multi-view data but lack generative priors for hallucinating unseen regions. HY-World 2.0 addresses this by unifying both paradigms into a single multi-modal framework that handles text, single-view images, multi-view images, and video as input, producing explorable 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) worlds for robotics simulation, game development, and environment mapping. Methodology: HY-World 2.0 is a four-stage generation pipeline plus a reconstruction backbone. Stage 1 (HY-Pano 2.0) generates a 360° equirectangular panorama from text or image inputs. Stage 2 (WorldNav) plans multi-type camera trajectories (regular, surrounding, reconstruction-aware, wandering, aerial) using geometric and semantic scene parsing to cover occluded structures and avoid coverage gaps. Stage 3 (WorldStereo 2.0) is a keyframe-based video generation model that synthesizes novel views along planned trajectories using two memory mechanisms: Global-Geometric Memory (GGM), which injects 3D point cloud features into attention, and an improved Spatial-Stereo Memory (SSM++) that stitches multi-view reference features via spatial concatenation rather than temporal concatenation. A Keyframe-VAE replaces the standard Video-VAE to handle geometric keyframe inputs, and DMD post-distillation reduces inference steps while preserving quality. Stage 4 (WorldMirror 2.0) is a feed-forward reconstruction model that estimates per-frame depth and normals from multi-view inputs. Key improvements include Normalized RoPE (maps patch positions to [-1,1] regardless of resolution, converting extrapolation to interpolation), explicit depth-to-normal supervision coupling the two prediction heads, and a learned depth mask head for invalid pixel prediction. The 3DGS composition stage uses MaskGaussian probabilistic pruning to simultaneously achieve rendering efficiency (77% Gaussian count reduction) and visual fidelity. WorldLens provides the interactive rendering platform with collision detection and IBL lighting. Key Results: On panorama generation, HY-Pano 2.0 achieves best CLIP-T (0.258 vs 0.250 for HY-World 1.0) and leads on Q-Align quality/aesthetics for both T2P and I2P tasks. On single-view scene reconstruction (Tanks-and-Temples, MipNeRF360), WorldStereo 2.0 achieves highest F1 (41.43 / 51.27) and AUC (58.19 / 65.79), surpassing SEVA, Gen3C, Lyra, and FlashWorld. Camera control outperforms all video-based competitors with RotErr 0.492, TransErr 0.968, ATE 1.768 (vs WorldStereo 1.0: 0.762, 1.245, 2.141). WorldMirror 2.0 reduces 7-Scenes point map accuracy error from 0.043 to 0.033 at medium resolution and from 0.079 to 0.037 at high resolution (where WorldMirror 1.0 degrades catastrophically due to position extrapolation), while camera pose AUC@30 drops only marginally vs. the 20-point collapse in v1.0. End-to-end world generation runs in ~712 seconds on NVIDIA H20 GPUs, compared to ~5 hours for video2world's ICP-based composition. Qualitative comparisons with closed-source Marble 1.0 show superior adherence to input conditions, sharper textures, and better geometric consistency under large viewpoint changes. Strengths: - Unified generation + reconstruction framework with full open-source release (weights, code, technical details), enabling reproducibility unlike competing closed-source systems (Marble) - Normalized RoPE is a simple but high-impact fix that makes WorldMirror resolution-agnostic, eliminating a fundamental limitation of absolute integer position encoding at inference time - Multi-type trajectory planning (WorldNav) systematically addresses coverage gaps in 3D scene completion, including occlusion-aware surrounding trajectories and aerial BEV views Weaknesses / Limitations: - The four-stage pipeline has substantial complexity and many independently-trained components (HY-Pano, WorldNav, WorldStereo, WorldMirror, 3DGS), making end-to-end fine-tuning or error propagation analysis difficult - Comparison with Marble is purely qualitative on 3DGS rendering — no standardized public 3DGS generation benchmark exists, making the "comparable to closed-source" claim hard to verify rigorously - Depth scale ambiguity in WorldMirror still requires a RANSAC-based linear alignment hack against panoramic point cloud guidance, with heuristic outlier detection; this fragility is inherited by the entire composition pipeline

#137 — Moderately Innovative (52)

Seedance 2.0: Advancing Video Generation for World Complexity (111 ▲ )

Team Seedance, De Chen, Liyang Chen, Xin Chen, Ying Chen, Zhuo Chen, Zhuowei Chen, Feng Cheng, Tianheng Cheng, Yufeng Cheng, Mojie Chi, Xuyan Chi, Jian Cong, Qinpeng Cui, Fei Ding, Qide Dong, Yujiao Du, Haojie Duanmu, Junliang Fan, Jiarui Fang, Jing Fang, Zetao Fang, Chengjian Feng, Yu Gao, Diandian Gu, Dong Guo, Hanzhong Guo, Qiushan Guo, Boyang Hao, Hongxiang Hao, Haoxun He, Jiaao He, Qian He, Tuyen Hoang, Heng Hu, Ruoqing Hu, Yuxiang Hu, Jiancheng Huang, Weilin Huang, Zhaoyang Huang, Zhongyi Huang, Jishuo Jin, Ming Jing, Ashley Kim, Shanshan Lao, Yichong Leng, Bingchuan Li, Gen Li, Haifeng Li, Huixia Li, Jiashi Li, Ming Li, Xiaojie Li, Xingxing Li, Yameng Li, Yiying Li, Yu Li, Yueyan Li, Chao Liang, Han Liang, Jianzhong Liang, Ying Liang, Wang Liao, J. H. Lien, Shanchuan Lin, Xi Lin, Feng Ling, Yue Ling, Fangfang Liu, Jiawei Liu, Jihao Liu, Jingtuo Liu, Shu Liu, Sichao Liu, Wei Liu, Xue Liu, Zuxi Liu, Ruijie Lu, Lecheng Lyu, Jingting Ma, Tianxiang Ma, Xiaonan Nie, Jingzhe Ning, Junjie Pan, Xitong Pan, Ronggui Peng, Xueqiong Qu, Yuxi Ren, Yuchen Shen, Guang Shi, Lei Shi, Yinglong Song, Fan Sun, Li Sun, Renfei Sun, Wenjing Tang, Boyang Tao, Zirui Tao, Dongliang Wang, Feng Wang, Hulin Wang, Ke Wang, Qingyi Wang, Rui Wang, Shuai Wang, Shulei Wang, Weichen Wang, Xuanda Wang, Yanhui Wang, Yue Wang, Yuping Wang, Yuxuan Wang, Zijie Wang, Ziyu Wang, Guoqiang Wei, Meng Wei, Di Wu, Guohong Wu, Hanjie Wu, Huachao Wu, Jian Wu, Jie Wu, Ruolan Wu, Shaojin Wu, Xiaohu Wu, Xinglong Wu, Yonghui Wu, Ruiqi Xia, Xin Xia, Xuefeng Xiao, Shuang Xu, Bangbang Yang, Jiaqi Yang, Runkai Yang, Tao Yang, Yihang Yang, Zhixian Yang, Ziyan Yang, Fulong Ye, Bingqian Yi, Xing Yin, Yongbin You, Linxiao Yuan, Weihong Zeng, Xuejiao Zeng, Yan Zeng, Siyu Zhai, Zhonghua Zhai, Bowen Zhang, Chenlin Zhang, Heng Zhang, Jun Zhang, Manlin Zhang, Peiyuan Zhang, Shuo Zhang, Xiaohe Zhang, Xiaoying Zhang, Xinyan Zhang, Xinyi Zhang, Yichi Zhang, Zixiang Zhang, Haiyu Zhao, Huating Zhao, Liming Zhao, Yian Zhao, Guangcong Zheng, Jianbin Zheng, Xiaozheng Zheng, Zerong Zheng, Kuan Zhu, Feilong Zuo · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 16 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Seedance 2.0 demonstrates dominant empirical results across an impressive breadth of multimodal tasks, and the audio generation quality gap over competitors is substantial. However, as a product technical report with zero architectural or methodological disclosure, it contributes no reproducible scientific insight. The evaluation on the team's own benchmark (SeedVideoBench 2.0) further limits the ability to assess genuine innovation versus engineering scale.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: The dominant paradigm in video generation has been producing short clips with limited controllability. Professional content creation workflows demand rich multi-modal control signals, native audio-video joint generation, and physically plausible motion for complex scenes. Existing models, including ByteDance's own Seedance 1.x series, lack the breadth of multimodal input support (text + image + video + audio simultaneously), the motion fidelity needed for complex human interactions, and the audio generation quality required for production-grade use. The gap addressed here is the transition from short, weakly-controlled clips to a unified, highly controllable audio-video synthesis engine applicable to billion-scale user products. Methodology: Seedance 2.0 adopts a unified, large-scale architecture for multi-modal audio-video joint generation supporting four input modalities: text, image, audio, and video. The model natively generates 4–15 second clips at 480p/720p and outputs multi-track binaural audio (background, ambient, dialogue) synchronized to visual content. The reference-and-control suite covers subject identity, motion, style, visual effects/creative reference, video editing, and video continuation/extension — 20 of 22 identified input modality combinations, of which 7 are exclusive to this model (all visual effects/creative reference variants and all continuation/extension variants). Evaluation is conducted via SeedVideoBench 2.0, an upgraded benchmark adding multimodal task following, narrative quality, multilingual audio coverage, and objective/subjective split evaluation with domain experts from advertising and game production. Human preference evaluation is also performed via the Arena.AI Elo-based leaderboard. A "Seedance 2.0 Fast" accelerated variant is provided for low-latency scenarios. The paper does not disclose architectural specifics (transformer variant, parameter count, training data, or diffusion formulation). Key Results: On SeedVideoBench 2.0, Seedance 2.0 ranks first across all evaluated dimensions in T2V, I2V, and R2V tasks against Kling 2.6/3.0, Sora 2 Pro, Veo 3.1, Wan 2.6, Vidu Q2 Pro, and Kling O1. In T2V (1–5 scale), it scores 3.75 on motion quality vs. a runner-up of 3.10 (Kling 3.0) and 3.56 on audio prompt following vs. 2.92 (Sora 2 Pro), with a 97.55% usability rate on motion and 93.75% on audio quality. Satisfaction rates (score ≥ 4) exceed 51% on every T2V dimension — no competitor exceeds 44% on any single dimension. Audio is the most differentiating axis: audio quality satisfaction is 62.05% vs. below 10% for all competitors. In fine-grained T2V motion (30 categories), it ranks first on 29/30, peaking at 4.43 on multi-entity feature match and 4.25 on framing/composition. In I2V, it scores 3.35/3.46/3.31 on motion/prompt-following/image-preservation while competitors cap at 3.18. For R2V, it leads on all five dimensions — editing consistency 3.54 vs. Kling 3.0's 3.37, reference alignment 3.03 vs. 2.37, motion quality 3.24 vs. 2.36. On Arena.AI, it ranks #1 on both T2V and I2V leaderboards with Elo scores of 1450 (±15) and 1449 (±11), leading second-place Veo 3.1 by 79 points on T2V at 720p vs. competitors operating at 1080p. Known weaknesses include video extension (extension task-following score 1.93 vs. Veo 3.1's 2.78), minor deformation artifacts in edge-case motions, audio distortion in multi-speaker scenes, and joint image-audio conditioning (scores around 2.3 for both supported models). Strengths: - Broadest multimodal task coverage of any evaluated model (20/22 input modality combinations, 7 unique to this system), enabling real production workflows that no competitor can replicate end-to-end. - Dominant and consistent audio generation quality — the largest performance gap over competitors is on audio, where most rivals score below 3.0 on a 5-point scale while Seedance 2.0 exceeds 3.5 across all audio dimensions; this is a genuine capability differential, not a marginal improvement. - Achieves #1 on the Arena.AI human preference leaderboard at 720p, outperforming models operating at 1080p, indicating that motion coherence and temporal quality matter more perceptually than raw resolution. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper is a product technical report with no disclosure of architecture, parameter count, training data composition, or loss functions, making it impossible to scientifically reproduce or analyze the system; comparisons are conducted entirely on the team's own benchmark (SeedVideoBench 2.0), creating potential evaluator bias. - Video extension is a clear weak point: task-following scores 1.93 (31.82% 3-point rate) vs. Veo 3.1's 2.78 (88.89%), and the model only supports extension of arbitrary uploaded videos rather than generating its own first — a meaningful capability gap for long-form content workflows; video continuation also has known issues with color consistency, multi-subject omission, and duplication.

#138 — Moderately Innovative (52)

PubSwap: Public-Data Off-Policy Coordination for Federated RLVR

Anupam Nayak, Baris Askin, Muhammed Ustaomeroglu, Carlee Joe-Wong, Gauri Joshi · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: PubSwap scores 52 for addressing the genuinely important but underexplored problem of federated RLVR with a practical system design. The Balanced aggregation strategy is a reasonable solution and the negative result about FedProx failing in autoregressive RLVR is useful. However, the approach largely combines existing components (LoRA communication, GRPO optimization, public data coordination) without introducing fundamentally new mechanisms, and the small experimental scale (4 clients, ≤4B models) leaves open whether the approach is viable at realistic federation sizes.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) — the post-training paradigm behind strong reasoning models — is almost exclusively studied in centralized settings. In practice, however, the data needed for domain-specific reasoning (medical records, financial reports, legal documents) is distributed across institutions that cannot share it due to privacy regulations. Federated learning is a natural fit, but applying RLVR in this regime faces two compounding problems: (1) full-model synchronization is prohibitively expensive at LLM scale, and (2) performing many local gradient steps between rounds under heterogeneous data distributions causes severe client drift, degrading the shared global model. Prior work on federated LLM training focuses almost entirely on supervised fine-tuning; the RLVR regime introduces additional complexity from clipped policy-ratio objectives, stale rollout reuse, and reward-variance dynamics that make standard drift-mitigation techniques (e.g., FedProx) ineffective. Methodology: PubSwap combines two complementary mechanisms. First, all clients perform local GRPO steps using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA, rank 32) rather than full fine-tuning; only the low-rank matrices are communicated and averaged via FedIT-style inexact aggregation, reducing per-layer communication from O(2md) to O(2r(m+d)). Second, a small shared public dataset available to all clients and the server is used to periodically inject off-policy coordination steps. Within each PubSwap period of τ_swap steps, the last step is a "public step": the server broadcasts a batch of public prompts; every client generates K responses and sends them to the server; the server aggregates and returns responses to each client; clients then perform a GRPO update using the returned response set. Two aggregation strategies are studied: Random (server uniformly samples K answers from the NxK global pool) and Balanced (client uses its own responses if it already has ≥ K/2 correct; otherwise replaces up to K/2 − c_n of its incorrect responses with correct responses drawn from other clients). Balanced is designed to maximize reward variance within each client's response set while minimizing off-policy drift, since it only replaces locally incorrect responses. The paper also provides a theoretical drift analysis showing that the Balanced step's additional inter-client drift is controlled by α (fraction of replaced responses) × β (GRPO gradient sensitivity to one replacement), both of which are small when clients have not drifted far apart. Key Results: Experiments are run on 4 clients with heterogeneous Dirichlet splits (α=0.3 standard, α=0.1 high heterogeneity), using Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B, Qwen3-1.7B, Qwen3-4B-Instruct, and Llama3.2-3B-Instruct. On math reasoning (MATH and DeepMath benchmarks), FedAvg-PubSwap with Balanced aggregation consistently matches or outperforms both FedAvg+GRPO and FedProx+GRPO across all local-step settings (τ ∈ {10, 40, 90, 120}). Gains are most pronounced at large τ (high communication efficiency): on Qwen3-1.7B / DeepMath at τ=120, PubSwap achieves 55.8 vs. FedAvg's 50.7 and FedProx's 47.7 Pass@1. On medical reasoning (Llama3.2-3B / MedQA+MedMCQA), PubSwap scores 58.1–70.2 vs. FedAvg's 56.0–67.4 across τ settings. Notably, FedProx consistently fails to improve over vanilla FedAvg+GRPO and often underperforms it, suggesting that parameter-space proximity constraints do not translate to policy-space proximity in autoregressive models. Results hold under higher heterogeneity (α=0.1) with similar relative gains. Ablations on swap period (τ_swap ∈ {2,4,8}) show robust performance across all values, with τ_swap=4 marginally best in some settings. Strengths: - Tackles a practically important and underexplored problem (federated RLVR with private data), and provides a complete, working system rather than a theoretical sketch. - The Balanced aggregation strategy is well-motivated: it maximizes reward variance while keeping updates close to the local policy, directly addressing the on-policy constraint that makes off-policy GRPO updates problematic. - Identifies and empirically documents a concrete failure mode of FedProx in the RLVR setting (parameter-space constraints do not imply policy-space constraints in autoregressive models), which is a useful negative result for the community. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Small-scale evaluation: experiments use only 4 clients and relatively small models (1.5B–4B parameters); it is unclear whether gains persist at realistic federation scales (dozens or hundreds of clients) or with larger models where LoRA rank may be insufficient to capture the full RLVR update. - The theoretical analysis is intentionally simplified (SGD instead of AdamW, single gradient update per step, objective-level rather than data-distribution-level heterogeneity), limiting the gap to formal convergence guarantees and leaving key quantities (L_priv, L_pub, β) uncharacterized in terms of data statistics. - The method requires a meaningful shared public dataset with verifiable rewards — an assumption that may be hard to satisfy in the most privacy-sensitive domains (e.g., rare diseases, proprietary financial instruments), yet no analysis is provided of how performance degrades as public data quality or size diminishes.
Search Results 2604.12160 PDF
#139 — Moderately Innovative (52)

Accelerating Triangle-Connected Truss Community Search Across Heterogeneous Hardware

dl.acm.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Scores 52 for the triangle type classification that cleanly eliminates the k-iteration bottleneck, enabling single-pass batch processing with formal correctness guarantees. The tensor abstraction for hardware portability is practically useful. However, this is primarily a systems-level acceleration of an existing index structure (EquiTree) rather than a new algorithmic paradigm, and the AMD GPU results highlight that the portability claims are limited by framework maturity.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Community search in graphs — finding cohesive subgraphs containing specific query vertices — is a fundamental task in network analysis. The Triangle-connected k-Truss Community (k-TTC) model strengthens the standard k-truss by requiring edges to be connected through chains of adjacent triangles, yielding tighter communities with provably smaller diameters. The state-of-the-art index, EquiTree, achieves time-optimal search in O(|A|) but its CPU-based sequential construction takes hours on billion-edge graphs (e.g., ~5964s on Orkut), and online search still takes seconds per query. The core bottleneck is the iterative per-k processing, which is fundamentally at odds with the batch-parallel execution model of modern hardware accelerators like GPUs. Methodology: The paper proposes a tensor-based framework (TETree/TETruss) that reformulates EquiTree construction, k-TTC search, and index maintenance as sequences of hardware-agnostic tensor operations executable via deep learning runtimes (PyTorch/TensorFlow), enabling deployment on NVIDIA and AMD GPUs without hardware-specific code. The central algorithmic insight is a triangle classification into three types based on the trussness of constituent edges: internal triangles (all edges same trussness) define supernode membership; marginal triangles (two edges same trussness) both merge edges into supernodes and create inter-supernode edges; external triangles (all edges distinct trussness) create superedges only. Theorem 1 proves processing order is irrelevant, allowing all triangles to be processed in one batch, eliminating the O(k_max) iteration loop of prior methods. Index refinement (merging conflicting supernodes into k-partial classes) is handled by a batch-based merging strategy that processes maximal active supernodes in parallel rounds, proven to converge with each round permanently stabilizing processed supernodes. Online search uses an Optimized Parallel CC (OPCC) algorithm that updates connected-component labels directly from sorted superedge tensors and applies a single Compress pass, avoiding the layer-by-layer traversal of prior label-propagation approaches. Dynamic maintenance identifies affected edges, recomputes trussness on the affected subgraph, and applies the triangle-centric creation and batch merging on only the affected region. Key Results: Evaluated on seven real-world graphs from 88K to 261M edges (Facebook, DBLP, Youtube, Catster, LiveJournal, Orkut, Weibo). For EquiTree construction, TETree achieves a 336× average speedup over sequential SETree and a 16.76× speedup over the basic tensor variant TETree-Basic. For EquiTruss construction, TETruss achieves 154× speedup over multi-core CPU MCETruss and 19.59× over a native CUDA re-implementation of MCETruss. For k-TTC search (1000 random queries, k=4), TETree achieves 108× average speedup over SETree, with query times dropping from 3.2s to 22ms on Orkut. TETree-OPCC outperforms LP-based and two-fold search by 3.21× and 11.73× on average. The approach scales to the 261M-edge Weibo graph where CPU baselines exceed 2 hours; TETree completes construction in 12.2s. On AMD 7700XT, performance is reduced relative to NVIDIA A100 due to suboptimal PyTorch ROCm backends but still substantially outperforms CPU. Batch-based merging contributes up to 6.15× speedup within the advanced algorithm. Cost-effectiveness and energy efficiency analyses confirm that speedup ratios far exceed the 2× price and 2.5× TDP ratios of GPU vs CPU hardware. Strengths: - The triangle type classification (internal/marginal/external) is a clean theoretical contribution with formal proofs that fully eliminates the k-iteration bottleneck and enables single-pass batch processing - Hardware portability via tensor abstraction is practically valuable — the same Python code runs on NVIDIA and AMD GPUs and is not coupled to CUDA primitives - Handles graphs exceeding GPU memory through principled batch partitioning (Theorem 1 guarantees correctness regardless of partition order), demonstrated on 261M-edge Weibo Weaknesses / Limitations: - The advanced algorithm's memory footprint grows substantially with batch size since all triangles are materialized simultaneously; the paper trades the k-loop for memory, and peak GPU memory for large graphs is not reported beyond OK/WB - AMD GPU results are noticeably weaker (e.g., TETree on AMD 7700XT takes 6.9s on Catster vs 2.2s on NVIDIA 3090), and the authors attribute this to PyTorch's immature ROCm support rather than any algorithmic issue — limiting practical heterogeneous portability claims - The refinement phase iteration count R is bounded by k_max_n (distinct trussness values per connected component) but can still be significant on dense graphs like Catster (34 creation iterations, 34 refinement iterations vs k^n=205), and the theoretical worst-case analysis does not tighten this bound
Citations
#140 — Moderately Innovative (52)

FlexVector: A SpMM Vector Processor with Flexible VRF for GCNs on Varying-Sparsity Graphs

Bohan Li, Shengmin Li, Xinyu Shi, Enyi Yao, Francky Catthoor, Simei Yang · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: FlexVector's insight of pushing irregular dense-row access handling from the DRAM-cache interface down to the register file level is a well-motivated architectural choice that achieves 3.78x speedup with 50x less area than large-buffer alternatives. The hybrid METIS edge-cut plus intra-tile vertex-cut preprocessing is tightly co-designed with hardware constraints. However, it remains a fairly incremental contribution within the crowded GCN accelerator space, limited to integer precision and node-classification inference only.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) formulate inference as two-stage sparse-dense matrix multiplication (SpMM), but real-world graphs exhibit power-law degree distributions that create severe irregularity: a few super-nodes have hundreds of neighbors while the vast majority have very few. Existing unified-engine GCN accelerators (e.g., GROW, GCNAX, AWB-GCN) either rely on large on-chip caches (hundreds of KB) to absorb irregular DRAM accesses or adopt dynamic runtime rebalancing that incurs significant control overhead. The core gap is that handling irregular dense-row accesses at the DRAM-cache level requires large, power-hungry buffers, while static dataflows fail to adapt across graphs with varying sparsity. The paper asks: can irregularity be pushed down to the register file level, eliminating the need for oversized caches while still achieving high performance? Methodology: FlexVector is a hardware-software co-design built around a domain-specific vector processor. The central innovation is a software-managed flexible Vector Register File (VRF) split into a fixed region (holding high-reuse dense rows identified at compile time via a top-k column-nonzero analysis, Algorithm 2) and a dynamic region (handling low-reuse rows fetched on misses). A double-VRF scheme partitions the dynamic region into two halves to overlap data movement with computation. The execution model uses a coarse-grained ISA that operates at the granularity of a full sparse row times a dense submatrix—reducing instruction count 3–20% versus fine-grained per-nonzero control. A hierarchical dataflow coordinates row-wise product at the buffer-VRF level (exploiting full-row register access without multi-banked VRFs) with inner-product at the DRAM-buffer level (maximizing output-stationary reuse). Graph preprocessing uses METIS-based inter-tile edge-cut to partition graphs into VRF-capacity-sized tiles, followed by an intra-tile vertex-cut (Algorithm 1) that splits high-degree nodes to balance nonzeros per row and reduce VRF depth requirements. The design is implemented in SystemVerilog, synthesized at 28nm/1GHz via Synopsys Design Compiler, with SRAM/VRF modeled via CACTI 7.0 and DRAM modeled as HBM 1.0 (128 GB/s, 7 pJ/bit). Key Results: Evaluated on five datasets (Cora, CiteSeer, Pubmed, Reddit, Yelp) against a GROW-like baseline with matched buffer capacity (2KB dense buffer, 256B sparse buffer): FlexVector achieves 3.78× geometric mean speedup and 40.5% lower energy at only 4.7% higher area. The sparsity-aware fixed-dynamic VRF boundary algorithm (Algorithm 2) reaches within 2% of the best static k configuration across all tested VRF depths. Ablation decomposition shows that multi-buffering alone contributes 3.34× speedup; double-VRF adds 5.1%; vertex-cut adds 0.3% speedup while reducing VRF area 3.1%; flexible fixed region adds 7.4%. Against the large-buffer GROW-like† variant (512KB dense buffer), FlexVector is 1.54× slower but uses 50× less area and 7.2× less energy—making the tradeoff favorable under practical area budgets. DRAM access reduction ranges 3.0–8.6× over GROW-like at matched buffer sizes. Sensitivity analysis shows VLEN=512 bits is a sweet spot: 6.77–7.26× speedup with 1.77–1.81× area overhead and ~50% energy reduction relative to the VLEN=64, D=6×2 baseline. Strengths: - The VRF-centric approach to handling irregular accesses is architecturally well-motivated: shifting repeated dense-row lookups from the DRAM-buffer interface to the buffer-VRF interface achieves large DRAM access reductions (3–8.6×) with a tiny on-chip footprint (2KB vs. 512KB in GROW). - The hybrid preprocessing (METIS edge-cut + intra-tile vertex-cut) is tightly co-designed with hardware constraints—partitioning to VRF capacity rather than buffer capacity is a crisp, principled distinction from prior work. - The sparsity-aware dynamic k selection (Algorithm 2) is practical and effective, reaching near-optimal performance without exhaustive search, demonstrating genuine adaptivity across tile sparsity variations. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The comparison baseline is a re-implemented GROW-like system rather than the original GROW RTL, raising questions about faithful reproduction of GROW's run-ahead and HDN caching mechanisms; results may not fully reflect GROW's actual optimizations. - FlexVector underperforms GROW-like by 24.2% at large buffer configurations (m=2273, 512KB), meaning the design is only advantageous under tight area/buffer budgets—a regime that may not hold in all deployment contexts, particularly for server-class inference. - The architecture currently supports only integer (8-bit and 32-bit) precision, excluding floating-point GCN workloads, and evaluation is limited to node-classification GCN inference; generalizability to other sparse workloads (e.g., transformer attention, link prediction) is undemonstrated.
Search Results 2604.10113 PDF
#141 — Moderately Innovative (52)

Lineage-based Reuse and Memory Management for Multi-backend ML Systems

sigmodrecord.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: MEMPHIS extends lineage-based reuse to heterogeneous backends (CPU, GPU, Spark) within a unified framework, which is a meaningful engineering contribution. The compiler-level integration of prefetch, async broadcast, and workload-aware eviction is thorough. However, it builds heavily on prior LIMA work and is tightly coupled to SystemDS, limiting broader impact and uptake.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern ML pipelines exhibit high computational redundancy due to iterative hyperparameter tuning, AutoML exploration, repeated data preprocessing, and mini-batch DNN training. While prior work (notably LIMA) addressed fine-grained reuse for local CPU operations, no unified framework existed for eliminating redundancy holistically across heterogeneous backends — CPUs, GPUs, and distributed systems like Apache Spark. Each backend has fundamentally different execution models (eager vs. lazy vs. asynchronous), memory characteristics, and data exchange costs, making naive cache transplants either ineffective or catastrophically expensive (e.g., materializing 12K Spark RDDs eager-style is 10x slower than no caching; GPU memory allocation/free overhead is 4.6x–9x that of actual compute). Methodology: MEMPHIS introduces a hierarchical lineage-based reuse cache integrated into Apache SystemDS. The core abstraction is a backend-agnostic lineage DAG built incrementally at runtime via a TRACE/REUSE/PUT API. Each lineage item captures opcode, data characteristics, and input dependencies, with hashing for O(1) probe and non-recursive equality checks with memoization. Cache entries wrap backend-specific pointers (in-memory matrix blocks, RDD handles, GPU device pointers, or disk-spilled binaries), enabling seamless cross-backend data movement. For Spark, the system handles lazy evaluation via speculative/delayed caching (defer until nth hit), lazy garbage collection of dangling RDD and broadcast references, and a cost-based eviction policy scoring RDDs by (hits + misses + jobs) × compute_cost / size. For GPUs, MEMPHIS maintains Live/Free pointer lists to eliminate cudaMalloc/cudaFree overhead through pointer recycling, with eviction scoring based on recency, lineage DAG height (favoring input data pipeline reuse), and compute cost. The compiler is extended with three new mechanisms: (1) prefetch and async broadcast operators that trigger Spark/GPU jobs concurrently with CPU execution, propagating lineage to prefetch threads for deferred caching; (2) workload-aware cache management including eviction injection between loop pattern shifts, RDD checkpoint placement for iterative algorithms, and automatic delay-factor tuning per basic block based on estimated reuse potential; and (3) a maxParallelize operator linearization algorithm that schedules longer remote operator chains first to maximize CPU–backend overlap. Key Results: On hyperparameter tuning of cross-validated linear regression (HCV, 5–100 GB synthetic data), MEMPHIS achieves up to 9.6x speedup over baseline SystemDS by reusing X^T X and X^T y across folds and overlapping execution via prefetch. LIMA is limited to inputs ≤20 GB (local only); HELIX performs near baseline due to lack of fine-grained reuse. Async operators alone yield 2x speedup and MEMPHIS is ~20% faster than its non-async variant. On Hyperband model selection (HBAND, 5–20 GB), MEMPHIS yields 2.5–2.6x speedup, reusing ~4K RDDs, 2K Spark actions, and 40K local matrices, outperforming HELIX and LIMA by ~40%. On GPU transfer learning for vision models (TLVIS, CIFAR-10 and ImageNet), MEMPHIS achieves 2–3x speedup over baseline, beats specialized PyTorch by 1.5x (PyTorch with torch.compile hits OOM), and handles 30K/17.5K pointer recycles without degrading reuse. Even a 900 MB lineage cache consistently delivers 1.2x speedup; scaling to 5 GB or 30 GB yields up to 1.6x. Lineage tracing overhead is negligible for realistic input sizes (≥8 MB), with 1.1–3x reuse gains depending on redundancy rate (20–80%). Strengths: - The unified lineage abstraction cleanly handles backend heterogeneity without requiring workload-specific heuristics, enabling principled reuse across CPU, Spark, and GPU within a single framework. - The combination of compiler-level rewrites (prefetch, async broadcast, eviction injection, checkpoint placement, delay-factor tuning) with runtime cache management is well-integrated and addresses root causes of per-backend overhead rather than papering over them. - Empirical evaluation is thorough and honest: micro-benchmarks quantify individual overheads (tracing, probing, allocation), and end-to-end experiments cover realistic heterogeneous workloads at 5–100 GB scale with comparisons against five distinct prior systems. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The framework is tightly coupled to Apache SystemDS's compilation infrastructure; applicability to Python-hosted eager libraries (NumPy, PyTorch eager mode) requires instrumentation not yet addressed, limiting immediate uptake where most practitioners operate. - Reuse potential is entirely workload-dependent and the delay-factor heuristic (threshold at >80% reusable blocks) is coarse — no cost-based optimizer jointly optimizes operator scheduling and cache placement, which the authors acknowledge as future work but which could substantially change the performance profile on mixed workloads. - Evaluation uses only synthetic datasets for the Spark-heavy experiments (HCV, HBAND), which may not capture real-world data skew, schema complexity, or the shuffle overhead patterns that often dominate practical Spark workloads.
Citations
#142 — Moderately Innovative (51)

CLEAR: Context Augmentation from Contrastive Learning of Experience via Agentic Reflection

Linbo Liu, Guande Wu, Han Ding, Yawei Wang, Qiang Zhou, Yuzhe Lu, Zhichao Xu, Huan Song, Panpan Xu, Lin Lee Cheong · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 51 for its practical contribution of decoupling context augmentation from the execution agent, enabling improvement of black-box proprietary models. The combinatorial data augmentation strategy and cross-model transferability results are solid engineering contributions. However, the core idea of training a model to generate better prompts is not deeply novel, the compute requirements are substantial, and evaluation is limited to two benchmarks.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM agents operating in sequential decision-making environments rely heavily on task-relevant context to make good decisions. Existing context engineering approaches—including RAG and prompt-optimization methods like ACE—either retrieve past experience verbatim or produce static, general-purpose guidance. Both strategies place substantial adaptation burden on the execution agent: it must reason about how retrieved or generic guidance applies to the specific new task at hand. This burden is especially problematic when the execution model is weaker or when the new task differs substantially from training instances. The paper asks whether a dedicated, trainable model can absorb this adaptation work instead, generating context that is already tailored to the new task rather than forcing the execution agent to re-interpret retrieved history. Methodology: CLEAR trains a lightweight Context Augmentation Model (CAM) that, given a new task description, generates auxiliary context that is prepended to the execution agent's prompt. The CAM is trained in three phases without touching the execution agent's weights. Phase 0 (data generation): each training task is executed m=6 times to collect a grouped replay buffer of trajectories and rewards. A reflection agent (Claude-Sonnet-4 with a shell tool for selective file access) performs contrastive analysis over these trajectories—comparing successful vs. failed executions—and produces a task-specific instruction summary. A combinatorial subsampling trick (choosing 3-of-6 runs, C(6,3)=20 times) expands the dataset 20-fold. Phase 1 (SFT): a Qwen3-32B model is fine-tuned on the (task, instruction) pairs from Phase 0 using LLaMAFactory. Phase 2 (RL): the SFT-initialized CAM is further optimized via GRPO, where rewards come from actually running the frozen execution agent on CAM-augmented task descriptions in the live benchmark environment, using Amazon Bedrock AgentCore for high-concurrency rollout. The key architectural decision is that CAM is fully decoupled from the execution agent: any proprietary or open-source LLM can serve as the executor without weight access. Key Results: On AppWorld TEST-N, CLEAR improves Task Goal Completion (TGC) from 72.62% (baseline) to 81.15% and Scenario Goal Completion (SGC) from 52.38% to 66.67% using Claude-Sonnet-4 as the executor—outperforming both RAG (72.02% TGC) and ACE (74.40% TGC), despite ACE using AppWorld-specific prompt engineering while CLEAR's prompts are benchmark-agnostic. On WebShop-40k, CLEAR improves average reward from 0.68 (baseline) to 0.74, versus 0.73 for RAG. Ablation confirms all three phases are independently necessary: removing RL drops TGC from 81.15% to 74.21%, removing contrastive learning (using only single trajectories for SFT) drops it further to 68.65%, and using the raw Qwen3-32B without any fine-tuning yields 67.40%. CAM transferability experiments show that a CAM trained entirely on Claude-generated trajectories still outperforms all baselines when the execution agent is switched to DeepSeek-V3.1 at test time (+1.68 TGC, +5.35 SGC over baseline). Latency overhead is modest: CAM adds ~1.2s per task invocation and increases total task runtime from 63.4s to 77.2s on average. Strengths: - The decoupling of CAM from the execution agent is practically important: it enables improving proprietary black-box agents (GPT, Claude, Gemini) without weight access, which is the dominant deployment scenario. - The combinatorial data augmentation strategy (20× expansion via subsampled trajectory groups) is a clean, well-motivated solution to the data scarcity problem inherent in fine-tuning from a bounded training set. - The CAM transferability result—gains hold when switching execution models entirely—suggests the CAM learns genuinely task-structural knowledge rather than artifacts of a specific model's behavior. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Training cost is substantially higher than competing training-free methods (ACE, RAG): it requires m×|train| agentic rollouts, full-parameter fine-tuning of a 32B model on 8 B200 GPUs, and 15 epochs of GRPO RL—making the approach inaccessible to researchers without AWS-scale compute. - The WebShop evaluation uses a reduced 40k-item variant rather than the full benchmark (millions of items), which the authors acknowledge may be inherently easier; results on the full benchmark are not reported, limiting external validity. - CLEAR is evaluated only on two benchmarks (one coding/API interaction task, one product retrieval task); it is unclear whether the approach generalizes to domains with more open-ended or knowledge-intensive tasks where the contrastive signal between trajectories may be noisier or harder to summarize as concise instructions.
Recommended 2604.07487 PDF
#143 — Moderately Innovative (48)

Understanding the Effects of Safety Unalignment on Large Language Models

John T. Halloran · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: A thorough empirical benchmarking study that applies two existing unalignment methods (jailbreak-tuning and weight orthogonalization) to six models across multiple harm dimensions. The finding that WO produces more capable adversarial attackers while preserving helpfulness is practically important, but the paper proposes no new methods—its contribution is comparative analysis and a partially effective SFT mitigation.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Safety alignment (SA) is the primary mechanism by which deployed LLMs are prevented from fulfilling harmful requests. Two distinct unalignment methods—jailbreak-tuning (JT) and weight orthogonalization (WO)—have been shown independently to substantially degrade these guardrails. However, prior work evaluated each method in isolation and focused almost exclusively on refusal rates, leaving critical questions unanswered: Which method produces models that are more capable of active harm (adversarial attacks, cyber attacks)? How does each method affect hallucination rates and general helpfulness? How do reasoning models (vs. instruction-tuned models) respond? This paper conducts the first direct, comprehensive comparison of JT versus WO across both harmful and helpful capability dimensions. Methodology: Six LLMs are evaluated: three instruction-tuned models (Qwen3-4B, Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-14B) and their reasoning counterparts (Qwen3-4B-Thinking, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-14B). JT follows Bowen et al. (2025): a 5,000-sample dataset with 98% benign (BookCorpus) and 2% malicious (PKU-SafeRLHF with jailbreak triggers) samples; fine-tuned for 5 epochs using Q-LoRA (r=64, α=128). WO follows Arditi et al. (2024): compute the model's refusal direction as the mean-difference vector between harmful and harmless prompt activations across layers, then orthogonalize all weight matrices against that direction (W' ← W − rr⊺W); extended here to support Qwen3 and DeepSeek tokens. Refusal is measured via StrongREJECT (323 samples). Adversarial attack ability is measured using AutoDAN-Turbo (200 HarmBench goals, k=20 tries, targeting Qwen3-4B and Llama-3.1-8B as victims). Cyber attack ability uses CyberSecEval 3 (1,000 MITRE ATT&CK-derived requests, gpt-4o as judge). Hallucination is measured via TruthfulQA and TofuEval. General helpfulness covers ARC-E, ARC-C, HellaSwag, PIQA, Winogrande, MMLU, and IFEval. A mitigation experiment applies supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on OpenHermes (243K samples, 1 epoch, lr=1e-5) to WO-unaligned models. Key Results: On refusal degradation, the two methods are roughly tied: each outperforms the other on exactly three of six models. WO reduces refusal rates by 74.4% on average for instruction-tuned models and 29.3% for reasoning models; JT reduces them by 62.3% and 45.3%, respectively—reasoning models are harder to unalign by either method. On adversarial attack capability (AutoDAN-Turbo ASR), WO-unaligned models outperform their JT counterparts in the majority of evaluations, with an average 27.7% higher ASR; for reasoning models alone, WO yields 40.2% higher ASRs. JT actually decreases adversarial ASR in six evaluations (four of which involve reasoning models). On cyber attacks (CyberSecEval 3), WO improves ASR across all six models while JT degrades it for half. On hallucinations, JT increases TruthfulQA and TofuEval rates by an average of +8.9 and +41.2 percentage points; WO increases them by only +3.6 and −1.7 points (WO actually reduces hallucinations in half the TofuEval evaluations). On general helpfulness (averaged across seven benchmarks), JT causes a 13.4% average decrease; WO causes only 2.2%—a six-fold difference. SFT mitigation of WO reduces AutoDAN-Turbo ASRs by an average of 45.3% and recovers 40.5–69.8% of refusal guardrails for IT models, with negligible effects on hallucination rates or helpfulness. Cyber attack mitigation via SFT is mixed: effective for half the models, ineffective for the other half. Strengths: - First systematic head-to-head comparison of JT and WO across harmful capability dimensions beyond refusal rate, including adversarial attacks, cyber attacks, hallucinations, and helpfulness—providing a much more complete threat model. - Extends the WO codebase to support reasoning models (Qwen3, DeepSeek-R1), revealing a previously unstudied and practically important asymmetry: WO is especially potent at boosting reasoning models' adversarial attack capabilities (+40.2% over JT). - Proposes and validates a practical mitigation (SFT on benign instruction data) that substantially curtails WO's most dangerous capability—adversarial attack success—without significantly degrading helpfulness or increasing hallucinations. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The study is limited to open-weight models up to 14B parameters; it is unknown whether findings generalize to larger models or to closed-source frontier models where WO currently requires white-box weight access—a constraint that substantially limits WO's present real-world threat but is treated as temporary. - SFT mitigation of cyber attack capabilities is inconsistent (effective for only half the models), and the paper does not explain why or provide a reliable mitigation path for this attack category, leaving a significant open gap in the proposed defense.
Recommended 2604.02574 PDF
#144 — Moderately Innovative (48)

Fast Heterogeneous Serving: Scalable Mixed-Scale LLM Allocation for SLO-Constrained Inference

Jiaming Cheng, Duong Tung Nguyen · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The constraint-aware heuristic design cleanly separates feasibility prerequisites (M1, M3) from quality optimizations (M2), and the two-stage stochastic evaluation revealing that cost-minimal exact solutions are fragile under operational uncertainty is a practically important finding. However, the methodology is standard operations research (MILP + GRASP-style heuristics) applied to a new domain, and the entirely synthetic evaluation limits confidence in real-world applicability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deploying LLM inference at scale requires jointly optimizing four tightly coupled decisions: selecting foundation models, provisioning heterogeneous GPU instances, configuring tensor/pipeline parallelism (TP/PP), and routing workloads across configurations—all under hard latency, accuracy, and budget SLOs. Existing exact MILP formulations (Helix, Jiang et al.) guarantee optimality but scale exponentially and require minutes to hours at production scale. System-level heuristics (DynamoLLM, Kim et al.) are fast but either fix parallelism a priori or decompose the problem into independent subproblems, ignoring the tight coupling where GPU memory limits feasible TP degrees, TP choice affects prefill/decode latency, and delay/error bounds jointly restrict allocatable workload. Naive greedy strategies that rank solely by cost routinely produce infeasible solutions under these coupled constraints. Methodology: The paper formulates the joint placement problem (PDM) as a MILP with a two-phase delay model separating TTFT (prefill) and generation (decode) latency under joint TP/PP configuration. Bilinear terms arising from continuous routing fractions multiplied by binary TP/PP selectors are linearized via McCormick envelopes, requiring only a single linearization layer. The core contribution is two constraint-aware heuristics built on three shared mechanisms: (M1) TP-aware feasibility selection, which discards any (model, GPU-tier) candidate lacking a (TP, PP) configuration that simultaneously satisfies GPU memory and delay SLOs; (M2) cost-per-effective-coverage ranking, which scores candidates by marginal cost divided by the maximum allocatable workload fraction constrained by both error and delay budgets; and (M3) parallelism upgrade for active GPUs, which adds GPUs to an already-loaded configuration rather than activating a new instance when delay is exceeded. The Greedy Heuristic (GH) applies M1–M3 in a two-phase single-pass: a greedy set-cover pre-allocation phase ensures every query type has at least one feasible configuration, followed by sequential workload assignment sorted by arrival rate. The Adaptive Greedy Heuristic (AGH) augments GH with multi-start construction (8 deterministic orderings plus R adaptive random starts), relocate-based local search (up to L=3 passes moving assignments to cheaper feasible alternatives), and GPU consolidation (redistributing queries from lightly loaded instances and deactivating them). Both heuristics are evaluated using a two-stage stochastic framework: Stage 1 fixes placements from nominal parameters; Stage 2 re-solves routing as a pure LP under 500 perturbed scenarios (±25% delay/error, ±20% arrival rates). Key Results: On instances calibrated with the Azure LLM Inference Trace 2025 (6 query types, 6 Llama-3.x models from 1B–70B, 10 GPU tiers across A6000/RTX4090/A100/H100 at FP16/INT8/INT4), GH solves in under 0.01–0.9 seconds and AGH in 0.015–2.3 seconds across problem sizes from (4,4,5) to (20,20,20), while the exact MILP exceeds the 600-second time limit at (15,15,10). AGH achieves over 260× speedup over the solver on large instances while closely matching optimal cost. Under 1.5× out-of-sample stress, AGH produces lower actual cost and SLO violation rates than the exact solver, because the constraint-aware mechanisms naturally provision headroom—the MILP's cost-minimal placement incurs large unmet-demand penalties when parameters deviate. Under tight budgets (δ=$72), AGH reduces cost by 70% and SLO violations by 74% relative to GH. Ablation confirms M1 and M3 are feasibility prerequisites (their removal produces memory or delay violations); removing M2 preserves feasibility but inflates cost by over 50%. Rolling re-optimization every 5 minutes with AGH saves up to 48% over a static MILP solve under high demand volatility (σ=0.05), while reducing cost variance by 4–8×. Strengths: - The distinction between feasibility prerequisites (M1, M3) and quality optimizations (M2) is non-trivial and empirically validated; ablation shows M1/M3 removal causes infeasibility, not merely cost increase, which cleanly separates this from standard GRASP-style heuristics. - The two-stage stochastic evaluation is methodologically sound: fixing placements then re-solving routing as a pure LP under 500 scenarios reveals that cost-minimal exact solutions are fragile under operational uncertainty, while heuristic conservatism provides implicit robustness—a practically important finding often ignored in optimization papers. - Rolling re-optimization enabled by sub-second runtimes demonstrates a compounding operational advantage: AGH-5min at high volatility beats both static MILP and static AGH, with dramatically lower cost variance, making the speed advantage quantitatively consequential rather than theoretical. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The simulation setup is entirely synthetic: demand parameters are calibrated from trace statistics but no real cluster deployment is reported. The delay model uses a simplified memory-bandwidth-bound decode formula without accounting for batching dynamics, KV cache eviction, or prefill/decode disaggregation effects present in real serving engines like vLLM or DistServe. - The problem scale tested (up to 20 query types × 20 models × 20 GPU tiers) may not reflect production heterogeneity where hundreds of model variants, spot instance pricing volatility, and network topology constraints interact; the paper does not analyze how solution quality degrades as the action space grows beyond these dimensions. - The MILP formulation and heuristics assume deterministic, separable workload routing fractions and a static planning horizon, ignoring queuing effects, request-level batching, and preemption—factors that substantially affect actual TTFT and generation latency in deployed systems.
Search Results 2604.07472 PDF
#145 — Moderately Innovative (48)

DARE: Diffusion Large Language Models Alignment and Reinforcement Executor

Jingyi Yang, Yuxian Jiang, Xuhao Hu, Shuang Cheng, Biqing Qi, Jing Shao · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 48 as a well-executed infrastructure contribution that consolidates fragmented dLLM post-training codebases into a unified framework, enabling the first controlled cross-algorithm comparisons for diffusion language models. The empirical finding that ELBO-based RL methods exhibit systematic instability compared to one-step approximations is a useful insight. However, the paper introduces no new algorithms or theoretical contributions, and its long-term value is contingent on community adoption and maintenance of an academic open-source project.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Diffusion large language models (dLLMs) — which replace autoregressive left-to-right generation with iterative denoising over masked token sequences — are maturing rapidly, but their post-training ecosystem is severely fragmented. Each RL or alignment method ships as a paper-specific codebase with its own model fork, rollout implementation, reward interface, and evaluation scripts. This fragmentation makes cross-paper comparisons unreliable (algorithmic differences are entangled with execution differences), raises the engineering burden for reproducing prior work, and makes it impossible to reuse LLM RL frameworks directly, since those assume sequential decoding, exact sequence log-probabilities, and autoregressive serving engines. dLLMs instead require diffusion-aware forward/reverse processes, ELBO-style likelihood surrogates, and model-family-specific rollout backends. Methodology: DARE is a unified post-training and evaluation framework built on top of verl (for distributed training) and OpenCompass (for evaluation), extended with dLLM-specific execution layers. It supports two dominant dLLM paradigms: masked diffusion language models (MDLMs: LLaDA, Dream, LLaDA-MoE) and block diffusion language models (BDLMs: SDAR, LLaDA2.0, LLaDA2.1). The unified training executor is structured around three abstractions — workers (rollout, actor, reward/verifier, reference policy, critic), dataflow (how prompts, responses, rewards, and log-probability signals move), and workflow (the outer PPO-style optimization loop). Algorithm-specific logic is confined to a small set of plug-in hooks (forward corruption process, trajectory construction, likelihood estimator, policy loss), while rollout orchestration, reward dispatch, checkpointing, and evaluation are shared. DARE integrates eight RL algorithms (VRPO, D1, Coupled-GRPO, MDPO, CJ-GRPO, SPG, BGPO, EBPO) plus SFT, PEFT, and preference optimization. Systems optimization is treated as model-aware: for MDLMs, training uses flash_attn_varlen_func to eliminate padding overhead, while rollout decouples backends and combines Fast-dLLM with KV-cache reuse. For BDLMs, rollout is accelerated via LMDeploy/SGLang and training uses FlexAttention with fused cross-entropy kernels. Key Results: On the systems side, switching MDLM SFT from eager execution to flash_attn_varlen_func reduces per-iteration latency from ~22.1s to ~10.8s (≈2× speedup). Rollout optimization is even more impactful: Fast-dLLM with KV-cache drops rollout latency from ~161.6s to ~73.4s (≈2.2×), yielding an end-to-end RL pipeline speedup of ~4× for the MDLM path. For the BDLM path, DARE achieves >14× RL pipeline acceleration. On algorithm comparisons, no single method dominates across all tasks: for LLaDA-8B-Instruct, CJ-GRPO is best on GSM8K (85.6 vs. 76.5 baseline), Coupled-GRPO leads on MATH (41.0 vs. 34.6 baseline) and Countdown (77.9 vs. 16.8 baseline), VRPO leads on code (HumanEval: 52.4 vs. 46.9 baseline), and BGPO leads on Sudoku (42.6 vs. 26.2 baseline). Training curve analysis reveals that ELBO-based methods (SPG, BGPO) are more prone to reward collapse due to high variance in Monte Carlo ELBO estimates under limited sample budgets, while D1, Coupled-GRPO, and CJ-GRPO exhibit more stable optimization. BGPO shows late-stage collapse on LLaDA math; SPG degrades sharply on Dream math and code. Coupled-GRPO and CJ-GRPO are identified as the best stability-effectiveness tradeoffs. Strengths: - Directly addresses a real infrastructure gap: consolidating 8+ paper-specific dLLM RL codebases into one executor with shared rollout, reward, and evaluation, enabling genuinely controlled algorithm comparison for the first time. - Model-aware systems optimization is technically principled — decoupling rollout and training attention backends for MDLMs is a non-obvious but important design choice that yields substantial end-to-end speedups (4× for MDLMs, >14× for BDLMs). - The empirical findings are practically useful: revealing that ELBO-based RL methods are systematically more unstable than ELBO-inspired one-step methods is an actionable insight for algorithm designers, not merely a benchmark leaderboard. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper is primarily an infrastructure contribution with no new algorithmic ideas; its value depends entirely on sustained maintenance and community adoption, neither of which is guaranteed for academic open-source projects. - Algorithm comparisons are conducted only on LLaDA and Dream backbones for the RL experiments — SDAR and LLaDA2.x results are limited to baseline benchmarking, leaving it unclear whether the stability and ranking findings generalize to block diffusion models. - The evaluation scope is narrow (math, code, and two planning tasks), and the fixed-length generation constraint of MDLMs (acknowledged as a limitation) is not addressed within DARE itself, limiting its coverage of emerging variable-length dLLM methods.
Recommended 2604.04215 PDF
#146 — Moderately Innovative (48)

Tool Retrieval Bridge: Aligning Vague Instructions with Retriever Preferences via Bridge Model

Kunfeng Chen, Luyao Zhuang, Fei Liao, Juhua Liu, Jian Wang, Bo Du · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The paper makes a practically relevant observation that academic tool-retrieval benchmarks use unrealistically specific instructions, and the retriever-agnostic bridge model design is sensible engineering. However, the core methodology — SFT followed by DPO on a rewriter model — applies well-established techniques to a straightforward rewriting task, and the synthetic benchmark (GPT-4o rewrites) introduces distributional biases that weaken claims about real-world applicability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Tool learning for LLMs relies on retrieving relevant tools from large repositories before invoking them. Existing tool retrieval benchmarks (e.g., ToolBench) are generated by LLMs prompted with API names and parameters, producing instructions that are overly specific — they mention exact API names, parameter names, and technical details. Real users, by contrast, describe goals in vague, outcome-focused language (e.g., "find me a list of all available coupons" rather than "fetch all coupons using the 'coupons' API"). This benchmark-to-real-world style gap is critical but under-explored. The paper quantifies the damage: across four retrievers and three ToolBench subsets, vague instructions cause up to 50.87% relative NDCG drops compared to the detailed benchmark instructions. Methodology: The paper makes two contributions. First, it constructs VGToolBench by prompting GPT-4o to rewrite all ~196K ToolBench instructions to remove tool-specific information while preserving intent, yielding a one-to-one vague counterpart for every ToolBench example across I1 (single-tool), I2 (intra-category multi-tool), and I3 (inter-category multi-tool) splits. Second, it proposes the Tool Retrieval Bridge (TRB): a lightweight bridge model (LLaMA-3.2-3B) inserted before any retriever that rewrites a vague instruction into a more specific one. Training proceeds in two stages: (1) Supervised fine-tuning on (vague instruction → original ToolBench instruction) pairs using a standard language modeling loss; (2) DPO-based reinforcement learning where N=4 candidate rewrites are sampled per query, each scored by the retriever using average NDCG@5 and NDCG@10 against ground-truth tools, and the highest/lowest scoring pair forms a preference tuple. The DPO loss then pushes the model toward rewrites that directly improve retrieval rank quality. PPO and GRPO were explored but found inferior in practice, attributed to sparse reward signals. Key Results: TRB is evaluated on four retrievers: BM25, TF-IDF, AdaEmbedding (OpenAI text-embedding-ada-002), and ToolRetriever (BERT fine-tuned on ToolBench). On VGToolBench, TRB yields relative average NDCG improvements of: BM25 +87%/+111.51%/+36.47% on I1/I2/I3; TF-IDF +36.53%/+45.95%/+55.68%; AdaEmbedding +14.47%/+16.20%/+11.00%; ToolRetriever +14.37%/+21.01%/+18.57%. Notably, BM25+TRB on VGToolBench (NDCG avg. 24.31 on I1) surpasses vanilla BM25 on the original detailed ToolBench (23.64), demonstrating that RL alignment can overcome the vagueness penalty. On the real-world BFCL-live benchmark (human-curated), TRB achieves +4.45% tool retrieval gain and +5.71% tool calling accuracy gain. Ablation confirms that both SFT and RL stages are necessary: SFT-only consistently underperforms the full two-stage model across all retrievers. Strengths: - Identifies and rigorously quantifies a practically important but overlooked gap between academic benchmarks and real-world tool retrieval, with systematic preliminary analysis across four retrievers and three task types. - Retriever-agnostic design: TRB improves all tested retrievers — sparse (BM25, TF-IDF), dense (AdaEmbedding, ToolRetriever), and advanced architectures (hybrid, reranker, ColBERT) — without modifying the retriever itself. - Real-world validation on BFCL-live (human-curated, out-of-distribution) prevents the work from being purely synthetic, and downstream tool-calling accuracy gains (+5.71%) demonstrate pipeline-level value beyond retrieval metrics. Weaknesses / Limitations: - VGToolBench is synthesized by GPT-4o, not collected from real users, introducing systematic synthetic biases (e.g., GPT-4o's particular paraphrasing patterns may not reflect the true distribution of real user vagueness). The enhanced instructions produced by TRB also sometimes inject hallucinated API names (visible in Figure 6), which could mislead retrievers in domains not seen during training. - The bridge model adds measurable inference latency (0.43–1.79s per query depending on model size), which may be prohibitive in high-throughput or latency-sensitive agentic systems. The paper's proposed mitigations (caching, lighter architectures) are left to future work, and no quantitative analysis of throughput impact at scale is provided.
Citations 2604.07816 PDF
#147 — Moderately Innovative (48)

FORGE:Fine-grained Multimodal Evaluation for Manufacturing Scenarios (82 ▲ )

Xiangru Jian, Hao Xu, Wei Pang, Xinjian Zhao, Chengyu Tao, Qixin Zhang, Xikun Zhang, Chao Zhang, Guanzhi Deng, Alex Xue, Juan Du, Tianshu Yu, Garth Tarr, Linqi Song, Qiuzhuang Sun, Dacheng Tao · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 13 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Scores 48 as a competent benchmark contribution that fills a genuine gap in fine-grained manufacturing evaluation with real 3D point clouds. The bottleneck analysis identifying domain knowledge rather than visual grounding as the limiting factor is a useful empirical finding. However, benchmark papers are inherently incremental in methodology, the workpiece diversity is narrow, and the reliance on synthetic defects and lossy multi-view projections limits the practical conclusions that can be drawn.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: The manufacturing sector is increasingly adopting Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) for autonomous decision-making, yet existing benchmarks fail to reflect the rigorous demands of real industrial environments. Current evaluations use coarse-grained semantics (e.g., 'this is a screw') rather than the fine-grained specificity required in practice (e.g., exact model numbers like M10 vs. M20). This gap, compounded by data scarcity and absent 3D modalities, leaves practitioners unable to assess whether MLLMs are ready for deployment in manufacturing pipelines such as mixed-model assembly lines, quality inspection, and material sorting. Methodology: FORGE introduces a multimodal benchmark and dataset comprising ~12,972 samples with real-world 2D images and 3D point clouds of 14 workpiece categories, annotated with fine-grained attributes including exact model numbers. Three tasks are defined: Workpiece Verification (WORKVERI), Structural Surface Inspection (SURFINSP), and Assembly Verification (ASSYVERI). Since general MLLMs lack native 3D encoders, all point cloud data is rendered as three-view (3V) orthographic projections (front, side, top). Evaluation is conducted under three progressively informative settings: Zero-Shot, Reference-Conditioned (Ref-Cond), and In-Context Demonstration (ICD). The benchmark evaluates 18 state-of-the-art MLLMs spanning open- and closed-source families. A systematic bottleneck analysis decouples visual grounding from domain reasoning by testing single-image coordinate-to-letter localization and cross-image correspondence tasks. Additionally, a supervised fine-tuning (SFT) experiment on Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct using an out-of-distribution scenario split validates the dataset as a training resource. Key Results: Across all 18 models, performance on model-number-level subtasks is substantially lower than on workpiece-type-level subtasks, confirming that fine-grained discrimination remains unsolved. On WORKVERI image zero-shot, top closed-source models (GPT-5: 74.72%, Gemini-3-Flash: 72.22%, O3: 76.18%) fare reasonably for workpiece-type detection but drop significantly on model-number-level tasks (e.g., GPT-5 drops from ~99% on workpiece-type to ~52% on model-number). SURFINSP is near-random for most models, with even Gemini-3-Flash reaching only 53.6% zero-shot on crack detection and most models collapsing on fine-grained defect types. The bottleneck analysis reveals that visual grounding is not the limiting factor: Gemini-3-Flash achieves 98.9% on single-image grounding and top models exceed 79% on cross-image correspondence. Instead, insufficient domain-specific knowledge is identified as the primary bottleneck. SFT on the 3B Qwen2.5-VL model yields a 90.8% relative improvement (+25.6 points) on WORKVERI three-view zero-shot, bringing a 3B model on par with Qwen3-VL-235B (78x larger), and a 27.1% relative gain on ASSYVERI image. Strengths: - Fills a clear gap: first multimodal manufacturing benchmark with real-world 3D point clouds and model-number-level annotations, directly addressing the coarse-granularity limitation of prior work. - The three-part bottleneck analysis (visual grounding, domain reasoning, 3D rendering vs. text serialization) is methodologically rigorous and produces a actionable insight—domain knowledge, not perception, is the limiting factor—that directly guides future research. - The SFT experiment with out-of-distribution scenario splits demonstrates that the dataset encodes transferable domain knowledge, establishing FORGE as both an evaluation resource and a training resource. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Workpiece diversity is narrow: the dataset focuses heavily on common fasteners (screws, nuts, washers, connectors), which may not represent the breadth of real manufacturing environments including machined parts, castings, or electronic assemblies. - 3D modality is accessed exclusively via multi-view projection, a lossy transformation that discards volumetric information; the benchmark does not evaluate specialized 3D-language models, limiting conclusions about the 3D understanding frontier. - Defects in SURFINSP are synthetically generated via algorithmic deformation, not actual manufacturing defects—this introduces a domain gap that likely explains the near-random model performance and limits practical validity of the surface inspection task.
Recommended 2604.07413 PDF
#148 — Moderately Innovative (47)

Dual-Pool Token-Budget Routing for Cost-Efficient and Reliable LLM Serving

Xunzhuo Liu, Bowei He, Xue Liu, Andy Luo, Haichen Zhang, Huamin Chen · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Dual-pool routing addresses a real production pain point — context window over-provisioning — with an elegantly simple solution. The closed-form savings formula and self-calibrating per-category EMA are practical contributions. However, the core idea (partition fleet by context length) is straightforward systems engineering rather than algorithmic innovation, evaluation relies entirely on simulation rather than real hardware, and the 18-20pp gap between predicted and actual savings is substantial.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Production LLM serving fleets (specifically vLLM) configure every GPU instance for the worst-case context window (e.g., 64K tokens), even though 80–95% of real-world requests fit under 8K tokens. This configuration–traffic mismatch wastes 4–8× throughput capacity: on an A100 serving Llama-3-70B, a 64K context window allows only 16 concurrent sequences versus 128 at 8K. Beyond cost, the mismatch triggers reliability failures — OOM crashes, preemption storms, and request rejections — when the fleet approaches high utilization. Existing mitigations like chunked prefill address compute scheduling but leave the memory provisioning problem unsolved: KV-cache is still allocated for the full sequence context regardless of chunk size. Methodology: The paper proposes dual-pool token-budget routing, a fleet-level dispatch mechanism that partitions a homogeneous vLLM fleet into two specialized pools: a short-context pool (Cmax=8K, 128 concurrent sequences) and a long-context pool (Cmax=64K, 16 sequences). Routing is based on the estimated total token budget Ltotal = ⌈|r|/ĉk⌉ + r.max_output_tokens, where |r| is request byte length and ĉk is a per-category bytes-per-token ratio (code ~3.5, CJK ~2.0, English prose ~4.5). This ratio is learned online via exponential moving average (β=0.95) from usage.prompt_tokens feedback, eliminating the need for a tokenizer at the routing layer. To handle asymmetric routing errors (sending a long request to the short pool causes preemption; the reverse merely wastes some throughput), the router uses a conservative estimate ĉ_route = ĉk − γσ̂k that biases borderline requests toward the long pool. A load-aware spillover mechanism handles burst overloads. The authors also derive a closed-form savings formula: ΔG/G_homo = α(1 − 1/ρ), where α is the short-traffic fraction and ρ is the throughput ratio between pools, enabling pre-deployment cost estimation without simulation. Key Results: Evaluated on 100K-request traces (Azure LLM Inference Dataset and LMSYS-Chat-1M) serving Llama-3-70B on A100s via a discrete-event simulator calibrated against Vidur. On the Azure trace at 1,000 req/s, GPU instances are reduced from 358 to 208 (41.9% reduction, $2.86M/year savings at AWS p4d pricing). On LMSYS, reduction is 31.3% (358→246 GPUs). Preemption drops 5.4× (47.3‰ → 8.7‰), OOM drops 5.3×, and the success rate rises from 99.69% to 99.95%. Latency also improves: P50 TTFT by 33% (0.42s→0.28s) and P99 TTFT by 6% (1.82s→1.71s); TPOT improves 11% at P50 and 7% at P99. Per-category EMA calibration converges within 50 observations per category to within 3.5% of the true ratio, reducing mis-routing from 4.1% (global static) to under 1%. Savings are scale-invariant (38.9–41.9% across 100–2,000 req/s). A case study with Qwen3-235B-A22B on AMD MI300X at 10,000 req/s projects $15.4M/year savings (1,576→1,096 GPUs, 30.5% reduction). The closed-form formula overestimates realized savings by 18pp (60% predicted vs. 42% simulated for Azure) due to queuing effects, integer GPU counts, and load imbalance — the authors frame it correctly as an upper bound for pre-deployment auditing. Strengths: - Addresses cost and reliability simultaneously through a single root-cause fix (configuration–traffic mismatch), achieving co-benefits rather than the usual latency–cost tradeoff. - Self-calibrating token estimation via per-category EMA is practically important: it eliminates the tokenizer dependency at the routing layer, which is a real constraint in multi-model fleets, and handles multilingual traffic (CJK) that a global ratio would systematically misroute. - Closed-form cost model (ΔG/G = α(1−1/ρ)) allows practitioners to estimate savings from observable workload statistics before any infrastructure change, with correct framing as an upper bound. - O(1) dispatch overhead and full composability with PagedAttention, chunked prefill, and prefill–decode disaggregation make this deployable as a thin layer over any existing vLLM fleet. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation relies entirely on simulation (calibrated against Vidur) rather than real hardware experiments; the throughput numbers (µ=11.2 req/s/inst for the short pool) and their ratios ρ are taken as given rather than measured under varying real workload conditions, which may not capture memory bandwidth contention, NCCL communication overhead, or thermal throttling at scale. - The cost model gap (60% predicted vs. 42% actual for Azure, 51% vs. 31% for LMSYS) is explained qualitatively but not fully quantified — the 18–20pp discrepancy from queuing and ceiling effects is substantial and could mislead practitioners who apply the formula to sizing decisions rather than just opportunity auditing. - The two-pool design does not handle the case where output length is unknown or systematically underestimated at request time (e.g., streaming APIs where max_output_tokens is set to the model's maximum as a default), which could route many requests incorrectly to the long pool and erode the α fraction significantly in practice.
Search Results 2604.08075 PDF
#149 — Moderately Innovative (47)

Optimizing RAG Rerankers with LLM Feedback via Reinforcement Learning

Yuhang Wu, Xiangqing Shen, Fanfan Wang, Cangqi Zhou, Zhen Wu, Xinyu Dai, Rui Xia · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 47 because while the sequential MDP formulation for reranking and the reference-anchored deterministic baseline are technically sound contributions, the core idea of using RL to align a retrieval component with downstream task performance is a well-explored direction. The modest absolute gains (1-2 F1 points) requiring substantial training infrastructure (6 GPUs, vLLM deployment) raise questions about practical cost-benefit, and the omission of simpler distillation baselines (DPO-style) leaves the necessity of the full RL machinery undemonstrated.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard RAG pipelines insert a reranker between retrieval and generation to surface the most relevant documents. However, rerankers are trained on static human-annotated relevance labels (optimizing IR metrics like NDCG) in complete isolation from the downstream LLM reader. This creates a fundamental misalignment: topical relevance as measured by IR metrics does not equal generative utility — a long historical article may score higher than a concise factual entry, yet the latter is what the LLM needs to answer correctly. The paper addresses this train/deploy objective mismatch directly. Methodology: The authors propose ReRanking Preference Optimization (RRPO), which recasts document selection as a finite-horizon Markov Decision Process. At each step t, the agent (a pointwise reranker fθ) selects one document from the remaining candidate pool; after k selections, the assembled context is fed to a frozen LLM reader and scored via EM/F1/Hit against ground truth, providing the reward signal. The policy is implemented by normalizing softmax reranker scores over remaining candidates at each step, yielding a proper sequential probability. Training uses a PPO-clip objective with a KL divergence penalty to the reference policy. The key technical contribution is a reference-anchored deterministic baseline: instead of learning a separate critic network (which the authors show fails on discrete, high-variance QA rewards), the value V(st) at step t is computed by rolling out the reference policy greedily for t steps and evaluating the assembled context with the LLM — a learning-free, stable baseline. Advantage estimation uses GAE (γ=0.99, λ=0.95), with sliding-window normalization for further stability. Training uses 4×RTX 3090 for the reranker and 2×A6000 running vLLM for the reader, with retrieval caching and LLM response replay reducing inference calls by ~50%. Key Results: On HotpotQA, RRPO applied to the gte-multilingual-reranker-base raises F1 from 41.23 to 43.22 (+1.99) and EM from 28.24 to 30.03. On AmbigNQ, F1 improves from 48.55 to 49.67 (+1.12) and EM from 35.51 to 36.56. RRPO outperforms RankZephyr (a 7B listwise LLM reranker) on both benchmarks (HotpotQA F1: 46.38 vs. 44.49; AmbigNQ F1: 52.18 vs. 51.58) at the top-3 setting.
