Gains are consistent across encoder-only (jina, bge) and decoder-only (qwen3) base rerankers. RRPO trained with Qwen2.5-3B as supervisor (RRPO-3B) still improves over the baseline and transfers to 7B and 14B readers. When stacked on top of Query2Doc query expansion, RRPO adds further orthogonal gains (HotpotQA F1: 44.10 vs. 42.06 for gte alone). Gains are statistically significant (paired t-test, p < 0.01). On harder multi-hop datasets (2WikiMultiHopQA, MuSiQue), improvements are positive but modest, bounded by BM25 recall. Strengths: - The sequential MDP formulation for reranking is conceptually clean and directly addresses the combinatorial set-utility problem that single-action bandits and listwise approaches handle poorly. - The reference-anchored deterministic baseline is a pragmatic solution to the notorious critic instability in discrete reward RL, avoiding a separate value network entirely while providing a strong, interpretable anchor. - Generalization is thoroughly validated: the policy transfers across model families (Qwen, Llama, GLM), scales (3B–14B), and even proprietary closed-source readers (GPT-4o, Claude-3.5-Sonnet, Gemini-2.5-Flash) without reader-specific fine-tuning. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Performance is hard-bounded by initial retrieval recall: on MuSiQue, where BM25 recall is low, RRPO gains are marginal, and the paper acknowledges it cannot compensate for missing relevant documents in the candidate pool. - The experimental setup uses only BM25 as the initial retriever and relatively small reranker base models; it is unclear how RRPO scales with dense retrievers or large reranker backbones, and the gains on absolute numbers are modest (1–2 F1 points), which may not justify the substantial training infrastructure (6 GPUs, vLLM deployment, multi-epoch RL). - The comparison omits SFT baselines entirely — while the authors provide a methodological justification (no gold passage labels in the DPR setup), this leaves open whether a simple distillation approach using LLM-judged preference pairs (DPO-style) would perform comparably at lower complexity.
Recommended 2604.02091 PDF
#150 — Moderately Innovative (47)

EXAONE 4.5 Technical Report (34 ▲ )

Eunbi Choi, Kibong Choi, Sehyun Chun, Seokhee Hong, Junwon Hwang, Hyojin Jeon, Ahra Jo, Hyunjik Jo, Yeonsik Jo, Joonkee Kim, Seonghwan Kim, Soyeon Kim, Sunkyoung Kim, Yireun Kim, Yongil Kim, Changhun Lee, Haeju Lee, Jinsik Lee, Kyungmin Lee, Sangha Park, Kwangrok Ryoo, Minju Seo, Sejong Yang, Heuiyeen Yeen, Hwan Chang, Stanley Jungkyu Choi, Yejin Choi, Kyubeen Han, Joonwon Jang, Kijeong Jeon, Geunyeong Jeong, Gerrard Jeongwon Jo, Jiyeon Jung, Daeseong Kim, Dohoon Kim, Dohyun Kim, Hyunseo Kim, Minu Kim, Myoungshin Kim, Youchul Kim, Byungoh Ko, Christopher Lee, Edward Hwayoung Lee, Honglak Lee, Jiyoung Lee, Sangeun Lee, Seungwon Lim, Woohyung Lim, Jueun Mun, Jaewoo Park, Jimin Park, Jinho Park, Yongmin Park, Wooseok Seo, Yongwoo Song, Sihyuk Yi, Kyungjae Yoo, Sangyeon Yoon · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 13 Apr 2026
Why this rank: EXAONE 4.5 is a competent large-scale VLM engineering effort achieving strong results at 33B dense scale, but reads as a system report combining established techniques (GQA, 2D RoPE, MTP, DPO, GRPO) rather than introducing methodological novelty. The competitive performance against 7x larger models reflects careful engineering and data curation.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Existing open-weight large language models lack native multimodal (vision-language) capabilities required for industrial applications such as manufacturing quality control, document parsing, and engineering diagnostics. Prior VLMs often rely on small vision encoders (~600M parameters) that require aggressive token reduction, degrading spatial and semantic fidelity. EXAONE 4.5 addresses this gap by introducing LG's first open-weight Vision-Language Model (VLM), built on top of the EXAONE 4.0 32B hybrid reasoning backbone, targeting industrial deployment scenarios with emphasis on document understanding, multilingual capability (6 languages), and long-context reasoning up to 256K tokens. Methodology: EXAONE 4.5 integrates a custom-trained 1.2B-parameter vision encoder—built from scratch using an autoregressive objective inspired by OpenVision2—into the EXAONE 4.0 32B language decoder. Key architectural choices include: (1) Grouped Query Attention (GQA) applied within the vision encoder (unusual, as GQA is typically decoder-only) to improve hardware utilization and reduce attention complexity; (2) 2D Rotary Positional Embeddings (2D RoPE) in the vision encoder to capture spatial image structure, while the language decoder retains standard 1D RoPE; (3) Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) from K-EXAONE to improve decoding throughput (disabled at inference); and (4) native resolution image processing via an MLP projector. Pre-training proceeds in two stages—Stage 1 (1.57×10²³ FLOPs, 420B image + 400B text tokens) for foundational modality alignment, and Stage 2 (6.43×10²² FLOPs) for perceptual refinement emphasizing documents, OCR, and STEM content. Data curation is domain-specific: bilingual image captions, interleaved web documents, OCR/document parsing tasks, grounding/counting with normalized bounding boxes, STEM/reasoning with Long CoT data, and a specialized Korean multimodal corpus. Context extension to 256K tokens is achieved within the SFT stage using Context Parallelism, leveraging the 128K-capable base LLM as a stable initialization. Post-training combines SFT (multilingual, multi-domain, NON-REASONING + REASONING modes), offline preference optimization (DPO for vision tasks, GROUPER for text tasks, β=0.1), and multimodal GRPO-based RL with task-specific vision reward functions and zero-variance filtering. Key Results: On vision benchmarks, EXAONE 4.5 33B (dense) outperforms Qwen3-VL-235B (7× larger, MoE) on MATH-VISION (75.2 vs. 74.6), WE-MATH (79.1 vs. 74.8), CHARXIV-RQ (71.7 vs. 66.1), and BLINK (68.8 vs. 67.1). It also surpasses the closed-weight GPT-5 mini on MMMU-PRO (68.6 vs. 67.3), MATH-VISION (75.2 vs. 71.9), AI2D (89.0 vs. 88.2), and OMNIDOCBENCH v1.5 (81.2 vs. 77.0). On language benchmarks, EXAONE 4.5 achieves the top score on LiveCodeBench V6 (81.4) across all compared models, and ranks second on AIME 2026 (92.6) behind only Qwen3.5-27B (93.2). On agentic tool use (τ²-BENCH weighted average), it scores 72.0 vs. 57.0 for Qwen3-VL-235B. Notable weaknesses: BABYVISION (18.8), long-context AA-LCR (50.6), and IFBENCH instruction following (62.6) all lag behind much larger models or closed-weight GPT-5 mini. Strengths: - Achieves competitive or superior performance vs. 7× larger MoE models (Qwen3-VL-235B) and strong closed-weight models (GPT-5 mini) on document understanding and mathematical vision tasks at 33B dense scale, demonstrating strong compute efficiency. - Custom 1.2B vision encoder trained from scratch with GQA and 2D RoPE avoids the token truncation bottleneck of smaller encoders without sacrificing deployment efficiency. - Integrated context extension within SFT (rather than as a separate phase) produces stable 256K-token capability; the approach is architecturally principled given the 128K base LLM initialization. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Long-context understanding (AA-LCR: 50.6) and advanced instruction following (IFBENCH: 62.6, IFEVAL: 89.6) significantly trail GPT-5 mini (68.0 and 74.0/92.8) and Qwen3.5-27B (67.3 and 76.5/95.0), suggesting the 256K context capability has not yet translated to strong long-context reasoning. - BABYVISION score (18.8) is near-random, well below all baselines including the much smaller Qwen3.5-27B (44.6), indicating fundamental gaps in low-level visual reasoning that the document-centric data focus likely exacerbates. - The NC (non-commercial) license prohibits commercial deployment and competitive model development, sharply limiting practical industrial adoption despite the paper's stated industrial motivation.
Recommended 2604.08644 PDF
#151 — Moderately Innovative (47)

Attention Sink in Transformers: A Survey on Utilization, Interpretation, and Mitigation (55 ▲ )

Zunhai Su, Hengyuan Zhang, Wei Wu, Yifan Zhang, Yaxiu Liu, He Xiao, Qingyao Yang, Yuxuan Sun, Rui Yang, Chao Zhang, Keyu Fan, Weihao Ye, Jing Xiong, Hui Shen, Chaofan Tao, Taiqiang Wu, Zhongwei Wan, Yulei Qian, Yuchen Xie, Ngai Wong · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 14 Apr 2026
Why this rank: A well-executed first comprehensive survey of the attention sink phenomenon across Transformer families, offering a useful three-axis taxonomy (utilization, interpretation, mitigation) and practical decision trees for practitioners. However, as a purely taxonomic and synthetic contribution with no new experiments, benchmarks, or theoretical results, it represents organizational rather than methodological novelty and builds entirely on existing work.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Attention Sink (AS) is a pervasive phenomenon in Transformer architectures wherein a disproportionate fraction of attention mass concentrates on a small subset of specific yet semantically uninformative tokens — typically the first few tokens (BOS, initial positional tokens) in autoregressive LLMs, or low-information background patches in Vision Transformers. AS distorts training and inference dynamics, complicates mechanistic interpretability, and exacerbates downstream failure modes including hallucinations, adversarial vulnerability, and KV cache inefficiency. Despite rapid proliferation of AS-related studies across NLP, vision, and multimodal domains, no systematic synthesis existed prior to this work. The paper fills this gap with the first comprehensive survey of the field. Methodology: The authors survey over 180 papers and organize the AS literature around three orthogonal dimensions. (1) Fundamental Utilization (§3) — how practitioners exploit AS in existing systems: Sink Token Preservation (retaining sink tokens as stable KV cache anchors for streaming LLM inference, e.g., StreamingLLM), Attention Redistribution (explicitly shifting attention mass from sinks to semantically informative tokens, e.g., via full/partial redistribution or adaptive calibration such as ZeroTuning and ACT), Learnable Prefix Tokens (inserting trainable tokens that absorb excess attention during training, e.g., register tokens in DINOv2/VGGT, CushionCache for quantization), and Sink Token Repurposing (exploiting sink representations for novel purposes such as backdoor detection, adversarial robustness, geometric token selection). (2) Mechanistic Interpretation (§4) — four causal theories: Softmax Limitations and No-Op Theory (softmax forces attention allocation even for irrelevant tokens, producing no-op sinks), Outlier Circuits (massive scalar activation anomalies at specific hidden dimensions bias attention logits toward sink positions), Implicit Attention Bias (model weights and positional encodings implicitly favor certain positions), and Geometric Anchoring (sink tokens function as stable centroids in representation space). (3) Strategic Mitigation (§5) — architectural approaches to suppress AS at the source: Gated Attention Mechanisms (input-dependent multiplicative gates that suppress unwanted sinks, e.g., GLA, Hawk, DeltaNet variants), Modified Softmax Functions (capped softmax, sparsemax, temperature scaling, or attention entropy regularization), Learnable Attention Bias (explicit bias terms added to attention logits to redirect mass away from sinks), Pre-training Interventions (auxiliary outlier-penalization losses, outlier-safe optimizers, gated normalization), and hybrid approaches. The survey also maps each technique to nine application domains with actionable practitioner guidelines. Key Results: As a survey, the paper does not report new experimental benchmarks. Its principal contributions are taxonomic and synthetic. The authors identify that AS manifests differently across architectures: in autoregressive LLMs it arises at positional token 0/1 due to causal masking and Softmax constraints; in ViTs it emerges on background patches; in MLLMs it appears on both visual and textual sink positions, contributing to visual hallucinations. Mitigation strategies requiring training from scratch (Gated Attention, Modified Softmax) consistently reduce or eliminate AS but are impractical for already-trained models. Preservation-based strategies (retaining sink tokens in KV cache) enable 4-8× cache compression in LLM inference with minimal perplexity loss according to cited work. Learnable prefix/register tokens improve dense prediction and attention map quality in ViTs (e.g., DINOv2 with registers shows cleaner attention maps and improved segmentation performance). The survey synthesizes practical decision trees: for KV cache compression, preserve initial AS tokens; for hallucination reduction, apply attention redistribution to visual sinks; for quantization, inject learnable prefix tokens to absorb activation outliers. Strengths: - First unified survey of AS across all major Transformer families (LLMs, MLLMs, MoE, ViTs, diffusion transformers, 3D transformers), covering 180+ papers with a principled 3-axis taxonomy that is both conceptually coherent and operationally useful for practitioners - The application guidelines section (§6) translates abstract mechanisms into concrete, architecture- and task-specific recommendations, providing immediate value to engineers dealing with KV cache compression, hallucination, quantization, and adversarial robustness - Insightful synthesis of mechanistic theories — particularly the connection between Outlier Circuits, Softmax constraints, and implicit positional bias — provides a more complete causal picture than any single prior paper, and correctly identifies that these are complementary rather than competing explanations Weaknesses / Limitations: - The survey lacks meta-analysis or empirical cross-comparison: it does not quantify which mitigation strategies deliver the best accuracy/overhead tradeoff across standardized tasks, leaving practitioners without a principled basis for method selection beyond qualitative guidance - Coverage of emerging hybrid linear-attention architectures (Mamba, RWKV, RetNet, Hawk) is acknowledged as sparse, which is a significant omission given that these architectures partially address AS by design and represent the next wave of Transformer-adjacent models - The paper's taxonomy conflates methods at different intervention levels (inference-time heuristics vs. pre-training architectural changes vs. fine-tuning adapters) without a unified cost-benefit framework, making it difficult to reason about tradeoffs systematically
Recommended 2604.10098 PDF
#152 — Moderately Innovative (47)

On Optimizing Multimodal Jailbreaks for Spoken Language Models

Aravind Krishnan, Karolina Stańczak, Dietrich Klakow · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: JAMA scores 47 as a competent but largely expected combination of two established gradient-based attack methods (GCG for text, PGD for audio) applied jointly to spoken language models. The finding that multimodal attacks outperform unimodal ones, while empirically validated, is not surprising given the expanded attack surface. The gradient energy analysis and representation subspace findings add mechanistic depth, but the white-box assumption, small training set, and absence of any defense contribution limit the paper's broader impact.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Spoken Language Models (SLMs) that jointly process speech and text inherit safety vulnerabilities from their LLM backbones while also gaining an enlarged attack surface via the audio modality. Prior jailbreak work optimizes a single modality in isolation — typically audio — while leaving the other modality present but unoptimized. This creates a false sense of security: robustness to unimodal adversarial optimization does not imply robustness in multimodal settings, where a real adversary can simultaneously craft malicious text and audio. The paper asks whether jointly optimizing both modalities produces synergistic attacks beyond what either modality achieves alone. Methodology: The authors introduce JAMA (Joint Audio-text Multimodal Attack), a white-box framework that combines two established gradient-based methods: Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) for discrete text suffix optimization and Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) for continuous audio perturbation. At each step, both the text suffix tokens and the imperceptible audio perturbation (ε=0.001, L2 norm) are updated jointly by minimizing a shared loss computed as the average over a batch of Q=8 malicious queries encouraging affirmative model responses. The models' weights are frozen; gradients flow only to the inputs. Four SLMs are evaluated — Audio Flamingo 3, Qwen2 Audio (7B), Gemma 3N (E2B), and Qwen2.5 Omni (7B) — across four audio initialization types (two Switchboard conversational speech samples, audiobook, music) using 480 test samples from AdvBench, assessed via LLaMA Guard 3. The authors also analyze gradient energy dynamics during training to motivate a faster sequential variant, SAMA (Sequential Audio-text Multimodal Attack), which runs GCG first (1000 steps) then PGD (1000 steps) without joint conditioning, achieving 4×–6× speedup. Key Results: JAMA surpasses unimodal baselines by 1.5×–10× depending on model and audio type. For Qwen2 Audio with music initialization, the GCG-only baseline already reaches ~91% jailbreak rate, so JAMA primarily acts as a saturating amplifier. More striking is Gemma 3N, which resists GCG-only at 3.0% and PGD-only at modest rates, but JAMA with music initialization and long PGD sequences drives jailbreak rates to 68%+. Gradient energy analysis shows GCG dominates early optimization (sharp loss drop from discrete token updates), followed by PGD-driven smooth refinement — the temporal ordering motivating SAMA. t-SNE and PCA analyses of last-layer representations confirm that GCG and PGD attacks occupy separable subspaces, JAMA occupies a distinct third subspace, and a 2-component linear classifier separates all four conditions (benign, GCG-only, PGD-only, joint) at 99% accuracy. Music audio consistently outperforms speech-based PGD initialization, attributed to broader frequency coverage. SAMA achieves within ~10% of JAMA's jailbreak rate on average at large GCG/PGD lengths while requiring 4×–6× less compute on a single H100 GPU; in some Gemma 3N configurations with switchboard audio, SAMA even outperforms JAMA by ~20%, likely by avoiding opposing gradient interactions. Strengths: - Clean conceptual framing: the observation that unimodal safety benchmarks overestimate multimodal robustness is well-motivated and the experimental design directly tests this claim across four diverse SLMs. - Rigorous analysis beyond attack success rates: gradient energy dynamics, t-SNE/PCA representation analysis, and training loss curves together build a coherent mechanistic picture of how JAMA operates. - SAMA provides a practical contribution — a nearly-as-effective attack that is substantially cheaper, making future multimodal adversarial evaluation more accessible. Weaknesses / Limitations: - White-box assumption limits real-world applicability: JAMA requires full gradient access to model weights and differentiable feature extractors, which is unavailable for deployed commercial SLMs; the authors had to rewrite numpy spectrogram extractors in PyTorch for compatibility. - Training on only 8 samples and evaluating on AdvBench may not generalize to more diverse harmful query distributions; the small training set and high variance (standard errors often ±10–15%) weaken confidence in precise jailbreak rate comparisons. - The paper evaluates attack efficacy but offers no corresponding defense contributions — it diagnoses a serious vulnerability without proposing or evaluating mitigations, leaving the gap between offense and defense unaddressed.
Recommended 2603.19127 PDF
#153 — Moderately Innovative (47)

Hybrid Multi-Source RAG for Context-Aware Conversational Data Access

ceur-ws.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: The late-fusion architecture combining vector and graph RAG with prompt-driven orchestration is a practical and interpretable design, and the entity resolution subroutine addresses a real problem with Freebase-style identifiers. However, the core idea of combining structured and unstructured retrieval is well-explored, the evaluation relies on LLM-as-a-Judge with the same model family, and the benchmark is small.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard RAG systems rely exclusively on dense vector retrieval over unstructured text, which handles semantic similarity well but cannot perform explicit relational reasoning, multi-hop traversal, or precise entity resolution. Pure graph-based RAG fills that gap but produces rigid, sometimes uninterpretable outputs and struggles with underspecified queries. The paper addresses how to unify both modalities in a single conversational agent without joint embedding or reranking, which blur interpretability, or static merging rules, which lack adaptability. Methodology: The authors propose a modular late-fusion RAG architecture with three components: (1) a Vector RAG pipeline that embeds queries with a transformer model and retrieves top-k chunks via approximate nearest-neighbor search from a Qdrant vector store; (2) a Graph RAG pipeline that translates natural-language queries into Cypher queries via prompt-guided LLM reasoning (Llama-4-Scout-17B) and executes them against a Neo4j knowledge graph; and (3) an Entity Resolution subroutine that detects raw Freebase-style identifiers in graph outputs and issues a narrowly scoped follow-up Cypher query to replace them with canonical human-readable labels. A prompt-driven orchestration layer (implemented in Flowise) runs both pipelines sequentially, then feeds their outputs to a final LLM that merges, selects, or falls back according to a few explicit rules encoded in a prompt (merge when both are relevant, prefer the more specific source in conflict, fall back to vector when graph returns nothing). No additional model training is performed; all adaptation is through prompt engineering. The system was evaluated on a manually curated set of ~100 query–answer pairs derived from WikiGraphs (Wikipedia articles paired with Freebase subgraphs in three complexity variants: max256, max512, max1024), plus an additional ~80 pairs for the max1024 configuration. Scoring used LLM-as-a-Judge (Llama-4-Scout-17B) on relevance, faithfulness, and fluency (1–10 normalized to 0–1). Key Results: Hybrid RAG outperforms both single-source baselines on the primary dimensions: relevance 0.92 vs. Vector RAG 0.71 and Graph RAG 0.68; faithfulness 0.90 vs. Graph RAG 0.88 and Vector RAG 0.75; fluency 0.67, comparable to Vector RAG 0.68 and above Graph RAG 0.57. Crucially, Hybrid RAG achieves the lowest variance across all three metrics (relevance var 0.039, faithfulness var 0.035, fluency var 0.024), indicating more consistent behavior. Stratified by query type, Hybrid RAG dominates on relation-centric queries (relevance 0.955) and adversarial/underspecified queries (faithfulness 0.960), where each single-source method has a clear weakness. Latency for the orchestration/generation stage remains stable across dataset scales (1105 ms / 1065 ms / 1097 ms for max256/512/1024). Full-scale Hybrid RAG mean latency is ~4.9 s vs. ~1.9 s for Graph RAG and ~0.8 s for Vector RAG, with sub-linear scaling. Strengths: - The late-fusion, prompt-driven orchestration preserves modality independence and interpretability, avoiding the semantic blurring of joint-embedding approaches and the rigidity of static reranking or pruning pipelines. - The Entity Resolution subroutine is a practically important contribution: it cleanly handles the identifier-leakage problem endemic to Freebase-style knowledge graphs without requiring schema changes or post-processing heuristics. - The evaluation is methodologically careful: matched-graph subsets (G_common) are used to isolate the effect of datastore scale from graph-structural variation, and queries span four distinct categories with ground truths drawn from both textual and graph sources to prevent modality bias. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation relies entirely on LLM-as-a-Judge (the same model family used in the pipeline), which introduces circular self-evaluation bias; no human annotation baseline or automatic reference-matching metric (e.g., ROUGE, EM) is reported to cross-validate the LLM scores. - The benchmark is small (~100–180 query–answer pairs total) and domain-specific (WikiGraphs/Freebase general knowledge), so generalization claims to other graph schemas or domain-specific knowledge bases remain unsubstantiated. - Hybrid RAG runs both retrieval pipelines unconditionally in sequence before fusion, incurring Graph RAG's Cypher-generation and traversal latency (~1.9 s) on every query even when the graph will return nothing useful; an early-routing or lazy-activation strategy could cut latency significantly without affecting quality.
Citations
#154 — Moderately Innovative (47)

FAIR Data Workflow Implementation and Assessment for Ion-Exchange Chromatography in Plasma Science

datascience.codata.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: A well-executed engineering contribution delivering end-to-end FAIR workflow artifacts for ion-exchange chromatography with a novel tri-modal assessment approach. However, the workflow is partially implemented at publication time, the FAIR assessments are self-evaluated with visible scoring weights, and the core techniques (JSON schemas, RDF, SHACL, eLabFTW) are established tools assembled rather than invented.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Plasma science is inherently multidisciplinary, involving physicists, chemists, biologists, engineers, and clinicians who must share and interpret data across disciplinary boundaries. Ion-exchange chromatography (IC) is a central analytical method in plasma biotechnology — used to measure reactive oxygen and nitrogen species in plasma-treated liquids for applications in agriculture, food processing, medicine, and microbiology — yet its data workflows were entirely manual, ad hoc, and non-standardized, leaving FAIR compliance near zero. The paper addresses this gap by designing and implementing a structured FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) data workflow for IC in the plasma science context, and rigorously evaluating how much FAIR compliance is achieved at each implementation phase. Methodology: The workflow is decomposed into three sequential phases. Phase I uses Adamant (a JSON schema-based metadata editor) to collect IC measurement metadata using purpose-built schemas (for anion and cation exchange chromatography, grounded in ASTM E1151-93), then stores the resulting JSON files in the electronic laboratory notebook eLabFTW via its REST API. Phase II develops an IC ontology (IC-O) using the Methontology methodology in Protégé v5.6.4; the ontology comprises 671 axioms, 118 class entities, 8 object properties, 5 data properties, and 25 individuals, and is mapped to IC metadata schema fields via IRIs — extending the existing Plasma-O plasma ontology. Phase III serializes the semantically enriched metadata as RDF triples and imports them into an ArangoDB graph database, where nodes correspond to ontology classes and edges to ontology relationships, enabling cross-domain queries linking plasma source parameters to IC measurement outcomes. FAIR compliance is evaluated three ways: (1) manual checklist against the 15 Wilkinson et al. FAIR principles; (2) the ARDC 12-question self-assessment tool; and (3) the Data Stewardship Wizard (DSW) with the Common Knowledge Model v2.6.10 (~200 questions). Key Results: For the current implementation (Phase I complete, Phase II ontology finalized but not yet fully integrated, Phase III not yet deployed): the manual FAIR principle review shows all Findability (F1-F4) and all Accessibility (A1, A1.1, A1.2, A2) criteria satisfied, while all three Interoperability criteria (I1-I3) and two of four Reusability criteria (R1.1 provenance, R1.2 licensing) remain unmet. The ARDC assessment scores the current implementation at 43% overall (Findability 76%, Accessibility 20%, Interoperability 35%, Reusability 35%), and projects 83% upon full implementation. The DSW assessment — which covers more ground through ~200 questions — scores the current state at 67% findability, 62% accessibility, 75% interoperability, and 69% reusability, with the full implementation projected to reach 80–100% across all sectors. The discrepancy between tools is significant: the ARDC tool penalizes heavily for absent Dublin Core / DDI schema adoption and local data storage, while the DSW rewards partial ontology use and existing metadata richness. Neither tool projects 100% compliance even for full implementation, largely because full W3C provenance (PROV-O) and complete decoupling of measurement data from local LIMS storage are not planned. Strengths: - End-to-end workflow with concrete, publicly released artifacts (metadata schemas on GitHub/Zenodo, IC-O ontology, Python scripts) that the target community can adopt immediately - Tri-modal FAIR assessment (Wilkinson principles + ARDC + DSW) provides a nuanced, multi-granularity view of compliance gaps and is more informative than any single tool alone - Domain-specific IC ontology (671 axioms, 118 classes) extends the existing Plasma-O, enabling cause-and-effect knowledge graph queries linking plasma source configuration to IC-measured ionic composition — a genuine enabler of interdisciplinary plasma research Weaknesses / Limitations: - The workflow is still partially implemented: Phase III (ArangoDB graph database) and the automated reproducible data evaluation pipeline are not yet deployed, meaning the interoperability and reusability benefits remain largely aspirational at the time of publication - The FAIR self-assessments were performed by the workflow authors themselves, and the DSW tool exposes scoring weights during questionnaire completion, introducing acknowledged bias that could inflate scores — no independent or external evaluation is reported - Measurement data remains on local device LIMS storage and is not yet published in open repositories with proper licensing and provenance, meaning the reusability and accessibility gaps are real operational deficiencies, not just assessment artifacts
Search Results
#155 — Moderately Innovative (47)

KG4CUT: an ontology to facilitate cutting tool selection and interoperability

link.springer.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: KG4CUT contributes a process-oriented MillingTask-as-transformation model that advances beyond static taxonomic ontologies, and the end-to-end pipeline from PDF catalogue ingestion to queryable knowledge graph demonstrates practical deployability. However, the core methodology applies established ontology engineering (LOT, OWL 2, HermiT) to a narrow domain (solid carbide end mills from two vendors), and the purely symbolic reasoning with vendor-specified parameters limits adaptability beyond catalogued conditions.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Selecting cutting tools for milling is a knowledge-intensive task governed by the interplay of tool geometry, workpiece material, machine capabilities, and operational constraints. The problem is exacerbated by a fragmented landscape of vendor-specific PDF catalogues that use inconsistent nomenclature, making cross-vendor comparison difficult—especially for less-experienced operators facing a skilled-labor shortage. Existing manufacturing ontologies (MASON, MaRCO, InVor, MSDL) are either too high-level to capture fine-grained tool geometry and cutting parameters or focused on machine-tool allocation rather than cutting-tool selection. No publicly available, FAIR-compliant, OWL ontology existed specifically for milling cutting tool representation and recommendation. Methodology: The authors develop KG4CUT, an OWL 2 application ontology for solid carbide end mills, following the Linked Open Terms (LOT) methodology through iterative, agile-inspired sprints. The ontology is organized around five main modules: (1) a CuttingTool core hierarchy (MillingTool → SolidEndTool / ModularTool, with subclasses BallNose, Chamfered, Flat-end, Radiused) carrying geometric datatype properties conformant with ISO 13399; (2) a ToolType taxonomy (Rougher, Finisher, Chamfering) enabling multi-role assignment; (3) WorkingPieceMaterial classes organized per ISO 15608 groupings with preferred/optional application distinctions; (4) a MillingTask class modeling machining operations as explicit input-output transformations (MillingTool + CuttingParameters + RawGeometry → FinalGeometry), enabling geometry-driven tool retrieval; and (5) machine-level components reused from the InVor ontology. Logical consistency is enforced by 30 first-order axioms and verified via the HermiT reasoner; structural quality is checked with the OOPS! pitfall scanner. The ontology is published open-source with a persistent w3id.org URI and Widoco-generated documentation. A data-extraction pipeline using pdfplumber parses tabular data from Sandvik Coromant and Kennametal catalogues into RDF triples, producing a queryable knowledge graph. A web application wraps the graph for structured tool search. Validation uses a 21-expert survey across two machining scenarios (groove/slot and cavity), comparing ontology-assisted selection against manual catalogue lookup, using a Good / Partially Effective / Bad outcome categorization. Key Results: In Case 1 (groove/slot), KG4CUT increased "Good" outcomes from 66.7% to 81.0% and reduced "Bad" outcomes from 33.3% to 19.0%—a 42.9% relative error reduction. In Case 2 (cavity), good outcomes improved by 33% relative without increasing errors. Mean task completion time fell from ≈22 min to 8 min in Case 1 (63.6% reduction) and from ≈8 min to 5.5 min in Case 2 (≈30% reduction). Vendor diversity increased: in Case 1, users choosing Kennametal exclusively dropped from 90.5% to 57.1%, a 33.4% increase in cross-vendor consideration. The system received a user satisfaction score of 4.3/5. FAIR compliance scored 75% overall, with Accessibility and Interoperability both above 80%; the weakest dimension was Reusability (0/2 on provenance metadata). 15 of 21 participants expressed interest in adopting KG4CUT in their organization. The work advances the ontology from TRL 1-2 to TRL 4. Strengths: - Process-oriented modeling of MillingTask as a transformation (inputs: tool + parameters + raw geometry; output: final geometry) is a genuine conceptual contribution over prior static taxonomic ontologies, enabling geometry-driven SPARQL queries. - Grounded integration of two ISO standards (ISO 13399 for tool parameters, ISO 15608 for materials) directly addresses the cross-vendor nomenclature inconsistency that makes manual catalogue use error-prone. - End-to-end pipeline—from PDF catalogue ingestion through RDF population to a query interface—demonstrates practical deployability beyond a purely theoretical ontology contribution. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation cohort of 21 participants is small, drawn from a convenience sample in two European countries; statistical power is insufficient to support strong generalization claims. The reported percentage improvements are computed on small absolute counts (e.g., 14/21 tools), inflating apparent effect sizes. - Scope is restricted to solid carbide end mills from only two vendors; modular tools, turning, drilling, and grinding tools are excluded. The ontology is inherently static and cannot represent dynamic phenomena such as tool wear, cutting forces, or thermal distributions, limiting applicability for real-time or physics-coupled decision support. - The reasoning framework is purely symbolic/taxonomic. Cutting parameter recommendations are vendor-specified ranges retrieved verbatim from catalogues rather than computed from physics-based or data-driven models, meaning the system cannot adapt to novel workpiece conditions outside the catalogue's documented scope.

#156 — Moderately Innovative (47)

QuanBench+: A Unified Multi-Framework Benchmark for LLM-Based Quantum Code Generation (109 ▲ )

Ali Slim, Haydar Hamieh, Jawad Kotaich, Yehya Ghosn, Mahdi Chehimi, Ammar Mohanna, Hasan Abed Al Kader Hammoud, Bernard Ghanem · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 14 Apr 2026
Why this rank: QuanBench+ makes a sound methodological contribution by controlling task intent across three quantum frameworks to disentangle API familiarity from quantum reasoning ability, and the KL-divergence acceptance criterion with empirically calibrated thresholds is well-motivated. However, it is an incremental extension of the existing QuanBench benchmark with only 42 tasks, workshop-level scope, and no quantum-specific models evaluated.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Evaluating LLMs on quantum code generation has been hampered by single-framework benchmarks (Qiskit HumanEval, QHackBench, QuanBench), which conflate two distinct failure modes: poor quantum reasoning versus poor API familiarity with a specific framework. Without controlling task intent across frameworks, it is impossible to attribute failures to one cause or the other. This paper fills that gap by introducing a unified benchmark that holds task objectives constant while varying the target quantum programming framework. Methodology: QuanBench+ adapts 42 tasks from the original QuanBench benchmark into framework-aligned prompts for Qiskit, PennyLane, and Cirq. Tasks span three categories: Quantum Algorithms (31), State Preparation (6), and Gate Decomposition (5). Correctness is evaluated via executable functional tests using Pass@1 and Pass@5; for probabilistic outputs, KL divergence between the generated and canonical measurement distributions serves as the acceptance criterion, with a threshold τ=0.05 calibrated from 1000 repeated canonical executions at the 99.7th percentile. Unitary fidelity is explicitly excluded as a metric because functionally equivalent circuits can differ globally in their unitaries. Twelve frontier and open-weight LLMs are evaluated (GPT-5.1, Gemini 3 Pro, DeepSeek R1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, etc.) with greedy decoding for Pass@1 and temperature 0.8 for Pass@5. A feedback-based repair loop (up to 5 attempts) is also evaluated, feeding runtime exceptions or wrong-answer signals back to the model for correction. Key Results: One-shot Pass@1 peaks at 59.5% (Gemini 3 Pro, Qiskit), 54.8% (Gemini 3 Pro, Cirq), and 42.9% (GPT-5.1, PennyLane), confirming a consistent framework difficulty ordering: Qiskit easiest, PennyLane hardest, Cirq intermediate. With up to 5 feedback repair rounds, the best scores rise to 83.3% (GPT-5.1, Qiskit), 76.2% (Gemini 3 Pro, Cirq), and 66.7% (GPT-5.1 and Gemini 3 Pro, PennyLane). Pass@5 narrows but does not close framework gaps (GPT-5.1 reaches 76.2%/64.3%/57.1% across frameworks). Error analysis reveals that 71.7% of first-attempt failures are semantic (wrong answers 46.7%, logic errors 25.0%), while surface errors (syntax, missing gates, shape mismatches) are secondary. Post-feedback, total wrong tasks drop from 977 to 665, but residual failures are even more semantically concentrated (wrong answers 53.4%, logic errors 22.0%), confirming that feedback fixes coding mistakes but not reasoning gaps. Prefill (providing boilerplate imports and signatures) helps mid-tier models more than frontier models, indicating that setup friction, not deep reasoning, is what prefill addresses. Strengths: - The controlled cross-framework design isolates framework-specific familiarity from portable quantum reasoning, which is a more scientifically valid evaluation than any single-framework benchmark. - The use of KL divergence with empirically calibrated thresholds (from 1000 canonical executions) is a principled, well-motivated approach to handling probabilistic quantum outputs, with a sound argument for excluding unitary fidelity. - The error taxonomy (semantic vs. syntactic vs. framework-use) combined with pre- and post-feedback distributions provides genuine mechanistic insight into where models fail and why repair succeeds or plateaus. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The benchmark is small (42 tasks), heavily skewed toward quantum algorithms (74%), which limits coverage of state preparation and decomposition and may overweight multi-step algorithmic reasoning in aggregate metrics. - The paper is a workshop paper with limited scope: no fine-tuned or quantum-specific models (e.g., Qiskit Code Assistant) are included in the main comparison, and framework versioning (Qiskit v0.46.0, PennyLane v0.43.1) may cause failures attributable to stale API recall rather than reasoning deficits, confounding the very distinction the benchmark aims to make.
Recommended 2604.08570 PDF
#157 — Moderately Innovative (47)

Do diacritics matter? evaluating the impact of arabic diacritics on tokenization and llm benchmarks

aclanthology.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: This paper fills a genuine gap by systematically studying diacritization effects on Arabic LLM tokenization and performance across nine models, and the counterintuitive finding that retaining wild diacritics is harmless challenges conventional preprocessing. However, the methodology is standard benchmarking (zero-shot multiple-choice evaluation) with no novel techniques, and the scope is limited to MSA and zero-shot settings.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Arabic diacritics are orthographic marks that convey phonological, lexical, and morphosyntactic information, but their prevalence varies widely across domains — dense in children's books, poetry, and religious texts, but nearly absent in news and user-generated content. While many Arabic NLP pipelines strip diacritics during preprocessing to sidestep orthographic variation, this practice has unknown consequences for modern LLMs. The paper addresses a critical gap: no prior work had systematically studied how varying degrees of diacritization affect subword tokenization and downstream benchmark performance across a broad set of Arabic LLMs. Methodology: The authors evaluate nine LLMs — five Arabic-centric (AceGPT-v2-8B, ALLaM-7B, Fanar-9B, Jais-13B, Jais-2-8B), three multilingual (Aya-Expanse-8B, Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen3-8B), and GPT-4o — under three controlled diacritization conditions: (i) all diacritics removed (undiac), (ii) naturally occurring diacritics preserved (wild), and (iii) fully diacritized text (full*) produced by a BERT-based morphological disambiguation system with 88.9% word-level accuracy. Evaluation uses three Arabic benchmarks: ArabicMMLU (14,455 samples), ArabCulture (3,482 samples), and AraTrust (522 samples), all run zero-shot. Tokenization analysis measures fertility (subwords per word), token density (characters per subword), tokenization consistency (% equal subword counts, Jaccard similarity), and layer-wise cosine similarities of word representations across diacritization conditions using the Wild2MaxDiacs dataset. Key Results: Fertility increases monotonically from undiac to wild to full across all tokenizers. The Jais tokenizers are worst-affected under full diacritization, reaching fertilities of 9.3–10.1 subwords/word vs. 1.9 undiacritized, approaching character-level tokenization because individual diacritic Unicode characters are absent from their vocabularies. ALLaM (57.5% Arabic vocabulary) and Fanar show the best tokenization robustness to wild diacritics (24.6% and 22.3% equal subword counts undiac-wild). Jaccard similarities confirm near-zero overlap under undiac-full for all models. Layer-wise cosine similarity analysis shows representations converge across layers due to anisotropy, but undiac-full divergence persists at lower layers. On benchmarks, removing wild diacritics has minimal and mostly statistically insignificant impact on performance. Full diacritization, however, consistently and significantly degrades performance across models: macro-average drops from 62.8 (wild) to 56.2 (full*) across benchmarks. Jais Chat (13B) suffers the most — 14.4 points on ArabicMMLU, 9.7 on ArabCulture, 26.2 on AraTrust under full vs. wild. GPT-4o is the most robust, with only a 0.7-point macro drop. Counterintuitively, retaining wild diacritics often yields equal or slightly better performance than stripping them, challenging the conventional preprocessing practice. Strengths: - First systematic, multi-model study connecting tokenization behavior to benchmark performance under controlled diacritization conditions, filling a documented gap in Arabic LLM evaluation. - Rigorous statistical testing (McNemar's test) to distinguish meaningful performance differences from noise, avoiding overclaiming. - Analysis spans both surface-level tokenization metrics and internal representation similarity, providing mechanistic grounding for observed performance degradation. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is strictly zero-shot multiple-choice; it is unknown whether findings generalize to generation tasks, few-shot settings, or fine-tuned models where the model has seen diacritized text in context. - The full* condition relies on an automatic diacritizer with 88.9% word-level accuracy, introducing noise that conflates true diacritization effects with diacritization errors, potentially inflating the measured performance drop. - All three benchmarks are Modern Standard Arabic; the impact of diacritics on dialectal Arabic — where diacritization tools are far less mature and diacritic conventions differ — is entirely unexplored.

#158 — Moderately Innovative (47)

ODUTQA-MDC: A Task for Open-Domain Underspecified Tabular QA with Multi-turn Dialogue-based Clarification

Zhensheng Wang, ZhanTeng Lin, Wenmian Yang, Kun Zhou, Yiquan Zhang, Weijia Jia · arxiv.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: ODUTQA-MDC addresses a genuine practical gap by combining open-domain table retrieval, multiple underspecification types, and dynamic multi-turn clarification into a single benchmark. However, the core techniques—template-based query generation, BIO tagging for slot filling, BM25 retrieval, and multi-agent LLM pipelines—are standard components assembled rather than novel contributions. The narrow domain (Chinese real estate) and artificial constraints on the clarification loop further limit the generalizability and realism of the benchmark.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Real-world tabular question answering systems must handle queries that are underspecified due to spelling errors, vague intent, or missing scope information (e.g., omitting city or district names). Existing text-to-SQL datasets (Spider, WikiSQL) assume well-formed queries, and prior work on underspecification (AmbiQT, Ambrosia, PRACTIQ) is limited to closed-domain settings, static dialogues, or single underspecification types. There is no benchmark that jointly addresses open-domain table retrieval, multiple simultaneous underspecification types, and dynamic multi-turn clarification to resolve them before SQL generation. Methodology: The paper introduces ODUTQA-MDC, a task and benchmark for Open-Domain Underspecified Tabular QA with Multi-turn Dialogue-based Clarification. Three components are provided: (1) ODUTQA, a dataset of 25,105 QA pairs over 209 PostgreSQL tables from real estate, land auction, and enterprise finance domains, constructed by injecting underspecification into 76 templates via template restructuring (SELECT clause vagueness) and value modification (FROM/WHERE clause errors — Missing, Error, Unmatch subtypes); LLM-based paraphrasing with forbidden-keyword constraints preserves the injected ambiguity. (2) A fine-grained annotation scheme with intent/slot BIO labels (from spoken language understanding) plus three underspecification detection labels: binary intent label, triple-based scope label [content, slot_type, error_type], and triple-based condition label. (3) A dynamic clarification interface that gates information release on correct detection, uses an LLM (Qwen2.5-72B) to paraphrase responses naturally, and validates keyword preservation with up to 5 retries before falling back to a fixed template. Building on this, MAIC-TQA is proposed: a four-agent pipeline consisting of (i) an SLU Module (fine-tuned BERT for intent detection and slot filling), (ii) a Scope Validator Agent (LLM with a slotSearchTool that cross-references database table captions), (iii) a Table Retrieval Agent (LLM generates a table caption summary; retrieval proceeds via exact match then BM25), and (iv) a SQL Generation and Validation Agent (LLM with in-context SQL examples; empty results trigger condition underspecification detection and one round of WHERE-clause clarification). All agents share the full dialogue history and iterate clarification requests before final SQL execution. Key Results: On underspecification detection, BERT achieves 99.78% accuracy / 99.22% F1 for intent (SELECT) detection. For scope (FROM) detection, Kimi K2 achieves the highest accuracy (82.60% fixed, 81.63% dynamic) while Doubao Seed 1.6 achieves the best F1 (89.46% fixed, 89.23% dynamic). Mixed underspecification is substantially harder due to error accumulation, with best accuracy of 61.21% (Doubao, fixed). On table retrieval, MAIC-TQA raises F1 from ~60% (SLUTQA baseline) to 89–97% across all tested LLMs. On SQL generation, ECR improves by up to 19% and Pass@1 improves by 33+ percentage points over the clarification-free SLUTQA baseline (e.g., Qwen3 32B: 24.20% → 57.89% Pass@1 in dynamic mode). Ablation shows scope clarification is the most critical for ECR (−16.92% when removed) while condition clarification most impacts Pass@1 (−28.59% when removed). Strengths: - The dataset construction methodology — injecting underspecification programmatically at template level rather than modifying database schemas — cleanly controls underspecification type and enables automatic annotation of all labels, avoiding expensive crowdsourcing. - The dynamic clarification interface provides a principled balance between reproducibility (deterministic what-to-clarify logic gated on gold labels) and linguistic realism (LLM-based paraphrasing with keyword verification), substantially more rigorous than prior static dialogue datasets. - Comprehensive ablation and multi-model evaluation across five LLMs (open- and closed-source) clearly quantifies the contribution of each clarification module and establishes strong baselines for the new task. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The dataset is entirely drawn from a single vertical (Chinese real estate/land auction) with narrow SQL complexity — no GROUP BY, HAVING, or subqueries — limiting the generalizability claims; the gap between template-generated questions and genuine user queries may not be fully bridged by LLM paraphrasing alone. - The user simulator trades behavioral realism for reproducibility: it only provides clarification when the system's detection label exactly matches the ground truth, does not model user confusion, goal drift, or chitchat, and cannot test robustness to errors in the clarification loop itself — a significant gap from real deployment scenarios. - The clarification loop is artificially limited (one iteration per underspecification type), which simplifies the benchmark but does not reflect the multi-round, interleaved clarification common in realistic dialogue.

#159 — Moderately Innovative (46)

S-Eval: Towards Automated Safety Evaluation with Enhancement for Large Language Models

dl.acm.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: S-Eval is an impressive systems-level engineering effort with the most granular published safety taxonomy (102 risk types) and large-scale evaluation (220K test cases, 29 models), but its individual components — fine-tuned evaluator LLMs, jailbreak attack catalogs, and constitutional defense prompting — are extensions of established techniques rather than conceptual innovations. The softmax-derived confidence scoring is an elegant design choice, but the overall contribution is primarily one of scale and integration.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in sensitive domains, their tendency to generate harmful, biased, or illegal content poses serious societal risks. Existing safety evaluation frameworks suffer from four compounding problems: (1) coarse-grained, non-unified risk taxonomies that fail to capture fine-grained safety dimensions; (2) evaluation benchmarks with weak riskiness that rely on multiple-choice formats or incomplete jailbreak coverage; (3) lack of end-to-end automation, requiring expensive manual labor; and (4) no integration of targeted defense mechanisms alongside vulnerability discovery. The gap between rapidly evolving LLMs and static safety benchmarks motivates a fully automated, adaptive, and comprehensive safety evaluation system. Methodology: S-Eval is a three-component automated safety evaluation framework. First, it establishes a four-level hierarchical risk taxonomy spanning 8 top-level dimensions (Crimes and Illegal Activities, Hate Speech, Physical and Mental Health, Ethics and Morality, Data Privacy, Cybersecurity, Extremism, Inappropriate Suggestions) and 102 leaf-node risk types, grounded in global AI safety policies (NIST AI RMF, EU AI Act, Singapore MGF, China CAC). Second, an expert testing LLM (Mt), built by supervised fine-tuning Qwen-14B-Chat on curated risk data, automatically generates both base risk prompts (via definition-based, knowledge-based, and rewriting-based strategies) and attack prompts across 10 jailbreak methods (Positive Induction, Reverse Induction, Code Injection, Instruction Jailbreak, Goal Hijacking, Instruction Encryption, DeepInception, In-Context Attack, Chain of Utterances, Compositional Instruction Attack). Test quality is enforced via a combined semantic similarity + Levenshtein distance filter and multi-model riskiness scoring. Third, a safety critique LLM (Mc), also fine-tuned on Qwen-14B-Chat from a 100K bilingual QA dataset annotated by GPT-4 and reviewed by human experts, outputs safety tags, softmax-derived confidence scores, and natural language explanations. Finally, a two-stage constitutional defense uses a risk classifier (90.5% precision on second-level categories) to retrieve risk-type-specific constitutional principles — each with four components: general safety rules, high-risk scenario prevention, proactive guidance, and principle confidentiality — and injects them non-intrusively into the LLM context at inference time. Key Results: The benchmark comprises 220,000 test cases (20,000 base risk prompts in Chinese/English, 200,000 attack prompts) and was used to evaluate 29 LLMs (>670K queries). Mc achieves balanced accuracy of 92.23% (Chinese) and 88.23% (English), substantially outperforming GPT-4-Turbo (78.00%/72.36%), LLaMA-Guard-2 (76.23%/69.32%), and rule matching (74.12%/70.19%). The benchmark elicits substantially lower safety scores than competing benchmarks (AdvBench, HH-RLHF, Flames, SafetyPrompts, Astral), with 95% score distribution intervals of 30.62%/50.36% vs. AdvBench's 5.74%/7.53%, demonstrating far greater discriminating power. Across models, closed-source LLMs generally outperform open-source ones; ErnieBot-4.0 leads (79.70% Chinese, 87.60% English) while Gemini-1.0-Pro and Yi-34B-Chat are weakest. The Compositional Instruction Attack achieves the highest average attack success rate, and adaptive multi-attack combinations succeed on nearly all models (>95% ASR). Constitutional defense raises GPT-4o from 54.00% to 99.70% safety score in Chinese and DeepSeek-R1 from 30.90% to 99.10% in English, with only 1.70% of defended responses rated inferior to originals and classifier latency of ~50ms (RTX 2080 Ti) to ~10ms (H20). Reasoning LLMs show elevated risk from intermediate chain-of-thought steps, and knowledge distillation substantially degrades safety alignment. Safety degrades monotonically with language resource scarcity, and higher decoding temperature/top-p consistently reduces safety. Strengths: - The four-level, 102-risk taxonomy is the most granular published safety taxonomy aligned to multiple international regulatory frameworks, enabling nuanced per-dimension diagnosis that reveals large within-model variation (e.g., Yi-34B-Chat: 81% on Data Privacy vs. 25% on Ethics and Morality). - Full end-to-end automation — from test generation through evaluation and defense — with adaptive update capability requiring only ~20 seed prompts for new risk types and ~1,000 fine-tuning samples for new attacks, making it practically deployable at Alibaba's production scale (10M+ user queries). - The softmax-derived safety confidence score (probability of the 'safe' token at first decode step) is an elegant design choice: it avoids requiring explicit numerical supervision during fine-tuning while yielding a continuous risk signal with tunable threshold for domain-specific safety requirements. - The constitutional defense achieves near-complete safety recovery across diverse model families (open-source, closed-source, reasoning, distilled) with negligible utility degradation (5.1×10⁻⁴% negative impact on real-world traffic) and without requiring model retraining. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The safety critique model Mc is itself a fine-tuned LLM (Qwen-14B-Chat), meaning its judgment inherits potential biases from its training data and GPT-4 annotations; the 7.77–11.77% balanced accuracy gap below perfect leaves non-trivial false positive/negative rates for industrial use, and the paper does not report Mc's performance breakdown across individual risk dimensions. - Constitutional defense effectiveness degrades for lower-capability models (Qwen3-0.6B, Gemma-7B-it, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-LLaMA-8B remain below other post-defense models) because the mechanism depends on the underlying model's instruction-following ability — a circularity that limits the approach precisely where safety is most deficient. - The benchmark and critique model are bilingual (Chinese/English), and while cross-lingual experiments show safety degrades with lower-resource languages, S-Eval itself does not natively support evaluation or defense in those languages, limiting applicability for global deployment contexts.
Recommended
#160 — Moderately Innovative (46)

DRLLMS: Network-Adaptive Reasoning Control for Interactive LLM Streaming

dl.acm.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: DRLLMS cleanly frames TTFA as the right user-centric metric for reasoning models and the measurement study motivating the work is solid. However, the technical approach—fine-tuning for three discrete CoT length buckets and selecting via a static lookup table—is relatively straightforward, and the coarse discretization, heuristic QoE parameters, and narrow evaluation on small math-only models limit the contribution's depth and generalizability.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Interactive LLM services stream token-by-token responses, but reasoning models (e.g., DeepSeek-R1) front-load lengthy chain-of-thought (CoT) before producing any user-consumable answer. This pre-answer reasoning phase dominates perceived latency—the paper's measurement study on DeepSeek-R1-Distill models shows CoT accounts for the vast majority of total response tokens and that Time-to-First-Answer (TTFA) nearly equals end-to-end latency. Under degraded network conditions (low bandwidth, high latency, packet loss), this bottleneck compounds severely. Existing CoT-length control methods target static or task-centric length reduction and ignore network dynamics entirely; system-level approaches (dynamic batching, MoE routing) treat responses as monolithic units without distinguishing reasoning from answer content. Methodology: DRLLMS introduces a two-stage framework. In the training stage, CoT lengths are first calibrated by sampling QwQ-32B eight times per question and computing the 25th/50th/75th percentiles of resulting CoT lengths, defining three discrete levels: concise, moderate, and detailed. These targets are embedded as natural-language instructions in a chat template. The base model (DeepScaleR-1.5B-Preview) is then fine-tuned on the MATH dataset using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). The reward function combines a binary correctness indicator with a relative length penalty: r = I(y = y*) − |L_response − L_target| / L_target, incentivizing both accuracy and adherence to the length target. In the inference stage, a lightweight grid-search optimizer consults precomputed lookup tables (T_Acc, T_TTFA) indexed by bandwidth and CoT level, scores each option via QoE = α·Acc + (1−α)·e^{−β·TTFA} + C_irr (with α=0.6, β=0.001), and selects the CoT level maximizing QoE given the observed bandwidth. Key Results: The fine-tuned model successfully separates CoT length distributions across concise/moderate/detailed instructions on MATH-500, Olympiad Bench, AMC, and AIME 2025. Accuracy degrades only modestly with shorter CoT: on MATH-500 the model at concise level achieves roughly Pass@1 ≈ 0.65–0.70 versus ~0.75–0.80 at detailed, remaining close to the pre-fine-tuning baseline. QoE table results show that at 16 Mbps, detailed CoT achieves QoE=0.884 (optimizer selects it); at 2 Mbps, moderate wins with QoE=0.856; at 1 Mbps, concise wins with QoE=0.822. TTFA under concise instruction at 1 Mbps is roughly 115s versus 390s for detailed—a ~3.4× reduction. Prompt engineering alone (without RL fine-tuning) is shown to be ineffective for length control, validating the necessity of the post-training step. Strengths: - Clean problem framing: TTFA as a user-centric metric is more meaningful than TTFT for reasoning models, and the measurement study empirically grounding the motivation is thorough. - Practical system design: the inference-time optimizer is extremely lightweight (grid search over 3 options using lookup tables), adding negligible overhead. - The GRPO-based approach avoids a separate value model, making fine-tuning memory-efficient; and the combined reward (correctness + length penalty) is well-motivated. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The lookup tables are built from static, pre-measured network presets rather than from real-time profiling, making the system brittle to network conditions not represented in the table (e.g., fluctuating or novel bandwidth values require interpolation or re-measurement). - The coarse three-level CoT discretization (concise/moderate/detailed) is a significant simplification; task difficulty varies continuously and the paper does not show whether the model can adapt CoT length within a level to actual problem complexity rather than just following the prompt bucket. - Evaluation is limited to small math-focused models (1.5B–7B parameters) on math benchmarks; generalization to open-ended tasks, larger models, or non-math domains is unaddressed, and the QoE hyperparameters (α, β) are set heuristically without sensitivity analysis.
Search Results
#161 — Moderately Innovative (46)

Template-Theorems Graph Construction to Enhance Mathematical Reasoning Capabilities of LLM

ojs.aaai.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: The template-based abstraction layer for theorem retrieval is a reasonable idea that addresses a real gap in semantic vs. structural similarity for math RAG. However, the absolute gains on strong models are modest (1-2 points on MATH500), the OOD evaluation stays within the same math-competition genre, and the multi-LLM pipeline introduces significant construction overhead that is not characterized.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLMs have achieved strong performance on elementary mathematical reasoning but continue to struggle with complex, advanced problems. The core issue is a retrieval-application gap: models acquire extensive mathematical theorem knowledge during pre-training but fail to retrieve and apply the right theorems when solving novel problems. Standard retrieval-augmented approaches using surface-level semantic similarity (cosine similarity, BM25) exacerbate this by matching superficially similar problems rather than structurally equivalent ones — a problem humans solve naturally by recalling analogous experiences and associated theorems from memory. Methodology: The paper proposes a two-component framework: (1) a template-theorems knowledge graph construction pipeline and (2) a graph retrieval-augmented generation mechanism. The knowledge graph stores abstract problem templates (generalized representations stripping away specific numerical/surface details) linked to relevant mathematical theorems. Construction proceeds in two stages: a Base Generation stage uses DeepSeek-R1 with few-shot prompts to extract templates and theorems from a seed dataset (MATH training set), and an Advanced Generation stage uses GPT-4.1 to synthesize new templates across difficulty levels independent of specific problems, boosting diversity and reducing single-model bias. Quality is enforced via answer validation (filtering hallucinated solutions against ground truth), consistency validation (comparing theorems generated from actual vs. template questions via embedding similarity threshold), step quality control (difficulty-conditioned step count filtering), and theorem matching against an authoritative library. At inference time, a query problem is first distilled into abstract templates offline, then the graph is queried via top-k similarity retrieval with a minimum threshold. Retrieved theorems are aggregated and refined into a theorem table, and the most compatible template is re-selected against that table before both are injected as context for the answering LLM. Key Results: Evaluated on five benchmarks spanning in-domain (MATH500) and out-of-domain (MMLU-Pro-Math, AMC10, AMC12, AIME24) settings with GPT-4.1, GPT-4o, and DeepSeek-R1. Against zero-shot and 8-shot CoT baselines: GPT-4.1 improves from 89.2/73.27/85.50/43.2 to 91.4/77.88/86.23/48.4 on MATH/AMC10/AMC12/AIME24. DeepSeek-R1 improves from 96.4/85.25/94.2/70.0 to 97.4/85.71/96.38/76.2. GPT-4o improves from 73.4/55.80/50.72/13.20 to 76.4/58.52/54.35/16.15. Gains are consistent across all models and notably present on OOD datasets. Ablations confirm each pipeline stage contributes meaningfully, with advanced generation being most critical for OOD generalization. A separate experiment shows that combining both template and theorem knowledge outperforms either alone (e.g., AMC10: +Tem=75.58, +The=76.50, +Both=77.88 vs. original 73.27). Efficiency analysis shows reduced inference cost time versus few-shot CoT, attributed to shorter, more targeted context. Strengths: - The problem-template abstraction layer as an intermediate retrieval step is well-motivated: it bridges the structural-similarity gap that pure semantic retrieval misses, and the ablation clearly validates its necessity. - Multi-stage quality control (answer validation, consistency validation, step count filtering, theorem matching) directly addresses LLM hallucination in the KB construction phase — a real and often ignored vulnerability in graph-from-LLM approaches. - Evaluation breadth is commendable: five datasets across difficulty levels, three distinct model families (GPT-4o, GPT-4.1, DeepSeek-R1), both in-domain and OOD settings, with consistent gains throughout. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The knowledge graph is seeded entirely from the MATH training set, and the claimed OOD generalization is still to datasets within the same mathematical reasoning genre (AMC, AIME, MMLU-Pro-Math) — genuine out-of-distribution transfer to different domains (physics, programming) is asserted but not demonstrated. - Absolute gains are modest on already-strong models: e.g., DeepSeek-R1 improves by +1.0 on MATH500 (96.4→97.4) and GPT-4.1 by +2.2. On AIME24 where reasoning is hardest and gains could matter most, the improvement is +5.2 for GPT-4.1 but only +1.5 for GPT-4o, suggesting the method's benefit is uneven and potentially correlated with the backbone's pre-existing theorem recall ability. - The pipeline depends on multiple LLM calls (DeepSeek-R1 for base generation, GPT-4.1 for advanced generation, GPT-4o-mini for evaluation), introducing cascading cost and API dependency that is not fully characterized; the reported efficiency improvement over few-shot reflects inference-time context length reduction but ignores KB construction overhead.
Recommended
#162 — Moderately Innovative (45)

Active Imitation Learning for Thermal-and Kernel-Aware LFM Inference on 3D S-NUCA Many-Cores

Yixian Shen, Chaoyao Shen, Jan Deen, George Floros, Andy Pimentel, Anuj Pathania · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The kernel-specific Oracle design is well-motivated by the empirical finding that different LFM kernels exhibit dramatically different sensitivity to cache distance, and the active learning loop achieves an efficient accuracy-overhead tradeoff. However, the work targets hypothetical 3D S-NUCA hardware that does not exist at the tested scale, making all results simulation-dependent, and the cross-domain combination of techniques (MoGPR, imitation learning, MC Dropout) is competent assembly rather than methodological novelty.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Running Large Foundation Model (LFM) inference on CPUs is increasingly attractive due to GPU scarcity and cost, but 3D-stacked Static Non-Uniform Cache Architecture (3D S-NUCA) many-core processors — which integrate High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM) atop many-core chips to alleviate memory bandwidth bottlenecks — introduce two compounding challenges: severe thermal hotspots from high power density in stacked silicon layers, and non-uniform LLC access latencies that cause micro-architecturally identical cores to exhibit significantly different performance. Existing thermal management approaches rely on oversimplified analytical models or generic machine learning methods not tailored to the kernel-level diversity of LFMs, leaving a gap in jointly optimizing thread migration and DVFS for LFM workloads under thermal safety constraints. Methodology: The paper proposes AILFM, an Active Imitation Learning (AIL) framework that learns a lightweight scheduling policy by selectively querying a computationally expensive but accurate Oracle. The Oracle is a Mixture of Gaussian Process Regression (MoGPR) model with one GPR expert per LFM kernel type (Embedding, Self-Attention, FFN, LM Head). The Oracle predicts migration utility — expected performance gain minus cold-start cache penalty — given source and target core state vectors encoding IPS, LLC MPKI, Average Manhattan Distance (AMD), and power budget. A lightweight fully connected neural network (3 hidden layers, 64/32/32 neurons, found via NAS) serves as the deployable learning policy. Monte Carlo Dropout is applied at inference time to estimate predictive uncertainty; when uncertainty exceeds a threshold τ, the agent queries the Oracle for a labeled action rather than acting autonomously. The training loss combines supervised imitation loss on Oracle-queried states and a self-imitation loss on autonomous decisions. Thermal enforcement relies on DVFS (via the 3D-TTP power budgeting framework) every 100 ns and thread migration decisions every 500 ns. The key insight motivating kernel-specific modeling is empirically validated: Self-Attention IPS degrades >35% from AMD 3.0 to 4.5 due to K/V cache sensitivity, while FFN degrades less than 6.5%, making a single unified model inadequate. Key Results: Experiments run on the CoMeT simulator modeling a 64-core 4×4×4 3D S-NUCA system with 64 GB HBM at 25.6 GB/s, evaluated on five LFMs (BERT-base, ViT-base, LLaMA3.2-1B, Gemma-2B, DeepSeek-2.4B) across input lengths of 128–1024 tokens and thermal thresholds of 75°C and 85°C. AILFM outperforms four baselines — 3QTM (RL-based), NeuroTAP (analytical), DNaPE (cold-core migration heuristic), and DLFM (direct learning without Oracle) — achieving up to 31% better execution performance versus DNaPE at Tth=75°C, and up to 35% better than DNaPE on DeepSeek-2.4B at 1024 tokens. Peak temperature is maintained at approximately 72.5°C under a 75°C threshold (mean), versus NeuroTAP which frequently violates the threshold (77–80°C). AILFM achieves 96.7% migration decision accuracy versus the Oracle's 97.3%, while reducing overhead from 24.6% (Oracle) to 3.2%. Runtime overhead stays below 5% even at full 64-core load. Scalability holds from 64-core to 216-core topologies, with 3D S-NUCA yielding 1.49× and 1.64× speedups respectively versus a 2D planar baseline. Ablations confirm both kernel-aware modeling and active querying are necessary: removing either raises Tpeak by ~5–7°C and degrades accuracy by ~6%. Strengths: - The kernel-specific Oracle design is well-motivated by empirical characterization (Table 1) showing dramatically different AMD sensitivity across LFM kernel types, and the MoGPR achieves lower utility MAE (0.31) and faster execution (79.1s) than Random Forest or MLP alternatives. - The active learning loop is efficient: by using MC Dropout uncertainty to gate Oracle queries, AILFM achieves near-Oracle accuracy (96.7% vs 97.3%) at 7.7× lower overhead (3.2% vs 24.6%), making real-time deployment practical. - Comprehensive ablation study clearly isolates the contribution of each design choice, and scalability experiments across 2D/3D topologies and core counts provide meaningful generalization evidence. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is entirely simulation-based (CoMeT simulator); no real 3D S-NUCA hardware exists at the scale tested, so the thermal model fidelity and actual DVFS/migration overheads on silicon may differ from simulated results. - The training data and Oracle are built from only three LFMs (ViT-base, BERT-base, LLaMA3.2-1B) at a single sequence length (L=256) and thermal threshold (70°C), then tested across five models and varying conditions — the generalization claim would be stronger with a broader and more disjoint train/test split. - The framework assumes model parallelism with fixed sequential kernel execution order, which enables deterministic kernel identification at runtime; this may not hold for more complex LFM serving scenarios involving batching, speculative decoding, or pipeline parallelism.
Search Results 2604.11948 PDF
#163 — Moderately Innovative (45)

Dive into Claude Code: The Design Space of Today's and Future AI Agent Systems (6 ▲ )

Jiacheng Liu, Xiaohan Zhao, Xinyi Shang, Zhiqiang Shen · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: A thorough reverse-engineering study that yields a useful architectural framework (values → principles → implementation) and the interesting observation that 98.4% of the codebase is deterministic infrastructure. The OpenClaw comparison generates genuinely useful design guidance by showing how deployment context drives inverse architectural bets. However, as an analytical case study of an existing system rather than a new method, the novelty is primarily in the framework of analysis rather than in new technical contributions.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Production AI coding agents like Claude Code have grown from autocomplete tools to fully agentic systems that autonomously plan, execute shell commands, read/write files, and iterate — yet Anthropic publishes no detailed architectural documentation. This creates a gap: practitioners building agent systems lack grounded insight into how one widely adopted production system answers recurring design questions about safety, context management, extensibility, and delegation. Prior work has focused on benchmarks and frameworks rather than source-grounded architectural analysis. The paper attempts to close this gap using direct analysis of Claude Code's publicly available TypeScript source (v2.1.88, ~1,884 files, ~512K lines). Methodology: The authors perform a three-tier evidence study: (A) product documentation, (B) code-verified claims citing specific source files and functions, and (C) reconstructed claims from community analysis. They identify five human values motivating the architecture (human decision authority, safety, reliable execution, capability amplification, contextual adaptability), trace them through thirteen design principles, and map those to seven high-level components and five subsystem layers. A running example ('Fix the failing test in auth.test.ts') threads through all subsections. They then compare Claude Code against OpenClaw — an independent open-source multi-channel personal assistant gateway — across six architectural dimensions (system scope, trust model, agent runtime, extensibility, memory, multi-agent routing) to show how the same recurring design questions produce different answers under different deployment contexts. Six open directions for future systems are synthesized from the empirical and architectural literature. Key Results: The central finding is that only ~1.6% of Claude Code's codebase constitutes AI decision logic; the remaining 98.4% is deterministic operational infrastructure (permission gates, tool routing, context management, recovery logic). The architecture gives the model maximum decision latitude within a rich harness rather than constraining its choices via explicit planners or state graphs. Concrete findings include: (1) a deny-first permission system with 7 modes (5 external + auto ML classifier + internal bubble) and seven independent safety layers; (2) a five-layer compaction pipeline (budget reduction → snip → microcompact → context collapse → auto-compact) that executes before every model call; (3) four extensibility mechanisms at graduated context costs (hooks at zero cost, skills at low cost, plugins at medium, MCP servers at high); (4) subagent isolation via separate context windows and sidechain JSONL transcripts, with summary-only returns to parents; (5) append-only JSONL session persistence with deliberate non-restoration of permissions on resume. The OpenClaw comparison reveals inverse architectural bets: Claude Code uses per-action safety evaluation while OpenClaw uses perimeter-level access control; Claude Code treats the agent loop as the system center while OpenClaw embeds the loop inside a gateway control plane. User behavior data shows 93% of permission prompts are approved (approval fatigue), and auto-approve rates rise from ~20% at <50 sessions to >40% by 750 sessions. Causal analysis of adjacent tools found code complexity increases +40.7% (p<0.001) and a 40.7% initial velocity spike that dissipates to baseline by month three. An EEG study found weakened neural connectivity persisting after AI removal.
