Strengths: - Rigorous source-level analysis with specific file/function citations (Tier B evidence), providing verifiable architectural claims rather than inference from documentation alone - The values → principles → implementation tracing framework is coherent and generates testable predictions (e.g., bounded context will produce pattern duplication in generated code) - The OpenClaw comparative analysis concretely demonstrates how deployment context determines architectural choices, yielding generalizable design guidance - Identifies security vulnerabilities (CVE-2025-59536, CVE-2026-21852) arising from pre-trust initialization ordering — a temporal dimension the spatial permission diagram misses - Raises a substantive evaluative concern about long-term human capability atrophy that goes beyond the system's stated values, supported by RCTs, causal studies, and EEG evidence Weaknesses / Limitations: - The analysis is a static snapshot of v2.1.88; feature flags (TRANSCRIPT_CLASSIFIER, CONTEXT_COLLAPSE, etc.) create build-time variability, meaning different production builds may behave substantially differently than what is described - As a reverse-engineering study, it cannot confirm design intent, which production flags are enabled, or runtime prevalence of specific code paths — the gap between source analysis and actual deployed behavior is never bridged empirically - The OpenClaw comparison is asymmetric: Claude Code is a mature commercial product while OpenClaw is an open-source project in active development, making architectural comparisons partly apples-to-oranges - The paper relies almost entirely on third-party studies for empirical claims about outcomes (code quality, cognitive effects), none of which directly study Claude Code — architectural parallelism is asserted rather than demonstrated - The 1.6% decision-logic ratio cited throughout is sourced from community analysis (Tier C evidence), yet is used as a headline quantitative finding without independent verification
Recommended 2604.14228 PDF
#164 — Moderately Innovative (45)

TRACER: Trace-Based Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing for LLM Classification (5 ▲ )

Adam Rida · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 17 Apr 2026
Why this rank: TRACER's core insight — repurposing production billing traces as free training data for surrogate classifiers — is practically clever and eliminates a genuine annotation bottleneck. The parity gate's correct refusal on MNLI demonstrates responsible safety-by-design. However, the approach combines well-known techniques (classical ML surrogates, confidence-based routing, logistic regression acceptors) in a relatively straightforward pipeline, and the evaluation is limited to intent classification tasks where embedding-space separability makes the problem tractable.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Production teams increasingly use LLMs as zero-shot classifiers, paying per-query API costs at scale. Every LLM classification call also produces a labeled input–output pair (a "trace") already retained in logs for billing and compliance. This free, growing dataset could train a lightweight classical ML surrogate to handle a large fraction of future traffic at near-zero marginal cost. Prior learning-to-defer (L2D) and LLM routing methods require labeled data collected before deployment — TRACER removes this bottleneck entirely. Methodology: TRACER fits a surrogate classifier f and a binary acceptor a on accumulated LLM traces, producing a hybrid classifier that routes each input to f when acceptor confidence exceeds a threshold τ, and defers to the teacher LLM otherwise. Surrogates are trained on BGE-large-en-v1.5 embeddings (1024-dim, precomputed offline) using a pool of classical ML classifiers (logistic regression, SGD, MLP, decision trees, random forests, extra-trees); the best is selected by macro-F1 on a held-out validation split. The acceptor is a logistic regression on four uncertainty features (top-1 probability, top-2 probability, margin, normalized entropy), providing tighter teacher-agreement guarantees than raw probability thresholding. Deployment is governed by a parity gate: a candidate pipeline is promoted only if its teacher agreement (TA) on a held-out shadow split meets a user-specified threshold α and exceeds a 5% coverage floor. A continual learning flywheel accumulates new traces from deferred calls (which are naturally concentrated near the decision boundary), refitting from scratch at each update with no manual labeling. At each refit, five interpretability artifacts are generated: slice summaries (per-class handled vs. deferred rates), representative example cards, contrastive boundary pairs (same label, opposite routing), temporal deltas across refits, and disagreement cards. Key Results: Experiments use Claude Sonnet 4.6 as the teacher across three tasks. On CLINC150 (150-class intent, 18K traces), the surrogate achieves 100% coverage at all α levels (0.80–0.95), fully replacing the LLM teacher; ground-truth end-to-end accuracy is 92.4% vs. teacher-only 93.1%. On Banking77 (77-class intent, 10K traces), TRACER achieves 100% coverage at α≤0.85 and 83.2% coverage at α=0.95 with 95.9% teacher agreement, delivering an estimated 83% cost reduction ($7,900/year saved on 10K queries/day). On MNLI (3-class NLI), the parity gate correctly refuses deployment across all nine tested configurations (2K–10K traces, α∈{0.85,0.90,0.95}), demonstrating that frozen sentence embeddings cannot capture the compositional reasoning NLI requires. The flywheel at α=0.95 on Banking77 starts at 73.4% coverage on Day 1 (2,001 traces) and grows to 83.4% by Day 4. TRACER achieves tighter TA than the confidence-threshold baseline at α=0.95 (0.959 vs. 0.951). A calibration–test gap is observed on CLINC150: the parity gate passes at calibration TA 0.952 but test TA is 0.930, below the α=0.95 target. Strengths: - The teacher-trace flywheel elegantly turns a pre-existing production artifact (billing logs) into free training data, eliminating the annotation bottleneck that blocks all prior L2D methods. - The parity gate's conservative design (falls back to full teacher reliance when no candidate clears) and correct refusal behavior on MNLI demonstrate genuine safety-by-design rather than just claimed safety. - Routing to classical ML rather than a cheaper LLM achieves sub-millisecond CPU inference — a qualitatively different cost structure, not just a marginal improvement. - The five interpretability artifact types address a gap in XAI: explaining the routing partition rather than individual predictions, giving operators actionable operational insight. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is restricted to intent classification benchmarks where embedding-space class boundaries are well-separated; the single negative result (MNLI) is not surprising and the applicable task range remains narrow and uncharacterized. - The baseline is a simple confidence-threshold logistic regression with full-hindsight access to all traces — no comparison to learned routing methods (RouteLLM, FrugalGPT, Hybrid LLM) or knowledge distillation approaches, making it hard to assess where TRACER sits in the broader landscape. - The calibration–test gap on CLINC150 at α=0.95 (gate passes at 0.952, test TA is 0.930) is a real reliability concern that is acknowledged but not resolved; the proposed mitigation (conservative α or conformal prediction) is left to future work. - The surrogate imitates the teacher's errors rather than ground truth, and all experiments use a single embedding model (BGE-large-en-v1.5); whether conclusions generalize across encoders is untested.
Recommended 2604.14531 PDF
#165 — Moderately Innovative (45)

Lumbermark: Resistant Clustering by Chopping Up Mutual Reachability Minimum Spanning Trees

Marek Gagolewski · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: Scores 45 as a competent combination of mutual reachability distances with divisive MST cutting, producing a clean algorithm with interpretable parameters. The tie-breaking resolution for mutual reachability MSTs is a genuine theoretical contribution, but the overall approach is a relatively straightforward bridge between HDBSCAN's distance smoothing and existing MST-based partitioning methods. Evaluation limited to d≤3 leaves the practical value for real-world high-dimensional tasks undemonstrated.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Density-based clustering methods like HDBSCAN operate on mutual reachability minimum spanning trees (MSTs) and offer excellent robustness to noise and varying cluster densities, but they cannot produce a user-specified number of clusters k. Conversely, MST-based algorithms that do accept k (e.g., single linkage, Genie, ITM) are typically defined over Euclidean MSTs and lack the smoothing benefit of mutual reachability distances. A further technical obstacle is that mutual reachability distances introduce ties, making the MST non-unique and its topology highly ambiguous — a problem that does not affect HDBSCAN but would break any algorithm that depends on the specific tree structure. The paper fills this gap by (1) resolving the MST ambiguity with a principled tie-breaking rule, and (2) introducing a new divisive algorithm that combines user-specified k with the robustness of mutual reachability distances. Methodology: The paper first formalizes the ambiguity in mutual reachability MSTs and proposes an adjusted distance d'_M(i,j) = max{d(i,j), c_M(i), c_M(j)} + δ·d(i,j), where δ is infinitesimal. This modification breaks ties by preferring edges with smaller original Euclidean distances, producing trees topologically closer to plain Euclidean MSTs and implemented via a custom comparator in the quitefastmst package (parallelized Prim's O(n²) algorithm with K-d tree acceleration). On top of this canonical MST, the Lumbermark algorithm is defined as a divisive procedure: leaves are first stripped from the tree to suppress outliers and border points; a minimum cluster size s = f·|T'|/k is computed from the leaf-pruned tree size, the requested cluster count k, and a tunable min_cluster_factor f ∈ [0,1]; edges are then greedily removed in decreasing weight order, subject to the constraint that all resulting connected components have size ≥ s; the process terminates when k−1 edges have been cut. If f=0 and a Euclidean MST is used, the algorithm reduces to standard single linkage. The time complexity of the partitioning step given the MST is O(nk), with O(n) memory. A scikit-learn-compatible Python package and an R package are released. Key Results: On 61 benchmark datasets (n < 10,000, d ≤ 3) with reference partitions, Lumbermark (f=0.25, M=5) achieves a mean adjusted Rand index of 0.91 on the 25 independently labeled datasets (median 0.98) and 0.89 on all 61, outperforming the prior state-of-the-art Genie (mean 0.86/0.87) as well as spectral clustering (0.83/0.69), Gaussian mixtures (0.75/0.67), k-means (0.71/0.58), and all linkage variants. Even with plain Euclidean distance (M=1), Lumbermark (mean AR 0.89) edges out Genie_G0.3_M3 (0.87) with simpler parameterization. The proportion of "good" fits (AR ≥ 0.95) is 15 out of 25 for Lumbermark vs. 12 for the best Genie variant. Run times are competitive: at n=1M, d=2, k=2, Lumbermark takes 1.23s vs. 0.66s for fast HDBSCAN and 2.27s for standard HDBSCAN; at d=10 performance degrades (43.85s) due to the O(n²) MST cost in high dimensions, similar to HDBSCAN. Ablation over M shows small values (M ≤ 10) work best; the benefit of mutual reachability over raw Euclidean (M=1) is modest but consistent. Strengths: - Theoretically clean resolution of the MST ambiguity problem via a provably metric adjusted distance, enabling the MST-based clustering framework to operate correctly in the mutual reachability setting. - Simple, interpretable algorithm with only two tunable parameters (M and f), both with clear semantic roles and recommended defaults (M=5, f=0.25), making it more robust to misconfiguration than Genie (which is sensitive to its Gini threshold G). - Comprehensive empirical evaluation against 13 competing algorithms across 61 datasets using the established adjusted Rand index, with separate analysis of balanced vs. imbalanced and third-party vs. all labels to guard against evaluation bias. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Scalability in high dimensions is problematic: the O(n²) MST computation dominates at d=10, n=1M (≈44s with 6 threads), making the method impractical for high-dimensional data without approximate MST techniques, which are acknowledged as future work. - The algorithm is only quasi-hierarchical: clusterings at different values of k are not globally nested, so users cannot browse a single dendrogram across all resolutions — a meaningful practical limitation compared to fully hierarchical methods. - Evaluation is restricted to low-dimensional datasets (d ≤ 3), which the paper acknowledges is a deliberate methodological choice but leaves open the question of whether the gains over Genie transfer to higher-dimensional, real-world tasks.
Citations 2604.07143 PDF
#166 — Moderately Innovative (45)

iHyperG: Incremental Hypergraph Partitioning on GPU

tsung-wei-huang.github.io · karypis - new results
Why this rank: iHyperG is a competent systems contribution that adapts incremental partitioning and GPU-accelerated data structures to the hypergraph setting. The delta-based CSR overlay and single-pass rebalancing are engineering-sound, but the core ideas — incremental modification, cut-criticality scoring, warp-level parallelism — are extensions of known techniques rather than conceptual departures.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern CAD workflows—placement, routing, timing analysis, logic simulation—rely heavily on hypergraph partitioning, which is computationally expensive. Existing GPU-accelerated hypergraph partitioners (e.g., HyperG) support only full hypergraph partitioning (FHP), requiring a complete restart from scratch after every circuit modification. This is a critical bottleneck for iterative optimization loops (e.g., incremental timing-driven placement, logic synthesis), where the partitioner is invoked thousands or millions of times with small, localized changes. Prior GPU work on incremental graph partitioning (iG-kway) cannot be directly adapted to hypergraphs because hyperedges are multi-pin structures requiring fundamentally different data representations and refinement strategies—naively applying graph methods leads to excessive GPU memory overhead and incorrect cut-critical vertex selection. Methodology: iHyperG introduces three core technical contributions. First, a delta-based hypergraph data structure: rather than rebuilding the bidirectional CSR (V2E/E2V arrays) after each modification, iHyperG maintains a base hypergraph H and a delta hypergraph δH that records only updated incidences of modified vertices and hyperedges. This avoids full CSR reconstruction and limits memory overhead. Concurrent CUDA streams compute δV2E and δE2V in parallel, with each modified vertex assigned to a GPU warp that uses shared memory to buffer incidence updates and CUDA warp-level primitives (__all_sync, __ballot_sync, __popc, __ffs) to perform compaction-based deletion and first-empty-slot insertion in O(incidence_size/32) warp-parallel steps. Second, a single-pass rebalancing algorithm: when modifications create overweight partitions, rather than iteratively moving vertices between partitions (which causes contention), iHyperG moves the minimum required vertices from each overweight partition into a pseudo-partition in one parallel pass. Vertices are prioritized by a cut-criticality score s(v) = c(v) − n(v), where c(v) counts hyperedges for which v is the sole pin in its partition (cut-critical) and n(v) counts those where it is not. Vertices with higher scores are preferentially moved to reduce cut size. Third, incremental hypergraph refinement: only newly inserted vertices and cut-critical vertices of modified hyperedges are placed in the pseudo-partition and reassigned. For each such vertex v, a warp evaluates every feasible partition pi and selects the most suitable partition as the one minimizing Δλ(v→pi)—the number of v's incident hyperedges whose connectivity would increase. Non-adjacent vertices are processed in parallel using G-kway's sequence-based strategy to avoid concurrent moves of adjacent vertices causing inconsistencies. The full pipeline runs FHP once with HyperG to obtain an initial partition, then applies IHP at each subsequent modification round. Key Results: Evaluated on 18 industrial ISPD98 VLSI benchmarks scaled to 2–12M vertices and edges, with 100 incremental iterations of 25 modifiers each at k=2. iHyperG achieves average speedups of 190× (up to 402×) in hypergraph modification time and 83× (up to 106×) in partitioning time over HyperG (which re-runs full partitioning from scratch). Cut quality is essentially identical: average cut size improvement is 1.00 (within ±2% across all benchmarks), with iHyperG occasionally finding slightly better cuts due to warm-starting from a high-quality initial partition. Speedups are consistent across k ∈ {2, 4, 8, 16, 32}. Incrementality analysis shows modification speedup decreases gracefully as modifier count per iteration grows (from 10 to 1K), while partitioning speedup remains stable regardless of modifier count or k, because incremental refinement focuses only on the small set of cut-critical vertices whose status actually changes. Strengths: - The delta-based data structure is a technically clean solution to the GPU CSR mutability problem: it avoids both the full rebuild of HyperG and the excessive memory overhead of iG-kway's bucket-list approach, maintaining both V2E and E2V delta overlays concurrently via CUDA streams. - The cut-criticality score and single-pass rebalancing elegantly sidestep the iteration-until-convergence problem that plagues multi-pass GPU rebalancing, achieving balance in one pass without sacrificing quality. - The speedups are large, consistent across benchmarks and k values, and grow proportionally with iteration count—exactly the regime that matters most for real CAD optimization loops. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The baseline comparison (HyperG extended to IHP via naive full CSR rebuild + repartition) is weak. A stronger baseline would be a CPU-based incremental hypergraph partitioner or at minimum a GPU implementation that only rebuilds the delta portion of the CSR rather than the whole structure. The paper acknowledges this but does not quantify how much of the 190× modification speedup comes from the delta structure vs. avoiding a neighbor structure rebuild. - Experiments use synthetically expanded circuits (random vertex/hyperedge insertions scaled 100–1000×) rather than actual incremental CAD workloads; the modifier pattern in real timing optimization or logic synthesis may be structured rather than random, potentially affecting both speedup and cut quality in ways not captured here. - The single-pass rebalancing and greedy vertex assignment used in IHP are heuristic and one-shot—there is no iterative refinement within a single incremental step, which could leave cut quality degraded when modifications are large or clustered, a scenario not systematically explored.
Search Results
#167 — Moderately Innovative (45)

NovoExpert-2: State-of-the-Art ADMET Prediction via Gradient-Boosted Trees on MapLight Fingerprints and GIN Embeddings

chemrxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: NovoExpert-2 demonstrates exemplary benchmark discipline — honest correction of a prior metric error, strict TDC protocol adherence, and transparent method-phenotype matching analysis. SOTA on 5/22 endpoints with clear insight into why fingerprint-based methods win on substructure-driven CYP tasks. However, the methodology is a straightforward combination of known representations (Morgan, Avalon, GIN embeddings) with standard CatBoost, and per-endpoint model selection introduces unmitigated test-set bias.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Accurate in silico prediction of ADMET (absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, toxicity) properties is a central bottleneck in computational drug discovery. The Therapeutics Data Commons (TDC) ADMET benchmark provides 22 standardized endpoints for cross-method comparison. Prior work (the authors' own NovoExpert-1) claimed a state-of-the-art CYP2D6 result based on a metric error — reporting AUROC (0.864) when the TDC leaderboard uses AUPRC, a more appropriate metric for the heavily imbalanced CYP datasets (only 16.9% positive rate on CYP2D6). The correctly measured NovoExpert-1 AUPRC was ~0.64, well below the 0.750 leaderboard SOTA. This paper corrects that error and presents a properly evaluated successor that genuinely advances the leaderboard. Methodology: NovoExpert-2 is a dual-method pipeline. The primary method (CatBoost+MapLight+GIN) concatenates four fingerprint/descriptor sets — Morgan ECFP (1024 bits), Avalon (1024 bits), ErG pharmacophore (315 dims), and RDKit 2D descriptors (~210 dims) — with 300-dimensional GIN supervised masking embeddings extracted from the DGL-LifeSci pretrained model zoo, producing a 2,873-dimensional feature vector fed to a CatBoost ensemble (5 seeds, depth 8, 2000 iterations, learning rate 0.03, scale_pos_weight for class imbalance, early stopping on validation). The secondary method is Chemprop v2, a directed message-passing neural network (D-MPNN) that learns end-to-end task-specific representations. For each of the 22 endpoints, the stronger of the two model families is reported. Evaluation strictly follows the TDC official protocol: official train/test splits, 5-seed ensembling, and per-endpoint metrics (AUPRC, AUROC, MAE, or Spearman as specified by TDC). Key Results: NovoExpert-2 achieves SOTA on 5 of 22 endpoints. CYP2D6 Veith: AUPRC 0.778 vs. 0.750 (+0.028, all 5 seeds individually exceed prior SOTA, σ=0.0020). CYP3A4 Veith: AUPRC 0.916 vs. 0.900 (+0.016). CYP3A4 Substrate Carbonmangels: AUROC 0.660 vs. 0.656 (+0.004). Clearance Hepatocyte AZ: Spearman 0.511 vs. 0.487 (+0.024). DILI: AUROC 0.922 (Chemprop v2 ensemble) vs. 0.916 (+0.006). The first four wins use CatBoost+MapLight+GIN; DILI is the only endpoint where Chemprop v2 outperforms the fingerprint pipeline (CatBoost ensemble: 0.906). On the remaining 17 endpoints, NovoExpert-2 is competitive but below SOTA; largest gaps are on small-dataset regression tasks: VDss Lombardo (−0.157), Half Life Obach (−0.135), and Bioavailability Ma (−0.097). Strengths: - Rigorous benchmark discipline: strict adherence to TDC official splits, per-endpoint metrics, and 5-seed ensembling, with transparent disclosure of the prior metric error and its correction. - Practical insight into method-phenotype matching: the paper clearly explains why CatBoost+fingerprints wins on substructure-driven CYP endpoints (local pattern capture, explicit class weighting, dataset size well-matched to model capacity) and why Chemprop wins on the mechanism-complex DILI endpoint (end-to-end learned global representations). - Full code, trained models, and prediction files released under MIT license, enabling independent reproduction. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Per-endpoint model selection between two families introduces test-set selection bias; the authors acknowledge this but do not fully mitigate it — a proper held-out validation set for model selection would be needed for unbiased evaluation, particularly for DILI where the margin is only +0.006. - No per-endpoint hyperparameter tuning within either model family; a single fixed CatBoost configuration is used across all 22 diverse endpoints, leaving performance on small-dataset regression tasks (VDss, Half Life, Bioavailability) substantially below SOTA with no principled remedy offered. - Single-author study from a commercially conflicted organization (author is founder of NovoQuantNexus, which commercializes the NovoExpert suite) with no independent reproduction of results — the paper explicitly acknowledges this as a limitation but does not resolve it.
Citations
#168 — Moderately Innovative (44)

BERT-as-a-Judge: A Robust Alternative to Lexical Methods for Efficient Reference-Based LLM Evaluation

Hippolyte Gisserot-Boukhlef, Nicolas Boizard, Emmanuel Malherbe, Céline Hudelot, Pierre Colombo · arxiv.org · Recommended articles
Why this rank: Scores 44 as a well-executed but conceptually straightforward contribution. The empirical diagnosis of regex failure rates across 36 models is thorough and practically valuable, and the BERT encoder achieves impressive accuracy at minimal cost. However, the core idea — distilling an expensive LLM judge into a small encoder — is a standard knowledge distillation application, and the method's scope is limited to English benchmarks with objectively verifiable answers, excluding the open-ended generation tasks where evaluation is most challenging.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Standard LLM evaluation in zero-shot generative settings relies on regex-based answer extraction followed by lexical matching (exact match, ROUGE-L, MathVerify). This approach conflates a model's actual problem-solving ability with its compliance to output formatting constraints. Models that produce correct but differently formatted answers get penalized, distorting benchmark rankings. The alternative — LLM-as-a-Judge — fixes the formatting sensitivity but introduces heavy computational overhead and sensitivity to judge model choice. The gap between these two extremes — cheap-but-brittle lexical evaluation and expensive-but-robust LLM judging — motivates a better middle ground. Methodology: The paper first quantifies the extent of regex failure across 36 open-weight models (135M–70B parameters) on 15 benchmarks spanning multiple-choice, context extraction, and open-form math tasks. It then proposes BERT-as-a-Judge: a fine-tuned BERT-scale encoder (EuroBERT 210M) trained on approximately 1M synthetically labeled question-candidate-reference triplets. Labels are generated by Nemotron-Super-v1.5 with 97.5% average agreement against 3,212 human annotations. Training uses binary cross-entropy for one epoch on 8 MI250x GPUs (~20 GPU hours). At inference, the encoder takes the concatenated question, reference, and candidate as input and outputs a binary correct/incorrect judgment in ~200ms on an Apple M1 CPU. The paper compares BERT-as-a-Judge against regex baselines and LLM judges of varying scales (Qwen-3 and Gemma-3, 0.6B–32B), with and without chain-of-thought intermediate generation. Key Results: Regex parsing failure rates are severe and highly model-dependent: Llama-3 70B fails to parse over 60% of open-form math outputs; Qwen-3 32B fails ~20%. Even with successful parsing, lexical matching induces large accuracy drops — Gemma-3 4B loses 30.8% on context extraction due to verbose but correct outputs. Rank distortions are extreme: Qwen-3 32B drops 18 positions on multiple-choice, Gemma-3 4B climbs 6 positions — pure artifacts of formatting sensitivity rather than capability differences. BERT-as-a-Judge achieves 99.7% accuracy on ARC-Easy, 98.5% on MMLU, 98.8% on GSM8K, and 93.7% on MATH, outperforming regex by +21.1% on CoQA, +20.3% on MATH, and +10.4% on ARC-Challenge. It matches or exceeds the best LLM judges (Qwen-3 32B, Gemma-3 27B with chain-of-thought) at orders-of-magnitude lower inference FLOPs. Small LLM judges (0.6B) consistently underperform even regex, confirming that LLM-as-a-Judge requires substantial scale to be viable. Out-of-domain generalization to unseen model families (Ministral-3, LFM-2, EuroLLM, Apertus) degrades minimally — OOD accuracy typically within 0.5–2% of in-distribution. Training with only 100K samples suffices for multiple-choice and math, with 2 GPU hours total. Strengths: - The empirical diagnosis of regex failures is rigorous and wide-scale (36 models × 15 tasks), with rank-change analysis that directly demonstrates the practical harm to leaderboard validity — not just average accuracy gaps. - The proposed method is well-matched to the task: bidirectional encoders are architecturally suited for classification over fixed-length inputs, and the synthetic labeling pipeline with human validation provides a scalable and credible training signal. - Strong generalization results — both to out-of-domain tasks (TruthfulQA, CoQA, ASDiv excluded from training) and to completely unseen model families — give the approach real deployability beyond its training distribution. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The scope is confined to English benchmarks with objectively verifiable answers. The method is not evaluated on open-ended generation (summarization, translation, instruction following), which constitutes a large fraction of real-world LLM evaluation needs. - The ground-truth labels themselves come from Nemotron-Super-v1.5, a very large LLM judge — meaning BERT-as-a-Judge is distilling an LLM judge, not replacing the need for one during dataset construction. The 97.5% agreement validates the proxy but does not eliminate circular dependence on expensive LLM judgments at the data creation stage.
Recommended 2604.09497 PDF
#169 — Moderately Innovative (44)

Habitat-GS: A High-Fidelity Navigation Simulator with Dynamic Gaussian Splatting (12 ▲ )

Ziyuan Xia, Jingyi Xu, Chong Cui, Yuanhong Yu, Jiazhao Zhang, Qingsong Yan, Tao Ni, Junbo Chen, Xiaowei Zhou, Hujun Bao, Ruizhen Hu, Sida Peng · arxiv.org · Daily papers of 15 Apr 2026
Why this rank: Habitat-GS makes a solid engineering contribution by integrating 3DGS rendering into the Habitat ecosystem with practical design choices (zero-copy CUDA-OpenGL interop, offline avatar pre-baking, proxy capsule collisions). The mixed-domain training asymmetry finding is actionable. However, the work is primarily systems integration rather than methodological innovation, limited to navigation-only tasks, and the avatar diversity (six identities with similar walking motions) is too narrow to validate generalization claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Training embodied AI agents for real-world deployment requires simulation environments with high visual fidelity and realistic dynamic human presence. Existing open-source simulators (Habitat-Sim, iGibson, AI2-THOR, ThreeDWorld, SAPIEN) rely on mesh-based rasterization, which produces a significant Sim-to-Real visual domain gap that degrades navigation policy transfer. Their humanoid avatar support is likewise mesh-constrained, limiting agents' ability to learn socially compliant navigation. The one industrial alternative with 3DGS support (NVIDIA Isaac Sim) is closed-source and requires RT Core GPUs, making it unsuitable for research on standard datacenter hardware. Habitat-GS addresses all three gaps simultaneously within the Habitat ecosystem. Methodology: Habitat-GS extends Habitat-Sim along two orthogonal axes while preserving full API compatibility with Habitat-Lab. For scene rendering, a CUDA tile-based 3DGS rasterizer is integrated into Habitat's OpenGL sensor pipeline via a zero-copy CUDA–OpenGL interoperability mechanism (Map–Render–Unmap), eliminating CPU-side data transfers and enabling real-time photorealistic RGB-D output. A depth compositing pass correctly resolves occlusion among 3DGS scenes, mesh objects, and multiple gaussian avatars. For dynamic human modeling, a gaussian avatar module pre-bakes canonical gaussian attributes (positions, SH coefficients, opacities, scales, rotations, LBS weights) offline from existing avatar methods (AnimatableGaussians, GaussianAvatar) and deforms them at runtime using a lightweight CUDA Linear Blend Skinning kernel driven by SMPL-X pose sequences from the GAMMA motion generator—entirely avoiding neural network inference at runtime. Since 3DGS lacks explicit geometry, collision detection uses a proxy capsule mechanism: per-bone capsule primitives are precomputed for every trajectory frame, stored as a [T, C, 7] tensor, and injected into Habitat-Sim's NavMesh path planner each step as dynamic obstacles, decoupling visual appearance from navigation physics. The system supports scalable asset import from self-reconstructed scenes, public 3DGS datasets (InteriorGS), and generative pipelines (Marble). Key Results: A VLM-based quality assessment using Gemini 3.0 Pro (240 screenshots, 48 batches) shows 3DGS scenes substantially outperform mesh scenes on rendering quality (6.99 vs 3.65), realism (5.66 vs 3.98), and scene diversity (8.47 vs 7.08) on a 10-point scale. On point-goal navigation (PointNav) with DD-PPO and ResNet+GRU agents trained for 5×10^7 steps, pure GS training (Config B) reaches 70.7% SR / 58.49 SPL on the GS test set versus 61.3% / 52.09 for mesh-only (Config A), but converges slower. Mixed-domain training (20M+80G, Config E) achieves the best GS test result (79.6% SR, 68.38 SPL, 2.698m DTG) while maintaining mesh test parity (~59.6% SR). At full convergence (10^8 steps), GS-only training (77.6% SR on GS test) already exceeds mesh-only (61.7% SR on GS test) while nearly matching it on mesh test (59.8% vs 62.8%), confirming asymmetric domain transfer. For avatar-aware navigation, agents fine-tuned with gaussian avatars reduce collision rate from 6.713% to 4.746% and personal space intrusion from 0.092 to 0.077 on in-domain GS tests; benefits generalize cross-domain to mesh environments (CR 2.342% vs 2.521%). In the human tracking task (TrackNav), GS-trained agents achieve average collision counts of 5.68 versus 11.93 for mesh-trained agents. Rendering benchmarks on an RTX 4090 show 120.9 FPS at 1M Gaussians and >50 FPS with 1–2 avatars in typical RL training conditions, with linear memory scaling. Strengths: - The visual–navigation decoupling design is principled and practical: it sidesteps the fundamental absence of explicit surface geometry in 3DGS while preserving compatibility with Habitat-Lab's mature task and training infrastructure without code modification. - The offline pre-baking of canonical gaussians and proxy capsules eliminates all runtime neural network inference, achieving real-time avatar rendering and collision-aware navigation simultaneously—an engineering contribution that makes the system usable at RL training scale. - The mixed-domain training finding is actionable and well-validated: the asymmetry (GS→mesh transfer works, mesh→GS does not) has clear practical implications for how future embodied AI training pipelines should be structured, and is confirmed across both a practical budget (5×10^7 steps) and full convergence (10^8 steps). Weaknesses / Limitations: - The visual–navigation decoupling, while pragmatic, means physical interactions beyond obstacle avoidance (grasping, pushing, manipulation) are out of scope; the system is fundamentally a navigation-only platform, limiting its applicability to the broader embodied AI research agenda. - Avatar diversity is extremely limited: only six AnimatableGaussians identities are used (three train, three test), all exhibiting similar walking motions from GAMMA. This raises questions about whether the reported collision avoidance gains generalize to the diversity of human appearances, body types, and motion patterns encountered in real deployment scenarios.
Recommended 2604.12626 PDF
#170 — Moderately Innovative (44)

Fully Homomorphic Encryption on Llama 3 model for privacy preserving LLM inference

Anes Abdennebi, Nadjia Kara, Laaziz Lahlou · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: This paper scores 44 as primarily an engineering scaling exercise — applying existing FHE tooling (Zama's concrete-ml) to LLaMA-3 rather than introducing new cryptographic or ML techniques. The noise-averaging observation for long-context generation is a genuinely interesting empirical finding, but the partial encryption design (only attention projections, leaving FFN and norms in plaintext) substantially undermines the 'fully homomorphic' privacy claims. The accuracy metric (token-match vs. baseline) is non-standard and does not assess actual generation quality.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLMs deployed in sensitive domains (healthcare, finance, government) process user data in plaintext, exposing it to prompt injection, data extraction, model theft, and private information extraction attacks. Existing encryption techniques are also vulnerable to future quantum computing attacks that could break classical cryptographic schemes. The paper addresses the gap of applying post-quantum secure, lattice-based Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) to a modern production LLM's inference pipeline — an area previously unexplored at this scale due to the computational complexity of FHE operations over deep transformer networks. Methodology: The authors modify the LLaMA-3 (8B) inference pipeline by injecting FHE operations from Zama's concrete-ml library, which uses Ring Learning With Errors (RLWE)-based FHE with Programmable Bootstrapping (PBS). The approach quantizes attention weights to 2-bit integers (required because concrete-ml operates on integer arithmetic, not floats) and replaces the softmax function with polynomial approximations compatible with FHE. Two model variants are built: SingleHeadQLlamaModel, which encrypts only the first of 32 decoder layers' attention mechanism, and MultiHeadsQLlamaModel, which encrypts all attention heads. The DualArray abstraction from concrete-ml maintains both floating-point and integer representations during a cleartext calibration pass that determines quantization parameters (scaling factors, zero-points). After calibration, the FHE circuit is compiled once, with PBS nodes statically inserted wherever non-linear operations or noise refreshes are needed. Inference follows a client-server model: the client encrypts embeddings and sends them with an evaluation key to the server, which executes the encrypted attention computation; the client then decrypts and dequantizes the result. Experiments use 79 text prompts across three CPU machines (AMD EPYC 7413, Intel i7-12700K, Intel i9-14900K) and evaluate two regimes: short-context (1–10 new tokens) and long-context (70–500 new tokens) generation. Key Results: In the long-context regime (top-k=500), SingleHeadQLlamaModel achieves 98.2% token-match accuracy vs. the plain LLaMA-3 baseline in 0.236s per inference on the i9-14900K, reaching approximately 80 tokens/second. MultiHeadsQLlamaModel achieves 97.7% accuracy in 0.825s. Counterintuitively, long-context generation outperforms short-context: at top-k=10, SingleHead achieves only 81.7% and MultiHead achieves 87.4% accuracy. The authors attribute this to noise averaging — ciphertext packing and periodic key-switching distribute and stabilize noise across longer sequences, improving the effective precision ratio. Compile time is a one-time cost of 3.5–8.6s (single-head) and 12–26s (multi-head). The implementation requires only 5,208 PBS operations for a 6-token sequence, compared to 11,622 for the prior FHE GPT-2 baseline, representing a ~55% reduction in cryptographic intensity. Cache profiling reveals that KV-caching causes per-token inference time to remain nearly constant across all top-k values, with working sets fitting primarily in L1 and LLC despite elevated L2 miss rates (~21–36%) from large ciphertext buffers. Strengths: - Demonstrates practical FHE-secured inference on a modern production-scale LLM (LLaMA-3 8B) with sub-second latency on commodity CPU hardware, advancing feasibility well beyond prior GPT-2 scale work. - The novel observation that long-context generation benefits from noise averaging (higher accuracy at larger top-k) is technically grounded and practically useful for deployment targeting summarization or report generation tasks. - The modular partial-encryption design (encrypting 1 of 32 layers) offers a principled privacy/performance trade-off adaptable to application security requirements. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The accuracy metric — token-match rate against the plain model's output — is not a standard NLP quality measure; it conflates correctness with fidelity to the baseline model's specific token choices. Semantic quality, perplexity, or downstream task accuracy on benchmarks (MMLU, HellaSwag) are absent, making it impossible to assess actual generation quality degradation from aggressive 2-bit quantization. - The approach only encrypts attention projections in selected layers while leaving feed-forward layers, RMS norms, and the final linear/softmax in plaintext. This partial encryption leaves large portions of intermediate computation exposed, significantly overstating the privacy guarantee relative to the 'fully homomorphic' framing in the title. Side-channel attacks, model extraction via black-box queries, and adversarial input attacks are explicitly noted as unmitigated by FHE.
Search Results 2604.12168 PDF
#171 — Moderately Innovative (44)

Surface vs. Structural Similarity: Aligning Computational Similarity Metrics with Human Judgments

scitepress.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The grounding in cognitive theory (surface vs. structural similarity) to design a two-dimensional annotation scheme is a meaningful conceptual contribution over single-metric validation approaches. However, the execution is a straightforward correlation study using synthetic stimuli generated by the same model families being evaluated, introducing circularity concerns. The single-domain scope and absence of cross-validation further limit the novelty.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: HCI systems for search, discovery, and recommendation rely on computational similarity metrics, but these metrics are typically validated against a single undifferentiated measure of human similarity judgment. Cognitive science has long established that human similarity judgments decompose into at least two distinct components: surface similarity (observable attributes such as color, shape, and layout) and structural similarity (shared relational structure such as causal relationships and role correspondences). The gap between this two-level cognitive theory and single-metric computational validation means it is unclear which dimension of human judgment any given metric actually captures, potentially leading to recommender systems that systematically mismatch user expectations. Methodology: The study operationalizes the surface/structural distinction in the book domain: surface similarity is defined as similarity in cover design (cover image + title text), and structural similarity as similarity in synopsis content. Computational metrics are instantiated as: (1) ViT cosine similarity for cover images, (2) SBERT cosine similarity for titles, and (3) SBERT cosine similarity for synopses. A composite surface metric combines title-SBERT and ViT via standardized regression weighting. Stimuli consisted of 108 fictional books (titles, synopses, and AI-generated covers created via ChatGPT-5 and Canva), clustered into 12 sets of 9 to yield 432 within-set pairs spanning a range of similarity levels. Human annotations were collected via crowdsourcing from 119 native Japanese speakers (105 retained after consistency screening) who rated all pairs on 7-point Likert scales separately for cover designs and synopses. Pearson and Spearman correlations between computational metrics and mean human ratings were computed with Bonferroni correction. Key Results: All four computational metrics showed statistically significant positive correlations with the corresponding human judgments (all p_adj < .001). The composite surface metric achieved the highest alignment (r = .672, ρ = .645), followed by ViT alone (r = .605, ρ = .570). Structural similarity via SBERT synopses yielded a moderately strong correlation (r = .533, ρ = .527), while title-only SBERT was weakest (r = .372, ρ = .358). Surface metrics consistently outperformed the structural metric. The authors attribute the surface advantage to the alignment between ViT's global patch-based representation and human rapid visual processing (which also captures global color/shape cues). The lower structural correlation is attributed to SBERT's known limitations: vector-space clustering artifacts inflating cosine similarities for unrelated pairs (anisotropy), and sensitivity to surface cues rather than relational/causal structure in BERT-based models. Strengths: - Principled grounding in cognitive theory (Gentner's structure-mapping, Holyoak & Koh surface/structural distinction) to design a two-dimensional annotation scheme, as opposed to prior work using a single undifferentiated rating. - Rigorous annotation design with 105 participants, 432 stimulus pairs, repeated-trial consistency screening, Bonferroni correction, and both Pearson and Spearman statistics. - Practical implication is clearly articulated: surface and structural similarity can be tracked as separate signals in HCI interfaces to enable user-steerable, explainable recommendations. Weaknesses / Limitations: - All stimuli are AI-generated synthetic fictional books (ChatGPT-5 + Canva), not real books. This introduces a circularity: the same embedding models (SBERT, ViT) used to measure computational similarity were also used to construct and select the stimuli, which may artificially inflate observed correlations. - Participants are exclusively native Japanese speakers recruited from a single crowdsourcing platform, limiting cultural and demographic generalizability. The paper acknowledges this but does not quantify the potential confound. - The operationalization of "structural similarity" via synopses is a proxy, not a direct measure of relational structure. Synopses are narrative summaries that mix surface topic cues with relational content; the degree to which annotators actually engaged with causal and role structure versus topic overlap is uncontrolled. - The study is entirely correlational within a single domain with a narrow pair-selection procedure; no cross-validation or held-out evaluation is performed, leaving predictive generalization unassessed.
Search Results
#172 — Moderately Innovative (44)

Combination of Ontology -Based Retrieval Augmented Graphs and Inductive Reasoning for Knowledge Fusion of Intelligent Systems

scitepress.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: Onto-GNN's formal integration of ontology, knowledge graph, and GNN-based inductive reasoning into a unified architecture is well-motivated, with each component's contribution isolated via ablation on standard benchmarks. The relation weight formulation combining semantic, frequency, and hierarchical signals is principled. However, the absence of LLM-based RAG baselines, unreported hyperparameters, and a user study without controls significantly weaken the empirical claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Traditional keyword-based retrieval systems for e-learning fail to capture deep semantic relationships between concepts, producing superficial or incomplete responses. Ontology-based systems provide structured domain semantics but lack AI-driven, multi-step reasoning. Knowledge graphs offer flexibility but struggle with hierarchical and interdependent relations. Static rule-based systems cannot infer complex relation patterns—e.g., a query about BCNF due to transitive dependencies may surface only Functional Dependencies while missing Primary Keys or multivalued dependencies. The paper targets this gap by combining ontology-based knowledge structure with GNN-powered inductive reasoning for context-aware educational chatbots. Methodology: The authors propose Onto-GNN, a three-layer architecture built on the LogCo contrastive reasoning model. The foundation is Rela-Data, a formal ontology model encoding hierarchical concepts (CH, at high/mid/low abstraction levels), hierarchical relations (RH, as vertical/horizontal/cross-hierarchical triples), operators, deductive rules, problems, and solving methods, fused with a relational database. From Rela-Data, a two-layer knowledge graph (Rela-KG) is extracted, distinguishing intra-object edges (Rel_intra) from multi-hop inferential edges (Rel_infer). Relation weights are computed by combining Complex-valued relational embeddings (scoring function via real part of multi-linear product), normalized frequency scores, and hierarchical type coefficients. Weighted shortest paths between entity pairs are extracted via Dijkstra's algorithm, and a contrastive loss is defined over positive/negative path pairs using a similarity score penalized by the deviation between path weight and relation weight. At retrieval time, an NLP query processor extracts entities and relations, maps them to graph nodes, performs L-layer message-passing aggregation (Eq. 6), and selects the top-k ranked subgraph paths as the answer. Key Results: Ablation over four configurations (MLogCo, MHier, MRela, MOntoGNN) on WN18RR and FB15K-237 shows additive gains from each component. The full Onto-GNN achieves MRR 0.442 / Hits@10 0.510 on WN18RR (+10.5% MRR over LogCo baseline at 0.400) and MRR 0.366 / Hits@10 0.500 on FB15K-237 (+8.3% over baseline at 0.338). In a user study with 82 undergraduate students across 5 chapters of a Fundamentals of Database Systems course, the chatbot received Likert ratings of 4.1–4.9 / 5.0 across four dimensions (Generality, Usability, Naturalness, Formality), with the highest scores on normalization content (Usability 4.7, Formality 4.9). Strengths: - Principled, formally defined integration of three complementary representations (ontology, knowledge graph, GNN), with each component's marginal contribution empirically isolated via ablation. - The relation weight formulation is well-motivated, combining semantic similarity, usage frequency, and ontological hierarchy into a single scalar that directly influences path selection and contrastive training. - Dual evaluation strategy: standard KG completion benchmarks (WN18RR, FB15K-237) provide objective comparison, while the chatbot user study grounds the approach in a real pedagogical deployment. Weaknesses / Limitations: - No comparison against modern LLM-based RAG baselines; the sole point of comparison is the LogCo model, leaving unclear whether the gains are competitive with transformer-based retrieval augmentation systems. - The educational evaluation relies entirely on subjective Likert-scale surveys from 82 students with no control group, no pre/post learning outcome measurement, and no comparison against existing e-learning chatbots—making it impossible to attribute learning improvements to the proposed system. - Critical hyperparameters (λ1, λ2, λ3 in the weight formula; αve, αho, αcr for hierarchy coefficients) are described as tuned but never reported, which prevents reproducibility and makes it unclear how sensitive the model is to these choices. - The ontology for the Fundamentals of Database Systems course is hand-crafted and domain-specific; the paper acknowledges generalization to other domains as future work but provides no evidence or analysis of the effort required to construct such knowledge bases at scale.
Search Results
#173 — Moderately Innovative (44)

Combining content information with collaborative filtering for publication venue recommendation

link.springer.com · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The dual-integration design embedding text into both the rating computation and neighborhood construction of CF is clean and interpretable, and the cross-dataset analysis explaining why text-based ratings succeed in diverse corpora but fail in homogeneous ones is substantive. However, the reliance on static TF-IDF with a 5000-term vocabulary is dated, and the cold-start exclusion contradicts the stated motivation.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Researchers face increasing difficulty identifying appropriate publication venues given the explosive growth of academic publishing. While content-based methods exist, they over-specialize by ignoring community behavior, and pure collaborative filtering (CF) suffers from sparsity and cold-start problems. This paper addresses profile-based venue recommendation — recommending venues aligned with a researcher's overall publication trajectory, not a single manuscript — by hybridizing CF with textual content from papers. The key gap is that prior hybrid approaches treat content as auxiliary features in complex citation/coauthorship networks; this work instead embeds textual signals directly into the two core CF components: rating computation and neighborhood construction. Methodology: The framework extends standard user-based CF (Eq. 2: weighted average of neighbor ratings, normalized over the full neighborhood) with two orthogonal content integration strategies. First, text-based ratings (rT): instead of counting publications per venue, the author-venue rating is the cosine similarity between the author's TF-IDF profile (concatenation of all their paper titles/abstracts) and the venue's TF-IDF profile (all papers published there). Second, text-based neighborhoods: author similarity is computed either directly via cosine between author TF-IDF profiles (nTF), or indirectly via similarity between each author's vector of venue-similarity scores (nTV). These are combined with two baseline neighborhood types — publication-based (nP, cosine over venue-rating vectors) and coauthorship-based (nC/nCC, weighted coauthorship counts up to two hops). The 2×5 grid of rating×neighborhood combinations, plus a purely content-based baseline (k=1, rT, no collaborative component), is evaluated with neighborhood sizes k∈{2,5,10,20,30}. Text preprocessing uses stopword removal, stemming, document-frequency filtering, and a 5000-term TF-IDF vocabulary. Evaluation uses RR, Recall@10, Precision@10, MAP@100, and NDCG@100 on two datasets: PMSC-UGR (309K biomedical papers, 1002 journals, 18K authors) and CORD-19 (130K COVID papers, 11K venues, 7K authors), with temporal train/test splits and a ground-truth restricted to newly visited venues. Key Results: Content integration consistently outperforms purely behavioral baselines with statistical significance (Wilcoxon tests, p<0.05 across most metric/configuration pairs). On PMSC-UGR, the best content-augmented configurations (nTF-rP-30 and nCC-rT-2) achieve Recall@10 ≈ 0.300 and 0.290, respectively, versus 0.278 for nP-rP-30 (classical CF) and 0.209 for nCC-rP-30 — gains of ~8% and ~39%. The purely content-based baseline (n, Recall@10=0.285) also outperforms classical CF in most metrics. On CORD-19, nTF-rP-30 is the clear winner (Recall@10=0.130, RR=0.176), outperforming nP-rP-30 (0.121, 0.160) and nC-rP-30 (0.115, 0.162) significantly, while rT ratings underperform rP (nC-rT-30 Recall@10=0.096 vs. nC-rP-30=0.115). A key cross-dataset finding: combining text-based neighborhoods and* text-based ratings simultaneously is counterproductive on both datasets — redundant content signals cancel out. The effect of content also depends critically on dataset characteristics: in the topically diverse PMSC-UGR, rT ratings are highly discriminative; in the topically concentrated CORD-19, venue profiles become homogeneous, removing rT's discriminative power while nTF neighborhoods remain beneficial. Strengths: - Dual-integration design is principled and interpretable: text enriches exactly the two components (ratings and neighborhoods) that drive the CF scoring formula, without requiring external resources like citation networks or GNNs. - Rigorous experimental protocol with two structurally distinct datasets, temporal train/test splits, novel-venue-only ground truth, five metrics, sensitivity analysis over k, and Wilcoxon significance testing across all 51 configurations. - The analysis of why rT succeeds on PMSC-UGR but fails on CORD-19 (inter-venue semantic variance argument) is substantive and yields actionable hypotheses (broader venue profile construction, adaptive hybrid selection). Weaknesses / Limitations: - Text representations are static TF-IDF bags-of-words with a 5000-term vocabulary — no temporal decay, no author sub-profile modeling for multidisciplinary researchers, and no dense embeddings (e.g., SciBERT). This likely causes the redundancy problem and limits discrimination in homogeneous corpora. - The evaluation is restricted to authors with sufficient publication history and first-time venue discovery; performance for early-career or highly prolific multi-domain researchers is not characterized separately, and the filtering thresholds (≥100 articles/venue for PMSC-UGR, ≥20 articles/author for CORD-19) mean cold-start scenarios are effectively excluded despite being cited as a motivation.
Search Results
#174 — Moderately Innovative (44)

Agrisemantics: The Current State of Adoption

tandfonline.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: Provides the first empirically grounded, multi-stakeholder adoption study of agrisemantics with strong methodological rigor (κ=0.939, mixed-methods triangulation). The finding that tool limitations dominate barriers is actionable. Yet this is fundamentally a descriptive qualitative study with no technical contribution, no baseline comparison to other domains' semantic adoption rates, and significant geographic skew.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Agricultural data is massively fragmented—stored in proprietary silos, described with heterogeneous schemas, and costly to integrate. The absence of integrated agricultural data has been estimated to cost India alone $65 billion, while poor data quality costs organizations globally ~$12.9M/year. Agrisemantics—the use of shared semantic resources (ontologies, controlled vocabularies, linked data) such as AGROVOC and the Crop Ontology to enable interoperability across agri-food systems—has received sustained investment from FAO and international bodies for decades. Yet despite mature resources existing, actual adoption remains poorly documented and apparently limited. This study addresses that gap by empirically characterizing the barriers and drivers shaping uptake among practitioners and academics. Methodology: A qualitative mixed-methods study conducted 2020–2025 combining 28 semi-structured expert interviews (46.7% response rate from 60 invited; ~1 hour each) with a web-based survey of 76 practitioners. Participants spanned academia, government, NGOs, and private industry across multiple world regions, though 51.85% of interviewees were European and 75% were male—acknowledged as selection biases. Interview transcripts were analyzed via inductive thematic coding (Fereday & Muir-Cochrane 2006) producing 13 granular codes organized into four coarse categories: Organizational/Cultural Barriers (193 total citations), Technical/Infrastructure Challenges (189), Drivers of Adoption (200), and Economic/Resource Constraints (71). Inter-annotator reliability on a 5-interview subsample reached Cohen's κ = 0.939. Questionnaire responses were analyzed descriptively and triangulated against interview themes. Key Results: Adoption remains at an early, fragmented stage. Tool Limitations was the single most-cited barrier (102 citations), dominated by complaints that tools like Protégé are 25 years old, require deep semantic expertise, and lack usability for non-experts. Domain-specific drivers (127 citations) were the most frequently cited positive factor—food safety, traceability, data provenance, and digitalization all create bottom-up operational incentives. Organizational barriers collectively dominated: decision-maker resistance (50), internal gatekeeping (51), loss of momentum (53), and academic–industry divide (39) totaled 193 citations. Government/policy support (62 citations) was acknowledged as helpful but insufficient on its own to drive adoption. Individual champions (11 citations) played catalytic but fragile roles. From the survey: 48.7% reported using agrisemantics in their work; 75% conceived it narrowly as a shared vocabulary; only 36.8% recognized reasoning capabilities. Knowledge/skills gaps (48.7%) and lack of technical understanding within organizations (56.3%) were the top survey-reported barriers. AGROVOC and the Crop Ontology were the dominant resources in use; Africa showed the lowest adoption (33.3%), partly because major resources do not represent African crops. No quantitative performance benchmarks are reported—this is a qualitative adoption study. Strengths: - Provides the first empirically grounded, multi-stakeholder account of agrisemantics adoption rather than advocacy or descriptive cataloguing of resources - Strong inter-annotator reliability (κ = 0.939) and explicit thematic saturation provide methodological credibility for a qualitative study of this scale - Mixed-methods triangulation (interviews + survey) with geographic diversity beyond the Western-skewed interview sample adds breadth to depth Weaknesses / Limitations: - Heavy geographic and demographic skew (51.85% European, 75% male interviewees) limits global generalizability, particularly for the Global South where agrisemantics gaps may differ fundamentally - The study does not assess whether adoption has changed over the 2020–2025 study window; interviews conducted primarily 2020–2023 may not reflect more recent developments including the rapid rise of LLMs, which the authors briefly speculate about but did not investigate - No comparison or control condition: without data on adoption rates of analogous semantic technologies in other domains (e.g., biomedical ontologies), it is difficult to judge whether agrisemantics underperforms expectations or mirrors a general pattern of semantic web adoption failure in industry
Search Results
#175 — Moderately Innovative (43)

A-IO: Adaptive Inference Orchestration for Memory-Bound NPUs

Chen Zhang, Yan Ding, Haotian Wang, Chubo Liu, Keqin Li, Kenli Li · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: A-IO applies the well-known model routing paradigm (FrugalGPT, RouteLLM) to NPU-specific constraints, with the genuinely useful contribution being the precise quantification of NPU-specific bottlenecks (static graph compilation killing speculative decoding, dequantization negating quantization). The routing logic itself — entropy thresholding over a 1B probe — is straightforward, and evaluation on a single model family with HuggingFace Transformers rather than production engines limits practical relevance.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deploying large language models (LLMs) on heterogeneous NPU platforms (specifically Huawei Ascend 910B) faces a severe memory-bound bottleneck during autoregressive decoding. Each generated token requires fetching the entire model's weights from High Bandwidth Memory (HBM), rapidly exhausting bandwidth. The paper identifies three compounding problems: (1) a "Model Scaling Paradox" where neither a small 1B nor large 7B model alone achieves Pareto-optimal performance across diverse request types; (2) fine-grained speculative decoding incurs intolerable kernel synchronization overhead under NPU static graph compilation, collapsing joint throughput to ~4 TPS; and (3) storage-only weight quantization (W8A16) fails to reduce active memory bandwidth due to mandatory FP16 dequantization at runtime, yielding zero TPS improvement. Methodology: A-IO (Adaptive Inference Orchestration) is a coarse-grained, request-aware macro-scheduling framework that co-resides a 1B probe model and 7B backbone in Ascend 910B's 64GB HBM (~2GB + ~14GB, well within capacity). On each request arrival, the 1B probe performs a single forward pass using a template-driven classification prompt to identify task domain (Code, QA, Math). Shannon entropy H(X) is computed against a threshold τ=0.45 (calibrated on 500 queries) to measure routing confidence. Based on semantic category, entropy, and context length L_ctx, A-IO applies two routing decisions: (a) Model Routing — short-context (≤2K) code tasks with H(X)≤0.45 go to the 1B model; all others (QA, Math, long-context, high-uncertainty) route to the 7B backbone; (b) Strategy Routing — PLD (Prompt LookUp Decoding with N=6, L=2) is enabled for QA/Math on the 7B model but strictly disabled for code tasks to prevent syntax-induced accuracy collapse. The key insight is that for 512-token generation tasks, routing to the 1B probe reduces cumulative HBM data transfer from ~7.1 TB to ~1.0 TB, directly relieving the bandwidth wall. Routing overhead is ~17.4 ms total (template encapsulation 2.5ms, single-token prefill 11.8ms, logic 0.7ms, hot-switch 2.4ms), representing only ~1.45% of typical 7B generation latency. Key Results: On individual benchmarks (2K context), A-IO (Actual) achieves: C-eval 79.35% / 19.80 TPS, MMLU 88.10% / 16.95 TPS, GSM8K 82.15% / 17.30 TPS, Human-eval 67.10% / 20.85 TPS — closely tracking the theoretical oracle while outperforming all static baselines in combined accuracy-throughput. Under mixed workloads, A-IO dominates: Scenario A (code-centric) yields 70.85% accuracy at 19.80 TPS vs. 68.04% / 16.75 TPS for static 7B; Scenario B (knowledge-centric) yields 76.50% / 18.15 TPS vs. 75.30% / 16.86 TPS for static 7B; Scenario C (long-context mixed) yields 87.32% / 13.40 TPS vs. 87.31% / 11.20 TPS for static 7B — a 19.6% throughput gain with equivalent accuracy. The 1B probe achieves 92.0% classification accuracy (Code recall 94%, QA 89%, Math 93%), and the entropy threshold bounds accuracy degradation from misrouting to under 1.5% vs. a theoretical oracle. Ablation confirms all three components (model routing, PLD switch, entropy fallback) each contribute meaningfully. Strengths: - Identifies and precisely quantifies a genuine NPU-specific engineering reality (static graph compilation preventing speculative decoding, dequantization overhead negating quantization) rather than relying on theoretical arguments - The coarse-grained routing design is hardware-appropriate: a single-token probe forward pass imposes negligible overhead (~15ms) while delivering meaningful bandwidth savings (7.1 TB → 1.0 TB per batch) - Ablation study is rigorous; error-penalty analysis explicitly factors in probe misclassification rates rather than reporting only oracle performance Weaknesses / Limitations: - The evaluation is restricted to Open-Pangu 1B/7B models on a single NPU platform (Ascend 910B); generalizability to other model families (e.g., LLaMA, Qwen) or NPU architectures is unvalidated - The routing scheme is brittle to ambiguous or multi-intent queries, and the binary context-length threshold (≤2K vs. 32K) is coarse — no evaluation of intermediate context lengths (e.g., 4K–16K) where routing boundaries would be less clear - The paper explicitly lacks comparison against any GPU-based dynamic routing frameworks (FrugalGPT, RouteLLM), justifying this by NPU portability constraints; while the reasoning is sound, it limits the reader's ability to assess A-IO's absolute competitiveness against the broader state of the art - All experiments use HuggingFace Transformers rather than production-grade inference engines (vLLM, MindIE), which means reported TPS numbers may not reflect deployable performance and the baselines may be unfairly weak
Search Results 2604.09752 PDF
#176 — Moderately Innovative (42)

Watt Counts: Energy-Aware Benchmark for Sustainable LLM Inference on Heterogeneous GPU Architectures

Mauricio Fadel Argerich, Jonathan Fürst, Marta Patiño-Martínez · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: Watt Counts provides the largest open empirical dataset of LLM inference energy across GPU architectures, and the finding that GPU efficiency rankings invert entirely between batch and server scenarios due to idle power dominance is actionable and non-obvious. The mixed-effects regression identifying KV head count as the strongest architectural energy driver beyond model size is a useful design insight. However, this is fundamentally a measurement and benchmarking contribution rather than a methodological advance.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: LLM inference deployments consume substantial and growing energy, yet system operators lack empirical data to make hardware-aware deployment decisions. While training energy has received attention, inference dominates total operational energy because models serve requests continuously. Existing benchmarks either cover too few GPUs, restrict to batch scenarios only, omit GPU-level energy measurements, or are not publicly available. This leaves practitioners unable to reason about energy-performance trade-offs across heterogeneous GPU fleets. Methodology: The authors introduce Watt Counts: an open dataset and reproducible benchmark covering 50 open-source LLMs (0.1B–30B parameters, dense and MoE) across 10 NVIDIA GPUs spanning five architectures (Volta through Hopper), in both batch and server deployment scenarios. Energy is measured via NVML through the EnergyMeter library at 10 Hz, without external power meters, validated against RAPL measurements showing GPU accounts for 78.7–92.5% of total system energy. The benchmark uses vLLM as the inference engine with SQuAD v1.1 QA prompts, Poisson-distributed server load at two rates (λ=0.017 and λ=0.3), and five independent iterations per configuration after thermal stabilization. Three research questions structure the analysis: (RQ1) which GPU architectural features correlate with energy efficiency, (RQ2) which LLM architectural properties predict energy consumption, and (RQ3) how deployment scenario shifts the optimal GPU selection. For RQ2, mixed-effects regression models are used to disentangle model size effects from architectural features (hidden size, layer count, attention heads, KV heads), with features residualized against log(parameters) to avoid multicollinearity. Key Results: The dataset comprises 5,000+ experiments across 370 LLM-GPU pairs and 14M+ power draw samples. RQ1 (batch): The H100 achieves lowest energy per token for 90% of models; however, the L4 and A30 beat it for small models due to their low TDPs (72W and 165W), demonstrating that TFLOPS/Watt is a poor system-level proxy. The H200 achieves highest throughput (38–58% higher than H100) due to 25% more memory bandwidth, but this does not translate to best energy efficiency. RQ2: Energy scales sublinearly with active parameters (exponent ~0.237–0.282, p<0.001; a 10× parameter increase raises energy/token ~1.7×). Beyond size, KV head count has the strongest independent effect on energy (β=0.121, p<0.001), attention head count also increases energy (β=0.070, p=0.004), while wider hidden size reduces energy (β=−0.098, p=0.026). RQ3 (server): GPU rankings invert entirely. Low-power GPUs (T4: 12W idle, L4: 17W idle, A30: 27W idle) dominate server efficiency rankings because idle periods represent 53–96% of server time. Informed GPU selection versus blindly using the H200 can cut server-scenario energy by up to 70% (e.g., A30 vs. H200 for Llama 3.1 8B at low load) with negligible latency impact at TTFT <70ms, and up to 20% in batch scenarios (H100 over H200 for XLarge models). Strengths: - Largest open empirical dataset of LLM inference energy, enabling data-driven hardware selection that was previously impossible due to lack of cross-GPU, cross-model data. - Rigorous methodology: thermal stabilization between runs, NVML validation against RAPL, 5-minute server iterations shown to be within <2% of 1-hour measurements, and reproducible without external power meters. - Quantitative modeling goes beyond rankings: the mixed-effects regression with residualized architectural features yields interpretable, statistically grounded insights (e.g., KV head count as the strongest architectural energy driver beyond size), useful for model design decisions. - Actionable practitioner guidance: TTFT-constrained GPU selection tables directly translate findings into deployment decisions. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Single-GPU scope only: multi-GPU deployments with tensor/pipeline parallelism, NVLink, and interconnect effects are excluded, limiting applicability to models above ~30B parameters or production clusters. - All models evaluated at 16-bit precision; quantized inference (INT8, INT4), which is widespread in practice, is not covered and could substantially shift energy rankings, particularly for memory-bandwidth-constrained GPUs. - Only vLLM is evaluated as the inference engine; engine-specific optimizations in TensorRT-LLM or SGLang may alter relative GPU rankings for specific model-GPU pairs.
Search Results 2604.09048 PDF
#177 — Moderately Innovative (42)

K-STAMM: a knowledge-enhanced spatial–temporal attention model with multimodal fusion for pneumonia prediction

nature.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: K-STAMM's time-decay LSTM for irregular EHR sampling is a principled design choice, and the rigorous bootstrap CI evaluation adds credibility. However, the individual components (DenseNet for imaging, LSTM for sequences, self-attention for features, TransE for knowledge) are well-established, and the multimodal fusion reduces to simple concatenation despite the paper's framing of cross-modal attention. Single-institution evaluation on MIMIC limits generalizability claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Accurate pneumonia prediction from clinical data requires integrating highly heterogeneous sources: longitudinal electronic health records (EHRs), chest X-ray images, clinical narrative text, and domain knowledge. Most existing multimodal transformer-based models struggle with three specific challenges: (1) multimodal alignment across modalities with incompatible semantics, (2) temporal irregularity in EHR visit sequences, and (3) limited incorporation of structured biomedical knowledge. The paper targets this gap by proposing a unified framework that fuses all four data types while explicitly addressing each of these challenges. Methodology: K-STAMM integrates four parallel components into a single patient representation. For imaging, a DenseNet-121 backbone pretrained on ImageNet extracts 1024-dimensional feature vectors from chest X-rays (224×224 input, standard augmentation). For temporal EHR modeling, a two-layer time-aware LSTM handles irregular sampling via an exponential decay mechanism: h_t = LSTM(x_t, h_{t-1} · e^{-Δt}), where Δt is the inter-visit time gap; hidden size is 256 with dropout 0.3. For spatial EHR modeling, a 4-head self-attention transformer (embedding dim 128, 2 layers) captures cross-feature dependencies without requiring explicit graph construction. For domain knowledge, clinical concepts from both structured EHR fields and clinical notes are mapped to UMLS Concept Unique Identifiers (CUIs) via MetaMap, then embedded as 128-dimensional TransE vectors; these embeddings are frozen during training. The four modality embeddings are fused via simple feature concatenation followed by a fully connected layer and sigmoid output. Training used Adam (lr=1e-4), ReduceLROnPlateau scheduler, batch size 32, up to 30 epochs with early stopping at patience 5. Experiments used MIMIC-IV (EHR + text) and MIMIC-CXR (chest radiographs) with 70/15/15 patient-level stratified splits. Key Results: On the held-out test set, K-STAMM achieves AUROC 0.953 (95% CI: 0.947–0.959), AUPRC 0.962 (0.956–0.968), F1 0.910 (0.902–0.918), accuracy 0.912, precision 0.889, and recall 0.934. Against baselines: image-only DenseNet-121 (AUROC 0.842, F1 0.737), ClinicalBERT text-only (0.862, 0.751), EHR Transformer (0.881, 0.766), early fusion multimodal (0.903, 0.814), and late fusion multimodal (0.927, 0.851). K-STAMM improves AUROC by +0.111 over image-only and +0.026 over late fusion. Ablation shows removing temporal attention costs −0.037 AUROC (largest single-component drop), removing cross-modal attention costs −0.026, and removing knowledge embeddings costs −0.015. All differences are statistically significant (non-overlapping bootstrap CIs, p < 0.05). Training converged smoothly from 71% to 91% accuracy over 30 epochs with minimal train/val divergence. Strengths: - Principled handling of temporal irregularity via time-decay LSTM rather than ad-hoc padding or uniform resampling, directly addressing a known failure mode in EHR modeling. - Graph-free spatial modeling via self-attention avoids the computational overhead and graph construction assumptions of GNNs while still capturing inter-feature dependencies. - Rigorous evaluation with bootstrap confidence intervals and paired hypothesis testing for all ablations, making significance claims credible rather than speculative. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is confined to a single institution (MIMIC-IV + MIMIC-CXR from Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center); no external validation on independently collected data, raising genuine generalizability concerns the authors themselves acknowledge. - Multimodal fusion reduces to simple feature concatenation — the claimed 'cross-modal attention' in the architecture diagram is architecturally modest compared to the paper's framing; there is no learned cross-modal alignment mechanism (e.g., contrastive objectives or co-attention) between imaging and EHR spaces. - UMLS knowledge embeddings are static (non-trainable TransE vectors), meaning the knowledge component cannot adapt to the clinical task or the specific patient population; this design choice is pragmatic but limits the model's ability to learn task-relevant ontological relationships.
Search Results
#178 — Moderately Innovative (41)

GPVI: An Internal Index Based on Grouping Partition for Clustering Validity Validation: J. Li, X. Pan

link.springer.com · karypis - new results
Why this rank: GPVI's parameter-free design and MST-based handling of non-convex geometry address real usability pain points in clustering validation. However, the O(N²) complexity limits practicality, the evaluation uses only small datasets (≤2000 points), and the methodology of judging index quality by external ARI of selected partitions conflates index quality with the candidate clustering pool — a fundamental evaluation concern that weakens the empirical contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Internal clustering validity indices (CVIs) are essential in unsupervised learning because ground-truth labels are typically unavailable. Most existing internal indices — such as Silhouette Coefficient, Davies-Bouldin, Calinski-Harabasz, and even newer ones like DBCV and VIASCKDE — assume convex or Gaussian cluster geometry, require hand-tuned hyperparameters, or can only address one challenging property (e.g., non-convexity) at a time. This paper targets the gap of a single parameter-free index that simultaneously handles non-convex shapes, cluster imbalance, mild noise, overlap, and mixed-shape distributions. Methodology: The core idea is "grouping partition": each cluster in a candidate solution is decomposed into sub-clusters by (1) computing its Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) and (2) removing long edges identified by an adaptive Three-Sigma rule (threshold decremented from 3σ to 1σ if no cuts are found). This sub-cluster structure yields two quantities per cluster: sub-cluster intra-distance (weighted average edge length within sub-clusters) and sub-cluster inter-distance (weighted average cutting-edge length). Their arithmetic mean becomes the cluster-level intra-class dispersion S_intra.
