#187 — Incremental (37)

AI That Moves With You: A Review of Interactive Technologies Powered by Large Foundation Models for Mobility Impairment

dl.acm.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: This scoping review fills a legitimate gap by synthesizing FM-based assistive technologies for mobility impairment, and the four FM integration roles (reasoner, orchestrator, planner-actuator bridge, accelerator) provide useful design vocabulary. However, it is methodologically a standard PRISMA-ScR literature review over only 26 papers, and the synthesis inherits the validity limitations of the small, lab-based studies it surveys.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Mobility impairments affect hundreds of millions of people worldwide, yet interactive assistive technologies have historically relied on narrow, hand-crafted pipelines and rigid input-output modalities. The rapid emergence of large foundation models (FMs) — including LLMs, LVMs, and VLMs — creates new opportunities for flexible, adaptive assistive systems. Despite a surge of research prototypes from 2022 onward, no prior work had systematically synthesized how FMs are being integrated into interactive technologies specifically for mobility-impaired populations. This gap motivates a scoping review to map the design space, identify patterns, and surface open challenges. Methodology: The authors conducted a PRISMA-ScR scoping review across five databases (ACM DL, IEEE Xplore, PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science) covering January 2020 to May 2025. From 6,249 initial records, 26 full papers were included after deduplication, abstract screening, full-text assessment, and snowballing. Inclusion required a practical interactive system implementing an FM for users with mobility impairments; standalone model evaluations, conceptual frameworks, and non-FM-based systems were excluded. The authors adopted a functional definition of mobility impairment encompassing BLV, motor, neurological (Parkinson's), and DHH populations. A structured codebook with 18 codes across six dimensions (research objectives, evaluation, model class, interactive technology, impairment type, challenges) was applied collaboratively by three authors. Synthesis used a mixed narrative-tabular approach with an alluvial plot to visualize relationships across model class, technology type, impairment, and problem domain. Key Results: The 26 papers span 2022-2025, with 89% published in 2024-2025, reflecting explosive recent growth. GPT-4/4o dominated as backend (51.9% of papers); VLMs appeared in 33.3%. Specialized perception and planning modules (SAM, YOLO, DepthAnything, Whisper) were the most widely adopted auxiliary tools (73.1%), while full fine-tuning was rare (7.7%). Prompt engineering was near-universal (92.3%); RAG appeared in 23.1% and safety-oriented techniques in 34.6%. Technology platforms split across mobile/web (42.3%), conversational agents (26.9%), wearables (15.4%), and robotics (15.4%). BLV users were the dominant target population (53.8% of papers), while wheelchair users received minimal direct attention (3.8%). Problem domains concentrated on information access and comprehension (33.3%), navigation and environment understanding (29.6%), and health self-management (29.6%); physical assistance was largely underexplored (7.4%). FM integration roles were categorized as reasoner (59%), orchestrator (19%), accelerator (11%), and planner-actuator bridge (4%). Notable systems include SpeakFaster (accelerated AAC for ALS via LLM abbreviation expansion, achieving measurable keystroke savings over 6 months), VoicePilot (natural language control of an assistive feeding robot, task success evaluated with 11 older adults in a care facility), and WanderGuide (map-less indoor robot guide for blind users, evaluated in a museum setting). Technical challenges include latency and compute constraints in multimodal pipelines, fragile multi-stage retrieval, LLM reasoning limits (context window loss, spatial grounding failures), and narrow domain datasets. Ethical challenges include privacy and bystander exposure from continuous sensing, hallucination risks in safety-critical health and robotic contexts, dataset bias skewed toward English and high-resource settings, and tension between AI autonomy and user agency/dignity. Strengths: - Provides the first systematic synthesis of FM-based interactive technologies for mobility impairment, filling a genuine gap and offering a reproducible codebook and alluvial visualization that reveals non-obvious cross-cutting patterns (e.g., wheelchair users appearing in information access rather than physical assistance domains). - The functional rather than clinical definition of mobility impairment is well-motivated and broadens scope appropriately to include BLV navigation, Parkinson's gait, and DHH vestibular dysfunction alongside motor impairments. - The four FM integration roles (reasoner, orchestrator, planner-actuator bridge, accelerator) constitute a concrete and useful design vocabulary that goes beyond prior taxonomies, with clear implications for HCI evaluation frameworks. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The final corpus of only 26 papers is quite small; the authors acknowledge rigorous exclusion criteria may have missed relevant work, and no formal inter-rater reliability was computed, making coding reproducibility uncertain. - The corpus is heavily skewed toward BLV users (54%) and mobile/web platforms, leaving wheelchair users (3.8%), exoskeleton/prosthesis users, and direct physical assistance nearly unstudied — the review documents this gap but cannot resolve it, limiting actionable guidance for the most acute mobility needs. - Most included studies have small participant counts (often N < 15), short durations, and lab or proxy settings (blindfolded sighted participants, mock clinical documents), so the review's synthesis inherits these validity limitations and cannot draw reliable conclusions about real-world deployment effectiveness.
Citations
#188 — Incremental (36)

OQuaRE-KG: an OQuaRE inspired framework for knowledge graph quality assessment

ceur-ws.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: OQuaRE-KG fills a genuine gap by providing the first unified, hierarchical quality framework bridging ontology quality, data quality standards, and FAIR principles for knowledge graphs. The drill-down diagnostic capability is practically useful. However, the metric scaling lacks empirical grounding, evaluation is confined to a single biomedical project, and several quality characteristics yield flat, non-discriminative scores.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Knowledge graphs (KGs) are increasingly central to semantic data integration and AI systems, yet quality assurance for KGs remains fragmented. Existing tools address isolated aspects — schema validation (SHACL, ShEx), ontological reasoning (HermiT), linked data metrics (Luzzu), or ontology quality (OQuaRE) — without offering an integrated, reproducible framework that handles the dual nature of KGs (schema-level ontology + instance-level data). The gap is that ontology quality metrics do not transfer directly to KG quality, since instantiated graphs may diverge from their schema depending on data sources. Methodology: OQuaRE-KG adapts the OQuaRE ontology quality framework (itself based on ISO/IEC 25000 SQuaRE) to KGs. The framework defines 7 top-level quality characteristics (structural, functional adequacy, compatibility, transferability, operability, reliability, maintainability), 16 subcharacteristics, and 28 metrics. Metrics are computed directly from RDF graphs using SPARQL-accessible properties and are normalized to a Likert scale [1,5]: binary metrics map to 1 or 5; [0,1]-range metrics are binned into 20% intervals; other ranges are first normalized to [0,1]. Subcharacteristic and characteristic scores are averages of their constituent metric scores. The framework integrates sources from ISO/IEC 25012, FAIR principles, linked data quality surveys (Zaveri et al.), and OQuaRE. It was applied to five biological KGs from the BioGateway knowledge network related to cis-regulatory modules (CRM), ranging from 12,933 to 1,622,550 triples. Key Results: The five KGs — crm, crm2tfac, crm2phen, crm2gene, and all (union) — cluster into three quality groups. crm and all score highest and identically (crm dominates >90% of all). crm2tfac and crm2gene score the same but lower. crm2phen scores lowest for functional adequacy (trustworthiness score of 1 vs. 3 for all others), attributed to the absence of evidence annotation properties in its source data. All five graphs score identically on compatibility (3), transferability (1), operability (1), reliability (2), and maintainability (1), reflecting shared metadata/process decisions across the project. Key differentiating metrics are: (1) entities-with-no-type ratio, which drives differences in consistency, interpretability, inference, and clustering subcharacteristics for the smaller relation graphs (crm2tfac, crm2phen, crm2gene) whose entity definitions reside in other graphs; and (2) the evidence metric for trustworthiness. At metric level, crm and all achieve 16 metrics at score 5 and 9 at score 1; crm2phen achieves only 13 at score 5 and 10 at score 1. Strengths: - Provides the first unified, hierarchical quality framework for KGs that coherently bridges ontology quality (OQuaRE/SQuaRE), data quality (ISO/IEC 25012), linked data evaluation, and FAIR principles into a single model covering both schema and instance levels. - The three-tier hierarchy (characteristics → subcharacteristics → metrics) enables drill-down diagnosis: the framework correctly traced quality differences to the single metric 'entities with no type', offering actionable feedback to KG developers. - Evaluation on a real, multi-graph biomedical KG demonstrates discriminative power — the framework successfully differentiates graphs that differ semantically (relation-only graphs lacking type definitions) from those that are structurally complete. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The metric-to-quality-score scaling is a simple uniform-interval function with no empirical grounding; the authors acknowledge this prevents strong interpretation of scores and that thresholds for 'good' vs. 'poor' quality are not yet defined — a critical limitation for practical adoption. - The evaluation is confined to five graphs from a single biomedical project sharing the same schema and curation pipeline, making it impossible to assess generalizability across heterogeneous domains or KG construction methodologies; no comparison to existing frameworks (e.g., Luzzu, Zaveri taxonomy) is provided. - Several characteristics (transferability, operability, maintainability, reliability) yield flat scores across all tested graphs and appear to measure project-level metadata choices rather than per-graph quality, limiting their diagnostic utility in same-project evaluations.
Search Results
#189 — Incremental (35)

ConfigSpec: Profiling-Based Configuration Selection for Distributed Edge--Cloud Speculative LLM Serving

Xiangchen Li, Saeid Ghafouri, Jiakun Fan, Babar Ali, Hans Vandierendonck, Dimitrios S. Nikolopoulos · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: The bonus-token effect explanation for why K=2 dominates cost and energy efficiency is a clean structural insight that unifies observations across devices. However, this is a 6-page workshop paper presenting an analytical framework with fixed-parameter assumptions (constant T_verify, no batching) rather than a deployed system, and the closed-form metrics, while useful, are relatively straightforward derivations from standard speculative decoding throughput models.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Deploying large language models at the edge requires speculative decoding—where a lightweight draft model on an edge device proposes tokens verified by a heavyweight target model in the cloud—but practical deployment exposes a large, non-trivial configuration space. Practitioners must jointly choose the draft model family, model size, quantization level, speculative length K, and edge hardware. These choices interact in counter-intuitive ways: a larger draft improves acceptance rate but hurts throughput and energy; a longer speculative sequence helps on fast devices but hurts on slow ones. No prior work systematically characterizes these conflicts or provides principled configuration selection across the full joint space. Methodology: ConfigSpec is an analytical profiling framework that avoids repeated full-system deployment. For each (draft model, edge device, target model) triple, it measures three primitives: drafting throughput v_d (tokens/s), draft–target acceptance rate α(K) at varying speculative lengths, and edge device power draw P. These feed into three closed-form metrics: (1) goodput G(K) = (K·α(K)+1) / (K/v_d + T_verify), which captures verified token throughput under a round model with fixed cloud verification latency T_verify; (2) cost efficiency η_cost = (α(K) + 1/K) / p, accepted tokens per dollar under token-priced billing; and (3) energy per verified token E = P·(K/v_d) / (K·α(K)+1), counting only on-device drafting energy. The framework exhaustively enumerates the joint (M, Q, K) space with K ∈ {2,...,10}, evaluated across three edge platforms (Raspberry Pi 4B, Raspberry Pi 5, Jetson AGX Orin) and two target LLM families (Llama-3.1-70B, Qwen3-32B), using Databricks Dolly 15K prompts. Draft models span 0.6B–8B parameters with Q4_K_M to Q8_0 quantization via llama.cpp. Key Results: The analysis surfaces three structurally conflicting optima. First, goodput is maximized by the smallest, fastest draft (Llama-3.2-1B or Qwen3-0.6B at Q4) at a device-dependent K: K=2 on RPi 4B, K=6–7 on RPi 5, K=8–10 on Jetson. Despite a ~22× raw speed gap between Jetson and RPi 4B, goodput only spans 3.1× (7.65 vs. 2.44 tok/s), because T_verify compresses the range. Second, cost efficiency is device-independent and universally peaks at K=2 with the largest draft (8B), driven by the bonus-token effect: at K=2 the one guaranteed output token contributes 1/K=0.5 yield, whereas at K=10 it contributes only 0.1. This yields α(2)+0.5≈1.26 accepted tokens per verified token—the global maximum. Third, energy efficiency mirrors goodput's model choice (smallest draft) but also locks to K=2, since minimizing local drafting time minimizes joules per round while the bonus-token effect keeps yield high. The conflicts are large: on RPi 5, switching from cost-optimal (8B, K=2) to goodput-optimal (1B, K=6) improves throughput 2.9× and energy 7.8× but sacrifices 46% cost efficiency. The Jetson's energy-optimal config (0.39 J/tok) still achieves G=4.60 tok/s—60% of its peak—making it a practical default for battery-constrained deployments. Strengths: - The three closed-form metrics are analytically grounded and directly interpretable, enabling fast configuration sweeps without repeated cloud deployments. - The bonus-token effect explanation for why K=2 dominates both cost and energy is a clean, non-obvious structural insight that unifies seemingly disparate observations across devices and model families. - The exhaustive joint (M, Q, K) enumeration across three real edge platforms and two LLM families provides empirical breadth, with Table 2 surfacing concrete, actionable configuration recommendations per deployment objective. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The paper is a 6-page workshop paper, so the framework is analytical rather than a deployed system; T_verify is treated as a fixed scalar parameter (0.5s) rather than measured under realistic batching, queuing, and network variability, which can substantially alter goodput rankings. - Cost modeling assumes token-priced billing without batching, explicitly acknowledged as a simplification; real cloud inference is billed under continuous batching where per-token cost depends on batch size and concurrency, potentially reversing the K=2 dominance result. - Power measurement is unavailable for Raspberry Pi 4B (noted as a hardware limitation), leaving energy results incomplete for the most resource-constrained platform, which is precisely where energy constraints are most binding.
Search Results 2604.09722 PDF
#190 — Incremental (35)

Networking-Aware Energy Efficiency in Agentic AI Inference : A Survey

Xiaojing Chen, Haiqi Yu, Wei Ni, Dusit Niyato, Ruichen Zhang, Xin Wang, Shunqing Zhang, Shugong Xu · arxiv.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: This survey attempts to unify algorithmic, hardware, and networking dimensions for agentic AI energy efficiency, but its taxonomy is largely inherited from prior LLM efficiency surveys without convincingly demonstrating how agentic systems change the optimization landscape. The cross-layer co-design section, its most distinctive contribution, remains aspirational rather than empirically grounded.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Agentic AI systems—autonomous pipelines integrating Perception, Reasoning, and Action into closed-loop feedback loops—impose compounding energy demands that differ fundamentally from static, single-pass LLM inference. Unlike traditional AI where the bottleneck is computational FLOPs, Agentic AI creates a dual bottleneck: memory-bound inference (DRAM access costs ~640 pJ per access vs. <1 pJ for arithmetic) and persistent communication overhead from agent-server-edge interactions. Existing surveys either address algorithmic compression (quantization, pruning) without the networking dimension, or treat AI as a black-box workload in edge/wireless settings without accounting for the iterative, multi-step nature of agentic reasoning. This survey attempts to bridge both dimensions under one unified framework. Methodology: The paper proposes an energy accounting framework that characterizes the four modules of Agentic AI—Perception (compute-bound, dominated by vision encoder FLOPs), Reasoning (memory-bound, dominated by KV cache and weight access), Action (communication-bound, dominated by multi-agent synchronization), and Memory (bandwidth-bound, dominated by KV cache fragmentation)—and then organizes energy-reduction techniques into a four-pillar taxonomy: (1) Model Simplification covering quantization (3–8 bit), pruning (structured and unstructured), knowledge distillation, sparse MoE activation, and action-space reduction; (2) Computation Control covering token length control, early exit/adaptive depth, layer skipping, speculative decoding, and workload scheduling; (3) Input and Attention Optimization covering token pruning, sparse attention (FlashAttention), and KV cache compression and reuse; and (4) Hardware-Aware Inference covering mixed-precision scheduling, DVFS, and memory/I/O co-scheduling. A fifth dimension covers cross-layer co-design—jointly optimizing model parameters, wireless transmission, and edge resources through split inference, adaptive offloading, collaborative caching, semantic communication, and retrieval-augmented communication. Key Results: As a survey, the paper synthesizes results from ~143 cited works rather than presenting new experiments. Representative findings include: 3–4 bit quantization cuts energy by up to 79% versus FP16 but degrades complex reasoning tasks (e.g., GSM8K) by 20–30%, illustrating the efficiency-accuracy trade-off that remains unresolved. Token pruning (AIM framework) reduces FLOPs by 6.8× and prefill time by 8× while retaining 99.7% of baseline accuracy on video benchmarks. Speculative decoding (EdgeLLM) achieves up to 9.3× per-token speedup without accuracy loss. SwapMoE reduces memory by 67% and latency by 50% for MoE deployment on consumer devices. Multi-agent DNN decoupling reduces inference latency by up to 56% and energy by up to 72% in collaborative settings. DVFS (FUSE) reduces latency by 7–36.8% under the same energy budget. GreenLLM energy-aware pruning cuts energy and latency by over 30% on Llama-7B. These figures collectively demonstrate that no single technique suffices and that multiplicative gains require stacking complementary methods. Strengths: - First survey to jointly address algorithmic, hardware, and networking dimensions for Agentic AI energy efficiency, filling a genuine gap relative to prior work that treats these as disjoint concerns. - The per-module energy accounting framework (compute-bound Perception, memory-bound Reasoning, communication-bound Action, bandwidth-bound Memory) is a useful structural contribution that grounds optimization choices in hardware realities. - The forward-looking agenda—federated green learning, carbon-aware agency, 6G-native Agentic AI, and energy harvesting for self-sustaining systems—is well-motivated and points to genuinely open problems. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The four-pillar taxonomy (quantization, pruning, early exit, KV caching, etc.) is largely inherited from prior LLM efficiency surveys and is not specific to Agentic AI; the paper does not convincingly demonstrate how the closed-loop, multi-step nature of agentic systems changes which techniques are most effective or how they should be composed. - The cross-layer co-design section, the most distinctive claimed contribution, is the thinnest empirically—most cited works are isolated system proposals rather than results from integrated agent deployments, leaving the claimed 'holistic' optimization framework as largely aspirational rather than demonstrated. - The survey covers a very broad literature with limited critical synthesis; many subsections read as annotated bibliographies rather than distilled principles, making it difficult to extract actionable design guidance for practitioners building real agentic systems.
Search Results 2604.07857 PDF
#191 — Incremental (35)

A knowledge-driven semantic framework for non-functional requirement engineering in intelligent healthcare systems

link.springer.com · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: The two-layer ontology architecture (HDO + NFRO) with embedded BERT classification and SWRL conflict detection is a reasonable hybrid approach. However, the framework is validated on a single case study with only 20 stakeholders, some reported performance figures are acknowledged as 'assumed example values' rather than measured, and the BERT classifier is trained on generic software requirements with minimal healthcare adaptation (50 examples).
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Non-functional requirements (NFRs) such as reliability, privacy, explainability, and performance are critical to trustworthy AI-driven healthcare systems, yet they are systematically underserved by traditional requirements engineering (RE) methods. The core problem is that existing approaches either formalize NFRs without automation (ontology-only) or automate classification without semantic grounding (ML-only), yielding poor traceability, weak conflict detection, and limited regulatory alignment. In AI-enabled healthcare, this gap is especially acute because system behavior evolves post-deployment (e.g., model drift alters latency and explainability requirements), heterogeneous data sources complicate elicitation, and regulations such as HIPAA and GDPR impose strict auditability demands that static UML-based RE cannot satisfy. Methodology: The paper proposes a five-component hybrid framework that tightly integrates ontology engineering with BERT-based machine learning. The central architectural novelty is a two-layer ontology: (1) a Healthcare Domain Ontology (HDO) built on SNOMED CT, HL7 FHIR, and LOINC, modeling clinical entities such as Patient and ClinicalData; and (2) a Non-Functional Requirement Ontology (NFRO) grounded in the DOLCE foundational ontology, defining NFR classes (Security, Reliability, Usability, Scalability, Explainability) with attributes for priority, stakeholder, risk, and measurement metric. Both layers are implemented in OWL 2 using the NeOn methodology and linked via object properties (e.g., 'requires', 'affectsComponent', 'isImposedBy'). NFR elicitation uses BERT-base-uncased fine-tuned on the PROMISE NFR dataset (625 requirements, 11 classes, 80/20 split, 5-fold CV, learning rate 3e-5, batch size 32, 4 epochs) to classify and recommend missing NFRs via cosine similarity on embeddings. Conflict detection employs SWRL rules over the ontology (e.g., privacy vs. performance trade-offs) combined with Description Logic reasoners (HermiT, Pellet), while ML-based ranking dynamically prioritizes mitigation strategies using historical stakeholder decisions. SPARQL queries provide end-to-end traceability linking each NFR to its originating stakeholder, use case, or system component. The case study target is an AI-enabled Intelligent Clinical Decision Support System (ICDSS) for chronic heart failure (CHF), with a 3-layer feedforward neural network (128→64→32→1, ReLU, dropout 0.3) as the clinical prediction engine. Key Results: Evaluated against a baseline of manual NFR handling with spreadsheets and UML, the framework demonstrated: a 64% increase in NFR coverage (14→23 distinct NFRs), identifying 9 new NFRs including explainability and compliance requirements; a 64% improvement in traceability score (2.8→4.6 on a 1–5 Likert scale); a 42% reduction in validation time (12→7 hours per iteration); and a 91% stakeholder satisfaction rate (vs. 68% baseline), statistically validated via Wilcoxon signed-rank test (p<0.05). Conflict detection rate increased from 21.4% (3/14) to 30.4% (7/23), with the increase attributed to early surfacing of latent trade-offs. The BERT classifier achieved average F1=0.87 across five NFR categories (Security 0.89, Performance 0.91, Usability 0.86, Reliability 0.83, Explainability 0.82), significantly outperforming SVM (F1=0.76; paired t-test t=4.5, df=4, p<0.01). Domain-adaptive augmentation with 50 healthcare-specific requirements reduced healthcare-term misclassifications by 15%, improving F1 from 0.71±0.04 to 0.82±0.03 on those terms. Strengths: - The two-layer ontology architecture (HDO + NFRO) is well-motivated and modular, enabling independent updates to domain or NFR layers without global propagation — a meaningful engineering advantage over monolithic ontologies. - Embedding AI (BERT, ML ranking) directly within the ontology reasoning layer rather than as a preprocessing step enables genuine semantic traceability propagation and explainable conflict justification via SWRL rule firing paths — a meaningful departure from prior loosely-coupled hybrid methods. - The empirical evaluation is relatively thorough for this subfield, combining quantitative metrics (F1, traceability score, validation time), statistical testing (paired t-test, Wilcoxon), and multi-stakeholder qualitative assessment across developers, clinicians, and regulatory experts. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The entire empirical validation rests on a single healthcare case study (ICDSS/CHF) with only 20 stakeholder participants, severely limiting external validity; results are explicitly characterized as 'indicative rather than conclusive,' and generalizability to other clinical domains remains undemonstrated. - The BERT classifier is trained on the PROMISE NFR dataset, a collection from generic software projects not specific to healthcare, and the domain adaptation involves only 50 augmented examples — far too small to reliably establish healthcare-domain generalization, and healthcare-specific NFR categories (e.g., evolving regulatory terminology) remain underrepresented. - Several reported performance figures (TP=88, FP=12, FN=15 in the case study) are described as 'assumed example values' from a cited baseline rather than independently measured on the ICDSS system, raising concerns about the authenticity of the primary case-study classification results.
Search Results
#192 — Incremental (34)

OASIS: Optimal Allocation Strategy for Inference Services in Cloud Environments

frachtenberg.org · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: OASIS provides a useful empirical characterization of LLM inference energy efficiency across GPU configurations and query types, and the SISO/SILO/LISO/LILO taxonomy concretely quantifies throughput differences missed by uniform provisioning. However, the methodology is straightforward profiling followed by lookup-table provisioning with single-parameter sweeps, and the runtime validation on only 153 requests over 4 hours is too narrow to substantiate the general claims.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Cloud providers deploying language models as inference services face a multi-dimensional optimization challenge: minimizing hardware and energy costs (both capex and opex) while satisfying P95/P99 latency SLOs under highly variable, heterogeneous workloads. GPUs draw substantial power even when underutilized, real-world request rates can spike to 36 queries/second from 3,000 concurrent users, and query types vary dramatically in input/output token counts—each with different compute and memory profiles. Existing work addresses individual concerns (DVFS-only, batching-only, or hardware benchmarking) but no prior system jointly integrates pre-deployment profiling, query-type-aware routing, hardware selection, and MIG-based multi-tenancy under a unified framework. Methodology: OASIS employs a two-phase methodology. Phase 1 (pre-deployment profiling) systematically sweeps each model across hardware configurations (A100, H100), GPU clock frequencies (855–1410 MHz), batch sizes (16–64), and inference variants (standard, prefix caching, chunked prefill), separately for each of four query types (SISO, SILO, LISO, LILO). For each configuration, OASIS measures the Maximum Serviceable Request Rate (MSRR)—the peak query rate that keeps P95 latency within the SLO—along with power and energy per token. Results populate a Hardware Cost Table (HCT). Phase 2 (runtime provisioning) monitors live query arrival rates, classifies each query by token count, computes the fractional GPU capacity required as RR(QT)/MSRR, and selects either MIG partitioning (for sub-GPU loads) or full GPU allocation (for loads ≥ 1 GPU). Multi-tenant packing via NVIDIA MIG enables true hardware isolation between co-located models. The system uses vLLM with continuous batching as the serving backend. Notably, parameter optimization is done one dimension at a time (no joint optimization), which the authors acknowledge as a limitation deferred to future work. Key Results: Four empirical findings from Phase 1 profiling on A100/H100 with Llama-3-8B, Qwen-7B, Mistral-7B, Yi-6B, and starcoder2-15b: (1) GPU frequency scaling from 1320 MHz to 855 MHz reduces power by 39% (239.4W → 145.2W) with only 7% throughput loss and 34% improvement in energy per token; (2) LISO workloads achieve 2.1× higher throughput than SILO (e.g., Qwen-7B: 6.77 vs. 1.84 req/s), confirming that query-type-aware routing is essential; (3) energy efficiency varies 1.4× across 7B-15B models at identical GPU frequency (86.1–118.4 mJ/tok), with Mistral-7B being most efficient and starcoder2-15b least; (4) H100 delivers only 12% higher throughput than A100 but consumes 89% more power, making A100 preferable for energy-conscious deployments. For MIG-based multi-tenancy, running two models (Llama-3-8B + Qwen-7B) on a single MIG-partitioned A100 reduces aggregate power by 42–43% (467W → 270W for LISO, 467W → 265W for SILO) while meeting SLOs for LISO; SILO workloads operate at the edge of SLO compliance under MIG. Phase 2 runtime validation on a 4-hour BurstGPT campus trace (153 requests, CV=1.70 burstiness) shows MIG achieves 12.4% mean power reduction (188.2W → 164.9W, p < 10⁻¹⁸) with identical throughput (15.44 vs. 15.45 tok/s) and SLO compliance (P99 latency 11.5s vs. 20s threshold), saving 93.1 Wh and projecting to $10.67/day cost savings and 34.8 kg CO₂/day avoided emissions at campus scale. Strengths: - The four-dimensional query classification (SISO/SILO/LISO/LILO) is a practical and well-motivated taxonomic contribution that concretely quantifies throughput differences (2.1×) missed by uniform provisioning strategies. - The runtime validation uses a real-world workload trace (BurstGPT, 213-day Azure OpenAI logs) with statistically rigorous confirmation (t-test p < 10⁻¹⁸, Mann-Whitney p < 10⁻¹⁹) rather than synthetic benchmarks, lending credibility to the energy savings claims. - The MIG vs. full-GPU decision rule (fractional GPU requirement) is principled, simple to implement, and grounded in empirically measured MSRR values rather than heuristics. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Phase 1 profiling performs only single-parameter sweeps; joint optimization across frequency, batch size, and scheduling policy is explicitly deferred. This means OASIS may not find true global optima, and the individual parameter sensitivities reported could interact non-monotonically in ways not captured. - The Phase 2 runtime experiment uses a single 4-hour trace with only 153 requests (average 41 req/hr), which is a lightweight, narrow workload. The paper conflates this low-demand scenario (where MIG trivially wins) with a general validation of dynamic provisioning; no evaluation is presented for peak-load transitions where MIG-to-full-GPU switchover latency and re-deployment overhead could violate SLOs. - MIG for SILO workloads already violates the 20s SLO in the multi-tenancy experiment (21.85s and 20.01s), undermining the claim that OASIS maintains SLO compliance broadly—the paper qualifies this but the runtime algorithm as specified does not include a mechanism to detect and avoid such violations proactively.
Search Results
#193 — Incremental (34)

HaCOO: Hashed coordinate sparse tensor Storage for rapid data insertion, access, and updates

aimsciences.org · 5 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: HaCOO addresses a real gap — dynamic insertion for sparse tensor formats — and the decision to adopt ALTO's bit-packing as a unified compression/hash scheme is clever engineering. However, the paper critically fails to benchmark its primary claimed advantage (insertion throughput), the LBNL performance regression is left unexplained, and the evaluation covers only four tensors with no quantitative speedup numbers, making it impossible to assess the actual contribution.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Sparse tensor storage formats are critical for large-scale data analysis in fields such as data mining, machine learning, signal processing, and neuroscience. Existing high-performance formats—COO, HiCOO, CSF, and ALTO—are fundamentally static: they require sorting nonzeros and building fixed data structures that cannot accommodate incremental updates or insertions without a full rebuild. Sorted COO requires O(log M) binary search plus O(M) shifting to insert; ALTO provides no insertion mechanism at all. This gap is acutely felt in dynamic workloads such as streaming data or n-gram counting over sliding windows, where new tensor entries arrive continuously and full regeneration is prohibitively expensive. Methodology: The paper presents HaCOO (Hashed Coordinate), a mode-agnostic sparse tensor format that stores nonzeros in a separate-chaining hash table, using Bob Jenkins' One-at-a-Time hash applied to linearized indices. Hash table size is always a power of two (for fast modulo) and doubles when load factor exceeds 0.7, giving amortized O(1) insertion and retrieval. A key contribution of this paper's C++ implementation (hacoo-c) is replacing HaCOO's original Morton encoding with ALTO's adaptive bit-interleaving encoding, which uses fewer bits (sum of log2(In) bits across all N modes) and enables hardware-accelerated bit-gather/scatter operations. This also lets the ALTO-encoded index serve directly as the hash key, unifying compression and hashing. The parallel MTTKRP algorithm (the dominant kernel in CP-ALS decomposition) partitions hash buckets across OpenMP threads for lock-free local accumulation, then performs a parallel row-wise reduction into the global factor matrix. Key Results: Experiments on four FROSTT benchmark tensors (LBNL, NIPS, UBER, CHICAGO, ranging from 1.7M to 5.3M nonzeros) run on the Delta supercomputer using 16 threads show mixed but broadly competitive results. HaCOO outperforms ALTO on NIPS and CHICAGO across all modes. On UBER, the two are roughly equivalent with ALTO slightly faster. On LBNL—the most sparse tensor (density 4.2×10⁻¹⁴, 5-mode, 1.7M nnz)—HaCOO is significantly slower than ALTO. The authors note this result is unexplained: LBNL has the fewest nonzeros yet HaCOO performs worst, contradicting the expectation that runtime should scale with nnz count. No quantitative speedup ratios are reported; comparisons are only shown graphically. Strengths: - Fills a genuine gap: provides O(1) amortized insertion and retrieval for sparse tensors, a capability absent from all other high-performance formats (ALTO, HiCOO, CSF). - Clever engineering decision to adopt ALTO's bit-packing encoding instead of Morton, reducing index storage to the theoretical minimum and enabling hardware-efficient encode/decode while unifying compression with the hash function. - Parallel MTTKRP design partitions work by hash buckets, which hashing naturally load-balances across threads, avoiding synchronization overhead during the compute phase. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Evaluation is thin and insufficiently rigorous: only four tensors tested, no quantitative speedup numbers reported (results are graph-only), no comparison against CSF or HiCOO, no insertion/update benchmarks despite that being the primary claimed advantage. - The LBNL performance regression is unexplained and unresolved—the authors openly admit they do not understand why HaCOO underperforms on the sparsest tensor, which undermines confidence in the format's predictability. - No measurement of the core differentiating feature: dynamic insertion performance. The paper never benchmarks actual insertion throughput, update latency, or streaming scenarios, making it impossible to quantify the claimed O(1) benefit in practice.
Citations
#194 — Incremental (33)

Symphony as Compute Ontology : Extending Insight into OpenShift and NVIDIA AI Factories

kevindjohnson.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: The compute ontology framing offers a useful conceptual distinction between placement facts and semantic compute intelligence, and the ELIM architecture's polyglot design is genuinely flexible. However, this is fundamentally a single-author advocacy paper for a commercial IBM product, with all cited performance figures drawn from prior bare-metal work rather than validated in the OpenShift environment that is the paper's central subject. The contribution is more taxonomic and argumentative than technically novel.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Kubernetes and its enterprise distribution Red Hat OpenShift are the standard substrate for enterprise AI infrastructure, yet they are fundamentally container placement engines, not compute ontologies. Kubernetes answers only four questions: what containers exist, what resources they requested, where they run, and whether they are alive. These are static placement facts — they say nothing about what a workload is actually doing, how well, or who governs it. NVIDIA's acquisition of Run:ai addresses GPU-aware container scheduling but still operates at the placement layer; after pod placement, Run:ai's visibility ends. NVIDIA's own inference stack consequently requires three separate products (Run:ai for placement, Dynamo for inference routing, llm-d for distributed coordination), each patching gaps the others leave. This paper argues the gap is categorical, not incremental, and cannot be closed by Kubernetes extensions. Kueue, the strongest Kubernetes-native governance extension, is evaluated and found to operate at alpha API maturity with zero capability in five of six ontological dimensions. Methodology: The paper proposes IBM Spectrum Symphony as a compute ontology — a typed, governed, semantically rich model of the compute domain — that extends insight into OpenShift and NVIDIA AI factory infrastructure without replacing either. The ontology rests on four architectural pillars. First, ELIM (External Load Information Manager) provides the perceptual layer: site-definable executables in any language report typed metrics (Boolean, Numeric, String) with semantic direction (higher = more load vs. more capacity) at sub-second intervals, generalizing Songnian Zhou's 1987 doctoral load index from per-host CPU metrics to arbitrary resource types including GPU (38+ vLLM inference metrics), neuromorphic, and quantum. Second, consumer hierarchies form a tree-structured governance ontology with unlimited depth, per-consumer per-resource-group policy vectors, proportional lending/borrowing, and one-second rebalancing cycles (EGO_DISTRIBUTION_INTERVAL=1). Third, SOAM provides a 7-phase service lifecycle (Register through DestroyService) with independent per-phase failure policies, session continuity, and proactive boundary escalation across four levels (PROACTIVE/SEVERE/CRITICAL/HALT). Fourth, the EGO resource broker unifies OpenShift pods, bare-metal GPU hosts, cloud burst instances, and heterogeneous accelerators under a single scheduling domain. The paper compares Symphony against Kueue v0.17 and Run:ai v2.24 via feature-by-feature analysis across six ontological dimensions and three engagement modes (Ontology Enrichment, Governance, and Subsumption). ELIM agents deploy as a DaemonSet on OpenShift GPU node pools, providing perceptual depth equivalent to native Kubernetes operators without modifying OpenShift's runtime, networking, or security model. Key Results: The comparative analysis produces three principal findings. First, Kueue addresses the hierarchical governance dimension alone, at v1alpha1 API maturity with documented defects (GitHub Issue #3948 shows broken reclaimWithinCohort preemption), no periodic rebalancing of running workloads, and workload-level preemption triggered only on new pending submissions — leaving perception, routing, lifecycle, cross-substrate scaling, and coordination entirely unaddressed. Second, every Run:ai v2.24 capability is replicable within Symphony's ELIM architecture with equal or greater depth; Run:ai uses exclusively public GPU APIs (NVML, DCGM open-sourced Apache 2.0, standard CUDA interposition via LD_PRELOAD), and its fractional GPU enforcement is implementable by any engineering team using documented CUDA techniques. Third, Symphony provides capabilities with no Run:ai equivalent: per-request inference routing using four weighted ELIM scoring algorithms completing in approximately 10 ms (vs. Run:ai's pod-level placement at ~100 ms); semantic model-tier routing with a KNN classifier at 91.1% accuracy yielding 74–81% cost savings by directing queries to smaller models; KV cache-aware routing with GPFS-mediated cross-model transfer at 340 ms for 4,096-token contexts (8.2x improvement over recomputation at 2.8 s); and cross-substrate scheduling across OpenShift, bare-metal, and cloud burst under one consumer hierarchy. Production deployments at major financial institutions process over 400 million tasks per day with 1 ms scheduling latency; through LSF, the same ontology managed 27,648 GPUs across 4,608 nodes at Oak Ridge Summit. Strengths: - The compute ontology framing is analytically sharp: the distinction between placement facts and semantic, typed compute intelligence is substantive, not rhetorical, and the structural parallel to Palantir Foundry's data ontology is precise and clarifying rather than merely metaphorical. - The ELIM architecture's polyglot, minimal-contract design (any executable writing typed metrics to stdout participates) is genuinely flexible and extensible — the perception depth is bounded by implementation language capability, not by the protocol, enabling native-operator-grade Kubernetes API access from within the ontology. - The 39-year implementation lineage from Zhou's 1987 load index through Utopia, Platform LSF, and Platform Symphony provides unusual architectural continuity for a system targeting cutting-edge AI factory infrastructure, and the production deployment evidence at financial-institution scale (thousands of consumers, billions of tasks) is concrete. Weaknesses / Limitations: - This is Part I of a two-part paper; all performance figures cited (10 ms routing, 340 ms KV transfer, 91.1% accuracy) come from prior bare-metal demonstrations rather than the OpenShift integration described here — the paper explicitly defers OpenShift-context empirical validation to Part II, making the central architectural claims unvalidated in the target environment. - The paper is authored by a single IBM advocate with an evident commercial interest in positioning Symphony over NVIDIA's Run:ai/Dynamo stack; the Run:ai comparison relies on publicly documented capabilities and the open-source KAI Scheduler rather than direct testing of the commercial platform, and the analysis acknowledges the commercial product may contain undocumented capabilities not captured. - Deploying Symphony alongside OpenShift introduces a second resource management framework of comparable operational complexity (EGO broker, LIM/ELIM infrastructure, SOAM service managers), and the three engagement modes while pragmatic represent integration patterns whose real-world operational overhead — especially Mode 3, where Symphony governs Run:ai as a typed resource — is not evaluated.
Search Results
#195 — Incremental (33)

An adversarial framework with dual genetic optimization for similarity-aware matrix factorization in recommendation systems

iapress.org · karypis - new results
Why this rank: The dual-role GA addressing both neighborhood selection and hyperparameter optimization as separate offline steps is a coherent integration idea. However, the generator is architecturally a weighted k-NN predictor rather than a genuine generative model, single-seed results with no variance estimates undermine all claimed improvements, and performance degradation at higher cutoffs on sparse data reveals fundamental limitations.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Collaborative filtering recommenders face three persistent problems: (1) matrix factorization (MF) models are predominantly linear and cannot capture complex user-item relationships; (2) GAN-based recommenders suffer from training instability, mode collapse, and hyperparameter sensitivity; and (3) explicit similarity information—the cornerstone of neighborhood-based CF—is rarely integrated into generative frameworks. Prior work applying genetic algorithms to recommenders treated it as a single-objective tool (either similarity computation or hyperparameter tuning, never both), leaving a systematic gap in unified evolutionary optimization across the pipeline. Methodology: The paper proposes GaSimGAN, a three-phase collaborative filtering framework. Phase 1 computes Pearson correlation-based user-user (or item-item) similarity matrices, requiring at least τ=5 co-rated items per pair to ensure statistical reliability. Phase 2 employs a genetic algorithm (GA) to optimize the neighborhood size k and similarity weighting scheme by minimizing MAE on the training set—this runs once as a preprocessing step. Phase 3 is a GAN where the generator is a deterministic, noise-free conditional MF model: it predicts a user's interaction vector as a similarity-weighted sum over the k-nearest neighbors identified in Phase 2. The discriminator is an autoencoder (inspired by EBGAN) that uses reconstruction error as an energy function rather than binary classification, providing richer gradients. A feature matching loss term (weighted by α) aligns the latent representations of real and generated profiles. Separately, a second offline one-shot GA run optimizes the full hyperparameter configuration θ=(μG, μD, λG, λD, m, α) on a validation set before training begins; these hyperparameters are then frozen throughout GAN training. The framework has user-based and item-based variants (GaSimGAN_user and GaSimGAN_item). Baselines are CAAE, CFGAN, GANMF, and WGAN-DO. Key Results: Evaluated on MovieLens-1M (4.25% density), HetRec2011 (4.00% density), and LastFM (0.28% density) with 80/20 splits and full-candidate-set ranking. At cutoff 5, GaSimGAN_user improves Precision over GANMF_user by 4.7% on ML-1M (0.4356 vs. 0.4161) and 3% on HetRec2011 (0.6046 vs. 0.5870), though declines 4% on LastFM. GaSimGAN_item achieves a 10% Precision gain over GANMF_item on HetRec2011 (0.6636 vs. 0.6028) at cutoff 5. The GA for hyperparameter optimization outperforms Simulated Annealing and PSO on MAP across all three datasets. At cutoff 20, GaSimGAN_item shows a 17% Precision improvement over GANMF_item on ML-1M (0.3526 vs. 0.3024). However, at cutoff 50, GANMF_item surpasses GaSimGAN_item on MAP and NDCG for HetRec2011 and LastFM, while GaSimGAN_item retains a 40% Precision advantage on ML-1M. Training times are substantial: ~364 min (ML-1M), ~527 min (LastFM), and ~1,539 min (HetRec2011), with the offline GA hyperparameter search adding another ~78–117 min not counted in training time. Strengths: - The dual-role GA—applied to both neighborhood selection and hyperparameter optimization as separate offline one-shot steps—is a coherent integration that addresses two distinct sources of instability without coupling them to the adversarial training loop. - Using an autoencoder-based discriminator with hinge loss rather than a binary classifier provides smoother, more informative gradients, and the feature matching loss explicitly aligns generated and real profiles in latent space. - The paper is thorough in reporting performance across four cutoffs (5, 10, 20, 50) and three datasets with different sparsity characteristics, and honestly reports cases where GaSimGAN underperforms. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The generator is a deterministic similarity-weighted average—essentially a weighted k-NN predictor rather than a true deep generative model—which limits its capacity to synthesize novel interaction patterns beyond what is already encoded in the neighborhood structure; the "GAN" framing is architecturally thin on the generator side. - Reproducibility is severely compromised: all results come from a single run with a fixed seed due to "significant computational cost," with no variance estimates or significance testing, making it impossible to assess whether observed improvements over GANMF and WGAN-DO are statistically meaningful. - Performance degrades at higher cutoffs (k=50) on sparse datasets (HetRec2011, LastFM), where GANMF_item outperforms on MAP and NDCG, suggesting the GA-optimized local neighborhoods overfit to dense interaction regions and do not generalize to long-tail items—a critical failure mode for recommendation at scale.
Search Results
#196 — Incremental (32)

Using Synthetic Data for Machine Learning-based Childhood Vaccination Prediction in Narok, Kenya

Jimmy Bach, Yang Li, Yaqi Liu, John Sankok, Rose Kimani, Carrie B. Dolan, Julius N. Odhiambo, Haipeng Chen · arxiv.org · 10 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: This paper applies an existing tabular diffusion model (TabSyn) to vaccination prediction in a LMIC clinic setting — a valid application but methodologically incremental, using default hyperparameters on standard classifiers with an extremely sparse feature set. The privacy motivation is stated but never formally evaluated, and the finding that synthetic data roughly matches real data performance is consistent with prior TabSyn results rather than surprising.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Childhood vaccination gaps remain severe in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), particularly among nomadic populations like the Maasai in Narok County, Kenya. Community Health Partners (CHP), the largest community health provider in the region, relies on paper-based MOH 510 registries that are difficult to digitize, incomplete, and raise data re-identification risks for a small, identifiable population. Existing ML work on vaccination prediction overwhelmingly uses national-level survey data (DHS), which obscures sub-national heterogeneity and may not generalize to community clinic settings. No prior work had applied tabular diffusion-based synthetic data generation to vaccination coverage modeling, leaving the dual challenge of data scarcity and privacy unaddressed. Methodology: The authors digitized 8 years of paper vaccination records (n=6,913 after cleaning from 9,718) across four CHP clinics. Features include child age, gender, home village (with geospatial filtering to Narok County), clinic location, and day of first visit; outcomes are binary vaccination indicators for 17 vaccines. Two classifiers are evaluated — Logistic Regression and XGBoost with default hyperparameters — under three training conditions: real data only, synthetic data only, and real+synthetic combined. Synthetic data are generated using TabSyn (Zhang et al., ICLR 2024), a latent diffusion model that first trains a VAE (with Transformer encoder/decoder) to embed mixed-type tabular data into a continuous latent space, then applies score-based diffusion in that latent space. One TabSyn model is trained per vaccine (17 total), each on the training split only. Five independent synthetic samples are drawn per model to estimate variance. SMOTE oversampling is tested as an additional ablation for class imbalance. Synthetic data quality is assessed via column-wise KS-test / Total Variation Distance and pairwise Pearson / Contingency Similarity scores. Key Results: For early-schedule vaccines with high coverage (BCG: 95.6%), XGBoost achieves F1=0.885, Precision=0.924 on real data; training on synthetic data alone yields F1=0.887±0.007 — effectively no degradation. For mid-schedule vaccines (ROTA1), F1 on real data is 0.784 and on synthetic 0.779±0.004. Performance drops meaningfully for late-schedule vaccines with severe class imbalance and low coverage (MR1 coverage 37.8%: F1=0.729 real, 0.705±0.011 synthetic; MR2 coverage 13.7%: F1=0.817 real, 0.810±0.005 synthetic). Augmenting real data with synthetic data does not consistently improve over real-only training. TabSyn achieves column-wise density scores of 0.963 (age) to 0.999 (vaccination outcome) and pairwise correlation scores averaging ~0.95, with age-related pairs being the weakest (age/village: 0.853). The VAE+diffusion models train in ~90 minutes on CPU and generate a full synthetic dataset in ~20 seconds. Feature importance is consistent between real- and synthetic-trained XGBoost models, with first visit day, age, and gender as top predictors. Strengths: - First application of tabular diffusion-based synthetic data (TabSyn) to vaccination coverage prediction in a LMIC community-clinic setting, directly addressing both data scarcity and privacy for a vulnerable nomadic population. - Rigorous evaluation of synthetic data utility across 17 vaccines and three training regimes (real, synthetic, mixed), with 5-sample variance estimation — providing a thorough empirical characterization of where synthetic substitution holds and where it degrades. - Practically grounded: the entire pipeline (digitization, model training, sampling) is demonstrated to run on commodity CPU hardware in under two hours, making it feasible for low-resource deployment without cloud infrastructure. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The feature set is extremely sparse — only age, gender, village, clinic, and first visit day — because the paper registries contain little else. This likely explains the modest F1 scores (0.68–0.89) and limits the models' actionability for identifying at-risk children beyond broad demographic strata. - Hyperparameters for both classifiers and TabSyn are left at defaults to simplify cross-condition comparison, but this conflates model quality with training data quality; tuned baselines could meaningfully alter conclusions about synthetic data parity. - The synthetic-data privacy claim is asserted but not formally quantified: no membership inference, attribute inference, or differential privacy analysis is performed, leaving the actual privacy guarantee uncharacterized despite this being a stated primary objective.
Citations 2604.08902 PDF
#197 — Incremental (32)

Data Ontologies for Data-Driven Decision-Making

nap.nationalacademies.org · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: The DATA Framework addresses a real practitioner gap by elevating organizational and cultural factors alongside technical ones, but the work is essentially a qualitative synthesis of surveys (9 respondents) and case studies producing guidelines rather than new technical contributions. No quantitative evidence of ontology benefits is provided, and the recommended ROI metrics are deferred entirely to future work.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: State Departments of Transportation (DOTs) face a persistent challenge converting accumulated data into actionable business assets. Their information systems are built on siloed architectures with independent, distributed data sources that do not integrate across functional areas. Legacy systems — while rich in embedded organizational knowledge — are costly to maintain and resist integration with modern platforms, creating data silos and knowledge loss. As performance-based management demands cross-functional analytics, the absence of semantic interoperability between legacy and emerging systems impedes data-driven decision-making. No established, practitioner-ready framework existed to guide DOTs through ontology development; prior knowledge engineering solutions were largely technology-focused and failed to capture business semantics. This NCHRP Project 23-27 study fills that gap by developing a conceptual framework and implementation guide for transportation agency staff at all organizational levels. Methodology: The research proceeded in two phases. Phase I (Tasks 1–5) involved a targeted literature review spanning early 2000s to present covering ontology theory, knowledge organization frameworks, knowledge engineering solutions, and State DOT legacy system examples; targeted surveys sent to 18 State DOTs (9 responded: Arkansas, Florida, Indiana, Iowa, Minnesota, Texas, Utah, Vermont, Washington); and follow-up in-depth interviews via Microsoft Teams. Phase II (Tasks 6–11) synthesized findings into five case studies drawn from real DOT implementations (Indiana HCIOntology, WSDOT data catalog, Disease Ontology, transit ontology, FDOT cloud migration), developed an ontology development methodology with competency questions, and produced the final guide. The central methodological contribution is the DATA Framework — a four-pillar cyclical process: Designing a plan (clarify purpose, confirm domain, classify stakeholders, create plan), Assembling the ontology (define scope, gather and classify information), Testing the ontology (verification cases, validation, deployment), and Adapting to change (repositories, update protocols, versioning, communication). The framework draws on the Domain-Entity-Relation-Attribute (DERA) knowledge organization framework, Computational Sophistication (CS) approaches for extracting business semantics from legacy systems, and standards including RDF/OWL and IFC schemas. Key Results: The survey revealed that most State DOTs have little to no formal ontology experience — the majority of respondents were novices — with only one agency (WSDOT) operating a maintained, production ontology using WebProtégé and the GIST upper ontology. Key empirical findings include: (1) legacy systems universally lack internal data standards and documentation, obstructing ontology development; (2) federal compliance mandates (e.g., FHWA's ARNOLD requirements) are the dominant driver of legacy data migration efforts, not proactive knowledge management; (3) common migration challenges are poor data quality, limited access, staff turnover-induced knowledge loss, and vendor system incompatibility; and (4) no DOT had documented enterprise-wide ontologies supporting cross-functional decision-making. Qualitative case study analysis identified best practices organized along the DATA framework pillars: leveraging existing upper ontologies (GIST, Disease Ontology) as starting points, using SPARQL queries and HermiT reasoner for validation, building ontology libraries (analogous to BioPortal), and embedding ontologies into applications (e.g., Indiana's HCIOntology dynamically generating inspection forms). Recommended future research focuses on ROI quantification tools, stakeholder collaboration protocols for cross-agency ontology standardization, and capacity-building programs. Strengths: - The DATA Framework provides a practically grounded, cyclical methodology explicitly tailored to the organizational realities (staff turnover, leadership buy-in, resource constraints) of public transportation agencies, filling a genuine gap not addressed by technology-centric knowledge engineering literature. - The synthesis of survey data from 9 State DOTs with diverse maturity levels and the accompanying detailed case studies (VTrans, WSDOT, FDOT, MnDOT, ARDOT, Iowa DOT) provide rich, context-specific practitioner guidance that generic ontology literature cannot offer. - Organizational cultural factors — governance structure, leadership support, workforce management, and reward communication — are treated as co-equal with technical factors, correcting a known failure mode in prior implementation efforts. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The report produces no quantitative evidence of ontology benefits (efficiency gains, cost savings, decision quality improvements); recommended ROI metrics are deferred to future research, leaving practitioners without defensible business cases to secure leadership buy-in. - The survey sample (9 of 18 targeted DOTs responded) is small and self-selected toward agencies already engaged with data modernization, likely overstating sector readiness and understating the challenges facing less data-mature agencies; findings cannot be generalized across all 50 state DOTs.
Search Results
#198 — Incremental (32)

Digital Technologies for Steelmaking

sustainsteel.ac.uk · semantic integration ontologies - new results
Why this rank: Scores 32 for its structured connection of Industry 4.0 reference architectures and Hybrid AI to steelmaking, which provides useful cross-domain synthesis. However, as a survey report with no novel technical contributions, no benchmarks, and only one steelmaking-specific Hybrid AI paper cited, the intellectual contribution is limited to organizing existing knowledge for an industrial audience.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: The steel industry produced 1.88 billion tonnes of steel in 2024, generating over 3.5 billion tonnes of CO2 — roughly 11% of global emissions. Achieving decarbonization targets by 2050 requires not only new metallurgical processes but also fundamental digital transformation of steelmaking operations. The industry still largely operates under Industry 3.0 architectures based on siloed, hierarchical control systems (ERP → MES → SCADA → PLC) that require costly multi-layer integration for every new asset. Meanwhile, although AI and ML are extensively deployed for process control, quality prediction, and fault detection, they suffer from critical limitations: black-box opacity, inability to incorporate domain knowledge, and lack of context-awareness. This state-of-the-art report, produced by the EPSRC-funded SUSTAIN hub in partnership with British Steel, Tata Steel, Liberty Steel, and Sheffield Forgemasters, surveys the digital technologies necessary to bridge these gaps — specifically Industry 4.0 reference architectures, Asset Administration Shells (AAS), and Hybrid AI combining ML with semantic reasoning. Methodology: The report is organized as a structured literature survey with two main pillars. The first covers Industry 4.0 (I4.0) digital infrastructure, centering on the Reference Architecture Model for Industry 4.0 (RAMI 4.0) — a 3D framework with axes for architectural layers (Asset, Integration, Communication, Information, Functional, Business), lifecycle/value stream (type vs. instance), and hierarchy levels (Field Device to Connected World). Within RAMI 4.0, the report focuses on the Asset Administration Shell (AAS) — the standardized digital representation of a physical asset that enables I4.0-compliant interoperability — surveying AAS implementations in manufacturing and steelmaking, including Semantic AAS (SAAS) using RDF, OWL, and ontologies. The second pillar surveys AI/ML in steelmaking using a Scopus database query ("artificial intelligence" OR "machine learning" AND steel/steelmaking), yielding 11,523 documents and 123 survey papers from 2020–2025. The report documents ML applications across blast furnaces, EAF, BOF, continuous casting, rolling, and surface defect detection, then systematically analyzes data limitations and interpretability failures before surveying the emerging field of Hybrid AI (Neuro-symbolic AI), which combines statistical ML with semantic reasoning via ontologies and SWRL rules. Key Results: The Scopus survey reveals explosive growth in AI/ML steelmaking publications: 14 review papers in 2021 rising to 43 in 2024, with 26 already indexed by May 2025. ML techniques span neural networks, SVMs, random forests, deep learning, and YOLO-based object detection, applied to datasets including SEVERSTAL (12,568 surface defect images) and Northeastern University (1,800 images). AAS implementations are found to vary widely across standards (RDF, OWL, OPC-UA, AutomationML) with no dominant approach; 13 SAAS papers are surveyed and mapped to steelmaking applicability. The survey of Hybrid AI identifies approximately 10 relevant works combining ML with ontological reasoning — covering domains from landslide detection to Alzheimer's diagnosis to smart manufacturing — but only one paper (Beden et al. 2023) directly targets steelmaking, converting a Random Forest model into SWRL rules applied over an RDF knowledge graph of cold-rolling data to produce explainable, human-in-the-loop predictive analytics. Key data limitations documented include: incomplete or unstructured operator annotations, sensor noise requiring filtering, and data distributed across incompatible schemas requiring expert integration. The core ML limitation is the black-box problem: high-accuracy deep neural networks are not interpretable, while post-hoc tools (LIME, SHAP) explain outputs without making models inherently transparent. Strengths: - Systematically connects each AAS implementation surveyed to a concrete steelmaking use case, providing practical transfer guidance rather than merely cataloging general manufacturing results. - Clearly motivates the Hybrid AI direction by tracing a logical chain: I4.0 requires interoperability → interoperability requires semantic context → pure ML lacks semantic context → Neuro-symbolic AI bridges the gap. - Grounded in active industrial partnerships (British Steel, Tata Steel), lending credibility to the problem framing and ensuring the survey targets industrially relevant challenges. Weaknesses / Limitations: - As a survey report rather than a research paper, it presents no novel technical contributions; the Hybrid AI section in particular is thin, with most cited works coming from unrelated domains (healthcare, social media, food quality), and only one steelmaking-specific hybrid AI implementation referenced. - Quantitative comparison of methods is absent throughout: no benchmarks, no performance numbers contrasting approaches, and no analysis of which ML or AAS techniques perform best under what conditions in steelmaking.

#199 — Incremental (31)

NovoExpert-1: State-of-the-Art CYP2D6 Prediction via Message-Passing Neural Networks on the TDC ADMET Benchmark

chemrxiv.org · 5 new citations to your articles
Why this rank: NovoExpert-2 is a competent engineering contribution that concatenates established molecular fingerprints with pretrained GIN embeddings and applies CatBoost, achieving narrow SOTA margins on 5 of 22 TDC endpoints. The honest correction of the prior NovoExpert-1 metric error is commendable scientific practice, but the methodology is a straightforward combination of existing tools without novel algorithmic ideas.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Accurate in silico prediction of ADMET (absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, toxicity) properties is a central challenge in computational drug discovery. The Therapeutics Data Commons (TDC) ADMET benchmark provides 22 standardized endpoints for rigorous cross-method comparison. Prior leaderboard leaders have used gradient-boosted trees on concatenated molecular fingerprints (the MapLight family). This work seeks to push further by combining fingerprint features with pretrained GIN graph embeddings and introducing a complementary D-MPNN method, while also correcting a metric error from the authors' own prior report (NovoExpert-1 mistakenly compared AUROC against an AUPRC leaderboard baseline for CYP2D6). Methodology: NovoExpert-2 is a dual-method system. The primary method concatenates MapLight-style features — Morgan ECFP (1024 bits), Avalon (1024 bits), ErG (315 dims), and RDKit 2D descriptors (~210 dims) — with 300-dimensional GIN supervised masking embeddings from the DGL-LifeSci pretrained model zoo, yielding a 2,873-dimensional feature vector fed to a CatBoost ensemble (5 random seeds, depth 8, learning rate 0.03, up to 2,000 iterations with early stopping). Class imbalance is addressed via scale_pos_weight. The secondary method is a Chemprop v2 directed message-passing neural network (D-MPNN), also ensembled over 5 seeds. For each of the 22 TDC endpoints, the authors train both methods under the official TDC evaluation protocol and report whichever method scores higher. Critically, they correctly apply per-endpoint metrics (AUPRC for CYP Veith endpoints, AUROC for others, MAE or Spearman for regression), matching TDC's official benchmark conventions throughout. Key Results: NovoExpert-2 achieves new state-of-the-art on 5 of 22 TDC ADMET endpoints. The four wins using CatBoost+MapLight+GIN are: CYP2D6 Veith (AUPRC 0.778 vs. 0.750, +0.028), CYP3A4 Veith (AUPRC 0.916 vs. 0.900, +0.016), CYP3A4 Substrate Carbonmangels (AUROC 0.660 vs. 0.656, +0.004), and Clearance Hepatocyte AZ (Spearman 0.511 vs. 0.487, +0.024). The fifth win uses Chemprop v2: DILI (AUROC 0.922 ensemble vs. 0.916, +0.006). On the remaining 17 endpoints, NovoExpert-2 falls below SOTA, with the largest gaps on VDss Lombardo (−0.157), Half Life Obach (−0.135), and Bioavailability Ma (−0.097) — all small-dataset regression tasks (<1,200 training examples). The authors also show that their prior NovoExpert-1 CYP2D6 claim was wrong: the reported 0.864 AUROC cannot be compared to the leaderboard's 0.750 AUPRC, and re-evaluation gives approximately 0.64 AUPRC, which would not have beaten SOTA. Strengths: - Rigorous metric discipline: the paper explicitly corrects its own prior AUROC/AUPRC confusion and demonstrates throughout that endpoint-specific metric alignment is essential for valid leaderboard comparisons on imbalanced datasets. - The dual-method design is well-motivated: fingerprint+GBT for substructure-driven tasks (CYP metabolism), D-MPNN for mechanistically complex toxicity phenotypes (DILI), with transparent disclosure of both scores when method selection occurs. - The 5-seed ensemble protocol with early stopping per seed provides reliable variance estimates and mitigates overfitting — especially valuable on small-dataset endpoints like CYP3A4 Substrate (534 training examples), where ensemble averaging yields +0.011 above per-seed mean. Weaknesses / Limitations: - Per-endpoint method selection (best of two model families) introduces test-set selection bias that is only partially mitigated by disclosing both scores; a proper held-out validation set for model selection prior to test evaluation is not implemented. - No per-endpoint hyperparameter tuning is performed within either model family — a single CatBoost and a single Chemprop configuration are applied to all 22 tasks, which likely leaves significant performance on the table for small-dataset regression endpoints where specialized architectures dominate. - The work is a single-author preprint without independent reproduction, the claimed SOTA values are snapshot-dependent (April 2026 leaderboard), and the improvement margins on several winning endpoints are narrow enough (e.g., +0.004 on CYP3A4 Substrate, +0.006 on DILI) that leaderboard updates could easily overturn them.
Citations
#200 — Incremental (31)

LEMON: LLM -enabled monitoring for microservices orchestration

orca.cardiff.ac.uk · inference optimize llm faster inference throughput - new results
Why this rank: LEMON applies a straightforward two-stage SLM classifier plus RAG pipeline to microservices monitoring — a reasonable but incremental combination of existing techniques. The intent taxonomy covers only four metrics, evaluation uses a single Minikube cluster with author-only evaluators, and no alternative approaches are compared against, making the contribution difficult to distinguish from a standard application of fine-tuned LLMs to a domain-specific task.
Show full summary & analysis
Problem & Motivation: Modern microservice architectures deployed across cloud-edge environments generate enormous volumes of telemetry that overwhelm traditional monitoring tools like Prometheus, which rely on hand-crafted PromQL queries, static threshold alerting, and offer no contextual reasoning. Managing these systems requires deep query-language expertise from operators and cannot adapt dynamically to evolving workloads. The paper argues for a paradigm shift toward intent-driven, self-managing orchestration, and positions intelligent natural language monitoring as the essential "sense" capability that must precede autonomous "act" capabilities such as auto-scaling or remediation. Methodology: LEMON introduces a four-component architecture: (1) a User Interaction Module offering ChatUI, CLI, and web portal interfaces; (2) a Monitored Microservice Cluster (Kubernetes) as the data source; (3) a Data Aggregator that periodically queries Prometheus, transforms raw metrics into standardized units, and indexes structured JSON snapshots into a Qdrant vector database using Multilingual-E5-large-instruct embeddings (1024 dimensions, cosine similarity); and (4) a two-stage LLM-Query Service. The first stage uses a fine-tuned Small Language Model from the Qwen3 family (0.6B–4B parameters) for intent classification, producing structured JSON objects identifying monitoring intents (cpu_utilization, memory_utilization, cpu_core_count, energy_consumption) and entities (pods, nodes, time windows). The second stage constructs a RAG prompt by retrieving matching vector DB snapshots using exact-match filters derived from the classified JSON, then passes this enriched prompt to a separate LocalLLM for human-readable response synthesis. Fine-tuning uses Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) via the Unsloth framework, enabling 1–9 hour training cycles on consumer-grade hardware (RTX 4090). A key architectural choice is strict read-only pre-materialized retrieval, deliberately avoiding live tool-use or PromQL generation to eliminate prompt injection risks and hallucination of executable code. The paper also contributes a 9-dimension taxonomy of LLM-enabled microservices orchestration covering orchestration functionality, LLM integration style, interaction model, training/adaptation, frameworks, deployment strategy, use cases, identified problems, and security. Key Results: Fine-tuning dramatically improves JSON validity rate: all LEMON models exceed 98% across model sizes, while base Qwen3 models degrade to 80.7% for the 4B variant due to reasoning chains exhausting the 2048-token context window before completing JSON output. Accuracy (strict full-object match against ground truth) improves by 21.1% for the 0.6B model (39.9%→61.0%), 14.3% for the 1.7B model (62.9%→77.2%), and 15.3% for the 4B model (63.0%→78.3%). End-to-end human expert fidelity evaluation (4 experts, 20 queries, 5 repetitions each, 0–5 scale) shows overall fidelity scores of 3.15 (0.6B), 3.58 (1.7B), and 3.91 (4B) for the LocalLLM response generation stage; accuracy sub-scores are notably lower at 2.28, 2.95, and 3.53 respectively, while all models score above 4.3 on linguistic formulation. Vector DB retrieval contributes only 0.062 seconds latency. Total end-to-end latency ranges from 3.9s (0.6B+0.6B) to 13.4s (4B+4B). The paper recommends LEMON-1.7B + Qwen3-1.7B as the practical deployment configuration, achieving 7.675s latency with substantially better classification accuracy than the 0.6B tier. Strengths: - The two-stage architecture (fast SLM classifier → RAG-grounded response generator) is well-motivated: it avoids both the latency of invoking a large model for a simple classification step and the hallucination/injection risks of live tool-use or generative PromQL. - The empirical sweep across all nine model-size combinations provides genuinely useful deployment guidance for practitioners, especially at the resource-constrained edge. - GRPO-based fine-tuning via Unsloth on consumer hardware is a practical and reproducible contribution, with training completing in 1–9 hours. Weaknesses / Limitations: - The intent taxonomy is extremely narrow — only four monitoring intents (CPU utilization, CPU core count, memory utilization, energy consumption) — limiting practical coverage and generalization; the system cannot handle custom time-range aggregations or metrics outside the pre-materialized schema. - All experiments are conducted on a single simulated Minikube cluster on one workstation; there is no evaluation on real multi-node, multi-cluster, or heterogeneous edge-cloud production environments, making external validity claims speculative. - Human fidelity evaluation is performed by the authors' own research team of four domain experts rather than independent evaluators, and absolute accuracy scores remain modest (3.53/5 for the best model), indicating significant room for improvement before production deployment. - The paper does not compare LEMON against any alternative end-to-end approaches (e.g., direct RAG without intent classification, Tool-Use LLM agents, or NL-to-PromQL translation systems), making it difficult to assess whether the architectural choices are actually superior to simpler baselines.
